findings.claim: (1) The reverted workaround calls model_chunk.free_overlap_buffers() then torch.cuda.empty_cache() unconditionally on every save_checkpoint_and_time() invocation, regardless of whether async checkpointing is enabled. (2) torch.cuda.empty_cache() forces a full device synchronization and releases all cached blocks back to the CUDA driver; on the very next training iteration those blocks must be re-requested from the driver (cudaMalloc), which is expensive and stalls the training stream. free_overlap_buffers() additionally waits on any pending param_gather_handle and drops the overlap param-gather buffers, forcing them to be re-allocated. This adds a recurring per-checkpoint throughput hit that is only justified for the async-save D2H-headroom scenario the comment describes. (3) Guard the block so it only runs when async checkpoint save is actually in use (e.g. args.async_save / ckpt_format-dependent), or document that the revert is a temporary correctness workaround with a TODO to remove once the async worker OOM is fixed upstream.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-iteration step time immediately after a checkpoint save with and without this block (async_save on vs off), and measure checkpoint interval throughput regression from empty_cache-induced cudaMalloc churn.

findings.claim: (1) torch.cuda.empty_cache() is called on every checkpoint save even when no overlap buffers were freed (e.g. non-distributed-optimizer / no overlap_param_gather configs where free_overlap_buffers is a no-op). (2) In those configurations empty_cache provides little benefit but still pays the sync + allocator-release + re-cudaMalloc cost. (3) Consider only calling empty_cache when at least one model chunk actually freed buffers, or gate on ddp_config.overlap_param_gather.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare checkpoint-adjacent iteration latency with overlap_param_gather=False to quantify the wasted empty_cache cost.

findings.claim: (1) `args.skip_prompt_log_probs` is set to True unconditionally in `MegatronLocal.launch`, but `args.return_log_probs` is also set to True, and `get_inference_config_from_model_and_args` now computes `materialize_only_last_token_logits = not (args.return_log_probs and not args.skip_prompt_log_probs)`. With both True this evaluates to `not (True and not True)` = `not False` = True, correctly enabling last-token-only materialization for RL. This is consistent with the per-request `skip_prompt_log_probs=True` sent in `base_generate`. The logic is coherent; the risk is only if some RL request path does NOT set per-request `skip_prompt_log_probs=True` while the engine-level `materialize_only_last_token_logits=True` — the engine asserts (dynamic_engine.py ~1013) that materialize_only_last_token_logits must be False when prompt log probs are requested, which would raise at runtime. (2) If any RL code path issues a request needing prompt log probs, it will now hit that assertion and fail. (3) Recommend a comment/assert tying the two settings together, or validating that all RL requests always pass skip_prompt_log_probs=True.

(1) The new expression `materialize_only_last_token_logits = not (args.return_log_probs and not args.skip_prompt_log_probs)` changes behavior only for callers who set both `return_log_probs=True` and `skip_prompt_log_probs=True`: previously that combination gave `materialize_only_last_token_logits=False` (full logits materialized for all tokens), now it gives True (only last token). (2) For a user who was relying on receiving full per-token logits while also skipping prompt log probs, this silently changes what is materialized and could change downstream generation-token logprob availability. However, skip_prompt_log_probs=True inherently means prompt logits are unneeded, so this is the intended optimization. (3) Confirm no existing non-RL script depended on `return_log_probs=True` + `skip_prompt_log_probs=True` while still needing full-sequence logits; if so document the change.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Inference throughput / peak memory for a long-prompt short-generation workload with return_log_probs=True and skip_prompt_log_probs=True, comparing before/after (expect reduced logit-projection compute and memory since only last-token logits are materialized).

findings.claim: (1) In FusedSharedExpertMLP._fused_grouped_swiglu_forward, the TP/SP collective calls (gather_from_sequence_parallel_region, copy_to_tensor_model_parallel_region, reduce_scatter_to_sequence_parallel_region, reduce_from_tensor_model_parallel_region) are invoked WITHOUT passing group=self.tp_group, unlike the non-fused overlap path (pre_forward_comm/post_forward_comm) which explicitly passes group=self.tp_group. (2) These functions default group=None which resolves to the global tensor-model-parallel group from parallel_state, not necessarily self.tp_group. When the shared expert receives a distinct tp_group via pg_collection (the whole point of the ProcessGroupCollection plumbing), the fused path will scatter/gather/reduce over the wrong process group, producing silently wrong activations and gradients across TP ranks. (3) Pass group=self.tp_group to all four collective calls in _fused_grouped_swiglu_forward to match the overlap path and the intended tp_group semantics.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run TP=2 shared-expert forward/backward with use_grouped_gemm_for_shared_expert=True and compare output/grads against SharedExpertMLP baseline for numerical equivalence.

findings.claim: (1) In FusedSharedExpertMLP.forward, the shared-expert gate is applied as output = output * sigmoid(linear(hidden_states, gate_weight)) using the raw pre-TP-comm hidden_states, while the non-fused SharedExpertMLP.forward applies the gate identically on hidden_states — this part matches. However, in the overlapped path the fused linear_fc1_forward_and_act consumes self.cached_fc1_input (the TP-gathered input), whereas gate_score in the base pre_forward_comm is computed from the original ungathered input; verify the fused overlapped path still computes gate_score in pre_forward_comm (inherited) on the ungathered input and applies it in get_output. Since FusedSharedExpertMLP does not override pre_forward_comm/get_output, gate handling in overlap mode relies on inherited behavior — confirm cached_fc1_input semantics are unchanged so the inherited gate path stays correct. (3) Add a shape/dtype assertion or unit test covering use_shared_expert_gate=True with the fused overlapped path to guard the interaction.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test FusedSharedExpertMLP with moe_shared_expert_gate=True in both overlap and non-overlap modes vs baseline.

findings.claim: (1) _fused_grouped_swiglu_no_comm allocates a new tokens_per_expert tensor via torch.full and a scales tensor via torch.ones on every forward call. (2) These are tiny host-initiated device allocations inside the training-loop forward; torch.full/torch.ones with dynamic total_tokens create per-step allocations and, more importantly, a data-dependent tensor shape (scales of shape [total_tokens]) that can break CUDA graph capture and add kernel-launch overhead. (3) Consider caching/reusing these buffers or documenting CUDA-graph incompatibility; at minimum verify this path is excluded from CUDA graph capture.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile shared-expert forward step time with/without fused path and check CUDA graph capture compatibility.

findings.claim: (1) _fused_grouped_swiglu_ops is stored as a 1-tuple (self._make_fused_grouped_swiglu_ops(),) and later unpacked with (ops,)=... / seq,=... . (2) Wrapping a single nn.Module in a tuple adds indirection with no abstraction value and is easy to misread; the reason (avoiding nn.Module auto-registration of the child so weights aren't double-counted) is undocumented. (3) Either store it directly as an attribute with object.__setattr__ / a clear comment explaining the tuple-wrapping is deliberate to avoid Module registration, or add a comment documenting the intent.

(1) Two new config fields use_grouped_gemm_for_shared_expert and moe_shared_expert_glu_interleave_size are added with defaults (False / None) so existing configs are unaffected — good. (2) However, the fused path hard-requires fp8/fp4 with mxfp8/nvfp4 recipe (raises ValueError otherwise in _get_fused_grouped_swiglu_recipe) and TE>=2.14.0; enabling use_grouped_gemm_for_shared_expert in a bf16 run will raise at first forward rather than at config validation time. (3) Add validation in TransformerConfig.__post_init__ (or arg validation) that use_grouped_gemm_for_shared_expert requires fp8/fp4 + supported recipe and moe_shared_expert_glu_interleave_size set, to fail fast at construction rather than mid-training.

(1) _make_fused_grouped_swiglu_ops builds GroupedLinear with device='meta' then assigns op.weight0 = fc1_weight/fc2_weight to reuse existing parameters. (2) The fc1 out_features is set to fc1_weight.size(0) * tp_world_size while fc2 out_features is fc2_weight.size(0) (no *tp_world_size) — this asymmetry is intentional (fc1 column-parallel, fc2 row-parallel) but undocumented and fragile; if the resharding/interleave assumptions change it will silently produce wrong shapes. (3) Add a comment explaining why fc1 uses *tp_world_size (column-parallel logical full width) and fc2 does not, and add a shape assertion that op.weight0 matches the declared in/out features.

(1) When use_symm_mem is enabled, self._unsharded_model_weight.rendezvous(gather_axis) is called inside unshard_parameters() on every forward pass, and partial_grad.rendezvous(reduce_axis) is called on every reduce_gradients() call. symm_mem.rendezvous() is a synchronizing, group-wide operation that registers/exchanges buffer handles. (2) Because the unsharded buffer's storage is released and reallocated each iteration (release_storage/reallocate_storage), the symmetric-memory handle must be re-established each step, adding a per-step, per-parameter-group host+collective overhead that can erode or negate the throughput benefit of symmetric-memory kernels, and adds a synchronization point that harms comm/compute overlap. (3) Consider allocating the staging buffers once with persistent storage under the pool and rendezvousing them a single time at construction (or caching the rendezvous result keyed by storage), rather than per-step. If per-step rendezvous is truly required by the storage lifecycle, document the cost and benchmark net throughput vs. the ring path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile end-to-end FSDP step time with use_symm_mem=True vs False on 8+ GPUs, measuring per-step host overhead and NCCL kernel time; verify symmetric-memory staging yields net throughput gain rather than being offset by repeated rendezvous.

findings.claim: (1) In the symmetric-memory path, gradient reduction uses ReduceOp.SUM followed by an explicit local_buffer.div_(grad_divisor) to emulate AVG, where grad_divisor = mesh.size(reduce_axis). When main_grads_dtype is a low-precision dtype (e.g. bf16, the default when main_grads_dtype is unset), the reduce-scatter now accumulates the SUM of world_size gradients into bf16 before dividing, whereas the default AVG path divides during/before accumulation. (2) SUM-then-divide in bf16 changes the numeric result relative to NCCL AVG: larger intermediate magnitudes lose more mantissa bits and, at large world sizes, can overflow or lose precision, silently altering gradients and potentially causing training divergence that does not appear in the small 2-rank parity test. (3) Perform the div_ in fp32 (upcast the reduced buffer, divide, cast back) or, when main_grad dtype is low precision, keep the AVG semantics via a fp32 staging buffer for the reduce-scatter. At minimum add a comment/assertion documenting the precision assumption and test at larger world sizes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-parameter gradient values (max abs diff) between AVG path and SUM+div_ symmetric path in bf16 across world_size in {2,8,32}; assert relative error stays within acceptable tolerance.

findings.claim: (1) grad_divisor is computed for both the SUM and AVG branches (as mesh.size(reduce_axis) or 1), and reduce_axis is recomputed via changed_mesh_axis even in the default AVG path where it is only needed to conditionally call rendezvous. (2) In the non-symm_mem path, partial_op is AVG so grad_divisor is always 1 and the div_ calls are no-ops, but the changed_mesh_axis scan and RuntimeError guard now run on every reduce_gradients() call for all users, adding minor overhead and complexity to the common path. (3) Guard the reduce_axis computation and grad_divisor logic behind `if self._symm_mem_pool is not None` so the default path is unchanged, keeping the AVG-only behavior isolated.

(1) The MTP hidden-states caching in forward() is now guarded by `if inference_context is not None:`; if `inference_context` is None while `in_inference_mode or is_spec_decode` is True, the hidden states are silently NOT cached (previously `self._decoder_hidden_states_cache = hidden_states` always ran). (2) Downstream MTP speculative decoding relies on the cached hidden states; a silent skip could produce wrong/absent MTP output or a downstream None. (3) Add an explicit assert that `inference_context is not None` in the inference/spec-decode branch, or document why None is safe here. Verify all inference callers always pass a context.

(1) The MTP decoder hidden-states buffer moved from a model attribute `unwrapped_model._decoder_hidden_states_cache` to a context attribute `context.mtp_decoder_hidden_states`. Any external/downstream code or user scripts that read/set `_decoder_hidden_states_cache` on the model (a public-ish serial-MTP contract) will silently break: `has_mtp` will now be False and MTP hidden states will never be picked up. (2) This is an API/contract change in megatron/core with no backward-compat shim/deprecation. (3) Consider keeping a property alias on the model that proxies to `context.mtp_decoder_hidden_states`, or document the migration and grep for external consumers.

(1) `mtp_decoder_hidden_states` is pre-allocated to worst-case `(max_tokens, 1, hidden_size)` for block-scope CUDA graphs. On every forward the `copy_()` writes only `[:hidden_states.shape[0]]` but the buffer persists at full max_tokens size for the entire session. (2) With large max_tokens (default 16384) and large hidden_size, this is a persistent GPU allocation (e.g. 16384*8192*2B = 256MB in bf16) reserved even during decode steps that only use a few rows. (3) Confirm this worst-case sizing is required; if only decode-sized batches use block-scope graphs, size the buffer to the CUDA-graph max decode token count instead of max_tokens to save memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory delta with block-scope CUDA graphs enabled vs disabled for MTP inference across hidden_size in {4096,8192} and max_tokens=16384.

findings.claim: (1) The block-scope branch does `inference_context.mtp_decoder_hidden_states[: hidden_states.shape[0]].copy_(hidden_states)` guarded only by an assert that the buffer is not None. If `hidden_states.shape[0]` exceeds `max_tokens` (buffer's first dim) the slice silently truncates to the buffer length and `copy_()` will raise a shape-mismatch, or worse silently mis-fill if broadcasting rules apply. (2) A shape mismatch during graph capture is hard to debug. (3) Add an explicit assert `hidden_states.shape[0] <= inference_context.mtp_decoder_hidden_states.shape[0]` with a clear message, and assert hidden_size/dtype match.

(1) The MTP hidden-states caching logic (block-scope copy_() vs eager direct-assign) is now duplicated verbatim in both gpt_model.py and hybrid_model.py. (2) Duplicated correctness-sensitive logic tends to drift (e.g. one gets an assert/shape check the other lacks). (3) Extract a small helper (e.g. `cache_mtp_decoder_hidden_states(inference_context, config, hidden_states)`) shared by both models.

The new `elif self.is_hybrid_model and self.config.enable_prefix_caching:` branch (memory-only mode) only emits a warning; it does NOT force `prefix_skip_tokens = 0` as the warning text and the added test's comment claim. The warning says 'Prefill skipping is therefore disabled (prefix_skip_tokens is forced to 0)', but no code enforces this. In this branch `self.mamba_slot_allocator` stays None. Inspecting the prefill path (lines ~2627-2665 and ~2923-2941), `prefix_skip_tokens` is computed from `num_matched * block_size_tokens` based on KV-prefix matches independent of the Mamba allocator, and the Mamba state restore at line 2925 is guarded by `restore_block_count > 0 and self.mamba_slot_allocator is not None`. If KV prefixes are matched (which memory-only mode explicitly deduplicates) then `prefix_skip_tokens > 0` and prefill tokens are skipped, but the Mamba recurrent state is NEVER restored (allocator is None) — the skipped tokens' Mamba state is silently lost, producing incorrect model outputs, not merely 'forgone latency benefit'. Verify whether KV-prefix matching can produce nonzero `prefix_skip_tokens` when the mamba allocator is None; if so this is a correctness bug (silent divergence for hybrid models in memory-only mode). Fix: either genuinely force `prefix_skip_tokens = 0` on the hybrid+no-mamba-budget path, or disable KV-prefix matching for hybrid models in this mode.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit/e2e test: hybrid model, enable_prefix_caching=True, prefix_caching_mamba_gb=None, submit two requests sharing a long prefix, assert the second request's generated tokens match a no-cache baseline (correctness), not just that a warning is emitted.

findings.claim: Behavior change: when the Mamba prefix-cache budget is too small for even one durable slot, `_allocate_mamba_cache` now raises `ValueError` instead of logging a warning and returning (previously it fell back to `mamba_slot_allocator = None`, i.e. graceful degradation). This is a backward-incompatible change: existing configs/scripts that set a small `prefix_caching_mamba_gb` (e.g. the tests previously used 0.001/0.05 GB) that used to silently disable Mamba caching will now hard-fail at context construction. This is defensible (the old path could OOM by over-allocating scratch), but it should be called out as a breaking config change and the raise threshold now also depends on `max_requests` (scratch scales with it), so configs that worked before can break purely due to auto-derived max_requests. Consider whether a documented fallback-to-memory-only path is preferable to a hard raise for robustness in serving.

The durable-slot sizing `max_slots = (total_bytes - scratch_bytes) // per_slot_bytes` reserves scratch space, but the actual scratch buffers in `MambaSlotAllocator.__init__` are sized `MAX_INTERMEDIATE_OFFSETS_PER_REQUEST * context.max_requests` slots per buffer, and there are TWO scratch buffers (intermediate_ssm_out AND intermediate_conv_out) plus the durable pair (ssm_states + conv_states). `per_slot_bytes = num_mamba_layers * (conv_size + ssm_size)` correctly represents one slot of a conv+ssm pair, so `scratch_bytes = scratch_slots * per_slot_bytes` correctly accounts for both intermediate buffers together, and durable likewise. This appears consistent. However, verify the budget also does not omit the CPU/GPU bookkeeping tensors (offsets/counts/block_ids sized to max_requests) — these are small but not counted. Low risk; flagging for explicit confirmation that the 'budget covers both buffers' claim in the docstring is exhaustive.

The preview computation in the logging block (lines ~773-789) duplicates the exact scratch/durable split logic that `_allocate_mamba_cache` performs (lines ~1655-1668). The comment even says 'Mirror the split done in _allocate_mamba_cache'. Duplicated arithmetic can drift out of sync (e.g. if the reservation formula changes in one place only), silently making the logged preview inconsistent with the actual allocation. Extract the split into a small helper (e.g. `_compute_mamba_slot_split(mamba_gb) -> (durable_slots, scratch_slots)`) called by both the preview logger and `_allocate_mamba_cache`.

The added docstrings/comments in config.py and arguments.py hardcode the scratch factor as '3 * max_requests slots', where 3 is the literal value of MAX_INTERMEDIATE_OFFSETS_PER_REQUEST. If that constant changes, these user-facing docstrings become stale/misleading. Prefer referencing the constant name (MAX_INTERMEDIATE_OFFSETS_PER_REQUEST * max_requests) rather than the magic number 3 in prose that can silently drift.

(1) The newly checkpointed function `_gated_norm_and_a2a` wraps both the RMSNorm AND the HP-to-CP `tensor_a2a_hp2cp` all-to-all collective inside `CheckpointWithoutOutput.checkpoint(...)`, meaning the all-to-all communication will be re-executed during the recompute pass in backward. Existing users of `CheckpointWithoutOutput` (e.g. `absorbed_mla.py` qkv_up, `moe/experts.py` activation, `transformer_layer.py` norm) checkpoint only local compute (norm / activation / up-proj), never a collective. Re-running a collective during recompute adds extra communication traffic in the backward pass and, more importantly, requires that all CP ranks reach the recompute call in lockstep; if the recompute is triggered lazily by autograd on only some ranks (e.g. under uneven pipeline/grad-accum scheduling or when `norm_out` feeds a branch that is selectively backpropagated), it can deadlock on the all-to-all. (2) This risks hangs and extra comm cost that partially negate the memory savings. (3) Confirm the recompute region should include the a2a, or restrict the checkpoint to just `_apply_gated_norm` and keep the transpose/a2a outside the checkpoint region.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-GPU (CP>1) GDN forward+backward with recompute_modules=['gdn_norm_out'] measuring backward-pass communication volume and step time vs. baseline; verify no deadlock under pipeline parallel + grad accumulation.

findings.claim: (1) `_gated_norm_and_a2a` is defined as a closure capturing `batch`, `seq_len`, `cu_seqlens_q`, `packed_seq_params`, and `self` from the enclosing forward scope, and it is passed to `CheckpointWithoutOutput.checkpoint`, which stores the function and its tensor args to re-invoke during backward. Only `core_attn_out` and `gate` are passed as explicit (detached/saved) tensor arguments; the closure-captured Python values are frozen at forward time. This is generally fine for scalars but `cu_seqlens_q` is a tensor captured by closure rather than passed as a checkpointed input — if recompute assumes it is regenerated/detached like the explicit args, there can be a subtle mismatch (e.g. version-counter / autograd tracking on the captured tensor). (2) Risk of incorrect gradients or autograd errors when packed-seq (thd) path is used with recompute. (3) Verify the thd/packed path is covered by a test (the added test only exercises `packed_seq_params=None`) and consider passing `cu_seqlens_q` as an explicit checkpoint arg.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Correctness test of gdn_norm_out recompute with packed_seq_params qkv_format='thd' comparing output/grads bit-exactly against non-recompute baseline.

findings.claim: (1) `norm_out_checkpoint` is stored as an instance attribute (`self.norm_out_checkpoint`) rather than a local variable, and is only reset to a fresh `CheckpointWithoutOutput()` inside the `if self.recompute_norm_out` branch of forward. If forward is ever called re-entrantly or the object is reused across microbatches before backward consumes the prior checkpoint, the attribute is overwritten and the previously registered recompute state could be lost. Existing patterns (absorbed_mla `self.qkv_up_checkpoint`) follow the same convention, so this matches the codebase, but combined with the a2a-in-recompute concern it increases the surface for subtle interleaving bugs. (2) Low risk given single-checkpoint-per-forward usage. (3) Consider a comment noting the assumption that backward for a given forward consumes the checkpoint before the next forward overwrites it.

(1) `self.norm_out_checkpoint = tensor_parallel.CheckpointWithoutOutput()` is instantiated on every forward pass when recompute is enabled. If `CheckpointWithoutOutput.__init__` triggers the lazy JIT compilation of the `share_storage` C++ extension only on first use (it is cached globally), the per-forward object allocation itself is cheap, but re-check whether any per-instance setup could be hoisted. (2) Minor overhead. (3) If the object is cheap, no change needed; this is only worth noting for consistency with the 'setup constant across training should be moved to __init__' rubric — but re-instantiation per forward is the established pattern for these one-shot checkpoint handles, so likely intentional.

(1) `_release_tensor_storage(loss_node_inputs_to_release)` is only invoked at line 471 after `type(...).run(...)`; however, the loss-node inputs are consumed only by `torch.autograd.backward(b_output_tensor[0], ...)` at line ~446, which completes before `.run()` (the schedule-plan backward uses `b_grad = loss_node.get_grad()`, not the loss-node input activations). (2) Deferring the release until after `.run()` keeps the loss activation storage alive across the entire combined fwd/bwd compute, reducing the peak-memory benefit this PR intends. (3) Consider releasing immediately after the loss backward / `get_grad()` call, or add a comment justifying why the release is intentionally deferred (e.g., stream-ordering safety with `record_stream`). This is not a correctness bug — the release is safe where it is — but the memory win is smaller than an earlier release would give.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory (torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated) for a MoE model with overlap_moe_expert_parallel_comm=True and PP>1, comparing release-after-run vs release-immediately-after-loss-backward.

findings.claim: (1) `_release_tensor_storage` unconditionally calls `record_stream(torch.cuda.current_stream())` before `untyped_storage().resize_(0)`, but the loss-node inputs may have been produced/consumed on a compute stream that is not necessarily `current_stream()` at the call site (the combined schedule uses dedicated comp/comm streams via `get_comp_stream`/`get_comm_stream`). (2) If the storage is freed while still in use by another stream, this can cause a use-after-free / memory corruption; conversely recording against the wrong stream provides no protection. (3) Confirm that at the call site the current stream is the one that last used these tensors (the loss backward runs via `torch.autograd.backward` on the default/current stream, so this is likely correct); if not, pass the correct stream explicitly, mirroring how `ScheduleNode._forward` records against `self.stream` rather than `current_stream()`.

(1) `_build_head_perm_for_split_sections` is decorated with `@lru_cache(maxsize=8)` and keyed by `(split_sections, cp_size, device)`, but it is called every forward pass from `GatedDeltaNet.forward` with `torch.cuda.current_device()` as the device arg. `torch.cuda.current_device()` returns an int (device index), so caching works, but the head permutation depends only on constant module attributes (`qk_dim_local_tp`, `v_dim_local_tp`, `num_value_heads // tp_size`) and `cp_size` — all fixed at `__init__`. (2) Recomputing/rebuilding this constant index tensor per forward (even if cached, the cache lookup + hashing of a tuple happens each step, and the tensor lives on GPU) is avoidable setup on every forward pass, which the rubric flags as a Suggestion-level issue (setup constant across training should move to __init__). (3) Precompute `head_perm` once in `__init__` and store as a registered buffer / attribute; likewise the split_sections tuple.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark GDN forward step time with cp_size>1 comparing per-forward lru_cache lookup vs. precomputed __init__ buffer, to confirm negligible vs. measurable launch overhead.

findings.claim: (1) `_build_thd_cp_a2a_perm` is called in the `thd` branch using `cu_seqlens_q` (which is `_resolve_cu_seqlens(..., cp_size=self.cp_size)` — i.e. the CP-adjusted/padded cumulative seqlens) and `seq_len` (the global sequence length). Inside the helper `t_local = t_global // cp_size` and `local_starts = cu[:-1] // cp_size`. The correctness of the fused permutation hinges on `cu_seqlens_q` being the *global* (already cp-multiplied) cumulative seqlens and each per-sequence length being divisible by `2*cp_size` (used in `halves = seq_lens // (2 * cp_size)`). If any sequence length is not divisible by `2*cp_size`, the integer `pos_in_seq // half_i` mapping silently produces a wrong permutation (or division-by-zero if `half_i == 0` for a short sequence), corrupting the token ordering after the a2a with no assertion. (2) This is a silent correctness hazard for packed sequences with lengths not aligned to `2*cp_size`; the old per-sequence loop path did not require this alignment in the same way. (3) Add an explicit assertion that every `seq_lens % (2 * cp_size) == 0` (or document the padding contract) inside `_build_thd_cp_a2a_perm`.

(1) In `_build_thd_cp_a2a_perm`, `half_i = halves[seq_idx]` is used as a divisor in `natural_chunk = pos_in_seq // half_i`. For any sequence whose length is < `2*cp_size`, `halves` is 0, causing an integer division-by-zero on GPU (or undefined behavior). There is no guard. (2) A packed batch can contain very short padded sequences; this would crash or silently corrupt indexing. (3) Assert `(halves > 0).all()` (equivalently each sequence length >= 2*cp_size) or handle short sequences explicitly.

(1) `_build_thd_cp_a2a_perm` is NOT cached (unlike `_build_head_perm_for_split_sections`) and is recomputed every forward pass, building several intermediate GPU tensors via `torch.arange`, `torch.bucketize`, `torch.diff`, `torch.where`, and a scatter to build `inv`. It also depends only on `cu_seqlens`, `cp_size`, `seq_len`. (2) With static packing patterns these index tensors are constant across steps; recomputing them each forward adds kernel-launch overhead in the training loop and cannot be CUDA-graph friendly if cu_seqlens vary. (3) Consider caching keyed on cu_seqlens (e.g. via a small memo) or precomputing when the packing layout is static, and add a note on CUDA-graph compatibility given the data-dependent index construction.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare GDN thd-format forward step time with fused single-a2a+permute vs. per-sequence loop across representative packed-sequence layouts and cp_size in {2,4}.

findings.claim: (1) The comment on the cp2hp branch reads `# # Pre-permute head dim ...` with a doubled `# #` prefix (leftover artifact). (2) Minor, but a stray double-comment marker is confusing. (3) Remove the extra `#`.

(1) In the fused path, the `thd_cp_a2a_inv` tensor computed inside the cp2hp block is later used in the closure `_gated_norm_and_a2a` (the hp2cp path) via `norm_out_hp.index_select(0, thd_cp_a2a_inv)`. This creates an implicit dependency: `thd_cp_a2a_inv` must be defined for the hp2cp branch to be valid, and it is only defined when `self.cp_size > 1` AND the thd branch was taken during cp2hp. If the two branches ever diverge (e.g. code changes so packed_seq_params differs between the two points), `thd_cp_a2a_inv` would be an undefined name / NameError. (2) Fragile cross-scope variable capture that couples the two a2a sites; a future edit could introduce a runtime NameError. (3) Consider recomputing `thd_cp_a2a_inv` locally in the hp2cp branch or passing it explicitly, and assert cp_size>1 consistency.

(1) The new assertion hard-fails any config with cross_entropy_loss_fusion=True and cross_entropy_fusion_impl='te', with no deprecation window. (2) This is a breaking behavior change: existing training scripts / launched jobs that pass '--cross-entropy-fusion-impl te' (which was a documented, supported, and CI-exercised option) will now crash at startup instead of silently downgrading. Since 'te' is still the accepted value in the Literal type and CLI choices, users get a hard AssertionError rather than a graceful fallback or deprecation warning. (3) Consider either (a) auto-downgrading to 'native' with a warn_rank_0 for one release before removing, or (b) removing 'te' from the argparse choices / Literal so the failure is a clear 'invalid choice' rather than a runtime assertion. At minimum document this as an intentional breaking change in release notes. Note this is a deliberate stability fix, so severity is bounded.

(1) With the new assertion added in ModelParallelConfig.__post_init__, the branch `if self.config.cross_entropy_fusion_impl == 'te':` inside compute_language_model_loss (lines 157-179, including the te_parallel_cross_entropy call, CUDA-graph capturability check, TE version assertion, and torch.as_strided relabel) is now unreachable dead code: cross_entropy_loss_fusion=True together with cross_entropy_fusion_impl='te' can never construct a valid config. (2) Dead complexity is misleading to readers and can mask the fact that TE CE fusion is disabled; it also carries a stale is_cg_capturable path that will never execute. (3) Consider removing or clearly commenting the TE branch as disabled, so the runtime code matches the config-level restriction. If the intent is to re-enable soon, add a TODO with the tracking issue.

(1) The assertion added in validate_args duplicates the assertion now present in ModelParallelConfig.__post_init__; core_transformer_config_from_args builds a ModelParallelConfig and would raise the same AssertionError. (2) Duplicated invariants can drift out of sync (e.g. one message updated but not the other) and add maintenance overhead. This is minor — early validation in arguments.py gives a cleaner CLI-facing error, so the duplication is defensible. (3) Optionally rely on the single source of truth in ModelParallelConfig, or add a comment noting the intentional early-check duplication so future edits keep both messages consistent.

(1) This test case is named `...tp1pp4emp16etp1cp1...` (TP1) but the model_config sets `--tensor-model-parallel-size: 2`, `--pipeline-model-parallel-size: 4`; unrelated to this diff but note the test switches from `--moe-enable-deepep: true` to `--moe-flex-dispatcher-backend: hybridep`. (2) HybridEP requires `NUM_OF_HYBRID_EP_RANKS_PER_NVLINK_DOMAIN` for NVLink-domain detection and this PR sets it to 8; however with EP16 across a single NVLink domain assumed size 8, `group.size()=16` must be divisible by 8 (16%8==0, OK) implying 2 nodes — verify the SM (single-node?) test actually has 2 NVLink domains, otherwise `_hybrid_ep_num_nodes` will report internode RDMA and `_validate_hybrid_ep_ib_tx_depth` may raise. (3) Confirm the ranks-per-NVLink-domain value matches the physical H100 topology of the CI runner for this `_sm` test.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the deepseekv3_proxy_flex hybridep test on the target H100 node and confirm HybridEP buffer init succeeds and IB tx-depth validation does not raise.

findings.claim: (1) The DeepEP install now uses `git fetch origin $DEEPEP_COMMIT; git checkout FETCH_HEAD` on the default branch instead of the previous `--branch hybrid-ep` + pinned commit. (2) `git fetch origin <sha>` only works if the remote allows fetching arbitrary object SHAs (uploadpack.allowReachableSHA1InWant / allowAnySHA1InWant); many GitHub repos allow this but if the commit is not on an advertised ref the fetch can fail, breaking the CI image build non-deterministically. (3) Consider `git fetch --depth=1 origin <sha>` if supported, or fetch a named branch/tag and then checkout the SHA, to guard against fetch-by-SHA being disabled.

(1) The DeepEP build for `lts` image type is skipped via early `exit 0`, but the preceding `ENV HYBRID_EP_MULTINODE=1`, `RDMA_CORE_HOME`, and `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` are still set unconditionally at image level for lts images. (2) Prepending `/usr/local/cuda/lib64/` to `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` globally can shadow other CUDA libs and affect runtime library resolution for all downstream lts consumers who never install DeepEP. (3) Confirm this LD_LIBRARY_PATH change is intended for all image types, or scope it to non-lts builds.

(1) `nvidia-nvshmem-cu13==3.4.5` is installed and its lib dir symlink now uses `ln -sf` (idempotent) — good. However the DeepEP build uses `TORCH_CUDA_ARCH_LIST="9.0 10.0 12.0"` which includes sm_90/sm_100/sm_120; the DeepEP install is skipped only for lts, so dev images built for pure-H100 (sm_90) still compile sm_100/sm_120 kernels, increasing build time. (2) This is a build-throughput cost, not correctness. (3) If H100-only, consider restricting arch list to `9.0` for that image variant.

(1) The new max-inflight throttle enqueues `group_to_offload._offload_event` into `self._offload_pending_by_name[gname]` and later has the main stream `wait_event` on older events. But `OffloadTensorGroup` objects are created fresh each warmup and reused across iterations; a single `torch.cuda.Event` is stored per group. After a group is offloaded once and its event enqueued, if the same group object is re-committed within the same iteration (deduplicated groups / margin logic exists), `record_offload_event` re-records the SAME event object, so an already-enqueued event in the FIFO now refers to the newer record — the main stream may then wait on the wrong (later) point, silently under- or over-synchronizing. (2) This can cause activation D2H copies to not be complete before the GPU buffer is reused, producing silently wrong activations under full-iteration CUDA graphs — exactly the path where record_stream is not relied upon. (3) Enqueue a per-commit event snapshot (e.g., allocate a fresh Event per commit or track (group, generation) identity) rather than reusing the group's single mutable `_offload_event`, and add an assertion that each enqueued event is distinct.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Full-iteration CUDA graph run with fine-grained offloading and max_inflight_offloads=1 vs None; compare loss/activation correctness across iterations and verify no data race on reused offload events (compute-sanitizer racecheck).

findings.claim: (1) The new validation in `TransformerConfig.__post_init__` asserts that when `fine_grained_activation_offloading` is enabled, `cuda_graph_impl` must be in `("transformer_engine", "full_iteration")` and that `cuda_graph_warmup_steps > 0`. Previously fine-grained activation offloading may have been usable without CUDA graphs at all (no such assertion existed). (2) This turns previously-valid configs into hard failures at construction time, breaking existing training scripts that used fine-grained offloading without CUDA graphs. (3) Confirm this restriction is intended; if offloading-without-CUDA-graph was supported before, gate these asserts on `cuda_graph_impl is not None` / CUDA graphs actually being enabled, or add a deprecation/migration note.

(1) `_drain_offload_pending` calls `torch.cuda.current_stream()` and issues `cur.wait_event(old_evt)`. During full-iteration CUDA graph capture this host-side call executes at capture time and records the wait into the graph, which is intended; but if `_offload_pending_by_name` still holds events across a capture boundary (it is cleared only in `reset()` at iter boundary, not per-capture), a captured wait could reference an event from a non-captured region. (2) The reset() comment claims the FIFO is cleared at iter boundary to avoid waiting on stale prior-iteration events, but if capture and iteration boundaries do not coincide this guarantee may not hold. (3) Verify reset() is always invoked between the last commit of one capture and the first of the next, or clear the FIFO at capture-start explicitly.

(1) `_drain_offload_pending` early-returns when `self._max_inflight_offloads is None`, but the only caller already guards the call with the identical `is not None` check. (2) The redundant guard is harmless but adds indirection. (3) Drop one of the two checks (keep the guard inside the helper for safety, and remove it at the call site, or vice versa).

(1) The TE>=2.15 fallback allocates the persistent flag tensor as torch.float32 (torch.empty(1, dtype=torch.float32)) and fills it with a bool via fill_(skip). The original TE classmethod set_skip_fp8_weight_update_tensor historically allocated this scalar as a bool tensor (torch.tensor(skip)/bool dtype). (2) If any TE 2.15 kernel or code path reads quantization_state.skip_fp8_weight_update_tensor assuming a bool/int dtype (e.g. via a bitwise/typed read or a kernel that indexes by dtype), a float32 tensor at that data pointer would be silently misinterpreted, potentially causing incorrect skip-weight-update behavior during FP8 cudagraph replay (either always skipping or never skipping the weight refresh). (3) Confirm the dtype TE 2.15 expects for quantization_state.skip_fp8_weight_update_tensor and match it (likely torch.bool) rather than hardcoding float32; if float32 is genuinely required, add a comment citing the TE source that reads it as float. The included unit test only asserts float32 == 1.0 semantics against the value the helper itself wrote, so it cannot catch a dtype-contract mismatch with TE's consumer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run gpt_static_inference_tp1_pp1_583m_fp8_cudagraphs under TE>=2.15 and verify FP8 weight-update skipping across microbatches produces bit-identical logits vs TE<=2.14 (numerical correctness, not just no-crash).

findings.claim: (1) In the TE>=2.15 branch the helper accesses FP8GlobalStateManager.quantization_state and its skip_fp8_weight_update_tensor field with no existence guard; the else-branch is entered whenever the classmethod is absent, which is assumed to be exactly TE>=2.15. (2) If a future/intermediate TE version removes the classmethod but does not expose quantization_state (or names the field differently), this raises AttributeError deep inside cudagraph replay rather than a clear compatibility error. (3) Consider guarding with a version check (is_te_min_version('2.15')) or a hasattr(FP8GlobalStateManager, 'quantization_state') check that raises an explicit, actionable error message about the unsupported TE layout.

(1) `num_sms_preprocessing_api` only takes effect when the HybridEP buffer is first created inside `HybridEPDispatch.forward` (via `init_hybrid_ep_buffer`, which only creates `_hybrid_ep_buffer` if it is None). The `_hybrid_ep_buffer` global is a process-wide singleton reused across all MoE layers/calls. (2) This means the value from the *first* layer to run wins, and later differing `num_sms_preprocessing_api` values are silently ignored; the parameter is threaded all the way to the API call but only consumed on the first buffer construction. If the intent is a per-call SM count, this is a correctness bug; if per-run, it's fine but should be documented. (3) Confirm whether the SM count is buffer-init-time-only; if so, document that `moe_hybridep_num_sms_preprocessing` is process-global and cannot vary per layer.

(1) `num_sms_preprocessing_api` in `HybridEPDispatch.forward` and `hybrid_ep_dispatch` defaults to the magic literal `108`, while all sibling SM/block args (`num_sms_dispatch_api`, `num_sms_combine_api`, `num_blocks_permute`, ...) default to `None` and are only forwarded to the buffer when not None. (2) The inconsistent default means callers not passing the arg force 108 SMs (rather than deferring to the DeepEP/HybridEP library default), and the number 108 (SM count of A100/H100) is hardware-specific and will be wrong for other GPUs. (3) Consider defaulting to `None` for consistency and letting the underlying library pick its default, or centralize the 108 constant. Note the config default `moe_hybridep_num_sms_preprocessing: int = 108` also can never be None, unlike the sibling `moe_hybridep_num_sms: Optional[int] = None`.

(1) New config default `moe_hybridep_num_sms_preprocessing = 108` is hardware-specific (matches A100/H100 SM count) and applies unconditionally to any HybridEP user. (2) On GPUs with fewer/more SMs this hardcoded default may be suboptimal or exceed available SMs, silently affecting throughput; unlike the analogous `moe_hybridep_num_sms` which defaults to None (library-chosen). (3) Consider defaulting to None and letting the HybridEP library choose, or document why 108 is a safe cross-hardware default.

This PR adds `fallback_to_persistent_buffer=self.ddp_config.fsdp_db_use_persist_buf_on_alloc_fail` to the `transpose_weight_alloc` FixedPoolAllocator init, which was previously missing while `weight_alloc` and `main_grad_alloc` already had it. This is the intended bug fix: without this argument, the MXFP8 transpose-weight buffer defaulted to `fallback_to_persistent_buffer=False`, so any asymmetric (outlier) FSDP unit's transpose buffer would fall through to the dynamic backup allocator (StorageResizeBased path) instead of being persistently allocated. That reintroduces per-step allocation/free churn and memory fragmentation for large asymmetric layers (e.g. embeddings) precisely for the FP8 transpose cache, defeating the purpose of `fsdp_db_use_persist_buf_on_alloc_fail`. The change correctly aligns transpose_weight_alloc with the other two allocators. Reviewers should confirm the semantics match the other two call sites (which the diff context and grep confirm they do). No regression risk since the default of the config field is False.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable fsdp_double_buffer=True + fsdp_db_use_persist_buf_on_alloc_fail=True with an FP8/MXFP8 model containing asymmetric layers (e.g. embedding vs transformer blocks); measure per-step CUDA allocator activity (torch.cuda.memory_stats num_alloc_retries / allocation count) and step time before/after to confirm the transpose buffer no longer churns through the backup allocator.

findings.claim: The new test asserts persistent buffers exist only for `weight_alloc` and `main_grad_alloc` (the loop over `alloc_attr in ("weight_alloc", "main_grad_alloc")`), but the fix targets `transpose_weight_alloc`. The subsequent loop only checks that `transpose_weight_alloc.backup_allocator.buckets` is empty, which is also satisfied when no transpose buffer was ever allocated at all (e.g. if the test model does not trigger FP8 transpose caching). Consequently the test does not positively verify that the transpose-weight outlier unit landed in the persistent GlobalMemoryBuffer, so the exact regression this PR fixes may not be directly covered. Consider adding `transpose_weight_alloc` to the persistent-keys assertion loop (guarded by whether FP8/MXFP8 transpose is active) so the fix is exercised end-to-end.

(1) `reset_cuda_graph` unconditionally calls `gc.collect()` on every invocation. (2) `gc.collect()` is a full cross-generation garbage collection that traverses the entire Python object graph and can take tens to hundreds of milliseconds; if this reset is invoked per training step during paged-stash reruns (the runner reruns a step on stash overflow/over-budget), it introduces a large per-step CPU stall that kills throughput and can also break CUDA-graph timing. (3) Either make the `gc.collect()` conditional (only when a graph was actually deleted), or document that reset is a rare event; verify the call frequency in the runner path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure wall-clock of reset_cuda_graph() with and without gc.collect(); measure step-time impact when paged-stash reruns are triggered.

findings.claim: (1) `preprocess_for_paged_stash()` returns `paged_stash_init_chunk_handler(...)`, but at both call sites in `forward` (line ~530) and the model-chunk-schedule path (line ~816) the return value is discarded (`self.preprocess_for_paged_stash()` with no assignment). (2) If the returned chunk handler is meant to be held/registered for the forward-backward to consume (as the name 'handler' suggests), silently dropping it means the paged-stash chunk bookkeeping is either a no-op or relies entirely on a hidden global side-effect, which is fragile and hard to reason about — a mismatch here could cause stash/pop pairing errors or silent memory corruption. (3) Confirm `paged_stash_init_chunk_handler` operates purely via global side-effect (in which case it should not return a handler, or the callers should be documented as intentionally discarding); otherwise capture and use the return value.

(1) `getattr(config, "moe_paged_stash", False)` is used defensively in three schedule functions even though `moe_paged_stash` is a declared field on TransformerConfig. (2) Using getattr-with-default hides typos and defeats the dataclass contract; if the field is always present, a direct `config.moe_paged_stash` is clearer and would surface config-plumbing bugs. (3) Use direct attribute access unless `config` can legitimately be a non-TransformerConfig object here (in which case document why).

(1) In TEGroupedMLP fused path, `avg_num_tokens = int(max_num_tokens // cap_factor)` computes an integer estimate via floor division by a float capacity factor. (2) `max_num_tokens // cap_factor` with a float divisor yields a float; wrapping in int() truncates, and for cap_factor slightly above 1.0 this floor can underestimate the average token count, mis-sizing the paged-stash heuristic and potentially triggering avoidable overflow reruns. Also the stray comment '# if the number of tokens is 0, pad the hidden states to 256' above appears orphaned/misleading relative to the surrounding code. (3) Verify the rounding direction matches the paged-stash sizing intent (round vs floor) and remove/fix the stale comment.

(1) The paged-stash path assumes `moe_expert_rank_capacity_factor` is set when `moe_paged_stash` is enabled (comment: 'moe_expert_rank_capacity_factor is required when moe_paged_stash is enabled'), but the code falls back to `avg_num_tokens=None` when cap_factor is None/<=0 rather than asserting. (2) Silently passing `avg_num_tokens=None` into the stash context when the documented prerequisite is violated can lead to incorrect buffer sizing rather than a clear early failure, making misconfiguration hard to diagnose. (3) Add an explicit assert/validation at config-validation time (or here) that `moe_expert_rank_capacity_factor` is a positive float whenever `moe_paged_stash` is True.

(1) CUDA_GRAPH_ROUNDER default is changed from 8 to 2. (2) This is a global constant that also governs step rounding in the LINEAR positive-N path (_calculate_token_counts_linear via `rounder`) and the exponential path. Reducing the rounder from 8 to 2 changes the generated token-count grids for ALL distributions, not just the new exponential default, silently altering the set of captured CUDA graph shapes for existing users who set num_cuda_graphs explicitly. Shapes no longer aligned to multiples of 8 may reduce reuse/hit-rate of previously-tuned graph sets and change memory footprint. (3) If the intent is only to change the default distribution, keep CUDA_GRAPH_ROUNDER=8 for the LINEAR/legacy path (or make the rounder per-distribution) so LINEAR remains apples-to-apples with main as the docstring claims.

(1) In mixed prefill+decode mode under the new EXPONENTIAL default, the code now iterates over a geometric grid `p_values = {1,2,4,...,max_requests}` for EACH prefill token count `size`, whereas the previous code created a single mixed dimension per size (fixed prefill_req_count). (2) This multiplies the number of captured mixed graphs by roughly log2(max_requests), substantially increasing CUDA-graph capture time and graph-pool memory for large max_requests — a real regression for users who enable mixed prefill graphs. (3) Confirm this blow-up is intended/bounded; consider documenting the expected graph-count growth and gating it, and add a benchmark of capture time & pool memory vs. max_requests.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure CUDA graph capture time and pool_reserved_bytes for mixed-prefill enabled configs across max_requests in {64,256,1024} comparing EXPONENTIAL vs LINEAR distributions.

findings.claim: (1) `_cuda_graph_mempool_bytes()` calls `torch.cuda.memory_snapshot()` on every graph-capture iteration to compute per-iteration pool deltas, and walks all segments filtering by pool id. (2) `memory_snapshot()` is a relatively heavy call (serializes the full allocator state); doing it once per captured graph inflates total capture/warmup wall-clock time, which can be significant when hundreds of graphs are captured (and the exponential+grid change increases graph count). This is warmup-only, not the steady-state inference loop, so impact is bounded. (3) Consider gating the per-iteration snapshot logging behind a verbose/debug flag, or only computing final pool bytes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time the full cuda-graph warmup loop with and without the per-iteration _cuda_graph_mempool_bytes() call for a config that captures 100+ graphs.

findings.claim: (1) `_cuda_graph_mempool_bytes()` compares `seg_pool_id == pool_id` where `pool_id = CudaGraphManager.global_mempool` is the value returned by `torch.cuda.graph_pool_handle()` (an opaque tuple) and `seg_pool_id` is read from `torch.cuda.memory_snapshot()` fields (`segment_pool_id`/`private_pool_id`/etc.). (2) The exact key name and value representation of the pool id in the snapshot dict are not part of a stable PyTorch API and may differ (e.g. tuple vs list, different field name across versions), causing the filter to silently match nothing and report pool bytes as 0 — misleading memory diagnostics rather than a correctness bug in capture. (3) Add a one-time warning if the pool is created but no segments match, and pin/verify the expected snapshot field name for the supported torch version.

(1) In the exponential branch, `rounder = CUDAGraphBatchDimensionBuilder.CUDA_GRAPH_ROUNDER` is assigned and used in the new loop, but the pre-existing `cuda_graph_step_size` computation (lines using CUDA_GRAPH_ROUNDER directly and round_up_to_nearest_multiple) is left in place above the new exponential logic even though `cuda_graph_step_size` is no longer consumed by the exponential path (which builds `sizes` by halving). (2) This leaves dead/unused computation (`cuda_graph_step_size`) in the exponential path, adding confusion and a misleading impression that step-size logic still applies. (3) Remove the now-unused `cuda_graph_step_size` computation from the exponential branch, or scope it to where it is actually used.

(1) The auto-size (-1) exponential branch computes `num_halvings = int(math.log2(max(2, cuda_graph_max_tokens)))` and `auto_n = max(MIN_GRAPHS, num_halvings + HEADROOM)`, then recurses. Because the inner loop dedupes rounded/TP-aligned values and then trims from the middle to at most `num_cuda_graphs`, the final count can be fewer than the log2 estimate for small max_tokens or large tp_size, but the docstring/comment claims `log2(max_tokens)` total graphs. (2) Minor: the produced graph count may not match the documented expectation, which can confuse users tuning capture memory; not a functional bug. (3) Align the comment/docstring with the actual (post-dedupe, post-trim) count behavior.

(1) The module-global default for MSC is flipped from `_FeatureFlag(_msc_available)` to `_FeatureFlag(default=False)`. Any code path that consults `MultiStorageClientFeature.is_enabled()` WITHOUT going through `megatron/training/arguments.parse_args()` (e.g. standalone dist-checkpointing users of `megatron/core`, notebooks, or downstream frameworks that import core directly) will now silently get MSC=off even when the package is installed and even when they were previously relying on it being auto-enabled. Previously, importing the module alone enabled MSC when installed. (2) This is a behavior/back-compat change for core-library consumers who don't parse Megatron training args; regular filesystem paths and `msc://` URLs that used to route through MSC will now use plain `open()`/FileSystemReader. (3) Document this core-level default change prominently and, if core consumers are expected, provide a programmatic opt-in note in MSC_Integration.md (the doc currently only mentions the `--enable-msc` training flag).

(1) When `--enable-msc` is passed but the `multistorageclient` package is not installed, the code calls `MultiStorageClientFeature.enable()` then checks `is_enabled()` (which returns False because `msc is None`) and raises RuntimeError. However `enable()` has already set the module-global `_enabled = True`. The process aborts here so it is usually harmless, but if any caller catches this RuntimeError and continues (e.g. a wrapper that treats args parsing as recoverable), the feature flag is left in an inconsistent `_enabled=True, msc=None` state where `is_enabled()` is False but `import_package()` would raise a confusing 'package not available' error. (2) Leaves global state partially mutated on the error path. (3) Reset the flag before raising, e.g. call `MultiStorageClientFeature.disable()` in the failure branch, or check package availability before calling `enable()`.

(1) The deprecation handling forces `args.enable_msc = False` when `--disable-msc` is passed, silently overriding an explicitly-passed `--enable-msc`. A user passing both `--enable-msc --disable-msc` will get MSC disabled with only a deprecation warning, not an error about conflicting flags. (2) This preserves legacy semantics but could confuse users who explicitly opted in; the deprecated no-op flag is documented in argparse help as 'a no-op' yet it actually overrides `--enable-msc`, contradicting the help text. (3) Either make the deprecated flag a true no-op (as its help claims) or emit a clearer conflict warning when both are set; align the argparse help string with the actual override behavior.

(1) When MSC is enabled, requesting `cache_metadata=True` (via `ckpt_assume_constant_structure=True`) now silently falls back to `MultiStorageFileSystemReader`, which re-reads `.metadata` on every checkpoint load instead of using the cached reader. (2) For frequent-load workloads (e.g. many resumes, VPP reconfiguration, or eval loops) with MSC enabled, this removes a metadata-caching optimization and adds repeated metadata I/O per load — potentially costly against object storage with high per-request latency. The added warning informs the user but the perf regression is real when opting into MSC. (3) Consider implementing metadata caching in the MSC reader path (or wrapping it) so `cache_metadata=True` is honored under MSC rather than discarded.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure checkpoint load latency with MSC enabled and ckpt_assume_constant_structure=True across repeated loads of the same checkpoint dir, comparing metadata re-read cost vs cached path.

findings.claim: (1) `cuda_graph_use_single_mempool` default is flipped from `False` to `True` in TransformerConfig, but this same flag is also consumed by the pre-existing `local` cuda-graph implementation in megatron/core/transformer/cuda_graphs.py (see `CudaGraphManager.global_mempool` at lines ~1413-1571 and the arguments.py wiring). The original docstring explicitly warned this flag `may greatly increase the number of cudagraphs created when the number of microbatches is high` because in single-mempool mode each cudagraph may only be used once per step. (2) Flipping the default silently changes behavior for every existing `local` cuda-graph user who relied on the default: it can dramatically increase the number of captured cudagraphs and memory/capture-time overhead, and removes cross-microbatch graph reuse. This is a silent training-behavior/perf regression for a large existing user base, not just an opt-in for the new full-iteration+optimizer sharing feature. (3) Keep the default `False` (or gate the new behavior behind a separate flag) so existing `local`-impl users are not silently affected; if a default change is truly intended, document the impact and update the `local` path deliberately.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run `local` cuda_graph_impl training with num_microbatches high (e.g. 32) and compare number of captured cudagraphs, capture time, and memory_reserved with old default (False) vs new default (True).

findings.claim: (1) The docstring for `cuda_graph_use_single_mempool` is rewritten to describe ONLY the new full-iteration/optimizer graph-pool sharing (`For cuda_graph_impl "local" ... cuda_graph_scope "full_iteration" only`), but the same field continues to drive `CudaGraphManager.global_mempool` in the `local` per-module cuda-graph path (cuda_graphs.py). (2) The new docstring is now misleading/incomplete: it no longer documents the field's effect on the `local` per-module capture path, so a reader will not realize toggling this flag also changes local-impl mempool behavior. A wrong/incomplete docstring on a shared config field is worse than none. (3) Either split into two distinct config fields (one for full-iteration+optimizer pool sharing, one for local-impl single mempool) or expand the docstring to cover both consumers.

(1) `get_shared_capture_stream()` / `get_shared_graph_pool()` are process-wide lazy singletons never reset, but `delete_cuda_graphs()` in cuda_graphs.py resets `CudaGraphManager.global_mempool = None` and the OptimizerCudaGraphWrapper `__del__` deletes its graph. The new shared pool/stream are not cleared on teardown, and the full-iteration graphs (`FullCudaGraphWrapper.cuda_graph[...]`) are class-level and also never reset. (2) With `use_single_mempool=True`, optimizer capture and full-iteration capture now share `_shared_graph_pool`. Because captures happen at different warmup-step boundaries and are replayed independently, sharing a single mempool between an optimizer graph and the forward/backward graph requires the captures to be created in a well-defined, non-interleaved order and never freed while the pool is alive; if the optimizer graph is recaptured or destroyed (e.g. via `__del__`) while the full-iter graph still references the shared pool, replaying the surviving graph can read freed/overwritten pool memory, producing silently wrong results. (3) Verify capture ordering and lifetime guarantees; document that both graphs must outlive the shared pool, and ensure teardown resets `_shared_graph_pool`/`_shared_capture_stream` consistently with graph deletion.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable optimizer_cuda_graph + full_iteration cuda_graph with use_single_mempool=True, run several hundred steps, and compare loss curve / logits against use_single_mempool=False to detect memory-pool aliasing corruption.

findings.claim: (1) `get_shared_capture_stream()` docstring says `Call after the target CUDA device is selected`, but the function lazily creates `torch.cuda.Stream()` on first call from whichever call site runs first (full-iter vs optimizer wrapper). (2) If the first caller runs before the device is finalized in some path, the stream is created on the wrong/default device and every subsequent capture silently uses it, since the singleton is never re-created. (3) Consider passing the device explicitly or asserting `torch.cuda.current_device()` matches expectations when creating the stream.

(1) The unit test `test_hybrid_moe_model.py` GOLDEN_CONFIG was updated to expect `cuda_graph_use_single_mempool: True`, which merely tracks the default flip rather than exercising the new pool-sharing path. (2) There is no test that actually asserts the shared pool/stream are reused across full-iteration and optimizer captures (e.g. that `get_graph_pool(True)` returns the same handle on repeated calls and that `get_shared_capture_stream()` returns an identical Stream object). (3) Add a focused unit test asserting singleton identity of `get_shared_graph_pool()`/`get_shared_capture_stream()` and that `get_graph_pool(False)` returns fresh handles.

(1) The NVSHMEM local-copy stream synchronization was moved from immediately after the local copies (inside `run()`, before the remote pipeline) to the end of `run()`, after `self._remote.schedule()/run()/clear_requests()`. The stated intent is to overlap local copies with the remote NVSHMEM pipeline. (2) Risk: `self._remote.schedule()`/`run()` contain host-side collectives (dist.all_gather_object, nvshmem barrier_all). During the window between the local `copy_` on `_local_copy_stream` and the final `wait_stream`, the local destination tensors are being written asynchronously; if any op inside the remote pipeline (or a caller after run()) touches those destination tensors on the default stream before the wait_stream lands, there is a read-before-write race. In the parent code the wait_stream happened before the remote pipeline, guaranteeing local copies were visible. The new ordering is only safe if no destination tensor of a local copy is also touched by the remote path — that invariant is not asserted. (3) Fix: verify local-copy dst tensors are disjoint from remote recv dst tensors (they should be, per task_id), and add a comment/assert documenting this invariant; otherwise keep the wait before the remote run.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Non-collocated MXFP8 refit with a mix of local (same-rank) and remote transfers; verify bitwise-identical destination weights vs. the pre-change ordering across repeated runs.

findings.claim: (1) The new MXFP8 dequant prefetch issues ALL `_ensure_sendable` dequants eagerly on a side `prefetch_stream` up-front (one Event per param), before any send is submitted. (2) For models with many MXFP8 params this materializes every dequantized BF16 buffer simultaneously, raising peak memory to roughly the full BF16 model size on the send side — the parent code dequantized lazily via `_get_sendable` and cleared the cache, so at most the in-flight params were resident. Given the writeback path already worries about MXFP8 BF16 peak (empty_cache call), this front-loaded allocation could regress peak memory on large MoE models. (3) Consider chunking the prefetch or bounding the number of outstanding dequants.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Peak GPU memory (torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated) during refit of a large MXFP8 MoE model, comparing eager prefetch vs. lazy dequant.

findings.claim: (1) A new `close()` method was added to the `CopyService` base (no-op default) and overridden in NVSHMEM to call `self._remote.finalize()`. However `clear_service_cache()` in refit.py still finalizes NVSHMEM via the old `hasattr(service, '_remote') and hasattr(service._remote, 'finalize')` path rather than calling the new `service.close()`. (2) This leaves two divergent cleanup paths; if a future backend relies on `close()` for resource release, `clear_service_cache()` will not invoke it, silently leaking backend resources. (3) Update `clear_service_cache()` to call `service.close()` uniformly.

(1) The base `CopyService.__init__` now stores the process group as `self.group`, but NVSHMEM previously used `self._group`; after switching to `super().__init__(group=group)` the attribute is now `self.group`. (2) This is a public-attribute rename in megatron/core library code — any external code (or subclass) reading `service._group` will get AttributeError. Confirmed `_group` was only referenced at the assignment site within this file, so internal usage is fine, but the attribute-name change is still an API-surface change. (3) If external compatibility matters, keep a `self._group = group` alias or document the rename.

(1) The NCCL mem-pool deregistration cleanup is placed inside the final `if should_exit:` block in `train()`, which only executes when training exits via an exit condition (signal handler / duration / iterations). Normal training completion, exceptions, or paths that break out of the loop without setting `should_exit=True` will never reach this code. (2) Consequently the ProcessGroupNCCL destructor can still call abort()->ncclCommDeregister on window-registered handles for those exit paths, reproducing the very crash this PR intends to fix (`NCCL WARN Deregister: Could not find handle`). (3) Move the deregistration into a guaranteed-cleanup path (e.g. `destroy_global_state()` / a finally block around the training loop, or DDP teardown), not just the `should_exit` branch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a training job to natural iteration completion (not signal-triggered exit) with nccl_ub enabled and verify no NCCL deregister crash on process teardown.

findings.claim: (1) `torch.distributed.barrier()` is called on the default (global WORLD) process group, while the actual deregistration uses each buffer's `data_parallel_group`. If ranks reach this `should_exit` block at different times or a subset of ranks take a different exit path, the collective barrier on WORLD plus the per-DP-group deregister ordering can deadlock or mis-synchronize. (2) A hang at shutdown wastes cluster allocation and can leave jobs stuck. (3) Consider whether the barrier is needed at all here, and if so scope/synchronize it consistently with the groups used for deregistration; ensure all ranks unconditionally execute the same collective sequence.

(1) An unconditional `torch.distributed.barrier()` is added on every exit, even when `nccl_ub` is disabled and no buffer has an `nccl_mem_pool` (the common case). (2) This adds a global synchronization at shutdown for all users regardless of whether they use NCCL UB, and forces a collective even when nothing needs deregistration. (3) Guard the barrier + deregistration loop behind a check that at least one buffer actually has a non-None `nccl_mem_pool` (or behind `args`/ddp_config.nccl_ub), so non-UB runs are unaffected.

(1) The `expandable_segments:True` incompatibility guard for `nccl_ub` is now suppressed for torch >= 2.11.0a0 via `is_torch_min_version("2.11.0a0")`. This relies on an unreleased/nightly torch behavior change lifting the incompatibility. (2) If the fix in torch 2.11 is incomplete or the version pin is wrong, users on 2.11 nightlies could silently run an unsupported/broken nccl_ub + expandable_segments combination instead of getting the previous clear ValueError. (3) Confirm the exact torch version/PR that resolves this and add a comment referencing it next to the version check so the magic version string is traceable.

(1) `self.nccl_mem_pool` is initialized to None and set to the created pool only in the `nccl_ub` branch; it is otherwise never read within param_and_grad_buffer.py (only consumed externally in training.py via getattr). (2) This is acceptable as a stored handle for later deregistration, but the attribute has no docstring/comment explaining its purpose and lifetime, making the cross-module dependency (training.py teardown) non-obvious. (3) Add a brief comment noting that nccl_mem_pool is retained so the pool can be deregistered from its data_parallel_group at shutdown to avoid NCCL abort()-time deregister failures.

(1) The assertion enforcing `cuda_graph_max_tokens == max_requests * (num_speculative_tokens + 1)` was relaxed to `>=`, and the original comment explaining WHY the strict equality was needed ('This is required for correctly syncing EP ranks: prefill and decode graph pools must have the same token count granularity') was deleted rather than addressed. (2) Under EP, `adjust_batch_dims_for_expert_parallelism` all-reduce-MAX's the local token_count across EP ranks; if prefill/mixed graphs are now captured at token counts up to `max_tokens` (much larger than the decode pool bound) while decode graphs remain capped at `max_requests*(spec+1)`, ranks that reduced to a larger token_count must still find a matching captured graph on every rank. If the two pools no longer share a common token-count granularity, a MAX-reduced token count on one rank may map to a captured graph that another rank did not capture, causing a graph-lookup mismatch / hang / silent fallback divergence across EP ranks. The deleted comment strongly suggests this invariant is load-bearing. (3) Confirm (and document in code) why the granularity-sync requirement is preserved when `cuda_graph_all_prefills=True` extends the prefill pool beyond the decode bound; add an explicit test running with EP>1 and cuda_graph_all_prefills=True, not just single-process token-count assertions.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-rank EP>1 dynamic inference run with --inference-cuda-graph-all-prefills and mixed prefill+decode batches; verify no graph-lookup mismatch/hang and identical outputs vs eager.

findings.claim: (1) The user-facing CLI flag `--inference-dynamic-batching-cuda-graph-max-tokens` (default 16384) is removed outright with no deprecation path. (2) Any existing user launch script or benchmark harness that passes this flag will now fail hard with 'unrecognized argument', breaking existing inference deployment scripts silently at startup. This is a backward-incompatible argument removal per the rubric's 'Config/argument changes: Renamed or removed arguments without deprecation path'. (3) Either keep the flag as a deprecated no-op with a warning (e.g. via the existing deprecate_args machinery), or document the removal prominently in release notes/CHANGELOG and error with a helpful message pointing to --inference-cuda-graph-all-prefills.

(1) The new floor `cuda_graph_step_size = max(cuda_graph_step_size, tp_size)` guards against a zero step size when `cuda_graph_max_tokens < num_cuda_graphs`, which is correct. However, after flooring the step to `tp_size`, `cuda_graph_max_tokens` is subsequently rounded down to a multiple of tp_size; if the rounded max is below the step, `range(step, max, step)` yields empty and only the appended max is used. (2) This edge path is exercised only by the new single-process test with tp_size==max_tokens; for tp_size>1 with tiny max_tokens the produced list may collapse to a single graph, which is acceptable but worth an explicit assertion/comment so future changes don't silently drop coverage. (3) Add a brief comment noting the intended single-graph collapse behavior for max_tokens<=tp_size.

(1) The bias aggregation was changed from `torch.stack(bias_chunks, dim=0).sum(dim=0)` to `bias_chunks[0]`, justified by the comment 'elements in bias_chunks are the same for all chunks'. (2) Why it matters: the *original* prefill code summed the per-chunk biases. If the biases were truly identical, the original code was silently wrong (it would multiply the bias by num_chunks), OR the biases are NOT identical for the chunked case and the new code drops (num_chunks-1) of them. The MLP bias returned by `self.mlp(chunk)` is the fc2/output bias, which is a per-hidden-dim parameter broadcast over the sequence — it is indeed independent of the chunk contents, so `bias_chunks[0]` is the correct single copy. This means the OLD prefill path (torch.stack().sum()) was already producing a bias scaled by num_chunks and this diff silently *changes prefill numerics* too (prefill was previously included via the shared branch). Verify whether the pre-existing prefill behavior was buggy; if so this is a behavior change to the prefill path masquerading as a training-only feature and should be called out / tested against the pre-change prefill golden values. (3) Fix: confirm bias broadcasting semantics (bias shape is [h], not [s,b,h]); document that the previous sum was a bug; ensure prefill regression tests (which used torch.ones input) still pass with the new bias-selection, since the test input was changed from ones to randn simultaneously, masking any prefill numeric regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare prefill outputs before/after this commit for a layer with add_bias_linear=True and mlp_chunks_for_prefill=4, using the OLD test's torch.ones input, to confirm whether the bias-sum change alters prefill numerics.

findings.claim: (1) Chunked MLP during training slices the pre_mlp_layernorm_output along the sequence dim (dim=0) and calls self.mlp(chunk) independently per chunk, then concatenates. This is only mathematically equivalent to the unchunked MLP when the MLP is token-independent along the sequence dimension. For a plain MLP this holds, but for MoE layers (self.is_moe_layer) routing, capacity, aux-loss and expert dispatch are computed *per forward call* over the tokens present. Chunking changes the token population seen by the router in each call, altering capacity/token-dropping and aux-loss normalization. (2) Why it matters: silently different training dynamics / wrong aux loss when a user enables mlp_chunks_for_training>1 on an MoE model. The new should_chunk_mlp_for_training guard only excludes IdentityOp, not MoELayer, so MoE layers WILL be chunked. (3) Fix: guard chunked training against MoE layers (e.g. `and not self.is_moe_layer`) or document/verify router/aux-loss invariance under sequence chunking. The added test only covers a dense MLP (get_gpt_layer_with_transformer_engine_submodules, no experts), so MoE equivalence is untested.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run an MoE TransformerLayer forward/backward with mlp_chunks_for_training=1 vs 4 and assert identical outputs, aux-loss, and gradients; expect divergence, confirming the finding.

findings.claim: (1) Chunked training executes `outputs = [self.mlp(chunk) for chunk in chunks]` as a Python loop over num_chunks sequential MLP calls, then torch.cat / bias selection. (2) Why it matters: this increases kernel launch count by ~num_chunks and serializes GEMMs that were previously one large GEMM, reducing GPU utilization for the throughput benefit of lower peak activation memory. This is an inherent memory/throughput tradeoff, but there is no warning that enabling mlp_chunks_for_training>1 costs throughput, and it may interfere with CUDA graph capture (dynamic loop / variable chunk count via min()). (3) Fix: document the throughput/memory tradeoff in the config docstring and verify CUDA-graph compatibility (num_chunks derived from tensor .shape[0] is host-side and fixed per shape, so likely capturable, but should be confirmed).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-layer forward+backward throughput and peak memory for mlp_chunks_for_training in {1,2,4,8} on a large ffn_hidden_size to quantify the memory-vs-throughput tradeoff.

findings.claim: (1) num_chunks = min(mlp_chunks_for_training, pre_mlp_layernorm_output.shape[0]); tensor.chunk() does NOT guarantee equal-sized chunks when seq_len is not divisible by num_chunks — the last chunk is smaller. This is fine for a token-independent dense MLP, but combined with the bias_chunks[0] selection it is only correct because bias is chunk-independent. (2) Why it matters: no correctness bug for dense MLP, but if seq_len < num_chunks the min() clamps to seq_len giving 1-token chunks; edge behavior (seq_len not divisible) is untested (test uses seq_len=32, chunks=4, evenly divisible). (3) Fix: add a test with a non-divisible sequence length (e.g. seq_len=30, chunks=4) to cover unequal chunk sizes.

(1) When mtp_use_repeated_layer=True, the new code sets mtp_layer_specs=[mtp_layer_spec] (a single-element list) and then skips the offset/num_layers_to_build slicing that would otherwise restrict which layers are built on this pipeline stage. As a result, whenever the function is reached with mtp_use_repeated_layer=True, a MultiTokenPredictionBlockSubmodules with exactly one layer spec will always be constructed. (2) This matters because get_gpt_mtp_block_spec_for_backend can be called on pipeline stages that should NOT host any MTP layers; in the repeated-layer branch the code no longer consults num_layers_to_build (which is computed at line 741 and would be 0 for non-MTP stages) to decide whether to build the block. Note the early return at line 742 (`if num_layers_to_build == 0: return None`) may cover the non-MTP-stage case — verify. If that early return does not fire for repeated-layer configs, non-MTP pipeline stages could incorrectly instantiate an MTP block.
