(3) Fix: confirm the `num_layers_to_build == 0` early return at line 742 fully guards the repeated-layer branch, or add an explicit stage check before building the single-element spec.

(1) In the non-repeated branch the assertion `len(mtp_layer_specs) == config.mtp_num_layers` is retained (guaranteeing all MTP layers are on the same pipeline stage), but in the repeated-layer branch this invariant is no longer checked. (2) This matters because the repeated-layer path constructs a single-element spec regardless of pipeline layout; if a user configures a pipeline layout that would split MTP layers across stages while using mtp_use_repeated_layer=True, the mismatch would go undetected and could silently produce a wrong number of MTP compute iterations vs. instantiated layers. (3) Suggest adding an equivalent guard/assert in the repeated-layer branch, or a comment documenting why the single-layer repeated path is inherently stage-consistent.

(1) The code uses two separate `if config.mtp_use_repeated_layer` / `if not config.mtp_use_repeated_layer` blocks back to back. (2) This adds redundant branching and re-evaluates the same condition twice, making the logic harder to follow. (3) Merge into a single if/else: build `[mtp_layer_spec]` in the repeated branch, and in the else branch build the replicated list, apply offset slicing, and the assert — all within one else block.

(1) The TP broadcast protocol was changed from a two-message scheme (num-messages length prefix + payload) to a single multipart send prepended with a `Headers.TP_BROADCAST` frame; the MP-coordinator now ALWAYS sends `send_multipart([TP_BROADCAST] + all_messages)` even when `all_messages` is empty, and subscribers unconditionally call `recv_multipart()` and take `frames[1:]`. (2) This changes the invariant that governed whether a receive happens: previously, when there were zero messages, the coordinator sent a length=0 header and subscribers skipped the blocking recv; now every drain iteration produces exactly one multipart message from the coordinator, and every subscriber does exactly one blocking `recv_multipart()`. If the drain loop is entered a different number of times on the coordinator vs subscriber (e.g. state-machine paths that early-`continue` in the disable_ep_consensus branch, or the coordinator entering drain on some iterations where subscribers do not, or vice versa), the subscriber `recv_multipart()` will block indefinitely or drift by one message — a hang/desync. Verify that drain is invoked exactly once per loop iteration on all MP ranks in every code path, including the new `disable_ep_consensus` early-continue path and the dummy_forward path. (3) Add an explicit assertion/contract that the drain function is called symmetrically across MP ranks, and confirm the new unconditional send/recv cannot be skipped on one side.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-rank TP>1 inference run under coordinator with interleaved idle/busy iterations; assert no MP-group desync/hang over many steps.

findings.claim: (1) In the non-disabled consensus path, the engine now only calls `_ep_establish_consensus` every 20 loop iterations (or when last global_work==0), caching `self._last_ep_consensus` in between and acting on the stale `all_pausing`/`global_work` flags on the 19 intervening iterations. (2) During those cached iterations the engine executes `dummy_forward` or `async_step` based on stale `global_work`, and crucially the `signal_consensus=(self.state == EngineState.PAUSING)` semaphore is only communicated to peers on consensus iterations — so a PAUSE that arrives mid-window is not propagated to EP peers for up to 20 steps, delaying pause by the stated 200-400ms and, more importantly, all EP ranks must agree on WHICH iterations are consensus iterations. If `_ep_consensus_loop_counter` can diverge across EP ranks (e.g. one rank took the RUNNING path a different number of times, or was reset), the `% 20 == 0` check fires on different iterations per rank, causing an all-reduce mismatch/deadlock (a peer entering the all-reduce while another skips it). (3) Verify `_ep_consensus_loop_counter` is guaranteed lock-step across all EP ranks in every state-transition path; if not, gate the consensus-skip on a globally-synchronized counter or the collective itself.
findings.suggested_benchmark: EP>1 run where a PAUSE arrives at various loop offsets; assert all EP ranks enter/skip the consensus all-reduce on identical iterations and no NCCL deadlock occurs.

findings.claim: (1) The new `disable_ep_consensus` branch documents (config docstring + arg help) that it is 'Only safe when EP coordination is not required (e.g. ep_world_size == 1)', yet the code does not assert `ep_world_size == 1` when the flag is set, and the branch's own comment claims it keeps EP collectives in sync via unconditional dummy_forward. These two rationales are contradictory: if it is only safe at ep_world_size==1, the dummy_forward-to-stay-in-sync argument is moot; if the dummy_forward argument holds, then the 'only safe at ep==1' warning is wrong. (2) A user setting the flag with ep_world_size>1 gets no guardrail, and the mismatched documentation invites incorrect usage that could deadlock or diverge. (3) Either add an explicit `assert self.ep_world_size == 1 or ...` guard when `disable_ep_consensus` is set, or reconcile the docstrings to state the actual supported configuration.

(1) In the `disable_ep_consensus` idle branch, each idle iteration performs `self.step_end_event.record()` followed immediately by `self.step_end_event.synchronize()`, a host-device sync every idle loop iteration. (2) A blocking CUDA sync on every idle spin iteration adds CPU-GPU synchronization overhead and can dominate the control loop when idle (the test even relies on many dummy_forwards firing within 0.2s). This is a per-iteration `.synchronize()` in the training/inference control loop — the exact anti-pattern flagged for throughput. (3) Consider recording events without a per-iteration synchronize, or synchronizing only when timing stats are actually consumed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure idle-loop iteration rate / CPU utilization with disable_ep_consensus=True; compare synchronize-every-iter vs deferred sync.

findings.claim: (1) On the UNPAUSING->RUNNING transition the code resets `self._last_ep_consensus = (0, False)` with a comment explaining stale all_pausing would otherwise re-pause. However `_ep_consensus_loop_counter` is NOT reset on this transition, so after unpause the `% 20 == 0` schedule resumes at an arbitrary phase; combined with the reset `_last_ep_consensus=(0,...)` this is fine on the immediate next iter (global_work==0 forces consensus), but confirm no other transition (SUSPEND/RESUME/STOP) leaves `_last_ep_consensus` holding a stale `all_pausing=True`/nonzero global_work that would be acted on before the next consensus. (2) A stale cached consensus tuple across an unhandled transition could cause a spurious pause or a skipped pause. (3) Audit every state transition that re-enters the RUNNING consensus path and reset `_last_ep_consensus` consistently (or centralize the reset).

(1) The new fast-path test only exercises fp32 conv_state/int_state, but the optimization introduced new dtype round-trips (native -> tl.float32 register -> `.to(out_dtype)` on store) that only differ from the original direct copy for reduced-precision dtypes (bf16/fp16). (2) The bf16/fp16 store path (`x_val_N.to(out_dtype)`) is the code most likely to hide a precision or dtype-mismatch regression, yet it is not covered by any test. (3) Add a parametrization over dtype in [torch.float32, torch.bfloat16, torch.float16] for the state_len==WIDTH fast path to lock in bit-exactness of the register-resident shift.

(1) The fast-path (`state_len == WIDTH`) and slow-path (else) blocks duplicate the int_state base-pointer construction and the shift semantics, roughly doubling the body of the sequence loop. (2) The duplication increases the surface for future edits to diverge between the two branches (e.g. a stride-formula fix applied to only one). (3) Consider factoring the int_state base-pointer computation into a shared local and adding a brief comment cross-referencing the two shift implementations so they stay in sync; this is optional given the branches are clearly labeled.

(1) The rewritten `failed` list comprehension reports `SymmetricMemoryManager.get_buffer(name).init_failure_reason` as the failure reason, but a buffer's `handle` can be `None` for two distinct reasons: (a) `SymmetricMemoryBuffer.__init__` failed (sets `init_failure_reason`), or (b) init succeeded but `maybe_get_tensor`'s `_can_allocate` returned False because the requested tensor doesn't fit in the buffer (in this case `init_failure_reason` is None). With the new self-sizing (`size_mb=_size_mb(...)`) each buffer is ceil'd to whole MB of the *exact* footprint, but symmetric-memory rendezvous / alignment overhead and multi-tensor packing may still make `_can_allocate` fail, and then the error reports 'unknown' with an NVLink/triton message that is misleading. (2) Users debugging an out-of-space allocation get an inaccurate root cause pointing at hardware/driver rather than buffer sizing. (3) Distinguish the two cases: when `handle is None` but `init_failure_reason is None`, report an 'insufficient symmetric memory / allocation too large' reason instead of 'unknown'.

(1) `SymmetricMemoryManager._default_size_mb` changed from 256 to 512. This default is shared by other callers (e.g. the 'tp' buffer in `tensor_parallel/inference_layers.py`, allocated via `get_buffer("tp", ...)` with no `size_mb`), so every inference-optimized TP path now allocates a 512 MiB symmetric-memory buffer per rank instead of 256 MiB. (2) This doubles pinned NVLink symmetric-memory footprint for all users relying on the default, which can push memory-tight configs into OOM or symmetric-mem rendezvous failure that previously worked. The MoE dispatcher no longer needs the default (it now passes explicit `size_mb`), so raising the shared default is an unnecessary side effect. (3) Keep `_default_size_mb=256` (or make the MoE path pass its own explicit size, which it already does) rather than changing the global default that affects unrelated TP consumers.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-rank symmetric-memory allocation for a TP-only inference_optimized model before/after; confirm the 'tp' buffer size and peak device memory.

findings.claim: (1) In the `failed` comprehension, `buf` is bound from the tuple (the returned dict with the `handle`) but the failure reason is fetched via a second `SymmetricMemoryManager.get_buffer(name)` registry lookup. The dict `buf` and the `SymmetricMemoryBuffer` object are two different views of the same buffer, which is easy to misread. (2) Minor readability/maintenance risk; the extra registry lookup relies on the buffer already existing (created above) which is true but implicit. (3) Consider capturing the `SymmetricMemoryBuffer` objects directly in the iteration tuple alongside their result dicts to avoid the second lookup.

(1) When `from torch.nn.functional import grouped_mm` fails, the fallback sets module-level `grouped_mm = getattr(torch, "_grouped_mm", None)`. `_bf16_grouped_mm` then calls `grouped_mm(x_bf16, weight.transpose(1, 2), offs=offs)`. The public `torch.nn.functional.grouped_mm` and the private `torch._grouped_mm` are not guaranteed to share the same signature/keyword-argument names (`offs=`) across torch versions. (2) If `torch._grouped_mm` on torch<2.10 does not accept the `offs=` keyword (or transposed-weight layout) identically, the BF16 inference path will raise at runtime only when actually invoked, not at import. (3) Verify `torch._grouped_mm`'s signature accepts `offs=` and the transposed weight layout for the targeted torch versions, or add a thin adapter that normalizes the call.

(1) In HybridEPDispatch.forward, the forward call passes `**({"fuse_permute_dispatch": fused} if fused else {})` — i.e. `fuse_permute_dispatch` is only forwarded to `dispatch_with_permute` when `fused=True`, but the backward pass of HybridEPDispatch calls `combine_with_unpermute` with `**({"fuse_unpermute_combine": ctx.fused} if ctx.fused else {})`. That is symmetric. HOWEVER, the earlier fallback block sets `fused=False` and `num_blocks_permute=None`/`num_blocks_unpermute=None` when the DeepEP version lacks `fuse_permute_dispatch`, but the buffer init call still forwards `num_blocks_permute`/`num_blocks_unpermute` into `init_hybrid_ep_buffer` -> HybridEPBuffer kwargs. Since the fallback zeroes these to None before init, this is consistent. Verify: when `num_blocks_permute` is provided but `fused=False` (user only sets num_blocks but not permute_fusion_into_hybridep), the num_blocks_* are passed to buffer init but `fuse_permute_dispatch` is NOT passed to dispatch_with_permute, so the block-count tuning would be allocated in the buffer but never used by an unfused permute path. This is a latent inconsistency worth confirming, though not necessarily incorrect.

(1) The default of `moe_hybridep_num_sms` was changed from `16` (int) to `None` (Optional[int]). (2) This is a silent default-value change: users relying on the previous hardcoded 16-SM behavior will now get 'the default from DeepEP', which may differ from 16 and silently alter HybridEP bandwidth/throughput and possibly numerics of scheduling. Previously HybridEPDispatch.forward also defaulted num_sms_dispatch_api/combine_api to 24 as function defaults; now they default to None and rely on the config path. (3) Confirm the DeepEP default matches the prior 16 SMs, or document the behavior change in release notes / add a deprecation note. The docstring still says '16 SMs can generally achieve good bandwidth' which is now misleading since 16 is no longer the effective default.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare HybridEP dispatch/combine throughput with moe_hybridep_num_sms=None (new default) vs =16 (old default) on a pure-NVL MoE config to quantify the silent behavior change.

findings.claim: (1) HybridEPDispatch.forward runs `inspect.signature(HybridEPBuffer.dispatch_with_permute)` and `import inspect`/`import warnings` inside the forward path whenever `fused` or num_blocks_* is set. (2) This is executed on every forward call (per MoE layer per microbatch), and inspect.signature is relatively expensive Python-side work plus is not CUDA-graph friendly (Python-side conditional/reflection inside a captured region can break capture or add host overhead). This adds kernel-launch-gap / host overhead in the training loop. (3) Cache the capability check once (e.g., a module-level boolean computed at import time or lazily memoized), rather than calling inspect.signature on every forward.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark HybridEPDispatch.forward host overhead per call with fused=True comparing per-call inspect.signature vs a memoized capability flag; also verify CUDA graph capture compatibility.

findings.claim: (1) The docstring for moe_hybridep_num_sms now says 'None uses the default from DeepEP. In pure NVL scenarios, 16 SMs can generally achieve good bandwidth.' but the default is None, not 16. (2) A stale/misleading docstring is worse than none — a reader may assume 16 is still applied by default. (3) Clarify that with None the DeepEP internal default is used and that 16 is only a recommendation to set explicitly.

(1) The assertion `assert self.tp_size * self.ep_size > 1` was moved from an unconditional check to inside the `if moe_flex_dispatcher_backend == 'deepep'` branch (now 'DeepEP dispatcher requires TPxEP > 1'). (2) This is a behavior change: the hybridep backend (and any other branch) no longer enforces TPxEP > 1. If HybridEP also requires TPxEP > 1, single-rank hybridep configs will now proceed past this guard and may fail later with a less clear error or produce wrong results. (3) Confirm HybridEP genuinely supports TPxEP == 1; if not, add an equivalent assert to the hybridep branch.

(1) HybridEPDispatch.forward inserts new positional-order parameters `num_blocks_permute`, `num_blocks_unpermute`, `fused` between the existing `num_sms_combine_api` and `num_permuted_tokens`. Callers using positional args (e.g. the `hybrid_ep_dispatch` wrapper forwards positionally) must match the new order. (2) Any external/internal caller of HybridEPDispatch.apply or hybrid_ep_dispatch relying on positional args for num_permuted_tokens/pad_multiple will now silently bind them to the wrong parameters. (3) The in-repo callers were updated, but flag that HybridEPDispatch/HybridEPCombine are @internal_api surfaces; confirm no other positional callers exist and prefer keyword-only args to prevent positional-order breakage.

(1) The prior code recomputed `-exp(A_log.float())` into `_A_neg_exp_cache` on every step when `torch.cuda.is_current_stream_capturing()` was True (i.e. during CUDA-graph capture); this PR removes that clause so the cache is now only refilled when `_A_neg_exp_cache_stale` is True. (2) This is the intended fix: unconditionally refilling during capture defeated the purpose of the persistent cache and forced an extra exp/copy kernel into the captured graph. Because the cache tensor is allocated in the default pool (per the __init__ comment) its address is stable across capture/replay, so relying purely on the staleness flag is correct as long as the flag is properly set whenever A_log changes. The key risk is a correctness regression if there is any path where A_log is mutated after a decode without setting the stale flag. During inference A_log (a weight) is frozen, and the paired change to `train()` marks the cache stale on switching to training mode, so subsequent inference will refill once. This appears correct, but confidence is bounded by not seeing an explicit re-mark on the first capture. (3) Confirm that the cache is guaranteed to have been populated (stale->refill) at least once before the first CUDA-graph capture/replay, otherwise the capture may bake in an uninitialized/stale value.

(1) `train(mode)` now only sets `_A_neg_exp_cache_stale = True` when `mode is True`, retaining the prior staleness state when `mode is False` (i.e. `.eval()`). (2) This is correct and desirable: `model.eval()` is frequently called before inference and previously always marked the cache stale, forcing a needless refill even when weights had not changed since the last refill. Retaining state on eval avoids redundant refills across repeated eval() calls, while switching to train() (where weights may update) correctly invalidates. (3) No fix needed; the change is sound. Consider adding a short unit test that toggles train()/eval() repeatedly and asserts the stale flag transitions match expectations, to lock in this behavior.

(1) This change silently forces `args.fsdp_manual_registration = True` whenever `--nccl-ub` and `--use-megatron-fsdp` are set, overriding the documented default of False and any user who intentionally left it off. (2) This is a default-value/behavior change: users relying on the prior default now get manual registration semantics, which require the manual `manual_buffer_registration()` call after the first iteration. Inside Megatron-LM's train loop this is handled, but if a user reuses the same args/ddp_config outside the built-in loop (custom driver, tools, or evaluation-only paths that build the model but do not run the registration step), buffers may never be registered, silently degrading or breaking NCCL UB communication. (3) Consider only auto-enabling when the built-in `train()` loop is actually used, or document this override prominently and provide an explicit opt-out (e.g., don't override if the user passed --fsdp-manual-registration explicitly / add a --no-fsdp-manual-registration flag).

(1) The block immediately after the new auto-enable code asserts `args.use_megatron_fsdp` and `args.nccl_ub` when `fsdp_manual_registration` is True. Since the auto-enable path only fires when both are already True, the subsequent asserts are always satisfied for the auto-enabled case, but the ordering leaves the auto-set silently indistinguishable from a user-set value. (2) Minor: this makes it impossible for later validation to distinguish user intent from auto-enable, which matters if a future check wants to warn only on explicit misuse. (3) Optional: track whether the value was auto-set (e.g., a separate flag) so downstream logic and logging can distinguish auto-enable from explicit user request.

(1) The added `getattr(args, "nccl_ub", False)` guard in the train-loop condition is now redundant given validate_args only sets `fsdp_manual_registration=True` when `nccl_ub` is also True, and the pre-existing assert enforces `nccl_ub` whenever `fsdp_manual_registration` is set. (2) Low impact — it is a harmless extra condition, but it adds indirection implying the two can diverge when they cannot. (3) Optional: drop the extra guard, or keep it as defensive coding but note it is redundant with the arguments.py invariant.

(1) `cpu_shm_mode` handling in `TorchDistSaveShardedStrategy.async_save` only injects `use_cpu_shm_for_gpu_tensors` into `async_writer_kwargs` inside the branch that constructs the nvrx `async_writer`; but the docstring says it 'Only applies with nvrx async strategy' while the strategy class is shared by both mcore and nvrx backends. If a user sets `cpu_shm_mode=True` but the effective backend is the mcore fallback (`HAVE_NVRX=False`), the flag is silently ignored with no warning. (2) This is a silent no-op that can mislead operators on MNNVL systems into believing fabric-handle avoidance is active when it is not. (3) Emit a warning (or raise) when `cpu_shm_mode=True` but the resolved async writer does not support/apply it, mirroring the explicit AssertionError already raised when the installed nvrx lacks the parameter.

(1) `init_persistent_async_worker` performs `inspect.signature(...).parameters` membership checks for `cpu_shm_mode` in three separate places (AsyncCallsQueue.__init__, warmup_persistent_caller, and torch.py FileSystemWriterAsync.__init__), and `save_checkpoint` in checkpointing.py duplicates the same `inspect.signature` guard against `use_cpu_shm_for_gpu_tensors`. (2) This scattered capability-detection via `inspect` is fragile and repetitive; a single helper (e.g. `_nvrx_supports_cpu_shm()`) would centralize the version-compat logic and reduce the risk of the three sites drifting apart. (3) Extract a shared capability-detection helper and reuse it across async_utils.py, torch.py and checkpointing.py.

(1) In `init_persistent_async_worker`, `cpu_shm_mode` is passed to `AsyncCallsQueue.__init__` conditionally (only if the parameter exists AND async_strategy=='nvrx'), but is NOT guarded by raising an error when the constructor lacks the parameter, whereas the later `warmup_persistent_caller` branch DOES raise an AssertionError if the parameter is missing and the user requested cpu_shm. This asymmetry means a partially-updated nvrx (constructor lacks param but warmup has it, or vice versa) could silently drop the flag at construction while asserting at warmup, producing inconsistent state. (2) Inconsistent/partial application of the cpu-shm setting could lead to fabric handles still being used in the worker despite the user request. (3) Make both the constructor and warmup paths use the same guard semantics (both silently skip, or both assert) for a consistent version-compat contract.

(1) In the prefill/mixed FA4 branch (first `if HAVE_FA4:` block, ~line 838), `flash_attn4_varlen_func` is called with `q` directly, but the surrounding code path (see the pre-existing `flash_decode_and_prefill` at line ~865) reshapes `q` into `(num_requests, tokens_per_request, H, D)` before this point, whereas the FA4 varlen interface expects a packed 2D/3D `(total_tokens, H, D)` layout with `cu_seqlens_q`. The decode branch (second FA4 block, ~line 919) explicitly does `q_varlen = q.reshape(-1, q.shape[-2], q.shape[-1])` before calling the same varlen function, but the first (prefill) branch does not. This inconsistency means one of the two branches is passing a wrongly-shaped `q` to a varlen kernel that indexes by `cu_seqlens_q`, producing silently wrong attention outputs during inference. (2) Wrong-shaped input to a varlen kernel corrupts inference outputs without an error. (3) Make the `q` layout consistent: apply the same `q.reshape(-1, H, D)` (or confirm the kernel accepts the batched layout) in the first FA4 branch, matching the decode branch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Numerical parity test: run dynamic-batching inference with HAVE_FA4 enabled vs FA3/FA2 backend on the same prompts and compare output logits within tolerance.

findings.claim: (1) The FA4 decode branch (~line 919) recomputes `softmax_scale` via `if getattr(self, 'softmax_scale', None) is not None: softmax_scale = self.softmax_scale else: softmax_scale = q.shape[-1] ** -0.5`, but `softmax_scale` was already computed identically a few lines above (the block that the diff hunk header shows at ~line 835). This duplicate recomputation inside the `else` branch is redundant and, more importantly, uses `q.shape[-1]` which — for the decode branch — is computed on the already-reshaped `q` (still last dim = head_dim, so value happens to match), but the duplication indicates the two branches were developed separately and risks divergence if the outer computation changes. (2) Redundant/duplicated scale logic is a maintenance and correctness hazard. (3) Remove the duplicate `softmax_scale` recomputation in the FA4 decode branch and rely on the value computed once at the top.

(1) FA4 `flash_attn4_varlen_func` is unconditionally called with `causal=True` in both new branches. In the decode/generation path the pre-existing FA2/FA3 kvcache call also uses `causal=True`, but for a single-token decode with a full KV cache the causal masking semantics depend on correct `cu_seqlens_q`/`seqused_k` alignment; the varlen causal mask aligns query position i to key position (seqlen_k - seqlen_q + i). If `max_seqlen_q`/`cu_seqlens_q` are constructed for the packed decode tokens but `seqused_k` reflects full cache length, the causal alignment must place the query at the end of the K sequence. This should be verified — an off-by-one in causal alignment silently corrupts decode outputs. (2) Incorrect causal alignment in varlen decode produces wrong tokens. (3) Add an assertion/test that FA4 varlen decode output matches the FA2/FA3 kvcache path numerically.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare FA4 vs FA3 decode-step logits for identical KV cache state.

findings.claim: (1) `flash_attn4_varlen_func` returns a 2-tuple unpacked as `output_total, _` — the second element (typically LSE/softmax stats) is discarded. This is fine, but the FA4 import and both call sites hard-code `num_splits=1`, disabling split-K, whereas the FA2/FA3 kvcache path uses `num_splits=0 if not self.batch_invariant_mode else 1` (0 = heuristic auto-split for better decode throughput). Forcing `num_splits=1` for FA4 may leave significant decode performance on the table for long-context / low-batch inference. (2) Suboptimal split-K reduces GPU utilization during memory-bound decode. (3) Consider mirroring the `num_splits=0`(auto) behavior for FA4 when not in batch-invariant mode, or document why FA4 requires num_splits=1.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode throughput (tokens/s) with FA4 num_splits=1 vs num_splits=0 across batch sizes 1/8/64 and context lengths 4k/32k.

findings.claim: (1) The import alias `flash_attn4_varlen_func` shadows conceptually the existing `flash_attn_varlen_func` (FA2) imported from `flash_attn`. Having both `flash_attn_varlen_func` and `flash_attn4_varlen_func` in scope with near-identical names and different calling conventions (FA4 uses `page_table`/`seqused_k`, FA2 uses different kwargs) is error-prone. (2) Confusable names invite passing FA2-style kwargs to the FA4 function or vice versa. (3) Keep FA4-specific naming but consider grouping the FA4 kwargs into a documented helper to reduce the chance of kwarg mismatch across the two call sites.

(1) Changing `_batch_indices_decode_buffer` from int32 to int64 breaks the existing unit tests in tests/unit_tests/inference/contexts/attention_metadata/test_mamba_metadata.py, which assert `torch.equal(metadata_context.batch_indices_decode, expected_decode)` where every `expected_decode` is constructed with `dtype=torch.int32`. `torch.equal` returns False when the dtypes differ, so at least test_update_decode_only_exact_match, test_update_decode_only_padded, test_update_chunked_enabled_no_prefill_reqs, test_update_mixed_batch_exact, test_update_mixed_batch_mtp, test_update_padded_prefill_and_decode, and test_update_chunked_prefill_mixed_padded will fail. (2) This is a silent CI break introduced by the optimization. (3) Either update the expected tensors to int64 in the test file, or keep the copy source cast so the assertions still match.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run pytest tests/unit_tests/inference/contexts/attention_metadata/test_mamba_metadata.py and confirm decode-index assertions still pass with the int64 buffer.

findings.claim: (1) `self._batch_indices_decode_buffer[:real_decode_count].copy_(active_mamba_indices[:real_decode_count])` now copies an int32 source (active_mamba_indices is created as int32 in callers/tests) into an int64 destination; `.copy_()` performs an implicit dtype cast so this is functionally fine, but the sibling buffers (request_to_mamba_state_idx, _batch_indices_prefill_buffer) remain int32. (2) The stated rationale is to let selective_state_update index directly without a per-layer upcast; however the prefill path (batch_indices_prefill) still goes through selective_state_update and remains int32, so the removed `.to(torch.int64)` upcast in mamba_mixer.py no longer protects the prefill batch_indices from the integer-overflow case the deleted comment warned about. Verify the prefill state_batch_indices are also safe (or promote _batch_indices_prefill_buffer to int64 for consistency). (3) Make decode/prefill index dtypes consistent, or document why only decode needs int64.

(1) The old decode path unconditionally upcast `batch_indices` to int64 with the comment 'prevent integer overflow errors in the case of large max request counts.' That upcast has been removed and now relies solely on `_batch_indices_decode_buffer` being int64. But `_ssm_decode`/`_decode` can also be reached via static-batching where batch_indices may be None or come from a different source than mamba_metadata.batch_indices_decode; the removed cast previously normalized ALL callers. (2) If any decode caller supplies an int32 batch_indices from a source other than the now-int64 decode buffer, the overflow protection is silently gone. (3) Confirm every path reaching selective_state_update in decode passes the int64 decode buffer, or retain a lightweight dtype guarantee at the kernel boundary.

(1) `_get_decode_A_neg_exp` refills the cache when `torch.cuda.is_current_stream_capturing()` OR the cache is stale. Refilling on every stream capture is intentional (address must be valid in the graph pool), but the copy `self._A_neg_exp_cache.copy_(-torch.exp(self.A_log.float()))` happens inside `torch.no_grad()` while capturing; during capture this launches the three elementwise kernels that the optimization was meant to avoid, and it runs once per capture per layer. This is acceptable (capture is one-time) but note the cache is NOT marked non-stale when refilled during capture only-because-capturing (the `self._A_neg_exp_cache_stale = False` line does run, so subsequent non-capture replays skip recompute) — verify that a weight update between two captures without an intervening `train()` call correctly invalidates: only `train()` sets stale=True, so eval-mode weight edits (e.g. RL parameter sync done in eval mode) would use a stale cache. (2) Could yield silently wrong A values after in-place weight updates during eval. (3) Provide an explicit invalidation hook for weight-sync paths, or document that A_log must not change while in eval mode.

(1) `torch.topk(scores, k=topk, dim=1, sorted=torch.is_grad_enabled())` disables sorted top-k during inference (grad disabled) for speed. This is correct only if all downstream consumers of this routing output are order-insensitive (they gather/scatter by index, not by rank position). If any consumer assumes the k selected experts are returned in descending-score order (e.g. for tie-breaking, top-1 shortcuts, or logging), disabling sort during inference silently changes behavior versus training. (2) Potential train/inference divergence in expert selection ordering. (3) Confirm no downstream code relies on sorted order; add a brief comment noting the order-insensitivity requirement.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare MoE routing_map / expert assignment between grad-enabled and grad-disabled runs on identical inputs to confirm identical expert sets and outputs.

findings.claim: (1) The `train(self, mode=True)` override only sets `_A_neg_exp_cache_stale = True` and forwards to super; it does not gate on `mode`. Calling `.eval()` (which is `train(False)`) also marks the cache stale, which is correct (transition into eval should force a refill) but the docstring 'weights may have updated' is imprecise for the eval transition. (2) Minor: a misleading docstring is worse than none per rubric. (3) Reword docstring to 'Invalidate the decode cache on any train/eval mode transition since weights or mode may have changed.'

(1) `current_platform.is_rocm()` is evaluated on every `all_gather` call, which is on the hot path for TP forward passes. (2) Impact is minor: it is a per-call platform attribute/bool lookup rather than a device sync, but it is a constant across the object's lifetime and re-checking it per call adds a small avoidable overhead on the exact path the PR is trying to speed up. (3) Consider caching the result in `__init__` (e.g. `self._all_gather_use_base = current_platform.is_rocm()`) and branching on the cached flag, matching how the file already resolves `current_platform.is_rocm()` once during construction. Low priority — the current cost is negligible and functionally correct.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark repeated CudaCommunicator.all_gather calls on ROCm to confirm the platform check is not measurable vs a cached bool; compare decode throughput before/after the fix to validate the regression is resolved.

findings.claim: (1) In MockIndexer.fill_indices, the 'sliding_window' pattern with requests concatenates per-request row blocks of size q_len each (rows.append per request, one row per query position), then assigns torch.cat(rows) to topk_indices_buffer[:num_tokens]. (2) If sum(request.q_len) != num_tokens the assignment will raise a shape-mismatch and abort the benchmark run; the code implicitly assumes num_tokens == total query tokens across requests but does not assert it. (3) Add an explicit assert that torch.cat(rows).shape[0] == num_tokens (or that sum(q_len)==num_tokens) with a clear error message, so a mis-specified batch fails loudly rather than with an opaque copy_ error.
findings.suggested_benchmark: python benchmark.py --config configs/mla_sparse_mha_vs_mqa.yaml with sparse_mla_topk_pattern: sliding_window

findings.claim: (1) BenchmarkConfig.use_cuda_graphs default flips from False to True. (2) Any code path or external caller that constructs BenchmarkConfig without explicitly setting use_cuda_graphs now silently benchmarks under CUDA graphs, which changes measured numbers and may fail for backends incompatible with graph capture, making old vs new benchmark results non-comparable. (3) Confirm this is intended for all benchmark modes; if so, note the default change in the config docstring / PR notes so prior result CSVs aren't compared against new ones.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare mean_time for a fixed backend/batch_spec with use_cuda_graphs default before vs after this change

findings.claim: (1) run_do_bench now calls torch.accelerator.synchronize() unconditionally after every benchmark, and gc.collect()+empty_cache() in the cudagraph branch. (2) This is fine for accuracy of timing collection but the gc.collect()/empty_cache() added per-run can materially slow a large sweep (the new config has ~470 batch_specs x backends x variants); it also only runs in the cudagraph branch, so memory-pressure behavior differs between graph and non-graph modes. (3) Consider gating the gc.collect()/empty_cache() cleanup behind a flag or doing it once per spec rather than once per run if sweep wall-time matters.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time the full mla_sparse_mha_vs_mqa.yaml sweep with and without the per-run gc.collect()/empty_cache()

findings.claim: (1) The new `_swiglu_limit_pad_aware_kernel` indexes `topk_ids_ptr + row` as a flat `[num_tokens]` array, where `num_tokens = input.shape[0]` equals `M * num_topk` rows of the activation buffer, but `topk_ids` is a `[M, num_topk]` tensor. The kernel assumes row `r` of the (M*topk, 2N) activation buffer corresponds to `topk_ids.flatten()[r]` (i.e. token-major, topk-minor). In the marlin path this activation buffer (`intermediate_cache1`) is produced by `moe_wna16_marlin_gemm` whose row ordering is driven by `sorted_token_ids`/`expert_ids` (block-aligned, expert-sorted layout), NOT the raw `topk_ids.flatten()` order. If the physical row order of `intermediate_cache1` does not match `topk_ids.flatten()`, the pad-aware mask (`expert_id != -1` and expert_map lookup) will be applied to the wrong rows, corrupting the SwiGLU output for routed tokens and/or computing garbage for slots that should be skipped. (2) This is a silent correctness bug producing wrong logits only under EP/padding, hard to detect in single-GPU tests. (3) Add an explicit assertion/comment documenting that `topk_ids` row order must match the activation buffer row order, and verify against the marlin GEMM output layout; if they differ, pass the permuted/sorted expert-id mapping instead of raw topk_ids.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Correctness test: run fused_marlin_moe with an expert_map (EP>1 simulated) and compare output against the non-pad-aware _swiglu_limit_torch path for identical inputs.

findings.claim: (1) The pad-aware Triton kernel loads gate and up as fp32 (`.to(tl.float32)`) and computes `silu_gate = gate / (1.0 + tl.exp(-gate))`, but the grid caps the row-parallel dimension at `min(num_tokens, 256)` CTAs in program_id(0). With BLOCK_SIZE=1024 the column dimension is `cdiv(hidden_size, 1024)` which for typical hidden_size (e.g. 2880 for gpt-oss) is only 3 tiles. For large num_tokens (prefill with many tokens x topk), 256 persistent CTAs must serially iterate over all rows, which can underutilize the GPU compared to the previous `torch.compile`d elementwise path that launches enough blocks to cover all rows. (2) Potential throughput regression at high token counts / large batch prefill. (3) Consider scaling the grid's row dimension with num_tokens (e.g. up to SM count * occupancy) rather than a hard cap of 256, or benchmark to confirm 256 saturates the target GPUs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark _swiglu_limit_pad_aware vs _swiglu_limit_torch across num_tokens in {256, 4k, 16k, 64k} with hidden_size=2880 on H100/MI300.

findings.claim: (1) The pad-aware kernel does not zero the output rows for skipped (unrouted / non-local) slots — when `should_compute` is False it simply does not write. The output buffer `intermediate_cache2` is allocated with `torch.empty` (uninitialized) in `_fused_marlin_moe`. Downstream, marlin GEMM2 reads only rows selected by `sorted_token_ids`/`expert_ids`, so uninitialized skipped rows are presumably never consumed — but this relies on an undocumented invariant. In contrast the old CUDA `silu_and_mul_with_clamp` writes all rows. (2) If any downstream path (e.g. a moe_sum over topk, or a reduction that reads all M*topk rows) touches the skipped rows, uninitialized memory produces NaNs/garbage. (3) Add a comment documenting that skipped rows are intentionally left untouched and that downstream consumers must not read them, or zero-fill defensively when correctness is uncertain.

(1) In the pad-aware kernel, `expert_id = tl.load(topk_ids_ptr + row)` is loaded unconditionally, then used as an offset into `expert_map_ptr` with `mask=expert_id >= 0`. When `expert_id == -1` (unrouted slot), the masked load returns `other=-1` so `local_expert_id` is -1 and `should_compute` becomes False — this is correct. However the initial unconditional load of `topk_ids_ptr + row` assumes topk_ids has exactly `num_tokens` elements; if `num_tokens` (= input.shape[0] = M*topk) exceeds `topk_ids.numel()`, this reads out of bounds. (2) Out-of-bounds read is UB / potential crash if the activation buffer has more rows than topk_ids has entries (e.g. padding rows in batched marlin). (3) Verify `topk_ids.numel() == input.shape[0]` holds for all callers (batched_fused_marlin_moe uses topk=1 and does not pass topk_ids), or add a bounds mask on the topk_ids load.

(1) The new `silu_and_mul_with_clamp` helper hardcodes `alpha=1.0, beta=0.0` in the CUDA fallback branch, duplicating the constants that were previously inline. The SWIGLUOAI_UNINTERLEAVE path still calls `torch.ops._C.silu_and_mul_with_clamp` directly with configurable alpha/beta. This creates two call sites for the same op with divergent alpha/beta handling, and the new helper name `silu_and_mul_with_clamp` collides conceptually with the torch op `torch.ops._C.silu_and_mul_with_clamp`, which is confusing. (2) Maintenance risk: a future change to alpha/beta semantics could be applied to one path and missed in the other. (3) Consider renaming the Python helper (e.g. `silu_and_mul_with_clamp_pad_aware`) to avoid confusion with the C++ op, and document why alpha/beta are fixed here.

(1) `determine_available_memory()` still calls `get_memory_node_info(cpu_core.numa_node)` (and prints error messages referencing `cpu_core.numa_node`) directly, while `__init__` was updated to fall back to `allowed_memory_nodes[0]` when `cpu_core.numa_node` is not a valid memory node. On s390x `cpu_core.numa_node` is now a synthetic book/socket group id that generally does NOT correspond to a real `/sys/devices/system/node/node{id}` memory node. (2) This makes the two code paths inconsistent: at `__init__` the requested memory is computed against the fallback node, but at KV-cache sizing the memory info is read from the synthetic book id — `get_memory_node_info` then silently falls back to system-wide psutil numbers, which can differ from the per-node numbers used at init, producing incorrect KV-cache sizing and misleading `node {cpu_core.numa_node}` error text. (3) Extract the same numa_node->valid-memory-node resolution used in `__init__` into a helper and reuse it in `determine_available_memory()` so both paths use `memory_node`.

(1) On s390x, `allowed_numa_nodes = cr_utils.get_visible_memory_node()` is computed and then immediately overwritten by `sorted(set(cpu.numa_node for cpu in logical_cpu_list))`. (2) The initial `get_visible_memory_node()` call (which reads /proc affinity and env) is dead work on s390x and can be confusing to readers about which value is authoritative. (3) Guard the initial assignment or restructure so the s390x branch does not first compute an unused value.

(1) The s390x branch remaps `numa_node` to book/socket group ids but never validates that the chosen `group_key` levels actually align with physical memory nodes; the comment says 'they no longer correspond to physical memory nodes'. Downstream `get_memory_node_info(group_id)` will silently fall back to system-wide psutil totals for every rank/group. (2) On a multi-book s390x box this means every worker sees the same (system-wide) memory totals rather than per-node totals, so `gpu_memory_utilization`-based KV sizing may over-commit memory across co-located workers. (3) Add a shape/validity assertion or explicit documentation that per-group memory accounting is intentionally system-wide, and consider clamping requested memory by local_world_size when numa_node is synthetic.

(1) The s390x regexes `r'"book":\s*-\s*(,|\n|\})'` / `"socket"` map bare `-` to 0, but the group-selection logic uses `len(book_values - {-1}) > 1` to decide the partitioning level. If lscpu emits `-` for book on a single-book machine, those entries become 0 (not -1), so `book_values` = {0} and the code correctly falls through to socket — however entries where book/socket parse to -1 for other reasons are excluded from `logical_book_socket_list`, which can drop CPUs silently. (2) Silently dropped CPUs reduce the usable core set for binding without any warning. (3) Log a warning when entries are filtered out due to -1 group/core/cpu values, mirroring the existing `_synthesize_cpu_list` fallback messaging.

(1) The new ROCm branch in storeCacheElems first materializes each fp32 element to the model dtype (rounded_t via Converter::convert) and then calls fp8::scaled_convert<cache_t, rounded_t, kv_dt>(rounded[i], 1.0f), whereas the NVIDIA/CUDA path converts directly from float. (2) This introduces an extra fp32 -> bf16 rounding step before the fp8 conversion on ROCm only, which is a numerical behavior change for the main K/V cache fp8 store on AMD: it can produce slightly different fp8 values than the CUDA path and than the pre-change code (double rounding fp32->bf16->fp8 vs fp32->fp8). (3) Confirm this double-rounding is intentional (the comment says it matches ROCm's model-dtype materialization before FP8 conversion). If the goal is only to select an amd-specific scaled_convert overload, verify that the bf16 intermediate does not degrade accuracy vs a direct float->fp8 conversion; add a test asserting parity with the reference dequant path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: tests/kernels/test_minimax_m3_sparse_attn_fp8_scale.py: compare fp8 KV-cache values written by the fused kernel on ROCm against a fp32->fp8 reference to quantify the double-rounding error.

findings.claim: (1) The pointer-resolution block was changed from `} else { /* isIK */ ... }` to `} else if (isIK) { ... } else { return; }`, and this early `return` sits before the PDL `cudaGridDependencySynchronize()` / `cudaTriggerProgrammaticLaunchCompletion()` calls. (2) The file's own comments repeatedly emphasize using guards 'not early-return' so every thread reaches the PDL trigger; an early return here means any warp that lands on this branch never issues griddepcontrol.wait/complete. (3) This is currently safe because with the new gating (kProcessIndex=false => idx_slots=0, and isIQ/isIK constexpr-gated to false) every valid slot is Q/K/V so the else is unreachable; however it is fragile. Consider making it a guard (skip work but still fall through to PDL) or add a static/assert-documented invariant that the else is unreachable, to avoid a future silent PDL deadlock if slots_per_token accounting drifts.

(1) MiniMaxM3SparseAttention now calls set_default_quant_scales(self, register_buffer=True) and sets self.calculate_kv_scales=False to mimic the generic Attention layer's per-layer descale contract for FP8 KV reads. (2) This duplicates the quant-scale initialization logic that _init_kv_cache_quant performs in the Attention base class; if the scale contract (buffer names, q/k/v_range, host-float mirrors) evolves, this hand-rolled copy can silently drift out of sync and produce wrong FP8 descale. (3) Consider factoring the shared init into a small helper that both Attention._init_kv_cache_quant and this sparse layer call, or add a comment cross-referencing set_default_quant_scales so the coupling is explicit and greppable.

(1) New attribute self._aiter_sparse_pa_cache_data_ptr caches self.kv_cache.data_ptr() to detect cache rebinds in _ensure_aiter_sparse_pa_kv_cache. (2) Relying on data_ptr() equality to decide whether to recompute key_cache/value_cache views is fragile: a freed-and-reallocated tensor can reuse the same address, which would incorrectly skip the recompute and leave self.kv_cache_k/self.kv_cache_v pointing at stale storage. (3) Verify kv_cache is bound exactly once via bind_kv_cache and never reallocated to the same address within a process; if that invariant is not guaranteed, key off the tensor identity/version rather than the raw data pointer, and document the assumption.

(1) `autotune_kwargs['skip_ops']` is forwarded to `fi_utils.autotune(**autotune_kwargs)`, which lazily calls `flashinfer.autotuner.autotune`. (2) If the installed FlashInfer build predates `skip_ops` support, this raises `TypeError: autotune() got an unexpected keyword argument 'skip_ops'` and breaks warmup for users on older FlashInfer versions — a silent regression relative to the prior unconditional call. (3) Guard by only passing `skip_ops` when the underlying `autotune` signature accepts it (e.g. `inspect.signature` check or a `has_flashinfer_*` capability helper), or document the minimum FlashInfer version required.

(1) `_flashinfer_autotune_skip_ops` iterates over `runner.get_model().modules()` and, per module, does nested getattr chains over `('quant_method','scheme')`. (2) This is a one-time warmup-path scan (not in the training/inference hot loop), so cost is negligible; however it returns on the FIRST CuTeDSL kernel found, which is correct and cheap in practice. Noting only that the scan runs unconditionally even when the env var is set — but that path already short-circuits before the loop, so there is no real inefficiency. This is informational; no action needed.

(1) `QwenGatedDeltaNetAttention.forward` / `forward_cuda` / `forward_hip` / `forward_cpu` / `forward_xpu` and `Qwen3NextAttention.forward` all drop the `output` (and `num_tokens`) argument and switch from an in-place buffer contract (`output[:num_tokens], _ = self.out_proj(...)`) to returning a fresh tensor. (2) This is a public method-signature change on model-layer classes; any external caller/subclass or plugin (`@PluggableLayer.register(...)`) that still passes `output=`/`num_tokens=` positionally or by keyword will break with a TypeError, and the semantic contract (write into a caller-owned buffer) is now different. (3) Confirm all call sites across the repo (not just qwen3_next.py) are updated, and if this is part of a stable layer API, add a compat shim or note the intentional break in the PR description.

(1) Previously the output was written as `output[:num_tokens]` where `output = torch.empty_like(hidden_states)` sized to the full (possibly CUDA-graph-padded) token dimension, and `num_tokens = hidden_states.size(0)`. The new code returns `output, _ = self.out_proj(core_attn_out)` whose leading dim equals `core_attn_out`'s token count. (2) If the caller/downstream (residual add in `Qwen3NextDecoderLayer`, and the `@support_torch_compile`d graph) expects the returned `hidden_states` to have the full padded row count matching `residual`, a mismatch in the token dimension would cause a shape error or silently wrong residual add under CUDA-graph padding. Since `num_tokens == hidden_states.size(0)` here, the shapes appear to match, but this should be verified against the padded-batch / CUDA-graph path where `output` may have been larger than `num_tokens`. (3) Add a shape assertion at the residual-add boundary and verify with a padded/graph-captured decode batch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run Qwen3-Next/Qwen3.5 decode with CUDA graphs enabled and a padded batch (num_actual_tokens < padded rows); verify output correctness and no shape mismatch in the residual add.

findings.claim: (1) The commit message states the goal is to fuse more RMSNorm and all-reduce. Removing the pre-allocated `self_attention_output = torch.empty_like(hidden_states)` buffer and returning the `out_proj` result directly lets the RowParallelLinear all-reduce + subsequent RMSNorm fuse, which is the intended win. (2)/(3) This is the beneficial change; recommend adding a targeted throughput/latency benchmark to the PR to confirm the fusion actually triggers and yields the expected speedup rather than just removing the buffer alloc.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark Qwen3-Next/Qwen3.5 decode+prefill tokens/s before vs after; confirm the RMSNorm+all-reduce fusion pass fires on the o_proj/out_proj output.

findings.claim: (1) reorder_w1w3_to_w3w1 changed from an out-of-place operation (returned freshly allocated torch.cat(...).contiguous() tensors, leaving inputs untouched) to an in-place swap that mutates `weight` and `scale` and returns the same objects. It is exported in __all__ as a public utility. (2) Any caller (including future/external ones) that relies on the previous behavior of preserving the input tensors, or that passes a tensor still referenced elsewhere, will now observe silently corrupted weights/scales. (3) Keep the in-place fast path but document the mutation prominently (done in docstring) and, if the util is meant to be general-purpose, consider an `inplace: bool = True` flag so the old non-destructive contract remains available; at minimum verify all reuse sites. Both in-tree callers reassign to the same names and do not reuse the inputs, so this is currently safe.

(1) The new implementation adds `assert weight.is_contiguous()` and `assert scale.is_contiguous()`. The previous cat-based implementation worked on non-contiguous inputs (split + cat + .contiguous() handles arbitrary strides). (2) If any caller reaches reorder_w1w3_to_w3w1 with a non-contiguous w13/w13_scale (e.g. a transposed or sliced view produced upstream during weight loading), it will now hard-assert instead of silently working, turning a previously tolerated input into a load-time crash. (3) Confirm the nvfp4/mxint4 weight tensors are always contiguous at the call site, or call `.contiguous()` defensively (which the caller can do cheaply if already contiguous) rather than asserting.

(1) The chunk loop applies weight-derived half/chunk boundaries to the `scale` tensor inside `for t in (weight, scale)`, implicitly assuming scale.size(dim) == weight.size(dim). (2) This holds for both current callers (dim=-2, the 2*intermediate dim, identical for weight and scale) and matched the old code's assumption too, so it is not a regression; but the invariant is now silent. (3) Add an explicit `assert scale.size(dim) == size` (or `assert scale.size(d) == weight.size(d)`) to make the shared-boundary assumption fail loudly if a future caller passes a scale with a different split-dim size.

(1) The PDL completion trigger `cudaTriggerProgrammaticLaunchCompletion()` was moved from the end of the kernel (after outputs are written) to immediately after `cudaGridDependencySynchronize()`, i.e. before any accumulation or store to `out`. (2) Under CUDA Programmatic Dependent Launch, a downstream consumer that uses `cudaGridDependencySynchronize` AND reads this kernel's output (`out`) may now legally begin executing and read partial/stale results, producing silently wrong router logits — the safety of the change rests entirely on the undocumented-in-code assumption that the only PDL-dependent consumer (NVFP4 activation quant) reads `hidden_states`/`mat_a` and never `out`. (3) This is a fragile invariant: if any future consumer of the router logits opts into PDL dependency, correctness breaks with no compile/runtime guard. Add an assertion or comment at the consumer side documenting that no PDL consumer may read `out`, or keep an ordering guarantee (e.g. only fire early when it can be proven the consumer reads only mat_a). Recommend a dedicated benchmark+correctness test that captures both this kernel and a PDL-dependent output consumer in one CUDA graph and replays under load to detect the race.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CUDA-graph replay of fp32_router_gemm followed by a PDL-enabled kernel reading its output; assert bit-exact logits over many replays vs a serialized baseline.

findings.claim: (1) The inline comment `# Tier 2: fp32 specialized kernel (H=3072, E=256, M<=32)` in `forward()` was not updated when the kernel gained (6144,128) and (6144,256) support. (2) A stale/misleading comment about which shapes hit the fast path can cause future maintainers to reason incorrectly about dispatch coverage. (3) Update it to reference `FP32_SUPPORTED_SHAPES` (or list all three (H,E) pairs) consistent with the class docstring that was updated.

(1) The comment directly above `FP32_SUPPORTED_SHAPES` still enumerates only `(3072, 256) -> MiniMax-M2/M2.5, (6144, 128) -> MiniMax-M3` while the set itself was extended with `(6144, 256)`. (2) Documentation drift between the comment and the code it annotates. (3) Add `(6144, 256) -> GLM-5.2` to the comment to match the set and the .cu/entry files.

(1) The module docstring was changed to 'Correctness baseline: F.linear in float32', but `_ref` already uses float32 and the newly added `test_topk_routing_consistency` uses a float64 reference (`mat_a.double() @ mat_b.double().t()`). The docstring now describes only one of two references and understates the baseline precision. (2) Minor, but an inaccurate test docstring can mislead about the tolerance/precision guarantees being validated. (3) Clarify that the top-k test uses an fp64 reference while the elementwise tests use fp32.

(1) The `build()` method (unchanged in this diff) still relies on `common_attn_metadata.seq_lens_cpu[:num_reqs].numpy()` to compute `clamped_seq_lens`/`clamped_context_lens` and the metadata fingerprint. `seq_lens_cpu` is a `@deprecated` property that performs an implicit H2D->CPU sync (`self.seq_lens.to('cpu')`). Under the newly-enabled `UNIFORM_BATCH` CUDA-graph mode this builder will now be exercised on decode batches that get captured/replayed, and any host-device sync inside the build path degrades the throughput the PR is trying to improve. (2) Impact: CPU-GPU sync on the critical decode path defeats the perf goal of this MTP/UNIFORM_BATCH change. (3) Fix: derive context/seq lengths from `query_start_loc_cpu` + a CPU-side seq_lens buffer (as sibling `flashmla_sparse` does via `seq_lens_cpu_upper_bound`) instead of the deprecated `.seq_lens_cpu` sync, or confirm this path is not hit under CG capture.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-phase throughput (tokens/s) for DSV3.2 sparse MLA on ROCm with cudagraph enabled, before/after; also profile for host-device syncs in build().

findings.claim: (1) `self.vllm_config = vllm_config` is stored on the builder but, per grep of the file at parent, the existing code does not reference `self.vllm_config` anywhere; sibling builders (flashmla_sparse) store it because they use `self.vllm_config.model_config.max_model_len` inside build helpers. (2) Impact: minor — an attribute added without a confirmed read path in this file may be dead state or an incomplete migration. (3) Fix: verify a use path exists (base class may consume it) or drop the assignment; if a follow-up will use it, add a TODO note.

(1) `_tilelang_cache_miss_key` mutates the monitored object during what should be a read-only cache-miss probe: when `jit_impl.mode == "auto"` it calls `impl._infer_jit_mode(...)`, then permanently sets `impl.mode = mode` and `func.set_mode(mode)` BEFORE `original_call` runs. (2) A JIT *monitor* is supposed to be observation-only; here it silently alters the state of the TileLang JITImpl. If parse_args later raises (caught by the bare except returning None), the object is left with `mode` permanently changed from "auto" to a concrete mode, which can change subsequent dispatch behavior for that kernel. It also depends on a private API (`_infer_jit_mode`) that can change or vanish across TileLang versions. (3) Compute the cache key without mutating jit_impl/func state (e.g. infer mode into a local and pass it through, or restore the original mode after probing), and guard the private-attr access.

(1) `_call_with_monitor` wraps `JITImpl.__call__`, which is the per-invocation kernel dispatch path (not just the compile path). On every kernel call after activation it runs `_tilelang_cache_miss_key`, which invokes `parse_args(*args, **call_kwargs)` (and possibly `_infer_jit_mode`) plus dict copies (`_tilelang_call_kwargs`) — even on cache HITS. (2) Unlike the CuTeDSL/Triton hooks which only do work when an actual compilation event fires, this adds constant per-call Python overhead to the inference hot path for all TileLang kernels, defeating the purpose of a low-overhead post-warmup monitor and adding host-side latency. (3) Consider hooking a lower-frequency compile entry point, caching the parsed key, or short-circuiting the probe as cheaply as possible; at minimum document/measure the per-call overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark JITImpl.__call__ latency for a cached TileLang kernel with the monitor active vs inactive, over many iterations, to quantify per-call parse_args overhead.

findings.claim: (1) The depth guard `_tilelang_jitimpl_compile_depth` (to suppress the JITKernel.__init__ log when compilation originates from a JITImpl call) is only incremented when `cache_key is not None`. If `_tilelang_cache_miss_key` returns None due to a caught exception in parse_args (a real cache miss that couldn't be keyed) but `original_call` still compiles and constructs a JITKernel, the JITKernel.__init__ hook will fire, potentially producing a log attributed at the wrong layer / with a different kernel name. (2) This yields inconsistent/duplicated warnings in edge cases where key computation fails. (3) Consider incrementing the depth around every JITImpl.__call__ that may compile, independent of whether the key could be computed.

(1) `_tilelang_kernel_name` duplicates the fallback logic already present in `_setup_cutedsl_jit_hook`'s `_compile_with_monitor` (getattr __name__, else class name, else '<unknown>'). (2) Duplicated name-resolution logic is a maintenance hazard and can drift. (3) Extract a shared `_resolve_kernel_name(obj)` helper used by both the CuTeDSL and TileLang hooks.

(1) Before this change, `_capture_ubatches` computed the CUDA graph key as `ubatch_metadata[0].num_tokens + ubatch_metadata[1].num_tokens`, summing only the first two ubatches, while the lookup key in `__call__` uses `sum(ubatch_slice.num_tokens for ubatch_slice in ubatch_slices)` over ALL ubatches, and the retrieval in `_dispatch`/replay path uses `self.cudagraphs[num_tokens]` with the full sum. (2) When `num_ubatches > 2` (the config exposes a `num_ubatches` property and the framework does not hard-cap it at 2 in this wrapper), the graph would be stored under a smaller key than the one used at replay lookup, causing a KeyError / graph miss (re-capture) or storing under a wrong shape bucket — a real correctness/robustness bug. This commit correctly replaces it with `sum(m.num_tokens for m in ubatch_metadata)`, matching the store key to the lookup key. (3) The fix is correct; no further change needed. This is a confirmed and appropriate bugfix.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DBO/ubatch full-cudagraph capture with num_ubatches>2 and verify captured graph is found on replay (no re-capture / KeyError) and that stored key equals sum over all ubatch num_tokens.

findings.claim: (1) `_maybe_disable_dynamic_sd_for_data_parallel` is invoked immediately before `_maybe_override_dynamic_sd_cudagraph_mode` in `__post_init__`, which is the correct order: after disabling dynamic SD (setting `num_speculative_tokens_per_batch_size = None`), `uses_dynamic_speculative_decoding()` returns False, so the subsequent cudagraph-mode override correctly skips the dynamic-SD path. This ordering dependency is load-bearing but undocumented. (2) A future refactor that reorders these two calls (or inserts anything reading `uses_dynamic_speculative_decoding()` between the two) would silently re-enable dynamic-SD cudagraph handling for a config where DP forced fallback, potentially reintroducing DP divergence. (3) Add a short comment noting that the disable call must precede any consumer of `uses_dynamic_speculative_decoding()` / `num_speculative_tokens_per_batch_size`.

(1) The fallback only mutates `speculative_config.num_speculative_tokens_per_batch_size = None` on the VllmConfig's own speculative_config, but the CudaGraphManager (`vllm/v1/worker/gpu/cudagraph_utils.py`) and Scheduler read `speculative_config.num_speculative_tokens_per_batch_size` / `uses_dynamic_speculative_decoding()` from `vllm_config.speculative_config`. As long as all consumers share the same SpeculativeConfig object instance this is fine, and the added test confirms `scheduler.dynamic_sd_lookup is None`. However, if any component holds a deep-copied SpeculativeConfig (e.g. draft-model config copies made in speculative post-init), the mutation would not propagate. (2) Risk: partial disablement where the scheduler falls back but a worker still captures dynamic-SD graphs, causing the exact DP deadlock this PR aims to prevent. (3) Verify no consumer operates on a copied SpeculativeConfig; consider asserting the invariant or documenting that the single shared instance assumption holds.

(1) This PR removes the `use_persistent` guard that the immediately-preceding commit (PR #47567, `4c3c17d43`) added to disable the persistent sparse-MLA kernel for chunked-prefill continuations. The removed comment explicitly stated: 'The persistent MLA kernel is numerically wrong for multi-token prefill batches; errors compound across chunked prefill and break long-context decode (vllm#47042).' After this change, `work_meta_data=self._mla_work_meta_data` is always passed (never None), so the kernel unconditionally takes the persistent work-stealing path for ALL batch shapes including multi-token chunked-prefill continuations. (2) If the underlying aiter kernel numerical bug for multi-token prefill has NOT actually been fixed on the kernel side, this re-introduces silent wrong attention outputs and training/inference divergence for long-context/chunked-prefill — exactly the regression #47567 was created to prevent. The PR only rekeys the metadata fingerprint (adding per-request context_lens and seg_lengths); rekeying the cache fingerprint does not by itself make the persistent kernel numerically correct for multi-token prefill. (3) Confirm the aiter kernel numerical bug (#47042) is fixed for multi-token prefill before re-enabling; otherwise retain the `is_chunked_continuation`/`use_persistent` gate, or gate persistent-path enablement on an aiter version check. Add a regression test covering a chunked-prefill continuation batch (query_len>1, seq_len>query_len).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Correctness/accuracy comparison: run DSv3.2 sparse-MLA on ROCm gfx950 with chunked prefill enabled on a long-context prompt (seq_len >> chunk size, query_len>1) and compare generated logits/tokens against a non-persistent (work_meta_data=None) reference run to verify numerical equivalence.

findings.claim: (1) `clamped_context_lens` is computed as `seq_lens_cpu[:num_reqs] - seg_lengths`, but `seg_lengths = np.diff(starts)` is derived from `query_start_loc_cpu` and has length `num_reqs` only if the number of query segments equals `num_reqs`. `seg_lengths` is computed from all query segments (`req_id_per_token` built from it spans `num_tokens`), so its length equals the number of requests in query_start_loc minus 1. If `common_attn_metadata.query_start_loc_cpu` includes padding/extra entries beyond `num_reqs`, `seq_lens_cpu[:num_reqs] - seg_lengths` will broadcast-mismatch or subtract misaligned per-request values, producing a wrong fingerprint. (2) A wrong fingerprint can cause the persistent metadata launch to be skipped when it should re-run (stale work metadata → wrong CU work assignment → wrong output) or run unnecessarily. (3) Assert `seg_lengths.shape[0] == num_reqs` before the subtraction, or slice `seg_lengths[:num_reqs]` explicitly, and add a shape assertion at this boundary.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Add a unit test with a batch where query_start_loc_cpu has more segments than num_reqs (padded) and verify metadata_key computation does not raise / produces correct alignment.

findings.claim: (1) The fingerprint now includes both `clamped_context_lens` (= clamped(seq_lens - seg_lengths)) and `clamped_seq_lens` (= clamped(seq_lens)) and `seg_lengths.tobytes()`. Since clamped_context_lens is derived from seq_lens and seg_lengths, and seg_lengths is included raw, there is redundancy in what uniquely determines the key. (2) Redundant key components add per-build CPU overhead (extra numpy minimum + tobytes on the hot metadata-build path) and obscure which quantities actually drive kernel-metadata determinism, making the correctness reasoning in the comment harder to verify. (3) Include only the minimal set of quantities that the kernel metadata truly depends on (e.g. per-token clamped KV lengths + num_heads + num_tokens), and document precisely which one saturates so reviewers can validate the fingerprint is collision-free.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark the CPU-side metadata_key construction (numpy minimum + tobytes calls) per build step to confirm it is negligible relative to kernel launch on the decode hot path.

findings.claim: (1) Removing `params_dtype=self.router_dtype` and `force_fp32_compute=self.router_dtype == torch.float32` changes the router gate matmul precision: previously, when router_dtype was fp32 (i.e. `moe_router_dtype == "float32"` or `model_type == "glm_moe_dsa"`), gate weights were materialized in fp32 and the GEMM was computed in true fp32; now the gate weight defaults to the model dtype (bf16) and only `out_dtype=fp32` is kept, so the GEMM becomes bf16xbf16 with fp32 accumulation (DSV3 Tier-1 kernel / cuBLAS Tier-3 / cast-to-bf16 Tier-4 in gate_linear.py). (2) For plain DeepSeek-V2/V3 the DSV3 specialized kernel already computed in bf16, so this is essentially the intended, behavior-preserving perf/memory optimization. However `_get_moe_router_dtype` explicitly forces fp32 for `glm_moe_dsa` with the comment 'Older GLM-5/5.2 configs require fp32 routing'; those models no longer get a true fp32 router matmul, only fp32 output accumulation from bf16 inputs, which can measurably alter routing (top-k expert selection) and is an accuracy risk contradicting that comment. (3) Confirm with the author that bf16-input + fp32-accumulation routing is numerically acceptable for GLM MoE DSA / any model relying on `moe_router_dtype=float32`; if not, retain fp32 weights for that path or narrow the removal to DSv2/v3-only as the PR title suggests.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare router logits / top-k expert selection agreement and end-to-end task accuracy (e.g. small eval set) for a GLM MoE DSA model and a DeepSeek-V3 model before vs after the change, plus router GEMM latency/memory.

findings.claim: (1) The comment on `_get_moe_router_dtype` still states GLM-5/5.2 'require fp32 routing' and returns torch.float32, but after this change fp32 `router_dtype` no longer produces an fp32 gate weight/matmul (only fp32 output). (2) A stale/misleading comment about a precision requirement is worse than none, since a future reader may assume the routing GEMM runs in fp32 when it now runs in bf16. (3) Update the comment/docstring to clarify that `router_dtype` only controls output/accumulation dtype now, not the gate weight storage or matmul input precision.

(1) In `AsyncGPUModelRunnerOutput.__init__` the parent code used `torch.Event()` (line 262) and `AsyncGPUPoolingModelRunnerOutput` used `torch.Event()` (line 395); the diff changes both to `torch.cuda.Event(blocking=True)`. This is more than the stated ‘add blocking’ change — it also switches from the generic `torch.Event` factory to the CUDA-specific `torch.cuda.Event`. (2) This matters because these two constructors previously produced *device-generic* events (`torch.Event` dispatches based on the accelerator/current device), so the change silently narrows behavior to CUDA-only and could break non-CUDA accelerator backends (e.g. ROCm dispatches through torch.Event too, and XPU/other backends). If any non-CUDA path constructs these classes, `torch.cuda.Event` will fail or run on the wrong device. (3) Confirm whether these async output classes are ever instantiated on non-CUDA backends; if so, keep `torch.Event(...)` and pass `blocking=True` through the generic factory instead of hardcoding `torch.cuda.Event`. Note the same `torch.Event()` → `torch.cuda.Event(blocking=True)` change was applied at line 721 (prepare_inputs_event).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instantiate AsyncGPUModelRunnerOutput on a non-CUDA accelerator (or verify current_platform is always CUDA in these code paths) to confirm no regression.

findings.claim: (1) The PR converts 6 CUDA events (async_copy_ready_event ×2, copy_event ×3, prepare_inputs_event) to blocking mode to avoid busy-polling the CUDA driver lock, but leaves several other synchronized events in the same file unchanged: `transfer_event` (line 876), `draft_token_ids_event` (line 896), `num_accepted_tokens_event` (line 897), and `valid_sampled_token_count_event` (line 906) all remain `torch.Event()` (non-blocking spin). (2) This matters because these events are also `.synchronize()`-ed on the host in the hot per-step path; if the goal is to stop CPU spinning on the CUDA driver lock (which causes TP straggler contention per the author’s own comment), leaving these as spin-wait events means the driver-lock contention problem is only partially fixed — spec-decode / KV-transfer steps still busy-poll. (3) Evaluate whether these remaining synchronized events should also use `blocking=True` for consistency, or document why they are intentionally left as spin events (e.g. they synchronize on tiny/near-instant copies where spin latency is lower).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Under TP>1 with spec decode enabled, profile CPU time spent in cudaEventSynchronize/driver-lock contention before vs after also converting transfer_event/draft_token_ids_event to blocking.

findings.claim: (1) Blocking (sleep) CUDA events trade CPU utilization for slightly higher wakeup latency: `cudaEventSynchronize` on a blocking event deschedules the thread and relies on an interrupt/OS wakeup rather than a busy spin, which typically adds a few microseconds of latency per synchronize. (2) This matters because in low-batch, latency-sensitive decode loops the added per-step wakeup latency could measurably reduce single-request tokens/sec even as it improves aggregate throughput under contention. (3) Recommend a latency-vs-throughput benchmark (single-stream low-QPS decode and high-QPS TP) to confirm the blocking events do not regress p50/p99 inter-token latency; consider gating behind a config flag if a regression appears.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare p50/p99 inter-token latency at QPS=1 and throughput at high QPS with TP>1, blocking vs non-blocking events.

findings.claim: (1) The tiled loop selects logprobs using `valid_canvas_len_np[start_req + li]` while `_compiled_sample_step` is now passed the *sliced* `valid_canvas_len[tile]` and *sliced* logits `logits[start_req*CL : end_req*CL]`. `flat_logits`/`argmax_tokens` (`scaled`) are per-tile with local row 0 corresponding to global decode index `start_req`, so `pos = li * CL` is correct relative to the tile, and `valid_canvas_len_np[start_req + li]` is the correct global length. This is consistent. However, the argmax_tokens indexing `argmax_tokens[local_idx]` uses `local_idx` (a tensor from `just_converged.nonzero()`, which is local to the tile), while `k_i` uses `start_req + li` (global). Verify `local_idx == li` and both index the same per-tile row: `local_idx` indexes into per-tile `scaled` (correct, tile-local) and `start_req + li` indexes the global numpy array (correct). The dual-index scheme is subtle and easily broken; add an assertion `argmax_tokens.shape[0] == end_req - start_req` at parallelism boundary to guard against future regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-tile logprobs correctness test: run with concurrency high enough to force group < num_decode and assert per-request logprobs are bit-identical to single-tile (group == num_decode) path.

findings.claim: (1) `current_platform.mem_get_info()` is called inside `sample()` on every decode step whenever `num_decode > 0`. (2) `mem_get_info()` issues a CUDA driver call (host-device query) that can force a sync point and adds per-step latency in the hot sampling loop; it also makes tile sizing data-dependent, which defeats CUDA-graph capture / `torch.compile` stability for the enclosing region and can trigger recompiles as `group` changes shape of `logits[start*CL:end*CL]` across steps. (3) Compute the tile budget once from a cached free-memory snapshot (e.g. at init or first call) or derive `group` from a static configured memory fraction / max_num_reqs, avoiding a per-step driver query and keeping tile shapes stable.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure decode-step latency and throughput at fixed batch size with vs without the per-step mem_get_info() call; also count torch.compile recompilations of _compiled_sample_step across a run with varying num_decode.

findings.claim: (1) The per-tile logprobs stashing loop calls `local_idx.item()` and `slot.item()` inside a Python `for` over `just_converged.nonzero()`, and `just_converged.any()` triggers a device-to-host sync per tile. (2) These `.item()`/`.any()` calls are host-device syncs in the sampling hot path; with tiling they now execute once per tile instead of once per step, multiplying the number of syncs by the number of tiles at high concurrency — the exact regime this PR targets. (3) Batch the conversion: move `just_converged` and the needed indices to CPU once per tile via a single `.tolist()`/`.cpu()` and avoid `.item()` in the inner loop; consider computing `valid_canvas_len`/slots on CPU up front.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile number of cudaStreamSynchronize / implicit syncs per decode step as a function of tile count (group), comparing single-pass vs multi-tile.

findings.claim: (1) The tile budget uses a hardcoded magic factor `bytes_per_req = CL * vocab_size * 4 * 10` with `budget = int(free * 0.5) // ...`. (2) The '~10 transient fp32 copies' and '0.5 of free memory' are undocumented empirical constants; if the fp32 pipeline in `_compiled_sample_step` changes the live-copy count, the transient bound silently breaks (OOM regressions) with no guardrail. (3) Extract these as named module constants with a comment referencing the specific tensors counted, and consider asserting/logging the chosen `group` so regressions are observable.

(1) Behavior change: `sampled.zero_()` / `num_sampled.zero_()` moved from inside `_compiled_sample_step` (which cleared unconditionally on every call) to the caller, executed once before the tile loop. In the parent, when `num_decode == 0` the compiled step still ran and cleared outputs; now with the guard `if num_decode > 0` the compiled step is skipped entirely but the caller-side `zero_()` still runs unconditionally, so prefill/non-decode slots are still zeroed — this is preserved. However, verify that with `num_decode == 0` the parent actually invoked `_compiled_sample_step` (it received `decode_slots` possibly empty). If the parent relied on the compiled call for any other in-place state update when there were zero decode slots, skipping the whole loop now drops that side effect. (3) Confirm no state tensor (step_tensor/is_encoder_phase/history) update is required when num_decode == 0; if it is, restore an unconditional path.

(1) The comment claims 'Tiling is bit-identical to a single pass.'
