(2) This is only true if `_compiled_sample_step` has no cross-request/global reductions over the decode dimension and no dependence on batch size for numerics; the function mutates shared state tensors (canvas, step_tensor, is_encoder_phase, history) indexed by slot. If any operation inside normalizes or reduces across the full `[num_decode*CL]` dimension (e.g. a global softmax/normalizer over all decode rows, or top-k over the batch), splitting into tiles would NOT be bit-identical. (3) Verify no global (cross-tile) reduction exists inside `_compiled_sample_step`; if confirmed independent-per-request, keep the claim, otherwise the claim is misleading and correctness is broken.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Bit-exact comparison of canvas/argmax_canvas/sampled/num_sampled state and returned logprobs between group==num_decode and group==1 across several denoising steps.

findings.claim: The condition for disabling the persistent FlashInfer autotune cache was broadened from 'DeepEP backends only' to 'any distributed run' (get_world_group().world_size > 1). Previously, for multi-rank non-DeepEP setups, use_persistent_cache stayed True: only rank 0 ran the full autotune _dummy_run at max_num_batched_tokens and broadcast the resulting cache to all ranks. Now every rank runs the full autotune dummy run for any world_size > 1. WHY IT MATTERS: FlashInfer autotune _dummy_run at max tokens is expensive; forcing it on all N ranks (instead of leader-only + broadcast) increases warmup/startup time and also loses the on-disk cache reuse benefit across restarts for all multi-GPU deployments, not just DeepEP. This is a broadening of behavior that affects every distributed user. FIX: Confirm this is intended (the new comment says it is, to keep collectives synchronized); if leader-only + broadcast was working for non-DeepEP backends before, consider retaining it and scoping the every-rank path to the backends that actually deadlock, or document the startup-cost regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure kernel_warmup / flashinfer_autotune wall-clock time on a 8-GPU (TP=8) non-DeepEP FlashInfer deployment before vs after this change to quantify added startup cost.

findings.claim: (1) The `Gemma4MTPDecoderLayer.mlp` (`Gemma4MLP`) is now constructed with `quant_config=quant_config` (previously `quant_config=None`), and the attention `q_proj`/`o_proj`, `embed_tokens`, `pre_projection`, `post_projection`, and `lm_head` are all switched from `None`/`vllm_config.quant_config` to the draft-specific `get_draft_quant_config(vllm_config)`. (2) This is a correctness fix (the draft model was ignoring quantization), but it changes the effective weight/module layout and the checkpoint keys expected for these submodules (quantized layers introduce scale/quant params) — existing unquantized Gemma4 MTP draft checkpoints may still load fine (quant_config resolves to None when the draft is not quantized), but the switch from `vllm_config.quant_config` (target config) to `get_draft_quant_config` for the decoder layers changes behavior for setups where target and draft quant configs differ. Verify that `get_draft_quant_config` returns the correct config (draft-specific, falling back appropriately) so previously-working draft configs are not silently altered. (3) Confirm the draft quant config resolution and add/verify a test for a quantized target + unquantized draft (and vice versa).

(1) `lm_head` now receives `quant_config=self.quant_config`, but immediately after, when `tie_word_embeddings` is true, `self.lm_head.weight = self.model.embed_tokens.weight` overwrites the head weight with the embedding weight. (2) If the resolved draft quant_config quantizes `ParallelLMHead` differently than `VocabParallelEmbedding` (e.g. different param shapes/dtypes for quantized weights), tying by direct `.weight` assignment could produce a shape/dtype mismatch or bypass the head's quantization method. For unquantized drafts this is a no-op, but with a quantized draft the tie may become inconsistent. (3) Verify that when the draft is quantized, the embed_tokens and lm_head quantization methods are compatible for weight tying, or skip quant_config on whichever of the tied pair is not the source of truth.

(1) For the mnnvl backend, use_oneshot is now None, so trigger_completion_at_end evaluates to `(None is True) or num_tokens > PDL_ADVANCE_LAUNCH_TOKENS`, i.e. it is False for small batches (num_tokens <= PDL_ADVANCE_LAUNCH_TOKENS). (2) The adjacent comment documents that the one-shot Lamport all-reduce produces NaN for small token counts when trigger_completion_at_end is False; if FlashInfer's AUTO strategy internally selects one-shot for MNNVL small-batch shapes, this could re-expose that instability, since vLLM no longer forces trigger_completion_at_end=True on those shapes. (3) Confirm with FlashInfer whether the MNNVL AUTO path uses the same Lamport one-shot kernel; if so, consider keeping trigger_completion_at_end=True for small-batch MNNVL, or document why it is safe. Likely intentional given MNNVL may use a distinct kernel path, hence low severity.

(1) use_oneshot can now be None (mnnvl case) and is passed directly to flashinfer_comm.allreduce_fusion(use_oneshot=...). (2) If the installed FlashInfer version does not accept None for use_oneshot (treating None as AUTO), this would raise or misbehave at runtime; the correctness of the fix hinges on the external API contract, which cannot be verified from this repo. (3) Add a shape/type note or version guard, or an assertion/comment documenting that use_oneshot=None is the FlashInfer-sanctioned way to request the AUTO strategy. Confidence is low because the PR intent and comment strongly imply this is the supported API.

(1) `token_to_req_indices(buffer)` caches the result on the per-batch `CommonAttentionMetadata` instance, but the cached tensor is a SLICE OF THE FIRST CALLER'S `buffer`. The three DeepSeek-V4 builders each pass a DIFFERENT persistent buffer: `CompressorMetadataBuilder.token_to_req_indices`, `DeepseekSparseSWAMetadataBuilder.token_to_req_indices`, and `DeepseekV4FlashMLAMetadataBuilder.req_id_per_token_buffer`. Whichever builder runs first populates its own buffer and stores a slice of it in `_token_to_req_indices_cache`; every subsequent builder gets a CACHE HIT and receives the FIRST builder's buffer, silently ignoring the `buffer` argument it passed in. (2) This is only correct if a single `CommonAttentionMetadata` instance is NOT reused across more than one builder that supplies a distinct buffer. If the same `common_attn_metadata` object is shared by multiple builders in a batch (the usual pattern, since the base metadata is built once and passed to each layer group's builder), the returned tensor aliases a foreign builder's buffer. This breaks the explicit CUDA-graph address-stability contract documented in sparse_swa.py ('Ensure all metadata tensors maintain fixed memory addresses for CUDA graph compatibility') — during CUDA graph replay each builder must write into and hand back ITS OWN fixed-address buffer, but the cache makes all builders point at one builder's buffer, and which builder 'wins' can differ between capture and replay depending on build order, producing stale/garbage req indices at replay. (3) Fix: do not cache across differing buffers — either key the cache by buffer identity, always copy the cached values into the passed-in `buffer` and return `buffer[:num_tokens]`, or scope the cache to a single builder. At minimum assert `self._token_to_req_indices_cache.data_ptr()==buffer.data_ptr()` on cache hit to fail loudly instead of silently aliasing.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DeepSeek-V4 (compress_ratio path that activates both Compressor and SparseSWA/FlashMLA builders) under CUDA graphs with a mixed decode/prefill batch; verify token_to_req_indices consumed by each backend matches the batch layout across capture+replay, and compare output logits vs. the pre-cache implementation.

findings.claim: (1) The old FlashMLA path in sparse_mla.py did `self.req_id_per_token_buffer.fill_(0)` (zero the WHOLE buffer) before copying, whereas the new `token_to_req_indices` only zeros the tail region `buffer[num_mapped_tokens:num_tokens]` and leaves `buffer[num_tokens:]` untouched. (2) The removed comment explicitly stated 'Zero-fill for cudagraphs'. If the FlashMLA kernel or C128A kernel reads padded positions beyond `num_actual_tokens` up to the captured graph's fixed token count (CUDA graph replay uses a fixed padded batch size), those trailing slots now retain stale request indices from a previous build instead of 0, potentially indexing into out-of-range block-table rows. (3) Fix: preserve the full-buffer zero-fill semantics for the CUDA-graph-padded region (zero `buffer[:padded_capture_size]` or at least up to the captured num_tokens), or confirm the kernels never read past `num_actual_tokens`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CUDA-graph decode replay on DeepSeek-V4 with a batch smaller than the captured (padded) batch size; assert kernels reading req_id_per_token beyond num_actual_tokens see zeros, not stale indices.

findings.claim: (1) On the cache-MISS path the copy from CPU to the passed-in `buffer` uses `non_blocking=True` (async H2D), and the result slice is cached and returned immediately. On the cache-HIT path, the previously-computed cached slice is returned. There is no synchronization or event guarding the non_blocking copy; correctness relies on the copy and the consuming kernel being enqueued on the same stream. This matches prior behavior for the first caller, but a SECOND builder hitting the cache receives a tensor whose async copy was enqueued during the FIRST builder's build on possibly a different stream context; if builders run on different CUDA streams the consuming kernel may race the copy. (2) Silent wrong results / non-determinism. (3) Verify all builders share the same stream, or record/wait an event when returning a cached buffer populated by a non_blocking copy issued in a different build call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Stress DeepSeek-V4 multi-builder path and compare per-token req indices consumed by later builders against a synchronous reference.

findings.claim: (1) The cache stores `buffer[: max(num_mapped_tokens, num_tokens)]` then returns `self._token_to_req_indices_cache[:num_tokens]`; on a subsequent call with a LARGER num_actual_tokens (should not happen within one batch, but the guard `shape[0] >= num_tokens` allows it) the assertion passes yet the cached slice was sized by the earlier smaller num_tokens, so returning `[:num_tokens]` of a too-short cache would silently truncate rather than error. (2) Fragile invariant. (3) Since num_actual_tokens is fixed per CommonAttentionMetadata instance this is currently safe; add a comment stating num_actual_tokens is invariant for a given instance so the cache reuse is provably correct, or assert equality of num_tokens against the value used to build the cache.

(1) In `preprocess`, the guard `if njit is None: raise RuntimeError(...)` references the name `njit`, but `njit` is only bound at module scope inside the `if is_numba_available():` branch (via `from numba import njit, prange`). In the `else` branch (numba unavailable) neither `njit` nor `prange` is defined at all. (2) Consequently the guard cannot behave as documented: when numba is available `njit` is a decorator object (never `None`), so the check is dead; when numba is unavailable, evaluating `njit` raises `NameError` instead of the intended `RuntimeError('numba is required...')`, producing a confusing traceback. (3) Replace the guard with `if not is_numba_available(): raise RuntimeError('numba is required for fused Kimi image preprocessing')`, or define `njit = prange = None` in the `else` branch so the `is None` test is meaningful.

(1) Each media item is decoded/converted to PIL twice per request path: `media_tokens_calculator` calls `_ensure_media_type` (which runs `_to_pil` — base64 decode / PIL open / RGB convert) to compute token counts, and `preprocess` independently calls `_ensure_media_type` again on the same item to build patches. For base64/bytes images this means the image is decoded twice. (2) Image decode + RGB conversion is a non-trivial CPU cost and this is on the preprocessing hot path the PR is explicitly trying to accelerate. (3) Consider caching the decoded PIL/`_ensure_media_type` result (e.g. mutate the media dict in place so the second call is a no-op, which `_ensure_media_type` already partially does when passed a PIL Image, but the token-count and preprocess paths receive independent dict copies) or decode once and reuse.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark KimiK25FusedVisionProcessor end-to-end on a batch of base64-encoded images, comparing single vs double PIL decode cost.

findings.claim: (1) `numba_workqueue_threading_layer` restores `numba.config.THREADING_LAYER = previous_config` on exit, but the docstring correctly notes the threading layer choice is sticky once launched. If any code path enters this context, launches a parallel kernel under 'workqueue', then exits and a later caller sets THREADING_LAYER to a different value expecting it to take effect, they will be silently ignored — the restore-on-exit gives a false impression that the config is mutable. (2) This is mostly a documentation/expectation risk rather than a bug since selection is process-sticky. (3) Consider not restoring `numba.config.THREADING_LAYER` (only the env var) or expanding the comment at the restore site to reiterate that the restore is cosmetic, to avoid future confusion.

(1) `is_numba_available()` is invoked in `KimiK25ProcessingInfo.__init__` to choose `processor_cls`, and again inside `kimi_k25_vision_fused.py` at module import time (and implicitly relied on by the njit gating). `_has_module` performs an importlib find_spec each call. (2) Minor redundant work; low impact but adds indirection. (3) Optional: cache the result (e.g. `functools.lru_cache` on `is_numba_available`) so repeated probes are cheap; note the `KimiK25ProcessingInfo` is itself constructed per model load so the cost is small.

(1) `navit_resize_image` computes `new_w`/`new_h` via `int(width * scale)` (truncation toward zero) whereas typical HF NaViT-style processors and the reference Kimi image processor may use rounding; if the original remote HF processor rounded, token counts and patch grids from this fused path could differ by one patch on certain sizes, causing a mismatch between `media_tokens_calculator` output (used for prompt-token expansion) and actual `pixel_values`/`grid_thws` patch counts. (2) Any divergence between predicted token count and produced patch count would misalign media placeholder tokens with vision embeddings. (3) Verify against the reference Kimi processor that truncation (not rounding) matches its `new_w`/`new_h` computation for edge sizes; add a parity test comparing token counts and grid_thws against the remote HF processor across a range of image dimensions.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Parity test: for a grid of (width,height) sizes, compare navit_resize_image num_tokens/new_w/new_h and produced grid_thws against the reference remote HF Kimi processor.

findings.claim: HummingFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel.apply_scaled_mm is an abstract method it must implement, but its body is only `pass` (returns None). The base FP8ScaledMMLinearKernel.apply_weights() calls self.apply_scaled_mm(...) and returns its result. In this subclass apply_weights is fully overridden to call HummingMethod.forward_layer, so apply_scaled_mm is dead code today — but it exists only to satisfy the abstractmethod and silently returns None. Risk: any future/base code path that dispatches through apply_scaled_mm (e.g. manual fusion or as_quantized_activation paths) will silently get None and crash or corrupt output. Fix: either raise NotImplementedError in the stub, or make apply_weights not rely on the base contract; do not leave a silent `pass`.

HummingExpertsBase.__init__ changed `self.num_experts = self.layer.num_experts` to `self.layer.local_num_experts`. In the parent snapshot RoutedExperts has no `num_experts` attribute at all (only global_num_experts / local_num_experts), so the old line would have raised AttributeError — meaning this path was previously never exercised (or crashing). Now self.num_experts becomes the per-EP-rank local expert count. This value flows into estimate_local_valid_shape_m() as `num_experts = self.num_experts; return ceil(global_valid_shape_m * num_experts / global_num_experts)`, and into get_buffer_metas()/moe_align_block_size. Under expert parallelism (EP>1) local != global, so this changes tuning-shape estimation and workspace sizing. Verify the tuning-shape formula (global_valid_shape_m * local / global) is the intended per-rank token estimate, and that get_buffer_metas / moe_problem_size (which assert w1.size(0)==num_experts) are consistent with local expert count across all EP configurations.

get_humming_moe_gemm_type() return type changed from `str | None` to `str` and its default changed from None to 'indexed'. Previously, when VLLM_HUMMING_MOE_GEMM_TYPE was unset, the function returned None and is_supported_config() skipped the gemm-type filter (`if preferred_gemm_type is not None`), allowing any humming experts class to match. Now it always returns 'indexed', so with the env unset only INDEXED experts pass the `preferred_gemm_type == gemm_type` check and grouped_contiguous experts are silently rejected by default. This is a behavioral default change: users who relied on auto-selection between indexed/grouped now get indexed forced. Confirm this is intended and won't regress configs that only support grouped_contiguous.

HummingInt8ScaledMMLinearKernel.process_weights_after_loading mutates the weight in place with `weight.data = weight.data + 128` (shifting int8 to a uint8/offset domain expected by humming). If process_weights_after_loading is ever invoked more than once on the same layer (e.g. re-quantization, checkpoint reload without re-instantiation, or a fallback path that re-runs weight prep), the +128 shift is applied repeatedly, silently corrupting weights and producing wrong outputs with no error. Fix: guard with an idempotency flag (e.g. a layer attribute set after conversion) or perform the shift as part of the one-shot convert_linear_layer_to_humming_standard step.

HummingNvFp4LinearKernel.process_weights_after_loading does `layer.weight_global_scale = torch.nn.Parameter(1.0 / layer.weight_global_scale, ...)` to undo the CT scheme's inversion. This is also non-idempotent (reciprocal applied twice restores the inverted value) and additionally deletes layer.weight (`del layer.weight`) after aliasing to weight_packed only when weight_packed is absent. If any downstream code still references layer.weight (checkpoint save, weight introspection, or a second kernel selection attempt), it will fail with AttributeError. Verify process_weights_after_loading is guaranteed to run exactly once and that no consumer reads layer.weight after this rename.

In vllm/config/model.py the quantization override probe list adds 'mxfp8' immediately after 'modelopt_mxfp8'. The surrounding comment says heavy backends (which import Triton/MXFP4) should be probed last to avoid unnecessary imports during override detection. Confirm the new 'mxfp8' probe does not trigger a heavy import (humming/triton) earlier than intended in the ordering; if it can import a heavy backend it should be moved after the 'Ensure heavy backends are probed last' marker.

get_global_valid_shape_m() calls `ctx.dp_metadata.num_tokens_across_dp_cpu.sum().item()` — a .item() host-device sync — inside the per-forward MoE preparation path (via estimate_local_valid_shape_m -> prepare_humming_moe_kwargs, called each forward). Repeated .item() sync in the training/inference loop can serialize the pipeline and hurt throughput at high request rates. If num_tokens_across_dp_cpu is already a CPU tensor this is cheap, but confirm it does not force a device sync; otherwise cache or hoist the reduction.

(1) The docstring of _riscv_supports_rvv() still describes the old cpuinfo-primary logic ("we check that at least one supported zvl<N>b is advertised"), but the function now treats the C++ compile-time check (cpu_attn_has_isa) as ground truth and uses cpuinfo only as a fallback. (2) A stale docstring that contradicts the implementation misleads future maintainers about the detection precedence, especially subtle given this PR's whole point is that cpuinfo alone was unreliable. (3) Update the docstring to state that the C++ compile-time check is authoritative and cpuinfo parsing is only a fallback when the op is unavailable.

(1) The new cpuinfo fallback returns True whenever zvl128b/zvl256b is advertised, without excluding the forced-scalar build case (VLLM_RVV_VLEN=0, compiled -march=rv64gc, no RVV kernel). In that build the C++ check returns False, the fallback reads cpuinfo, finds zvl128b, and reports RVV as supported. (2) This can select the 'rvv' ISA path for a binary that has no RVV attention kernel, risking a runtime dispatch failure. Note this edge case also existed pre-change (old code returned True on zvl128b before consulting C++), so it is not a regression, but the refactor was an opportunity to fix it. (3) Since cpu_attn_has_isa is now consulted first and is authoritative, consider only using the cpuinfo fallback when the torch op itself is unavailable (import/attribute error), not when it explicitly returns False — an explicit False from a loaded _C should be trusted.

(1) The clamp only handles _best GREATER 256 (512/1024 -> 256). This is consistent with the C++ runtime check now accepting 128/256, so behavior is coherent. However, the foreach still iterates ITEMS 128 256 512 1024 and takes the largest match before clamping; a system advertising both zvl256b and zvl512b will detect 512 then clamp to 256, which is correct, but the clamp warning message ('clamping to 256') is emitted for VLEN=1024 hardware too where 256 may not be the closest supported multiple of the hardware VLEN. (2) Purely a messaging/clarity nit — functionally the build is correct. (3) Optionally clarify in the warning that RVV kernels only support 128/256 so the build targets 256 regardless of larger hardware VLEN.

In `_init_candidates` (cudagraph_utils.py) the DSD decode_query_lens are derived as `x[2] + num_new_sampled_tokens_per_step` where `x[2]` is the schedule entry's `num_speculative_tokens` (K) and `num_new_sampled_tokens_per_step = self.decode_query_len - self.vllm_config.num_speculative_tokens`. This is correct only when the scheduler clamps every schedule K to `<= vllm_config.num_speculative_tokens`. `build_dynamic_sd_schedule_lookup` clamps to `min(vllm_num_speculative_tokens, K)`, but the schedule stored in `num_speculative_tokens_per_batch_size` (read here directly) is the raw, un-clamped schedule. If a schedule entry declares K > num_speculative_tokens, the computed decode_query_len exceeds the actual max decode_query_len used at runtime, causing capture of an unreachable graph and (worse) potentially skipping capture of the real max shape. Recommend clamping each schedule K by `min(K, self.vllm_config.num_speculative_tokens)` before computing decode_query_lens, mirroring the scheduler's dense-lookup clamping.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Init CudaGraphManager with a DSD schedule containing K > num_speculative_tokens and assert captured decode graphs match the runtime-selected uniform_token_count values.

findings.claim: The new DSD candidate-generation loop in `_init_candidates` now iterates `product(capture_sizes, lora_capture_cases)` and, for each, loops over every entry in `decode_query_lens` (one per DSD schedule entry, up to max_spec_tokens+1 distinct query lengths). For each (num_tokens, decode_query_len) it does an `if desc not in descs_by_mode[decode_mode]` membership check which is O(len(list)) on a Python list — turning the whole thing into roughly O(capture_sizes * query_lens^2). For large max_num_seqs and many capture sizes this makes graph-capture setup meaningfully slower (though it is one-time startup cost, not per-step). Consider using a set for the dedup check instead of `desc not in list`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time CudaGraphManager.__init__ candidate generation with DSD enabled, max_num_seqs=512, max_spec_tokens=7, and a full capture_sizes grid; compare list-membership vs set-based dedup.

findings.claim: `round_up(num_tokens, decode_query_len)` is used to align capture sizes to a multiple of decode_query_len for MRv2, replacing the removed `adjust_cudagraph_sizes_for_spec_decode` call. Since num_tokens comes from `capture_sizes`, many original capture sizes will round up to the same rounded_num_tokens (collapsing distinct sizes), and rounded sizes above max_cg_capture_size / max_decode_tokens / max_num_reqs are skipped. This is functionally intended, but note the removal means MRv2 no longer expands `cudagraph_capture_sizes` itself — verify that no other code path (e.g. inductor compile sizes, capture progress accounting) still assumes capture_sizes were pre-adjusted to be multiples of decode_query_len as they were before this PR.

`resolve_cudagraph_mode_and_sizes` gains a new positional-capable parameter `use_v2_model_runner` inserted before `tensor_parallel_size`. Both in-tree callers were updated to keyword args, but any external/plugin caller passing `tensor_parallel_size`/`kv_cache_config`/`max_num_reqs` positionally after `uniform_decode_query_len` will now silently bind `tensor_parallel_size` to the `use_v2_model_runner` slot (a bool vs int mismatch that may not error). Since this is a public-ish config method, consider making `use_v2_model_runner` keyword-only or appending it at the end of the signature.

The `backend="cute-dsl"` kwarg is newly added to the external `flashinfer.mxfp8_quantize` call on the SM100+ (Blackwell) code path, with no version/feature guard. (1) Issue: this hard-codes a dependency on a flashinfer version whose `mxfp8_quantize` accepts a `backend` kwarg and supports the `cute-dsl` value for the *quantize* op (note the existing `cute-dsl` usage in flashinfer.py is on the separate `mm_mxfp8` GEMM, not the quantize entrypoint). (2) Risk: on installations with an older/mismatched flashinfer, this raises TypeError (unexpected kwarg) or ValueError (unsupported backend) on the primary Blackwell quantization path, i.e. a hard runtime failure for existing users who previously worked. (3) Fix: gate the kwarg behind a flashinfer version/capability check (e.g. inspect the signature or check a flashinfer version constant) and fall back to the default backend, or document the minimum required flashinfer version and add an explicit error message.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On an SM100 device, run mxfp8 activation quantization through mxfp8_e4m3_quantize with the pinned flashinfer version to (a) confirm the cute-dsl backend is accepted and (b) benchmark quantize throughput vs the previous default backend to validate the claimed improvement.

findings.claim: (1) `_use_v2_model_runner_default` now returns True for essentially all dense generate models via `or not model_config.is_moe`, so the ModelRunner V2 code path is enabled by default for every non-MoE, non-hybrid, non-attention-free architecture — not just the curated list in DEFAULT_V2_MODEL_RUNNER_ARCHITECTURES. (2) This silently changes runtime behavior for a very large set of models (any dense CausalLM) that were previously served by V1; if V2 has correctness/perf gaps for untested architectures, users get regressions with no opt-out other than VLLM_USE_V2_MODEL_RUNNER=0. The removal of Qwen3/Llama/Mistral from the explicit set is now covered by the broad `not is_moe` clause, but MoE dense models newly excluded (they still require the explicit allow-list). (3) Consider gating the broad default behind a validated compatibility check or documenting the rollout and ensuring the env override is clearly surfaced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Boot a spread of dense architectures (Gemma2, Phi, GPTNeoX, Falcon, etc.) and verify V2 model runner produces identical logits/outputs vs V1 and no throughput regression.

findings.claim: (1) The new default relies on `model_config.is_moe`, `is_hybrid`, and `is_attention_free`. The code uses `getattr(model_config, "is_hybrid", False)` and `getattr(..., "is_attention_free", False)` (defensive default) but accesses `model_config.is_moe` directly without getattr. (2) If any code path constructs a model_config-like object lacking `is_moe` (the tests use SimpleNamespace and always set is_moe, but real objects vary), this raises AttributeError, whereas is_hybrid/is_attention_free are treated as optional. This asymmetry is inconsistent and could break on objects that expose is_moe conditionally. (3) Either use getattr for is_moe as well for consistency, or drop the getattr on the hybrid/attention_free checks if all three are guaranteed attributes of ModelConfig.

(1) `DEFAULT_V2_MODEL_RUNNER_ARCHITECTURES` now contains only MoE architectures (DeepseekV2, Qwen2Moe, GraniteMoe) after removing the dense entries. Dense models are covered by `not is_moe`. (2) This makes the explicit set semantically an MoE allow-list, but its name still reads as a generic 'default V2 architectures' set, which is now misleading given the broad `not is_moe` fallthrough. A future reader may add a dense arch here redundantly or misunderstand the selection logic. (3) Rename/comment the frozenset to clarify it is the MoE opt-in allow-list, and document that dense models are enabled by default via the is_moe branch.

(1) `max_concurrent_batches` returns `pp_size + 1` when async scheduling and V2 are enabled (tests now assert `pp_size + int(async and v2)`). Enabling V2 by default for all dense models therefore increases the number of concurrent in-flight batches by one for the common async+PP case, raising activation/KV working-set memory. (2) The extra batch is intentional (to overlap async scheduling) but broadening V2 to all dense models makes this memory cost apply broadly and may push borderline deployments into OOM after this default flip. (3) Confirm memory headroom is accounted for in KV cache sizing when V2 becomes the default; add a note/benchmark for PP + async memory footprint.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory / KV cache blocks for a dense model with async_scheduling + PP>1 under V1 vs V2 defaults.

findings.claim: (1) `get_max_image_tokens()` (via `get_num_image_tokens`/`_get_vision_info`) uses `_get_video_max_pixels`/image-processor size only through the new `get_image_size_with_most_features` path, but the new `get_image_size_with_most_features` still reads from the image processor while the image path now derives max_pixels via `get_max_image_tokens()`. Meanwhile the new `_get_video_max_pixels` reads `video_processor_config["size"]` and `image_processor_config["size"]` via `dict[...]` indexing, which raises KeyError if the config uses `shortest_edge`/`max_pixels`-only keys rather than `size`. (2) For repos whose image/video preprocessor config stores the resize spec under a different key (e.g. only `max_pixels`, or `size` absent after fallback), `image_processor_config["size"]` and `video_processor_config["size"]` will raise KeyError at startup, breaking model load. (3) Guard with `.get("size")` and raise a clear ValueError, or fall back to loading the processor as before.

(1) `get_mm_max_tokens_per_item` now short-circuits to `return None` when `_get_processor_class_name() == "Glm4vProcessor"`, relying entirely on `processor_config.json`/`preprocessor_config.json`/`tokenizer_config.json` containing a correct `processor_class` field. Previously it instantiated the actual processor and used `isinstance(processor, Glm4vProcessor)`. (2) If a checkpoint has a stale/absent/incorrect `processor_class` (common for community fine-tunes or when `processor_config.json` is missing but the resolved processor is actually a Glm4vProcessor subclass), the config-string check will disagree with the runtime `isinstance` check. The code partially handles the `None` case by falling back to instantiation, but it does NOT handle the case where `processor_class` is present but reports a different string (e.g. a Glm4v subclass name or 'Glm46VProcessor'), so behavior silently changes for those checkpoints. (3) When `processor_class_name` is non-None but not exactly 'Glm4vProcessor', also verify via a lightweight class-name subclass check or document that only the exact string is treated as base Glm4vProcessor.

(1) The dummy inputs builder now derives `image_token = tokenizer.decode([hf_config.image_token_id])` and uses `hf_config.video_token_id` instead of `hf_processor.image_token` / `hf_processor.video_token_id`. This assumes the HF config exposes `image_token_id` and `video_token_id` attributes and that `tokenizer.decode([image_token_id])` reproduces exactly the processor's `image_token` string. (2) For Glm46V/AutoGLM variants where the processor's `image_token`/`video_token_id` differ from the raw config token ids (or where decode adds/strips whitespace or special-token markup), the generated dummy text will use a different placeholder than the real processor produces, causing profiling/token-count mismatches or dummy-input validation failures at startup. (3) Confirm `hf_config.video_token_id` equals `hf_processor.video_token_id` for all supported GLM4V variants (note the file elsewhere distinguishes Glm4vProcessor vs Glm46VProcessor video/image token semantics at lines ~1404-1409), and that `tokenizer.decode([image_token_id])` round-trips to `hf_processor.image_token`.

(1) `_get_video_max_pixels` and `get_max_image_tokens`'s new path call `BaseVideoProcessor.get_video_processor_dict(...)` / `ImageProcessingMixin.get_image_processor_dict(...)` directly on every invocation without caching, whereas the old code went through `get_image_processor`/`get_video_processor` which are backed by `lru_cache`d factories. (2) During startup metadata reads these may be called multiple times, re-parsing the config from disk/hub each call; while lighter than full processor init (the PR's goal), the repeated dict loads are avoidable. (3) Cache the parsed config dicts (e.g. via an lru_cache helper keyed on model+revision) to avoid redundant file reads.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure startup metadata-read time for GLM4V with and without caching of get_image_processor_dict/get_video_processor_dict.

findings.claim: (1) The new `_get_video_max_pixels` and the image-side logic in `_get_...` duplicate near-identical override/merge/longest_edge logic (mm_kwargs max_pixels override, size override merge, `_get_longest_edge`). (2) Duplication increases the chance the image and video paths diverge on future edits. (3) Extract a shared helper that takes a config-dict loader callable plus config_name and returns the resolved longest_edge.

(1) The pooled sliding-window conversion uses `cdiv(sliding_window, block_pool_size)` on the *token-count* window. The KV manager derives blocks per request from `SlidingWindowSpec.max_admission_blocks_per_request`, which computes `cdiv(sliding_window - 1 + max_num_batched_tokens, block_size) + 1`. Since the pooled spec's `block_size` is unchanged here (block-pooling stretches block_size only in the builder/kv-shape override, not in the spec returned by `get_kv_cache_spec`), scaling the window by `block_pool_size` while leaving `block_size` unchanged is the right knob to reduce reserved blocks. This appears correct given the test asserts `cdiv(750,4)==188` and 13 admission blocks. (2) Worth confirming: because the underlying attention builder replaces `block_size = block_size * block_pool_size`, the effective pooled block covers `block_pool_size` real tokens; expressing the window in pooled units (cdiv) is a conservative round-up so no window truncation occurs. The `cdiv` (round-up) is important — a floor would under-reserve and could evict in-window KV. This looks intentional and correct. (3) No fix needed; consider a one-line assertion or comment that `cdiv` (not floor) is required to avoid under-reserving in-window blocks, to guard against future edits.

(1) When PDL is now enabled (pdl_flag = num_tokens <= 16), the grouped_topk_fused_kernel has multiple early-return paths that bypass cudaTriggerProgrammaticLaunchCompletion(). Specifically: the `token_id >= num_tokens` return (line ~476), the `warp_id >= n_group_i32 || num_warps < n_group_i32` return (~490), and the `warp_id != 0` return (~528) all occur AFTER cudaGridDependencySynchronize() but return without ever calling cudaTriggerProgrammaticLaunchCompletion(). Only the `!proceed` path (~614) and the normal terminal path (~671) issue the launch-completion trigger. (2) Why it matters: with programmaticStreamSerializationAllowed enabled, PDL is per-grid, not per-block — the trigger only needs to fire from one block/thread for dependent-kernel launch, so this is generally NOT a hang (blocks that early-return are fine as long as at least the terminal path fires). HOWEVER, the `warp_id != 0` return path means only warp 0 continues; warps 1..n return early which is expected. The genuine risk is the interaction: since PDL was NEVER exercised before this PR (caller always passed false), enabling it for num_tokens<=16 exercises these code paths for the first time. The terminal trigger paths are gated behind `warp_id == 0` continuing, which does happen, so dependent launch should fire. (3) Fix: verify via test that at least one thread reaches a cudaTriggerProgrammaticLaunchCompletion() on every possible input in the num_tokens<=16 regime (including the not-proceed and degenerate n_group cases), since this is the first activation of the PDL path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run grouped_topk with num_tokens<=16 under a PDL-enabled stream with a dependent kernel and assert no deadlock/incorrect ordering; compare output against the num_tokens>16 (PDL-off) path for identical inputs.

findings.claim: (1) This PR changes the behavioral default: previously grouped_topk always launched with enable_pdl=false; now it launches with pdl_flag = (num_tokens <= 16) = true for small batches. This activates the programmatic-dependent-launch code path in grouped_topk_fused_kernel for the first time in production. The PTX->runtime-intrinsic swap (griddepcontrol.wait -> cudaGridDependencySynchronize, launch_dependents -> cudaTriggerProgrammaticLaunchCompletion) is semantically equivalent per the CUDA docs, but the PDL path itself was effectively dead code until now. (2) Why it matters: enabling PDL requires the caller/stream to actually set up a dependent launch; if downstream kernels are launched on the same stream without the matching launch attribute, PDL degrades gracefully (no-op) — but any correctness edge in the grid-dependency-synchronize placement (e.g. the comment 'all prolog can be put before acqbulk') is now live. (3) Fix: confirm the 16-token threshold is validated empirically for the target arch and that enabling PDL yields a measured speedup rather than being a no-op; document why 16 was chosen.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark grouped_topk end-to-end latency for num_tokens in {1,2,4,8,16,17,32} with and without pdl_flag to confirm the <=16 threshold actually improves latency (dependent-kernel overlap) and does not regress the boundary cases.

findings.claim: (1) PDLEnableTokens is a threshold/limit, not a boolean-style enable; the name reads like a flag. (2) Minor readability: a maintainer may misread it as 'PDL enabled: tokens'. (3) Suggest a clearer name like MaxTokensForPDL or PDLTokenThreshold, and consider referencing where the empirical value came from beyond 'The empirical value for small batch'.

(1) The new constant PDLEnableTokens is inserted immediately before `namespace warp_topk {` with the blank separator line removed, tucking the declaration against the namespace opener. (2) Purely cosmetic — no functional impact — but it slightly reduces readability of the constant block. (3) Restore the blank line between the constant group and the namespace for consistency with the surrounding style. (Note: formatting-only; low priority.)

(1) In `use_trtllm_attention`, the new SM90+FP8 decode branch (`elif current_platform.is_device_capability(90) and kv_cache_dtype.startswith("fp8")`) unconditionally sets `use_trtllm = True` regardless of `num_tokens`, whereas the general decode branch keeps the `num_tokens <= 256` guard that limits TRTLLM to small batches. (2) This means for SM90 + FP8 KV cache, XQA decode is selected even for large decode batches where it may be slower or where the guard was intentionally chosen for perf/correctness reasons, and it also bypasses the `kv_cache_dtype == "auto"` gate that the normal decode path uses. (3) Confirm intended: if the num_tokens cap should still apply on SM90, add `and num_tokens <= 256` to the SM90 branch; otherwise document why XQA decode is preferred for all batch sizes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode throughput on SM90 (Hopper) with fp8 KV cache across batch sizes 1..1024 comparing XQA vs FlashInfer-native decode.

findings.claim: (1) `supports_trtllm_attention` is decorated with `@functools.cache` but now takes a new argument `is_prefill: bool = False`; the cache key now includes this arg, which is fine, but the test suite's `_clear_supports_cache` fixture and existing call sites that call `supports_trtllm_attention()` with no arg will cache the `is_prefill=False` result separately. More importantly, callers previously relying on the SM100-only semantics now get `True` on SM90 for decode. (2) Any external/plugin code calling `supports_trtllm_attention()` (no args) on SM90 will now receive `True` (decode supported), silently changing behavior for code that assumed it meant SM100 TRTLLM available. (3) Verify all in-tree callers pass an explicit `is_prefill`, and note this semantic change in a changelog/deprecation note since it is a public util.

(1) The single `q_data_type` metadata field was split into `q_data_type_prefill` and `q_data_type_decode`, but the forward path historically asserted `attn_metadata.q_data_type == query.dtype` and passed `self.q_data_type` into wrapper `.plan()` calls (lines ~993, 1048, 1162, 1236, 1265, 1323 in parent). The diff shown is truncated before the build/forward sections, so it is not verifiable here that every prior use of `q_data_type` was correctly routed to the phase-specific field (prefill plan → prefill dtype, decode plan/assert → decode dtype). (2) If any prefill-path plan/assert accidentally uses the decode dtype (or vice-versa) on SM90 with FP8 KV cache — where the two dtypes intentionally differ (FP8-Q prefill vs BF16/FP16-Q decode) — FlashInfer will either raise a dtype-mismatch assert or silently compute attention with a mismatched query dtype, corrupting outputs. (3) Ensure the truncated build/forward code routes `q_data_type_prefill` to all prefill wrappers/asserts and `q_data_type_decode` to all decode wrappers/asserts; add explicit shape/dtype asserts at both boundaries.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Correctness test on SM90 with fp8 KV cache: compare FlashInfer XQA-decode + native-prefill outputs against a reference (FA/eager) backend for a mixed prefill+decode batch.

findings.claim: (1) `get_q_data_type` is called twice in the builder `__init__` (for prefill and decode), and the new `logger.info_once` block calls `current_platform.get_device_capability()` plus builds an `arch` string on every builder construction. These are one-time setup costs at init (not per-forward), so impact is minor, but the per-phase `get_q_data_type` reads `vllm_config.cache_config.cache_dtype` and calls `force_use_trtllm_attention()` (which calls `get_current_vllm_config()`) each time. (2) Repeated `get_current_vllm_config()` lookups in setup are cheap but the pattern of duplicating the whole dtype-selection logic per phase could drift. (3) Minor: cache `force_use_trtllm_attention()` and `cache_dtype` locally and pass into `get_q_data_type`, or compute both dtypes in one pass; not a hot-path issue.

(1) `FlashInferTrtllmAPIDecode` replaces `TRTLLMDecode` and now holds a `kernel: FlashInferDecodeKernel` discriminator plus the same fields; the builder also introduces `flashinfer_trtllm_api_decode_kernel` and `can_use_xqa_or_trtllm_gen_decode`. These names are long and mix abbreviation styles ('API', 'trtllm', 'xqa'). (2) Consistency/readability in distributed/attention code benefits from clear full names; 'FlashInferTrtllmAPIDecode' embeds the mechanism rather than the concept. (3) Consider `FlashInferTRTLLMDecodeMetadata` or similar, and keep the enum-vs-dataclass naming consistent; advisory only.

(1) In `get_supported_kernel_block_sizes`, `use_large_pages` now ANDs `current_platform.is_device_capability_family(100)` with `can_use_trtllm_attention(...)`. Since large pages (>=128) require the SM100 trtllm-gen path, this is correct, but `can_use_trtllm_attention` on SM90 can now return True for decode while large pages are still gated off — the added SM100 check is what enforces that. (2) The SM100 gate is now duplicated in several places (`supports_sink`, `supports_kv_cache_dtype`, the page-size assert in build). (3) Consider a single helper like `_is_trtllm_gen_platform()` to centralize the `is_device_capability_family(100)` checks and avoid divergence; advisory.

(1) The new guard `and not current_platform.is_device_capability_family(120)` disables the cooperative top-K path on SM120 (Blackwell RTX / GB20x consumer parts). This is a targeted correctness workaround (the cooperative_topk kernel presumably misbehaves or is unsupported on SM120). (2) It matters because leaving SM120 on the cooperative path would produce wrong top-K indices or a kernel failure; skipping it falls back to the persistent/other top-K path, which is the safe behavior. The change is minimal and consistent with existing `is_device_capability_family(120)` guards used elsewhere in the codebase (e.g. deep_gemm.py, marlin_utils.py, batched_deep_gemm_moe.py). (3) No fix needed — the change is correct and low-risk. Worth verifying that the fallback path (`use_persistent_topk`, which requires topk_tokens in (512, ...)) or the default `top_k_per_row` path actually engages on SM120 so that decode top-K is not silently skipped for shape/topk combinations that only the cooperative kernel handled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the DeepseekV32 sparse indexer decode top-K path on an SM120 device (num_rows<=32, aligned logits, topk_tokens=512/1024/2048) and verify top-K indices match a reference dense argsort, and that a non-cooperative fallback path executes without regressing latency.

findings.claim: (1) The new GeluAndMul.forward_cpu falls back to `self.native(x)` when `self.op` is falsy, but GeluAndMul (and its CustomOp base) has no method named `native`; the native implementation is called `forward_native`. (2) Any code path that reaches this branch will raise AttributeError at runtime instead of running GELU, breaking CPU inference for GeGLU models. Currently the branch is only reachable if `self.op` is None (e.g. an unexpected `approximate` value or a future refactor), but it is latent broken code. (3) Change `return self.native(x)` to `return self.forward_native(x)` to match the actual method name.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instantiate GeluAndMul on ARM CPU with self.op forced to None and call forward; verify it does not raise AttributeError and matches F.gelu reference.

findings.claim: (1) The newly added `torch.ops._C.gelu_tanh` op (GELUTanh.forward_cpu) is never covered by the new test; only `gelu_tanh_and_mul` is tested. The C++ `gelu_tanh` is a non-gated variant (`is_gated=false`) invoking `gelu_tanh_act`, and the whole point of the PR is the new `fast_tanhf_f32x4` NEON tanh path. (2) The fast tanh approximation (2.08 ULP, special-case handling around |x|>9.01, sign propagation via bit tricks) is numerically delicate; an unexercised op risks silently-wrong activations for models routed through GELUTanh on ARM. (3) Add a dedicated test for `torch.ops._C.gelu_tanh` (and/or GELUTanh.forward_cpu) comparing against F.gelu(x, approximate='tanh') across the same edge-case value grid used for gelu_tanh_and_mul.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Add test_cpu_gelu_tanh comparing torch.ops._C.gelu_tanh against F.gelu(approximate='tanh') over wide-range inputs incl. |x|>9.01 and negatives.

findings.claim: (1) `csrc/cpu/cpu_tanhf_neon.hpp` (a header) is added to the VLLM_EXT_SRC source list alongside .cpp files. Header files are not translation units and should be `#include`d (as it already is from cpu_types_arm.hpp), not compiled directly. (2) Depending on the build system/toolchain this either produces a redundant precompiled-header/no-op object or a build warning/error, and at best adds a pointless compilation entry; it does not achieve the intended purpose of building the tanh routine (which comes in via the header include). (3) Remove the .hpp entry from VLLM_EXT_SRC; the include in cpu_types_arm.hpp is sufficient.

(1) On ARM CPU the platform now force-appends `+gelu_and_mul` to custom_ops, which enables GeluAndMul.forward_cpu -> forward_cuda path (using torch.ops._C.gelu_and_mul / gelu_tanh_and_mul) instead of forward_native. This changes default behavior for existing ARM CPU users who previously ran the native GeGLU path. (2) If the custom CPU kernel's numerics differ from native F.gelu (particularly the new fast-tanh path), this silently alters model outputs for users relying on defaults. (3) Confirm the enabled CPU kernels match native within tolerance and document the default change in release notes / config docs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare GeluAndMul outputs with and without +gelu_and_mul on ARM CPU for approximate='none' and 'tanh' across representative model activations.

findings.claim: (1) File is missing a trailing newline at end of file ('No newline at end of file'). (2) Minor, but can trigger linter/compiler warnings and diff noise. (3) Add a trailing newline.

(1) The Triton kernel default for USE_FNUZ is False and FP8_MAX defaults to 448.0, but the CuteDSL FP8 path (fused_indexer_q_rope_quant_fp8_cutedsl) is NOT updated to receive the platform fp8 dtype/fp8_max; its cute kernel hardcodes torch.float8_e4m3fn semantics and 1.0/448.0 scale. (2) On the ROCm/gfx942 target this commit is fixing, cutedsl is an NVIDIA-only path (guarded by has_cutedsl(), NVIDIA cutlass), so this is not a live bug for gfx942 — but the divergence means the two backends are no longer numerically equivalent for fp8_max/dtype. This is worth confirming: if has_cutedsl() ever returns True on a platform whose fp8_dtype is fnuz, index_q_fp8 would be allocated as e4m3fnuz while the cute kernel would emit e4m3fn bytes reinterpreted, producing wrong quantization. (3) Add an assertion in fused_indexer_q_rope_quant that the cutedsl path is only taken when fp8_dtype == torch.float8_e4m3fn, or thread fp8_max/use_fnuz into the cutedsl helper.

(1) fp8_max for the fnuz path is set to 224.0 (Python side) while the Triton kernel's FP8_MAX defaults to 448.0; these must match the actual representable max of the chosen dtype. e4m3fnuz max finite value is 240.0, not 224.0. Using 224.0 slightly under-utilizes the fnuz dynamic range but is safe (conservative). e4m3fn max is 448.0 which is correct. (2) A too-small fp8_max just makes the scale slightly larger than optimal, marginally increasing quantization error rather than causing overflow — low correctness risk but sub-optimal. (3) Confirm the intended fnuz max; if 240.0 is intended, use 240.0; if 224.0 is a deliberate safety margin, add a comment explaining it.

(1) FP8_MAX and USE_FNUZ are given kernel-side defaults (448.0 / False), but every caller now passes them explicitly. (2) Defaulting FP8_MAX=448.0 while USE_FNUZ defaults False is self-consistent for the OCP path, but a future caller that passes only USE_FNUZ=True without FP8_MAX would silently use 448.0 for a fnuz tensor whose max is ~240, producing values that saturate/overflow the fp8 range. (3) Consider removing the defaults so callers must pass both together, or make the kernel derive one from the other, to prevent an inconsistent (USE_FNUZ=True, FP8_MAX=448) combination.

(1) In DeepseekV4Model.__init__, the instance attribute `self.has_tilelang = HAS_TILELANG_MHC` was removed and all references in DeepseekV4Model.forward replaced with `layer.use_fused_mhc`. This is a subtle behavior change: previously the model-level post step gate was `self.has_tilelang` (module-global), now it is `layer.use_fused_mhc` which additionally depends on `self.hidden_size % 256 == 0` per DecoderLayer. Because all layers share the same hidden_size this is consistent in practice, so the model path and the layer's forward() branch (`_forward_fused_post_pre` vs `_forward_unfused_post_pre`) now agree — which is the correct fix. (2) Risk: correctness depends on the invariant that `layer.hc_post`/`_forward_unfused_post_pre` executes the aiter mhc_post at exactly the point where the previous fused path deferred the post to the model loop. Verify the unfused path does NOT double-apply hc_post: _forward_unfused_post_pre already applies hc_post internally (lines 406/414) and returns (x, None, None, None), so at model.py:584 `layer.use_fused_mhc` is False and hc_post is skipped — consistent. When use_fused_mhc is True the fused path defers the final post to model.py:584. This is internally consistent. (3) No fix required, but a shape/dtype assertion at the pre/post boundary and a unit test comparing aiter vs tilelang vs torch on a hidden_size%256==0 config would guard against future regressions.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Numerical parity test: run DeepseekV4DecoderLayer with HAS_AITER_MHC=True vs HAS_TILELANG_MHC=True on a hidden_size%256==0 config and compare hidden_states/logits within tolerance.

findings.claim: (1) The aiter mhc_pre/mhc_post path is now the DEFAULT ROCm path (whenever HAS_AITER_MHC and hidden_size%256==0), re-enabling exactly the code that the removed TODO comments said was disabled due to an accuracy bug at large token counts ('It has accuracy problem at large number of tokens', referencing ROCm/aiter commit b639cb63). The diff does not add any aiter-version guard or gating on token count, and there is no evidence in this diff that the referenced aiter bugfix has landed / is required as a minimum aiter version. (2) Risk: if the deployed aiter version predates that bugfix, DeepSeek V4 inference on ROCm silently produces wrong outputs at large batch/prompt sizes — a silent correctness regression rather than a crash. (3) Fix: gate HAS_AITER_MHC on a minimum aiter version that includes the b639cb63 fix (e.g. check aiter.__version__ or feature-probe), and/or add a large-token accuracy test in CI before making this the default path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Accuracy test on ROCm/gfx9 with a large number of tokens (e.g. 8k-32k) comparing aiter mhc_pre/mhc_post output against the torch reference (mhc_pre_torch/mhc_post_torch) to confirm the previously-reported accuracy bug is fixed in the pinned aiter version.

findings.claim: (1) `HAS_AITER_MHC = is_aiter_found_and_supported()` is used to enable calling `torch.ops.vllm.mhc_pre_aiter` / `mhc_post_aiter`, but `is_aiter_found_and_supported()` only verifies platform==ROCm, on_mi3xx() arch, and that the aiter package is importable — it does NOT verify that the specific aiter build actually registers/implements the mhc_pre/mhc_post kernels. (2) Risk: on a ROCm gfx9 system with an aiter version that lacks these specific ops, the new default path would raise at runtime (missing op / attribute error) inside the forward pass instead of falling back to tilelang/torch. Previously these calls were commented out so this could not happen. (3) Fix: feature-probe for the actual mhc_pre_aiter/mhc_post_aiter kernel availability (or catch import/registration failure) when computing HAS_AITER_MHC, rather than relying only on package presence.

(1) The public-ish attribute `self.has_tilelang` on DeepseekV4DecoderLayer, DeepseekV4Model, and DeepSeekV4MultiTokenPredictorLayer was renamed/removed in favor of `use_fused_mhc` (kept only on the DecoderLayer). Model.has_tilelang and MTP layer's has_tilelang attributes were deleted. (2) Risk: any external code or tests referencing `.has_tilelang` on these modules will break; the semantics also changed (has_tilelang was purely tilelang availability, use_fused_mhc additionally excludes the aiter-preferred case). (3) Fix: this is internal AMD model code so risk is low, but confirm no other module/test references `.has_tilelang` on these classes; a grep-based check is warranted.

(1) `register_cutedsl_warmup_provider(self)` in `FlashAttnPrefillBackend.__init__` stores the backend instance in a module-level `weakref.WeakSet` (`_CUTEDSL_WARMUP_PROVIDERS`). (2) At `mla_attention.py:1569-1571` the runtime prefill backend used for forward is a `.clone()` of the one stored in `static_forward_context`, and the `MLAAttention.prefill_backend` instance created at init is the one that registers. Because the WeakSet only holds a weak reference, if the registering backend instance is not strongly referenced at the time `cutedsl_warmup()` runs (called from `kernel_warmup(worker)` later during worker init), it may be garbage-collected and silently dropped from the WeakSet, so no compile units are enumerated and the warmup silently no-ops (logs 'no compile units were requested'). This defeats the entire feature with no error. (3) Confirm the registering instance is guaranteed strongly reachable (e.g. via `compilation_config.static_forward_context`) for the full duration until `kernel_warmup` runs; if not reachable, use a strong registry or register the persisted instance. Add a test asserting compile units are found post-init.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instantiate an MLA model with FA4 enabled, run kernel_warmup, and assert cutedsl_warmup compiles >0 units (guards against WeakSet dropping the provider).

findings.claim: (1) `cutedsl_warmup()` calls `torch.accelerator.synchronize()` and gates on `current_platform.is_cuda()`, but the FA4 compile-only path only strongly-requires CUDA. This is fine, but note `_compile_cutedsl_warmup_units` synchronizes once after the loop under `torch.inference_mode()`. Compile-only JIT may enqueue no real GPU work, making the sync a harmless no-op. (2) Minor: no correctness impact. (3) Consider whether the synchronize is needed at all for compile-only units to avoid a spurious device sync during warmup; if it is only there to flush lazy compilation, document that.

(1) In `cutedsl_warmup()`, `_collect_unique_compile_units` already dedups by `unit.key`, but `_iter_fa4_mla_prefill_compile_requests` and FA4's own JIT cache also dedup. There are three layers of dedup (per-provider request dedup by `request_key`, cross-provider dedup by `unit.key`, and FA4 JIT cache). (2) Not a correctness issue but adds indirection; `unit.key` is derived from `request.key` which is `compile_spec.request_key()`, so the two Python-side dedups are largely redundant when a single provider is registered. (3) Consider collapsing to a single dedup layer to reduce maintenance surface.

(1) `_fa4_architecture_family_from_compute_capability` raises `ValueError` for any CUDA capability that is not exactly (9,0), major==10, or (12,0) — e.g. SM89 (Ada, 8.9), SM86, or future (11,x)/(13,x). (2) This function is invoked from `iter_fa4_mla_prefill_compile_specs` during warmup whenever the FA4 backend is selected. If FA4 backend selection ever admits a capability outside this set (or on a newer GPU), warmup will crash the whole worker startup with a ValueError rather than skipping gracefully. (3) Prefer returning None / skipping warmup for unknown capabilities (consistent with `_supports_fa4_mla_prefill`'s graceful skip), or assert that FA4 selection already restricts to these families and document that invariant.

(1) `FA4MLAPrefillCompileContext.effective_v_head_dim` returns `qk_head_dim` when `requires_v_padding` is True, i.e. the padded V dim, but the field `kv_nope_head_dim` is set by the caller to `qk_nope_head_dim + v_head_dim` (see flash_attn.py) which is the concatenated KV latent dim, not a 'nope' head dim. (2) The name `kv_nope_head_dim` is misleading — it is the combined nope+v dimension used to compute `v_stride`, not a nope-only dimension. A wrong/misleading name here risks incorrect stride computation if later edited. (3) Rename to something like `kv_c_head_dim` or `kv_combined_head_dim` and update the docstring.

(1) check_and_update_config overrides shutdown_timeout from 0 to 5 whenever the user left it at its default 0. (2) shutdown_timeout==0 is the documented 'abort' mode (see vllm/v1/engine/core.py: mode = 'abort' if shutdown_timeout == 0 else 'drain'); forcing it to 5 silently switches XPU deployments into 'drain' mode, changing server shutdown behavior for every XPU user relying on the default and adding up to a 5s drain/grace window on every shutdown. Because the check is `== 0`, a user cannot distinguish 'I explicitly want abort/0' from 'default 0' — they cannot opt back into abort on XPU. (3) Consider gating this behind an explicit sentinel/None default or documenting the behavior change, and only apply the override when the value was truly unset rather than any 0.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end XPU server shutdown latency with shutdown_timeout default before/after this change; confirm added grace window.

findings.claim: (1) The reset path now runs gc.collect() + empty_cache() + synchronize() for XPU (previously ROCm-only). (2) gc.collect() and device synchronize() are relatively expensive host-side operations; if this reset path is invoked frequently (e.g. per profiling run or per reset), it adds nontrivial latency on XPU. (3) Confirm this branch is only hit on shutdown/reset (infrequent) and not in any hot loop; if hot, guard it or make the gc.collect() conditional.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile frequency of GPUModelRunner.shutdown/reset calls on XPU and cost of gc.collect()+synchronize().

findings.claim: (1) XPUWorker.shutdown logs two info-level messages (start and done) with rank/local_rank on every worker shutdown. (2) These add log noise at INFO level for a routine lifecycle event across all ranks; the sibling GPU Worker.shutdown does not emit such logs. (3) Consider demoting to debug level to keep parity with the base worker and avoid per-rank INFO spam.

(1) The fix adds torch.cuda.current_stream().synchronize() after every DeepEP HT dispatch and combine when running ROCm DBO. This is a full host-side blocking synchronize inside the hot MoE forward path, executed twice per MoE layer per ubatch. (2) A full stream synchronize forces the CPU to wait for all queued GPU work to complete, which defeats DBO's entire purpose (overlapping compute/comm across microbatches) and serializes the pipeline, likely causing a significant throughput regression on ROCm. It also breaks communication-computation overlap. (3) If correctness requires ordering the workspace reuse, prefer a finer-grained mechanism (e.g. a CUDA event recorded after the HT kernel and a stream wait on the comm/compute stream, or double-buffered workspaces) instead of a global host synchronize. At minimum document the measured throughput cost and confirm no cheaper ordering primitive suffices.
findings.suggested_benchmark: ROCm DP+EP DeepEP high-throughput DBO end-to-end decode throughput (tokens/s) with and without the added stream synchronize; also profile per-layer MoE latency.

findings.claim: (1) Setting comm_sms = 0 for ROCm DeepEP HT DBO means the SMControlContextManager set_comm_sms/set_compute_sms lambdas remain no-ops (both gated on comm_sms > 0), so the SM-control context becomes inert but is still constructed and entered/exited per call. (2) Minor: the context manager machinery runs with no effect; not a correctness issue but adds trivial overhead and indirection. (3) Consider documenting that on this path SM control is fully disabled (comm_sms==0 short-circuits both set_* lambdas) so future readers do not assume CU reservation is active.

(1) The uses_rocm_deepep_ht_dbo predicate is computed identically in two files (vllm/config/vllm.py and vllm/v1/worker/gpu_ubatch_wrapper.py's _create_sm_control_context). (2) Duplicated multi-condition platform/backend checks tend to drift out of sync when one is updated (e.g. if another backend name is added), risking inconsistent behavior between the config-validation gate and the SM-control gate. (3) Extract a single helper (e.g. on ParallelConfig or a shared util) that both call sites use.

(1) When async_scheduling is explicitly enabled and uses_rocm_deepep_ht_dbo is true, the new branch raises ValueError before the existing eagle-speculative-decoding compatibility check. This is intended behavior (hard-fail on incompatibility), but verify ordering does not mask a more specific/earlier expected error and that the exception path is covered. (2) If another incompatibility should take precedence the user gets a less relevant message. (3) Add a unit test asserting the ValueError is raised for the ROCm DeepEP HT DBO + explicit async scheduling combination.

(1) In the weightless path (weight is None), rms_norm now allocates a fresh torch.ones(x.shape[-1]) tensor on every forward call, and fused_add_rms_norm does the same. (2) This adds a per-forward-pass device allocation and a full elementwise multiply-by-ones inside the XPU _C kernel, which is pure overhead compared to the previous native path that skipped the multiply; the shape-[hidden_size] ones tensor is constant across steps for a given hidden size. (3) Consider caching the ones weight (e.g. a small dtype/device/size-keyed cache) instead of reallocating each call, if the weightless path is exercised in a hot loop. This is XPU-only and the correctness is fine (multiply by 1.0 == weightless RMSNorm), so it is a minor performance note.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark rms_norm / fused_add_rms_norm on XPU with weight=None over many iterations comparing the torch.ones-per-call path vs a cached-ones path for hidden sizes typical of served models (e.g. 4096, 8192).

findings.claim: (1) This revert re-introduces synchronous, init-time host-memory pinning: CUDA/ROCm CPU tensors are now allocated with pin_memory=True (torch.zeros(..., pin_memory=pin_memory)), and pin_mmap_region()/cudaHostRegister runs synchronously before the handlers are constructed, whereas the reverted commit (#45850) moved this to a background thread precisely because pinning multi-GB regions blocks worker initialization for seconds. (2) Impact: worker/engine startup latency regresses noticeably (the reverted comment explicitly noted 'Pinning here would block worker initialization'); for large KV offload regions this can add multiple seconds of serial startup per worker. (3) Fix: this is an intentional BugFix revert (the background-thread approach presumably had a correctness/lifetime bug), so the regression is likely accepted — but the follow-up should re-land the async pinning with the underlying thread-safety bug fixed rather than leaving startup permanently slower. Confirm the revert is intended to trade throughput/startup for stability.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure CPUOffloadingWorker.__init__ wall-clock time on CUDA with a large num_cpu_blocks (e.g. tens of GB of CPU KV) before/after this commit to quantify the startup regression from synchronous pinning.

findings.claim: (1) In pin_mmap_region, the cudaHostRegister failure warning formats the raw cudart result object with %d: logger.warning('...(code=%d)...', rank, result) passes `result` rather than `result.value`. (2) Impact: the log will either raise a formatting TypeError or print an unhelpful repr instead of the numeric error code, obscuring diagnosis when pinning actually fails (a real-but-rare event). Note the same file's cleanup path in shared_offload_region.py uses `result` too, but the reverted _pin_cpu_tensors correctly used `result.value`. (3) Fix: change the logging argument to `result.value` to match the earlier convention.

(1) pin_mmap_region reads region.rank into a local `rank` and dereferences the private member region._base.data_ptr() from outside the SharedOffloadRegion class; combined with directly setting region.is_pinned = True, the pinning logic reaches into private state of SharedOffloadRegion rather than delegating to a method on that class (which already owns the symmetric cudaHostUnregister/is_pinned reset in cleanup()). (2) Impact: encapsulation leak — future changes to SharedOffloadRegion internals (_base, total_size_bytes, is_pinned) can silently break this free function; the register/unregister lifecycle is now split across two files. (3) Fix: add a region.pin() method on SharedOffloadRegion mirroring its cleanup() unpinning path and call it here.

(1) The new v1 block-table warmup is gated on `worker.use_v2_model_runner`, but `kernel_warmup` receives a `Worker` (gpu_worker) and the pre-change code always accessed model-runner-level attributes; `use_v2_model_runner` does exist on the worker (set at gpu_worker.py:168), so the attribute access is valid, but the guard `not worker.use_v2_model_runner` silently skips warmup for the v2 model runner path. (2) If the v2 runner also uses the same v1 block-table slot-mapping Triton kernel (`_compute_slot_mapping_kernel` in block_table.py, shared by BlockTable used by both runners), the intended JIT warmup will not happen on v2 and the first real request pays JIT cost — a partial perf regression of the stated goal. (3) Confirm whether v2 model runner uses the same BlockTable.compute_slot_mapping kernel; if so, warm it for both paths or document why v2 is excluded.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time-to-first-token on a v2-model-runner (VLLM_USE_V2_MODEL_RUNNER=1) DSv4 config vs v1, measuring first-request JIT latency for the slot-mapping kernel.

findings.claim: (1) `warm_v1_block_table_kernels` constructs a `BlockTable` with `cp_kv_cache_interleave_size=1` and relies on PCP/DCP groups being initialized; the real runtime block table may use a different `cp_kv_cache_interleave_size` and non-trivial CP world sizes, which are `tl.constexpr` in `_compute_slot_mapping_kernel` (TOTAL_CP_WORLD_SIZE, TOTAL_CP_RANK, CP_KV_CACHE_INTERLEAVE_SIZE). (2) Triton specializes/JITs a distinct kernel per distinct constexpr combination, so warming only interleave_size=1 with the warmup-derived CP sizes will NOT prime the kernel variant actually used at runtime when CP is enabled or interleave_size differs — the warmup is a partial no-op for those configs. (3) Derive `cp_kv_cache_interleave_size` and CP world sizes from the real runner/vllm_config rather than hardcoding 1, or document that CP configs are intentionally not covered.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare first-request latency with warmup on a context-parallel (CP>1) DSv4 config to verify the slot-mapping kernel is actually pre-JITed.

findings.claim: (1) `max_num_blocks_per_req` is derived from `max(num_tokens, max_tokens)` where `num_tokens` (clamped to <=8) is always <= `max_tokens`, so `max(num_tokens, max_tokens)` always equals `max_tokens`; likewise `max_num_batched_tokens=max(num_tokens, max_tokens)` is always `max_tokens`. (2) This is dead complexity: the `max(...)` wrappers add indirection with no behavioral effect, making the intent (which token count is being warmed) harder to read. (3) Replace `max(num_tokens, max_tokens)` with `max_tokens` directly, or clarify intent.

(1) `sparse_mla_triton_warmup_if_needed` uses a fixed `num_tokens = _clamp_warmup_tokens(8, max_tokens)` for warming prefill/combine kernels, while real prefill traffic uses much larger query lengths; the warmed Triton kernels for `_compute_prefill_metadata_kernel`, `build_prefill_chunk_metadata`, and `combine_topk_swa_indices` are keyed by grid/constexpr shapes (e.g. `M`, `N`, `PADDED_TOP_K`, `BLOCK_SIZE=_next_power_of_2(num_prefills)`), not by num_tokens. (2) Since the M/N/topk warmup cases are enumerated explicitly and num_tokens only affects tensor sizes (not constexpr specialization for these kernels), 8 tokens is likely sufficient — but the prefill-metadata kernel's `BLOCK_SIZE` is specialized on `num_prefills` only and the combine kernel `M`/`N` are runtime args (not constexpr), so specialization coverage should be double-checked. (3) Verify that the runtime-varying `M`/`N`/`num_tokens` do not trigger a fresh JIT at first real request; if they can, expand coverage or document the invariance.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instrument Triton JIT cache hits on first real DSv4 prefill request after warmup to confirm no cold-compile for combine_topk_swa_indices / build_prefill_chunk_metadata.

findings.claim: (1) `sparse_mla_triton_warmup_if_needed` wraps the entire warmup in a broad `except Exception: logger.warning(...)`. (2) This is defensively correct for a warmup path (a failure should not break startup), but a silent broad catch can also mask a genuine kernel/shape bug so the warmup silently becomes a no-op and the perf benefit is lost without a hard signal — future regressions in kernel signatures (e.g. the argument-order coupling to `build_prefill_chunk_metadata` / `combine_topk_swa_indices`) would be swallowed. (3) Consider logging at a level/counter that surfaces in CI, or add a test that asserts the warmup path executes the kernels without exception on a representative DSv4 config.

(1) This PR flips FlashInfer autotune from always using the non-persistent-cache branch (the old module constant `_FLASHINFER_USE_PERSISTENT_CACHE = False` forced everyone through the simple rank-synchronous path) to defaulting `use_persistent_cache = True`, so all non-DeepEP configs now take the rank-0-only tune + broadcast path. (2) This is a real behavioral change bundled into a version-bump PR; if the FlashInfer 0.6.13 cache-collision fix (referenced in the removed TODO about `use_8x4_sf_layout=True/False`) is not actually present or complete in 0.6.13, re-enabling the persistent cache can cause rank 0 to select and broadcast an invalid tactic, silently producing wrong kernel dispatch. (3) Confirm the upstream FlashInfer fix is in 0.6.13 before enabling; consider guarding on the flashinfer version or keeping an env override to disable persistent cache.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run FlashInfer autotune on Hopper/Blackwell with flashinfer 0.6.13 and verify persistent cache produces identical tactics vs non-persistent path (no accuracy regression on gsm8k).

findings.claim: (1) The `deepep_a2a_backends` set enumerates only `deepep_high_throughput`, `deepep_low_latency`, `deepep_v2`, but the All2AllBackend Literal also includes `mori_high_throughput`, `mori_low_latency`, `nixl_ep`, and `flashinfer_*` backends. (2) The stated reason for skipping persistent cache is that dispatch/combine can time out when only rank 0 autotunes while other ranks race ahead; this same rank-divergence hazard plausibly applies to MoRI and nixl_ep EP backends, which would still take the persistent (rank-0-only) path and could hit the same collective timeout. (3) If the timeout risk is intrinsic to any all2all EP kernel, consider gating on `parallel_config.use_all2all_kernels`/EP-active rather than a hardcoded DeepEP-only set, or document why MoRI/nixl_ep are exempt.

(1) In batch_lookup(), PyList_Size(path_list) can return an empty list where n==0; also more importantly the code calls PyList_GetItem() and PyUnicode_AsUTF8AndSize() with the GIL held (correct), stores raw const char* pointers into `paths`, then releases the GIL and dereferences them in _batch_lookup(). The pointers returned by PyUnicode_AsUTF8AndSize point into the interned UTF-8 cache of the str objects, which remain alive via the borrowed `path_list` reference for the duration of the call, so this is safe. However, PyUnicode_AsUTF8AndSize returns UTF-8 that may contain embedded NUL bytes; access(2) will silently treat such a path as truncated at the first NUL, whereas the previous os.path.exists() path would raise ValueError('embedded null byte'). (2) This is a behavioral divergence between the C fast-path and the Python fallback: the same key set can yield different lookup results (a false HIT/MISS) depending on whether the extension is built, producing non-deterministic KV-cache hit behavior. (3) Reject strings whose UTF-8 length (second out-param of PyUnicode_AsUTF8AndSize) differs from strlen, or document that paths are guaranteed NUL-free by FileMapper.get_file_name().

(1) The C fast-path uses access(path, F_OK) while the Python fallback uses os.path.exists(). These are subtly non-equivalent: os.path.exists follows symlinks and returns False for broken/dangling symlinks (target missing) — as does access(F_OK). But for paths where a parent component lacks execute/search permission, both return False, so they match there. The genuine divergence is that access() uses the process's real UID/GID while os.path.exists()/stat() uses the effective UID/GID; under setuid/setgid execution or when EUID != RUID the two can disagree on existence-visibility. (2) In such deployments the built-vs-fallback code paths would report different KV lookup results, causing silent cache correctness drift that only manifests in specific privilege contexts. (3) Use faccessat(AT_FDCWD, path, F_OK, AT_EACCESS) or stat()/lstat() semantics to match os.path.exists exactly, or document that vLLM never runs setuid.

(1) The stated motivation is releasing the GIL for the entire access(2) batch. But the Python fallback now eagerly materializes `paths = [self._tier.file_mapper.get_file_name(k) for k in keys]` before dispatching, whereas the previous implementation returned a lazy generator that computed file names on demand. When _HAS_BATCH_LOOKUP_C is False, this converts a lazy generator into an eager list of all paths, increasing peak memory transiently for large batches. (2) Minor; batch sizes here are bounded by per-step lookup counts, so impact is small, but it removes the short-circuit laziness previously available to the zip(keys, hits) consumer. (3) Keep the generator form in the fallback branch: `return (os.path.exists(self._tier.file_mapper.get_file_name(k)) for k in keys)`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark batch_lookup over N=10k keys comparing C extension vs Python fallback wall-clock and peak RSS.

findings.claim: (1) The docstring/@note for batch_lookup claims 'File existence via access(2)' and the manager.py comment says 'GIL released for the entire faccessat() batch', but the implementation uses access(2), not faccessat(2). (2) A stale/misleading comment: reviewers auditing symlink/permission semantics may assume faccessat with AT_* flags is used. (3) Align the comment in manager.py to say access(2), or switch the implementation to faccessat to match the comment (also addressing the RUID/EUID semantics note above).

(1) On failure of PyList_New (result==nullptr) or a partially-filled result, the function returns nullptr, which is correct. However there is no early handling when PyList_Size returns 0 vs the empty-vector path — this is fine functionally. The more notable robustness gap: PyList_SetItem steals the reference to the value from PyBool_FromLong, which is correct, but if PyBool_FromLong ever returned nullptr (it does not for a C long), SetItem would store nullptr — benign here. No action strictly required. (2)/(3) Optional: none — flagged only for completeness; drop if noise.
