(1) Adding Mxfp4MoeBackend.EMULATION to _get_priority_backends_for_gpt_oss() places the emulation backend into the automatic (moe_backend='auto') selection path, and the emulation experts' is_supported_config effectively always returns True (OCP_MXQuantizationEmulationTritonExperts._supports_quant_scheme returns True unconditionally, and it derives from TritonExperts which is broadly device-supported). (2) This changes default behavior: on hardware where no native MXFP4 backend qualifies, auto-selection will now silently fall through to the slow dequantize-on-the-fly emulation path (the class itself warns it 'may be slower than native quantized MOE') instead of raising NotImplementedError. That is precisely the opt-in behavior PR #45896 removed. Silently selecting emulation can produce large throughput regressions for users who previously would have gotten an explicit error and chosen a supported config. (3) Confirm this silent auto-fallback is intended for all gfx targets (not just gfx950). If emulation should remain opt-in on MI300/MI250, gate its inclusion in the priority list on device capability rather than adding it unconditionally, or keep requiring --moe-backend emulation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run gpt-oss MXFP4 MoE decode throughput on a device lacking native MXFP4 support (e.g. MI300/MI250) with moe_backend='auto' before/after this change; compare tokens/s to quantify the emulation fallback regression vs. the prior explicit-error behavior.

findings.claim: (1) The NotImplementedError message in select_mxfp4_moe_backend no longer mentions the emulation opt-in ('To use the emulation backend ... pass --moe-backend emulation'). (2) Since EMULATION was added to the gpt_oss priority list, on CUDA the emulation backend is only reachable in the gpt_oss auto path if its is_supported_config passes; on the generic CUDA path this NotImplementedError is still raised, and users no longer get guidance that --moe-backend emulation exists as a research/debug escape hatch. This makes the error less actionable. (3) If the emulation escape hatch is still valid, keep a shortened hint in the error message pointing to --moe-backend emulation.

(1) The removed restriction previously constrained supported kernel block sizes to [16, 32, 64] for hybrid models with fp32 mamba cache to avoid the NaN-propagation problem in FlashAttention (Dao-AILab/flash-attention#1974); the diff now returns [MultipleOf(16)] unconditionally. (2) If the underlying FA NaN bug is not actually fixed in the pinned flash-attn version, hybrid models with fp32 mamba_ssm_cache_dtype/mamba_cache_dtype could silently produce NaN outputs at block sizes other than 16/32/64 (e.g. 128), a silent correctness regression. (3) Confirm via a hybrid-model correctness test with a non-{16,32,64} block size and fp32 mamba cache that outputs are NaN-free, and ideally reference the FA fix that made this restriction obsolete in the commit/PR. This is low-confidence because the removal is intentional and made by the author of the original NOTE, so the FA issue was very likely resolved.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Correctness (not perf): run a hybrid model (e.g. with fp32 mamba cache) using block_size=128 under FlashAttention and assert output logits contain no NaN/Inf versus the [16,32,64] baseline.

findings.claim: (1) `_config_hash_cache` is a module-level global dict keyed by `id(config)` that is never evicted. (2) Python `id()` values are recycled after an object is garbage-collected, so if a `ModelConfig` (or other SupportsHash) object is freed and a new object is later allocated at the same address, `compute_hash_cached` returns a *stale hash for a different config*, silently mapping `prepare_forward` to the wrong `EplbModelState` (or to none). This is a correctness hazard in addition to being an unbounded (small) memory leak. (3) Use a `WeakValueDictionary`/`weakref`-keyed cache, or store the cached hash directly on the config object (e.g. a cached attribute) rather than an identity dict keyed by `id()`.

(1) `add_model` stores the model_state under `model_config.compute_hash()`, but `prepare_forward` looks it up with `compute_hash_cached(model_config)`. (2) These are only guaranteed to agree if the exact same `model_config` object identity is used at both sites AND `compute_hash()` is deterministic; because the cache is keyed by `id()` and populated lazily by `prepare_forward`, a first-time lookup computes the hash fresh and should match — but if a *different* ModelConfig instance with equal content is passed to prepare_forward (common for draft models where `draft_model_config` may be a distinct object), the identity cache never benefits and, worse, if two distinct config objects hash equal the store key path and lookup path use different code paths (`compute_hash` vs `compute_hash_cached`) which can silently diverge if one is later changed. Recommend using `compute_hash_cached` consistently on both the store (`add_model`) and lookup (`prepare_forward`) sides to guarantee identical keys.

(1) In `prepare_forward`, the ubatch clamping `val = max(0, min(num_unpadded_tokens, ts.stop) - ts.start)` assumes `ts.start`/`ts.stop` are the global (batch-relative) token offsets and that `num_unpadded_tokens` counts real tokens contiguously at the front of the global batch. (2) If padding tokens are interleaved per-ubatch rather than packed at the global tail (e.g. each ubatch has its own trailing padding), this clamp assigns all real-token budget to the earliest ubatches and zero to later ones, undercounting expert load for later ubatches and biasing the EPLB rebalancing. (3) Verify the padding layout: if padding is per-ubatch, the count must be computed from each ubatch's own real-token count, not by clamping a single global count against slice bounds.

(1) `add_model` always allocates `num_unpadded_tokens_tensors` and `prepare_forward` calls `.fill_()` on every forward pass. The router kernel now unconditionally reads `num_unpadded_tokens_ptr` when `HAS_NUM_UNPADDED` is set. (2) The `.fill_(val)` on a scalar int32 tensor with a Python int `val` (computed from `.stop`/`.start`, host-side ints) is fine for CUDA-graph stability, but the added per-forward Python loop over `ubatch_slices` plus one `.fill_()` per ubatch introduces a small host-side overhead on the hot path for every forward even when EPLB recording is disabled for that step (`should_record_tensor==0`). (3) Consider skipping `prepare_forward` work when recording is disabled this step, or confirm the overhead is negligible relative to the forward.

(1) In the kernel, `is_unpadded = offs < num_unpadded_tokens * num_active_experts` computes the product in the kernel's default int type; if `num_unpadded_tokens` (int32) times `num_active_experts` overflows int32 for very large batches (num_unpadded_tokens * top_k > 2^31), the comparison against int64 `offs` becomes wrong and padded tokens could be recorded or real tokens dropped. (2) In practice topk*tokens rarely exceeds 2^31, but the mask depends on it. (3) Cast `num_unpadded_tokens` to int64 before multiplication, matching `offs.to(tl.int64)` used elsewhere in the kernel.

(1) `num_ubatches = max(1, self.parallel_config.num_ubatches)` allocates a fixed-size tensor list at `add_model` time. If DBO ubatch count can change across forward passes (e.g. dynamic dbo enable/disable per batch), `prepare_forward` indexing `tensors[i]` for `i` in `ubatch_slices` and the router indexing `tensors[dbo_current_ubatch_id()]` could go out of range when the runtime ubatch count exceeds the allocated size. (2) This would raise IndexError mid-forward. (3) Assert `len(ubatch_slices) <= len(tensors)` in `prepare_forward`, or size the list to the maximum possible ubatch count (2 for DBO).

In `with_gpu_sync_check.wrapper`, the RuntimeError re-mapping only fires in "error" mode, but the exception matching is fragile. torch raises the message "called a synchronizing CUDA operation" only in error mode; that string is compared with exact `==`. If torch changes the message wording (or appends context), the branch silently falls through and re-raises the raw error, defeating the friendly SYNC_ERROR_MESSAGE guidance. Impact: low functional risk but degraded UX for a debug feature. Fix: match on substring (`"synchronizing CUDA operation" in str(re)`) and/or pin against the torch version, and add a test asserting the exact message torch emits.

`gpu_sync_allowed(first_only=True)` records seen call sites in the module-global `_GPU_SYNC_ALLOWED_FIRST_SEEN` set keyed by (filename, lineno), and this set is never cleared. In multi-worker / repeated-engine-in-process scenarios (e.g. LLM created twice in one process, tests), the first-only suppression from a prior engine instance will incorrectly no-op the sync-allow for a later instance's genuinely-first call, causing spurious sync-check trips. Impact: false positives that could turn into RuntimeErrors in "error" mode. Fix: scope the seen-set per worker/engine instance or reset it in `enable_gpu_sync_check()`.

`_install_compile_time_sync_suppressors` monkey-patches `joint_graph_passes` across all already-imported torch modules by scanning `sys.modules` and replacing any attribute that `is _orig_joint`. This is a global, process-wide mutation of private torch internals that is never reverted. If a later torch code path (or a fresh import after the scan) rebinds/uses the original `joint_graph_passes` under an assumption it is the genuine function, or if two vLLM workers in the same process install wrappers over each other, behavior of the inductor compile pipeline could be subtly altered even when VLLM_GPU_SYNC_CHECK is later effectively inactive. The `_compile_time_suppressors_installed` guard only prevents double-install within one process but the patch persists for the process lifetime. Fix: narrow the scope, document the permanence, and guard against wrapping an already-wrapped function.

The `try:  # noqa: BLE001` in `_install_compile_time_sync_suppressors` has the `noqa` comment placed on the `try` line rather than on the bare `except Exception:` line, and the except uses `# pragma: no cover` instead. The blanket `except Exception: pass` silently swallows all failures of the monkey-patch install, so if torch internals move, the suppressor silently does nothing and post-warmup compiles inside execute_model will trip the sync check with confusing errors, with no log line explaining why. Fix: log at info/debug level on failure (as `trigger_inductor_lazy_init` does), so failures are diagnosable.

`with_gpu_sync_check.wrapper` calls `torch.cuda.get_sync_debug_mode()` and `torch.cuda.set_sync_debug_mode(mode)` on every `execute_model` / `sample_tokens` invocation once the gate is enabled. These are cheap host-side calls, but they only run when VLLM_GPU_SYNC_CHECK is set (a debug/opt-in feature), so there is no throughput impact in the default path (decorator returns `fn` unchanged when mode is None). No action needed; noting to confirm the default path is truly zero-overhead — verified: when `mode is None` the decorator returns the original `fn`, so production is unaffected.

`trigger_inductor_lazy_init` uses `inspect.signature(_inductor_lazy_init).parameters` to decide whether the private torch API takes a device arg, calling with `device` if it accepts any parameters. If a future torch version adds an unrelated keyword-only/optional parameter that is not `input_device`, passing the positional CUDA device could bind to the wrong parameter. Impact: low (best-effort, wrapped in try/except that logs and continues). Fix: check for the specific parameter name (e.g. `"input_device" in signature.parameters`) rather than parameter count/presence.

(1) The new `cpu_utils::get_max_threads()` fallback returns 1 and warns when built without OpenMP, but `omp_get_thread_num()` / `#pragma omp parallel for` sites elsewhere in these kernels are not similarly guarded — they compile to serial loops in that case, which is fine, but only `get_max_threads` is centralized. (2) Risk is low: the whole point of this PR is that macOS now builds WITH OpenMP (`-Xpreprocessor -fopenmp` sets `_OPENMP`), so the fallback branch is a defensive no-op path that is not exercised on the CI target. (3) Consider documenting that all other omp intrinsics used in these kernels (omp_get_thread_num, omp barriers) are only reached inside `#pragma omp` regions and therefore degrade to serial correctly without OpenMP, to make the single-thread guarantee explicit. Purely a documentation nicety.

(1) `cpu_types.hpp` still lacks a trailing newline at EOF (the `\ No newline at end of file` marker remains after the edit). (2) Impact is cosmetic but can trigger `-Wnewline-eof` under some clang configs and noisy diffs. (3) Add a trailing newline. (Style-adjacent; low priority.)

The primary optimization goal is only partially achieved. `string_args_event` scans the string incrementally via `take_json_string` (good, avoids O(n^2) rescanning per chunk), but it only emits a single `StringArgs { decoded }` event once `take_json_string` returns the *complete* length — on incomplete input it propagates `Incomplete` and `parse_buffered_event` returns `Ok(None)`. Meanwhile `decode_json_str(&text[..len])` allocates and deserializes the entire (up to 64KiB+) string in one shot at completion. So while the *scan* is now incremental, the *decode* remains a single O(n) allocation of the whole payload. This is correct and matches prior behavior (string args are still delivered whole, not streamed as deltas like object args), but reviewers/benchmark authors should confirm the benchmark measures the scan cost that was actually reduced. Impact: the win is eliminating repeated re-scans of the growing buffer across chunks, not eliminating the final full decode. Fix: none needed for correctness; just verify the bench name/assertions reflect scan-cost reduction rather than delta streaming.
findings.suggested_benchmark: granite4/long_string_arguments reuse_parser vs a synthetic baseline that rescans from index 1 each chunk, to confirm the O(n^2)->O(n) scan improvement

findings.claim: In `take_json_string`, the resume index selection uses `state.scanned_len <= bytes.len()` and otherwise returns `incomplete()`. The `scanned_len > bytes.len()` branch (returning incomplete) is effectively dead in the granite4 flow because the shared buffer only grows and is never drained until the string completes, so scanned_len can never exceed the current buffer length. It is a defensive guard rather than a reachable path. Not a bug, but if a caller ever reused a `JsonStringScanState` across a *drained/shrunk* buffer, resuming at a stale `scanned_len` that is <= the new (shorter) buffer length could skip legitimate bytes and produce a wrong boundary. Recommend a doc note that the state is only valid for a monotonically growing buffer, or a debug_assert tying scanned_len to the same buffer identity. Impact: latent misuse hazard for future callers.

The `'"'` arm re-fetches the just-inserted scan state via `let Some(Granite4ArgsScan::String(scan)) = args_scan else { unreachable!(...) }`. This works but is an awkward two-step (assign then re-match with an unreachable). It could be simplified by binding the state before storing, e.g. construct the `JsonStringScanState`, run `string_args_event` against `&mut scan` handling, then store — or use a mutable reference obtained from `args_scan.insert(...)`. `Option::insert` returns `&mut T` and would avoid the unreachable entirely. Impact: minor readability/robustness; the unreachable is a code smell that a future refactor could turn into a panic path.

(1) The `from aiter.ops.flydsl.moe_common import GateMode` import was moved out of the `try/except ImportError` block and is now executed unconditionally on every `rocm_aiter_fused_experts` call that reaches the `else` (non-tkw1) branch — including plain SILU/GELU and fp8/unquantized paths that never set a `gate_mode`. (2) Previously the import was guarded so that older AITER installs (that lack `aiter.ops.flydsl.moe_common`) would silently fall through with `gate_mode=""`. With this change, any AITER version without that module now raises `ImportError` at runtime for ALL AITER fused-MoE paths, not just MXFP4. This is a backward-compatibility regression for older/non-flydsl AITER builds. Note `_aiter_ops.py` already guards actually *passing* gate_mode via `fused_moe_supports_gate_mode()`, so the module-level import is the only new hard dependency. (3) Fix: keep the import lazy/guarded (wrap in try/except and only import when `use_mxfp4_w4a16` or `activation_interleave is not None`), or gate the import behind `rocm_aiter_ops.fused_moe_supports_gate_mode()`, preserving the previous silent-fallthrough behavior.

(1) The new `intermediate_pad` override path bypasses the AITER v0.1.13.post1 padding-rounding logic. When `moe_config.intermediate_pad` is set (e.g. MiniMax-M3 passes `intermediate_pad=0`), the computed value is fed directly to `rocm_aiter_ops.fused_moe(intermediate_pad=...)` and is NOT subjected to the `// 64 * 64 * (2 if tp_size==1 else 1)` rounding that the auto-computed branch still gets. (2) This is likely intentional for MiniMax-M3 (0 needs no rounding, and the whole point is a model-specific override), but the rounding comment/pin note explicitly warns that CK/FlyDSL MoE dispatch requires the pad to be a multiple of 64. If any future model passes a non-zero override that is not already 64-aligned (and doubled when tp_size==1), the kernel dispatch will silently mismatch AITER's expected padding, producing wrong results. (3) Fix: either apply the same rounding to the override value, or add an assertion documenting that a supplied `intermediate_pad` must already satisfy AITER's alignment (multiple of 64, doubled at tp_size==1).

(1) The new field `intermediate_pad` on `FusedMoEConfig` is ambiguous relative to the existing `intermediate_size_per_partition_unpadded` / `intermediate_size_per_partition` naming — `intermediate_pad` reads as a size, but is actually a manual override of the padding *delta* fed to the AITER kernel. (2) A future reader could confuse it with an intermediate size or assume it is auto-derived like the other fields, risking incorrect use. (3) Fix: rename to something like `intermediate_pad_override` and expand the `# Model specific override` comment to state units (padding elements) and that it bypasses the auto-computed rounding.

(1) The change replaces `self.a1_scale or self.a1_gscale` with `self.a1_scale`. This is a legitimate and important fix: `self.a1_scale` (via `FusedMoEQuantConfig.a1_scale`) returns a `torch.Tensor` when set, and evaluating `tensor or ...` invokes `bool(tensor)`, which (a) triggers a device-to-host `.item()` sync on a single-element tensor and (b) raises `RuntimeError: Boolean value of Tensor with more than one element is ambiguous` for multi-element scales. The sync is exactly what breaks CUDA graph capture (a host-device sync inside a captured region), matching the PR title. (2) Impact: correctly removes the graph-capture-breaking sync / crash. (3) No fix needed — this is the right change; noted here as verification that the diff addresses a real perf/correctness hazard rather than introducing one.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Capture a CUDA graph over TritonExperts.apply() with a static (non-None) a1_scale tensor and confirm no host-device sync / capture failure occurs, comparing against the parent commit.

findings.claim: (1) The new `a1_scale` property on `Nvfp4QuantizationEmulationTritonExperts` returns `self.a1_gscale` to preserve the old `a1_scale or a1_gscale` fallback for NVFP4 (where `quant_config.a1_scale` is None and the value lives in `a1_gscale`). However, `Nvfp4QuantizationEmulationTritonExperts` fully overrides `apply()` (nvfp4_emulation_moe.py) and its own body reads `self.quant_config.a1_gscale` directly, never `self.a1_scale`; and the base `TritonExperts.apply()` (which is the only reader of `self.a1_scale` at lines 248/343) is not invoked for this subclass. Verify that any code path actually reads `Nvfp4QuantizationEmulationTritonExperts.a1_scale` at runtime. If nothing reads it, this property is dead code (unused new identifier per the Mandatory Check). (2) Impact: dead-but-harmless override that adds confusion, or — if the base apply IS somehow reached — an intended behavior-preserving shim. (3) Fix: either confirm/document the reader (e.g. add a comment naming the concrete call site) or drop the property if unused.

(1) The `input_scale` fallback at line 343 still reads `self.a1_scale` (`... else self.a1_scale`). For a plain `TritonExperts` whose `quant_config.a1_scale` is a tensor, this is fine (no `or`), but note that after this fix line 248 no longer falls back to `a1_gscale`, whereas line 343 also does not. Confirm that for any config previously relying on the `a1_scale or a1_gscale` fallback at 248 (i.e. a1_scale=None but a1_gscale set) but running through base `TritonExperts` (not the nvfp4 subclass), quantization still receives the intended global scale. If such a config exists and uses base apply, the fix would now pass `None` where `a1_gscale` was previously used. (2) Impact: potential silent quantization scale regression for that narrow config. (3) Fix: verify no base-class TritonExperts config sets a1_gscale-without-a1_scale, or extend the property pattern accordingly.

get_default_config for fp8 block-wise quant now sets BLOCK_SIZE_N=64 when M<=8 and block_shape[0]%64==0, but the kernel's block-scale N indexing (offs_bsn = offs_bn // group_n) requires the N tile to align to the quant-block grid. With group_n=128 and BLOCK_SIZE_N=64, a single N tile (offs 0..63 vs 64..127) maps entirely to one scale index, so this is correct for the common 128 case. However, the guard only checks `block_shape[0] % 64 == 0` and hardcodes block_n=64; if block_shape[0] were e.g. 96 (which %64!=0 so falls through) it is fine, but there is no assertion that block_shape[0] % 64 == 0 also implies 64 divides the block cleanly for scale indexing. The condition `block_shape[0] % 64 == 0` was likely intended as `64 % block_shape[0] == 0 or block_shape[0] % 64 == 0` to match the benchmark's new two-way divisibility rule. Recommend making the alignment check in get_default_config consistent with the two-way divisibility rule added to benchmark_moe.py (bn%block_n==0 or block_n%bn==0) to avoid a mismatch between what the benchmark tunes and what the runtime default selects.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run block-FP8 fused MoE correctness test (compare against unfused reference) for block_shape=[128,128] at M in {1,2,4,8} with the new default config to confirm N=64 tiling matches scale indexing.

findings.claim: benchmark_moe.py now accepts N tiles narrower than block_n (e.g. 32) via the two-way divisibility rule (bn%block_n==0 or block_n%bn==0). For block_n=128 this now admits BLOCK_SIZE_N in {32,64,128}. The kernel scale indexing offs_bn // group_n is correct for any such tile since all elements of a sub-block map to the same scale, so this is algorithmically sound. Worth adding a shape/divisibility assertion or comment near the kernel's offs_bsn computation documenting that BLOCK_SIZE_N need not equal group_n but must divide (or be a multiple of) it, so future tuning does not introduce a tile like N=96 with block_n=128 that would straddle two scale blocks within one tile.

The committed JSON configs for several shapes (e.g. E=256,N=256 M=1/2 now BLOCK_SIZE_N=32) rely on the benchmark's relaxed divisibility to have been tuned. These are data-only tuning artifacts; correctness depends on the kernel handling N tiles smaller than group_n, which it does. No functional risk, but recommend a regression benchmark to confirm the claimed ~2x thread-block exposure actually improves decode latency at M<=8 on H100/H200 rather than regressing due to increased kernel-launch/grid overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-latency microbenchmark (M in {1,2,4,8}) for block-FP8 MoE on H200/H100 comparing pre-change BLOCK_SIZE_N=128 configs vs new BLOCK_SIZE_N=64/32 configs.

findings.claim: (1) The 2D-weight (batched/grouped) path in rms_norm does not verify that weight->scalar_type() == input.scalar_type(); the kernel reinterprets the weight pointer as scalar_t* (input's dtype). (2) If a caller passes a batched weight in a different dtype than the input, the memory is silently reinterpreted and produces wrong results (or misaligned reads). Note this is a pre-existing gap for the 1D path too, but the new 2D grouped path widens the surface. (3) Add STD_TORCH_CHECK(!weight.has_value() || weight->scalar_type() == input.scalar_type()) as fused_add_rms_norm already does. In the current DFlash call site k_norm weights share the input dtype so this is not a live bug, hence suggestion.

(1) The GPU-memory semaphore acquires only raw_frame_bytes = len(frame_idx) * height * width * 3 (uint8 RGB decoded output), but the actual peak VRAM footprint of decode_frames_pynvvideocodec is substantially larger: device_frames = torch.stack(torch_frames) allocates the decoded batch once, then device_frames.permute(0,3,1,2).contiguous() allocates a SECOND full-size copy before the first is freed (≈2x raw_frame_bytes peak), plus the persistent decoder surface pool (PYNVVIDEOCODEC_DECODER_GPU_MEMORY_BYTES = 128 MiB) and per-context driver allocation (PYNVVIDEOCODEC_CUDA_CONTEXT_BYTES ≈ 1.8 GiB). (2) The whole purpose of mm_ipc_gpu_memory_gb is to keep frontend GPU usage inside a budget carved out of the KV cache; systematically under-counting by >2x plus a fixed ~1.9 GiB overhead means the pool can admit work that overshoots the sequestered headroom and collides with engine KV-cache memory, causing engine-side OOM that gating was meant to prevent. (3) Charge the peak, not the final buffer: account for the transient contiguous copy (factor ~2 for the permute+contiguous), and either add the fixed decoder/context overhead constants to the acquired byte count or reserve them once at pool init.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode a large (e.g. 4K, many-frame) video via the pynvvideocodec backend with mm_ipc_gpu_memory_gb set tight, and measure torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated vs the acquired raw_frame_bytes to quantify the under-count.

findings.claim: (1) The defined module constants PYNVVIDEOCODEC_DECODER_GPU_MEMORY_BYTES (128 MiB) and PYNVVIDEOCODEC_CUDA_CONTEXT_BYTES (≈1.8 GiB) have no read/use path anywhere in the diff or codebase (grep at parent finds no other references, and they are newly added here). (2) These are exactly the fixed overheads the semaphore budget should account for; leaving them unused is either dead code or a sign the accounting is incomplete — a wrong/unused sizing constant is misleading to future maintainers sizing --mm-ipc-gpu-memory-gb. (3) Either wire them into the pool sizing / per-acquire accounting, or remove them; if intentionally deferred, add an explicit TODO + migration note.

(1) _torch_stream_context calls torch.accelerator.set_device_index(stream.device.index) on entry but the finally block only restores the previous stream (set_stream), not the previous device index. (2) In the frontend thread-pool executor, threads are reused across requests and across other torch operations; leaving the accelerator device index changed can silently redirect subsequent unrelated GPU work on that thread to the wrong device in multi-GPU setups. (3) Capture and restore the previous device index in the finally block alongside the stream restore.

(1) decode_frames_pynvvideocodec borrows a decoder slot twice sequentially — once in _read_source_metadata and again in _decode_to_pinned_host — for the same temp file path. With PYNVVIDEOCODEC_MAX_RETAINED_DECODERS = 1 the second borrow reuses the same slot, but because the metadata read and the frame decode are separate borrow/return cycles, an interleaving concurrent request can grab the slot in between and reconfigure the decoder onto a different source, forcing a reconfigure_decoder (or full rebuild) back to this file for the decode step. (2) reconfigure/rebuild is described as the dominant per-request cost, so under concurrency this defeats the decoder-reuse optimization. (3) Consider holding one borrowed slot across both metadata read and frame decode within a single request, or pass the metadata decoder handle through to the decode step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run concurrent pynvvideocodec decodes of distinct videos and count reconfigure_decoder / _construct calls per request to measure slot thrash under contention.

findings.claim: (1) maybe_init_mm_gpu_ipc_pool computes total_bytes = int(mm_ipc_gpu_memory_gb * GiB_bytes) // api_process_count; for small budgets and larger api_process_count this can floor to a very small (or, for sub-1-byte-per-process configs, zero) per-process budget, and MultiModalGPUMemoryPool.__init__ raises ValueError on total_bytes <= 0. (2) A user setting a nonzero but small --mm-ipc-gpu-memory-gb with several API-server processes would get a hard startup crash rather than graceful disable/clamp. (3) Guard for the floor-to-zero case (clamp to a minimum or raise a clearer configuration error naming both operands).

(1) The `prefill_scheduled` flag is set from the running-requests loop only (`prefill_scheduled |= request.is_prefill_chunk`), but the running loop iterates over `self.running` which is generally ordered decodes-then-prefills or in arbitrary order; more importantly it does NOT account for prefill chunks that come from NEW/resumed requests scheduled in the same step (the waiting loop below). The padding decision `(scheduled_running_reqs and not prefill_scheduled)` therefore may pad a new decode into a `1 + num_spec_tokens` shape even though the same step's batch will subsequently gain a prefill chunk from a newly-scheduled waiting request — producing exactly the mixed-length batch the PR is trying to avoid. Confirm whether new/resumed prefills are scheduled before or after this waiting-loop padding decision. (2) Impact: the CUDA graph uniformity guarantee is not actually held in mixed new-prefill + first-decode steps, so padding is wasted compute (extra num_spec_tokens dummy positions run through the model) with no benefit, or worse triggers a non-uniform batch anyway. (3) Fix: derive the mixed-batch condition from all prefills scheduled in the step (including new/resumed requests) or move the padding decision to after the full batch composition is known.

(1) For padded first-decode requests the code sets `scheduled_spec_decode_tokens[request_id] = [-1] * self.num_spec_tokens`, i.e. placeholder token id -1, and schedules `num_new_tokens = 1 + self.num_spec_tokens`. Downstream consumers (gpu_model_runner around lines 2161-2182 and 4253, and update_from_output around line 1926/1548) treat `scheduled_spec_decode_tokens` as real draft tokens to verify. A placeholder value of -1 must be explicitly filtered/ignored during acceptance sampling and when computing accepted-token counts / advancing num_computed_tokens; otherwise the worker may attempt to score/verify token id -1 (out-of-vocab indexing) or miscount accepted tokens. (2) Impact: potential out-of-bounds embedding lookup or silently wrong num_computed_tokens advancement for the padded request, corrupting its decode state. (3) Fix: verify the worker's spec-decode verification path masks entries equal to -1 (or padded requests), and that update_from_output does not count placeholder drafts as generated/accepted tokens. Add an assertion or explicit handling if the -1 sentinel is relied upon implicitly.

(1) The padding branch does `num_new_tokens = 1 + self.num_spec_tokens` and, on budget/length overflow, `break`s out of the waiting-request loop entirely ('Prefer to not schedule than schedule un-padded here'). Breaking rather than `continue`/skip means all remaining waiting requests in this loop are also abandoned for this step, not just the one that couldn't be padded. If the loop iterates a priority queue, a single first-decode request that can't be padded stalls scheduling of every lower-priority request behind it for the step. (2) Impact: throughput/latency regression and possible head-of-line blocking when token_budget is nearly exhausted or a request is near max_model_len. (3) Fix: confirm the enclosing control flow — if this is per-request, use skip/continue semantics or fall back to scheduling the request unpadded (num_new_tokens=1) instead of breaking the whole loop.

(1) When padding is applied, the request is charged `1 + num_spec_tokens` against `token_budget` and consumes num_spec_tokens extra KV/compute slots for placeholder tokens even though only 1 real token is decoded. In steady state with many first-decode requests (e.g. large prefix-cache-hit workloads) this inflates per-step token consumption by a factor tied to num_spec_tokens. (2) Impact: reduced effective batch size / throughput under bursty prefix-cache-hit decode arrivals. (3) Fix: this is likely an intentional tradeoff for cudagraph uniformity, but document the expected overhead and confirm it only triggers when a uniform spec-decode batch already dominates the step.

(1) The padding gate `(self.num_spec_tokens > 0 and self.dynamic_sd_lookup is None) and num_new_tokens == 1 and (scheduled_running_reqs and not prefill_scheduled)` bundles the dynamic-spec-decode exclusion (`dynamic_sd_lookup is None`) inline without explanation. Since dynamic SD assigns per-batch-size spec token counts (see lines 1053-1054), the uniform-shape assumption behind fixed 1+num_spec padding does not hold there. (2) Impact: correctness rationale is non-obvious to future maintainers; risk of the condition being 'simplified' incorrectly later. (3) Fix: add a comment explaining that padding is skipped under dynamic spec decode because the per-step spec token count is variable.

(1) The scalar fallback path launches with `dim3 grid(num_tokens)` (one block per token), whereas the new vectorized path correctly clamps the grid to 65535 and uses a grid-stride loop. (2) If num_tokens exceeds the CUDA max grid dimension in x (65535), the scalar kernel launch will fail with an invalid-configuration error; this is exactly the class of large-batch input (e.g. long-context or large prefill token counts) that the non-contiguous/unaligned fallback might legitimately receive. (3) Give `moe_sum_scalar_kernel` a grid-stride loop over tokens and clamp its grid the same way the vectorized kernels do, e.g. `dim3 grid(min((num_tokens+...)/..., 65535))`. Note: this grid-dim limitation pre-existed in the original `moe_sum_kernel`, but the PR fixes it only for the vectorized path, leaving the scalar path exposed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run moe_sum with a non-contiguous (transpose) input where num_tokens > 65535 and assert the launch succeeds and matches torch reference.

findings.claim: (1) `moe_sum` validates that the output is contiguous but never validates the input rank/that `input.stride(2)` exists. The kernel reads `input.size(1)` as topk and `input.stride(2)` as the hidden stride, assuming a 3-D [num_tokens, topk, hidden] input. (2) If a caller passes a 2-D or otherwise mis-ranked tensor, `stride(2)` is undefined behavior / out-of-range and the results are silently wrong rather than raising a clear error. (3) Add a `STD_TORCH_CHECK(input.dim() == 3, ...)` guard (and optionally `input.size(-1) == output.size(-1)`) at the top of `moe_sum` to make the contract explicit at the parallelism boundary.

(1) The recv-thread pool now shares a single `recv_request_queue` across N `KVCacheStoreRecvingThread` instances, so a given request's `ReqMeta` will be dequeued and processed by exactly one thread, but the per-request completion state is stored per-thread (each thread's own `finished_requests` set, filled via `set_finished_request`). `get_and_clear_finished_requests()` is then correctly OR-ed across all threads in `_get_finished`, and `get_block_ids_with_load_errors` is OR-ed across threads too — so far consistent. HOWEVER, ordering guarantees within a single request are lost: previously all chunks/batches for a given req_id were serialized through one thread's FIFO queue; now if `start_load_kv` enqueues multiple `ReqMeta` for the same req_id (e.g. multiple load chunks), different pool threads can process them concurrently and out of order. If the load path assumes in-order per-request processing (offset/high-water-mark tracking similar to the Sending thread's `_saved_offset`), concurrent out-of-order processing of the same req_id across threads can corrupt state or double-mark completion. (2) Risk: silent KV-load corruption / premature 'finished' signaling under multi-chunk loads. (3) Verify RecvingThread._handle_request is fully stateless per-request across chunks, or shard the queue by req_id (hash req_id to a fixed thread) so all work for one request stays on one thread.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-chunk load of a single request under VLLM_MOONCAKE_LOAD_RECV_THREADS>1; assert completion set and loaded blocks match single-thread baseline.

findings.claim: (1) `kv_recv_thread` (singular) was renamed to `kv_recv_threads` (list). This is an attribute-level API change on `MooncakeStoreWorker`. (2) If any external code, subclass, or monkeypatch (outside the diffed test) references `self.kv_recv_thread`, it now breaks with AttributeError. (3) grep the codebase for other `kv_recv_thread` references to confirm none remain; the test file was updated but library-internal or downstream consumers may not be.

(1) All N recv threads are started and then all N ready_events are waited on sequentially — correct. But `_get_finished` now iterates all N threads and takes `done_task_lock` per thread on every scheduler poll; and `get_block_ids_with_load_errors` acquires N locks per call. (2) With small N (default 1) this is negligible, but the per-poll cost scales linearly with thread count on a hot path. (3) Acceptable given the default of 1; consider only iterating threads when load_async and documenting expected N range.

(1) `VLLM_MOONCAKE_LOAD_RECV_THREADS` is read via `int(os.getenv(...))` with no lower-bound validation in envs.py; the worker clamps with `max(1, ...)`, so 0 or negative is handled, but a non-integer or absurdly large value (e.g. thousands) would either raise at parse time or spawn an unbounded number of daemon threads. (2) Misconfiguration risk: excessive thread creation degrades rather than improves throughput. (3) Consider clamping to a sane upper bound (e.g. min(N, some cap)) or documenting the recommended range.

(1) The PR changes QuickReduceRegime.NONE from value 4 to value 5 (and adds INT3=4) in vllm/distributed/device_communicators/quick_all_reduce.py, but the existing test tests/distributed/test_rocm_quick_reduce.py still asserts `QuickReduceRegime.NONE.value == 4` (line 514) and `set(QuickReduceRegime.__members__) == {"FP","INT8","INT6","INT4","NONE"}` (line 520). Both assertions will now fail. (2) This is a broken test left unupdated by the change — CI on ROCm will fail. (3) Update these assertions to `NONE.value == 5` and include `INT3` in the members set.

(1) The PR extends every list in `_QR_MIN_SIZE` from 4 entries to 5 entries (adding the INT3 threshold), but test_rocm_quick_reduce.py:696 asserts `len(min_sizes) == 4`. (2) This assertion will now fail since the lists now have 5 elements. (3) Update the expected length to 5 to match the new codec count.

(1) Renumbering `NONE` from 4 to 5 changes the integer value of an enum member that other code/tests may depend on. (2) While the current dispatch path guards NONE via string comparison before enum indexing, any external code persisting or comparing the raw enum int (e.g. cached configs, logs, third-party scripts) silently changes meaning. (3) Consider assigning INT3 a value that does not disturb existing ids (or document the id renumbering prominently and audit all `.value` consumers); at minimum ensure the C++ enum and all tests are kept in lockstep.

(1) In CodecQ3::send, the q2 payload is written with `__builtin_nontemporal_store(q2w, q2w_ptr)` but the q1 payload uses a plain store `*q1w_ptr = q1w` (non-nontemporal), unlike the consistent nontemporal stores in CodecQ6 for its multiple payload regions. (2) The mismatch is not a correctness bug but may cause the q1 byte to go through the normal cache path while q2 bypasses it, subtly reducing the intended streaming-store benefit and creating an inconsistency reviewers/maintainers must reason about. (3) Use `__builtin_nontemporal_store` for the q1 payload as well for consistency with the other codecs, or add a comment explaining why it differs.

(1) TWOSHOT_DISPATCH_TP2_ONLY throws std::runtime_error inside a host dispatch macro when world_size != 2. The Python layer already guards INT3 to world_size==2 (quick_all_reduce.py), so this branch should be unreachable, but if it were ever hit the exception is raised on the host launch path and may not be caught cleanly at the pybind boundary. (2) Defense-in-depth is fine, but a runtime string-throw for a condition that is a programming error is heavier than needed and duplicates the Python guard. (3) Consider a static_assert-style guard at instantiation or an early validation returning an error code consistent with the rest of the C++ API; at minimum verify the pybind layer surfaces this exception as a clean Python error.

(1) The new RVV dispatch block is inserted as `#elif defined(CPU_CAPABILITY_RVV) if constexpr (...) {...; return;} else` inside `_dequant_gemm_accum`, but the RVV path is only taken when `!sym_quant_act && N==BLOCK_N && ldb==BLOCK_N/2`. When `sym_quant_act` is true (or shapes differ) the code falls through the `else` into the scalar loop `{ for(m)... }`. That scalar loop uses `load_uint4_vnni<N,ldb>(B,k,n)` and `qzeros_b[n]` etc., which is correct, so the fallthrough is fine functionally. HOWEVER the `if constexpr` here reads runtime template params N/ldb that are compile-time constants for BLOCK_N=32/16, so for the W4A8 CPU entry (BLOCK_N=32) the `if constexpr` is always true when sym_quant_act is false — meaning the scalar reference path becomes dead for asymmetric activation on RVV. This is intended, but note there is no numerical fallback/validation path retained for RVV asymmetric; any bug in the RVV kernel silently produces wrong GEMM results with no scalar cross-check. (2) Risk: silent wrong results in quantized inference on RISC-V. (3) Recommend adding a unit test comparing RVV output against the scalar `_dequant_gemm_accum` reference for representative M/K, and gating the RVV path behind that validation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Numerical equivalence test: run int4_scaled_mm_cpu on RVV vs scalar reference for random weights/activations, assert max abs err within fp32 tolerance.

findings.claim: (1) `supports_amx = torch.cpu._is_amx_tile_supported()` is now called unconditionally on every `process_weights_after_loading` for all CPU architectures, and `use_w4a8` is enabled when `supports_amx or supports_riscv`. On a RISC-V CPU with `VLLM_CPU_INT4_W4A8=1` this now routes to `_process_gptq_weights_w4a8` and `ops.int4_scaled_mm_cpu`, but the C++ RVV kernel is only compiled/valid when built with RVV (`__riscv_v_min_vlen == 128 or 256`). If vLLM was built as a scalar RISC-V build (the newly-added `-DVLLM_RVV_VLEN=0` scalar option in cmake), `current_platform.get_cpu_architecture()==RISCV` is still true, `use_w4a8` becomes true, but `CPU_CAPABILITY_RVV` is NOT defined so `_dequant_gemm_accum` falls into the scalar loop — which is functionally correct but the dispatch premise (RVV present) is false. Worse, on a scalar build the whole w4a8 code path may not be exercised/tested. (2) Risk: enabling w4a8 on RISC-V scalar builds routes through an untested path / potential runtime failure. (3) Gate `supports_riscv` on actually having RVV (e.g. check VLEN>0 at runtime) rather than architecture==RISCV alone.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Build with -DVLLM_RVV_VLEN=0 and run W4A8 linear with VLLM_CPU_INT4_W4A8=1; confirm correctness or explicit fallback.

findings.claim: (1) `load_uint4_as_int8_rvv` uses `RVVI(__riscv_vsrl_vx_u8, LMUL_64)(packed, 4, ...)` to extract the high nibble then reinterprets the result as int8 via `vreinterpret_v_u8...i8`. The nibble value is 0..15 (unsigned), reinterpreted as int8 gives 0..15 (top bit never set for 0xf mask), so no sign issue — correct. But note `gemm_accum_uint4_rvv` subtracts `qzeros` (int8) from `b` producing values in range roughly -15..15 stored in int8, then `gemm_accum_uint8_int8_rvv` sign-extends to int16 and multiplies by activation `a` (uint8, 0..255) cast to int16. This exactly mirrors the scalar reference `A[m*lda+k] * (nibble - qzeros_b[n])`. Verified equivalent. This is NOT a bug — dropping. (2)/(3) n/a.

(1) The PR adds `16: 64` to `FI_ALLREDUCE_FUSION_MAX_SIZE_MB` for capabilities 100 and 103, and enables world_size=16 in `FI_SUPPORTED_WORLD_SIZES`, but does NOT add a `16` entry to `_FI_ALLREDUCE_ONE_SHOT_MAX_SIZES_MB`. (2) In `call_trtllm_fused_allreduce_norm`, `max_one_shot_size = _FI_ALLREDUCE_ONE_SHOT_MAX_SIZES_MB.get(cap, {}).get(world_size, None)` will return `None` for world_size=16, which makes `use_oneshot = (max_one_shot_size is None or ...)` unconditionally `True`. This means the one-shot Lamport all-reduce path is always selected for ws=16 even for tensors up to the full 64MB fusion size. The table's own comment states 'OneShot max size is at most 64MB / world_size (FlashInfer restriction)', i.e. one-shot at ws=16 should be capped at ~4MB. Forcing one-shot for large tensors at ws=16 risks exceeding the FlashInfer one-shot restriction (correctness/hang) or at minimum picking a suboptimal path for a multi-node (mnnvl) config the PR is explicitly tuning. (3) Add `16` entries to `_FI_ALLREDUCE_ONE_SHOT_MAX_SIZES_MB` for capabilities 100 and 103 (e.g. 4MB per the 64MB/ws rule, or the empirically-tuned value), matching the new fusion-size entries.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run allreduce+rms fusion at TP=16 (mnnvl, 2 nodes x 8 GPU, SM10.3) sweeping tensor sizes from 1MB to 64MB; verify one-shot vs two-shot selection and check for NaN/hang at large sizes where one-shot is currently forced.

findings.claim: (1) The docstring for `fi_allreduce_fusion_max_size_mb` embeds a hardcoded copy of the default table that only lists capabilities 90 and 100 with world sizes {2,4,8} and does not reflect capability 103 or the newly added world_size=16. (2) A stale docstring documenting behavior that no longer matches the actual `FI_ALLREDUCE_FUSION_MAX_SIZE_MB` (which now includes 103 and ws=16) can mislead users tuning this threshold. (3) Update the embedded table in the docstring to match, or replace it with a reference to `FI_ALLREDUCE_FUSION_MAX_SIZE_MB` to avoid future drift.

(1) The commit title says 'SM 10.3 (GB300)' but the diff adds `16: 64` to both capability 100 (SM 10.0) and 103 (SM 10.3) tables. (2) If world_size=16 was only validated on GB300 (SM 10.3), enabling the identical 64MB threshold for SM 10.0 without validation could silently select the FI fused path on unvalidated hardware and produce suboptimal or incorrect behavior. (3) Confirm ws=16 tuning was validated on SM 10.0 as well; if only GB300 was tested, restrict the addition to the 103 table (or add a comment noting the 100 entry is untested).

(1) The kernel now calls cudaGridDependencySynchronize()/cudaTriggerProgrammaticLaunchCompletion() (PDL grid-dependency intrinsics), but only the first kernel's launch path (LAUNCH_KERNEL_INST via cudaLaunchKernelEx with cudaLaunchAttributeProgrammaticStreamSerialization=1) sets the required launch attribute. cudaGridDependencySynchronize() inside a kernel that was NOT launched with the programmatic-stream-serialization attribute is undefined/no-op behavior; more importantly, the pre-existing packed_register_kernel (which was already using the equivalent griddepcontrol.wait/launch_dependents PTX, converted here to the CUDA intrinsics) is still launched via the ordinary triple-chevron <<<>>> syntax (lines ~525-555), which does NOT set the PDL attribute. (2) If that kernel's launcher was never PDL-enabled, then griddepcontrol.wait would hang waiting for a dependency signal that never arrives, or be undefined — but since the PTX form pre-existed and presumably worked, the risk is that the newly-added intrinsics in the *first* kernel are only correct when launched via the new Ex path. Verify that both PDL-annotated kernels are launched exclusively through a config that sets programmaticStreamSerializationAllowed=1. (3) Confirm the packed_register_kernel launch also uses cudaLaunchKernelEx with the PDL attribute, or the grid-dependency intrinsics inside it are inert/incorrect.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run per_token_group_quant_8bit and _packed on Hopper (sm_90)/Blackwell verifying numerical correctness plus a two-kernel back-to-back PDL overlap microbenchmark to confirm the wait/trigger pair is actually honored.

findings.claim: (1) cudaGridDependencySynchronize() is inserted near the top of per_token_group_quant_8bit_kernel BEFORE the __syncthreads() and the QuantizeGroup call, and cudaTriggerProgrammaticLaunchCompletion() is placed at the very end after QuantizeGroup. With PDL, cudaGridDependencySynchronize() blocks until the prior grid's global-memory writes are visible; it must be placed AFTER any use of the block/thread index setup but BEFORE the first global-memory read of `input`. In this kernel the input read happens inside ComputeGroupScale (called after the sync), so ordering appears correct. However, cudaTriggerProgrammaticLaunchCompletion() is only reached on the normal exit path — verify there is no early return before it that would leave the dependent grid never launched (there is none here, so this kernel is fine), but the same pattern in packed_register_kernel has early returns each guarded by their own trigger, which is the correct pattern. Reviewer should confirm the trigger is guaranteed to execute on all block-exit paths for the first kernel too.
findings.suggested_benchmark: PDL overlap microbenchmark comparing end-to-end latency of consecutive quant launches with/without PDL to confirm the trigger fires and dependent launch overlaps.

findings.claim: (1) The refactor introduces a nested LAUNCH_KERNEL_INST macro with two definitions (CUDA vs ROCm) selected by #ifdef USE_ROCM, but the ROCm path still forwards scale_num_rows/scale_stride unconditionally, whereas the original triple-chevron code omitted those trailing args for the non-column-major (row-major) instantiations. The kernel declares scale_num_rows/scale_stride with default values of 0, so passing them explicitly is behaviorally identical, but it is worth confirming the row-major path is unaffected. (2) Passing the extra args always is harmless given the defaults but slightly obscures that they are only meaningful for the column-major path. (3) A brief comment noting scale_num_rows/scale_stride are ignored for row-major would prevent confusion.

(1) PDL via cudaLaunchAttributeProgrammaticStreamSerialization and the cudaGridDependencySynchronize/cudaTriggerProgrammaticLaunchCompletion intrinsics require a sufficiently new CUDA toolkit (>=11.8) and are only meaningful on sm_90+. The #if guards the device intrinsics on __CUDA_ARCH__>=900 and the host launch on !USE_ROCM, but there is no toolkit-version guard on cudaLaunchKernelEx / cudaLaunchAttributeProgrammaticStreamSerialization on the host side. (2) On an older CUDA toolkit that still targets non-Hopper archs, the build could fail to compile the host launch path. (3) Consider guarding the Ex-based launcher on CUDART_VERSION as well, or confirm the project's minimum CUDA version already provides these symbols.

(1) The diff adds `has_initial_state` support to the compute (tinygemm) path of `causal_conv1d_fwd_varlen_kernel_impl` — the LAUNCH macro now passes `conv_states`/`has_initial_states_value`, and the final GEMM call at line ~377 now passes `has_initial_state[bs]`. However, the `update_conv_state(...)` call in the state-writeback loop (around line 371-376) still passes hardcoded `/* has_initial_state */ false`. (2) When a prefill chunk is CONTINUED across scheduler steps (the exact chunked-prefill scenario this PR enables), and the current chunk's `seqlen < width-1`, `update_conv_state` must copy the tail of the PRE-EXISTING conv_states into the new state; with `has_initial_states=false` it instead zero-pads that region (see `copy_stub` with x==nullptr), corrupting the carried conv state for short trailing chunks. This produces silently wrong outputs after the split boundary. (3) Change the writeback call to pass `has_initial_state[bs]` (mirroring the non-varlen `causal_conv1d_fwd_kernel_impl` at line 286 which correctly forwards `has_initial_state[bs]`).
findings.suggested_benchmark: test_causal_conv1d_fwd_cpu_two_call_split with a trailing split smaller than width-1 (e.g. total_tokens close to a boundary so tail < 3 tokens) to force the seqlen<width-1 branch in update_conv_state

findings.claim: (1) `_zero_block_ids` now iterates every KV cache group and, for each full-attention block id, calls `kv[block_id].zero_()` as a separate Python-level op per block. (2) On CPU this is a Python loop launching one zeroing op per (layer, block) pair; when many blocks are freed at once (long prefixes / large evictions) this is O(num_layers * num_blocks) tiny ops and can add measurable latency in the scheduler/runner critical path, plus zeroing whole blocks is more work than strictly needed. (3) Consider batching the zero across block ids (e.g. index the tensor with a block-id tensor once: `kv[torch.as_tensor(block_ids)] = 0`) to collapse the per-block Python loop into one vectorized op.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark _zero_block_ids with a hybrid model (many full-attention layers) freeing a few hundred blocks; compare per-block loop vs single fancy-indexed assignment

findings.claim: (1) `_postprocess_triton` early-returns (skips all C++ monkey-patches) when `HAS_TRITON` is true, but the new `batch_memcpy_kernel` monkey-patch was placed in `_postprocess_triton` UNCONDITIONALLY at the top (before the HAS_TRITON guard's return, in __init__ ordering). Verify: the patch `vllm.v1.worker.mamba_utils.batch_memcpy_kernel = cpu_tl.batch_memcpy_kernel` is added right after the sample_recovered_tokens patch which is INSIDE the block that runs only when triton is absent — but if triton-cpu IS present the real triton kernel should be used. The added lines appear to sit inside the non-triton fallback section, which is correct, but confirm the ctypes fallback is not installed when triton-cpu is available (otherwise it silently overrides a working triton kernel). (2) If mis-placed, a working triton-cpu install would be forced onto the slow ctypes memmove path. (3) Ensure the batch_memcpy patch is guarded by `not HAS_TRITON` like the other fallbacks.

(1) `_batch_memcpy_impl` uses `ctypes.memmove(dst, src, size)` where `size` comes from `sizes.tolist()`; the docstring/tests treat `sizes` as BYTE counts. (2) The Triton reference kernel copies `size` BYTES (it casts pointers to uint8 and iterates `range(0, size)`), so the ctypes fallback is consistent only if callers always pass byte sizes. If any caller passes element counts instead of byte counts, the fallback would under/over-copy silently. (3) Add an assertion or comment at the call site (mamba_utils.batch_memcpy) documenting that `sizes` must be in bytes, matching the ctypes.memmove contract.

(1) The batch_memcpy monkey-patch imports `vllm.v1.worker.mamba_utils` inside `_postprocess_triton` and assigns a module-level attribute; this is a global monkey-patch that mutates another module's namespace. (2) Global monkey-patching is fragile: any code already holding a reference to the original `mamba_utils.batch_memcpy_kernel` (e.g. `mamba_utils.batch_memcpy` closing over the name at import time) may or may not see the override depending on how it looks the symbol up — `batch_memcpy` references `batch_memcpy_kernel` by module-global lookup at call time, so it works, but this coupling is implicit. (3) Consider a cleaner injection point (e.g. a setter function in mamba_utils) and document the override, consistent with the existing HAS_TRITON guard pattern.

(1) This PR removes the guard that force-disabled prefix caching and chunked prefill for linear-attention models on AMX CPUs. (2) The removal now RELIES on the conv.cpp varlen `has_initial_state` fix and the GDN state handoff being correct for ALL linear-attention CPU models — not just qwen3.5. If any other linear-attention model on CPU still has an unpatched continuation path, it will now silently produce wrong results instead of being safely disabled. (3) Confirm the varlen conv writeback fix (see critical finding) fully covers the continuation case before removing the guard; otherwise scope the removal to validated model families.

(1) The incremental search skips previously-scanned positions of output_tok_ids based on cached start_search_pos/end_search_pos, but these positions are only valid if output_tok_ids grows monotonically. In speculative decoding, output_tok_ids can be truncated by `state["output_tok_ids"][:-spec_len]` (in update_state) and rejected draft tokens are replaced with different token ids on the next step. When output shrinks below the cached search position, `lo = max(0, search_pos - (seq_len-1))` can exceed `len(target_list) - seq_len`, yielding an empty search range so the marker is never found even though a genuine start/end marker may now appear within the previously-skipped region. (2) This would silently prevent the thinking budget from being enforced (or from ending) for a spec-decoding request, defeating the feature. (3) Reset/clamp start_search_pos and end_search_pos when len(output_tok_ids) < the cached position (i.e. detect output shrink), or bound the cached position by the current output length so re-materialized tokens in the boundary/overlap region are re-scanned.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Add a spec-decoding correctness test where draft tokens are rejected and replaced such that a think start/end marker straddles the boundary of the truncated region; assert start_thinking/end_thinking are still discovered as in the pre-change full-scan implementation.

findings.claim: (1) search_start is computed as `state["start_search_pos"] - (seq_len - 1)` where seq_len = len(self.think_start_token_ids). If the reasoning config supplies an empty start-token list, seq_len == 0 and `_find_last_sequence_index_from` early-returns -1 regardless, but for end tokens the guard `len(self.think_end_token_ids) == 0` already short-circuits earlier, so this path is only reachable with non-empty start tokens; still, the overlap window relies on the exact relationship search_start = cached_len - (seq_len-1). If seq_len > 1 and start_search_pos was set on an earlier step, the loop range upper bound `len(target_list) - len(token_ids)` combined with `lo` must correctly re-include boundary-straddling markers. Verify with a multi-token start marker test. (2) An off-by-one here would cause a multi-token marker spanning the previous/new boundary to be missed, silently disabling budget enforcement. (3) Add a unit test with a 2+ token start/end marker that appears split across two decode steps, confirming detection matches the original full-scan behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test: MockReasoningConfig with reasoning_start_token_ids=[A,B]; append A on step N and B on step N+1; assert start_thinking equals the index of A, matching pre-change behavior.

findings.claim: (1) This PR widens the capability window in `cutlass_group_gemm_supported` from `>= 100 && < 110` to `>= 100 && < 120`, so on NVIDIA Thor (compute capability 11.0 => version_num==110) the support-query op now returns true and the Python fused-MoE layer (vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/experts/cutlass_moe.py, via w8a8_utils.cutlass_group_gemm_supported) will select the CUTLASS grouped-GEMM path. However the *actual dispatch* function `cutlass_moe_mm` (same file, line 285) still guards with `if (version_num >= 100 && version_num < 110)` and was NOT updated in this diff. For version_num==110 the dispatch falls through all branches and hits STD_TORCH_CHECK_NOT_IMPLEMENTED('No compiled cutlass_scaled_mm for CUDA device capability: 110. Required capability: 90 or 100'). (2) This means the 'Re-enable FP8 MoE on Thor' fix advertises the kernel as available but then throws at runtime the first time an FP8 MoE forward runs on Thor — a hard failure that defeats the purpose of the PR and regresses any code that queries support then dispatches. (3) Update the dispatch guard in `cutlass_moe_mm` from `version_num < 110` to `version_num < 120` (and mirror any SM110-related runtime dispatch, e.g. cutlass_moe_mm_sm100 selection) so the runtime dispatch window matches the widened support query. Verify against a Thor (SM110) run of an FP8 grouped GEMM.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On an SM110 (Thor) device (or by stubbing get_sm_version_num()==110), call ops.cutlass_group_gemm_supported(110) and then invoke cutlass_moe_mm; confirm it dispatches to a compiled kernel instead of raising NOT_IMPLEMENTED.

findings.claim: (1) The MoE grouped-GEMM build block now compiles `grouped_mm_c3x_sm100.cu` for arch set `10.0f;11.0f` (CUDA>=13) / `10.0a;10.1a;10.3a` (CUDA<13), but the CUDA<13 arch list does NOT include an SM110 target (there is no `11.0a`/`11.x` entry). On a CUDA<13 toolchain (e.g. 12.8) that supports Thor, the support query will return true for cc 11.0 (via cutlass_group_gemm_supported) yet no SM110 gencode was emitted for the grouped-MoE kernel, so the kernel would run via a non-optimal/absent binary or fail to load. (2) This produces an inconsistency between the advertised capability window (up to <120) and the actually-compiled architectures on the pre-13 toolchain, risking a launch failure or silent fallback on Thor. (3) Confirm whether Thor requires a CUDA>=13 toolchain; if it can build on CUDA 12.x, add the appropriate SM110 arch (e.g. `11.0a`) to the CUDA<13 branch of the MoE SCALED_MM_ARCHS intersection, consistent with the scaled_mm SM100 block already updated to `10.0a;10.1a;10.3a`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Configure the build with a CUDA 12.8 toolchain and TORCH_CUDA_ARCH_LIST including 11.0; verify the grouped_mm_c3x_sm100.cu object contains SM110 gencode and loads on Thor.

findings.claim: (1) The condition uses the loop-local variable `num_computed_tokens` (line 776, = request.num_computed_tokens + num_new_local_computed_tokens + num_external_computed_tokens) rather than the original `request.num_computed_tokens`. This is intentional and correct: a fully-transferred remote-KV resume has num_computed_tokens == num_tokens, so `num_computed_tokens < request.num_tokens - 1` is False and it is NOT throttled (its single decode-like step has no local prefill compute), while a partially-matched resume with remaining local prefill IS throttled. (2) This behavior matches the two new tests. The concern to verify is that `num_computed_tokens` at this point already includes the external/local-cached tokens for the current admission so the boundary `num_tokens - 1` correctly distinguishes 'has local prefill compute this step' from 'only a single-token step'. No defect found after tracing; recording as a low-risk confirmation that the `- 1` boundary matches the num_tokens-with-spec convention used elsewhere (e.g. line 2385 sets num_computed_tokens = num_tokens - 1 to denote decode-ready).
findings.suggested_benchmark: DP prefill balancing throughput test with mixed P/D disaggregation (fully vs partially transferred remote-KV requests) confirming resume steps are not needlessly delayed.

findings.claim: (1) The new 'present' detection relies on a positional invariant: `candidate_meta[i]` corresponds to `res[i*ranks_per_candidate : (i+1)*ranks_per_candidate]`. This is only correct if, for every accepted chunk, exactly `ranks_per_candidate` (= `self._lookup_expected_per_key` = `tp_count * pp_size`) keys were appended to `candidate_keys` in the same iteration, contiguously, and no per-rank filtering occurred. The old code was position-independent because it grouped by the (group, hash) meta stored alongside every individual key. (2) If any code path (present or future) appends a different number of keys per chunk, or if the store returns a `res` whose length differs from `len(candidate_keys)`, the index math `res[i*ranks_per_candidate + j]` silently reads the wrong ranks or raises IndexError, producing wrong cache-hit lengths and silent KV correctness issues. (3) Add an explicit assertion `assert len(res) == len(candidate_meta) * ranks_per_candidate` before the comprehension to make the positional invariant explicit and fail loudly if violated.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test asserting lookup hit-length equality between old grouped-count logic and new positional logic across TP/PP/SWA configurations, including a case where res length is mismatched.

findings.claim: (1) Behavior change in the 'expected per key' count. Old code used `expected_per_key = max(1, tp_count * self.pp_size)` (clamped to at least 1), while the new code uses `self._lookup_expected_per_key = tp_count * self.pp_size` with no `max(1, ...)` clamp. (2) If `tp_count` (= `min(self.tp_size, self.num_kv_head)`) or `self.pp_size` were ever 0, `ranks_per_candidate` becomes 0; the `all(... for j in range(0))` then vacuously returns True, marking every candidate as present regardless of the store contents — silently reporting spurious cache hits. Also `key_prefixes` would be empty so no keys get generated, but the divergence from the previous clamped semantics should be confirmed intentional. (3) Preserve the `max(1, ...)` clamp on `self._lookup_expected_per_key` in `_init_lookup_key_prefixes`, or add an assertion that `tp_count >= 1 and self.pp_size >= 1`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test with degenerate tp_count/pp_size to confirm no spurious hits.

findings.claim: (1) `_init_lookup_key_prefixes` precomputes `self._lookup_key_prefixes` and `self._lookup_expected_per_key` from `self.tp_size`, `self.num_kv_head`, `self.pp_size`, and each `db.metadata` at __init__ time, caching the key prefixes for the lifetime of the worker. (2) If any of these attributes or a token_db's `metadata` (e.g. cache_prefix, model_name, group_id, pcp_rank, dcp_rank) is mutated after construction, the cached prefixes become stale and lookups will query wrong keys — a silent correctness regression versus the old code which rebuilt `metadata_templates` on every lookup call. The test itself sets `worker.num_kv_head = 1` and `worker.pp_size = 1` and then must manually call `worker._init_lookup_key_prefixes()`, demonstrating this staleness hazard exists in practice. (3) Document that these attributes are immutable post-init, and/or derive the prefixes lazily/invalidate the cache when metadata changes.

(1) The final `exists_set` comprehension uses `all(res[i*ranks_per_candidate + j] == 1 for j in range(ranks_per_candidate))` which does per-element Python-level indexing and comparison. (2) This is the intended micro-optimization target of the PR (avoiding repeated PoolKey/dataclasses.replace and dict accumulation), and it is a net improvement, but the per-candidate `all()` over a Python range still incurs Python-loop overhead proportional to total keys. For very large candidate sets it could be expressed via slicing (`all(x == 1 for x in res[base:base+ranks_per_candidate])`) or numpy for fewer bytecode ops. (3) Optional: consider slice-based iteration to reduce integer multiply/add per element; low priority.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark lookup() key-construction + present-detection time for large token_len (e.g. 8k tokens, TP=8, PP=2) before/after.

findings.claim: (1) In `_handle_jit_event`, `mode=='error'` raises `RuntimeError` unconditionally whenever any monitored JIT compilation occurs after warmup — for the CuTeDSL path this raise happens *inside* `cute.compile` (the monkeypatched `_compile_with_monitor` calls `_log_cutedsl_jit_compile` -> `_handle_jit_event` before delegating to `original_compile`), and for the Triton path inside the `jit_post_compile_hook`. (2) A single un-warmed shape/config in real inference would then abort the request (or worse, propagate out of a Triton internal hook whose exception-safety across releases is not guaranteed — the code itself notes the hook signature is volatile). Because CuTeDSL `cute.compile` results are cached via `@cache`/`functools.cache` at the callsites, the first legitimate compile of any not-warmed kernel during serving will hard-fail. (3) Consider whether `error` mode is intended to be a debugging/CI-only knob; if so, document that clearly and/or gate it, and ensure the error is raised where the caller can handle it rather than from within a third-party runtime hook. At minimum verify that raising from `jit_post_compile_hook` is safe across supported Triton versions.

(1) The CuTeDSL hook monkeypatches `cutlass.cute.compile` on the module object, but callsites bind the reference at import time via `from cutlass import cute` / `import cutlass.cute as cute` (e.g. `vllm/model_executor/layers/mamba/ops/gdn_chunk_cutedsl/__init__.py`). Attribute access `cute.compile` is dynamic so the patch is picked up, BUT any callsite that did `from cutlass.cute import compile as _compile` and called the bound name would bypass the monitor. (2) This creates silent gaps in coverage that are easy to introduce later, undermining the monitor's guarantee. (3) Consider documenting the requirement that callsites must call `cute.compile` via attribute access, or add a test asserting existing callsites do so.

(1) `_cutedsl_hook_installed` is a second idempotency flag in addition to `_active`; since `activate()` already early-returns when `_active` is True, `_setup_cutedsl_jit_hook`'s guard can never be reached twice in production — the extra module-global exists only to support the test reset fixture. (2) Redundant state adds a second place that must be reset/reasoned about (the tests indeed have to reset both). (3) Consider relying on the single `_active` guard, or add a comment noting the flag is purely for test isolation.

(1) The `jit_monitor_verbose` docstring was reworded from 'Log every Triton JIT compile with its dispatch key' to 'Log every monitored JIT compile with runtime details', but the new `_log_triton_jit_compile` only includes `key=` detail when `_verbose and key is not None`; for CuTeDSL verbose adds no runtime detail at all (event/fn_name only). (2) The docstring now over-promises 'runtime details' for backends where none are surfaced, which is mildly misleading to users tuning verbosity. (3) Either tighten the docstring or surface at least some CuTeDSL detail (e.g., the compile options/args) in verbose mode.

(1) `process_weights_after_loading` computes `weight_scale_swizzled = swizzle_mxfp8_scale(weight_scale_2d, M=N, K=K)` from the row-major `[N, K]` weight, but then transposes the weight itself to column-major `[K, N]` (`weight.contiguous().t()`) while leaving the scale swizzled for the original `[N, K]` layout. The Cutlass variant kept weight in `[N, K]` and transposed only at the `mm_mxfp8` call site (`weight.t()`). The new kernel pre-transposes the storage but reuses the same scale swizzle parameters, so the block-scale layout may no longer correspond to the operand-B memory layout that the cute-dsl `mm_mxfp8` expects. (2) If the scale swizzle does not match the transposed operand layout, the GEMM silently produces wrong results (no error), corrupting all outputs on Blackwell. (3) Verify against FlashInfer's cute-dsl `mm_mxfp8` operand-B/scale layout contract whether the swizzle should be computed on the transposed `[K, N]` shape (e.g. `swizzle_mxfp8_scale(..., M=?, K=?)`) or whether weight should stay `[N, K]` like the Cutlass path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Numerical correctness test comparing FlashInferCutedslMxfp8LinearKernel output against EmulationMxfp8LinearKernel for a range of (M,N,K) shapes on SM100.

findings.claim: (1) The new kernel is inserted at the FRONT of `_POSSIBLE_MXFP8_KERNELS[PlatformEnum.CUDA]`, ahead of the existing FlashInferCutlassMxfp8LinearKernel, making it the default MXFP8 backend on all CUDA devices where it reports supported (SM100/SM103 with cute-dsl installed). (2) This is a silent default-behavior change: existing users on Blackwell who were getting the Cutlass kernel will now get the cute-dsl kernel with no opt-in, changing numerics/performance characteristics. If the cute-dsl kernel is less mature this can regress correctness or throughput without warning. (3) Confirm the priority ordering is intentional and the cute-dsl kernel has been validated for correctness/perf parity; otherwise place it after the Cutlass kernel or gate it behind an explicit backend selection.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MXFP8 W8A8 linear microbenchmark (throughput + numerics) comparing cute-dsl vs cutlass kernel across typical hidden sizes on SM100.

findings.claim: (1) `is_supported` gates on `current_platform.is_device_capability_family(100)` (SM100 family only), but the priority-ordered `_POSSIBLE_MXFP8_KERNELS` selection relies on `is_supported` correctly returning False on unsupported hardware. `is_device_capability_family(100)` excludes SM120 (RTX Blackwell) where cute-dsl b12x paths exist elsewhere in the codebase; if MXFP8 cute-dsl is also intended for SM120 this silently disables it there, and if it is not intended the docstring/config claim 'SM100/SM103' is fine. (2) Impact: potential silently-missing acceleration on SM120, or a mismatch between the config.py docstring update ('NVFP4, MXFP8') implying broad support and the narrower runtime gate. (3) Verify the intended capability set and align the gate, docstring, and config comment.

(1) `can_implement` unconditionally returns `(True, None)` without validating that K and N satisfy the `min_dim`/block-size constraints that are only checked via `assert` at apply time. (2) Because kernel selection happens via `can_implement`, a layer with K<128 or N<128 (or K not divisible by MXFP8_BLOCK_SIZE) will be selected as the top-priority kernel and then crash with an AssertionError at the first forward pass rather than gracefully falling back to Marlin/Emulation. (3) Move the K/N/divisibility checks into `can_implement` so the framework can fall back to a lower-priority kernel instead of hard-failing at runtime.

(1) In `process_weights_after_loading`, `weight.contiguous().t()` stores the weight transposed; `.t()` returns a non-contiguous view, so `layer.weight` is a column-major (non-contiguous) tensor. (2) This is presumably intentional (comment says mm_mxfp8 expects column-major operand B), but unlike the Cutlass path there is no `.contiguous()` guard in `apply_weights`; if the cute-dsl kernel internally requires a contiguous buffer it may trigger an implicit copy or error each forward. (3) Confirm the cute-dsl `mm_mxfp8` accepts a transposed (non-contiguous) operand-B view; if not, materialize the desired memory layout once in process_weights (e.g. store as contiguous [K,N]) rather than a transpose view.

(1) The per-row tie workspace size check computes `kTieWsPerRow = TopK <= kBlockSize ? kMaxTies : TopK` and requires `workspace.size(0) >= num_rows * kTieWsPerRow * sizeof(Tie)`, but the kernel's `params.tie_ws` is a single base pointer that is NOT advanced per row (grid uses `blockIdx.x = row`, yet the launch passes the same `tie_ws` base to every cluster). Inside `large_topk`, `tie_ws[tp]` is written with `tp = prefix_equal + i` which is a *within-row* offset. If different rows share the same `tie_ws` base without a per-row stride of `kTieWsPerRow`, concurrent rows will corrupt each other's tie workspace. (2) This produces silently wrong top-k indices for multi-row (num_rows>1) decode batches whenever ties spill to global tie_ws (TopK=2048 path). (3) Verify tie_ws is offset by `blockIdx.x * kTieWsPerRow` somewhere in the kernel; if not, index tie_ws as `tie_ws[row * kTieWsPerRow + tp]`.

(1) The author's own comment at the DSMEM count-packing site warns: "`le_full` may exceed the per-block tie buffer cap; using it here creates holes in tie_ws and can make TopK=2048 refine unwritten workspace slots." The code packs `le` (capped at kMaxTies) into the count, but the global tie_ws write loop bounds `tp < (TopK <= kBlockSize ? kMaxTies : TopK)` uses `prefix_equal` derived from capped counts while `s_total_equal` (used to decide whether refinement is needed and how many ties to process) is computed from the *packed equal counts* which are also capped — the interplay must be verified. If any block's actual equal-count exceeds kMaxTies, tie_ws slots between the written entries and TopK are never initialized, and the TopK=2048 refinement path reads uninitialized workspace, producing wrong indices. (2) Silent correctness bug for high-collision logits (e.g. quantized/low-entropy scores, exactly the '10LSBits' test distribution). (3) Either guarantee the tie buffer cap is never exceeded for the supported k/seq_len ranges, or zero-initialize tie_ws and clamp refinement to the actually-written count.

(1) `launch_cooperative_topk_impl` takes `max_seq_len` but immediately discards it with `(void)max_seq_len; // Kept for signature parity with persistent_topk.` (2) This is a declared-but-unused argument that the rubric's Mandatory Unused-Argument check flags: it adds an ABI/interface obligation (callers must compute and pass max_seq_len) with no runtime effect, risking silent divergence from persistent_topk semantics if a future reader assumes it is honored. (3) Either use it (e.g. to validate seq_len bounds) or add an explicit TODO/migration note documenting that cooperative_topk intentionally ignores it.

(1) `get_device_prop()->major >= 10` is queried on every launch to decide `supports_cluster16`. (2) Repeated device-property queries per call add avoidable host overhead in the low-latency decode loop this kernel targets. (3) Cache the device-property lookup in a function-local `static int` (as persistent_topk's launcher does with `static int num_sms`), computing it once.

(1) The cooperative_topk source is only compiled when `COOPERATIVE_TOPK_ARCHS` is non-empty and CUDA>=12.0, guarded by `-DVLLM_ENABLE_COOPERATIVE_TOPK`; the op `cooperative_topk` is unconditionally declared in ops.h/torch_bindings but its implementation body is `#ifndef USE_ROCM` only (no `VLLM_ENABLE_COOPERATIVE_TOPK` guard shown in cooperative_topk.cu). (2) On CUDA<12.0 or unsupported archs the .cu is not compiled at all, so if torch_bindings.cpp still registers `cooperative_topk`, the extension will fail to link (undefined symbol). (3) Ensure the binding registration in torch_bindings.cpp is wrapped in the same `#ifdef VLLM_ENABLE_COOPERATIVE_TOPK` guard as the compilation condition.

(1) The stride/TMA-alignment check `stride % 4 == 0` and the `num_rows <= 32` check are enforced, but there is no check that `logits.stride(1) == 1` (contiguous inner dim) even though the kernel loads `row_input + off` as contiguous float and uses TMA bulk copies that assume unit inner stride. (2) A non-unit inner stride (e.g. a transposed/sliced logits view) would silently read wrong elements. (3) Add `STD_TORCH_CHECK(logits.stride(1) == 1, ...)` at the entry validation, matching the assumptions of the TMA path.

(1) `send_to_engine` was changed from a direct `transport::send_message(...).await` to `self.handle.spawn(async move { ... }).await?`, i.e. every single outbound message to the engine now incurs an extra task spawn onto the ZMQ runtime plus a cross-runtime wakeup/join round-trip.
