(2) This is on the hot path of every request submission (`call` -> `send_to_engine` with `EngineCoreRequestType::Add`) and every abort/utility call, so it adds per-request task-scheduling and inter-runtime hand-off latency and allocation overhead to the critical path — this partially works against the PR's stated throughput goal for high request rates. (3) Consider only offloading the socket work when the caller is not already on the ZMQ runtime, or driving `send_message` directly on the ZMQ runtime via a channel/mpsc into a long-lived sender task rather than spawn-per-message; at minimum benchmark spawn-per-send vs. a persistent sender loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure single-request p50/p99 submission latency and max sustained requests/sec (e.g. tiny model, short prompts, high concurrency) before vs. after the spawn-per-send change to quantify the added hand-off cost.

findings.claim: (1) The `request_runtime_layer` is applied to the whole router in `routes.rs`, so `RequestRuntimeService::call` runs for every HTTP request including lightweight ops routes (`/health`, `/metrics`, `/v1/models`), and for those it still does a `should_offload` linear scan over `OFFLOADED_PATHS` and (for offloaded paths) an `Arc<AppState>` clone + inner-service clone-swap + `Box::pin`. (2) For the non-offloaded fast path the cost is just the linear `contains` scan (cheap), but the boxed-future allocation added even for pass-through requests (`Box::pin(self.inner.call(req))`) is a per-request heap allocation on all HTTP traffic that did not exist before this layer. (3) Consider returning the inner future directly for the pass-through case (e.g. via an enum future) to avoid boxing on non-offloaded requests, or place the layer closer to only the offloaded routes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark throughput of `/health` and `/metrics` at high QPS before/after to measure the per-request Box::pin allocation overhead introduced on non-offloaded paths.

findings.claim: (1) `should_offload` matches the request path with an exact `contains` against a hardcoded `OFFLOADED_PATHS` list. (2) If routes are ever mounted under a prefix, or trailing-slash / case variants reach this layer, requests that should be offloaded will silently fall through to the HTTP runtime, quietly defeating the intended CPU-offloading behavior with no error (a silent performance regression rather than a crash). (3) Add a shape/coverage assertion or a test that ties `OFFLOADED_PATHS` to the actual router route table so the two cannot drift, and document the exact-match assumption.

(1) The added TODO comment questions whether the `spawn_blocking` for image preprocessing is still necessary now that request handling runs on a dedicated runtime. (2) If it is in fact redundant, keeping a `spawn_blocking` inside code that is now already offloaded to a multi-threaded request runtime adds an extra thread hand-off (blocking pool dispatch) per multimodal request for no benefit; if it is still needed, the TODO is noise. (3) Resolve the TODO before merge (either remove the `spawn_blocking` if preprocessing is short/non-blocking on the request runtime, or replace the TODO with a definitive comment explaining why blocking-pool offload is still required).

(1) The new shared `topk_indices_buffer` is allocated with `torch.empty(num_index_heads, max_num_batched_tokens, sparse_topk_blocks, dtype=torch.int32)` with **no `device=` argument**, so it is created on CPU. The indexer Triton/MSA kernels write GPU-side into this buffer and the attend impl reads it back GPU-side. (2) A CPU-resident buffer passed to a CUDA kernel either raises a device-mismatch error at first sparse-attention forward, or (if silently `.to(device)`-copied somewhere) breaks the intended zero-copy shared-buffer contract so the indexer's writes never reach the reader. This is on the model construction path for every MiniMax-M3 sparse model on ROCm. (3) Add `device=current_platform.device_type` (or the model's device) to the `torch.empty` call, matching how other vLLM decode buffers (e.g. `context_len_buffer` in the metadata builders, which pass `device=device`) are allocated on-GPU. Verify the analogous NVIDIA model.py allocation has the same fix.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instantiate MiniMaxM3Model with a sparse_attention_config and run one sparse-attention forward on GPU; assert topk_indices_buffer.is_cuda and that indexer writes are visible to the attend impl.

findings.claim: (1) The refactor changes the indexer/attend contract from a returned tuple `topk_idx = self.indexer(index_query); self.impl.forward(self, query, kv_cache, topk_idx, output)` to a side-effecting shared buffer: `self.indexer(index_query)` (return value discarded) then `self.impl.forward(self, query, self.kv_cache, output)` reads the buffer back. However, the base impls `MiniMaxM3SparseImpl.forward`, `MiniMaxM3SparseTritonImpl.forward`, and `MiniMaxM3SparseMSAImpl.forward` in common/sparse_attention.py and nvidia/sparse_attention_msa.py still take a `topk_idx` positional argument and unpack `decode_topk, prefill_topk = topk_idx`. If the diff did not also update all three impl signatures + call sites (and the buffer read path), this is a hard TypeError / stale-code path. (2) Silent divergence or crash on every sparse decode/prefill. (3) Ensure the impls' `forward` signatures, the abstract base, the MSA impl, and the buffer-read logic are all updated consistently; confirm decode vs prefill top-k are both routed through the single int32 buffer with correct slicing.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run sparse-attention forward for both a pure-decode and a mixed prefill+decode batch and check outputs match the pre-refactor tuple-return path.

findings.claim: (1) The shared `topk_indices_buffer` is sized `[num_index_heads, max_num_batched_tokens, sparse_topk_blocks]` and shared by ALL sparse-attention layers. Because it is a single reused buffer written by the indexer and read by the attend within the same layer's forward, it is only safe if every layer fully consumes its contents before the next layer's indexer overwrites it. Under CUDA-graph capture or if the indexer and attend for different layers can interleave (e.g. async prefetch, PP), the shared buffer creates a write-after-read hazard across layers. (2) Cross-layer aliasing would silently corrupt block selection for some layers, degrading accuracy without a crash. (3) Confirm the eager break serializes indexer-write -> attend-read within each layer and that no two layers overlap; document the single-buffer reuse invariant, or allocate per-layer buffers if overlap is possible.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-layer sparse model with cudagraph + spec-decode; compare topk selections per layer against a per-layer-buffer reference.

findings.claim: (1) `storeElemsFp8` casts fp32 RMSNorm outputs directly to E4M3 with a fixed ±448 saturation and NO per-tensor scale, relying on the comment's claim that 'RMSNorm outputs are O(1)'. Gemma-style RMSNorm scales by (1 + weight); if any learned weight channel is large, outputs can exceed the E4M3 dynamic range where relative precision is poor, and the index-Q / index-K dot products (used only for relative block ordering) could reorder blocks vs the bf16 path. (2) Silent accuracy regression in sparse block selection on the fp8 indexer path, hard to catch without an end-to-end eval. (3) Add an assertion or an offline check that index_q/index_k magnitudes stay well within E4M3 range for the target checkpoint, and gate the fp8 indexer behind a validated accuracy check; the comment asserting O(1) magnitude should be backed by data.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare top-k block selection (and downstream gsm8k / needle accuracy) between bf16 and fp8 indexer paths on MiniMax-M3-preview.

findings.claim: (1) `launchFusedMiniMaxM3` changed the `index_q_out`/`index_cache` parameters from typed `scalar_t*` to `void*` and now reinterpret_casts them per-instantiation inside the LAUNCH macro. This loses compile-time type checking at the launch boundary: a mismatch between `fp8_idx` and the actual tensor dtype (e.g. fp8_idx=false but index_cache is e4m3, or vice versa) would silently reinterpret bytes rather than fail to compile. (2) A future caller wiring dtype inconsistently gets silent memory corruption instead of a build error. (3) The torch-op wrapper already validates dtype consistency (the new STD_TORCH_CHECKs), which mitigates this; keep those checks as the single source of truth and consider a static_assert or debug check that `fp8_idx == (OUT_T is uint8_t)` inside the launch to preserve some type safety.

(1) The new num_max_tokens_per_rank is derived from get_forward_context().dp_metadata but does NOT compare against the buffer's init capacity (max_num_batched_tokens). The comment claims the value is "capped at the buffer's init capacity for prefill", but the code only rounds up to a power of two: `num_max_tokens_per_rank = 1 << max(n - 1, 0).bit_length()`. When the DP-max token count is not itself a power of two, the rounded-up value can EXCEED the buffer's init capacity. Example: if max_num_batched_tokens (buffer init) = 8192 but the actual DP-padded batch reaches a value like 6000 (non-power-of-2), the rounding produces 8192 which is fine, but if the buffer were initialized to a non-power-of-2 capacity (e.g. 5000) and n=5000, rounding gives 8192 > 5000, overflowing the recv buffer. (2) Feeding num_max_tokens_per_rank larger than the pre-allocated ElasticBuffer capacity risks an out-of-bounds recv write / kernel error, i.e. silent corruption or crash. (3) Clamp the rounded value: `num_max_tokens_per_rank = min(1 << max(n-1,0).bit_length(), buffer_init_capacity)` where buffer_init_capacity is the max_num_batched_tokens the buffer was sized for. Verify DeepEP dispatch tolerates num_max_tokens_per_rank > init capacity before relying on it.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DeepEP V2 decode path (use_cudagraph, do_expand=False) with a max_num_batched_tokens that is not a power of two and confirm the rounded num_max_tokens_per_rank never exceeds the ElasticBuffer's allocated recv capacity; assert no OOB/kernel failure.

findings.claim: (1) `n = int(dp_meta.num_tokens_across_dp_cpu.max())` calls `.max()` on num_tokens_across_dp_cpu and forces a Python int conversion of a tensor element inside the dispatch path. If num_tokens_across_dp_cpu is a CUDA tensor rather than a CPU tensor, `int(tensor.max())` triggers a device-to-host synchronization (implicit `.item()`), a classic throughput killer that also breaks CUDA-graph capturability. (2) The whole point of the do_expand=False/use_cudagraph mode is cudagraph safety and decode throughput; an accidental host sync here would defeat that. (3) Confirm num_tokens_across_dp_cpu is guaranteed CPU-resident (the name suggests so); if there is any path where it is on device, gate this computation to run outside the captured region or precompute the value host-side. Add an assertion that the tensor is on CPU.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the decode dispatch path with nsys/torch profiler and verify no cudaStreamSynchronize / D2H copy is introduced by the .max()/int() call; confirm CUDA graph capture still succeeds.

findings.claim: (1) When dp_meta is None the code falls back to `n = tokens.shape[0]` (the local per-step token count), but the comment stresses the value must be "uniform across ranks". In a multi-rank EP/DP setup without dp_metadata, different ranks can observe different local token counts, producing per-rank-divergent num_max_tokens_per_rank. If DeepEP requires this argument to match across ranks (as the comment implies for the max()-across-dp path), the fallback could cause a collective mismatch/hang. (2) Risk is a deadlock or incorrect dispatch when dp_metadata is unavailable. (3) Document why the dp_meta=None fallback is safe (e.g. single-rank only), or add an assertion that this branch is only reached with a single EP rank.

New assertion `assert layer.lora_config.fully_sharded_loras` in `_mcp_apply` also constrains the two non-merged callers that still route through `_mcp_apply`: `ColumnParallelLinearWithShardedLoRA.apply` and `MergedColumnParallelLinearWithShardedLoRA.apply` (S-LoRA classes). Those are only selected via `_fully_sharded_can_replace`, so `fully_sharded_loras` is guaranteed True there and the assert holds. HOWEVER, the assert reads `layer.lora_config`, which is set inside `create_lora_weights`. If `apply`/`_mcp_apply` is ever exercised on a layer instance whose `create_lora_weights` was not called (e.g. a partially-initialized layer in a unit test or a subclass that sets weights directly), the attribute access will raise AttributeError instead of the intended clear behavior. Confirm all `_mcp_apply` callers always have `lora_config` populated before `apply`. (1) added assert depends on `lora_config` being set; (2) risk of AttributeError on uninitialized layers; (3) consider `getattr(layer, 'lora_config', None)` guard or documenting the invariant.

Behavior change / backward-compat: `MergedColumnParallelLinearWithLoRA.apply` previously always called `_mcp_apply` (which performed a `tensor_model_parallel_all_gather` on the shrunk buffers) for the non-`_apply_base_forward` path; it now calls `super().apply(x, bias)` which routes to `BaseLinearLayerWithLoRA.apply` → `_apply_sync`/`add_lora_linear` with NO all_gather. This is the intended fix (the old unconditional all_gather was only correct for fully_sharded_loras; for non-fully-sharded merged-column with tp_size>1 the all_gather concatenated full-rank buffers producing a rank dimension of rank*tp_size that mismatched `lora_b_stacked`). The new path is correct for both non-fully-sharded (any tp) and fully_sharded+tp==1 (all_gather is a no-op there anyway). Note this also opts the merged-column path into the dual-stream async path (`VLLM_LORA_ENABLE_DUAL_STREAM`) inherited from the base `apply`, which `_mcp_apply` did not use — verify numerical equivalence and that async output shape/dtype matches for the merged (n_slices>1) case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: LoRA correctness regression test with MergedColumnParallelLinear (gate_up_proj) under fully_sharded_loras=False, tp_size>1, comparing outputs before/after; and a throughput comparison with VLLM_LORA_ENABLE_DUAL_STREAM=1.

findings.claim: In `_zero_padding`, w13_weight zeroing of the padded intermediate region uses `w13_half_size = layer.w13_weight.shape[1] // 2` where dim 1 is the (padded) w13 output dim (gate + up concatenated). The gate slice `[:, intermediate_size:w13_half_size, :]` and up slice `[:, w13_half_size + intermediate_size : 2 * w13_half_size, :]` correctly zero the two padded interior regions. This is correct only under the assumption that w13's layout is exactly [gate(padded_intermediate) | up(padded_intermediate)] contiguous in dim 1 and that padded_intermediate == w13_half_size. Verify that `w13_weight.shape[1]` is always even (2 * padded intermediate). If a partition edge case makes shape[1] odd, `// 2` silently mis-splits gate/up and zeros wrong rows, corrupting weights silently. Add an assertion `assert layer.w13_weight.shape[1] % 2 == 0`.

`_zero_padding` writes into `layer.w13_weight`/`layer.w2_weight` in-place (bf16/fp16) BEFORE the fp8 quantization in `process_weights_after_loading`. Since `per_block_cast_to_fp8` computes per-block scales from the (now-zeroed) padded weight blocks, zeroing the padding is what keeps padded blocks from producing spurious non-zero fp8 outputs — good. However this method is only guarded by `_already_called_process_weights_after_loading` at the caller. On an RL weight-reload path, if `_zero_padding` runs on weights that were already replaced with fp8 tensors, the in-place float assignments and the `hidden_size`/`intermediate_size` unpadded comparisons still assume the original padded high-precision layout. Confirm reload always re-materializes bf16 weights before calling this; otherwise document the invariant.

`_zero_padding` reads `layer.moe_config.hidden_dim_unpadded` and `intermediate_size_per_partition_unpadded` without asserting they are not None. In FusedMoEConfig.__post_init__ these default to the padded values when None, so `> intermediate_size` comparisons would be False and no zeroing happens (safe degradation). But if moe_config is ever constructed without running __post_init__ these could be None and the `>` comparison against padded shapes would raise TypeError. Other call sites (aiter_mxfp4, rocm_aiter) assert these are not None; add the same assertion here for consistency and a clearer failure.

`maybe_roundup_sizes` rounds hidden_size and intermediate_size_per_partition up to `weight_block_size[0]` (128). This is correct for per-block fp8 (blocks must tile evenly), but it enlarges w13/w2 weight tensors for any model whose dims are not already 128-multiples, increasing weight memory and GEMM cost. This is inherent to block quantization and matches the padding contract, but for models already 128-aligned it is a no-op (round_up returns the same value), so no regression there. No action needed unless padding introduces materially larger dims for common configs; consider a debug log of pre/post sizes to aid diagnosing unexpected padding.

(1) mark_non_evictable performs an unconditional `del self.evictable_blocks[key]`, which raises KeyError if the key is not present in the evictable list. (2) This relies on a strict invariant: every block with ref_cnt==0 must currently be in evictable_blocks. The invariant is maintained by complete_store/complete_load calling mark_evictable, but the asymmetry with mark_evictable (which uses idempotent assignment `evictable_blocks[key]=None`) makes the code fragile — any future path that lands a block at ref_cnt 0->1 without a prior mark_evictable, or a duplicate prepare_load, would crash training with a hard KeyError instead of degrading gracefully. (3) Either use `evictable_blocks.pop(key, None)` for symmetry, or add an explicit assertion/comment documenting the invariant so the KeyError is an intentional guard.

(1) `self.blocks` was changed from `OrderedDict` to a plain `dict`, and `touch()`/`insert()` no longer maintain LRU order in `self.blocks` (ordering now lives only in `evictable_blocks`). (2) Any external consumer that read `self._policy.blocks` for LRU ordering silently gets wrong results — the tiering test at test_tiering_offloading.py had to be migrated from `_policy.blocks.keys()` to `_policy.evictable_blocks.keys()`, confirming ordering semantics of the public-ish `blocks` attribute changed. Other callers/tools inspecting `blocks` ordering would break without a compile-time error. (3) Confirm no other consumer relies on `blocks` ordering, and consider documenting that `blocks` is now unordered while `evictable_blocks` carries LRU order.

(1) `insert()` adds to evictable_blocks only when `block.ref_cnt == 0`. In the manager's normal flow, insert is always called with a freshly allocated block whose ref_cnt is -1 (not ready), so the `ref_cnt == 0` branch is effectively dead for the manager's own use. (2) It is not obviously wrong (defensive for other callers), but it can mask a logic mismatch: newly inserted blocks are marked evictable later via complete_store->mark_evictable, so the insert-time branch is redundant with that path and could double-insert (harmless due to dict assignment, but confusing). (3) Add a comment clarifying which caller can pass a ref_cnt==0 block to insert, or drop the branch if the manager always inserts not-ready blocks.

(1) The new base-class hooks mark_evictable/mark_non_evictable are defined as concrete no-ops (return). ARC does not override them, so it silently relies on the default. (2) A silent no-op default means a future policy author can forget to maintain an evictable structure and get subtly wrong (non-LRU) eviction ordering with no signal. (3) Consider documenting in the base class that policies maintaining a separate evictable structure MUST override these, and note in the ARC class that its T1/T2/ghost-list design intentionally does not need them.

(1) MicroGemm<ISA::RVV> defines a pack_weight() method (mirroring VEC/AMX) but it is never invoked anywhere in the WNA16 code path: cpu_gemm_wna16_impl performs its own dequantize-into-b_buffer and does not call gemm_t::pack_weight, and the Python side (_process_gptq_weights_w4a16) does the packing in torch, not via this C++ helper. (2) This is dead code that suggests the RVV block layout was intended to be validated by the same packer used elsewhere; if a future change routes RVV through the pack_weight path (as cpu_fused_moe.cpp does for VEC/AMX), the RVV packer produces the plain [output/16, input, 16] layout, but the RVV gemm reads B with RVV_MGEMM_B_GROUP_STRIDE=16 and splits 32 channels as two 16-channel blocks separated by b_n_group_stride — the reviewer should confirm the layout contract matches. (3) Either remove the unused pack_weight or add a test/assert tying it to the actual B layout consumed by gemm_micro_rvv_mx32_ku4.

(1) The bf16 and fp16 loaders duplicate almost verbatim the logic already present in csrc/cpu/cpu_attn_rvv.hpp (load_row8_B_as_f32), including the identical zvfbfmin/zvfh fallback branches and the u16->u32<<16 bf16 emulation. (2) Duplicating the widening/reinterpret sequences risks the two copies diverging (e.g. one gets a correctness fix, the other doesn't). (3) Factor the scalar_t -> fixed_fp32x8_t 8-lane widening loaders into a shared header (e.g. in cpu_types_riscv_impl.hpp) reused by both the attention and micro-gemm RVV kernels.

(1) The scalar (non-zvfh) fp16 fallback loads into an alignas(32) float[8] via a scalar cast loop and then vle32; this path is untested for RISC-V targets lacking Zvfh and silently produces correct-but-slow results, but there is no runtime check that _get_isa_hint returned 'rvv' only when the vector unit actually supports the needed widening for the active dtype. (2) On a RISC-V target without Zvfh/Zvfbfmin the kernel still compiles (fallback) but a fp16/bf16 model will run the scalar per-element conversion inside the hot k-loop, degrading GEMM throughput substantially with no diagnostic. (3) Consider gating the isa_hint='rvv' selection (or emitting a one-time warning) when the runtime lacks Zvfh/Zvfbfmin for the requested activation dtype.
findings.suggested_benchmark: WNA16 GEMM microbench on a RISC-V core without Zvfh, fp16 activations, vs a scalar reference, measuring tokens/s.

findings.claim: (1) The micro-kernel is hardcoded to 8 output lanes (RVV_MGEMM_N8=8) via LMUL_256 fp32 vectors regardless of the actual VLEN of the RISC-V target; on cores with VLEN>128 (e.g. 256/512-bit vector registers) this uses only a fraction of each vector register, leaving FLOPs on the table. (2) Fixing NSize=32 processed as 4x8 lanes means wider-VLEN hardware is under-utilized in the WNA16 GEMM inner loop. (3) If the vec kernels elsewhere are VLEN-agnostic, consider making the RVV micro-gemm lane count derive from vlen (or document that 8-lane fixed width is an intentional VLEN=128 baseline).
findings.suggested_benchmark: WNA16 GEMM throughput on RISC-V cores with VLEN=128 vs VLEN=256/512.

findings.claim: (1) The Triton kernels `_minimax_qk_var_kernel` and `_minimax_rms_apply_kernel` index into `qkv` as `qkv_ptr + token * row_stride + idx`, i.e. they use `qkv.stride(0)` for the token dimension but implicitly assume the inner (feature) dimension has stride 1. There is no assertion or `.contiguous()` guard on `qkv`. (2) If `qkv` is passed as a non-inner-contiguous view (e.g. a strided/transposed slice), the kernels will silently read the wrong elements and produce numerically wrong q/k RMSNorm output with no error — a silent correctness bug. The pure-torch `_minimax_qk_norm_tp_eager` path handles arbitrary strides via `.split`, so the Triton path diverges from the reference for non-contiguous inputs. (3) Add `assert qkv.stride(-1) == 1` (or call `qkv = qkv.contiguous()`) at the top of `_minimax_qk_norm_tp_fallback` before launching the kernels, mirroring the layout the kernels assume.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Call `_minimax_qk_norm_tp_fallback` on a non-contiguous qkv view (e.g. `qkv[:, ::1]` after a transpose) and compare against `_minimax_qk_norm_tp_eager`.

findings.claim: (1) `_minimax_qk_norm_tp_fallback` still declares a `tp_rank` parameter but never uses it in the body (only `tp_world` is used for the folded `/tp_world` in the apply kernel). (2) An unused argument is dead surface area — a reader may assume rank-dependent behavior that does not exist, and it was flagged by the mandatory unused-new-argument check. It is retained only for call-site signature parity with the fused-kernel branch in `_minimax_qk_norm_fusion`. (3) Either drop `tp_rank` from the signature and the corresponding call-site argument, or add a short comment noting it is kept solely for interface parity with the fused path.

(1) `MiniMaxText01RMSNormTP.forward_qk` is a public `@staticmethod` whose behavior changed: it previously all-reduced the variance across the TP group when `tp_world > 1`, and now never all-reduces (it is treated as the tp==1 / already-reduced building block). (2) Any external caller relying on the old all-reduce semantics for tp_world>1 would now get per-shard (local) RMSNorm instead of the global-variance RMSNorm, i.e. silently wrong results. The in-repo test reference was updated to `_minimax_qk_norm_tp_eager`, and the only production caller (`forward_qkv`) now invokes `forward_qk` only on the tp==1 path, so in-tree usage is correct — but the contract of a public method silently changed. (3) Consider documenting the new precondition in `forward_qk`'s docstring ('assumes variance is either tp==1 or already reduced; does not all-reduce') to prevent misuse.

(1) The inline comments in the new `forward_qkv` tp-branch use full-width unicode characters ('Case 0： tp_size=1') mixing a CJK colon into English comments. (2) This is cosmetic but inconsistent with the surrounding ASCII comments and can confuse tooling/greps. (3) Replace with an ASCII colon: '# Case 0: tp_size == 1'.

(1) The new cpu_attn_has_isa("rvv") only returns true when __riscv_v_min_vlen == 128, but the RVV attention kernel is actually compiled for both VLEN=128 and VLEN=256 (cpu_attn_rvv.hpp guards on `__riscv_v_min_vlen == 128 || __riscv_v_min_vlen == 256`, matching generate_cpu_attn_dispatch.py). (2) This makes the compile-time detection inconsistent with what is actually built: on hardware that reports zve*/RVV without an explicit zvl<N>b flag and was compiled with VLLM_RVV_VLEN=256, the Python fallback `torch.ops._C.cpu_attn_has_isa('rvv')` will return False, silently disabling the RVV kernel and falling back to the scalar `vec` path — exactly the case this PR set out to fix, but only handled for VLEN=128. (3) Change the guard to `(__riscv_v_min_vlen == 128 || __riscv_v_min_vlen == 256)` to match the kernel's actual compile condition.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Build with -DVLLM_RVV_VLEN=256 on a RISC-V host whose /proc/cpuinfo lacks a zvl<N>b flag; assert _get_attn_isa returns 'rvv' (not 'vec') and measure CPU attention throughput vs the scalar path.

findings.claim: (1) cpu_attn_has_isa takes a generic `isa` string but only handles "rvv", returning false for every other value including valid ISAs like "amx"/"neon"/"avx512". (2) A caller reasonably assuming this is a general ISA-availability query would get wrong answers, and the generic name overstates the capability. (3) Either narrow the name/signature to reflect that it only answers the RVV question (e.g. cpu_attn_has_rvv()), or add a docstring/comment stating only "rvv" is currently supported and other inputs always return false.

(1) The docstring of _riscv_supports_rvv still says the RVV path 'is compiled whenever __riscv_v_min_vlen is defined' and that it checks 'at least one supported zvl<N>b is advertised', which no longer matches the rewritten body (which now also delegates to a C++ compile-time check and excludes VLEN>=512). (2) A stale docstring that contradicts the implementation misleads future maintainers about the detection contract, especially given the VLEN 512/1024 exclusion and the C++ fallback. (3) Update the docstring to describe the three-branch logic: exclude zvl512/1024, accept zvl128/256, else delegate to torch.ops._C.cpu_attn_has_isa('rvv').

(1) The fallback catches a bare `except Exception` around the torch.ops call, which will also swallow the case where the op is not registered (older/non-CPU builds) as well as genuine runtime errors, always returning False. (2) This silently disables RVV whenever the op lookup fails for any reason, and hides real bugs (e.g. a mis-registered schema) behind a False return with no logging. (3) Consider logging at debug level in the except branch, or narrowing the caught exception, so that unexpected failures are diagnosable rather than silently treated as 'RVV unsupported'.

(1) The connector's get_num_new_matched_tokens now returns (None, False) to signal an in-flight async lookup, and both connector.py and scheduler.py updated the return annotation to tuple[int | None, bool]. However, the core scheduler at scheduler.py:724 consumes this return value (num_external_computed_tokens) and feeds it into arithmetic like num_computed_tokens + num_external_computed_tokens and block allocation. If the core Scheduler does not have a matching change to interpret None as 'defer/skip this request this step', a None will propagate into integer arithmetic and raise TypeError, or worse silently mis-allocate. (2) Risk: crashes or wrong scheduling whenever lookup_async=True and a lookup is in flight — the exact new code path this PR enables. (3) Fix: verify the core Scheduler's handling of a None hit-count exists in this repo state; if the None-handling lives in a separate PR, gate lookup_async so it cannot be enabled without scheduler support, and add an integration test that exercises the deferred path through Scheduler.schedule(), not just the connector unit level.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable lookup_async=True end-to-end and run a scheduler step with an in-flight lookup; assert no TypeError and that the request is retried on a later step.

findings.claim: (1) In LookupKeyClient.lookup, when non_block=True and the future is not yet done, the method returns None WITHOUT deleting self.futures[req_id] — correct. But the caller (scheduler.get_num_new_matched_tokens) is invoked once per request per scheduler step, and the future is only removed in the finally block after future.result() is read. If a request is aborted/finished while its lookup is still in flight (future never .done() from the caller's perspective), the only cleanup is via client.discard(req_id) called from build_connector_meta on finished_req_ids. However, get_num_new_matched_tokens can return (None, False) for a waiting request that is then dropped from the waiting queue without ever appearing in finished_req_ids (e.g. request rejected/aborted before scheduling). (2) Risk: self.futures leaks Future entries and their captured block_hashes indefinitely, a slow memory leak proportional to aborted-while-pending requests. (3) Fix: ensure discard() is invoked on every terminal transition (abort/reject) of a request that may have a pending lookup, or add periodic reaping of done-but-unconsumed futures.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Submit many non-blocking lookups then abort the requests before completion; assert client.futures does not grow unbounded.

findings.claim: (1) All ZMQ socket I/O (both _lookup and _reset) is now routed through a single-worker ThreadPoolExecutor, whereas previously lookup ran synchronously on the scheduler thread and reset synchronously on the caller thread. This is only correct if self.socket is exclusively touched by the executor thread now. reset() wraps _reset via executor.submit(...).result() — good — but confirm no other code path still calls the socket directly from the scheduler thread. Mixing a direct scheduler-thread socket send with an executor-thread send on the same non-threadsafe ZMQ socket would interleave frames and corrupt the request/response protocol (ZMQ sockets are not thread-safe). (2) Risk: silent protocol corruption / hangs on the lookup RPC channel. (3) Fix: assert/document that the socket is only ever accessed from the executor thread, and route any remaining direct socket users through the executor.

(1) reset() now does self.executor.submit(self._reset).result(), which blocks the calling (scheduler) thread until the single executor worker becomes free AND the RPC completes. If a slow async lookup is currently occupying the single-worker executor, reset() will block behind it (head-of-line blocking on max_workers=1). (2) Risk: reset_prefix_cache/reset_connector_cache latency spikes; the intended benefit of async lookup (never blocking the scheduler) is partially defeated on the reset path. (3) Fix: consider a dedicated executor or priority handling for admin/reset RPCs, or document that reset must be called only after lookups are drained (the reset_store docstring already assumes no in-flight lookups, so this may be acceptable — worth confirming).

(1) In lookup(), the except clause returns 0 on any exception (treated as 'no external hit'), while the finally always deletes self.futures[req_id]. This silently converts a transient lookup RPC failure into a full recompute for that request. That is a reasonable fallback, but note it also means a failed non-blocking lookup that had already returned None once will, on the retry, resubmit a fresh lookup only if the future was consumed+deleted — which it is here, so behavior is a re-lookup on next step. Confirm this is intended vs. permanently marking the request as miss. (2) Impact: extra recompute on transient errors. (3) Fix: consider distinguishing transient vs permanent failures, or at least log at warning with rate limiting since error() per-request could be noisy under a store outage.

(1) The signature of `async_tensor_h2d` in `vllm/utils/torch_utils.py` was reordered from `(data, dtype, device, pin_memory=PIN_MEMORY)` to `(data, device, dtype=None)` — `dtype` and `device` swapped positions. (2) Any caller using positional args now silently passes `dtype` where `device` is expected and vice-versa, producing wrong-device tensors or a crash (`t.to(device=<torch.dtype>, dtype=<torch.device>)`). In `vllm/v1/worker/gpu/buffer_utils.py` there are two positional calls: `async_tensor_h2d(self._staged_write_contents, self.dtype, self.device)` (StagedWriteTensor.apply_write) and `async_tensor_h2d(contents, torch.int32, self.device)` (FusedStagedWriter.apply). Both must be updated to keyword form. (3) Fix: audit all positional call sites and convert to `async_tensor_h2d(data, device=..., dtype=...)`; the CPU monkeypatch in `vllm/v1/worker/cpu/shm.py` (which redefines `async_tensor_h2d(data, dtype, device, pin_memory=False)` with the OLD order) must also be updated to match the new signature or it will break the CPU worker path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run CPU worker path (vllm/v1/worker/cpu) and StagedWriteTensor/FusedStagedWriter unit tests to confirm signature consistency after the reorder.

findings.claim: (1) In `MeanPool.forward` (`vllm/model_executor/layers/pooler/seqwise/methods.py`), the new code computes `output_size=int(prompt_lens_cpu.sum())` for `torch.repeat_interleave`. (2) `prompt_lens_cpu` is a CPU tensor, so `.sum()` runs on CPU and `int(...)` is a cheap host read — this is fine and actually avoids a GPU->CPU sync that would otherwise happen (data-dependent output length). This is the intended optimization. However, verify `prompt_lens_cpu` is guaranteed CPU-resident; if a future refactor makes it a GPU tensor, `int(prompt_lens_cpu.sum())` would introduce a device sync in the hot path. (3) Add an assertion `assert prompt_lens_cpu.is_cpu` to lock the invariant, preventing a silent sync regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark MeanPool.forward on a batch of variable-length sequences, comparing wall time and CUDA sync count before/after.

findings.claim: (1) The new `async_tensor_h2d` handles a `torch.Tensor` input via `t = data.pin_memory() if PIN_MEMORY else data` then `assert t.is_cpu`. If a caller passes a tensor that is already on GPU (device), `data.pin_memory()` will raise/behave incorrectly and the `assert t.is_cpu` fails. (2) Several new call sites pass tensors (e.g. `flex_attention._offsets_to_doc_ids_tensor`, `gdn_attn` spec masks, mamba2 lists) — these are CPU today, but the function now silently assumes CPU input and offers no graceful error for GPU tensors, unlike the old list-only contract. (3) Consider tightening the contract with a clear error message when a non-CPU tensor is passed, or handle the already-on-device case by early-returning `data` when `data.device == target device`.

(1) In `vllm/multimodal/inputs.py` `MultiModalBatchedField._reduce_data` and `MultiModalFlatField._reduce_data`, the new single-tensor path returns `out.pin_memory()` when `pin_memory` is True. The comment says 'pinning unpinned memory will make it contiguous', so it only calls `.contiguous()` first when the tensor is non-contiguous AND already pinned. (2) Relies on the implementation detail that `torch.Tensor.pin_memory()` produces a contiguous result for non-pinned inputs — this is true in current PyTorch but is an undocumented invariant; a downstream consumer assuming contiguity could silently break if PyTorch changes behavior. (3) Add a brief assertion or comment referencing the PyTorch behavior relied upon, or unconditionally ensure contiguity before returning to be safe.

(1) `PIN_MEMORY = is_pin_memory_available()` is now evaluated once at import time in `vllm/utils/torch_utils.py`, replacing the previous WSL-string check and consolidating the many `is_pin_memory_available()` call sites into a module constant. (2) This is a correct simplification, but it changes semantics from per-call evaluation to import-time evaluation; if `is_pin_memory_available()` depends on runtime state (e.g. platform init order or CUDA availability set after import), the cached constant could be stale. (3) Confirm `is_pin_memory_available()` is deterministic and safe to evaluate at `torch_utils` import time (no dependency on later platform/device initialization).

PDL primitive ordering appears inverted/unsafe in the Triton kernel. The added block calls `gdc_launch_dependents()` BEFORE `gdc_wait()`, and both are placed before the kernel loads its input `o_ptr` (the RoPE input). Correct PDL usage is: (1) `gdc_wait()` first to block until the producing kernel has finished writing the inputs this kernel reads, then (2) `gdc_launch_dependents()` after the kernel is done consuming/producing so successors can launch. The companion CUDA kernel in this same PR gets the order right (`griddepcontrol.wait` at entry, `griddepcontrol.launch_dependents` at exit). Emitting `launch_dependents` before `wait` (and before reading `o`) risks a read-after-write race: a dependent kernel may start (and even a producer overlap) while this kernel has not yet synchronized on its own producer, potentially reading stale/partially-written `o`. This is exactly the class of non-deterministic PDL race the sibling compressor.py comment warns about ('launch_pdl=True caused a read-after-write race and non-deterministic output'). Fix: place `gdc_wait()` before any input load, and move `gdc_launch_dependents()` to after the final `tl.store` (mirroring the .cu kernel). Also note the early-return padding branch (`pid_token >= num_tokens`) occurs after this block, so unlike the CUDA kernel there is no launch_dependents on the padding-return path — verify that is intended.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Correctness regression test comparing fused_inv_rope_fp8_quant output on SM90 with PDL enabled vs disabled across repeated runs (check determinism); plus e2e DeepSeek-V4 accuracy on Hopper.

findings.claim: The gating semantics for enabling PDL changed and may enable PDL in a context where the producing kernel does not emit the corresponding grid-dependency signal. Previously `launch_pdl=False` was always passed on CUDA (PDL effectively off). Now `use_gdc = current_platform.is_arch_support_pdl()` unconditionally enables PDL (`launch_pdl=True` + `USE_GDC=True`) on all SM90+ archs. PDL is only safe if the kernel that produces `o` (the attention output feeding this fused inv-RoPE+quant) also participates in programmatic stream serialization (emits launch_dependents) OR if this kernel's `gdc_wait()` correctly serializes against it. If the producer is a standard (non-PDL) launch, enabling PDL here provides no correctness benefit and, combined with the ordering issue above, can introduce a race. Fix: confirm the upstream producer kernel is PDL-aware, or document why gdc_wait alone is sufficient.
findings.suggested_benchmark: A/B determinism + accuracy test on Hopper for the DeepSeek-V4 attention -> fused_inv_rope_fp8_quant path with PDL on vs off.

findings.claim: Two new constexpr parameters serve overlapping roles with inconsistent naming and casing: `USE_GDC` (controls whether the kernel body emits GDC primitives) and `launch_pdl` (Triton runtime metadata). They are always set together (`USE_GDC=use_gdc` and `launch_pdl=True` iff `use_gdc`). The mixed convention (SCREAMING_SNAKE vs lower_snake) plus two flags encoding the same decision is confusing. Consider deriving `launch_pdl` from `USE_GDC` or documenting clearly why both must exist (one is a body constexpr, one is a Triton launch attribute). Not a correctness issue but reduces the chance of them drifting out of sync.

`_apply_pdl` accepts an `enable: bool = True` parameter and logs enabled/disabled, but the only call site passes `True` (guarded by `is_arch_support_pdl()`); there is no code path that calls it with `enable=False`. The disable branch and the boolean parameter are dead at present. This matches the rubric's 'unused new argument' concern — the arg has no exercised use path beyond the single True call. Either add the disable call path (e.g. explicitly disabling PDL on unsupported archs after import) or drop the parameter and the disable logging to avoid speculative generality. If deferred intentionally, add a TODO.

(1) The core fix (`take_until_marker` + `MarkerScanState`) is correct and well-targeted: it replaces `take_until(0.., MARKER)` — which re-scans the entire accumulated buffer from position 0 on every chunk (because `parse_into` re-runs `parse_buffered_event` over the whole undrained buffer after each `push_str`) — with a resumable scan that remembers the last inspected offset. (2) This turns per-tool-body scanning from O(n^2) to O(n) for a large tool body streamed across many chunks, which is the stated goal and matches the new `long_tool_call_body` benchmark. (3) No fix needed; this is a positive observation. Confidence is not asserting a defect. One residual verification item: the `take_until_marker` body itself was truncated from the diff and is a new function; its handling of an end-marker prefix straddling a chunk boundary (buffer ends mid-marker, remainder arrives next chunk) must not advance the saved scan offset past the partial-prefix region — the added test `qwen_coder_streaming_handles_end_token_split_across_chunks` exercises exactly this and should be replicated for the other 6 parsers (deepseek_dsml, glm_xml, hy_v3, minimax_m2, minimax_m3) which reuse the same primitive but only got the state-threading change, not a split-marker regression test.
findings.suggested_benchmark: cargo bench --bench qwen3_coder -- long_tool_call_body (compare parent vs this commit; also add analogous long-body split-chunk benches for deepseek_dsml/glm_xml/minimax so the O(n) claim is validated per-parser)

findings.claim: (1) The `MarkerScanState` is now embedded in mode enum variants (e.g. `ToolBlock { invoke_end_scan }`) and mutated via `&mut self.mode` during parsing, while `parse_buffered_event` uses `alt(...)` with backtracking. (2) Correctness risk: if any `alt` branch that runs *after* `take_until_marker` mutates the scan state and then backtracks, or if the buffer were ever drained/rolled back without resetting the scan offset, the saved offset would desync from the buffer and silently skip or duplicate marker bytes — producing wrong tool-call boundaries with no error. In the current code this appears safe because the scan state only advances within the single terminal `invoke_event`/`tool_call_event` branch and the buffer is not drained until the full event (including end marker) is consumed. (3) Recommend an explicit invariant comment near each `ToolBlock { *_scan }` variant documenting that the offset is only valid while the buffer prefix is unchanged, and asserting the scan state is reset when the mode transitions out of the tool block, to guard future edits. Applies equally to glm_xml/hy_v3/minimax_m2/minimax_m3/qwen_coder.

(1) The change swaps the weight_quant_key from kFp8StaticTokenSym (GroupShape.PER_TOKEN = (1,-1)) to kFp8StaticChannelSym (GroupShape.PER_CHANNEL = (-1,1)), but process_weights_after_loading still contains a leftover reshape guarded on the ACTIVATION key: `if self.activation_quant_key.scale.group_shape == GroupShape.PER_TOKEN: weight_scale = weight_scale.view(-1, 1)`. This branch reshapes the WEIGHT scale based on the ACTIVATION group shape, which is unrelated to the weight-key change and is a pre-existing correctness smell that now sits alongside a semantically different weight key. (2) Previously the weight was tagged PER_TOKEN (row=1) yet stored as one fp32 value per output row (length N) — the docstring correctly notes PER_CHANNEL (-1,1) is the semantically correct shape for a per-output-channel scale, so this direction of change is correct. Verify the per_channel path still produces a correctly-shaped weight_scale for both AITER pre-shuffled and non-AITER (e.g. cutlass/xpu) kernels, since those kernels branch on weight_quant_key membership sets that previously did not include kFp8StaticTokenSym either. (3) Confirm downstream kernel selectors (scaled_mm/xpu.py checks `weight_quant_key in {kFp8StaticChannelSym, kFp8StaticTensorSym}`) now correctly match; the old kFp8StaticTokenSym would have failed that membership check, so this change likely fixes rather than breaks XPU/cutlass selection — but the behavior change should be validated on non-ROCm backends that were exercising the per_channel path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a Quark per-channel FP8 checkpoint on both ROCm (AITER) and CUDA/XPU backends; assert weight_scale shape (N,) and correctness of logits vs. the pre-change kFp8StaticTokenSym behavior.

findings.claim: (1) This changes the weight_quant_key value (kFp8StaticTokenSym -> kFp8StaticChannelSym) that is passed into init_fp8_linear_kernel, which drives kernel dispatch. (2) Risk: any backend previously matching kFp8StaticTokenSym for Quark per-channel weights will now match kFp8StaticChannelSym instead, silently altering which GEMM kernel is selected on all platforms (not just ROCm/AITER). If a non-ROCm kernel had a code path keyed on the old token key it would now be unselected. (3) Fix: confirm no kernel selector depended on kFp8StaticTokenSym for the Quark per-channel weight case (grep shows kFp8StaticTokenSym had essentially no kernel-selector usages, so impact is likely limited to correcting mislabeling), and add/verify a per-channel Quark kernel-selection test across backends.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Regression test asserting kernel selection for QuarkW8A8Fp8 per_channel weights across CUDA, ROCm, and XPU backends.

findings.claim: (1) The new sparse-index cache is keyed only by layer type ("swa_only"/"c128a"), which assumes every layer of the same type in a decode step produces identical sparse indices. But the C128A path is not layer-invariant: `build_flashinfer_mixed_sparse_indices` consumes `attn_metadata.c128a_global_decode_topk_indices` / `c128a_prefill_topk_indices` / `c128a_decode_topk_lens`, which are per-layer topk selections computed by each layer's compressor/indexer. The C4A comment in flashmla.py explicitly states "C4A: local indices differ per layer (filled by Indexer)" — and the C128A topk indices are likewise per-layer (each layer's compressor writes its own topk into the FlashMLA metadata / topk_indices_buffer). Caching under a single "c128a" key means only the FIRST c128a layer's sparse indices are computed, and all ~60 subsequent c128a layers silently reuse those indices, attending to the wrong compressed KV rows. (2) This produces silently wrong attention outputs across all but the first layer of each cached type — model quality degradation with no error. (3) Confirm whether all same-type layers genuinely share identical topk indices within a step; if not (as the C4A comment implies for compressed indices), the cache must be keyed per-layer (e.g. include self.prefix) or restricted to only the truly layer-invariant SWA-only path. The author deliberately left C4A uncached, which is a strong signal the C128A topk indices are also per-layer and should not be cached either.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end DSv4 C128A correctness: compare generated logits/output of a multi-c128a-layer model with vs without the cache; assert bit-identical or within tolerance. If they diverge, the cache is unsound for c128a.

findings.claim: (1) The cache lifetime is tied to the `DeepseekSparseSWAMetadata` instance (a `field(default_factory=dict)` on the dataclass). The metadata builder creates a fresh metadata instance per `build()` call, so the cache is per-step for the eager path. However, under CUDA graph capture/replay the metadata object (and thus this dict) is captured once and reused across replays with different token contents. The sparse_indices tensors stored in the cache are freshly-allocated (via `build_flashinfer_mixed_sparse_indices`) and are NOT the persistent CUDA-graph buffers; on replay the cache returns stale tensors from the capture step rather than recomputing. (2) If a captured c128a layer hits the cache on replay, it would read the capture-time indices, but even if the builder itself runs, the returned tensor addresses are non-deterministic across steps, which conflicts with CUDA-graph fixed-address requirements noted throughout this codebase. (3) Verify the cache dict is guaranteed to be freshly empty at the start of every step (it is, since build() constructs a new metadata), and verify no cross-replay reuse of the cached tensors occurs inside a captured region; document this invariant.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DSv4 with full CUDA graphs enabled and vary batch composition across steps; assert cached-path outputs match the no-cache baseline.

findings.claim: (1) The cache key "swa_only" is a boolean-derived string while the sibling keys "c128a"/"c4a" are layer-type names; the parent module already defines canonical constants `_LAYER_TYPE_SWAONLY`/`_LAYER_TYPE_C4A`/`_LAYER_TYPE_C128A` in sparse_swa.py for exactly this purpose. (2) Using ad-hoc string literals here duplicates the layer-type taxonomy and risks drift (e.g. "swa_only" vs "swaonly"). (3) Reuse the existing `_layer_type_for(self.compress_ratio)` helper / `_LAYER_TYPE_*` constants to derive the cache key so the naming stays consistent with the tile-scheduler cache that already keys on the same taxonomy.

(1) The new `group=group` kwarg is now passed to the external `flashinfer_comm.create_allreduce_fusion_workspace(...)`. Older installed FlashInfer versions may not accept a `group` parameter and would raise `TypeError`. (2) Impact: on version mismatch the whole workspace init fails and the AR+RMS fusion is silently disabled (falling back to unfused allreduce), with only a generic non-multicast warning that does not hint at a version/API mismatch — making the perf regression hard to diagnose. (3) Fix: either pin/validate the minimum flashinfer version that supports the `group` kwarg, or special-case the `TypeError`/unexpected-keyword path in the existing `except` block with a clearer, actionable log message.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run AR+RMS fusion init against the minimum-supported flashinfer version to confirm `create_allreduce_fusion_workspace` accepts `group=`.

findings.claim: (1) `reset_compile_wrapper()` re-invokes `TorchCompileWithNoGuardsWrapper.__init__()` on an already-initialized wrapper (elastic-EP path), and the new `__init__` code assigns `self._bytecode_hook_handle = register_bytecode_hook(...)` without first removing any previously registered hook. (2) When `VLLM_USE_BYTECODE_HOOK` is enabled, each elastic-EP reset registers a fresh bytecode hook and orphans the prior one (the old handle is overwritten and can no longer be `.remove()`d), which is exactly the kind of hook-pinning leak this PR is trying to fix — it just reintroduces it on the reset path. (3) In `__init__`, before registering, remove any existing handle: `if getattr(self, '_bytecode_hook_handle', None) is not None: self._bytecode_hook_handle.remove()` (or call `self.cleanup()`), then register the new hook.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable VLLM_USE_BYTECODE_HOOK, trigger repeated reset_compile_wrapper() (elastic EP scale up/down) and count registered bytecode hooks / GPU memory over iterations.

findings.claim: (1) The finalizer is registered only inside the `if not multiprocess_mode:` block (immediately after `self.model_executor = ...`), so the cleanup relies on that branch. This is correct for the leak (in MP mode the model lives in a subprocess), but note that `weakref.finalize(self, _cleanup_instance_caches, model)` holds a strong reference to `model` for the lifetime of the engine. (2) This is intentional and required (the finalizer must be able to touch the model after `self` is gone), and it does not create a cycle that blocks collection of `self`; however it does mean the model object cannot be freed by any path other than engine finalization even if `model_executor` is cleared earlier. (3) No change required, but consider documenting that the finalizer intentionally pins the model until engine GC, so future refactors that try to release the executor early are not surprised.

(1) `_get_driver_model_for_cleanup()` walks `model_executor.driver_worker.model_runner.model` via chained `getattr` with defaults. If any executor backend names these attributes differently or nests the model runner under a different path, the method silently returns None and the bytecode hooks are never cleaned up (leak reappears with no error). (2) This makes the fix silently ineffective on executor layouts other than the uniproc/driver-worker one, which is hard to notice because it fails open rather than raising. (3) Consider asserting/logging (debug) when `model is None` but bytecode hooks are expected (VLLM_USE_BYTECODE_HOOK set), or route cleanup through `collective_rpc`/`apply_model` which already abstracts driver-worker access.

(1) The MoE regime gate `_mxfp8_moe_tiles(T)` selects tiles based on `T` (number of input tokens), but the grouped GEMM actually operates on `M = T * top_k` routed rows, and grid m-dimension is `cdiv(sorted_token_ids.shape[0], block_m)`. (2) For a T just below the 1024 threshold with large top_k (e.g. T=512, top_k=8 => 4096 routed rows), the decode tiles (block_m=64, block_n=64, num_warps=4) are chosen for what is effectively a prefill-sized GEMM, potentially leaving throughput on the table on gfx950 — the linear kernel by contrast gates on the true row count M. (3) Consider gating on `M = T * top_k` (or `sorted_token_ids.shape[0]`) for consistency with the linear-kernel threshold, or document why token-count is the intended regime signal.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare MoE grouped-GEMM latency on gfx950 at T in {512, 1024} with top_k=8, sweeping decode-vs-prefill tiles to confirm the T-based cutoff picks the faster config.

findings.claim: (1) The three new tile params (`block_n`, `num_warps`, `num_stages`) are threaded through `_grouped_gemm_mxfp8` as loose keyword args and both call sites splat the same `tiles[...]` dict entries. (2) This is verbose and repetitive across the two GEMM call sites, increasing the chance the two calls drift apart. (3) Consider passing the `tiles` dict (or a small dataclass) directly to `_grouped_gemm_mxfp8` to keep the launch-config in one object. Minor; behavior is correct as written.

(1) The new prefill tile uses BLOCK_N=256 for the linear kernel and BLOCK_M=128; `dot_scaled` on CDNA4 has MFMA/microscaling tile-shape constraints and register-pressure limits with num_stages=2. (2) If a shape/tile combination fails to compile or silently falls back at runtime, it would only surface on gfx950 hardware not exercised by generic CI, risking a regression that is hard to catch. (3) Add a lightweight numerical/compile smoke test on gfx950 (or an assert that N/M tiling is valid) so the tuned tiles are validated in CI where the hardware is available. The Python logic itself is correct (masking handles arbitrary N/M).
findings.suggested_benchmark: On gfx950, run _mxfp8_dot_scaled_linear across the tuned shapes (8k/1k, 1k/1k) and assert bit/tolerance match vs the bf16 dequant fallback.

findings.claim: (1) FusedStagedWriter sizes its UVA buffer pools to max_writes = num_kv_cache_groups * max_num_reqs (see block_table.py __init__), but append_block_ids appends one staged write per group per request per call and staged writes accumulate until apply_staged_writes() clears them. If append_block_ids is called more than once per request between two apply_staged_writes() calls (e.g. multiple chunk appends in a step), the number of staged writes per group can exceed max_num_reqs. (2) copy_to_uva does dst[:n] = x with n = len(group_ids); if n > max_writes the CPU-side assignment silently indexes past the allocated buffer size or raises, corrupting/crashing the write path. (3) Either size the pool to the true worst-case number of staged writes, add an assert len(group_ids) <= max_writes, or coalesce multiple appends per request before staging.

(1) FusedStagedWriter.apply hardcodes contents_gpu = async_tensor_h2d(contents, torch.int32, self.device) and its group_ids/indices/starts/cu_lens pools are all torch.int32, whereas StagedWriteTensor supports int32/int64/float32 via self.dtype. (2) This makes FusedStagedWriter silently incorrect if used for a StagedWriteTensor whose dtype is int64 or float32 (block tables are int32 today, so it is currently fine, but the class is written as a general 'applies staged writes of several StagedWriteTensors' utility). (3) Derive the contents dtype from the tensors' dtype and assert all tensors share a dtype, or document that FusedStagedWriter is int32-only.

(1) apply() builds Python lists (group_ids, indices, starts, contents, cu_lens) via per-write extend loops in Python before the H2D copy. (2) This adds host-side Python overhead proportional to total staged content length per step, which can partially offset the kernel-fusion win the PR targets, and the .extend(content_base + cu_len for ...) generator runs in Python per element. (3) Consider pre-reserving list capacity or accumulating counts to bound the host work; benchmark the CPU-side prep time against the saved kernel-launch time for realistic num_kv_cache_groups.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark apply_staged_writes() host+device time for num_kv_cache_groups in {2,4,8} vs the pre-change per-table loop.

findings.claim: (1) `has_pending_push_work()` returns `bool(self._jobs) or self.manager.has_pending_work()`, but `self._jobs` tracks in-flight *transfer jobs* (loads/stores whose completion is reported by workers), NOT yet-created store jobs waiting to be built. The removed idle-flush code used `self._jobs.keys()` to flush; the new code relies on the engine continuing to step whenever `_jobs` is non-empty so those jobs get completed via `update_connector_output`. This works only if a pending store that has been *created* (and thus is in `_jobs`) will actually be completed by a future worker step even when no requests are scheduled. Verify that `build_connector_meta()` re-emits pending store/load jobs (e.g. via `_current_batch_jobs_to_flush` / `_build_store_jobs`) on these idle steps; otherwise `_jobs` stays non-empty forever, `has_pending_push_work()` never goes False, and the engine spins in a busy-loop (100% CPU) on idle. (2) A permanent busy-loop that never lets the engine idle is a severe throughput/energy regression and can starve other engines. (3) Add/verify a test that asserts the engine eventually stops stepping (has_pending_push_work returns False) after all deferred stores drain, and confirm idle steps make forward progress on `_jobs` rather than merely re-observing them.
findings.suggested_benchmark: With OffloadingConnector, submit one request that finishes with pending (incomplete) store transfers, then stop submitting. Measure whether the engine core loop keeps stepping indefinitely (CPU spin) vs. draining and idling; assert has_pending_push_work() transitions to False.

findings.claim: (1) The old code force-flushed *all* pending jobs (`self._jobs.keys()`) into `_current_batch_jobs_to_flush` when all tracked requests finished, guaranteeing their src GPU blocks were flushed before the KV cache manager could reallocate them. The new approach removes this and instead relies on the engine continuing to step. But the flush served a *correctness* purpose beyond completion: on the final step a finished request's blocks lose ref_cnt protection and can be re-allocated; without the flush, a store src block may be overwritten before the store's GPU->CPU copy is issued/completed. The PR keeps `_block_id_to_pending_jobs` (the reallocation-protection mechanism) but the tests now assert `_block_id_to_pending_jobs` is *non-empty* right after finish (previously it was empty because of the flush). Verify that on subsequent idle steps `build_connector_meta` still flushes jobs registered in `_block_id_to_pending_jobs` when their block is reallocated, so no store reads a stale/overwritten GPU block. (2) Missing flush-on-reallocation would corrupt offloaded KV (silent wrong cache hits later). (3) Confirm the reallocation-triggered flush path (top of build_connector_meta computing per-block pending jobs) still fires on idle steps with no scheduled requests.
findings.suggested_benchmark: test_fence_at_update_state_after_alloc / test_fence_at_build_store_jobs style: finish a request with incomplete transfers, reset prefix cache, reallocate the same GPU block in a new request, and assert the pending store is flushed before reallocation reuse.

findings.claim: (1) The method is named `has_pending_push_work` (mirroring the NIXL push connector), but for the offloading connector the pending work includes both store (push) and *load* (pull/promotion) jobs — `self._jobs` holds loads too, and `manager.has_pending_work()` includes secondary->primary promotions. (2) The name understates what keeps the engine stepping, which can mislead future maintainers into assuming only stores gate idle-stepping. (3) Consider a docstring note (already partially present) explicitly stating loads/promotions are also covered, or a more general name; low priority since it matches an existing base-class contract.

(1) The line `permute_indices = (permute_indices + rows // 2) % rows` silently folds the previously-explicit `swap_w13_to_w31(w13_weight)` (a full [w1;w3] -> [w3;w1] half-swap) into index arithmetic, but there is no comment explaining this. The old flow had a clear comment 'Swap halves to arrange as [w3; w1] (kernel expectation)'. (2) Without a comment, a future reader cannot tell that this cyclic row shift by rows//2 is equivalent to the removed swap, and could break the [w3;w1] kernel layout contract when refactoring. (3) Add a short comment noting this offset replaces swap_w13_to_w31 for the gated up/gate halves (equivalent to a cyclic row shift of rows//2 since there are exactly two halves).

(1) The saturation/backoff guard `prefill_capacity_bound` is only assigned inside the `if not defer_prefills:` block at the end of the waiting-queue processing (scheduler.py ~line 984), guarded further by an outer `if step_skipped_waiting:` context. This means the flag is only *updated* on non-throttled (release) steps, and its update depends on the surrounding control flow having entered the waiting section. (2) Risk: if a release step never enters the waiting-queue processing block (e.g. token_budget exhausted by running/decode requests before reaching the waiting loop, or `self.waiting` was already empty at loop entry), `prefill_capacity_bound` retains a stale value from a prior step. A stale `True` value permanently disables throttling (defeating DP balancing); a stale `False` value can leave prefills deferred while the queue keeps growing, delaying TTFT. The value should reflect the actual state of `self.waiting` after every release step regardless of which code paths executed. (3) Fix: compute `prefill_capacity_bound = bool(self.waiting)` unconditionally on every non-deferred step, outside the `if step_skipped_waiting:` block, so it can never go stale.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DP run with mixed decode-heavy + bursty prefill load; assert throttling re-engages after a capacity-bound step drains, and that prefill_capacity_bound is not stale across steps that skip the waiting loop.

findings.claim: (1) `defer_prefills` is gated on `any(not r.is_prefill_chunk for r in self.running)` — i.e. throttling only engages if at least one running request is a pure decode. But `prefill_capacity_bound` is set from `bool(self.waiting)` on release steps independently of whether any decode is running. On the *next* throttled step, if the running set contains only prefill chunks (no decodes), `defer_prefills` becomes False and the in-flight prefill chunk continues — which is the intended 'never waste a step' behavior, but the interaction with a stale-True `prefill_capacity_bound` means the guard-disable path and the no-decode-to-protect path can silently mask each other during debugging. (2) Risk: the DP-alignment invariant (prefills aligned across all DP ranks) can be violated if one rank has decodes running and another does not on the same cadence step, since the two ranks make different defer decisions from identical step_counter. This produces exactly the per-step forward-time imbalance the feature aims to remove. (3) Fix: document/verify that the alignment guarantee only holds when all DP ranks share the same running-composition, or make the defer decision independent of local running composition (rely solely on cadence + capacity guard).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Two DP ranks with asymmetric decode load; measure per-step forward time variance with prefill_schedule_interval=4 vs 1.

findings.claim: (1) In `DPEngineCoreProc._should_throttle_prefills`, throttling uses `self.step_counter % self.prefill_schedule_interval != 0`, and the comment claims 'On a fresh wave the counter is 0, so prefills are admitted immediately after idle.' However `step_counter` is incremented per forward pass and (per the existing comment) synchronizes finished with DP peers every N steps — it is not reset to 0 on idle/fresh-wave transitions unless separate logic resets it. (2) Risk: if step_counter is not actually reset on idle, the 'admitted immediately after idle' claim is false and the first prefill of a new wave may be delayed by up to interval-1 steps, adding TTFT latency after idle periods. (3) Fix: verify step_counter reset-on-idle behavior exists; if not, either reset it when the engine goes idle or update the comment to reflect actual behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DP engine idle -> single request; measure TTFT with prefill_schedule_interval=4 and confirm it is not delayed by cadence.

findings.claim: (1) `SchedulerInterface.schedule` signature changed from `schedule(self)` to `schedule(self, throttle_prefills: bool = False)`. (2) Risk: any third-party or out-of-tree Scheduler subclass overriding `schedule(self)` will still work when called positionally with the new arg because Python will raise a TypeError (extra positional arg) — the caller in core.py now always passes the argument positionally (`self.scheduler.schedule(self._should_throttle_prefills())`). A subclass whose override does not accept the parameter will break at runtime. (3) Fix: this is a mild API contract change in a public-ish interface; acceptable given the default, but note that subclasses must be updated. Consider keyword-only or **kwargs tolerance if external schedulers are supported.

(1) The PR removes `SlidingWindowManager.free()` override, so SWA now falls back to the base-class `SingleTypeKVCacheManager.free()`, which passes ALL request blocks (both hashed/cached and unhashed/scratch) in reverse allocation order to `block_pool.free_blocks()`. The new `free_blocks()` will then split them into hashed vs. unhashed and prepend all unhashed ones, append all hashed. (2) This changes SWA semantics: previously the removed override iterated `reversed(req_blocks)` and split cached/uncached separately; now the ordering guarantee for SWA-specific reuse is delegated to `free_blocks`. This is likely intentional and equivalent since `free_blocks` now performs the same hashed/unhashed partition, but for SWA the request's `req_to_blocks` contains null blocks (skipped windows) padded in. Verify that null blocks in SWA req_blocks are correctly ignored by `free_blocks` (they are, via `not block.is_null`), and that `num_cached_block.pop` — which the removed override did explicitly — is still handled. The base `free()` calls `pop_blocks_for_free()` which does `self.num_cached_block.pop(request_id, None)`, so this is covered. (3) Confirm via test that SWA free path is exercised; the removed override is now dead-equivalent — acceptable, but ensure no SWA-specific ordering (e.g. deferred null handling) is lost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: SWA prefix-caching eviction-order test (test_sliding_window_*) verifying free queue order after freeing a request with skipped windows

findings.claim: (1) The new `free_blocks` always calls both `prepend_n(blocks_without_hash)` and `append_n(blocks_with_hash)` unconditionally, even when one list is empty. `prepend_n`/`append_n` early-return on empty input so functional correctness is preserved, but the previous single-pass code did one queue operation; now every free does two linked-list splice operations plus builds two intermediate lists. (2) `free_blocks` is on the hot request-teardown path (every finished/preempted request). For the common full-attention case where all freed blocks are hashed, this adds an extra empty-list call and an extra list allocation per free. (3) Minor: guard the empty case is already handled internally, so impact is small; primary cost is the two per-call list allocations (`blocks_with_hash`, `blocks_without_hash`) replacing the single list comprehension. Consider whether the extra partition cost is justified; likely negligible but worth a microbenchmark given free_blocks is in benchmark_block_pool.py.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmarks/benchmark_block_pool.py free_blocks throughput before/after

findings.claim: (1) In `remove_skipped_blocks`, the previous code split removed blocks into cached vs uncached and freed uncached with `prepend=True` so scratch blocks were the next allocation candidates, cached blocks stayed behind them. The new code collects all removed blocks into one `removed_blocks` list (still in reverse index order, i-th descending) and relies on `free_blocks` to re-partition by hash and prepend the unhashed. (2) The ordering within `blocks_without_hash` now depends on iteration order in `free_blocks`, which iterates `ordered_blocks` and appends unhashed in encountered order, then `prepend_n` places them at the head preserving that order. Previously `remove_skipped_blocks` built `removed_uncached_blocks` in descending-i order and prepended. Verify the resulting head-of-queue order of scratch blocks is unchanged for SWA recycling correctness — a subtle reordering of scratch-block reuse could change which physical block gets reused first but should not affect correctness (all scratch, no cache value). Low risk but the ordering is now less explicit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: SWA remove_skipped_blocks eviction-order unit test

findings.claim: (1) The comment 'Blocks without hash always get evicted first - prepend them last to the tail' is confusing/contradictory: prepending puts blocks at the HEAD (front) of the free queue, not 'to the tail', and the head is the LRU/next-eviction end. (2) A misleading comment about eviction ordering in cache-management code is a likely source of future bugs since prepend vs append semantics are already subtle. (3) Reword to e.g. 'Unhashed blocks carry no prefix-cache value, so put them at the front (next to be reused/evicted); hashed blocks go to the tail so they persist as prefix-cache entries longer.'

(1) This PR removes AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer and replaces it with plain make_async(self._encode/_decode) wrappers, so each encode/decode request is now dispatched to the thread pool individually with no micro-batching. The removed class aggregated up to max_batch_size (32) concurrent requests within a 2ms window and issued a single batched tokenizer(prompts) / batch_decode(token_ids_list) call. (2) Under concurrent online serving (many simultaneous requests hitting the tokenize/completions path), losing batching increases per-token tokenizer overhead and thread-pool contention, and can regress throughput/latency exactly in the high-concurrency regime the batching was introduced for (see #34789 'Offload blocking tokenizer ops'). (3) If the removal is intentional (batching gave little benefit in practice / added complexity), the PR description should quantify the throughput impact and confirm with a concurrent-load benchmark before merging; otherwise the batching path should be retained.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Online serving benchmark (benchmark_serving) with high concurrency (e.g. 200+ concurrent short requests) on the /tokenize and /v1/completions endpoints, comparing request throughput and P99 tokenization latency before vs after this change.

findings.claim: (1) The public-looking method get_async_tokenizer() on BaseRenderer is removed, along with the AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer export from vllm.utils.async_utils. (2) While no in-tree callers remain (grep shows only base.py itself), these are importable symbols that downstream/plugin code or forks may reference; removing them without a deprecation shim is a breaking API change. (3) Consider keeping a thin deprecated get_async_tokenizer() alias (or documenting the removal in release notes) if backward compatibility for external callers is a concern; confidence is low because these appear to be internal helpers.

(1) The new _encode/_decode wrappers call self.get_tokenizer() on every invocation, and make_async is bound in __init__ over self._encode. (2) get_tokenizer() re-fetches self.tokenizer and re-checks the None guard on every encode/decode call inside the thread; this is minor per-call overhead. (3) Functionally correct, but the indirection (make_async(self._encode) where _encode just forwards to get_tokenizer().encode) could be simplified; this is cosmetic and does not affect correctness.

(1) The reshape-to-2D fix only reshapes allreduce_in, residual, and norm_out, but NOT quant_out and scale_out, even though these are also declared in mutates_args and passed to flashinfer_comm.allreduce_fusion (in the FP8/FP4 quant patterns that route through this same call_trtllm_fused_allreduce_norm function). (2) If the transformers backend passes a >2D input into a quant pattern, allreduce_in becomes 2D while quant_out/scale_out keep their original rank, producing a shape mismatch between the reduced input and the quantized output buffers — either a runtime error inside flashinfer or a silently wrong quantized result. (3) Either also reshape quant_out (and reconcile scale_out's expected layout) inside the dim()!=2 branch, or assert that quant patterns are never entered with dim()!=2, or restrict this codepath to the non-quant kARResidualRMSNorm pattern_code.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the AR+RMSNorm+FP8/FP4 quant fusion patterns with a 3D (batch, seq, hidden) input via the transformers backend and verify quant_out/scale_out are written correctly.

findings.claim: (1) The fix uses tensor.view(-1, hidden) to flatten the outer batch dim, but .view() requires the tensor to be contiguous (or view-compatible); it raises RuntimeError otherwise. The op outputs by in-place mutation, so replacing .view() with .reshape() is not a safe blanket fix because reshape may return a copy for non-contiguous inputs, which would break the mutation-visibility contract (flashinfer would write into the copy, not the caller's tensor). (2) If the transformers backend ever passes a non-contiguous >2D allreduce_in/residual/norm_out, either .view() throws or a naive reshape silently drops the mutated results. (3) Add an explicit contiguity assertion (or .contiguous() handling with copy-back) at this boundary, and document the assumption that these tensors are contiguous.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Feed a non-contiguous 3D input (e.g. a transposed/sliced hidden-state view) through the transformers-backend AR+RMSNorm path and confirm behavior.

findings.claim: (1) The reshape derives hidden from allreduce_in.shape[-1] and applies the same view(-1, hidden) to residual and norm_out, implicitly assuming all three share identical leading dims and hidden size. (2) If residual/norm_out ever differ in shape from allreduce_in (unexpected but not guarded), .view() will fail or reshape incorrectly, with no clear error message. (3) Consider asserting residual.shape == allreduce_in.shape (and norm_out.shape == allreduce_in.shape when provided) before the view, to make the shape contract explicit at this parallelism boundary.

(1) This PR adds an unrelated environment variable VLLM_RPC_TIMEOUT to the environment_variables dict, in a PR whose stated scope is fastsafetensors ParallelLoader weight loading. VLLM_RPC_TIMEOUT does not exist anywhere in the parent tree (confirmed via grep). (2) Introducing an unrelated, undocumented-in-scope env var risks a name collision or duplicate registration if another concurrent change also introduces it, and pollutes the fastsafetensors PR with orphaned behavior that no code reads (see separate unused-variable finding). It looks like a bad merge/rebase artifact. (3) Remove VLLM_RPC_TIMEOUT from this PR and land it in its own change where a consumer actually reads it.

(1) VLLM_RPC_TIMEOUT is added to the runtime environment_variables dict but is NOT declared in the TYPE_CHECKING type-stub block at the top of envs.py (only VLLM_FASTSAFETENSORS_QUEUE_SIZE was added there). (2) Every other variable in this module has a paired type annotation in the TYPE_CHECKING block; omitting it breaks the static-typing / IDE-autocomplete contract of the module and diverges from the established convention, and static checkers (mypy) will flag envs.VLLM_RPC_TIMEOUT accesses as unknown attributes. (3) Add `VLLM_RPC_TIMEOUT: int = 10000` to the TYPE_CHECKING block alongside the other declarations.

(1) The newly introduced VLLM_RPC_TIMEOUT has no reader in the tree (grep for VLLM_RPC_TIMEOUT / RPC_TIMEOUT at parent returns no matches, and this PR adds no consumer). (2) Per the Mandatory Check for unused new identifiers, an env var that is registered but never read is dead configuration surface: it advertises a knob to users that does nothing.
