(3) Either wire it into the zmq client code that the docstring describes, or drop it from this PR.

(1) The GDS-fallback logic only retries with nogds=True when the RuntimeError is caught before any tensor is yielded (guarded by `yielded`). However ParallelLoader uses a background producer thread; a GDS failure raised inside that thread may surface asynchronously only after several tensors have already been yielded to the consumer, in which case `yielded` is True and the code re-raises instead of falling back. (2) This is exactly the mid-stream failure the comment says it wants to avoid restarting on, but on a filesystem with no GDS support the original non-pipelined code retried per-file-sublist and could recover; now a late GDS error hard-fails the whole load. Whether this is a regression depends on ParallelLoader raising GDS errors eagerly (at construction / first shard) vs lazily. (3) Confirm ParallelLoader raises GDS-registration errors before the first yield; if not, detect GDS availability up front (probe) rather than relying on catching a RuntimeError mid-iteration.

(1) In the nogds fallback path, `yield from pl.iterate_weights()` is executed while still inside the outer try whose finally does `pl.close()`. This is correct, but note the second loader is created with keyword `nogds=True` while the first is created positionally as `_make_loader(nogds)`; the helper signature `_make_loader(nogds: bool)` accepts both, so no bug, just an inconsistency worth aligning for readability. (2) Minor readability/consistency issue only. (3) Call `_make_loader(True)` or `_make_loader(nogds=nogds)` consistently.

(1) VLLM_FASTSAFETENSORS_QUEUE_SIZE defaults to 0, which per the docstring preserves the non-pipelined footprint and therefore delivers NO pipelining/overlap benefit out of the box. (2) The stated performance motivation of the PR (overlap producing the next shard's device buffer with the consumer copy) is inactive unless users manually set the env var; most users will see identical performance to before while paying the added code complexity. (3) Consider defaulting to 1 (small, one extra shard-sized buffer) so the overlap benefit is realized by default, or document clearly in release notes that opt-in is required.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model load wall-time for a large sharded safetensors model (e.g. 70B) with VLLM_FASTSAFETENSORS_QUEUE_SIZE=0 vs 1 vs 2, and record peak load-time VRAM for each.

findings.claim: (1) The refactor makes weight separation lazy: `other_weights` and `expert_scale_weights` are now populated as a side effect of the `regular_language_model_weights()` generator, which is only fully consumed after `loader.load_weights(regular_language_model_weights())` runs to exhaustion. However, the code that consumes `expert_scale_weights` and `other_weights` (the `if expert_scale_weights:` block at ~line 1270 and the `_load_other_weights(other_weights, ...)` call at ~line 1276) relies on those lists being populated. This ordering is correct ONLY IF `AutoWeightsLoader.load_weights` truly consumes the input generator to exhaustion before returning. If AutoWeightsLoader ever short-circuits (e.g. stops early once all named parameters are matched, or on the first StopIteration of an inner iterator), the generator will be partially consumed and `other_weights`/`expert_scale_weights` will be silently incomplete — vision/projector and expert-scale weights would be dropped, producing a silently mis-initialized model. (2) Impact: silent wrong weights / degraded or broken model output on Llama4 multimodal + ModelOpt checkpoints, hard to detect. (3) Fix: verify AutoWeightsLoader.load_weights is documented/guaranteed to exhaust its iterator; if not, force exhaustion explicitly (e.g. materialize into a generator wrapper that asserts full consumption) or restructure so the side-effect buffers do not depend on undocumented consumption semantics.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a Llama4 multimodal (mllama4) ModelOpt FP8 checkpoint and assert all vision/projector and expert-scale params are present in updated_params after load_weights; compare to pre-refactor loaded param set.

findings.claim: (1) The public/private helper methods `_separate_and_rename_weights` and `_handle_expert_scale_broadcasting` are removed entirely rather than kept as thin shims. (2) Impact: any subclass or external code overriding or calling these methods breaks. These are underscore-prefixed private helpers on a model class, so external dependency is unlikely, but Megatron/vLLM model classes are sometimes subclassed for custom checkpoint handling. (3) Fix: low risk given naming convention; acceptable, but confirm no in-tree subclass references remain (grep across repo).

(1) Typo in the new comment: 'Stream thelanguage-model weights' — missing space between 'the' and 'language-model'. (2) Impact: minor readability, but the comment is load-bearing documentation explaining subtle lazy-consumption ordering, so clarity matters. (3) Fix: correct to 'Stream the language-model weights'.

(1) The correctness of the streaming refactor hinges on an undocumented behavioral contract of AutoWeightsLoader (that it fully drains its input iterator). The inline comment asserts this ('runs to exhaustion, so ... are fully populated as a side effect') but there is no runtime assertion enforcing it. (2) Impact: a future change to AutoWeightsLoader could silently break weight loading with no test failure at this call site. (3) Fix: add a lightweight sentinel/assertion (e.g. a flag set at the generator's final yield / after the for-loop) checked before consuming other_weights/expert_scale_weights, so partial consumption fails loudly.

(1) Ordering issue: the XPU branch is now checked first via `current_platform.is_xpu()`, but on the existing CUDA path the code previously short-circuited with `if not current_platform.is_cuda(): return None`. The new logic uses an if/elif/else where the final `else: return None` covers ROCm/CPU. This is functionally equivalent for CUDA and correctly returns None for other platforms, so no behavior regression here. However, the CUDA `get_device_capability()==None` -> return None path is preserved. This appears correct. Low real risk; noting for completeness that behavior for non-XPU/non-CUDA remains 'return None' as before. (2)/(3) No fix needed — verified equivalent.

(1) `SequenceParallelismPass` is now imported when `current_platform.is_cuda_alike() or current_platform.is_xpu()`, but in `configure()` the SP block also chains `AsyncTPPass` when `self.pass_config.fuse_gemm_comms` is True (see `if self.pass_config.enable_sp: ... if self.pass_config.fuse_gemm_comms: self.passes += [AsyncTPPass(config)]`). `AsyncTPPass` is only imported under `if current_platform.is_cuda():`. On XPU, `AsyncTPPass` is therefore undefined. (2) If XPU ever reaches that branch with `fuse_gemm_comms=True`, it raises NameError at configure time. XPU disables `fuse_gemm_comms` in xpu.py's `check_and_update_config`, so this is normally safe, but the coupling is fragile and relies on that disable staying in place. (3) Guard the `AsyncTPPass` usage with a platform/import check, or import it in the XPU branch too, to avoid a latent NameError.

(1) The XPU thresholds (min_hidden_size=4096, min_per_gpu_size_mb=8.0) are hardcoded inline in the function body, while CUDA uses the module-level `SP_MIN_HIDDEN_SIZE` / `SP_MIN_PER_GPU_SIZE_MB` dicts. The test file duplicates these as `_XPU_MIN_HIDDEN_SIZE`/`_XPU_MIN_PER_GPU_SIZE_MB` with a comment 'must match sequence_parallelism.py values', creating a manual-sync hazard. (2) If XPU thresholds are tuned later, the inline constants and test constants can drift silently. (3) Move XPU thresholds into named module-level constants (or the existing dicts keyed by a platform sentinel) and import them in the test.

(1) Removing `"enable_sp": "Sequence parallelism"` from `fusion_passes_to_disable` now enables SP on XPU by default at O2/O3 (where `enable_sp: IS_DENSE`). This is the intended feature, but it silently changes behavior for existing XPU users running dense models at O2+ — SP will now be applied where it previously was force-disabled. (2) Users relying on the prior implicit disable may see different numerics/perf. (3) Confirm SP correctness on XPU (reduce_scatter/all_gather via xccl custom-op collectives) is validated end-to-end, not just the threshold helper; add an integration test beyond the pure-Python threshold unit tests.

(1) The WSL2 cpu-offload warning string escapes percent signs as "%%" (e.g. "roughly 50%% of physical RAM") in a `logger.warning(...)` call that is invoked with a single pre-formatted string and NO positional args. (2) Python's logging only performs %-substitution when args are supplied; with zero args the message is emitted verbatim, so users will see the literal "50%%" and "%%USERPROFILE%%\.wslconfig" instead of "50%" / "%USERPROFILE%". This is cosmetically wrong and confusing in a user-facing safety warning. (3) Either drop the doubled percents (write "50%" and "%USERPROFILE%") or keep them only if the code is refactored to use lazy %-formatting with args.

(1) The same warning contains a grammatical error: "pinned memory ... default (limit can changed via ...)" is missing a verb (should be "can be changed"). (2) A garbled sentence in a prominent host-stability safety warning reduces clarity for the exact users who need to act on it. (3) Fix to "limit can be changed via %USERPROFILE%\.wslconfig".

(1) The new WSL2 warning about cpu-offload + pinned memory is gated on `bool(vllm_config.compilation_config.cudagraph_mode)`, but the underlying requirement — pinned/UVA memory for cpu_offload_gb>0 — applies regardless of CUDA graphs. Meanwhile CudaPlatformBase.is_pin_memory_available() returns False on WSL2 unless VLLM_WSL2_ENABLE_PIN_MEMORY=1, and is_uva_available() depends on pin memory. (2) A user who sets --cpu-offload-gb>0 on WSL2 without setting VLLM_WSL2_ENABLE_PIN_MEMORY=1 (and without cudagraphs) gets NO warning yet UVA offloading will silently be unavailable (is_uva_available()==False), potentially causing a confusing downstream failure or a silent fallback rather than an actionable message. (3) Consider warning/erroring whenever in_wsl() and cpu_offload_gb>0 and not VLLM_WSL2_ENABLE_PIN_MEMORY, independent of cudagraph_mode, so the offload requirement is surfaced to the user.

(1) `in_wsl()` is now decorated with `@functools.cache`. (2) The benchmark (benchmark_pin_memory.py) relies on being able to flip pin-memory availability at runtime via cache_clear() on is_pin_memory_available/is_uva_available, but in_wsl() has no such cache_clear escape hatch; if a test process ever needs to vary WSL detection (or a subprocess mocks platform.uname), the cached value cannot be reset. In production uname is static so caching is safe, but the cache is process-global and untestable. (3) Low risk; if runtime override of WSL detection is ever needed, expose in_wsl.cache_clear() or gate the mock before first call. Otherwise fine as-is.

(1) `_get_wsl_kernel_version` parses only the first 3 dotted components of the release string and uses tuple comparison against (4,19,121). If the kernel release has fewer than 3 numeric segments (e.g. "5.15-microsoft"), the resulting tuple (e.g. (5,15)) compares as (5,15) < (4,19,121) -> False, which is fine, but a malformed/short release with a low prefix could mis-classify. The broad `except Exception: return None` then treats parse failures as unsupported (safe). (2) Edge-case robustness only; behavior on failure is conservative (disables pin memory), so risk is low. (3) No change strictly required; optionally pad to 3 elements or document the assumption.

(1) The `q_start_pos` computation was changed from `casual ? (seq_len - q_token_num) : 0` to unconditionally `seq_len - q_token_num` in both the scheduler tile-position loops (lines ~447 and ~487) and the main loop (line ~1596). (2) For the non-causal (encoder/bidirectional) path, this alters query position semantics: previously non-causal queries were positioned starting at 0; now they start at `seq_len - q_token_num`. For pure encoder attention where `seq_len == q_token_num` these are equal, but for any non-causal path where `q_token_num < seq_len` (partial query over a longer KV, or the new dynamic-causal `req_causal=false` requests) the mask window centering via `calcu_kv_tile_pos` will now be computed relative to a shifted q position, potentially producing a wrong sliding-window KV range. (3) Confirm all non-causal callers always satisfy `seq_len == q_token_num`; if not, restore the causal-conditional q_start_pos for the bidirectional case, or verify the dynamic-causal test covers `q_token_num < seq_len` with `dynamic_causal[i]=false` AND a sliding window.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run test_cpu_attn.py with a mixed batch containing a dynamic_causal=False request where query_len < kv_len and a non-None sliding_window, comparing against ref_paged_attn.

findings.claim: (1) The public helper `cpu_attention_with_kv_cache` changed its `sliding_window` parameter type from `tuple[int, int]` to a scalar `int`, and `get_scheduler_metadata` op signature dropped `sliding_window_right`, replacing `(sliding_window_left, sliding_window_right)` with a single `sliding_window_size`. (2) This is a breaking API/ABI change to the custom op contract: any external caller or cached compiled op invoking the old positional tuple form will now pass a tuple where an int is expected, causing a runtime type error, and the reordered args (dynamic_causal inserted before k_scale/v_scale) will silently misalign positional callers. (3) Since these are `torch.ops._C` bindings updated in torch_bindings.cpp together, verify there are no other in-tree callers passing the tuple form and that this op is not part of a stable external contract; otherwise add a compatibility shim.

(1) `calcu_sliding_window_size(window_size, causal)` is now recomputed inside `calcu_kv_tile_pos` on every call, and `calcu_kv_tile_pos` is invoked once per q-tile per kv-tile in the hot attention main loop (lines ~1646, ~1760). (2) The left/right window sizes are constant for a given request/window across all tiles, so recomputing the branch on every tile adds redundant work in the innermost scheduling/compute path (minor, but it is a per-forward-pass constant recomputation). (3) The main loop already hoists `calcu_sliding_window_size` once per workitem-group at line ~1567 into `sliding_window_left/right`, but then passes `sliding_window_size` + `current_group_causal` back into `calcu_kv_tile_pos`, which recomputes it — pass the already-computed left/right values instead to avoid the redundant recomputation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark cpu_attention_with_kv_cache on a decode-heavy batch with sliding window enabled, before/after hoisting the window-size computation out of calcu_kv_tile_pos.

findings.claim: (1) `gelu_tanh_and_mul` replaced the scalar loop over `std::tanh` (16 elements via a temp buffer) with a vectorized `Sleef_tanhf16_u10` call, and a comment states the fast_exp form 'will generate wrong results' for diffusiongemma. (2) This is a correctness-motivated change that also affects performance: `Sleef_tanhf16_u10` (1.0 ULP) is more accurate but slower than a fast approximation; the choice trades throughput for numerical correctness in the MoE activation, which is on the per-forward critical path. (3) Confirm the accuracy requirement is real (diffusion Gemma sensitivity) and that u10 (vs u35 lower-accuracy) is the minimum precision needed; if u35 suffices it would be faster while still avoiding the fast_exp error.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark cpu_fused_moe gelu_tanh_and_mul throughput with Sleef u10 vs u35 vs the old scalar std::tanh loop, and verify diffusion Gemma output correctness with each.

findings.claim: (1) New local variables are misspelled: `is_dynamic_casual` and `dynamic_casual` (should be `causal`), while the field they populate is correctly spelled `dynamic_causal`. (2) The diff is explicitly cleaning up the historical `casual`->`causal` typo elsewhere (torch_bindings, cpu_attn.cpp, scheduler struct), so reintroducing the `casual` misspelling in the new Python locals is inconsistent and error-prone for future greps. (3) Rename `is_dynamic_casual`/`dynamic_casual` to `is_dynamic_causal`/`dynamic_causal`.

(1) The JSON dump of `tuned_config` is placed inside the `for num_tokens` loop rather than after it, so the full config file is re-serialized and overwritten on every token-count iteration (16 times per model). (2) This wastes disk I/O and, more importantly, the trailing log message `Tuned config for num_tokens={num_tokens} was stored...` is misleading because each write actually persists the entire accumulated config, not just that token count. (3) Move the `json.dump`/`open`/`print` block out one indentation level so it runs once per model after all token counts are tuned.

(1) `torch.randint(0, 255, ...)` uses an exclusive upper bound, so packed int4 weight bytes only cover 0-254 and never generate the value 255. (2) For an accuracy/perf tuning harness this slightly under-samples the weight value space (top nibble 0xF combined with 0xF never appears), which could mask edge-case correctness in the reference-vs-kernel `allclose` check. (3) Use `torch.randint(0, 256, ...)` to cover the full byte range.

(1) `w13.is_shuffled = True` / `w2.is_shuffled = True` attach an ad-hoc attribute onto a raw `torch.Tensor`. (2) Relying on dynamic tensor attributes to flag layout state is fragile (attributes are silently dropped by many tensor ops such as `.view`, `.contiguous`, `.to`), so downstream code reading `is_shuffled` may see it disappear after any subsequent transform and behave incorrectly. (3) If the kernel path depends on this flag, pass shuffle state explicitly as an argument instead of piggy-backing on tensor attributes; if it is unused, remove it.

(1) `_get_cached_wo_a_bf16` caches the dequantized bf16 weight on the module attribute `_dsv4_wo_a_bf16` keyed only by presence, without any invalidation for a changed `hidden_dim` / `n_local_groups` / `o_lora_rank`. The very first call fixes the cached tensor shape and dequant; any later call with a different shape argument silently returns the stale cached tensor. (2) This matters because the same `wo_a` module could in principle be invoked with different `hidden_dim` (derived at runtime from `o_ref.shape[-1]`), and the cache is never revalidated against the requested shape — a mismatch would produce a wrong einsum result rather than an error. In practice hidden_dim is static, so the risk is bounded, but there is no assertion guarding it. (3) Fix: assert the cached tensor shape matches the requested `(n_local_groups, o_lora_rank, hidden_dim)` before returning it, e.g. `assert cached.shape == (n_local_groups, o_lora_rank, hidden_dim)`.

(1) The old `_apply_inv_rope_ref` path preferred `rotary_emb.forward_native(..., inverse=True)` when available and only fell back to the GPT-J-specific `_apply_gptj_inv_rope_ref`. The new `_fused_inverse_rope_gptj` hard-codes the GPT-J (non-neox) even/odd interleaved layout and asserts `cos_sin_cache.shape[-1] == rope_head_dim`. (2) This matters because if the rotary embedding is ever configured with neox-style layout (is_neox_style=True) or a cos_sin_cache laid out as [P, 2*half] where the fused kernel's `cos|sin` split assumption doesn't hold, results will be silently wrong (the kernel reads cos at [pos, k] and sin at [pos, HALF+k] and assumes 2*k / 2*k+1 interleaving). The removed code path had a general fallback; the new one does not. (3) Fix: add an explicit assertion or guard that the rotary embedding is non-neox / GPT-J style, or retain a forward_native fallback for non-GPT-J layouts.

(1) The old `_apply_gptj_inv_rope_ref` explicitly handled the `rope_dim == 0` (no-RoPE) case by early-returning the input unchanged. The new `_fused_inverse_rope_gptj` asserts `rope_head_dim > 0 and rope_head_dim % 2 == 0`, hard-failing on `rope_head_dim == 0`. (2) This matters only if any DSv4 config path can invoke the o-projection with `rope_head_dim == 0`; the assertion converts a previously-supported (no-op) case into a crash, a behavior change. (3) Fix: confirm `rope_head_dim` is always > 0 for the ROCm/XPU DSv4 attention path (it appears fixed at construction); if so this is fine, otherwise restore a zero-rope passthrough.

(1) `_inverse_rope_gptj_kernel` uses a strided per-lane access pattern `2 * k` / `2 * k + 1` to load the even/odd RoPE lanes, which produces non-coalesced (stride-2) global loads/stores on the rope segment. (2) This matters for decode throughput on the hot per-step o-projection path — non-contiguous access reduces memory bandwidth efficiency vs. a contiguous vectorized load of the rope block followed by an in-register de-interleave. (3) Fix (optional): consider loading the contiguous rope block `[0..rope_dim)` once and de-interleaving in registers, or verify via profiling that the current stride-2 access is not a bottleneck relative to the einsum GEMM.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark _fused_inverse_rope_gptj on decode-shaped inputs (num_tokens in {1,64}, num_heads=128, head_dim per DSv4) comparing stride-2 vs contiguous-load-and-deinterleave kernel variants.

findings.claim: (1) The new `logger.info_once(...)` uses a parameterized message; `info_once` typically deduplicates on the (unformatted) message key, so only the first builder instance's values (next_n / use_flattening / use_fp4_indexer_cache) will ever be logged. Since this builder is constructed per attention layer / config, later builders with different next_n or a different flattening decision will be silently suppressed, making the log potentially misleading for debugging. (2) Impact is limited to observability, not correctness. (3) Consider logging once per distinct (next_n, use_flattening) tuple, or dropping to debug level, or documenting that a single representative line is intended.

(1) The QuantizedActivation contract is introduced end-to-end (kernels advertise input_quant_key(), expose_input_quant_key() writes layer.input_quant_key, and apply_weights() consumes via as_quantized_activation()), but there is NO producer anywhere in the codebase that constructs a QuantizedActivation, and no fusion call site reads the newly-set layer.input_quant_key attribute. Grep at parent and in this diff shows QuantizedActivation is only ever created in the unit test. (2) This means the entire new pre-quantized-input branch (qa is not None) is dead code at runtime — the layer.input_quant_key attribute set by expose_input_quant_key() has no meaningful read/use path outside tests. Per the Mandatory Check, this is a placeholder/deferred-usage pattern. (3) The dataclass docstrings do carry TODO(mgoin) notes acknowledging the contract is incomplete (layout/padding/scales not yet exposed to producers), so this is an intentional scaffolding PR; recommend confirming the plumbing is explicitly documented as a staged/incomplete contract and gated so no live path can route a plain tensor into the qa branch prematurely.

(1) In the FP8 pre-quantized branch (qa is not None), the code sets x_data/x_s from the QuantizedActivation and asserts x_data.dtype == fp8_dtype, then skips quant_fp8. However QuantFP8 was constructed in __init__ with num_token_padding=self.get_output_padding(); when the kernel quantizes in-kernel it applies that token padding, but a pre-quantized activation produced upstream is not guaranteed to carry the same num_token_padding. (2) If a producer emits an activation without the expected token padding, the scaled_mm may receive a differently-sized A matrix than the kernel would have produced internally, causing shape/stride mismatch or silently wrong output rows. (3) The contract should validate/encode the token-padding requirement (the TODO in QuantizedActivation notes padding is not captured); add an assertion on x_2d row count or document that producers must match get_output_padding().

(1) In the NVFP4 pre-quantized branch, when qa is not None the code uses qa.data/qa.scale and calls pad_nvfp4_activation_for_cutlass(x_fp4, weights_padding_bytes), but the in-kernel path calls scaled_fp4_quant(..., is_sf_swizzled_layout=True, padded_n=x.shape[-1] + weights_padding_bytes*2). The docstring says 'pre-quantized blockscales must use the swizzled layout' by convention, but nothing validates that qa.scale is actually in swizzled layout, nor that qa.data was produced with the same padded_n. (2) A producer supplying a non-swizzled blockscale or unpadded activation would produce silently wrong GEMM results (this is convention-only, per the TODO). (3) Encode the swizzle/padding requirement in the contract or add a shape/layout assertion at the boundary rather than relying on convention.

(1) as_quantized_activation raises AssertionError on key mismatch, and it is invoked inside apply_weights on the hot inference path. (2) assert statements are stripped under python -O, so in optimized deployments a wrongly-routed activation would silently proceed and be interpreted with the wrong quant scheme rather than failing loudly as the docstring promises. (3) Consider raising an explicit exception (ValueError) instead of assert for the mismatch check that the docstring describes as a hard safety guarantee.

(1) expose_input_quant_key(layer, kernel) takes an untyped 'kernel' parameter (no type annotation). (2) In distributed/kernel plumbing code an untyped duck-typed argument reduces clarity and static-checkability of the new public contract API. (3) Add a type annotation or a Protocol describing the required input_quant_key() method.

(1) make_async_with_semaphore accesses the private attribute executor._max_workers to size the asyncio.Semaphore. (2) _max_workers is an undocumented CPython implementation detail of ThreadPoolExecutor; if it is ever renamed/removed the code breaks, and it is unavailable on non-ThreadPoolExecutor executors even though the type hint is specifically ThreadPoolExecutor. (3) Pass max_workers explicitly as a parameter to make_async_with_semaphore (the caller already computes num_audio_preprocess_workers), or store it on the executor wrapper, rather than reaching into a private field.

(1) The new _async_wrapper uses asyncio.get_event_loop() instead of asyncio.get_running_loop(). (2) get_event_loop() is deprecated when called outside a running loop (Python 3.10+ emits a DeprecationWarning and its behavior of creating a loop is being removed); since _async_wrapper is always invoked from within an awaited coroutine, a running loop is guaranteed. (3) Use asyncio.get_running_loop() for correctness and future-proofing. (Note: existing make_async uses the same deprecated call, so this only mirrors current style — low priority.)

(1) A ThreadPoolExecutor (self._preprocess_executor) is created in __init__ for every OpenAISpeechToText instance, and shutdown() is only invoked from lifespan for app.state.openai_serving_transcription / openai_serving_translation. (2) If an OpenAISpeechToText subclass is instantiated in any other path (e.g. tests, or a code path that does not register it under those two exact app.state attribute names), the executor threads will leak because nothing calls shutdown(). (3) Consider registering shutdown via a weakref.finalize / __del__ safety net, or ensure the lifespan shutdown loop enumerates all serving objects generically rather than a hardcoded 2-name tuple.

(1) VLLM_MAX_AUDIO_PREPROCESS_WORKERS is added to compile_factors(), meaning its value participates in the torch.compile cache key / configuration hash. (2) This env var controls a frontend CPU thread-pool size for audio preprocessing and has no effect on compiled model artifacts; including it needlessly invalidates compilation caches when users tune it, and semantically pollutes the compile factor set. (3) Verify it truly needs to be a compile factor; if not, remove it from compile_factors().

(1) The test change in tests/kernels/quantization/test_cutlass_scaled_mm.py removes the guard `if m % 4 != 0 and current_platform.has_device_capability(100): return`, which previously skipped odd-M blockwise FP8 tests on SM100 (Blackwell). (2) The PR only adds odd-M (swap_ab) support to the SM90 dispatch (scaled_mm_blockwise_sm90_fp8_dispatch.cuh); there is no corresponding SM100/Blackwell blockwise dispatch change in this diff, and no SM100 blockwise kernel was found in the parent tree. If the SM100 code path still enforces `m % 4 == 0` (as the SM90 path did via STD_TORCH_CHECK), removing this skip will make the test hard-fail/abort on Blackwell for odd M. (3) Either keep the SM100 skip until SM100 also supports odd M, or confirm/add the equivalent swap_ab handling on the SM100 dispatch before removing the guard.

(1) The dispatch heuristic hardcodes `bool swap_ab = (a.size(0) % 4) != 0;` with the previous `// TODO: better heuristics` comment removed, but the M%4 threshold is a magic constant that duplicates the alignment reasoning previously encoded in the deleted `STD_TORCH_CHECK(m % 4 == 0)` and the Python `pad_multiple = 4`. (2) A bare `% 4` with no explanatory comment is easy to break silently if the swap_ab tile shape (Shape<_128,_16,_128>) or alignment requirements change; the coupling between the '4' and the non-swap kernel's M-alignment requirement is non-obvious. (3) Add a brief comment documenting why 4 is the threshold (non-swap kernel requires M divisible by 4 for the MN-major A scale layout / TMA alignment), or derive it from a named constant.

(1) The file scaled_mm_blockwise_sm90_fp8_dispatch.cuh ends without a trailing newline ('\ No newline at end of file'). (2) Missing final newline is a minor portability/tooling nuisance (some compilers/linters warn, diffs get noisier). (3) Add a trailing newline at end of file.

(1) The swap_ab path is selected for ALL odd-M cases (m%4 != 0), using a fixed small tile Shape<_128,_16,_128> with the Pingpong scheduler, regardless of M magnitude. (2) For odd M that is nevertheless large (e.g. m=513, 1025), forcing the small-N-tile swapped kernel may leave performance on the table versus the cooperative path; the '180-290% improvement' cited is for the low-M/decode regime. The heuristic does not consider M magnitude, only its divisibility. (3) Consider a heuristic that only swaps for small M (the decode/low-latency regime) and otherwise pads-to-4 or uses the cooperative kernel for large odd M; at minimum add a benchmark across a range of odd M values to confirm no regression at larger M.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Blockwise FP8 GEMM latency/throughput sweep over M in {1,2,3,5,7,17,33,129,513,1025} with representative N,K (e.g. 4096,4096) on SM90, comparing swap_ab kernel vs padded cooperative kernel.

findings.claim: SchedulerConfig.watermark's docstring/validator are inconsistent about the allowed range. (1) The Pydantic Field constrains the value to `ge=0.0, lt=1.0` (i.e. [0.0, 1.0)), but the docstring states "Must be in the range [0.0, 1.0)" in one sentence and the benchmark/config text elsewhere refers to it as "fraction of total KV cache blocks" — this is fine. However the real risk: a watermark close to 1.0 (e.g. 0.9) sets watermark_blocks = int(0.9 * num_blocks), leaving almost no allocatable blocks, which can make even a single waiting/preempted request permanently un-admittable while `self.running` is non-empty, deadlocking that request until running drains. (2) This matters because it can silently starve new admissions and hurt throughput/latency rather than help. (3) Recommend validating watermark_blocks against a sane upper bound (e.g. relative to blocks needed for one max-len request) and/or documenting/guarding the practical safe range (paper/PR suggests <=0.15).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Set --watermark 0.9 on a KV-constrained config and verify a single waiting request is still eventually admitted (no permanent starvation) when running queue never fully drains.

findings.claim: The watermark is only applied at the waiting/preempted admission call site (scheduler.py ~line 818), not at the running-request decode allocation site (scheduler.py ~line 472/474). (1) `has_scheduled_reqs=True` is the default in allocate_slots, so the running-decode call implicitly passes True, but the watermark gate only triggers for requests whose status is WAITING/PREEMPTED. Running requests have RUNNING status, so the watermark is correctly not applied to their per-step decode growth — this is intended. Confirm, however, that the watermark headroom is what running requests are meant to "grow into": the design reserves free blocks so admissions leave room for running decode. This is consistent, but verify there is no path where a PREEMPTED request being resumed via the running/decode allocation site (line 472) skips the watermark unintentionally, or conversely a resumed request at line 818 is over-gated.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Trace status transitions for a preempted-then-resumed request through both allocate_slots call sites and assert watermark is applied exactly once at admission.

findings.claim: New engine argument `--watermark` is added with default 0.0 (disabled), which preserves existing behavior — good. However `watermark` is a very generic name colliding conceptually with the pre-v1 vLLM `CacheConfig` block-manager 'watermark' (0.01 default) concept. (1) Users migrating scripts may assume semantics/default match the old value. (2) Silent behavior differences (old default 0.01 vs new 0.0) could confuse. (3) Suggest a more specific name like `--kv-cache-watermark` and/or a doc note referencing the historical watermark to avoid confusion.

watermark_blocks is computed as int(watermark * kv_cache_config.num_blocks) once in __init__ (good, not per-forward). But the per-call computation in allocate_slots recomputes `required_blocks = num_blocks_to_allocate + watermark_blocks` and the status-membership check on every allocate_slots call. This is cheap, but the `request.status in (WAITING, PREEMPTED)` tuple is constructed on each call; consider a module-level frozenset constant to avoid repeated tuple allocation on the scheduler hot path.

The `has_scheduled_reqs` argument semantics: it gates whether the watermark is applied ('only when there's at least one request already scheduled'). The rationale is that if nothing else is scheduled, admitting a request even beyond the watermark is fine (avoid deadlock when the queue is otherwise empty). This is a subtle but important invariant that prevents starvation, but it is only documented in a one-line arg docstring. Recommend expanding the docstring/comment to explain the anti-deadlock rationale (if no requests are running, the watermark must be bypassed or the request could never be admitted), so future refactors don't drop this guard.

(1) apply_gptq_marlin_linear (and the fp4/fp8/mxfp8 apply_* variants) now call marlin_pad_dim on the activation input and marlin_unpad_output on the result on every forward pass, and marlin_repacked_nk recomputes the padded shapes each call. (2) When a layer needs padding, F.pad allocates a new padded activation tensor and marlin_unpad_output does output[..., :size_n].contiguous() (a copy) on every forward, adding an extra allocation + memcpy in the hot GEMM path; this is exactly the degradation the module's own warning mentions. (3) At minimum cache the (padded_n, padded_k) values on the layer at weight-prep time instead of re-deriving them from the packed weight shape via marlin_repacked_nk every forward, and prefer the TODO'd approach of having marlin_gemm write directly into an unpadded caller buffer to eliminate the slice-copy. This is inherent to padded layers, but the recompute of shapes for every (even unpadded) layer is avoidable.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark apply_fp8_marlin_linear / apply_gptq_marlin_linear throughput for a padded layer (e.g. N or K not tile-aligned) vs an aligned layer, measuring extra latency from F.pad + output slice .contiguous() per forward.

findings.claim: (1) marlin_padded_nk picks the cheaper of two tile families with min(candidates, key=lambda nk: (nk[0]*nk[1], nk[0]+nk[1])). The apply-side paths (apply_gptq/awq/fp8/fp4/mxfp8_marlin_linear) recover padded_n/padded_k independently via marlin_repacked_nk from the packed weight shape. (2) If the tie-break/selection in marlin_padded_nk ever diverges from what marlin_repacked_nk reconstructs (e.g. rounding families that both land at the same padded_n but different padded_k, or a shape where round_up to lcm(128,group) vs lcm(64,group) produce equal area but different dims), the activation would be padded to a K that does not match the repacked weight K, producing wrong GEMM results or a shape mismatch. The two code paths must be guaranteed consistent. (3) Recommend that weight prep store padded_n/padded_k on the layer and the apply path read those stored values rather than reconstructing from the packed shape, to remove the invariant coupling between marlin_padded_nk and marlin_repacked_nk.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test asserting marlin_repacked_nk(gptq_marlin_repack(pad(...))) == marlin_padded_nk(size_n, size_k, group) across a sweep of (n, k, group_size) including several tile-misaligned shapes.

findings.claim: (1) can_implement now returns True for tile-misaligned shapes (relying on padding at weight prep), but the padding path in process_weights_after_loading only pads the qweight, scales, zero-points and bias; the act-order (has_g_idx) branch keeps the strict shape check. However for the zero_points branch, padded_grouped_k = padded_k // group_size is used as size_k for marlin_zero_points while the unpack still uses grouped_k = size_k // group_size — the marlin_pad_scales pads rows to padded_k//group_size. If padded_k is not divisible by group_size the pad_rows assertion in marlin_pad_scales would misfire, but marlin_padded_nk rounds K up to lcm(.,group) so this holds only for the non-g_idx path. (2) A group_size that does not evenly divide padded_k for AWQ zero-points would silently corrupt zero-point placement. (3) Add an explicit assertion that padded_k % group_size == 0 in the zero_points branch, and add a test covering AWQ (zero_points=True) with a tile-misaligned shape.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Correctness test: AWQ Marlin layer with zero points and a tile-misaligned (n,k) shape, compare dequantized output against an unpadded reference GEMM.

findings.claim: (1) ModelOptFp8LinearMethod.create_weights now sets layer.orig_dtype = params_dtype. prepare_fp8_layer_for_marlin reads layer.orig_dtype, so this addition is presumably to enable the FP8 Marlin fallback for ModelOpt FP8 checkpoints. (2) If any code path previously relied on orig_dtype being absent (e.g. a getattr(layer, 'orig_dtype', default) branch) the new always-present attribute could change behavior; and if the marlin prep is not actually reachable for this method, orig_dtype is an unused new field. (3) Verify the FP8 Marlin fallback is actually invoked for ModelOptFp8LinearMethod on non-FP8-capable GPUs; if not, remove the field or document why it is needed.

(1) marlin_padded_nk logs a warning_once whenever any layer requires padding, but the message is generic and fires once globally for the whole model. (2) Because it is warning_once at module scope keyed on the message string, a mix of padded and unpadded layers only warns once and gives no indication of which/how many layers are affected, making performance triage harder. (3) Consider including the offending (n, k) shape in the warning or emitting an info-level count of padded layers, so users can assess the throughput impact flagged by the message.

(1) The class docstring still states the DSV3 specialized kernel applies for 'M<=16' (line: '1. DSV3 specialized kernel (SM90+, fp32 out, M<=16, H=7168, E=256/384)'), but after this change the batch threshold is now architecture-dependent: 16 for Hopper (SM90) and 8 for Blackwell (family 100). (2) A stale docstring is misleading — a future reader on Blackwell will assume M<=16 dispatches to the kernel when the actual cutoff is 8. (3) Update the docstring to reflect the per-architecture cutoff, e.g. 'M<=16 on Hopper, M<=8 on Blackwell'.

(1) The new field `_dsv3_max_batch` is derived solely from `is_hopper` (`16 if is_hopper else 8`), so on non-Hopper/non-CUDA platforms it is set to 8 even though `allow_dsv3_router_gemm` will be False and the value is never read. (2) This is harmless (dead value gated by the eligibility flag), but the coupling is implicit and could confuse future maintainers who assume the value is only meaningful when the DSV3 path is active. (3) Optional: gate the assignment behind `allow_dsv3_router_gemm`/`is_blackwell` explicitly, or add a brief comment clarifying that the value is only consulted when DSV3 dispatch is enabled. Note the magic constants 8 and 16 could also be named class constants (e.g. DSV3_MAX_TOKENS_HOPPER / DSV3_MAX_TOKENS_BLACKWELL) for consistency with the existing FP32_MAX_TOKENS style.

(1) `torch.zeros([...], dtype=torch.int32, pin_memory=True)` allocates a fresh pinned-memory tensor on every metadata build (per forward step / per chunked-prefill batch), and the tensor is immediately filled in a Python `for i in range(num_chunks)` loop and later copied to device with `non_blocking=True`. (2) Pinned-memory allocation via cudaHostAlloc/registration is significantly more expensive than a pageable allocation; doing it every step in the hot metadata-build path can offset (or exceed) the H2D-overlap benefit the PR is trying to achieve, especially for small metadata tensors where the copy itself is tiny. (3) Confirm via profiling that the per-step pin_memory allocation cost is amortized; if not, consider a reusable pre-pinned buffer (allocated once, sized to the workspace bound) rather than allocating pinned memory each build.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench MLA chunked-prefill metadata build: measure per-step wall time and allocator cost of `torch.zeros(..., pin_memory=True)` and `.pin_memory()` vs pageable + non_blocking, across num_chunks/num_prefills, on the actual serving hot path.

findings.claim: (1) `.pin_memory()` is appended to the result of the `chunk_starts` arange/expand/mul expression, which creates the pageable tensor first and then copies it into a newly-allocated pinned tensor (an extra host-to-host copy + pinned alloc). (2) This adds allocation and copy overhead every build; for correctness the pin only helps if the subsequent `.to(device, non_blocking=True)` is actually overlapping with compute. (3) If the goal is a truly non-blocking copy, prefer constructing the tensor pinned in one shot where feasible, or reuse a persistent pinned staging buffer; also verify the non_blocking copy is not immediately followed by a sync that negates the overlap.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare end-to-end chunked-prefill throughput/latency with and without the `.pin_memory()` additions to confirm net positive; capture nsys trace to verify the H2D copies now overlap compute.

findings.claim: (1) For `non_blocking=True` H2D copies to be genuinely asynchronous, the source must be pinned AND the copy must be on a stream that is not immediately synchronized before the pinned source is reused/freed. `token_to_seq_tensor_cpu` is written in a Python loop after allocation; ensure no `.item()`/`.cpu()`/implicit sync between the pinned allocation, the loop fill, and the async copy would serialize things. (2) If a sync occurs (e.g. `max_token_num_over_chunk = chunk_total_token.max().item()` on the immediately preceding line already forces a device sync), the async benefit may be limited to a subset of the copies. (3) Verify the surrounding code path has no host-device sync that defeats the intended overlap.

(1) The memory pressure check is inverted. The docstring says memory should be released 'when system memory pressure is high'. High pressure means LOW available memory. But the code returns False (skips release) when `mem.available > (1 - _UMA_PRESSURE_THRESHOLD) * mem.total`, i.e. it skips release when available > 20% of total, and only releases when available <= 20% of total. That part is actually correct. HOWEVER the naming/threshold semantics are confusing: `_UMA_PRESSURE_THRESHOLD = 0.8` is described as a pressure threshold, but it is compared as `(1 - 0.8) = 0.2` fraction of *free* memory. If the intent was 'release when 80% of memory is used', that is equivalent to 'available < 20%', which matches. Re-verify: release triggers only when available memory drops below 20% of total. This is a very aggressive threshold — on a UMA device that already reports large 'used' memory (page cache etc.), releasing only at 20% free may be too late to prevent OS thrashing, which is the stated goal. Consider whether the threshold direction and value achieve the intent. (3) Add a unit test asserting release happens at high-usage and is skipped at low-usage, and document the threshold semantics explicitly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test mocking psutil.virtual_memory().available at boundary values (19%, 21% of total) to confirm release/skip behavior matches intent.

findings.claim: (1) `release_device_memory_under_pressure(target_device)` is invoked inside the per-module loop `for _, module in model.named_modules()`, so it runs once for every quantized module in the model (potentially hundreds/thousands of modules for large LLMs). Each call does `psutil.virtual_memory()` (a syscall) and, when triggered, `torch.accelerator.synchronize()` + `torch.accelerator.empty_cache()`. (2) `empty_cache()` releases the entire caching-allocator pool back to the OS and forces a full device synchronize; calling this repeatedly during weight loading serializes the GPU and causes cudaMalloc thrashing (repeatedly returning memory to OS then re-allocating for the next module), which can massively slow down weight loading — the opposite of the reserved-cache performance benefit. (3) Move the call outside the loop (e.g. once after the full loop, or throttle it, e.g. every N modules or based on elapsed time), so it does not run per-module.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time process_weights_after_loading on a large quantized model (e.g. many-layer AWQ/GPTQ model) on a UMA device under memory pressure, comparing per-module vs single post-loop release.

findings.claim: (1) `release_device_memory_under_pressure` calls `current_platform.is_integrated_gpu(device.index)` on every invocation, and (per the diff usage) is invoked once per module during weight loading. If `is_integrated_gpu` is not cached, this repeated platform query adds overhead in the hot loop. (2) On non-UMA (the common case) this is pure overhead per module. (3) Verify `is_integrated_gpu` is `@cache`d; if not, cache it, and/or short-circuit the whole feature via a single early check before entering the module loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark cost of current_platform.is_integrated_gpu() x number_of_modules for a large model.

findings.claim: (1) `release_device_memory_under_pressure` guards only `device.type != 'cuda'`, but the function body uses `torch.accelerator.*` APIs. On ROCm the platform reports device.type 'cuda' as well, which is fine, but for other accelerators the early return means the feature silently no-ops even where UMA might apply (e.g. XPU). (2) This limits the fix to CUDA/ROCm UMA only. (3) If UMA support on other backends is intended, generalize the device.type guard; otherwise document that this is CUDA-only intentionally.

(1) `torch.accelerator.empty_cache()` does not take a device argument, so on a multi-GPU host it empties the caching allocator for the *current* device context, not necessarily `device`. The preceding `synchronize(device)` and `memory_reserved(device)` are device-specific, but `empty_cache()` is not scoped to `device`. (2) If `target_device` differs from the current device, this could empty the wrong device's cache or fail to release the intended device. (3) Ensure the current device is set to `device` (e.g. via a device guard) before calling `empty_cache()`, or verify empty_cache semantics for the intended device.

(1) `find_longest_cache_hit_per_group` calls `manager_cls.find_longest_cache_hit(...)` without passing `dcp_world_size`/`pcp_world_size`, and computes `group_hit = len(blocks[0]) * spec.block_size` using the raw `spec.block_size`. The existing `UnitaryKVCacheCoordinator.find_longest_cache_hit` multiplies block_size by dcp/pcp world sizes and forwards those args. (2) If this code path is ever exercised with DCP/PCP > 1, the per-group hit length would be undercounted and the manager may partition blocks incorrectly, silently producing wrong prefix-cache hit reporting and potentially wrong block reuse. (3) Although `HybridKVCacheCoordinator.__init__` already asserts `dcp_world_size == 1` and `pcp_world_size == 1`, add matching assertions or forward the args here for defense-in-depth and consistency with the sibling method. Confirm the assertion in __init__ makes this safe today.

(1) `num_new_local_computed_tokens = max(per_group_hits)` uses the maximum across groups (i.e. the full-attention hit length), but the returned `new_computed_blocks` contains the per-group blocks where the Mamba group has 0 blocks. Downstream `allocate_slots`/`get_num_blocks_to_allocate` computes `num_local_computed_tokens = request.num_computed_tokens + num_new_computed_tokens` and passes `new_computed_blocks[i]` per group. For the Mamba group, `num_new_computed_tokens` (=max, the FA hit) will exceed `len(new_computed_blocks[mamba_group]) * block_size` (=0). (2) This inconsistency between the reported local-computed-token count and the actual per-group blocks can cause the Mamba manager's block accounting (allocate_new_computed_blocks / get_num_blocks_to_allocate) to mis-size allocations or mis-align the Mamba state, risking incorrect KV state or allocation. (3) Verify that every downstream consumer of `num_new_local_computed_tokens` tolerates a value larger than the per-group cached block coverage for the Mamba group; if not, pass per-group hit lengths through to the coordinator rather than a single scalar max.

(1) The new branch bypasses `KVCacheManager.get_computed_blocks`, which also handles `request.skip_reading_prefix_cache` (returns empty blocks / 0 when the request opts out of reading the prefix cache, e.g. prompt_logprobs or all-pooling requests). The new per-group path unconditionally calls `find_longest_cache_hit_per_group` and records prefix stats regardless of `skip_reading_prefix_cache`. (2) For Mamba hybrid + connector requests that set `skip_reading_prefix_cache`, this would incorrectly reuse cached blocks and produce wrong logprobs/pooling outputs. (3) Add the `if not self.enable_caching or request.skip_reading_prefix_cache` guard to the new branch, mirroring `get_computed_blocks`, before invoking `find_longest_cache_hit_per_group`.

(1) `find_longest_cache_hit_per_group` duplicates the `_get_block_hashes` helper and the per-spec-group lookup loop that already exist in the parent `find_longest_cache_hit`. (2) Duplicated block-hash-resizing and lookup logic risks drift: a future fix to hashing/eagle handling in one method won't propagate to the other. (3) Consider factoring the shared per-group lookup into a helper that both methods call, differing only in how they aggregate hit lengths (min vs per-group).

(1) The comment states `num_new_local_computed_tokens should be the FA hit length` and uses `max(per_group_hits)`. This assumes the attention (full-attention) group always yields the maximum hit length among all groups. For a hybrid model with both full-attention and sliding-window attention groups plus Mamba, the max may come from the FA group but the SWA group could differ; relying on `max` to mean 'FA hit' is imprecise. (2) A misleading comment can cause future maintainers to reason incorrectly about which group's hit is being used. (3) Clarify the comment to state the intent is 'the largest attention-group hit', or select the attention group's hit explicitly rather than relying on max.

parallel_agnostic collapses tp_size/pp_size to 1 AND rank to 0, but with tensor parallelism (tp>1) each rank holds a DIFFERENT head-shard of the same KV block. get_file_name() emits `{base_path}_r{self.rank}` -> with rank forced to 0 all TP ranks resolve to the identical file path for the same block hash. Under TP>1 this causes (a) concurrent writes from multiple ranks racing on the same file and (b) a rank loading another rank's head-shard, corrupting KV cache / producing wrong outputs. The claim in the comment that a single full-attention group is 'parallelism-invariant' is only true across RUNS with different tp, not across concurrent ranks WITHIN the same tp>1 run, unless the offloaded blocks are per-rank head slices. Verify whether the block payload is full (all-heads, allgathered) KV or a per-rank head-shard; if the latter, collapsing rank to 0 is a correctness bug. Fix: keep rank in the namespace (or in the per-block filename) even when parallel_agnostic collapses tp_size, OR only enable parallel_agnostic when tp_size==1.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run FS-tier KV offload with tensor_parallel_size=2 on a full-attention model, store then load blocks across ranks, and assert loaded KV matches per-rank source (correctness), not just file existence.

findings.claim: parallel_agnostic=True is now hardcoded for both fs and obj tier managers. This changes the base_path hash (tp/pp/dcp/pcp folded to 1, rank to 0) relative to the previous default (parallel_agnostic=False). Existing on-disk / object-store caches written by the prior version live under a different base_path and rank suffix and will no longer be found after upgrade -> silent cold cache (loss of all previously offloaded blocks) with no migration path. Fix: document the cache-layout change / bump a cache-format version, or provide a fallback lookup at the old path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Populate FS cache with the parent build, upgrade to this commit, and measure hit-rate on the previously stored keys.

findings.claim: `isinstance(spec, FullAttentionSpec) and not isinstance(spec, MLAAttentionSpec)` relies on MLAAttentionSpec subclassing FullAttentionSpec (confirmed at kv_cache_interface.py:353). This is correct but implicit/brittle: if the class hierarchy changes the guard silently breaks. Consider `type(spec) is FullAttentionSpec` or an explicit exclusion set, with a comment noting the subclass relationship dependency.

The reused local `spec` shadows/confuses with the parameter naming used elsewhere (offloading_spec, kv_cache_spec). A clearer name like `single_group_spec` would better convey that it is only the spec of the sole group when len(groups)==1.

(1) The new `maybe_update_config` re-enables `dynamic_mxfp4_quant = True` for DeepSeek-V3-family fp4 Quark checkpoints, overriding the `__init__` default of `False`. The `__init__` comment explicitly states the flag is 'kept disabled because the overhead of dynamic mxfp4 quantization negates the performance gains that come from shifting to mxfp4.' (2) This is a silent default-behavior/performance change: any deepseek_v3/deepseek_v32 fp4 Quark checkpoint that previously loaded with static (non-dynamic) MXFP4 will now incur dynamic re-quantization overhead at runtime, which the author's own comment says negates the mxfp4 speedup. Users relying on prior behavior get a throughput regression with no opt-out. (3) Either update/remove the now-stale `__init__` comment to document that the flag is conditionally enabled for this model family, or gate the enablement behind an explicit flag/env so the perf trade-off is opt-in.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a deepseek_v3 fp4 Quark checkpoint and measure decode throughput (tokens/sec) with dynamic_mxfp4_quant on vs off to quantify the re-quantization overhead.

findings.claim: (1) `from transformers import PretrainedConfig` is now imported at module top level, whereas it is only needed as a type hint for the `hf_config` parameter. (2) The base class `base_config.py` also imports it at top level so this is consistent, but importing heavy `transformers` symbols eagerly at module import can add import-time cost; the annotation could live under `TYPE_CHECKING` with a string/`Any` fallback like `vllm/config/vllm.py` does. (3) Consider guarding the import under `TYPE_CHECKING` to avoid an eager top-level `transformers` dependency purely for a type hint.

(1) `HybridKVCacheCoordinator.num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens` is assigned only inside `find_longest_cache_hit` (line ~688) but is never initialized in `__init__`. The scheduler reads it via `getattr(self.kv_cache_manager.coordinator, "num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens", 0)`, which masks the missing initialization with a default of 0. (2) Risk/impact: correctness relies on `find_longest_cache_hit` always being invoked (which resets the value) before every scheduler read; if a request path reads the attribute before any hit lookup has run for the current request (e.g. an early scheduling path or a code change), the scheduler will silently reuse the STALE value from a *previous, unrelated request*, adjusting `num_new_tokens` incorrectly. The `getattr` fallback also silently hides the fact that a non-mamba/unitary coordinator lacks this attribute entirely. (3) Fix: initialize `self.num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens = 0` in `HybridKVCacheCoordinator.__init__` (and/or the base coordinator), and read it as a plain attribute rather than via `getattr` so a missing attribute surfaces as a bug rather than being masked.

(1) `num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens` is per-request scratch state stored on the shared coordinator object, but it is set inside `find_longest_cache_hit` and consumed later in the scheduler loop for the *same* request. In the scheduler, `get_computed_blocks` (which calls `find_longest_cache_hit`) is only invoked when `request.num_computed_tokens == 0`; the value is then carried into `_mamba_block_aligned_split`. Because it is coordinator-global mutable state rather than being threaded through the return value / request, any interleaving where another request's `find_longest_cache_hit` runs between set and use would corrupt it. (2) Impact: wrong `num_new_tokens` scheduling for hybrid/mamba models — over- or under-scheduling prefill chunks, potentially breaking mamba block alignment. (3) Fix: return `num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens` from `find_longest_cache_hit`/`get_computed_blocks` alongside the existing `(blocks, hit_length)` tuple, or attach it to the request, instead of stashing it as coordinator state read via getattr.

(1) In the Simple hybrid fast-path (1 full attn + 1 other group, the comment 'one iteration suffices' path near line 620), the main loop that updates `longest_hit_length` may only run once or via the early-exit path; verify `longest_hit_length` is updated on ALL branches that assign into `hit_blocks_by_group`. The new `longest_hit_length = max(longest_hit_length, curr_hit_length)` (line 671) sits inside the block guarded by `if hit_blocks:` — if a group hits with `curr_hit_length > hit_length` but `hit_blocks` is falsy/empty on the winning branch, `longest_hit_length` stays 0 and the uncached-common-prefix heuristic under-reports. (2) Impact: the Marconi admission optimization silently no-ops (misses cache-admission opportunities) in edge cases — a missed-optimization/perf issue, not divergence. (3) Fix: confirm `longest_hit_length` tracks the max curr_hit_length across every iteration regardless of the `hit_blocks` truthiness guard, or document why the guard is correct.

(1) In `_mamba_block_aligned_split` the Marconi branch does `num_new_tokens = num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens` immediately followed by `num_new_tokens = num_new_tokens // block_size * block_size`. Since the guard already requires `num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens >= block_size`, and the coordinator computes `longest_hit_length - hit_length` where both are block-hit lengths (multiples of block_size for the relevant groups), the re-alignment may be a no-op. (2) Impact: minor — dead/redundant realignment adds a subtle assumption. (3) Fix: either fold into a single assignment `num_new_tokens = (num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens // block_size) * block_size`, or add an assertion/comment documenting that `num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens` is not guaranteed block-aligned (justifying the realignment).

(1) The new `getattr(self.kv_cache_manager.coordinator, "num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens", 0)` runs per waiting-request per schedule() step when `self.has_mamba_layers` is true. `getattr` with a default and string attribute lookup is slightly slower than a direct attribute access and obscures intent. (2) Impact: negligible per-call, but this is on the scheduler hot path. (3) Fix: initialize the attribute in the coordinator `__init__` (see other finding) and access it directly as `self.kv_cache_manager.coordinator.num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens`.

(1) The RISC-V INT8Vec16(const FP32Vec16&) conversion uses saturating narrow-clip intrinsics __riscv_vnclip_wx_i16 / __riscv_vnclip_wx_i8, but the FP32→INT32 step uses __riscv_vfcvt_x_f_v_i32 whose rounding mode depends on the current fixed-point/float rounding state, not the RNU mode passed to vnclip. dnnl_kernels.cpp always calls .clamp(i8_min_vec, i8_max_vec) before constructing INT8Vec16, so the values are already in [-128,127]; but the x86/ARM references round FP32→int using round-to-nearest-even. __riscv_vfcvt_x_f_v_i32 uses the dynamic FRM (frm CSR) which is round-to-nearest-even by default, so this is likely correct — verify FRM is not left in a non-default state by surrounding code. (2) Silent wrong int8 quantization output leads to accuracy loss in W8A8 GEMM. (3) Use the explicit-rounding-mode variant __riscv_vfcvt_x_f_v_i32_rm(..., __RISCV_FRM_RNE, ...) to make FP32→int32 rounding deterministic and match other backends.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Numerical test: compare static/dynamic_scaled_int8_quant on RISC-V vs reference torch int8 quant for random fp32/bf16/fp16 inputs incl. values on rounding half-way boundaries (e.g. x.5).

findings.claim: (1) The INT8Vec16 FP32→i32 conversion is performed with RVVI(__riscv_vfcvt_x_f_v_i32, LMUL_512) over VEC_ELEM_NUM=16 fp32 elements, then vnclip narrows i32(LMUL_512)→i16(LMUL_256)→i8(LMUL_128). This is only correct if fixed_i8x16_t (LMUL_128, 128 bits) actually holds 16 int8 lanes. For VLEN=128, LMUL_128=m1 gives 128 bits = 16 int8 lanes (OK). For VLEN=256, LMUL_128=mf2 gives 128 bits = 16 int8 lanes (OK). But the intermediate i32 is fixed_i32x16_t (LMUL_512 = 512 bits = 16 int32 lanes) and i16 must be 256 bits = 16 int16 lanes (LMUL_256). The def file only added fixed_i8x16_t and pre-existing fixed_i32x16_t; there is no fixed_i16x16_t typedef, and the intrinsic results are used directly (auto), so this compiles, but confirm __riscv_vnclip_wx_i16 with the LMUL_256 suffix produces a 256-bit m2/m1 register matching 16 int16 lanes at both VLEN=128 and VLEN=256. A VLEN/LMUL mismatch would silently truncate or corrupt lanes. (2) Wrong lanes → corrupted quantized weights/activations. (3) Add a static_assert on vector byte-width or an explicit fixed_i16x16_t typedef and use it, mirroring the existing fixed_i32x16_t pattern.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Build and run int8 quant unit test under both -DVLLM_RVV_VLEN=128 and =256 and verify lane-correct output.

findings.claim: (1) The oneDNN build gate in cmake was extended with `OR RVV_FP16_FOUND OR RVV_BF16_FOUND`, but the RISC-V scalar path (VLLM_RVV_VLEN=0) previously supported (the removed help text `-DVLLM_RVV_VLEN=0 force scalar, no RVV`) is now dropped, and the new FATAL_ERROR `VLLM_RVV_VLEN must be a positive integer` rejects VLLM_RVV_VLEN=0. This is a backward-incompatible change: users who explicitly built with VLEN=0 (scalar fallback) will now get a hard configure failure. (2) Breaks existing RISC-V build scripts relying on scalar fallback. (3) Either keep accepting 0 as a documented scalar mode, or clearly document the removal in release notes / migration guidance.

(1) The oneDNN GEMM build is now enabled when RVV_FP16_FOUND OR RVV_BF16_FOUND, but the torch_bindings.cpp quantization ops (release_dnnl_matmul_handler etc.) are gated on `defined(__riscv_v)` only, not on the same FP16/BF16 detection. If a RISC-V target defines __riscv_v but the cmake path did NOT enable oneDNN (neither RVV_FP16_FOUND nor RVV_BF16_FOUND), the bindings will reference oneDNN symbols that were not built, causing link errors. (2) Build/link failure on RVV targets without fp16/bf16. (3) Align the torch_bindings guard with the cmake oneDNN-enable condition (e.g. a dedicated preprocessor define set when oneDNN is built).

(1) The cmake validation `if(DEFINED VLLM_RVV_VLEN AND NOT VLLM_RVV_VLEN GREATER 0)` rejects non-positive values, but a non-numeric string (e.g. -DVLLM_RVV_VLEN=abc) evaluates `NOT abc GREATER 0` as true and produces the intended error — however the error message interpolates the raw string, which is fine. Minor: the check does not reject negative-but-defined values distinctly from zero; acceptable. No action strictly required. (2) Low impact. (3) Optionally validate it is a plain integer.

(1) This revert removes the warp-shuffle + vectorized-I/O optimization of silu_and_mul_per_block_quant, restoring the original one-block-per-group design: block dim == group_size, per-element scalar global loads/stores, __shared__ float shared_max[group_size], and a log2(group_size) shared-memory tree reduction with one __syncthreads() per level plus two extra __syncthreads() for the scale broadcast. (2) Per the removed comment/PR (#44173), the warp-shuffle path was ~2x faster and memory-latency-bound was eliminated; reverting reintroduces that regression on the SiLU+mul+block-quant path used in FP8/INT8 activation quantization, which sits in the hot inference/MoE path. It also reduces occupancy (one group per block of 64/128 threads vs 4 groups packed per 128-thread block) and drops the coalesced wide vector loads. (3) If the revert is due to a platform-specific (likely ROCm/AMD) correctness or build problem with __shfl_xor_sync / launch_bounds, prefer gating the optimized kernel behind a platform #ifdef rather than a blanket revert, so CUDA builds keep the faster path. At minimum confirm the numerical result is identical (fmaxf is order-invariant, so the group scale should remain bit-identical between the two reductions) so the revert is purely for perf/platform reasons and not masking a correctness bug.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark silu_and_mul_per_block_quant on representative shapes (num_tokens x hidden_size with group_size=64 and 128, fp8 and int8 outputs) comparing this reverted kernel vs the warp-shuffle version; measure GB/s and kernel latency on both NVIDIA and AMD GPUs.

findings.claim: (1) `MooncakeStoreConfig.load_from_env()` was renamed to `load_from_config()`, but the existing test `tests/v1/kv_connector/unit/test_mooncake_store_hma_e2e.py` (line ~100) still patches `MCfg.load_from_env.return_value` to set up the mock store config (`sc.protocol`, `sc.device_name`, `sc.global_segment_size`, etc.). Because `MCfg` is a MagicMock, `load_from_env` and `load_from_config` are two independent auto-created attributes, so `MCfg.load_from_config()` now returns a *different* MagicMock than the one the test configured. (2) The test will silently run against an unconfigured MagicMock config (protocol not 'tcp', segment sizes not set to real ints), which either changes what the E2E path exercises or breaks worker construction — a stale/broken test after this rename. (3) Update `test_mooncake_store_hma_e2e.py` to patch `MCfg.load_from_config.return_value` instead of `load_from_env.return_value`.

(1) `MooncakeStoreConfig.load_from_env()` is a public static method on a config class in production code; renaming it to `load_from_config()` with no deprecation shim breaks any external caller/subclass relying on the old name. (2) Downstream integrations or plugins that call `MooncakeStoreConfig.load_from_env()` will raise AttributeError. (3) Either keep `load_from_env` as a thin alias (`load_from_env = load_from_config`) for one deprecation cycle, or confirm this method has no external contract; the rename itself is otherwise a pure behavior-preserving refactor.

(1) The PR removes the entire Python-side worker RNIC selection path (`get_configured_worker_rnic`, `_get_explicit_worker_rnic`, `get_current_physical_gpu_index`) and stops overriding `store_config.device_name`, so `device_name` is now passed through unchanged to Mooncake's TransferEngine, delegating multi-NIC selection to the C++ side. This is an intentional behavior change per the commit message (use all HCAs instead of GPU-indexed RNIC selection). (2) Risk: any user who previously relied on a comma-separated `device_name` CSV indexed by physical GPU will now have that literal CSV string passed straight to Mooncake, which may fail to parse or behave differently. (3) Confirm Mooncake's TransferEngine accepts the previous CSV `device_name` format (or document that CSV device lists are no longer supported and should be a single name / empty).

(1) The mixin's get_top_tokens maps the draft argmax to a target id via `top = top + d2t[top]`, whereas the previous llama4_eagle.py fallback for the d2t case computed full scatter logits and took a global argmax over the target vocab (`compute_logits(...).argmax(-1)`). These are only mathematically equivalent when the scatter `targets = base + d2t` are all distinct AND no target position receives contributions from more than one draft id. In `compute_logits`, `logits_new[:, targets] = logits` uses advanced-indexing assignment; if two draft ids `k1,k2` map to the same target id, the last write wins, so the target-space argmax and the draft-space-then-remap argmax can disagree. (2) Silent divergence in draft token proposals for models whose d2t table is not injective — wrong speculative tokens lower acceptance rate (perf) and, if the remapped id lands out of target-vocab range, an out-of-bounds token id. (3) Add an assertion or documented invariant that d2t is injective (unique targets), or fall back to the scatter path when the invariant is not guaranteed.

(1) The old llama4_eagle path, when d2t was active, fell back to `compute_logits(hidden_states).argmax(dim=-1)`, i.e. an argmax over the FULL scattered target-vocab logits including any target ids that are NOT covered by the draft vocab (those are -inf and never win, which is fine). But the new mixin's `logits_processor.get_top_tokens(self.lm_head, hidden_states)` argmaxes only over the local shard of the DRAFT lm_head (size draft_vocab_size) and applies scale/soft_cap consistent with draft logits. This changes numerical behavior when `logits_processor.scale <= 0` or soft_cap interacts with the D2T remap: get_top_tokens raises for non-positive scale, whereas compute_logits().argmax historically tolerated it. Verify all D2T draft models (deepseek_eagle3, qwen3_5_mtp) have positive logit_scale so the new path does not raise where the old path silently worked.

(1) Multiple-inheritance MRO risk: `LocalArgmaxMixin` is inserted as the FIRST base for LlamaForCausalLM, Qwen3ForCausalLM, Qwen3_5MTP and Eagle3DeepseekV2ForCausalLM. Since the mixin defines `get_top_tokens`, it now shadows any `get_top_tokens` these classes or their bases previously provided, and takes precedence in method resolution. For Eagle3DeepseekV2ForCausalLM(LocalArgmaxMixin, DeepseekV2ForCausalLM) the base did not define get_top_tokens (relied on compute_logits path), so behavior changes from the full-scatter argmax to draft-space-argmax+remap. (2) A silent behavioral change for the deepseek eagle3 draft path that previously always used compute_logits().argmax; if the mixin remap is not exactly equivalent (see injectivity finding) this regresses acceptance. (3) Confirm each subclass previously used compute_logits().argmax for the D2T case and that the remap is exactly equivalent; add a unit test comparing get_top_tokens output vs full-logits argmax for a d2t-enabled draft model.

(1) `top = top + d2t[top]` executes an indexed gather on the GPU per draft step. `d2t` is an nn.Parameter of shape (draft_vocab_size,) on device, and `top` is a small [batch] int tensor, so this is a cheap fused op — not a concern by itself. However, verify `d2t[top]` does not trigger a host-device sync or a dtype mismatch: `top` returned from logits_processor.get_top_tokens is int64, d2t is torch.long — indexing is fine. No perf issue, but the addition assumes `top` indices are all < draft_vocab_size; a stale/padded index (from the num_org_vocab_padding masking path) could index out of bounds into d2t. Add a bounds guarantee/assert.

(1) The removed warning branch in llm_base_proposer.py previously informed users that enabling use_local_argmax_reduction with a d2t draft model would be BYPASSED (full-logits fallback). The new code unconditionally logs that local argmax reduction is used. If, for any model, the new mixin path is NOT actually wired (e.g. a draft model that has d2t but does not inherit LocalArgmaxMixin, such as llama_eagle3/qwen3_dflash which still define/use their own get_top_tokens), the log message now over-claims. (2) Misleading operator log; also masks the earlier explicit fallback semantics. (3) Confirm every d2t-bearing draft model now routes through the mixin (grep shows llama_eagle3.py and qwen3_dflash.py still have their own draft_id_to_target_id but were NOT modified to inherit the mixin) — verify their get_top_tokens handles d2t correctly, otherwise the optimization silently mis-maps tokens for those models.

(1) Coverage gap: `llama_eagle3.py` (Eagle3LlamaForCausalLM) and `qwen3_dflash.py` (DFlashQwen3ForCausalLM) both define `draft_id_to_target_id` (per grep) but were NOT changed to inherit LocalArgmaxMixin in this PR. With the warning/fallback removed from the proposer, if these models rely on the base `get_top_tokens` (logits_processor.get_top_tokens over the draft vocab) WITHOUT the d2t remap, the returned token ids stay in draft-vocab space and are never mapped to target vocab ids. (2) This produces silently wrong draft tokens (wrong vocab space) for those models when use_local_argmax_reduction is enabled — divergence / near-zero acceptance. (3) Either add LocalArgmaxMixin to these classes too, or verify they already apply the d2t remap in their own get_top_tokens; otherwise gate the optimization off for them.

(1) The vectorized loads/stores via reinterpret_cast to InVec/OutVec (alignas EPT*sizeof(scalar)) rely on the *base* tensor pointer being aligned to EPT*sizeof(scalar_t) (up to 8B). (2) The in-kernel per-element offsets are provably aligned (group_start, hidden_size, lane_id*EPT are all multiples of EPT), and the host code checks is_contiguous(), so freshly-allocated torch tensors (256B aligned) are safe. However, a contiguous *view* with a non-vector-aligned storage offset would trigger a misaligned wide access (undefined behavior / possible fault). (3) Consider adding a host-side STD_TORCH_CHECK that reinterpret_cast<uintptr_t>(input.data_ptr()) % (EPT*elem_size) == 0, or document that inputs must be base-aligned. Low risk given contiguity guarantees.

(1) `_inflight_prefill_reserved_blocks()` is called for every candidate async KV-connector load during `schedule()`, and it iterates over the entire `_inflight_prefills` set, calling `_request_remaining_blocks()` -> `coordinator.get_num_blocks_to_allocate()` per member. That inner call itself loops over all `single_type_managers`. So admitting N async loads while M prefills are in flight is O(N*M*groups) block-count computations per scheduler step. (2) This is on the hot scheduling path; with many concurrent PD/async-load requests it adds non-trivial per-step CPU overhead and can regress scheduler throughput. (3) Consider caching the summed reservation (incrementally maintained as requests enter/leave `_inflight_prefills`) instead of recomputing the full sum for each candidate load, since the sum only changes when the set membership changes within a step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Scheduler microbenchmark: many concurrent async-load requests (e.g. 256) with a large in-flight prefill set; measure schedule() wall time vs parent.

findings.claim: (1) A running prefill request is added to `_inflight_prefills` in the schedule() waiting-flow branch (line ~844) only when `num_computed_tokens + num_new_tokens < request.num_tokens`. However for requests scheduled from the running loop (existing chunked prefills already in self.running), there is no corresponding `_inflight_prefills.add()`. The set is only populated for newly-scheduled-from-waiting requests plus async loads. (2) This means the reservation gate under-counts blocks reserved for chunked prefills that were admitted before this change's set existed or that are progressing through the running loop, so the deadlock the PR targets can still occur when in-flight prefills are being scheduled via the running path rather than freshly from waiting. (3) Verify whether requests scheduled via the RUNNING loop that remain prefilling are also inserted into `_inflight_prefills`; if not, add them there too.

(1) Removal from `_inflight_prefills` in the update_from_output path uses `if not request.is_prefill_chunk: discard(request)`. But requests in `WAITING_FOR_REMOTE_KVS` state (added at line 820) are not scheduled for forward/output and never appear in this cached-request update loop until their recv finishes and they are re-scheduled. If an async-loading request fails its recv and is recomputed or aborted through a path other than `_free_request`/`_preempt`/finish, its entry may linger in `_inflight_prefills`, inflating the reservation and starving subsequent loads. (2) A stale reservation permanently reduces admittable blocks, degrading throughput or wedging new async loads. (3) Confirm every terminal transition for a WAITING_FOR_REMOTE_KVS request (including recv-failure/recompute paths) discards it from `_inflight_prefills`; the diff only covers `_preempt`, finished-request free, and the cached-update loop.

(1) `_request_remaining_blocks` computes remaining full-ISL blocks using `new_computed_blocks=self.kv_cache_manager.empty_kv_cache_blocks.blocks` and `total_computed_tokens=request.num_computed_tokens`, ignoring any prefix-cached / already-allocated blocks the in-flight request holds. Since `get_num_blocks_to_allocate` accounts for already-allocated blocks via internal req_to_blocks state for that request_id, passing empty new_computed_blocks is likely correct, but the reservation is a full-sequence remaining estimate that may over-reserve for sliding-window/chunked-local groups. (2) Over-reservation makes the gate more conservative than needed, potentially refusing admittable loads and reducing throughput (partially mitigated by `apply_admission_cap=True`). (3) Verify the estimate matches the actual remaining allocation for SWA/hybrid models; add a shape/semantics assertion or test for hybrid cache configs.

The indexer-fanout pattern's `_pattern` traces `torch.ops.vllm.rocm_unquantized_gemm(rms, indexer_weight)` with only 2 positional args, while the actual DSv3.2 call site and the test model call it with 3 args `rocm_unquantized_gemm(rms3, self.indexer_w[0], None)` (explicit bias=None).
