(1) The `"mori"` value in the `All2AllBackend` Literal (vllm/config/parallel.py) is removed and replaced by `"mori_high_throughput"`/`"mori_low_latency"` with no deprecation/alias path. (2) Any existing serving command or config that passes `--all2all-backend mori` (or sets the equivalent config field) will now fail Literal/pydantic validation, silently breaking previously-working ROCm MoE EP scripts. Note that removed backends `pplx`/`naive` were given a graceful fallback in `_validate_parallel_config`, but `mori` gets none. (3) Add a compatibility shim: keep `"mori"` accepted and remap it to `"mori_high_throughput"` (the previous default behavior used `InterNodeV1`) inside `_validate_parallel_config`, ideally with a deprecation warning, mirroring the existing pplx/naive fallback.

(1) The commit title/message advertises kernel selection 'via VLLM_MORI_INTERNODE_KERNEL', but the diff introduces no such environment variable — selection is actually driven by the `--all2all-backend` value (`mori_low_latency` → InterNodeV1LL, else InterNodeV1). (2) A misleading commit message/mechanism description makes it hard for users to discover how to select the LL kernel and may lead them to set a non-existent env var. (3) Update the commit message (and any docs) to describe the actual `mori_high_throughput`/`mori_low_latency` backend selection, or add the advertised env var if it was intended.

(1) The InterNodeV1LL branch reuses the same `warp_num_per_block`/`block_num` tuning constants as InterNodeV1 (the diff only swaps `kernel_type` inside the existing else-block, leaving the subsequent gfx942 sizing unchanged). (2) Low-latency kernels typically have different launch-geometry requirements than high-throughput kernels; reusing high-throughput block/warp counts for the LL kernel may be suboptimal or, worst case, incorrect for the LL kernel path. (3) Confirm with the MoRI kernel authors that InterNodeV1LL is valid with the same warp/block configuration; if not, branch the sizing per kernel type.

(1) The new `moe_sorting_dispatch_policy` argument is threaded through the `rocm_aiter_ops.fused_moe` static method and passed positionally to `torch.ops.vllm.rocm_aiter_fused_moe(...)`, but the custom op registration at `op_name="rocm_aiter_fused_moe"` (around line 1590) wires `op_func=_rocm_aiter_fused_moe_impl` / `fake_impl=_rocm_aiter_fused_moe_fake`. The op schema is inferred from the impl signature. The diff adds `moe_sorting_dispatch_policy` to `_rocm_aiter_fused_moe_impl` and `_rocm_aiter_fused_moe_fake`, so this should line up — BUT verify the `mutates_args`/schema declaration in the `direct_register_custom_op` call at line ~1590 does not hardcode an argument list. If the schema string (or arg count) is fixed there, the new positional arg will not be part of the registered op and the call `torch.ops.vllm.rocm_aiter_fused_moe(..., moe_sorting_dispatch_policy)` will raise at runtime. This block was not shown in the diff, so confirm the registration auto-derives args. (2) A mismatch would cause every AITER fused MoE forward pass on ROCm to crash. (3) Ensure the custom-op registration derives its schema from the (updated) impl signature and that no explicit arg-count/schema is out of date.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run an AITER fused MoE forward (e.g. Qwen3/DeepSeek-V3 on gfx9) with default VLLM_ROCM_AITER_MOE_DISPATCH_POLICY=0 and confirm torch.ops.vllm.rocm_aiter_fused_moe accepts the extra positional arg.

findings.claim: (1) `_rocm_aiter_fused_moe_impl` now unconditionally forwards `moe_sorting_dispatch_policy=moe_sorting_dispatch_policy` as a keyword to `aiter.fused_moe.fused_moe(...)`. Older/pinned AITER builds that do not yet expose the `moe_sorting_dispatch_policy` kwarg will raise `TypeError: fused_moe() got an unexpected keyword argument`. Unlike the existing `_check_aiter_mla_fp8_support` / `check_aiter_fused_qk_rmsnorm` patterns in this same file, which do capability probing before passing new kwargs, this call passes the kwarg unconditionally. (2) This breaks users on any AITER version that predates the new kwarg, even when they leave the env var at its default 0 (a no-op policy), turning a purely additive perf feature into a hard runtime break. (3) Only pass `moe_sorting_dispatch_policy` when a capability check (e.g. inspect.signature on aiter.fused_moe) confirms support, or when the policy is non-zero; otherwise omit the kwarg to preserve backward compatibility.

(1) `get_moe_dispatch_policy` is decorated with `@if_aiter_supported`, whose wrapper returns `None` when `is_aiter_found_and_supported()` is False. In `AiterExperts.apply`, the result is passed as `moe_sorting_dispatch_policy=rocm_aiter_ops.get_moe_dispatch_policy()`. If the decorator ever returns `None` here, `None` flows down to `_rocm_aiter_fused_moe_impl` where it is forwarded as `moe_sorting_dispatch_policy=None` to the aiter kernel (and the registered custom op declares the arg as `int`, so `None` may also violate the schema). (2) In the AiterExperts path aiter is always supported so this is unlikely to trigger in practice, but the type contract (`-> int` returning `None`) is unsound and could crash or mis-dispatch if invoked in a non-supported context. (3) Return an explicit `0` fallback instead of relying on the `@if_aiter_supported` None-return, or drop the decorator on this pure env-read accessor since it does not touch aiter APIs.

(1) `_MOE_DISPATCH_POLICY` is cached the first time `get_moe_dispatch_policy()` is called and never re-read. The value is read from `envs.VLLM_ROCM_AITER_MOE_DISPATCH_POLICY`, which is itself a lazily-computed cached env access. Caching at the class level means later mutation of `os.environ` (e.g. in tests or dynamic reconfiguration) has no effect. (2) Minor; env vars are normally fixed at startup, but this double caching can confuse test setups that patch envs. (3) Consider relying solely on `envs`' own caching and dropping the extra class-level cache, or document that the value is frozen at first access.

(1) `VLLM_ROCM_AITER_MOE_DISPATCH_POLICY` is parsed with `int(os.getenv(..., "0"))` with no validation of the allowed set {0,1,2}. An out-of-range or non-integer value (e.g. `3` or `foo`) either silently passes an invalid policy to the aiter kernel or raises an unhelpful `ValueError` deep in env initialization. (2) Misconfiguration produces either a confusing crash or undefined kernel behavior. (3) Clamp/validate the value to the documented {0,1,2} set (or at least raise a clear error naming the env var).

(1) `min_p` and `top_k` on `EngineCoreSamplingParams` gain `#[serde(default)]`, meaning a missing field now deserializes to the Rust `Default` (0.0 / 0). (2) Risk: for `min_p` and `top_k`, 0 is the correct no-op default, so this is benign; but note that `top_p` and `repetition_penalty` correctly use custom `default_top_p`/`default_repetition_penalty` returning 1.0 because 0.0 would be semantically wrong (fully-truncating nucleus / degenerate penalty). Confirm no other newly-defaulted field (e.g. `temperature`, which remains required — good) silently accepts a wrong-valued default. (3) Fix: none required if the intent is only to let the mock engine deserialize partial payloads; the asymmetry between plain `#[serde(default)]` and the custom 1.0 defaults is intentional and correct. Documenting the rationale near the fields would prevent a future contributor from 'simplifying' the 1.0 defaults away.

(1) mimalloc is installed as the process-wide `#[global_allocator]` in the cmd binary. (2) This is a deliberate throughput optimization for the allocation-heavy async frontend and generally a win; the only risk is a modest RSS increase and platform build friction (libmimalloc-sys pulls in `cc`, requiring a C toolchain at build time for this crate). (3) No fix needed; ensure CI build images for all target platforms have a C compiler available, otherwise the cmd crate build will start failing.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end frontend request throughput / p99 latency with and without the mimalloc global allocator under concurrent load.

findings.claim: (1) The batched `take_senders_for_outputs` correctly reduces the registry mutex to a single acquisition per output batch (vs per-output previously), and behavior is preserved because `senders_for_outputs` resolves/removes finished requests sequentially in the same order as the old loop. (2) Positive change; one subtle note: the returned `Vec<Option<...>>` allocates one entry per output even for the common single-output batch. (3) Optional: for the frequent small-batch case this allocation is negligible, but if profiling shows it matters, a `SmallVec`/inline fast path could avoid it. Flagged only for awareness — no action required.

(1) `benchmarks/kernels/benchmark_moe.py` is NOT in this PR's changed-files set, yet it does `from vllm.model_executor.layers.fused_moe.fused_moe import *` and then calls `disable_inplace()` (lines ~274, 282) and `fused_experts(..., inplace=inplace, ...)`. This PR deletes `disable_inplace()` from `utils.py`, removes it from `fused_moe.py`'s imports, and removes the `inplace` parameter from `fused_experts()`. (2) The benchmark will therefore fail at runtime with `NameError: disable_inplace` / `TypeError: fused_experts() got an unexpected keyword argument 'inplace'` — the MoE tuning tool is broken, and CI/tuning workflows relying on it break. (3) Update `benchmark_moe.py`: drop the `disable_inplace()` calls and the `inplace=`/`FusedMoEKernel(..., inplace=...)` argument to match the new API.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run `python benchmarks/kernels/benchmark_moe.py` (tune mode) to confirm it imports and executes without NameError/TypeError.

findings.claim: (1) `fused_experts_impl` previously allowed writing the MoE output back into the `hidden_states` input buffer via `inplace=True` (`out_hidden_states = hidden_states if inplace else torch.empty_like(...)`); this PR unconditionally does `out_hidden_states = torch.empty_like(hidden_states)`. The same is true for the marlin path (`output = torch.empty_like(hidden_states)`) and the modular impl (`output = torch.empty_like(hidden_states)`). (2) For paths that previously enabled inplace (e.g. unquantized/int8/wna16/fp8 non-flashinfer, where `disable_inplace()` was False on torch < 2.9), this adds one extra full-size activation allocation per MoE forward, increasing peak memory and allocator pressure for large-batch decode/prefill. This is the intended trade-off (torch>=2.9 custom-op aliasing constraint per issue #26378), so it is a deliberate correctness-for-memory tradeoff rather than a bug, but reviewers should be aware the memory-savings on older torch are being dropped for everyone. (3) If the memory regression matters, consider gating the empty_like on a still-supported inplace path; otherwise document the expected memory increase.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory and MoE-layer throughput for a large MoE model (e.g. Mixtral/DeepSeek) at high batch size, comparing before/after this commit on torch<2.9.

findings.claim: (1) The single remaining custom op is renamed from the pair `inplace/outplace_fused_experts` to `fused_experts_op` and registered as `op_name="fused_experts"`, while the public Python wrapper is also `fused_experts`. (2) Having a module-level function `fused_experts` and a torch custom op registered under the same string name `fused_experts` (invoked as `torch.ops.vllm.fused_experts`) is slightly confusing but functionally fine; the wrapper func being `fused_experts_op` avoids a real collision. Low risk. (3) Consider naming the op registration distinctly (e.g. `vllm.fused_experts_op`) for clarity, or leave as-is if consistency with prior naming is preferred.

(1) In `supports_hma_config`, `connector_cls = cls.get_connector_class(kv_transfer_config)` is computed unconditionally at the top, but it is only used in the non-MultiConnector branch; for the MultiConnector branch the result is discarded and the code dispatches to `MultiConnector.all_children_support_hma`. (2) Impact: `get_connector_class` triggers an `importlib.import_module` of the (Multi)Connector module purely to be thrown away on the MultiConnector path; negligible perf but adds dead work and slight confusion. (3) Fix: move `connector_cls = cls.get_connector_class(...)` into the `if kv_transfer_config.kv_connector != "MultiConnector"` branch, before `return supports_hma(connector_cls)`.

(1) Behavioral change for the empty-connectors edge case: the old `__init__` computed `self._all_support_hma = all(supports_hma(c) for c in self._connectors)`, which yields True for an empty connector list, whereas the new `all_children_support_hma` explicitly returns False when `connectors_config` is empty. (2) Impact: for a degenerate MultiConnector config with an empty `connectors` list, HMA support flips from True to False, which could change auto-disable behavior; the downstream `assert (... or self._all_support_hma)` would now also behave differently. In practice an empty MultiConnector is already broken elsewhere, so risk is low. (3) Fix: confirm empty `connectors` is intended to be treated as unsupported (returning False seems more defensible), and ensure the empty case is rejected earlier with a clear error rather than silently changing HMA semantics.

(1) `from vllm.config.kv_transfer import KVTransferConfig` was moved from the `TYPE_CHECKING` block to a top-level runtime import in factory.py. (2) Impact: this introduces an eager runtime import of the config module at factory import time; if any config-side module later imports the factory (directly or transitively), this could create a circular import. Currently `vllm/config/kv_transfer.py` does not import the factory, so it is safe today, but it removes the previous decoupling. (3) Fix: since `KVTransferConfig` is only referenced in a string annotation on `supports_hma_config`, it can remain under TYPE_CHECKING; keep the runtime import local to functions that instantiate it (as `multi_connector.py` already does) to preserve import-cycle safety.

(1) The new file `sparse_attn_compress_cutedsl.py` is placed under `common/ops/` and imports `cutlass`, `cutlass.cute`, `cuda.bindings.driver`, and `quack.compile_utils` at module top level. (2) This violates the established repo convention documented in `vllm/models/deepseek_v4/nvidia/ops/__init__.py`: modules that import cutlass/cutedsl at top level must live under `nvidia/ops/` and must not be imported on non-CUDA platforms; every existing CuTe DSL kernel (dequant_gather_k_cutedsl.py, fused_indexer_q_cutedsl.py) follows this and is only imported behind a `has_cutedsl()` guard. Placing this in the platform-agnostic `common/ops/` directory risks direct top-level imports of the module on non-CUDA platforms, which would raise ImportError. (3) Move the file to `vllm/models/deepseek_v4/nvidia/ops/sparse_attn_compress_cutedsl.py` to match the convention, and keep the lazy import wrapper.

(1) The three new public wrapper functions in `fused_compress_quant_cache.py` (`_compress_kv_sparse_attn_cutedsl`, `_norm_rope_insert_sparse_attn_cutedsl`, `_fused_kv_compress_norm_rope_insert_sparse_attn_cutedsl`) and the corresponding `SparseAttnCompress*` kernel classes have no read/use path in the reviewed snapshot — grep finds no caller (e.g. `compressor.py` still dispatches only to the Triton `_fused_kv_compress_norm_rope_insert_sparse_attn`). (2) Newly added identifiers without a runtime use path are dead code by the Mandatory Check; if the wiring is intentionally deferred to a follow-up PR, that should be documented. (3) Either wire the wrappers into the compressor dispatch (gated on `has_cutedsl()`, mirroring `fused_indexer_q.py`) or add an explicit TODO/migration note stating the integration is deferred.

(1) In `SparseAttnCompressNormRopeStoreC4Kernel.compile`, the parameter `kv_block_stride: int = 74752` is accepted but never referenced in the body (the kernel derives all KV offsets from `k_cache.stride[0]` at runtime). (2) A silently-unused argument invites callers to pass a value expecting it to take effect (the Triton path passes `KV_BLOCK_STRIDE=kv_cache.stride(0)` and relies on it), creating a latent inconsistency between the Triton and CuTe DSL paths. (3) Either remove the unused `kv_block_stride` parameter or add an assertion validating it against the runtime `k_cache.stride[0]`.

(1) The `min_scale = 1.0e-4` clamp in the C4 CuTe DSL FP8 quant path differs from the reference/Triton indexer behavior: `_fused_indexer_q_rope_quant_kernel` and the Triton sparse-attn kernel clamp absmax to `1e-4` (matching), but the MXFP4 reference clamps to `6.0 * 2^-126`. (2) Because this kernel is intended to be numerically equivalent to the existing Triton `_fused_kv_compress_norm_rope_insert_sparse_attn` (which uses `tl.maximum(block_absmax, 1e-4)` then `ceil(log2(absmax/FP8_MAX))`), any divergence in the scale-derivation math (here it computes `ue8m0 = ((bits + 0x7FFFFF) >> 23) & 0xFF` on the raw scale rather than `ceil(log2(...))+127`) could produce off-by-one exponents at block boundaries and silently different FP8 outputs vs. the Triton path. (3) Add a numerical parity test comparing the CuTe DSL C4 kernel output against the Triton `_fused_kv_compress_norm_rope_insert_sparse_attn` on identical inputs, verifying the UE8M0 scale bytes match exactly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Numerical parity: run Triton and CuTe DSL C4 compress kernels on identical random state_cache/positions and assert FP8 bytes + UE8M0 scale bytes are bit-identical.

findings.claim: (1) The XpuFusedMoe impl is constructed lazily on the first apply() call and cached in self.fused_moe_impl, capturing references to w1/w2 (and their scales/bias via self.w1_scale etc.), topk, activation, and EP config from that first call. Subsequent calls reuse the cached impl and no longer re-read w1/w2/topk/activation from the incoming arguments. (2) Risk: if any of these captured values change across calls for the same layer instance — e.g. weight tensors get replaced/reallocated after load (weight hot-swap, LoRA, quantization re-prep such as prepare_fp8_moe_layer_for_xpu re-running), or topk/activation vary — the stale cached impl would silently use the old weights, producing wrong outputs with no error. (3) Fix: document/verify the invariant that w1, w2, topk, activation, and EP config are immutable for the lifetime of an XPUExperts instance; alternatively add a cheap identity/shape guard (e.g. assert cached w13 is w1) or key the cache on the weight tensor identity so a change invalidates the cache. In current vLLM usage these are stable per-layer, so the optimization is likely safe, hence low severity.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run an XPU MoE model (e.g. Mixtral/Qwen-MoE) forward before and after this change and compare per-token logits for bitwise/tolerance equality, plus measure decode-step host overhead (CPU time per apply) to confirm the reduction.

findings.claim: (1) self.fused_moe_impl is typed XpuFusedMoe | None where XpuFusedMoe is only imported under `if current_platform.is_xpu():`. (2) Because this is an instance-attribute annotation inside __init__, the annotation expression is NOT evaluated at runtime, so there is no NameError on non-XPU platforms — confirming the change is safe there. This is a non-issue after analysis and requires no action; noting only to record that the import-guard interaction was checked. (3) No fix needed.

has_finished_requests() now computes len(self.waiting) + len(self.skipped_waiting) + len(self.running) on every call to determine if delayed-connector-cleanup requests remain. In the engine core busy loop, has_requests()/has_finished_requests() are polled every iteration. For request queues that are deques/lists, len() is O(1), so the added cost is small; however, this method is now non-trivially more expensive than the prior single len() check, and the counting couples has_finished_requests to internal queue structure. Impact: minor per-step overhead in the hot polling path, and fragility if a queue's len becomes non-O(1). Fix: consider tracking a dedicated counter/set of requests awaiting delayed connector free (e.g. reuse finished_recving_kv_req_ids-style bookkeeping) rather than deriving it by subtracting queue lengths from len(self.requests).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark scheduler busy loop steps/sec with use_kv_connector=True and many concurrent requests, comparing has_finished_requests() cost before/after.

findings.claim: The delayed-free detection derives 'finished but not-yet-freed' requests as len(self.requests) > len(self.waiting)+len(self.skipped_waiting)+len(self.running). This assumes every request in self.requests is in exactly one of those three queues unless it is a finished request pending connector cleanup. If any other transient state exists where a request is in self.requests but temporarily absent from all three queues (e.g. requests being moved between queues, or WAITING_FOR_REMOTE_KVS bookkeeping), this heuristic could report has_finished_requests()==True spuriously, keeping the engine polling loop awake unnecessarily. Impact: potential busy-wait (1ms sleep loop never exits) or extra scheduler steps. Fix: verify the invariant holds across all request lifecycle states, or track pending-delayed-free requests explicitly instead of by count subtraction; add an assertion documenting the invariant.

(1) The metadata precompute path now unconditionally calls cu_seqlens.cpu().numpy() and cu_window_seqlens.cpu().numpy() at the top of the function, whereas the parent code kept cu_seqlens on CPU only long enough and moved to device with non_blocking=True. (2) A .cpu()/.numpy() call is a blocking GPU->GPU->CPU sync; if either tensor originates on GPU, this forces a host-device synchronization on every metadata build, which the rubric flags as a throughput killer. However, in this model cu_seqlens is typically constructed on CPU already (from grid_thw on host), so the .cpu() may be a no-op. (3) Confirm cu_seqlens/cu_window_seqlens are already on CPU at this point; if they can be on GPU, gate the .cpu().numpy() conversion behind the FlashInfer backend check (matching maybe_compute_seq_lens/maybe_recompute_cu_seqlens which no-op for other backends) so non-FlashInfer backends do not pay a sync.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-step ViT metadata build latency (and any added CUDA sync) between FlashInfer and FLASH_ATTN backends on Qwen2.5-VL with a representative image batch.

findings.claim: (1) Qwen2_5_VisionAttention.forward changed the type annotation of sequence_lengths from torch.Tensor to torch.Tensor | None without a default; the sibling Qwen3_VisionBlock in qwen3_vl.py still declares sequence_lengths as torch.Tensor (non-optional) and passes it positionally. (2) This is a type-annotation-only change (still required positional arg), so it does not break callers, but the inconsistency between the two vision blocks (one Optional-with-default at block level, the other still non-Optional) could mislead future maintainers. (3) Consider aligning the annotation/optionality of sequence_lengths across Qwen2_5_VisionBlock and Qwen3_VisionBlock for consistency.

(1) The forward reads sequence_lengths_full/window via encoder_metadata.get(...) (returns None if missing) while the sibling max_seqlen_* keys are read via direct indexing. Using .get() silently tolerates absent keys, which for a fresh model always populates them; the defensive .get() is presumably for backward-compat with cached CUDA-graph replay buffers that predate these keys. (2) If a stale replay buffer lacks these keys, sequence_lengths becomes None and the FlashInfer wrapper asserts (assert sequence_lengths is not None), turning a missing-metadata bug into a hard crash rather than a clear error. (3) Confirm the EncoderCudaGraphReplayBuffers metadata_keys list (updated at line ~1651) guarantees these keys are always present when FlashInfer is used; if so, use direct indexing for consistency, otherwise document why .get() is needed.

(1) The inner accumulation loop in mhc_post_tilelang was changed from `for i_hci in T.serial(hc)` to `for i_hci in T.vectorized(hc)`, but the loop body is a reduction that accumulates into a single scalar (`x_local[i_hco, i1_h] += a_local[i_hci, i_hco] * b_local[i_hci, i1_h]`). (2) `T.vectorized` expresses independent SIMD lanes over contiguous elements; a loop with a loop-carried dependency onto the same accumulator location does not have well-defined vectorized semantics and can either fail to lower or silently produce incorrect matmul-like results (each x[i_n, hc, h] element is `c*d + sum_hci a*b`). Additionally the vectorized indices `a_local[i_hci, i_hco]` and `b_local[i_hci, i1_h]` stride along the FIRST (i_hci) tensor dimension, so accesses are non-contiguous, which violates the contiguity assumption of vectorized memory access. (3) Keep this reduction loop as `T.serial(hc)` (hc is small, typically 4). If vectorization is desired, it must be applied to a contiguous dimension via an explicit horizontal-reduction pattern, not to the reduction axis itself. Recommend numerical validation against the reference/aiter path before merging.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the mhc post correctness test comparing mhc_post_tilelang output against the reference implementation for hc_mult=4 and several hidden sizes/token counts; assert max abs error within bf16 tolerance.

findings.claim: (1) The pipelined loop `for i0_h in T.Pipelined(T.ceildiv(h, h_blk), num_stages=2)` was changed to `for i0_h in T.Serial(...)`, removing double-buffered software pipelining of the b/d global loads. (2) This removes overlap of the global-memory loads (`T.copy(b...)`, `T.copy(d...)`) with the compute of the previous h-block, which can reduce throughput on this memory-bound epilogue kernel; the benefit of removing the extra shared-memory staging (x_shared) may or may not offset the lost load/compute overlap. (3) Confirm via benchmark that the Serial variant is not slower than the Pipelined variant across the token/hidden-size regime this kernel runs in (num_tokens > fma_token_threshold path); if it regresses, retain pipelining while still dropping the redundant x_shared copy.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark mhc_post_tilelang latency (Serial vs Pipelined num_stages=2) for hidden in {2048,4096,8192} and num_tokens in {32,128,512}.

findings.claim: (1) load_hidden_states() opens the companion lock file with open(lock_path) (read mode) and unconditionally acquires fcntl.LOCK_SH, but the code path only creates a .lock file when the writer uses locking (use_synchronization_lock / use_lock). When use_lock is False the writer never creates a .lock file, so a reader calling load_hidden_states() will raise FileNotFoundError. (2) The documented workflow (docs list use_synchronization_lock=False as a valid batch mode) tells users to disable locking, yet the public reader helper always requires the lock file, so the documented mode is broken for anyone using load_hidden_states(). (3) Make load_hidden_states() tolerate a missing lock file (e.g. skip the flock / fall back to a plain load when the .lock file is absent), or document that load_hidden_states() must not be used when locking is disabled.

(1) In load_hidden_states(), the reader opens the lock file and takes a shared lock only *after* the file may or may not exist; there is a TOCTOU race in the online path: the async writer creates the target .safetensors and .lock files at some point, but a client that receives hidden_states_path and calls load_hidden_states() before the writer has created the lock file will hit FileNotFoundError rather than blocking. (2) The docstring explicitly promises it 'blocks (without polling) until the async write is complete', but blocking only works if the lock file already exists when the reader opens it; there is no wait-for-creation logic. (3) Ensure the writer creates the .lock file (and takes LOCK_EX on it) synchronously before returning hidden_states_path to the engine output, so the reader is guaranteed the lock file exists.

(1) The chunked-prefill incompatibility check hardcodes the connector name string 'ExampleHiddenStatesConnector' inside VllmConfig. (2) This couples generic config validation to a specific example connector by name; if the connector is renamed or a new hidden-state connector is added, the guard silently stops applying, allowing an unsupported (chunked prefill) configuration to run and produce wrong/incomplete extraction. (3) Prefer expressing the requirement as a connector capability/attribute (e.g. a class flag like requires_disable_chunked_prefill) queried generically, rather than string-matching the connector name in core config code.

(1) __init__ imports get_forward_context and creates worker-side async write infrastructure (copy stream, ThreadPoolExecutor) unconditionally regardless of KVConnectorRole. (2) On the scheduler-side role the thread pool and CUDA-stream machinery are never used, so this allocates a background thread pool (default 8 threads) needlessly in the scheduler process. (3) Guard the async-write executor/copy-stream creation on role == KVConnectorRole.WORKER so scheduler-side instances don't spawn writer threads.

(1) In benchmark_config, the same `state` tensor is fed to `selective_state_update` inside the CUDA graph, which updates it in place every replay; over `graph_batch_size` * (5 warmup + num_iters) replays the state values drift far outside the input distribution. (2) This does not corrupt timing (kernel cost is data-independent here), but if a future maintainer reuses `benchmark_config` for anything that reads back state, the accumulated in-place mutation would silently produce garbage. (3) Consider re-initializing or noting explicitly that the buffer contents are intentionally ignored for timing.

(1) save_configs stringifies effective_batch keys (`{str(k): v ...}`) when writing JSON, and the envs.py comment states the runtime loader ignores only `triton_version`. If the runtime config loader in mamba_ssm.py looks up configs by integer effective_batch key without str() coercion, the saved configs will never match and the kernel silently falls back to the hard-coded heuristic. (2) This would make the entire tuning effort a no-op at runtime with no error. (3) Verify the loader parses/normalizes JSON keys to the same type used for lookup (likely int) — recommend an integration test that round-trips a saved config through the loader.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Save a config via save_configs, then call selective_state_update with matching (headdim,dstate,cache_dtype,effective_batch) and assert the tuned BLOCK_SIZE_M/num_warps are actually applied (not the heuristic).

findings.claim: (1) `_SSM_CACHE_DTYPE_MAP` maps `bfloat16` -> `torch.float16` (with a comment that bf16 shares configs with fp16). Using this as the actual `state_dtype` for the state cache tensor in _make_inputs means when a user passes --mamba-ssm-cache-dtype bfloat16, validation/tuning runs with an fp16 state cache, not bf16. (2) fp16 and bf16 have different rounding/dynamic range; the tuned latency is essentially identical (same bit width) so config selection is unaffected, but the --validate correctness check runs against the wrong cache dtype, potentially masking a bf16-specific accuracy issue. (3) Separate the config-filename canonicalization (correctly fp16==bf16) from the runtime tensor dtype, or document that bf16 validation is deliberately proxied by fp16.

(1) `init_attn_backend` now calls `add_kv_sharing_layers_to_kv_cache_groups(...)` which mutates `kv_cache_config.kv_cache_groups` in place by appending the sharing layer names to their target's `KVCacheGroupSpec.layer_names` list. (2) Risk: if `init_attn_backend` (or the enclosing initialize-kv-cache path) is ever invoked more than once on the same `kv_cache_config` object (e.g. re-initialization, profiling re-runs, spec-decode/eagle re-entry), the same sharing layers will be appended again, producing duplicate entries in `layer_names`, which then double-count in the Phase-1 discovery loop and in `_reshape_kv_cache`. The v1 path (`gpu_model_runner.maybe_add_kv_sharing_layers_to_kv_cache_groups`) guards this by being called exactly once during `initialize_kv_cache`; the new v2 call happens inside `init_attn_backend` which is a more re-entrant point. (3) Fix: make the mutation idempotent (skip layers already present in the target group's `layer_names`), or perform the append once at a dedicated initialization site rather than inside `init_attn_backend`.

(1) The `_allocate_kv_cache` assertion was relaxed from `layer_names == set(kv_cache_raw_tensors.keys())` to `layer_names == (kv_cache_raw_tensors.keys() | shared_layers.keys())`. (2) Because shared layers were appended to a group's `layer_names` (via `add_kv_sharing_layers_to_kv_cache_groups`) but intentionally have no allocated raw tensor, this union is required. However the union also weakens the check: it would no longer catch the case where a *non-shared* layer is missing from `kv_cache_raw_tensors` but happens to appear in `shared_layers` keys, or where a `shared_layers` key is not actually part of any group. Consider asserting the two invariants separately: (a) every group layer that is not a shared layer has a raw tensor, and (b) every shared-layer key resolves to an existing target tensor. (3) This tightens the safety net without adding runtime cost.

(1) In `_reshape_kv_cache`, shared layers are aliased with `kv_caches[layer_name] = kv_caches[target_layer_name]`. This assumes the target layer's KV cache was materialized in the same call (it is skipped only for the shared layer itself). (2) If a shared layer's `kv_sharing_target_layer_name` points to a layer that is itself absent from `kv_caches` (e.g. target is an encoder-only layer with no KV spec, or target ordering/typo), this raises a `KeyError` with no descriptive message, making misconfiguration hard to diagnose. (3) Add an explicit assertion/error: `assert target_layer_name in kv_caches, f"KV-sharing target '{target_layer_name}' for layer '{layer_name}' has no allocated KV cache"`.

(1) `get_layers_from_vllm_config`'s `layer_names` parameter type was widened from `list[str] | None` to `Iterable[str] | None`, and the body now iterates the passed value directly in a dict comprehension. (2) Behavioral subtlety: when a caller passes a one-shot generator/iterator, iterating it once inside the comprehension is fine, but the previous code materialized `list(...keys())` only in the None branch; the new default `forward_context.keys()` is a live view — if `static_forward_context` is mutated during iteration the result differs. This is unlikely in practice but is a behavior change from the previously materialized list. (3) Confirm no caller relies on `layer_names` being consumed twice; the widening itself is a safe API relaxation (accepts more inputs) so backward compat for existing `list` callers is preserved.

(1) After the change, when VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT is set and CutlassNvFp4LinearKernel.is_supported() returns True, force_kernel is set to CutlassNvFp4LinearKernel and later validated via is_supported+can_implement. If the later validation fails (e.g. can_implement returns False for some future config, or is_supported becomes false at selection time under a different compute_capability), init_nvfp4_linear_kernel falls through to the normal auto-selection path (_POSSIBLE_NVFP4_KERNELS) which can pick a NON-deterministic kernel (FlashInferCutlassNvFp4, MarlinNvFp4, etc.). This silently violates the batch-invariance guarantee the user requested. (2) Batch-invariant mode is a correctness contract; silently selecting a non-invariant kernel produces batch-size-dependent outputs with no error. (3) When VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT is set, if the forced Cutlass kernel cannot be used, fall back explicitly to EmulationNvFp4LinearKernel (as the else-branch already does) rather than allowing generic auto-selection. Alternatively pass a batch-invariant-restricted possible_kernels list so no non-invariant kernel can be chosen.

(1) The Python side gates the batch-invariant CUTLASS path purely on CutlassNvFp4LinearKernel.is_supported(), which only checks cutlass_fp4_supported() (device capability >= SM100/SM120 FP4). However the native batch-invariant path only actually engages when vllm::vllm_is_batch_invariant() reads VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT at the time of the FIRST GEMM (the value is cached statically in batch_invariant.hpp). If the env var was not set in the process when the first FP4 GEMM ran, the C++ side stays in the M-based dispatch (non-invariant) even though the Python layer selected CutlassNvFp4LinearKernel believing it to be batch-invariant. (2) This creates a silent mismatch between the Python selection intent and native behavior, producing non-deterministic outputs under a config that claims determinism. (3) The test file documents this caching hazard, but production code has no guard; consider asserting/logging that VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT is set before the first kernel launch, or documenting that init must occur before any FP4 GEMM.

(1) The SM100 sm100_fp4_config_default now explicitly sets TileScheduler = cutlass::gemm::PersistentScheduler, whereas previously the GemmKernel was instantiated with `void` (letting CUTLASS pick the default scheduler). (2) For the non-batch-invariant large-M path this changes the instantiated kernel from the CUTLASS-default scheduler to an explicit persistent scheduler; if the previous default was different (e.g. a data-parallel/stream-K variant), large-M performance characteristics could change subtly. (3) Confirm the prior `void` default resolved to PersistentScheduler for this arch so the non-BI path performance is unchanged; if not, add a dedicated BI config as the comment itself recommends and leave the default path scheduler as `void`.

(1) In batch-invariant mode the SM100 path always uses sm100_fp4_config_default (TileShape 256x256x256, PerSm 128x256x256) regardless of M, even for very small M (e.g. M=1 decode). (2) For small-M workloads this large-tile config wastes SM occupancy and increases latency compared to the M16/M256 configs used in the normal path; batch-invariant serving of decode-heavy workloads will see throughput/latency regressions. (3) This is an intentional and documented tradeoff (fixed large-M tiling for invariance), but worth benchmarking the decode-path latency impact to confirm it is acceptable.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare decode latency (M=1..16) NVFP4 linear with and without VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT on SM100/SM120 to quantify the fixed-large-tile overhead.

findings.claim: This diff removes a `torch.accelerator.synchronize()` in `rearrange_expert_weights_inplace`, right after the intermediate `weights_buffer` is allocated and before `old/new_global_expert_indices.cpu().numpy()` and the per-layer transfer loop. The original author explicitly documented that this synchronize was empirically required (`NOTE(bowen): We need this synchronize to run, but I don't know why`). Because the copies into/out of the intermediate buffers use `copy_(..., non_blocking=True)` and run on custom CUDA streams (`torch.cuda.stream(cuda_stream)`), the synchronize likely served as a barrier ensuring prior GPU work (buffer allocation / prior-layer async copies / stream ordering) completed before the CPU began issuing the next round of stream-ordered P2P transfers. WHY IT MATTERS: EPLB weight rearrangement mutates live expert weights in place; a missing barrier can produce a race where reads/copies observe stale or partially-transferred buffers, causing silently wrong expert weights after rebalance (correctness bug that is intermittent and hardware/timing dependent, hard to reproduce in CI). Removing an empirically-load-bearing sync whose purpose was never understood is high-risk. FIX: Do not remove blindly. First identify what invariant the sync guaranteed (e.g. whether the CpuGpuEvent / consumed_event and communicator.execute() stream synchronization already cover it), and confirm via a multi-node EP stress test that rebalanced weights are bit-exact vs. the pre-change path. If the sync is truly redundant, document the analysis in the commit; the RFC tag suggests this is not yet fully verified.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-rank EP EPLB rebalance correctness stress test: run repeated rearrange_expert_weights_inplace cycles under load and assert rebalanced expert weights are bit-exact against a version that keeps the synchronize; also A/B rebalance latency to quantify the throughput win from removing the full-device sync.

findings.claim: If the removed `torch.accelerator.synchronize()` is genuinely dead/redundant, this is a legitimate performance improvement: `torch.accelerator.synchronize()` is a full-device blocking sync that stalls the CPU until all GPU work completes, and it ran once per `rearrange_expert_weights_inplace` invocation on the rebalance path. Removing it eliminates a host-device stall and lets the subsequent async, stream-ordered buffer copies and P2P transfers overlap. WHY IT MATTERS: reduces rebalance-induced pause in the serving/training loop. FIX: keep the removal only if the correctness concern above is resolved; otherwise the perf gain is not worth the divergence risk.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time a full EPLB rebalance sweep (all MoE layers) with and without the synchronize on a multi-GPU EP setup to measure the stall reduction.

findings.claim: (1) The auto-select branch for `communicator is None` was restructured so that the `use_async` case no longer maps to `torch_gloo`. Previously: `use_async` -> `torch_gloo`, else -> `torch_nccl`. Now (non-elastic-EP): if nixl available -> `nixl`, else -> `torch_gloo` for BOTH async and sync EPLB. (2) This is a silent behavior change for the *synchronous* EPLB default: previously synchronous EPLB defaulted to `torch_nccl` (device NCCL P2P, fast GPU-to-GPU), and now it defaults to either `nixl` or `torch_gloo` (CPU-staged gloo). For users relying on the default communicator with sync EPLB and no nixl installed, expert-weight transfers now go through CPU staging (D2H copy + gloo + H2D copy), which is substantially slower than the previous device-NCCL path. This can materially slow down (or change the throughput profile of) EPLB rebalancing steps. (3) If the intent is only to change the *async* default, restore the `torch_nccl` default for the synchronous (`not use_async`) case, or explicitly document that the sync default has changed and why.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable EPLB (sync, use_async=False) with default communicator on a multi-rank EP setup, with and without nixl installed; measure per-rearrange wall time and end-to-end throughput vs the parent commit's torch_nccl default.

findings.claim: (1) `is_nixl_available()` uses `importlib.util.find_spec(pkg)` to detect the package, which reports whether the package is *installed/importable as a spec*, whereas the actual runtime capability used by NixlEplbCommunicator (and `has_nixl()` in eplb_communicator.py) is `nixl_utils.NixlWrapper is not None`, i.e. whether the `nixl._api.nixl_agent` symbol can actually be imported and loaded. These two checks can disagree: `find_spec('nixl')` may succeed (top-level package present) while `NixlWrapper` loading fails (e.g. missing `_api` submodule, broken C-extension bindings, ImportError during load). (2) When they disagree, the config validator selects `communicator = 'nixl'`, but later `create_eplb_communicator`/`NixlEplbCommunicator.__init__` raises `RuntimeError('NIXL/ RIXL is unavailable.')`, turning a graceful fallback into a hard startup failure for users who merely have a partially-installed/broken nixl. (3) Reuse the existing capability check (`has_nixl()` / `nixl_utils.NixlWrapper is not None`) for the default-selection decision, or at minimum make `is_nixl_available()` verify the actual submodule can be imported, so detection and construction agree.

(1) The nixl-availability probe is performed inside the config validator via a deferred import of `is_nixl_available`, which calls `importlib.util.find_spec`. This runs once at config construction, so overhead is negligible; however `find_spec` can trigger package `__init__` side effects / filesystem scans. (2) Minor: this is a one-time cost, not in a hot path. (3) Acceptable as-is; noting only that the availability result is not cached and the doc/comment on the `communicator` field (still says 'None: Auto-select backend ("torch_gloo" for async, "torch_nccl" for sync)') is now stale and should be updated to reflect the new nixl/torch_gloo selection.

(1) The docstring for the `communicator` field on `EPLBConfig` still reads: 'None: Auto-select backend ("torch_gloo" for async, "torch_nccl" for sync)'. (2) After this change the auto-selection logic no longer matches that description (it now prefers nixl, falls back to torch_gloo, and no longer uses torch_nccl for the sync default), so the docstring is now misleading — a wrong docstring is worse than none for users trying to understand default behavior. (3) Update the docstring to describe the new selection: elastic-EP -> pynccl; otherwise nixl if available else torch_gloo.

(1) `current_platform.mem_get_info()` is now called once per resolution bucket inside `_process_image_input` (and once per video forward), replacing the old cached lazy-init that queried device memory only once. `mem_get_info` performs a CUDA driver call (cudaMemGetInfo) which forces a host-device sync and has non-trivial latency; on the image path it is invoked inside the `for patches, items in buckets.items()` loop, so a request carrying many distinct image resolutions triggers multiple driver syncs per forward. (2) This adds recurring CPU-GPU synchronization to the multimodal encode hot path, hurting throughput and potentially disrupting overlap. (3) If sizing off *currently-free* memory per bucket is the intent (the comment justifies re-reading), keep it but consider reading once outside the loop unless per-bucket accounting of prior-bucket activations is truly required; otherwise hoist the `mem_get_info()` call out of the bucket loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark Gemma4 multimodal decode/prefill throughput with a request batch containing many distinct image resolutions, comparing per-bucket mem_get_info vs a single hoisted call.

findings.claim: (1) The new cost model in `_encoder_chunk` computes `cost = patches_per_item * 4 * position_embedding_size * 8`. The docstring says the one_hot transient has shape `(chunk, patches, 2, position_embedding_size)` int64 = `chunk * patches * 2 * pos_emb * 8` bytes, and the extra `2x` (making the `4`) covers the concurrent bf16 cast + matmul output. However bf16 is 2 bytes and int64 is 8 bytes, so the cast buffer is `chunk*patches*2*pos_emb*2` (¼ of the int64 buffer), not equal to it — the `4` (=2*2) intentionally over-provisions. That is safe (conservative) but the derivation in the comment overstates the cast cost; if a future edit trims the factor based on the (incorrect) stated reasoning it could under-budget and reintroduce the OOM. (2) Risk is latent: comment/const mismatch invites an unsafe optimization later. (3) Clarify the comment to state the `4` is a conservative safety multiplier rather than an exact byte accounting, or make it a named constant.

(1) `budget = min(free_bytes // 2, total_bytes // 10)` caps the encoder transient at 10% of total device memory, but the removed logic used a fixed 5% budget lazily read once. On a tight-free-memory device the `free_bytes // 2` term dominates (the intended fix), but on a device where a lot is free early in profile_run the `total_bytes // 10` cap governs, roughly doubling the prior 5% budget. (2) This changes chunk sizing behavior (larger chunks) for large-memory GPUs versus the previous default, which is a behavioral change worth confirming is intended and does not risk OOM during the subsequent encoder+pooler passes the comment references. (3) Confirm the 10% cap is validated against peak encoder+pooler memory on high-memory GPUs.

(1) `load_weights` no longer sets `self._encoder_bytes_per_patch`; the field and the `_encoder_budget_bytes` field were removed from `__init__`. This is internal state with no external API contract, and grep confirms no other references remain, so the removal is clean. (2)/(3) No action needed — noting only that the removed side effect in `load_weights` (which previously returned `loaded` after setting per-patch cost) is now a pure return, preserving the return value; behavior is equivalent.

(1) NUM_TOKEN_CUTOFF is declared as `constexpr float NUM_TOKEN_CUTOFF = 1024;` but semantically represents a token count and is only ever compared against the integer `num_tokens_full` (`if (num_tokens_full < NUM_TOKEN_CUTOFF)`). (2) Using a float for an integral threshold forces an int->float promotion on every launch and is a type/semantics smell; while 1024 is exactly representable so there is no correctness bug today, a larger or non-power-of-two cutoff edited later could introduce a surprising rounding boundary. (3) Declare it as `constexpr int NUM_TOKEN_CUTOFF = 1024;` (and consider `SCREAMING_CASE`->`kNumTokenCutoff` to match the file's `k`-prefixed constant convention).

(1) The refactored RoPE load in processDeepseekV4Slot replaces the original per-element `VLLM_LDG(...)` (read-only __ldg data-cache path) with plain `*reinterpret_cast<float4 const*>(cos_ptr + half_base)` global loads for cos/sin. (2) cos_sin_cache is a read-only, heavily broadcast-shared constant table; dropping the __ldg / read-only cache hint can reduce L1/tex-cache residency and add DRAM traffic for the small-token path that now also routes through this helper. (3) Use the vectorized read-only load intrinsic (e.g. `__ldg` on a `const float4*`) to preserve the read-only cache hint while keeping the 16-byte vectorized access.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark the fused DSv4 qnorm-rope-kv kernel at num_tokens in {64, 2048} comparing float4 __ldg vs plain float4 loads for cos_sin_cache; measure kernel time and L2 read throughput.

findings.claim: (1) The file now ends without a trailing newline ('\ No newline at end of file' in the diff). (2) Missing final newline can trip POSIX text-tooling and some clang-format/lint checks and produces noisy diffs on the next edit. (3) Add a trailing newline at EOF. (Style-only; flagged only as a low-priority note.)

(1) The refactor makes the top-k step call `ops.top_k_per_row_prefill`/`ops.top_k_per_row_decode` unconditionally for both CUDA and XPU (in `sparse_attn_indexer`), where `ops` is now always `vllm._custom_ops`. Those wrappers dispatch to `torch.ops._C.top_k_per_row_prefill` / `torch.ops._C.top_k_per_row_decode` (the CUDA `_C` extension). Previously the XPU path used `xpu_ops.top_k_per_row_prefill`/`decode`, which were pure-torch implementations (removed in this diff). (2) If the `_C` (CUDA) extension does not register `top_k_per_row_prefill`/`top_k_per_row_decode` on an XPU build, the XPU sparse-attention path will fail at runtime with a missing-op error — the exact feature this PR claims to enable. (3) Verify that `torch.ops._C.top_k_per_row_{prefill,decode}` are actually available/registered in XPU builds; if not, keep an XPU-specific implementation (torch fallback or a `_xpu_C` op) instead of removing the pure-torch ones.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the DeepSeek-V3.2 sparse-attention indexer prefill+decode path on an XPU device and assert top_k_per_row ops resolve and produce correct topk_indices.

findings.claim: (1) This diff removes the pure-torch XPU implementations `xpu_ops.indexer_k_quant_and_cache` and `xpu_ops.cp_gather_indexer_k_quant_cache` from `vllm/_xpu_ops.py`, but `sparse_attn_indexer` still calls `ops.indexer_k_quant_and_cache(...)` and `ops.cp_gather_indexer_k_quant_cache(...)`, where `ops` is now unconditionally `vllm._custom_ops`. Those wrappers dispatch to `torch.ops._C_cache_ops.indexer_k_quant_and_cache` and the `_C` gather op. (2) On XPU, the `_C_cache_ops`/`_C` cache kernels are the CUDA C++ extension; if they are not built for XPU, the K-quant-cache and cp-gather steps that previously worked via the removed pure-torch fallbacks will now raise at runtime, breaking the XPU indexer. (3) Confirm the `_C_cache_ops.indexer_k_quant_and_cache` and cp-gather ops are registered on XPU builds; otherwise retain XPU-specific fallbacks for these two ops.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On XPU, exercise sparse_attn_indexer with skip_k_cache_insert=False and a chunked-prefill kv-gather path; verify indexer_k_quant_and_cache and cp_gather_indexer_k_quant_cache resolve.

findings.claim: (1) In the decode branch the XPU path derives `seq_lens_xpu = seq_lens[:, -1] if seq_lens.ndim == 2 else seq_lens` and passes it to `xpu_fp8_paged_mqa_logits`, but the subsequent top-k call `ops.top_k_per_row_decode(logits, next_n, seq_lens, ...)` is still passed the original 2D `seq_lens`. (2) The removed pure-torch `top_k_per_row_decode` indexed `seq_lens[row_indices]` assuming a 1D tensor of length batch_size; if the now-shared `_C`/kernel implementation expects the same 1D layout, passing a 2D `(B, next_n)` `seq_lens` here could produce wrong masking/indices, silently corrupting sparse top-k selection. (3) Verify whether the decode top-k kernel expects 1D vs 2D seq_lens and pass a consistent representation (likely `seq_lens_xpu`) into `ops.top_k_per_row_decode`.

(1) The new XPU fake impls compute output shapes from input shapes: `_xpu_fp8_mqa_logits_fake` returns `(q.shape[0], k_quant.shape[0])` and `_xpu_fp8_paged_mqa_logits_fake` returns `(batch_size*next_n, max_model_len)`. (2) If these do not exactly match the real kernel's returned logits shape (e.g. logits width for mqa should be number of KV rows, and the CUDA path returns a `[num_rows, ...]` layout consumed by `logits.stride(0/1)` in top-k), a mismatch would break torch.compile shape inference / cudagraph capture and downstream `num_rows = logits.shape[0]` / stride usage. (3) Confirm the fake shapes match the real `_xpu_C.fp8_mqa_logits` / `fp8_paged_mqa_logits` outputs, matching the CUDA deep_gemm equivalents.

(1) The constant `PDL_ADVANCE_LAUNCH_TOKENS = 16` with the identical comment `# The empirical value for small batch` is now duplicated verbatim in two files (this one and vllm/compilation/passes/fusion/allreduce_rms_fusion.py). (2) Duplicated magic constants tend to drift apart when one is tuned and the other is forgotten, silently producing divergent overlap behavior between the standalone all-reduce path and the fused all-reduce+RMSNorm path. (3) Define the constant once in a shared module (e.g. flashinfer_all_reduce.py) and import it into allreduce_rms_fusion.py.

(1) The standalone `all_reduce` path now unconditionally passes `launch_with_pdl=True` (previously the flashinfer_comm.allreduce_fusion call for the kAllReduce pattern used the flashinfer default, which was effectively not opting into PDL here). (2) Programmatic Dependent Launch (PDL) changes kernel launch semantics; enabling it unconditionally assumes every GPU/driver/kernel combination reachable through this standalone path supports PDL and benefits from it. If PDL is unsupported or the preceding kernel does not emit the grid dependency the completion may never be triggered, whereas the fused path already gates behavior through FlashInferFusedAllReduceParams.launch_with_pdl. (3) Confirm PDL is safe/always-beneficial for the standalone path across supported architectures, or gate `launch_with_pdl` behind the same capability/config used elsewhere rather than hardcoding True.

(1) `trigger_completion_at_end=num_tokens > PDL_ADVANCE_LAUNCH_TOKENS` is now computed and passed to allreduce_fusion, but the corresponding fake impl `call_trtllm_fused_allreduce_norm_fake` and the custom-op registration are unchanged. (2) This is correct only because `trigger_completion_at_end` is derived internally from `allreduce_in.shape` and passed directly to flashinfer, not threaded through the custom op signature; worth verifying that no torch.compile/inductor tracing path depends on this value being a graph input. (3) Confirm that computing `trigger_completion_at_end` from a runtime shape inside the custom op body does not cause specialization/recompilation issues under CUDA graphs or dynamic-shape compilation.

(1) The change replaces `first_placeholder = req_output_token_ids.index(-1)` (a forward scan finding the FIRST occurrence of -1 anywhere in the list) with a backward scan that counts only the TRAILING run of -1 placeholders. (2) These two are semantically equivalent ONLY if all -1 placeholders form a contiguous suffix of `req_output_token_ids` (i.e. no real token id equal to -1 appears earlier, and there is no non-placeholder token after a placeholder). The original `.index(-1)` would treat any earlier -1 as the start of the placeholder region, whereas the new loop counts only the trailing block. If the underlying invariant (placeholders are always a contiguous trailing block) holds, this is a correct and faster optimization; if it does not, the two produce different `first_placeholder`/`num_placeholders` values, changing which output tokens get overwritten and silently corrupting decode output. (3) Confirm and document the invariant that -1 placeholders only ever appear as a contiguous trailing suffix (never interior, and real token ids are never -1); consider adding an assertion in debug mode. The guard at line ~1036 (`if not req_output_token_ids or req_output_token_ids[-1] != -1`) already establishes the last element is -1, supporting the trailing-suffix assumption, which raises confidence this is intentional and correct.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark the async output placeholder update path (this method) with a long req_output_token_ids list and small trailing placeholder count, comparing forward .index(-1) vs backward scan; also run spec-decode + async-scheduling correctness/accuracy tests to confirm output equivalence.

findings.claim: (1) The optimization's benefit is that `.index(-1)` scans from the front (O(first_placeholder)) while the new loop scans from the back (O(num_placeholders)). (2) This is only a win when the placeholder suffix is much shorter than the non-placeholder prefix, which is the typical decode case (few trailing placeholders on a potentially long output list), so the change is directionally correct for throughput on the hot per-step decode path. (3) No fix needed; note that the new Python-level while loop still executes per request per step, so for large placeholder counts (async spec decode with many optimistic placeholders) it is no worse than the original but not asymptotically better than a vectorized approach.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-step decode throughput before/after with typical async-scheduling workloads (long generations, small spec placeholder counts).

findings.claim: (1) `_encoder_max_batch` uses `current_platform.get_device_total_memory()` which on `CudaPlatformBase` (and the base `Platform.interface`) raises `NotImplementedError` — only concrete subclasses override it, so this is generally fine on real hardware, but `current_platform` may be a platform (e.g. CPU) whose `get_device_total_memory` returns host RAM. More importantly, the budget is derived from TOTAL device memory, not FREE memory. During inference the KV cache and model weights already occupy the vast majority of device memory; 5% of TOTAL memory can far exceed what is actually free, so the computed `max_batch_size` may allow encoder batches that OOM on memory-constrained deployments. (2) Encoder OOM crashes the request/engine. (3) Base the budget on free memory (e.g. `get_current_memory_usage`/`mem_get_info`) or make the fraction/absolute cap configurable, and document the heuristic.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run Gemma4 image inference with many high-resolution images concurrently on a GPU sized so KV cache leaves <5% total memory free; verify no encoder OOM.

findings.claim: (1) The per-item activation-cost model `bytes_per_patch = hidden_size * 2 * num_hidden_layers` is a crude heuristic: it assumes 2 bytes/element (bf16/fp16) and that per-patch activation memory scales linearly with layer count, but it ignores attention working memory (which scales with seq_len^2 for the encoder), MLP intermediate size, batch-dim broadcast, and the fp32 vision-tower path noted elsewhere in the file ('the model may be bf16 while the vision tower outputs fp32'). If the vision tower runs in fp32, actual bytes/element is 4 not 2, doubling true memory use vs. the estimate. (2) Under-estimating cost lets `max_batch_size` grow too large and risks encoder OOM; over-estimating silently disables the batching optimization. (3) Derive bytes/element from the actual vision-tower activation dtype and account for attention memory, or add a conservative safety margin.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile peak encoder activation memory for a batch of N same-resolution images vs. the predicted budget; compare fp32 vs bf16 vision tower.

findings.claim: (1) `_encoder_bytes_per_patch` is initialized to 0 in `__init__` and only assigned in `load_weights()`. If any code path invokes `_process_image_input`/`_process_video_input` without `load_weights()` having run (e.g. certain test harnesses, or a weight-loading path that returns before reaching the new assignment), `cost == 0` and `_encoder_max_batch` silently falls back to returning 1, disabling the batching optimization entirely with no warning. (2) The performance feature silently no-ops, making regressions hard to detect. (3) Compute `_encoder_bytes_per_patch` in `__init__` from `config.vision_config` (all inputs are static config values, no weights needed) instead of in `load_weights`, or assert it is non-zero at first use.

(1) Batches are built via `torch.cat([item[1].unsqueeze(0) for item in chunk_items], dim=0)` for both pv and pp. (2) This is a two-step construction (unsqueeze then cat) that adds indirection; `torch.stack([item[1] for item in chunk_items], dim=0)` produces the identical result in one call and is clearer. (3) Replace unsqueeze+cat with `torch.stack`.

(1) The magic constant `0.05` (5% of total device memory) in `_encoder_max_batch` is undocumented as to why 5% was chosen. (2) A hardcoded, unexplained fraction is a maintenance hazard and prevents tuning per deployment. (3) Extract it to a named module-level constant (e.g. `_ENCODER_MEM_BUDGET_FRACTION`) with a comment justifying the value, or make it configurable.

(1) After the encoder now processes images in uniform-shape batches (the stated optimization), the pooling stage still loops per image (`for orig_idx in range(total_images)`), calling `vt.pooler` once per image with `unsqueeze(0)`, and likewise the video path loops per frame. (2) This retains per-item kernel-launch overhead in the pooling/standardization stage, partially undercutting the batching win — the pooler could operate on the batched hidden states directly per resolution bucket. (3) Consider batching the pooler call over each same-shape bucket (like the encoder) rather than looping one item at a time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark end-to-end vision embedding latency for a batch of same-resolution images with batched vs per-image pooler calls.

findings.claim: The ImportError fallback for `SparseMatrix` was changed from platform-conditional (ROCm-only -> legacy; else `raise`) to unconditional `use_legacy_triton_kernels = True`. Impact: on CUDA, if a user has a v3.6.0+ triton_kernels installed system-wide (SparseMatrix importable), the legacy path is NOT taken and the code takes the v3.6.0 branch that calls `_patch_make_bitmatrix_metadata()` and uses the SparseMatrix path — but the new fused `routing()` path is guarded by `use_legacy_triton_kernels and expert_map is None`, so v3.6.0 users skip the perf-fused path. Conversely, if `SparseMatrix` import fails on CUDA (e.g. a broken/partial install), the code now silently uses the legacy path instead of raising a clear error, which can mask a genuinely incompatible install and produce confusing downstream failures. Recommend: keep a diagnostic (warning_once) on the CUDA legacy-fallback branch so a broken install is still visible, matching the previous ROCm warning behavior.

The new fused `routing()` path (used when `use_legacy_triton_kernels and expert_map is None`) must produce numerically identical routing to the manual `topk + make_routing_data` path it replaces. The manual path applies `torch.softmax` over full logits when `sm_first` and relies on `make_routing_data`'s weight handling (topk_ids->int16, topk_weights->bf16, invalid weights set to -1). Whether `fused_routing(gating_output, topk, sm_first=sm_first)` matches the exact dtype casts and invalid-weight sentinel (-1) convention that matmul_ogs expects is not verifiable from this diff (routing() is external). If routing()'s gate-scale dtype or normalization differs, the gpt-oss output would silently change vs the prior path. Recommend a numerical equivalence test (existing MoE output vs reference) rather than only the mock-based routing-path test, since the mock test does not exercise the real fused_routing on this new branch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare gpt-oss MoE layer output (allclose in bf16) between the fused routing() path and the manual topk+make_routing_data path for renormalize=True and renormalize=False.

findings.claim: The rewritten test `test_expert_map_remap` only exercises the `expert_map is not None` branch (mock_expert_map is always set), and its docstring/comments still assert 'topk + make_routing_data' is used. The newly added and performance-critical branch — the fused `routing()` path taken when `use_legacy_triton_kernels and expert_map is None` — is now completely untested (the previous parametrized `expert_map_present=False` case was removed). This means the primary code path this PR introduces has zero test coverage; a regression in the fused path would not be caught. Recommend adding a test that patches `triton_kernels.routing.routing` and asserts it is called (and make_routing_data/topk are NOT called) when expert_map is None and legacy kernels are active.

Downgrading DEFAULT_TRITON_KERNELS_TAG from v3.6.0 to v3.5.1 is a build-dependency pin change. Users who built against v3.6.0 kernels (or have v3.6.0 installed system-wide) will now hit the legacy path at runtime. The v3.6.0 SparseMatrix branch and its `_patch_make_bitmatrix_metadata` monkey-patch are retained but become dead code for the default build. Ensure the tracking issue (triton-lang/triton#9969) is linked in a TODO (it is) and that CI is switched to build/test against v3.5.1 so the default path is what's actually validated in CI.

The `routing()` fused-path comment claims it saves kernel launches ('single launch, instead of the three separate kernels'). This is a real perf motivation but the branch is gated on `expert_map is None`; the EP (expert_map set) path still uses the slower topk+make_routing_data path unchanged. That is acceptable given remapping needs global->local IDs, but worth a brief note that the perf win intentionally does not cover EP so future readers don't try to 'unify' the branches and break the remap.

(1) `get_cuda_runtime_major()` reads `torch.version.cuda`, which is the CUDA toolkit version PyTorch was *built against*, not the actual CUDA driver/runtime version available on the machine. (2) The name `get_cuda_runtime_major` and the docstring in `_should_use_flashinfer_gdn_prefill` ("cuda_runtime >= 13") suggest a runtime/driver check, so a reader may wrongly assume it reflects the installed CUDA runtime. On a box where the driver supports CUDA 13 but PyTorch is a CUDA 12 build (or vice-versa), the gating decision may not match intent. (3) Rename to something like `get_cuda_toolkit_major`/`get_torch_cuda_major` and update the docstring to state it reflects the PyTorch build's CUDA version, or use the actual runtime version API if runtime semantics are truly intended.

(1) In the original code the JIT-compile warning was emitted whenever FlashInfer was selected (which was only ever SM90). After this change FlashInfer is also enabled on Blackwell (SM10.x), but `_log_gdn_backend_decision` guards the JIT warning with `current_platform.is_device_capability(90)`, so Blackwell users using FlashInfer get no first-run compile-time warning. (2) If the Blackwell FlashInfer path also incurs JIT/compile latency, users lose the heads-up; if it does not (precompiled cutlass), the behavior is correct. The inline comment ('JIT-compiled cutlass path is only used on SM90 (Hopper)') asserts the latter, so this is likely intentional. (3) Confirm the Blackwell path is genuinely precompiled; if it can JIT, extend the warning to cover SM10.x as well. Flagging only to ensure the comment's assumption is verified against the FlashInfer GDN implementation.

(1) The +1 offset applied in gpu_model_runner (`layer_ids = [i + 1 for i in dflash_config.get("target_layer_ids") or []]`) is intended to invert the -1 offset newly applied at config-construction time in algos.py (`target_layer_ids = [i - 1 for i in aux_layer_ids]`). Note that `eagle_aux_hidden_state_layer_ids` is set (in algos.py line 100) directly to the raw `aux_hidden_state_layer_ids` WITHOUT the -1 shift. So the two config keys now carry different semantics: `eagle_aux_hidden_state_layer_ids` = raw ids; `dflash_config.target_layer_ids` = raw-1. The gpu_model_runner's dflash branch re-adds +1 to recover the raw ids used to select which target-model layers produce aux hidden states — this is self-consistent for the aux-hidden-state layer selection path. (2) The risk: `dflash_config.target_layer_ids` is ALSO consumed directly by the DFlash model in qwen3_dflash.py line 261 as `num_features_to_use = len(drafter_config["target_layer_ids"])`. That consumer only uses the *length* of the list, so subtracting 1 from each element does not change `num_features_to_use` and is safe. Verify no other consumer of `dflash_config.target_layer_ids` interprets the element *values* as layer indices, in which case the now-shifted (i-1) values would index the wrong target-model layer. (3) Fix: confirm via grep that qwen3_dflash.py (len-only) is the sole value-consumer, and add a short comment in algos.py documenting that target_layer_ids values are intentionally offset by -1 relative to eagle_aux_hidden_state_layer_ids so the two keys are not accidentally conflated later.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run tests/v1/e2e/spec_decode/test_spec_decode.py::test_dflash_acceptance_rates and test_dflash_correctness; verify measured acceptance lengths meet the tightened 0.95/0.975 thresholds, confirming the aux-layer indexing now selects the correct target layers.

findings.claim: (1) The round-trip transform (subtract 1 in algos.py, add 1 back in gpu_model_runner.py) means the aux-hidden-state layer selection value that gpu_model_runner ultimately uses is identical to the raw `aux_hidden_state_layer_ids` already stored in `eagle_aux_hidden_state_layer_ids`. (2) Why it matters: the -1/+1 dance introduces indirection whose only purpose is to make `dflash_config.target_layer_ids` carry a different (model-facing) semantics; this is fragile and easy to break if either offset is edited without the other. Since the two paths must stay in lockstep, a future edit to only one site silently regresses acceptance rate (exactly the class of bug #40727 addressed). (3) Fix: consider having the gpu_model_runner dflash branch read from `eagle_aux_hidden_state_layer_ids` directly (raw ids, no +1) rather than deriving from `target_layer_ids`, so the model-facing offset lives in exactly one place and cannot desync from the aux-layer-selection path.

(1) The docstring for update_dflash still states that aux_hidden_state_layer_ids is 'Mapped to both eagle_aux_hidden_state_layer_ids ... and dflash_config.target_layer_ids', implying an identity mapping to both. After this change, target_layer_ids is now aux_layer_ids minus 1, not the same value. (2) A stale docstring that claims identical mapping is misleading and could lead a future maintainer to 'fix' the perceived inconsistency by removing the -1 offset, reintroducing #40727. (3) Fix: update the docstring to note that target_layer_ids uses DFlash's 0-based-relative indexing (aux_layer_ids - 1) while eagle_aux_hidden_state_layer_ids keeps the raw ids.

(1) The new attribute is annotated `self.ca_comm: None = None`, i.e. its declared type is the literal `NoneType` rather than an Optional of the custom all-reduce type used by `CudaCommunicator` (`self.ca_comm: CustomAllreduce | None = None`). (2) This is inconsistent with the sibling communicator and pins the attribute type so that any future assignment of a real all-reduce object would be flagged by type checkers; it also communicates intent poorly (reads as 'this must always be None' rather than 'XPU has no custom all-reduce yet'). (3) Prefer annotating as `CustomAllreduce | None = None` (or at minimum `Optional[...]`) to match `CudaCommunicator`, or add a brief comment noting XPU has no custom all-reduce so `ca_comm` is intentionally always None. Functionally the current code works because `graph_capture` only checks `if ca_comm is not None`.

(1) This PR removes the guard `elif parallel_config.world_size_across_dp > 1: cudagraph_mode = NONE` that previously disabled XPU graphs when data-parallel world size > 1 because 'XPU Graph doesn't support capture communication ops', as well as the FLASH_ATTN->PIECEWISE fallback. (2) If the underlying XPU graph capture still cannot capture cross-DP communication ops, removing this guard would let cudagraph capture proceed and either crash during capture or silently capture stale communication, causing wrong results in multi-DP XPU runs. (3) Confirm (via XPU runtime testing on world_size_across_dp>1) that XPU graph capture now correctly handles communication ops before removing the guard; if only single-DP is validated, keep the DP>1 disable. This is the substantive behavior change of the PR and cannot be fully verified from the diff alone.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run an XPU model with data_parallel_size>1 and VLLM_XPU_ENABLE_XPU_GRAPH=1 and verify correctness of outputs vs. eager/PIECEWISE mode.

findings.claim: (1) `_cudagraph_support` is now set to `AttentionCGSupport.ALWAYS` when `current_platform.is_xpu()`, mirroring the FA3 path. The adjacent comment block only documents FA2/FA3 rationale and does not mention the XPU case. (2) A reviewer/maintainer reading the comment will not understand why XPU is granted ALWAYS support (the removed xpu.py comment said 'FMHA sycl-tla kernels cannot be captured with XPU graphs, falling back to PIECEWISE' — the opposite claim), making the reasoning look contradictory with recently-removed code. (3) Add a short comment explaining that XPU FlashAttention now supports full cudagraph capture (why the earlier sycl-tla limitation no longer applies), so the ALWAYS classification is justified.

(1) This commit reverts the `_patch_should_realize_on_reuse` Inductor materialization-heuristic patch that was applied for torch 2.11.0. (2) Per the removed docstring, without this patch Inductor inlines chains of computations when compiling the full model graph, causing recomputation (e.g. the residual in `fused_add_rms_norm` recomputed on every use, cascading through the model) — a potential compile-time/runtime throughput regression specifically for users on torch 2.11.0. (3) If the revert is intended only to fix a regression the patch caused, confirm the recompute-inlining behavior is acceptable for 2.11.0 users, or gate the fix behind a follow-up; otherwise no action needed since this is a deliberate revert restoring prior behavior. This is an intentional maintainer revert scoped to a single torch version, so impact is limited.
findings.suggested_benchmark: torch.compile fullgraph compilation of a transformer model on torch 2.11.0, measuring compile time and steady-state decode throughput with vs. without the reverted patch to confirm the magnitude of any regression.

findings.claim: process_weights_after_loading sets self.logical_output_size = weight.shape[1] BEFORE checking whether the weight has already been padded. If this method is ever invoked twice on the same layer (e.g. reload/re-quantization paths, or a wrapper kernel that delegates), the second invocation reads the already-padded weight.shape[1] (== padded_n) and records logical_output_size = padded_n. The early-return (pad_k==0 and pad_n==0) then leaves logical_output_size stale/wrong, and apply_scaled_mm will slice output[..., :padded_n] instead of the true logical N, returning padded (garbage) columns to the caller. Fix: derive logical_output_size from layer.logical_widths (sum) / an immutable config field (e.g. c.weight_shape) rather than from the live weight tensor, or guard against re-padding by remembering that padding was already applied.

The per-channel weight_scale padding no longer reshapes a 1D scale into a 2D column vector. The old runtime code did: pad Bs.view(-1) then, if 1D and N>1, Bs = Bs.view(-1, 1) so cutlass_scaled_mm received a [N,1] per-channel scale. The new load-time code does padded_scale = pad(flat_scale).view(-1, *weight_scale.shape[1:]); when the incoming weight_scale is already 2D [N,1] (convert_to_channelwise output) this reproduces [padded_n,1] correctly, but if weight_scale arrives 1D [N] the result stays 1D [padded_n] and the (-1,1) reshape is lost. This is a behavioral divergence for any FP8 checkpoint that stores per-channel scale as 1D, potentially changing/breaking the cutlass scale broadcast. Fix: preserve the original 2D reshape behavior, or assert weight_scale is already the expected 2D shape.

In apply_scaled_mm, pad_k = padded_k - A.shape[1] is computed from the difference between the (statically padded) weight K and the runtime activation K. This assumes A.shape[1] always equals the original logical K. If an upstream path ever pre-pads the activation dimension, pad_k would be miscomputed (possibly negative). A defensive assert (padded_k >= A.shape[1] and 0 <= pad_k < 16) at the parallelism/kernel boundary would catch shape-contract violations early rather than passing a mismatched A/B to cutlass. Also consider asserting pad_n in [0,16).

This is the intended optimization and is correct in spirit: weight/scale padding is hoisted out of the per-forward hot path (apply_scaled_mm) into process_weights_after_loading, so the pad()+transpose()+contiguous() allocations happen once at load time instead of on every TTFT/decode call, matching the reported 13.5% TTFT improvement. No performance regression concern; noting for completeness that the runtime path now only pads the activation A (unavoidable, shape-dependent). No action needed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: FP8 (per-channel & per-tensor) linear with N or K not a multiple of 16; measure TTFT and verify output parity vs. the pre-change runtime-padding implementation

findings.claim: The new non-persistent-cache path calls `fi_utils.autotune()` with no arguments, whereas the original leader path called `fi_utils.autotune(tune_mode=True, cache=str(cache_path))`. FlashInfer's `autotune` context manager takes `tune_mode` as its first parameter; the pre-change code explicitly passed `tune_mode=True` to actually run the tuning benchmarks.
