If the default of `tune_mode` is not `True`, the new branch would open the autotuner context WITHOUT enabling tuning, so `_dummy_run` would just profile with default heuristics and no tactics would be recorded — silently defeating the entire purpose of `flashinfer_autotune` (perf regression to un-tuned kernels) while still paying the dummy-run cost. Recommendation: verify the FlashInfer `autotune` signature and, if `tune_mode` does not default to True, pass `fi_utils.autotune(tune_mode=True)` explicitly in the new branch to match the previously-tested behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a Hopper/Blackwell GPU, run flashinfer_autotune via kernel_warmup with the new code and confirm AutoTuner has recorded tuned tactics (e.g. non-empty configs / measured kernel selection) versus falling back to default heuristics; compare decode/prefill throughput of the resulting engine against the pre-#43119 tuned path.

findings.claim: The disable path is implemented as a module-level constant `_FLASHINFER_USE_PERSISTENT_CACHE = False` with an early-return branch that duplicates the dummy_run invocation, leaving the entire persistent-cache implementation (leader gating, cache path resolution, broadcast, load_configs) as dead code that can never execute. This is acceptable as a temporary hotfix and the TODO documents intent, but the constant is a compile-time toggle with no runtime/env control, so re-enabling requires a code edit + redeploy. Consider gating on an env var (e.g. VLLM_FLASHINFER_AUTOTUNE_PERSISTENT_CACHE) so the fix can be validated/toggled without a rebuild, and so the currently-unreachable cache code doesn't silently rot.

In the new branch every rank runs autotuning independently (no leader gating), so different ranks may independently select different kernel tactics for the same op. The original persistent-cache path deliberately broadcast rank-0's cache to all ranks specifically 'so all ranks dispatch the same kernel tactic.' Independent per-rank tuning can lead to divergent tactic choices across TP/PP/EP ranks. For collective-heavy kernels this could cause mismatched shapes/hangs or at minimum inconsistent behavior. Confirm that FlashInfer autotune tactic selection is deterministic given identical inputs across ranks; if not, the disabled-cache path reintroduces the cross-rank divergence risk that the persistent-cache design was meant to prevent.

(1) The new block_size_m tuning loop in `batched_fused_marlin_moe` uses `BATCH_TOKENS_MAX / b_m < 0.9` as the break condition, but `BATCH_TOKENS_MAX` is the max tokens per batch (typically a large value like 128/256/512), so `BATCH_TOKENS_MAX / b_m` is far greater than 0.9 for all candidate `b_m` in [8,16,32,48,64]; the condition is never true and the loop leaves `block_size_m` at 64. (2) This means the intended 'tune block_size_m to reduce padding overhead' optimization is effectively dead — it never triggers a smaller block size. Compare `fused_marlin_moe`, whose analogous loop divides by `E` (experts): `M * topk / E / block_size_m < 0.9`, which measures average tokens-per-expert per block. The batched version should divide by the average valid tokens per batch, not use raw `BATCH_TOKENS_MAX`. (3) Fix: use the actual per-expert token load (e.g. total valid tokens / num_experts) in the ratio, matching the semantics of the non-batched path, or drop the loop and document why 64 is fixed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark batched AWQ/GPTQ Marlin MoE decode throughput (small batch, e.g. 1-32 tokens) with the loop vs a fixed block_size_m=64, and log the selected block_size_m to confirm it ever changes.

findings.claim: (1) The 8-bit-activation clamp `block_size_m = max(block_size_m, 16)` is placed after the (currently no-op) tuning loop; if the tuning loop is ever fixed to select b_m=8, this clamp silently overrides it to 16. (2) Semantically this is intended (8-bit act path needs >=16), but combined with the broken loop condition above it makes the effective behavior 'always 64', masking the correctness of the clamp. (3) Verify the interaction once the loop condition is corrected; add a comment clarifying that the min-16 clamp is a hard kernel constraint for is_a_8bit.

(1) `make_wna16_moe_kernel` drops the `layer` parameter and `AutoGPTQMoEMethod` correspondingly stops passing `layer=layer`. Confirm no remaining caller passes `layer=` positionally/by keyword. The diff shows both the GPTQ and new AWQ call sites updated, so this appears consistent. (2) Removing an unused arg is a clean refactor; risk is only if an out-of-diff caller still passes it, which would raise TypeError at kernel construction. (3) Grep the tree for other `make_wna16_moe_kernel(` calls to ensure none still pass `layer=`.

(1) `process_weights_after_loading` now uses `replace_parameter(layer, 'w13_scales', w13_scale)` / `replace_parameter(layer, 'w13_qzeros', w13_qzeros)` etc. via `_replace_or_register_parameter`, delegating all shaping to the shared oracle. The AWQ-specific path `_process_awq_weights_marlin` returns `w13_input_global_scale`/`w2_input_global_scale` only when `input_dtype == torch.int8 and layer.num_groups_w13 > 1`; for the common fp16/bf16 activation case these are None and never registered, so `a1_gscale=getattr(layer, 'w13_input_global_scale', None)` in `get_fused_moe_quant_config` correctly resolves to None. Data-flow into `awq_marlin_moe_quant_config(a1_gscale=...)` -> `FusedMoEQuantDesc(alpha_or_gscale=...)` -> `quant_config.a1_gscale` -> `MarlinExperts.a1_gscale` -> `input_global_scale1` is consistent. No defect found, but the multi-hop plumbing (7 aliases) is fragile; a shape/dtype assertion at the marlin kernel boundary that input_global_scale is only non-None when input_dtype.itemsize==1 would catch future regressions. (3) Add an assert in _fused_marlin_moe that input_global_scale{1,2} is None unless input_dtype is int8.

(1) The PR removes the `* (use_gqa ? input.num_heads_kv : input.num_heads_q)` head-count multiplier from `kv_len_per_thread`. This is the core fix: `total_kv_len` is accumulated purely as a sum of per-Q-tile aligned KV lengths (line 456, no head multiplication), and inside the task-splitting loop `remaining_kv_len` is decremented by `curr_kv_len` (also no head multiplication). Previously the per-thread budget was scaled up by ~num_heads_kv while the subtracted quantities were not, so each thread's budget was ~num_heads_kv times larger than its fair share — meaning only ~thread_num/num_heads_kv threads actually received work and the rest sat idle (the reported thread underutilization). (2) The change restores unit consistency between the budget and the decrements, spreading work across all OMP threads. (3) The fix is correct as written. This finding documents that the change is a genuine load-balancing fix, not a regression; recommend a benchmark confirming even work distribution across threads.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run CPU attention (e.g. GQA model with num_kv_heads>1, high thread_num) and measure per-thread work-item counts / decode throughput before vs after; verify near-uniform distribution across OMP threads.

findings.claim: (1) `KMP_BLOCKTIME` default is raised from 1ms to 5ms and the three `KMP_*_BARRIER_PATTERN=dist,dist` env settings are removed. (2) These are IOMP performance-tuning defaults that silently alter runtime behavior for all IOMP CPU users; a higher blocktime keeps threads spinning longer (better throughput under contention but more power/CPU when idle), and removing the dist barrier patterns changes barrier granularity. The inline comment even notes blocktime=5 'masks thread underutilization', so its interaction with the scheduler fix is intentional. (3) No action needed beyond confirming the new defaults were benchmarked across the supported IOMP configurations; low risk since these are perf knobs rather than correctness settings.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare CPU decode/prefill throughput and idle-core CPU usage with KMP_BLOCKTIME=1 vs 5 and with/without the removed barrier-pattern envs on an IOMP build.

findings.claim: (1) The version guard is inconsistent with the stated intent. The docstring says "Only apply for 2.11, fix included in 2.12" but the guard uses `is_torch_equal("2.11.0")`, which is an exact-equality check against the release string 2.11.0. Any 2.11.x patch release (e.g. 2.11.1) or the common dev/nightly build strings (e.g. 2.11.0.dev...) will NOT match, so the Inductor materialization fix silently will not be applied. (2) This matters because the whole point of the patch is a large compile-time performance regression: without it Inductor inlines/recomputes the residual (e.g. in fused_add_rms_norm) and cascades recomputation through the model graph — a real throughput/latency regression. Users on 2.11.1 or nightly 2.11 would silently lose the fix. Note that elsewhere in this file the same author-base uses `is_torch_equal_or_newer("2.11.0.dev")` (HAS_OPAQUE_TYPE) to catch dev builds, showing 2.11 dev builds are in scope for this project. (3) Fix: gate with a range check such as `is_torch_equal_or_newer("2.11.0.dev") and not is_torch_equal_or_newer("2.12.0.dev")` (or equivalent) so all 2.11.x builds including nightlies get the patch while 2.12+ is excluded.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compile a model (e.g. Llama fused_add_rms_norm path) under torch 2.11.x and 2.11 nightly with and without the patch; measure end-to-end torch.compile output graph memory-traffic / decode throughput to confirm the patch applies and helps.

findings.claim: (1) `optimization_hint` is called on `self.data.get_numel()`, but the surrounding Inductor API used elsewhere in the reference PyTorch code is typically `V.graph.sizevars.size_hint(..., fallback=...)`. If `optimization_hint` is not a valid method on `sizevars` in torch 2.11, this monkeypatch will raise AttributeError at compile time for every reused tensor, which would either crash compilation or (if wrapped) disable it. (2) This matters because the patched method replaces StorageBox.should_realize_on_reuse for the entire compile, so an incorrect API name would break fullgraph compilation entirely on 2.11. (3) Verify the exact method name against the torch 2.11 `SizeVarAllocator` API (likely `size_hint`); if incorrect, correct it. This could not be verified from the vLLM repo alone since it depends on the torch internal API.

(1) The size-aware cost-model branch only fires when both `total_read_bytes > 0` and `output_bytes > 0`; otherwise it falls back to `self.num_reads() > config.realize_reads_threshold`. For a broadcast/scalar output where `optimization_hint` returns the fallback of 0, `output_bytes` becomes 0 and the model degrades to the old threshold heuristic — which is the exact behavior the patch aims to override. (2) This matters because in fullgraph compilation of the model these fallback cases may be common enough to leave part of the recompute-cascade regression unaddressed. (3) Consider whether a nonzero output-byte fallback (or explicit realize) is more appropriate when the numel hint is unavailable, and document the intended behavior for the fallback path.

(1) The FlashAttentionBackend.get_supported_kernel_block_sizes() only restricts the kernel block sizes to [16,32,64] when mamba_ssm_cache_dtype/mamba_cache_dtype == "float32", to avoid the FA NaN-propagation issue documented at Dao-AILab/flash-attention#1974. Now that "bfloat16" is a newly permitted value for MambaDType, a hybrid model configured with mamba_ssm_cache_dtype="bfloat16" bypasses this guard entirely and falls through to MultipleOf(16), i.e. it can select block sizes (e.g. 128) known to trigger the NaN propagation bug. (2) This can cause silently wrong / NaN outputs for hybrid mamba+attention models using the new bf16 ssm cache. (3) Verify whether the NaN-propagation restriction should also apply to bfloat16 (likely it applies to any non-model-dtype ssm cache dtype); if so, broaden the condition from `== "float32"` to also include "bfloat16" (or `!= "auto"`).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a hybrid mamba/attention model (e.g. Bamba/NemotronH) with --mamba-ssm-cache-dtype bfloat16 and a block size >64, check for NaNs in decode outputs.

findings.claim: (1) There is a config validation branch keyed on `mamba_ssm_cache_dtype != "float16"` in vllm/config/vllm.py (~line 862). Adding "bfloat16" as a valid MambaDType value changes which configurations flow through that branch. (2) If that check was written assuming only {auto, float32, float16} were possible, its behavior for the new "bfloat16" value may be unintended. (3) Confirm the surrounding logic at that line still behaves correctly (e.g. block-size / cudagraph gating) when the value is "bfloat16".

(1) `_topk_indices_torch` gains a new `row_starts: torch.Tensor | None = None` parameter, but the only remaining call sites in this diff (`top_k_per_row_prefill` / `top_k_per_row_decode` now go directly to `torch.ops._C.*`, and the fallback wrappers `_topk_indices_prefill`/`_topk_indices_decode` that used to call `_topk_indices_torch` were deleted). After this change grep shows no caller passes `row_starts`, so the new branch (`indices - starts`) is dead code with no runtime use path. (2) This violates the mandatory unused-new-argument check and hides intent: the CUDA-parity subtraction logic can never execute yet reads as if it is active. (3) Either remove `_topk_indices_torch` entirely if it now has no callers, or if it is retained as a fallback, wire a real caller that passes `row_starts` and add a test; otherwise add an explicit TODO/migration note.

(1) The generic-`topk_tokens` fallback path was removed: `_topk_indices_prefill`/`_topk_indices_decode` previously dispatched to the fused `top_k_per_row_*` kernel only for `topk_tokens in {2048}` and otherwise used the torch reference. The new code unconditionally calls `torch.ops._C.top_k_per_row_prefill`/`top_k_per_row_decode` regardless of `topk_tokens`. (2) If the fused C++ kernel only supports specific `topk_tokens` values (the removed `_TOPK_FAST_PATH_VALUES = {2048}` frozenset strongly implies this), then for any other `topk_tokens` the kernel will silently produce wrong indices or fail, breaking configurations that previously worked via the torch fallback. (3) Verify the fused kernel supports arbitrary `topk_tokens`; if not, keep a fallback to `_topk_indices_torch` for unsupported values.

(1) Both `MHCPreOp` and `MHCPostOp` unconditionally disable the fused aiter kernel (`mhc_pre_aiter`/`mhc_post_aiter`) and always fall back to the pure-torch `mhc_pre_torch`/`mhc_post_torch`, even when `hidden_size % 256 == 0` where the aiter path was previously used. (2) This is a deliberate correctness bugfix (aiter accuracy issue at large token counts), but it removes a fused-kernel fast path for all shapes on ROCm — a throughput regression for DeepSeek V4 MHC on AMD. The dead code is left commented out rather than gated behind a version check, so re-enabling requires a manual code edit. (3) Acceptable as a temporary correctness fix, but consider gating the aiter path behind an aiter-version check so throughput is automatically restored once the fixed aiter version is present, and file a tracking issue referencing the linked commit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare DeepSeek V4 MHC pre/post op throughput on ROCm (aiter path vs torch fallback) at hidden_size multiple of 256 across small and large token counts.

findings.claim: (1) `rocm_aiter_sparse_attn_indexer_fake` dropped the dummy `_flattened_kv`/`_k_fp8`/`_k_scale` allocations that previously simulated peak KV memory during the profile run, now returning `topk_indices_buffer` immediately. (2) The removed allocations had an explicit NOTE that they exist so `profile_run` computes correct memory usage; removing them may cause the ROCm profiler to under-estimate peak memory and over-allocate KV cache blocks, risking OOM at runtime. (3) Confirm that memory profiling for the sparse indexer is now accounted for elsewhere (e.g. inside `rocm_aiter_sparse_attn_indexer` real path during profiling); if not, restore the dummy allocation in the fake impl.

(1) The ROCm forward_hip path previously supported a non-aiter native fallback (`rocm_aiter_sparse_attn_indexer_native`) when aiter was disabled or `skip_k_cache_insert` was set; it now raises RuntimeError requiring `VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER=1`. (2) This removes the only ROCm code path that worked without aiter, so any user running DeepSeek V4 sparse attention on ROCm without aiter enabled will now hard-fail instead of falling back. This is a behavior/compatibility change for existing ROCm deployments. (3) If dropping the native fallback is intentional, ensure it is documented in release notes; otherwise retain a fallback for the aiter-disabled case.

The new XPU branch calls torch.ops._C.per_token_group_fp8_quant with only 8 positional arguments (x, x_q, x_s, group_size, eps, fp8_min, fp8_max, use_ue8m0), whereas the op schema registered in csrc/libtorch_stable/torch_bindings.cpp declares 10 arguments, with the two trailing ones being `dummy_is_scale_transposed` (column_major_scales) and `dummy_is_tma_aligned` (tma_aligned_scales). Two problems: (1) if XPU uses the same 10-arg schema, this call will fail at runtime with a missing-arguments error; (2) even if the XPU kernel package registers a distinct 8-arg schema, the XPU branch silently ignores column_major_scales / tma_aligned_scales. But the caller (per_token_group_quant_fp8) allocates x_s in column-major/TMA-aligned strided layout BEFORE this call when column_major_scales=True, and passes that pre-strided buffer. A kernel that assumes row-major output will then write scales into the wrong positions of the strided buffer, producing silently wrong block scales and corrupt GEMM results. Fix: forward column_major_scales and tma_aligned_scales to the XPU op (matching the CUDA branch), or add an explicit assert that column_major_scales is False / tma_aligned_scales is False on the XPU path and fall through to the Triton fallback otherwise.

forward_xpu now dispatches is_group_quant dynamic quantization to fp8_utils.per_token_group_quant_fp8 but does NOT pass tma_aligned_scales, unlike forward_cuda which forwards self.tma_aligned_scales. If a QuantFP8 instance on XPU was constructed with tma_aligned_scales=True (e.g. for a DeepGEMM path), the XPU path silently drops it and produces non-TMA-aligned scales, which the downstream block-scaled MM may consume incorrectly. Also note forward_xpu bypasses the use_ue8m0/deep_gemm packed-scale branch that forward_cuda handles first; verify XPU never needs per_token_group_quant_fp8_packed_for_deepgemm. Fix: mirror the full forward_cuda branch logic (including tma_aligned_scales and the deepgemm-packed pre-check) or delegate to forward_cuda for the group-quant case as it did before.

The new XPU branch duplicates the CUDA branch body (same torch.ops._C.per_token_group_fp8_quant call) but with a different argument count. If the intent is that XPU and CUDA share the kernel, the guard could be `if (current_platform.is_cuda() or current_platform.is_xpu()) and x.is_contiguous():` with a single argument list, avoiding divergence and the risk of the two branches drifting out of sync. The current split also raises the question of why XPU omits the last two args — this asymmetry should be documented if intentional.

(1) The new kernel parameter `launch_pdl: tl.constexpr` is declared and passed (`launch_pdl=use_gdc`) but is never read anywhere in the kernel body — only `USE_GDC` gates the `gdc_launch_dependents()`/`gdc_wait()` calls. The trailing comment `# triton metadata` implies it is meant to be interpreted by the Triton launcher as a reserved launch-metadata kwarg (to enable programmatic dependent launch / PDL). (2) Whether Triton actually recognizes a name declared as a `tl.constexpr` kernel parameter as launch metadata is version-dependent; declaring it in the JIT signature typically makes it a normal (unused) constexpr rather than a reserved launcher hint. If the installed Triton does not treat it as metadata, PDL is NOT enabled at the launch site while the kernel still issues `gdc_launch_dependents()`/`gdc_wait()` on SM90+, making those GDC ops ineffective (best case a no-op perf loss) or mismatched with the launch configuration. (3) Confirm against the pinned Triton version that `launch_pdl` is a recognized launch-metadata kwarg; if not, remove it and drive PDL via the supported launcher mechanism, or drop the GDC ops. Add a comment referencing the required Triton version.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark _pack_topk_ids_weights_kernel on an SM90/SM100 device with and without launch_pdl to confirm PDL is actually taking effect (nsys/ncu programmatic-launch attribute), and verify no correctness change vs the old torch.compile packing.

findings.claim: (1) The rewritten `trtllm_moe_pack_topk_ids_weights` adds a hard precondition `assert topk_ids.is_contiguous() and topk_weights.is_contiguous()` (and `assert topk_ids.shape == topk_weights.shape`). The previous torch.compile implementation operated via elementwise ops and worked on non-contiguous / broadcastable inputs. (2) Callers (trtllm_fp8_moe, trtllm_nvfp4_moe, trtllm_mxfp4_moe) pass topk_ids/topk_weights straight from routing; if any code path (e.g. a sliced or transposed view of topk tensors) is non-contiguous, this now raises instead of silently working — a behavior regression that only surfaces at runtime on specific shapes. (3) Either call `.contiguous()` internally on the flattened tensors (cheap when already contiguous) instead of asserting, or verify all call sites always pass contiguous tensors and keep the assert as a documented invariant.

(1) `enable_pdl: True` was added only to the modular kernel kwargs (`TrtLlmMxfp4ExpertsModular.apply`) but not to the monolithic path (`TrtLlmMxfp4ExpertsMonolithic.apply`), which also calls a trtllm flashinfer MoE kernel. (2) This is an inconsistency: if PDL is beneficial it is missed for the monolithic kernel; if it is not universally safe, it is worth documenting why only the modular path enables it. (3) Either add `enable_pdl` to the monolithic call as well or add a brief comment explaining why it is scoped to the modular path.

(1) `block_size` defaults to a fixed 1024 with a single 1D grid and no autotuning/next_power_of_2 handling; the packing kernel is trivially memory-bound and this may be fine, but the sibling kernel `_count_expert_num_tokens` in the same file uses `triton.next_power_of_2(min(numel,1024))`. (2) For very small token counts the fixed 1024 block launches an over-sized block (harmless but wasteful); for consistency and to avoid launching a partially-masked block, consider clamping block_size to the element count. (3) Optional: clamp `block_size = min(block_size, triton.next_power_of_2(n_elements))` or leave as-is if profiling shows no difference.

(1) BreakableCUDAGraphWrapper._capture never increments compilation_counter.num_cudagraph_captured, unlike CUDAGraphWrapper (cuda_graph.py:339) and gpu/cudagraph_utils.py:252. (2) gpu_model_runner.py lines 6187/6268 save and restore compilation_counter.num_cudagraph_captured around the profiling capture, and many tests assert on this counter; with breakable cudagraph enabled the counter stays 0 so any metric/telemetry or test relying on it will silently under-report the number of captured graphs. (3) Increment compilation_counter.num_cudagraph_captured (e.g. by capture.num_graphs) at the end of _capture to keep parity with the existing wrapper.

(1) In _capture, gc.collect() and torch.accelerator.empty_cache() are run unconditionally on every _capture call, i.e. once per distinct BatchDescriptor. The existing CUDAGraphWrapper explicitly disables gc.collect/empty_cache after the first graph (gc_disable path in cuda_graph.py) because 'running gc again and again ... will make cudagraph capture very slow.' (2) With many capture sizes/batch descriptors this reintroduces exactly the repeated-gc slowdown the piecewise path was fixed to avoid, inflating startup capture time substantially. (3) Only run gc.collect()/empty_cache() for the first descriptor capture (track a flag on the wrapper), mirroring the gc_disable optimization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure total cudagraph capture time (startup) with VLLM_USE_BREAKABLE_CUDAGRAPH=1 vs. piecewise cudagraph for a model with many capture sizes and layers.

findings.claim: (1) _capture assigns `entry.output = weak_ref_tensors(output)` where `output` is already the weak-ref result produced inside the capture context (`output = weak_ref_tensors(output)`). This double-weak-refs the output. (2) At minimum it is a redundant no-op-ish call; if weak_ref_tensor of an already-weak tensor produces a distinct object the stored entry.output may diverge from what is returned to the caller on capture. More importantly the function then `return output` (the in-context weak ref), so on the FIRST call the caller receives a weak-ref tensor whose backing storage is owned by the cudagraph pool and may be reclaimed/overwritten by the next descriptor's capture before the caller consumes it. CUDAGraphWrapper instead returns the strong `output` on capture and only stores a weak ref in entry.output. (3) Return the strong output from the captured run (do the weak-ref only for entry.output), and drop the redundant second weak_ref_tensors.

(1) eager_break_during_capture weak-refs the op's args/kwargs tensors and calls fn inside a lambda for replay. The docstring requires decorated ops to write into a caller-provided output buffer, but there is no assertion/verification that the op is in-place. (2) If a decorated op (or a future one) returns a fresh output tensor, replay will silently read/write the wrong (freed or reallocated) address, producing silently wrong results or a hang — exactly the failure the docstring warns about, with no guard. (3) Add a runtime check during capture that the eager op writes into a pre-existing static buffer (e.g. verify the returned tensor's data_ptr matches a provided output arg), or at least document/assert the contract at the call site.

(1) __exit__ swallows nothing but on an exception during capture (exc is not None) it still calls _end_segment(), which calls capture_end() on a graph that may be in an invalid/partial capture state, potentially masking the original error with a CUDA capture error. (2) A capture-time model exception could surface as a confusing CUDA error instead of the real cause, hindering debugging. (3) In __exit__, skip capture_end / abort the in-flight capture when exc_type is not None, and always clear the TLS active reference (already done in finally).

(1) The new breakable-cudagraph branch fires for any cudagraph_mode != NONE and !use_ubatching, and takes precedence over the has_full_cudagraphs() branch. There is no guard rejecting incompatible feature combinations (e.g. ubatching is excluded, but interactions with speculative decoding / encoder models that rely on CUDAGraphWrapper semantics are not checked). (2) Enabling VLLM_USE_BREAKABLE_CUDAGRAPH globally silently replaces the wrapper for all paths; unsupported combinations may fail obscurely at capture/replay time rather than at config validation. (3) Since this is explicitly experimental and env-gated, at minimum add a validation that logs/raises for known-incompatible configs, or document the supported matrix.

(1) The default sampling precision is silently changed from FP64 to FP32: previously gumbel_block_argmax unconditionally did `logits = logits.to(tl.float64)` and used the 64-bit uniform `tl_rand64`; now with the new default `use_fp64_gumbel=False`, logits stay fp32 and the uniform is drawn via `tl.rand` (a completely different random transform than `tl_rand64`). (2) This changes sampling outputs for ALL existing users, including those relying on a fixed `seed` for reproducibility — bitwise (and in the tie-prone limit, token-level) outputs will differ from the prior release. It is a behavior-altering default change for a core sampling path. (3) Confirm this reproducibility break is intended and documented in release notes; consider keeping FP64 as default if exact backward reproducibility is a contract, or clearly documenting the change.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Fixed-seed greedy+temperature sampling determinism test comparing FP32 vs FP64 gumbel outputs across a large vocab (e.g. 128k) to quantify token-level divergence rate.

findings.claim: (1) In the FP32 path, `u = tl.rand(gumbel_seed, block)` is clamped only from below with `_FP32_TINY` (0x1p-126, the smallest positive normal fp32). `tl.rand` returns values in [0,1) so u=1.0 (which would make -log(u)=0 and -log(0)=+inf) cannot occur; however, the smallest *nonzero* value tl.rand can emit is ~2^-32≈2.3e-10, so clamping to 1.18e-38 only ever fires for an exact u==0 draw and the intermediate log(u)≈-88 vs log(2.3e-10)≈-22 produces a much more extreme (and RNG-quantization-dependent) gumbel value than the fp64 path did. (2) This is a minor tail-distribution artifact, not a bug, but the clamp constant is finer-grained than the fp32 RNG resolution and so is effectively a no-op except on the exact-zero draw. (3) Consider clamping to the RNG resolution (e.g. 2^-32) rather than the fp32 min-normal so the exact-zero case matches the neighboring representable draws, or document that the exact-zero case is intentionally mapped to an extreme gumbel tail.

(1) The new `use_fp64` argument was appended to `gumbel_sample` and `rejection_sample` public signatures with a default of False. (2) External/downstream callers are unaffected due to the default, and internal callers were all updated, so this is fine; noting only that the default silently selects the new FP32 behavior for any caller that does not pass the flag. (3) No change required; flagging for completeness of the defaults-change review.

(1) Removing `ir_op_priority.set_priority()` and `vllm.ir.enable_torch_wrap(...)` from `set_forward_context` changes the contract: IR op dispatch priority and the torch-wrap flag are now assumed to be pre-installed globally by `WorkerBase.__init__` (via `set_default`/`set_default_torch_wrap`). Any caller that enters `set_forward_context` WITHOUT having constructed a `WorkerBase` will now run with default (empty) priority — `IrOp.dispatch` falls back to `native` and logs a 'Priority not set' warning, and torch-wrap keeps its module-load default (True) instead of the config value. (2) There are many such callers, especially test/benchmark harnesses (e.g. tests/kernels/moe/test_moe_layer.py, test_zero_expert_moe.py, tests/compile/* that don't independently call set_priority), which previously got the correct priority/wrap installed implicitly and will now silently dispatch to native or the wrong wrap mode. Standalone (non-Worker) inference/compilation entrypoints share the same risk. (3) Verify every non-worker `set_forward_context` path either constructs a worker first or explicitly installs priorities; consider keeping a lightweight guard (or documenting the new invariant that priorities must be installed before forward) and update affected tests.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a worker-less forward via set_forward_context (e.g. tests/kernels/moe/test_moe_layer.py) and assert the selected IR impl provider matches the configured priority rather than falling back to native.

findings.claim: (1) `IrOpPriorityConfig.set_priority()` is retained but `set_default()` is now the production path; a user who previously relied on per-forward re-installation of priority (e.g. mutating `ir_op_priority` fields between forward passes, or expecting the priority to be re-derived each forward from the live config) will no longer see updates take effect, because priority is installed exactly once at worker init and is no longer refreshed per forward. (2) This is a silent behavioral change for any runtime that mutates kernel priority after worker construction — the change will simply be ignored. (3) If runtime mutation of IR op priority is not supported, document this explicitly; if it is, provide an API to re-install `set_default()` after config changes.

(1) This is the core intended optimization and it is correct in direction: previously `set_forward_context` entered three context managers per forward pass — `ir_op_priority.set_priority()` (which iterates all op fields, calls `current_platform.import_ir_kernels()`, and re-filters/re-installs `_priority_impls` for every op on EVERY forward) plus `enable_torch_wrap()`. Moving this to a one-time `set_default()` at worker init removes per-forward ExitStack/context-manager overhead, repeated `import_ir_kernels()` calls, and repeated priority filtering from the hot path. (2) Positive: removes CPU-side per-forward Python overhead on the dispatch setup path. (3) No fix needed; confirming the perf claim is sound. Suggest a microbenchmark of set_forward_context enter/exit cost before/after.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark set_forward_context enter/exit latency (many small forward passes) before vs after; expect reduced CPU time per forward from eliminating import_ir_kernels + per-op set_priority context setup.

findings.claim: (1) `set_default()` calls `_iter_op_priorities()` which calls `current_platform.import_ir_kernels()` at `WorkerBase.__init__` time — this now runs before `init_device()`. Previously import_ir_kernels ran lazily on the first forward. (2) If any platform's `import_ir_kernels` implicitly depends on device/CUDA initialization state that is only established in `init_device()`, moving it earlier could fail or import CPU-only fallbacks. (3) Verify `import_ir_kernels` for CUDA/ROCm platforms has no dependency on device init having completed; if it does, move the `set_default()` call to after `init_device()`.

(1) The `IPCTrainerSendWeightsArgs.mode` field was renamed to `send_mode` with no backward-compat alias/deprecation shim. (2) Any existing caller constructing `IPCTrainerSendWeightsArgs(mode="ray", ...)` — or passing a dict `{"mode": ...}` into `trainer_send_weights` — will now fail with an unexpected-keyword `TypeError`, silently breaking downstream RL training scripts that pin to this public API. The docstring examples in this file still reference `mode`. (3) Either accept `mode` as a deprecated alias in `__post_init__`/`__init__` (map it to `send_mode` with a warning) or clearly document this as a breaking change and bump the relevant version note; also update the in-file docstring examples that still say `mode=`.

(1) `IPCWeightTransferUpdateInfo` was restructured: the `ipc_handles_pickled` field is removed, `ipc_handles` becomes a required positional field (no default) with a changed type union, and new `tensor_sizes`/`packed` fields are added. (2) This changes both the Python dataclass contract and the HTTP JSON wire format (`/update_weights` previously carried `ipc_handles_pickled`). A vLLM server and trainer on mismatched versions will fail to deserialize update payloads, and any code constructing this dataclass by keyword (`ipc_handles_pickled=...`) breaks. The removed `VLLM_ALLOW_INSECURE_SERIALIZATION` gate that lived in the old `__post_init__` must be preserved somewhere on the new HTTP receive path. (3) Verify the new HTTP path still enforces `VLLM_ALLOW_INSECURE_SERIALIZATION` before `pickle.loads`, and document/version the wire-format change so cross-version trainer↔server pairs fail loudly rather than silently mis-transferring weights.

(1) `import ray` was moved from a lazy import inside the `mode == "ray"` branch (previously at line 274) to an unconditional top-level module import. (2) `ipc_engine.py` is now unimportable in environments that do not have Ray installed, even for users who only use the HTTP or custom-callable `send_mode`, or who only import the module for its dataclasses (e.g. `IPCWeightTransferUpdateInfo`) on the vLLM server side. This is an unnecessary hard dependency broadening. (3) Keep the `import ray` local to the ray-send branch (or guard it) so HTTP/custom-callable users are not forced to install Ray.

(1) The `trainer_send_weights` docstring still documents `trainer_args` keys as `mode`, `llm_handle`, `url` and its Ray/HTTP examples still use `IPCTrainerSendWeightsArgs(mode="ray", ...)`. (2) After the `mode`→`send_mode` rename these examples are now wrong and will raise `TypeError` if copied — a misleading docstring is worse than none, and this is the public entrypoint most integrators copy from. (3) Update the docstring keys and both examples to use `send_mode`, and mention the new `packed`/`packed_buffer_size_bytes` options.

(1) The refactor changes the set of ROCm devices for which the OAI Triton MoE experts report support. The original gate `(9,0) <= (cap.major, cap.minor) < (11,0)` on ROCm accepted any device whose HIP capability tuple began with major 9 — which includes gfx906/gfx908 (both map to (9,0)) and gfx90a. The new `on_gfx9()` helper is backed by `_ON_GFX9 = any(arch in _GCN_ARCH for arch in ["gfx90a", "gfx942", "gfx950"])`, so gfx906/gfx908 are now EXCLUDED, and gfx90a is included via the string check rather than the (9,0) cap. (2) This is a behavior change beyond the stated 'widen to gfx12' intent: it silently narrows support on any gfx906/gfx908 (MI50/MI100-class) deployment that previously enabled these experts, which could flip such a device from the OAI Triton path to a fallback path (or to unsupported). (3) The PR message frames this as ROCm-scoped widening; confirm that dropping gfx906/gfx908 is intended and documented in the changelog/PR description, since the original comment explicitly claimed the window covered those cards. If gfx906/gfx908 were never actually working, add a code comment stating the intentional exclusion (the new comment does say 'on_gfx9() already excludes gfx906/gfx908', which supports this being deliberate — so the risk is primarily user-facing surprise rather than a code bug).

(1) The original guard used `p.is_cuda_alike()` (True for both CUDA and ROCm and any future cuda-alike platform), whereas the new helper only handles `p.is_cuda()` and `p.is_rocm()` explicitly and returns False otherwise. (2) If a new cuda-alike backend is ever added that is neither is_cuda() nor is_rocm(), it would now silently report unsupported where the old code may have accepted it based on the capability tuple. (3) This is almost certainly the intended narrowing (the two families are exhaustive today), so no change is strictly required — but a one-line comment noting that non-CUDA/non-ROCm cuda-alike platforms intentionally return False would prevent confusion during future porting.

(1) `has_triton_kernels()` is now called at both call sites as `_triton_kernel_moe_supports_current_device() and has_triton_kernels()`, but the original also short-circuited: on CUDA the device-capability check runs before `has_triton_kernels()`. Functionally equivalent and harmless. (2) Minor: since both `_supports_current_device` implementations are now byte-identical (`return _triton_kernel_moe_supports_current_device() and has_triton_kernels()`), consider folding the `has_triton_kernels()` check into the shared helper itself to avoid two call sites drifting apart later. (3) Move `has_triton_kernels()` into `_triton_kernel_moe_supports_current_device()` and have both classes call the helper directly. This is a readability/maintainability nit only.

(1) `use_v2_model_runner` is a plain @property that fully recomputes on every access, and the default path calls `_get_v2_model_runner_unsupported_features()`, which now includes `from importlib.metadata import entry_points` and `entry_points(group="vllm.logits_processors")` — an entry-point scan across all installed distributions. (2) This is potentially expensive (disk/metadata scan) and the property is read from multiple call sites (Scheduler.__init__, Worker.__init__, FlashInferMetadataBuilder.__init__, VllmConfig validation), so the cost may be paid repeatedly per process; if any caller reads it in a warmup/rebuild loop it becomes a hidden per-iteration cost. (3) Cache the result (e.g. compute once during __post_init__/validation and store on the instance, or use functools.cached_property, or hoist the entry_points() scan out of the hot check). At minimum verify no hot-loop caller reads this property.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time repeated `VllmConfig.use_v2_model_runner` access with several dozen installed distributions; compare against a cached-property variant.

findings.claim: (1) `use_v2_model_runner` is now evaluated during `__post_init__`/validation via `if self.use_v2_model_runner: self._validate_v2_model_runner()`. When the env var is unset and the model is a default-v2 candidate, the property internally calls `_get_v2_model_runner_unsupported_features()`; if unsupported features are present the default path silently returns False (only a warning_once), while the explicit-env path raises. This dual behavior is intentional, but note that `_validate_v2_model_runner` will now only run its raise-branch when the env var truthy OR the property already returned True — so features that make the property fall back to v1 will never hit the ValueError. Confirm this asymmetry (silent downgrade vs. hard error) is the intended contract and is documented, since users relying on VLLM_USE_V2_MODEL_RUNNER=1 get errors but default users get silent v1 fallback.

(1) `VLLM_USE_V2_MODEL_RUNNER` default changed from `bool = False` to `bool | None = None`, and the parser changed from `bool(int(...))` (defaulting '0') to `maybe_convert_bool(os.getenv(..., None))`. (2) Any external code reading `envs.VLLM_USE_V2_MODEL_RUNNER` and treating it as a strict bool (e.g. identity comparison `is False`, or passing to something type-checked) may break now that it can be `None`. The in-repo readers were migrated to `vllm_config.use_v2_model_runner`, but third-party/plugin code reading the env directly now sees a tri-state. (3) This is a deliberate behavior change enabling default-on for Qwen3; ensure it is called out in release notes as the env var semantics changed from 'off unless =1' to 'config-default unless explicitly set'.

(1) `_is_default_v2_model_runner_model` reads `model_config.is_moe` and `model_config.is_quantized`, but does not guard multimodal models. Qwen3 dense text architecture string `Qwen3ForCausalLM` is matched, yet a Qwen3-based multimodal or embedding variant reusing that architecture name (the test already covers pooling via runner_type, but not mm) could be incorrectly selected for v2 by default. (2) Silently routing an unsupported multimodal/config to the v2 runner-by-default could cause failures for users who never opted in. (3) Consider also gating on `model_config.multimodal_config is None` (or an explicit supported-feature allowlist) in the default-selection heuristic, matching the conservative intent of an oracle.

(1) The Qwen3.5 subclasses override the parent Qwen3VLForConditionalGeneration.__init__ via nn.Module.__init__ (they do NOT call super().__init__), so the parent's `self.model_config = vllm_config.model_config` assignment (qwen3_vl.py:1652) was previously not being run for these classes. This PR now adds `self.model_config = vllm_config.model_config` to both subclass __init__ methods. (2) This matters because the parent's SupportsEncoderCudaGraph path reads `self.model_config` (qwen3_vl.py:1807-1826 in get_encoder_cudagraph / dummy-input construction) — without this attribute the ViT CUDA graph capture path would raise AttributeError, which is exactly the bug this PR fixes to enable ViT full CUDA graph for Qwen3.5. The change looks correct and necessary. This finding merely confirms the added attribute has a real read/use path (Mandatory Check satisfied) — not a defect.

(1) `self._moe_model` is only assigned inside the `load_model()` block guarded by the surrounding code; the assignment `self._moe_model = moe_candidate` only happens when `is_mixture_of_experts(moe_candidate)` is True, but the subsequent EPLB-enable guard was changed from `is_mixture_of_experts(self.model)` to `self._moe_model is not None`. If `load_model()` is ever invoked more than once (e.g. weight reload / EEP scale-up-down path present in this file via initialize_layerwise_reload) `self._moe_model` is never reset to None, so a stale reference could persist. (2) More importantly, EPLB enable now depends on `_moe_model` being resolved during the same load pass; verify the resolution block always runs before both guards. Given the diff shows the resolution added immediately above the first guard this is likely fine, but the second guard at ~line 5050 relies on the same assignment surviving. Confidence that this is a real defect is moderate. (3) Consider resetting `self._moe_model = None` at the start of the resolution block to avoid stale references on reload.

(1) `get_language_model()` results are cached in a module-keyed global dict (`_language_model_by_module`) in interfaces.py, and the unwrapping only checks `isinstance(moe_candidate, SupportsMultiModal)`; if a VLM wrapper's language model is itself another wrapper (nested), a single unwrap will not reach the MoE module and `_moe_model` stays None, silently disabling EPLB for that model. (2) This would silently skip EPLB rebalancing for nested-wrapper VLMs without any warning. (3) Consider looping the unwrap until either a MoE model is found or no further language model can be resolved, and/or log a warning when `enable_eplb` is set but no MoE model was resolved.

(1) When `parallel_config.enable_eplb` is True but the resolution fails to find a MoE model (`_moe_model is None`), the code now silently does nothing, whereas the previous `is_mixture_of_experts(self.model)` check would have had identical behavior — however since the whole motivation is enabling EPLB for VLMs, a user who requests `enable_eplb` on a model where no MoE submodule is found gets no diagnostic. (2) Silent misconfiguration is hard to debug. (3) Emit a warning/log when `enable_eplb` is set but `_moe_model is None` after resolution.

(1) The merge buffer swap semantics must be verified for correctness. In the new code, `merge_output`/`merge_output_lse` are allocated once (via `torch.empty_like`) and then reused every iteration; `merge_attn_states` writes fresh results into these buffers, and the code swaps `output, merge_output = merge_output, output` so that the freshly-written buffer becomes `output` and the previous `output` becomes the scratch buffer for the next iteration. This is functionally equivalent to the old `torch.empty_like` per-iteration allocation because `merge_attn_states` reads from `prefix_output`/`suffix_output` (the old `output` and `attn_output`) and writes to a distinct destination buffer (`merge_output` != `output`), so there is no read/write aliasing within a single call. (2) The risk is a subtle write-into-source-buffer aliasing bug if the destination ever coincided with a source; here it does not. However, this optimization introduces a hard dependency on `merge_attn_states` treating `output` as a pure write target (never reading it) — this holds today per the kernel semantics but should be verified. (3) Confirm via unit test that chunked-prefill outputs are bitwise-identical before/after this change across >=3 chunks (iters), since the double-buffer swap only exercises correctly when there are 2+ merge iterations.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run chunked-prefill MLA correctness test with >=3 context chunks (e.g. long context DeepSeek-V3 prefill) and assert output/output_lse match the pre-change per-iteration allocation implementation bitwise.

findings.claim: (1) This is the core intended optimization: replacing two `torch.empty_like` allocations per loop iteration with a single pair of buffers allocated once on the first merge and reused via double-buffering (pointer swap). (2) Impact: for long-context chunked prefill with many context chunks (iters), the old code allocated 2 new tensors (output + output_lse) every iteration, causing repeated CUDA allocator activity and memory-pool churn in the hot attention path; the new code allocates at most one pair, reducing allocation overhead and peak fragmentation. This is a legitimate and correct performance win. (3) No fix needed; noted as a positive change. The only residual is that a `.contiguous()`/shape guarantee is implicitly assumed since `empty_like` copies stride from `output`, which the swap preserves.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark MLA chunked-prefill throughput / allocator time (torch.cuda.memory_stats num_alloc_retries and allocation count) on a long-context prefill (e.g. 32k+ context, small chunk_size forcing many iters) before/after.

findings.claim: (1) `merge_output_lse` is used and swapped but is only assigned inside the `if merge_output is None:` guard together with `merge_output`. This relies on the invariant that `merge_output` and `merge_output_lse` are always allocated together and stay in lockstep through the swaps. (2) If a future edit ever allocates one without the other, the code would raise `NameError`/`UnboundLocalError` since `merge_output_lse` has no initialization outside the guard (unlike `merge_output` which is initialized to `None` before the loop). This is a latent maintainability/robustness gap. (3) Consider also initializing `merge_output_lse = None` alongside `merge_output = None` before the loop for symmetry and to make the paired-allocation invariant explicit and lint-safe.

(1) The Phase-4 normed_x write path is only executed by blocks with n_idx < kNumTokens; but s_rsqrt[m] for a given block is only populated for m indices actually computed by *that block's* phase-3 loop. The comment claims 'Every block has every token's rsqrt[] in s_rsqrt already (computed independently in phase 3), so no cross-block synchronization is required.' This is TRUE only because every block runs the full phase-3 loop over all kNumTokens and computes rsqrt for all m — verify this holds: phase 3 does loop `for (m=0; m<kNumTokens; m++)` on tid==0 for every block, so s_rsqrt is fully populated per block. This appears correct, but the correctness hinges entirely on phase 3 iterating over ALL tokens in EVERY block (not just its own). Any future optimization that restricts phase-3 to a block's own token would silently break the normed_x output with no synchronization guard. (2) Risk: silent wrong normed_x rows feeding experts. (3) Add a static_assert/comment tightly coupling phase-3 full-M reduction to phase-4 dependency, or an explicit assertion.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Correctness check in benchmark_norm_router_gemm.py already compares fused vs unfused normed_x; run for all M=1..16.

findings.claim: (1) The fused GEMV computes logits[m,n] = rsqrt[m] * sum_k(x[m,k]*nw[k]*gw[n,k]) whereas the reference path is logits = gate_gemm(rms_norm(x)) where rms_norm rounds normed_x to bf16 BEFORE the GEMV. The fused kernel instead multiplies x (bf16->fp32) * nw (fp32) * gw in fp32 and applies rsqrt at the end, i.e. it never rounds the normalized activation to bf16 before accumulating logits. This is algebraically equivalent in exact arithmetic but numerically differs from the unfused reference which quantizes normed_x to bf16 first. (2) Risk: router logits differ from the two-kernel path by more than 1 ULP for edge cases; router top-k selection could flip an expert near a tie, changing outputs. The benchmark uses logits_atol=1e-2 which masks this. (3) Document the intended semantics (fused uses higher-precision normed_x internally for logits) and confirm router robustness, or round to bf16 mid-kernel to match reference exactly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare top-k expert selection (argmax/topk indices) fused vs unfused across many random seeds, not just logit magnitude.

findings.claim: (1) invokeNormRouterGemm hard-codes programmaticStreamSerializationAllowed = 1 (PDL always on), whereas the dsv3 reference kernel gates PDL behind getEnvEnablePDL(). (2) Risk: on setups where PDL is intentionally disabled (env var), the dsv4 kernel diverges from dsv3 behavior; PDL griddepcontrol may interact badly with unexpected launch contexts, and the behavior is no longer user-controllable. This is also a behavior inconsistency between two sibling kernels. (3) Use getEnvEnablePDL() to match dsv3_router_gemm_float_out.cu for consistency and controllability.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark with VLLM PDL env toggled on/off comparing dsv3 vs dsv4 launch behavior.

findings.claim: (1) The new custom op dsv4_norm_router_gemm has no register_fake / meta implementation, unlike other ops in this file. It allocates outputs via torch.empty then calls torch.ops._moe_C.dsv4_norm_router_gemm. If this op is called under torch.compile / meta tracing (the model uses @support_torch_compile), the missing fake kernel will break graph capture or fall back to eager. (2) Risk: torch.compile graph breaks or errors on the DSV4 routing path. (3) Note that dsv3_router_gemm also has no register_fake here, so this may be intentional (called outside compiled region). Verify the norm_gate_linear dispatch is outside the compiled graph; if inside, add a register_fake returning empty normed_x/logits with correct shapes/dtypes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the DSV4 model under torch.compile and confirm no graph break at the router.

findings.claim: (1) The op TORCH_CHECKs num_experts == kDsv4NumExperts (384) and hidden_dim == 7168, but the Python docstring in _custom_ops.py states 'num_experts in {256, 384}'. The kernel is only instantiated/valid for 384. (2) Risk: misleading docstring could lead a caller to dispatch with 256 experts, hitting a runtime TORCH_CHECK failure instead of falling back. (3) Fix the _custom_ops.py docstring to say num_experts == 384 only.

(1) In Phase 4, norm_weight is re-loaded from gmem and x_row is re-loaded and re-converted to float, duplicating the loads already done in Phase 1 for the writer block's own token. For the kNumTokens writer blocks this is a redundant second pass over x and nw. (2) Risk: minor extra gmem traffic in the low-latency path this kernel targets. (3) Could cache the writer block's own a_f/nw_f in registers/smem from phase 1, though register pressure may make the current re-read the better tradeoff — evaluate.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile phase-4 gmem traffic vs register-caching variant at M=1..16.

findings.claim: (1) `tokenspeed-mla==0.1.2` is added as an unconditional, exact-pinned hard dependency for ALL CUDA installs, not just Blackwell/R1 users. (2) The backend code already guards every import with try/except ImportError and treats absence as 'backend unavailable', so a hard requirement is unnecessary and forces every CUDA user (Ampere/Hopper, non-R1, non-fp8) to pull a niche Blackwell-only package; an exact `==` pin also prevents patch upgrades and can create resolver conflicts. (3) Move it to an optional/extras group (or a comment-only optional line) rather than a mandatory exact pin, matching how other optional accel deps are handled, and use `>=`/compatible-release instead of `==` if it must stay.

(1) The added `tokenspeed-mla==0.1.2` line has no trailing newline (diff shows `\ No newline at end of file`). (2) Missing final newline is a minor POSIX-compliance/diff-noise issue and some tooling appends to the last line. (3) Add a trailing newline.

(1) TOKENSPEED_MLA is inserted at auto-priority 2 on Blackwell MLA (immediately after FLASHINFER_MLA, ahead of CUTLASS_MLA), changing the default decode backend for DeepSeek-R1 + FP8-KV users who did not explicitly select a backend. (2) The author's own comment states it 'regresses at bs<=2' — i.e., for the common low-batch decode/latency-sensitive regime, auto-selection now silently picks a slower backend than the prior default, a behavior change for existing configs. (3) Consider gating the priority bump on batch size / expected concurrency, or place it below the previously-default backend so it only wins when explicitly selected, or document the low-batch regression prominently in release notes.

(1) `run_prefill_new_tokens` and `run_prefill_context_chunk` call `v = v.contiguous()` on every prefill invocation because `v` arrives as a non-contiguous split-view of `kv_nope`. (2) This forces a full copy of the value tensor on the prefill hot path for every layer/chunk, adding memory bandwidth and an allocation each call. (3) If the split producing `v` (in mla_attention.forward_mha / _compute_prefill_context) can be made to yield a contiguous slice (e.g. split along a leading dim or pre-arranging kv_nope layout), the per-call copy could be avoided; at minimum confirm the kernel truly requires contiguity vs. accepting strides. The author documented the tradeoff, so this is a suggestion.

(1) `_g_workspace` is a module-global dict keyed by `torch.device` that only ever grows (an entry per device is allocated and re-allocated when a larger size is needed, but never freed). (2) In multi-device / repeated-instantiation scenarios the workspace buffers persist for process lifetime, holding GPU memory even if a device's backend is no longer used; keying by device object also risks subtle duplicate entries if device objects differ (e.g. `cuda` vs `cuda:0`). (3) Normalize the device key (e.g. `torch.device(f'cuda:{idx}')`) and/or document the lifetime; minor since workspace is small and typically per-active-device.

(1) `TokenspeedMLABackend.supports_combination` re-derives R1 MLA dimensions from `hf_text_config` and hard-codes the (128,64,128) check inline, duplicating the logic already provided by `is_deepseek_r1_mla_compatible()` in prefill/selector.py. (2) Duplicated shape-check logic can drift out of sync if R1 dims or the source-of-truth helper change, producing inconsistent accept/reject decisions between prefill and decode selection. (3) Reuse the existing `is_deepseek_r1_mla_compatible(vllm_config)` helper (or a shared constant) instead of re-implementing the getattr/compare block.

(1) In `_PREFILL_BACKEND_CONFIG`, the `cudnn` entry is silently remapped to `mla_prefill_backend_enum: FLASHINFER` with a comment that cuDNN was removed. (2) A benchmark labeled 'cudnn' that actually runs FlashInfer produces misleading benchmark results attributed to the wrong backend and can confuse comparisons. (3) Either remove the `cudnn` benchmark entry entirely (since it no longer exists) or rename it to reflect the fallback so results are not mislabeled.

(1) The new `TOKENSPEED_MLA` prefill table row has an empty Description cell (the column after the backend name is blank). (2) An empty description in the backend reference table leaves users without a summary of what the backend is (CuTe DSL MLA prefill for Blackwell/R1). (3) Fill in the description cell, e.g. 'TokenSpeed CuTe DSL MLA'.

(1) Caching the workspace view in `__init__` and reusing it on every `trtllm_ragged_attention_deepseek` call is unsafe given the WorkspaceManager semantics. `get_simultaneous()` returns a *view* into `_current_workspaces[ubatch_id]`; when any other workspace consumer later needs a larger allocation, `_ensure_workspace_size()` sets that slot to `None`, calls `torch.accelerator.empty_cache()`, and allocates a brand-new tensor — orphaning all previously returned views. Previously the trtllm backend re-fetched a fresh view on every prefill call via `_get_workspace_buffer()`, so it always used the *current* (possibly resized) buffer. Now it always passes the stale `__init__`-time view. (2) If the shared workspace is grown by another consumer between this backend's construction and a prefill forward (which happens during warmup before `lock_workspace()`), the cached buffer points to freed/reused GPU memory, causing silent numerical corruption or use-after-free aliasing in the attention kernel. (3) Either re-fetch the view each call as before, or (preferred) reserve the size in `__init__` to satisfy warmup but still fetch the current view at call time, or explicitly document/guarantee that the workspace is locked at a fixed size before this backend is ever constructed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run MLA prefill (DeepSeek R1) on SM100 with TRTLLM_RAGGED backend alongside another workspace consumer that grows the shared workspace during warmup; verify prefill output numerics remain correct vs. re-fetching the view per call.

findings.claim: (1) `init_attn_backend` probes builders via `hasattr(builder, "_get_workspace_buffer")` and calls `builder._get_workspace_buffer()` to share a single workspace across attention backends. This PR removes `_get_workspace_buffer()` from `TrtllmRaggedPrefillBackend`. (2) While the probed object here is the *metadata builder* (not the prefill backend), the removed method was the only public-ish accessor for this backend's workspace; if anything (now or later) relied on that duck-typed hook to discover/share the trtllm-ragged workspace, that sharing path is silently dropped, potentially allocating a redundant workspace buffer. (3) Confirm no builder or wrapper delegates to the removed `_get_workspace_buffer`; if the hook was intended, replace it with a stable accessor (e.g. keep `_get_workspace_buffer` returning `self._workspace_buffer`).

(1) The stated intent (bugfix for warmup) is achieved by reserving the workspace at construction, but note the flashinfer backend already only consumed the workspace view at wrapper-creation time (first `prepare_metadata`), so behavior there is effectively unchanged — the eager `__init__` allocation is the meaningful fix. (2) For flashinfer this is benign because the FlashInfer wrapper internally retains the buffer once, but it is worth confirming that no workspace resize can occur between `__init__` and the first `plan()`/`run()` (otherwise the wrapper would hold a stale buffer just like the trtllm concern). (3) Add a short comment documenting that workspace reservation must happen before lock and that the wrapper caches the buffer, to prevent future regressions.

(1) This commit reverts PR #39917, which had replaced the routing-replay transfer mechanism with a GPU device cache plus an async device-to-host (D2H) pipeline. The revert reintroduces the older implementation based on multiprocessing.SharedMemory + fcntl file locking and synchronous `.cpu().numpy()` D2H transfers (the reverted file re-imports `fcntl`, `os`, `tempfile`, `shared_memory`, and `unittest.mock.patch`). (2) The pre-change (device-cache) implementation issued a non-blocking D2H copy on a dedicated CUDA stream and deferred the host scatter to the next step, so the transfer overlapped the next forward pass at ~2% throughput overhead (per the deleted docs). A synchronous `.cpu()` forces the GPU command queue to drain before the copy begins, stalling the main compute stream on every step where routing capture is active — a classic training/inference-loop throughput killer. This regression is only incurred when `--enable-return-routed-experts` is set, so it does not affect the default path. (3) If the revert is only meant to unblock a correctness/stability bug in the async pipeline, add a follow-up to reintroduce the async, non-blocking D2H (and cudagraph-static device buffer) once the underlying issue is fixed, rather than permanently reverting to the synchronous SharedMemory path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Throughput (tokens/s) of an MoE model (e.g. a DeepSeek/Qwen3-MoE) served with `--enable-return-routed-experts` before vs after this commit, at ISL=1024/OSL=1024, measuring the per-step D2H stall on the main compute stream.

findings.claim: (1) The revert reintroduces multiprocessing.SharedMemory + fcntl file-lock based transfer of routing data from GPU workers to the scheduler. (2) Beyond the synchronous D2H, the file-locking adds per-transfer syscall/lock-contention overhead in the hot path and (per the deleted design notes) is node-local, so it cannot support multi-node tensor-parallel setups where the scheduler runs on a different node than some workers — the async/device-cache path routed data through the Ray DAG instead. This is both a performance and a functional-scope regression for large (400B+) multi-node MoE deployments using routing replay. (3) Confirm the multi-node use case is intentionally out of scope for the reverted state; if not, restore the Ray-DAG-based transfer path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare routing-replay latency/throughput under multi-node TP with the SharedMemory path (this commit) vs the Ray-DAG device-cache path (parent).

findings.claim: (1) The revert removes `_validate_return_routed_experts()` and its call site, which had rejected unvalidated parallelism combinations (PP>1, PCP>1, DCP>1, async scheduling) when `--enable-return-routed-experts` was set. (2) Without this guard, enabling routed-experts capture under those configurations will no longer fail fast at config time; instead users may get silently-wrong routing data (e.g. under async scheduling the worker-side stop predicate reads a placeholder `-1` token, dropping routing) or a later crash. This is a behavior change for users on the boundary. (3) If the reverted (SharedMemory) implementation actually supports these configs, this removal is fine; otherwise reintroduce the config-time validation so unsupported combinations are rejected explicitly rather than producing incorrect routing arrays.

(1) The revert removes the API response fields `routed_experts` (on Completion/Chat choice) and `prompt_routed_experts` (on Completion/Chat response), plus the corresponding SDK output fields. (2) Any RL-training/consumer code written against PR #39917's response schema will now receive responses lacking these fields (KeyError / attribute None) — a breaking API/output-contract change for users of routing replay. Since this reverts a previously merged public field, downstream integrations built in the interim window will break with no deprecation path. (3) If routing replay is being fully rolled back, document the removal in release notes; if it will return, ensure the field names/shapes are preserved so the round-trip is stable.

(1) The abort is run in a daemon thread and joined with timeout=5.0, but there is no check of whether the join actually completed. If ncclCommAbort blocks longer than 5s (e.g. graphs not yet released, or a genuinely hung comm), join() returns while the daemon thread is still executing inside ncclCommAbort, holding a reference to self.comm. Meanwhile the code immediately proceeds to set self.available=False/self.disabled=True and the caller may free CUDA context / interpreter shutdown, leading to a use-after-free or crash in the still-running abort thread. (2) This reintroduces exactly the class of orphaned/crashing teardown the PR aims to fix, just moved off the main thread. (3) Capture the join result via abort_thread.is_alive() after join and log a warning if the abort did not complete within the timeout, and avoid tearing down state the daemon thread still depends on (e.g. keep a reference to self.comm alive, or gate cleanup on successful join).

(1) NCCL_CHECK inside ncclCommAbort raises RuntimeError on non-zero result. Because _abort runs in a separate thread, any such exception is not propagated to the main thread; it is only printed by the default threading excepthook and silently swallowed, so a failed abort looks identical to a successful one. (2) A silently failing abort can leave the communicator/GPU resources in a bad state without any signal to the caller, defeating the bugfix intent and making failures undebuggable. (3) Wrap the abort call to capture success/failure in a shared variable (or use a result container) and log/inspect it in the main thread after join.

(1) destroy() previously performed a clean collective ncclCommDestroy; it now unconditionally calls ncclCommAbort instead. ncclCommAbort is a forceful abort with different semantics (no collective sync, may skip flushing pending ops). For the normal coordinated shutdown case (all ranks alive, no orphaning) this changes teardown behavior for every user of PyNcclCommunicator, not only the orphaned-process edge case the PR targets. (2) Always aborting rather than cleanly destroying can mask real teardown ordering bugs and may leave in-flight collectives incomplete. (3) Consider keeping ncclCommDestroy for the normal path and only falling back to ncclCommAbort on timeout/known orphan scenarios, or document explicitly why abort is now always preferred.

(1) The 5.0s join timeout is a hardcoded magic constant applied on every communicator destroy. In the common case where the graphs are already dropped, the abort returns quickly; but if it does block, every destroy() now adds up to 5s of latency to shutdown, multiplied across communicators. (2) Slow/hanging shutdown across many ranks/communicators. (3) Factor the timeout into a named constant or env var and document the rationale; consider a shorter timeout given the abort is expected to return promptly once graphs are released.

(1) After this PR removes the `self.num_speculative_tokens` argument from the `apply_penalties` call (previously sampler.py:145), the instance attribute `self.num_speculative_tokens` assigned in `__init__` has no remaining reader anywhere in the codebase (grep confirms only the assignment site remains). (2) Impact is minor: it leaves a dead/assigned-but-unused attribute and stale intent, which can mislead future maintainers into thinking speculative-token count still influences penalty computation. (3) Fix: either drop the now-unused attribute assignment, or, if the constructor parameter must be retained for API/back-compat reasons, add a brief comment noting it is kept only for signature compatibility. Note: keep the `num_speculative_tokens` constructor *parameter* to avoid breaking callers.

(1) `_should_share` calls `torch.equal(w, target.weight)` on GPU tensors, which returns a Python bool and therefore forces a full device→host synchronization; the low-memory branch additionally does `w.cpu()` and `target.weight.cpu()`, copying the entire embedding/lm_head weight (vocab_size × hidden, potentially hundreds of MB) to host memory. (2) This is expensive, but it runs only once per model at load time inside `load_eagle_model`, not in the decode/training hot loop, so throughput impact is bounded. (3) If the intent is only to detect the common 'draft has no own copy' case, prefer relying on the existing `has_own_*` flags / identity (`w is target.weight` / `data_ptr()` comparison) and reserve the full `torch.equal` weight comparison for the genuinely ambiguous case, and document that this is a one-time load-time cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model load time for a large MTP/EAGLE draft (e.g. DeepSeek-V3 MTP) with and without the new _should_share weight comparison, including the low-memory .cpu() fallback path.

findings.claim: (1) Embedding sharing is now gated on `get_pp_group().world_size == 1`, whereas the parent code unconditionally shared the target embedding (guarded only by `has_own_embed_tokens`). This is a behavioral change: with PP>1 the draft embedding is never shared and always keeps/loads its own copy. (2) If a draft/MTP checkpoint omits its own vocab embedding (relying on sharing), then under PP>1 `draft_inner.embed_tokens` will remain the randomly-initialized `VocabParallelEmbedding` created in the model `__init__` (see DeepSeekMultiTokenPredictor.embed_tokens), producing wrong embeddings and silently degraded/garbage draft tokens. (3) Confirm every PP>1-capable draft checkpoint actually ships its own embed_tokens weights; if not, the PP guard must instead share the PP-rank-local target embedding rather than skip sharing entirely, or add an assertion that the draft owns embeddings under PP>1.

(1) The new topk_indices_buffer sharing replaces `draft_inner.topk_indices_buffer` with `target_inner.topk_indices_buffer`. This is a runtime scratch buffer (sized to `max_num_batched_tokens × index_topk`, written in-place by the sparse indexer), not a weight. Sharing a mutable scratch buffer between the target model and the draft model means the draft's sparse-attention indexer will read/overwrite the same memory the target uses. (2) If the draft (MTP) forward and target forward interleave (or if their token counts differ), one can clobber the indices the other expects, yielding wrong sparse-attention topk selection and silently degraded output — and it is unclear the draft is guaranteed to fully repopulate the buffer before use. (3) Verify the draft always writes the full slice it reads before consuming it (as the target does via the indexer), and if buffer reuse across target/draft is not provably safe within a single forward pass, keep separate per-model buffers or add an assertion/comment documenting the reuse invariant.

(1) The parent code selected the target embedding from the target *language* model's inner model (`target_language_model.model`), but the new code binds `target_inner = target_language_model.model` and then reads `topk_indices_buffer` from `target_inner`. However, in DeepSeek-V4/V32 the `topk_indices_buffer` is created on the top-level model (e.g. `deepseek_v4.py` sets `self.topk_indices_buffer` on the model object, and mla/deepseek_v2 store it on the attention/decoder module), not necessarily on `target_language_model.model`. (2) If `hasattr(target_inner, 'topk_indices_buffer')` is False on the real target structure, the sharing silently no-ops and the draft keeps its own (correctly sized) buffer — benign — but if the attribute lives one level up, the intended sharing never happens and any downstream code that assumed shared buffers (memory savings) is not realized. (3) Confirm the actual attribute location on target models that need this (DeepSeek MTP / V32) and read it from the correct object; add a test asserting the buffer is actually shared for those models.

(1) `_should_share` returns True when `not getattr(eagle, flag, False)`. Note `has_own_lm_head`/`has_own_embed_tokens` default to False in interfaces.py, and models like gemma4_mtp set `has_own_lm_head = True` as a class attribute. The `torch.equal` comparison path is reached only when the draft *claims* to own the weight but the tensors happen to be bit-identical, in which case it shares (drops the draft copy). (2) For a model that intentionally owns and fine-tuned an lm_head that merely starts numerically equal to the target (or is equal at load before any per-layer scaling), sharing would alias the draft's head to the target and drop the distinct copy, which could be surprising. (3) Consider comparing identity/data_ptr first and only treating exact `torch.equal` matches as share-eligible when the ownership flag is explicitly a load-time optimization; document precisely why equal weights justify discarding a self-declared 'own' copy.

(1) The token_type_ids builder was changed to use `self.optimistic_seq_lens_cpu[:num_reqs]` instead of the previous `self.seq_lens[:num_reqs]` (GPU) to avoid the GPU sync from `torch.arange(seq_lens[i])`. (2) Risk: `optimistic_seq_lens_cpu` holds the *optimistic* projected sequence lengths (the value assuming all speculative tokens from the previous iteration were accepted, see comment at line ~1938 and set at 5519), which can differ from the true `self.seq_lens` used elsewhere. If the token_type_ids builder must reflect the actual number of tokens in the current forward (e.g. actual prompt+generated length), using the optimistic (possibly over-counted) value could produce token_type_id vectors of the wrong length / wrong content, and the resulting `torch.cat(...)` sum-length may not match the model's actual input length. (3) Fix: confirm that at the point `_maybe_add_token_type_ids` runs, `optimistic_seq_lens_cpu` equals the true per-request seq_lens for the requests carrying token_type_ids (encoder/cross-encoder models generally have no accepted spec tokens, so this may be safe); if not guaranteed, mirror seq_lens to a CPU buffer specifically or assert equality.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a token_type_ids model (e.g. an encoder-decoder / classification-head model) with speculative decoding enabled and verify token_type_ids length and values match the non-optimized code path.

findings.claim: (1) `_run_ar` now always allocates the reduction tensor on CPU (`tensor_cpu = torch.zeros(...)`) and does `.to(device, non_blocking=True)`. But `_get_device_and_group` may return `device="cpu"` and the CPU process group when `disable_nccl_for_dp_synchronization` is set. (2) In that CPU path, `tensor_cpu.to("cpu", non_blocking=True)` is a no-op copy that may return the same tensor (or a copy) on CPU, which is then passed to `dist.all_reduce` on the CPU group — this is fine functionally, but note the `non_blocking=True` on a pinned=False CPU->CPU copy has no async benefit and the change added an extra allocation vs. writing directly. More importantly, for the GPU path the `.to(device, non_blocking=True)` copy of a *non-pinned* CPU tensor is effectively synchronous (non_blocking only helps for pinned memory). (3) Fix: allocate `tensor_cpu` with `pin_memory=True` for the GPU path so the H2D copy is genuinely async; or short-circuit the CPU-group case to build directly on CPU without the redundant `.to`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark _run_ar H2D copy latency with pinned vs non-pinned CPU source tensor; verify DP synchronization correctness with disable_nccl_for_dp_synchronization=1.

findings.claim: (1) In `gather_specific_token_logprobs`, per-request token lists are still written one at a time into `token_ids_cpu[req_idx, 1:num_tokens+1] = torch.as_tensor(token_ids)` inside a Python loop, then a single non-blocking H2D upload is done — this is a genuine improvement over the previous per-request GPU indexing. (2) Minor: this code path only runs for the generative_scoring API (`logprob_token_ids` set), so it is not on the hot decode path; the loop building the CPU matrix is fine. (3) No fix required — noting that `token_ids_tensor[:, 0] = sampled` after the async upload correctly fills column 0 on-device from the GPU `sampled` tensor, avoiding a D2H+re-upload. This is correct.

(1) `bincount` replaced `prompt_bin_mask[expanded_idx_mapping] = 0` / `output_bin_counts[...] = 0` with `index_fill_(0, idx_long, 0)`. Both tensors are 2D ([max_num_reqs, cdiv(vocab,32)] and [max_num_reqs, vocab]), and both original advanced-indexing assignment and `index_fill_(0, idx, 0)` zero entire rows selected along dim 0 — behavior is equivalent. `expanded_idx_mapping.long()` is added because index_fill_ requires a LongTensor index. (2) Correctness is preserved. Note: if `expanded_idx_mapping` ever contained duplicate indices, `tensor[idx]=0` and `index_fill_` both handle duplicates identically (idempotent set-to-zero), so no divergence. (3) No fix needed; flagged only to confirm the equivalence was verified.

(1) The new token_type_ids path builds a list of per-request CPU `torch.arange(...)` tensors then `torch.cat(..., out=token_type_ids_cpu)` into a pinned buffer and uploads non-blocking. This is correct and avoids the sync. (2) Minor performance note: `torch.empty(sum(seq_lens_cpu), ...)` allocates a fresh pinned buffer every call; for hot paths pinned allocation is comparatively expensive. Since this only runs for token_type_id models (rare) it is acceptable. (3) Optional fix: reuse a persistent pinned staging buffer sized to max_num_tokens if this becomes hot.

(1) `_warmup_ssd_kernels` is invoked from the `attn_metadata is None` branch of `conv_ssm_forward`, which is the profile-run path executed for every mamba layer once. But the SSD Triton kernels' autotune cache is keyed on dtype and head/state dimensions (as the code comment itself states), which are typically identical across all MambaMixer2 layers of a model.
