(3) Confirm cross-attention path never receives async-spec-optimistic metadata, or gate this on a field that is exact for decode rows; add an assertion/comment justifying exactness for the cross-attention case specifically.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Whisper/encoder-decoder model with spec-decode (if supported) and mixed prefill/decode batch; verify num_cache_decodes and slot_mapping match the pre-PR baseline.

findings.claim: (1) `InputBatch` gains a new non-default field `seq_lens_cpu_upper_bound: torch.Tensor` (input_batch.py) placed among existing fields; any external constructor of `InputBatch` that does not pass it (positionally or by keyword) will break. (2) This is a dataclass/NamedTuple-style contract change in a worker-internal structure; while internal, out-of-tree runners or tests constructing InputBatch directly will fail. (3) Consider giving it a default of `None` (like the metadata field) or auditing all InputBatch construction sites; the diff only updates the two in-repo builders.

(1) SlidingWindowSpec.head_size_v is declared with default `int = None` and only defaulted to head_size in __post_init__, but the SlidingWindowSpec.merge path is inherited from AttentionSpec/KVCacheSpec.merge (deepcopy of specs[0]) and does not carry head_size_v through the merge-time field equality check, so mismatched head_size_v across layers is not validated. More importantly, the new `head_size_v` field is compared for equality via the frozen dataclass but the surrounding code (e.g. UniformTypeKVCacheSpecs.is_uniform_type) only compares sliding_window, so two SlidingWindowSpec layers with different head_size_v would incorrectly be treated as uniform. (2) This risks allocating a KV cache page sized for one head_size_v but shared with a layer needing a different one, producing silently wrong cache layout / OOB reads. (3) Add head_size_v to the uniformity/merge validation for SlidingWindowSpec, mirroring how FullAttentionSpec.merge asserts head_size_v equality.

(1) `FlashAttentionDiffKVBackend.set_head_size_v(self.v_head_dim)` mutates a *class-level* attribute shared across ALL Attention layers/models using this backend. In MiMoV2, the SWA (compressed-softmax) layers and the normal-attention layers can have different v_head_dim (swa_v_head_dim vs v_head_dim). Because each MiMoV2Attention.__init__ overwrites the class variable, the last-constructed layer's v_head_dim wins for all layers' get_kv_cache_shape() and FlashAttentionDiffKVImpl (which reads FlashAttentionDiffKVBackend.head_size_v). If the two v_head_dims differ, KV cache shape and the FA version re-derivation for earlier layers become wrong. (2) Silent wrong KV cache allocation / attention output corruption for models with heterogeneous v_head_dim. (3) head_size_v must be per-layer (passed via head_size_v arg to Attention / stored on the impl / spec) rather than a mutable class attribute; the class-var approach only works when all layers share one v_head_dim.

(1) FlashAttentionDiffKVImpl.__init__ re-derives self.vllm_flash_attn_version using `FlashAttentionDiffKVBackend.head_size_v` (a class attribute) instead of the per-instance V head size. Combined with set_head_size_v being called per-layer at model build time, the value read here reflects whichever layer set it last, not necessarily this impl's layer. (2) The FA3->FA4 upgrade decision (sinks + hdim!=hdim_v) may be computed with the wrong head_size_v, selecting FA3 (which rejects s_aux for diff hdim) or an unnecessary FA4 for a given layer. (3) Pass the layer's actual head_size_v into the impl (e.g. via extra_impl_args or the backend instance) rather than reading the mutable class attribute.

(1) `flash_attn_supports_sinks()` now returns `get_flash_attn_version() in (3, 4)`, but it calls get_flash_attn_version() with no arguments (no head_size/head_size_v/has_sinks), so the returned version reflects the *default* selection, not the diff-kv-with-sinks path. On SM90 the default is FA3, which per the new comment 'rejects s_aux when hdim != hdim_v'. Thus this predicate can report sinks-supported (via FA3) while the actual diff-kv layer would need the FA3->FA4 upgrade that this call cannot trigger. (2) Callers using flash_attn_supports_sinks() as a capability gate may enable sinks on configurations where the concrete kernel invocation still rejects them. (3) Consider documenting that this is a coarse capability check, or thread head_size/head_size_v/has_sinks through when the caller has diff-kv context.

(1) The FA4 branch now forwards `learnable_sink=s_aux` to `_flash_attn_fwd`, but other diff-kv-relevant kwargs are still dropped in the FA4 path: q_descale/k_descale/v_descale, scheduler_metadata, and alibi_slopes are asserted None, while s_aux is now passed. There is no validation that the FA4 cute `_flash_attn_fwd` actually supports learnable_sink together with the diff head_size_v (qk!=v) varlen paged path used by DiffKV. If the cute kernel silently ignores an unsupported combination, sinks would be dropped without error. (2) Silent incorrect attention (sinks not applied) on the FA4 diff-kv path. (3) Add an assertion/feature check that the FA4 cute interface supports learnable_sink for the varlen+paged+diff-hdim case, or a version guard.

(1) The new FA3->FA4 upgrade block only triggers for `device_capability.major == 9` (SM90). On SM100+ (major>=10) the default is already FA4, but a diff-kv layer with sinks and head_size>128 (and !=192) can be downgraded to FA2 by the existing TMEM block below, and FA2 raises NotImplementedError on s_aux. There is no diff-kv+sinks safeguard for the SM100+ path. (2) On Blackwell a diff-kv sinks layer with large head_size would fall back to FA2 and then crash at runtime ('FA2 does not support s_aux'). (3) Consider handling the SM100+ diff-kv-with-sinks case explicitly rather than only SM90.

(1) In `apply_softcap` (cpu_attn_impl.hpp ~line 1289) the new `fast_exp_f16` is used to compute tanh via `vec = fast_exp_f16(vec); inv_vec = 1/vec; vec = (vec - inv_vec)/(vec + inv_vec)`, i.e. an intermediate value of a *softcap* computation, not a value that is 'downcast to FP16/BF16' as the reduced-precision routine's own documentation requires. (2) Risk: (a) the softcap-tanh output is fed back into the logits (multiplied by softcap_scale) and then re-enters the softmax; a lower-precision exp introduces error in BOTH the numerator and denominator of the tanh quotient that does not fully cancel, degrading logit accuracy before softmax; (b) `fast_exp_f16` clamps `exp(x)` to +inf for x>88.376. Here x = logit/softcap_scale. If any logit/softcap magnitude ever pushes the argument above the bound, `vec=inf` gives `inv_vec = 1/inf = 0` and `(inf-0)/(inf+0) = inf/inf = NaN`, silently corrupting the softcapped logit — whereas the intended tanh saturates to ±1. The prior `fast_exp` had the same inf-clamp, so this is a pre-existing edge case, but switching the softcap path to the *lower-precision* variant is a behavioral/accuracy change that is not clearly motivated by the PR's stated 'outputs downcast to FP16/BF16' rationale. (3) Fix: restrict `fast_exp_f16` to the softmax exp path (line ~1213 first loop) where the exp result is directly saved to `prob_buffer_t` (reduced precision), and keep the full-accuracy `fast_exp` in `apply_softcap`; or add an explicit accuracy justification/benchmark showing softcap results are within tolerance.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run CPU attention on aarch64 with FP16/BF16 query dtype and a model using attention logit softcapping (e.g. Gemma-2). Compare per-token logits and output tensors and end-to-end accuracy (e.g. logprob / perplexity) against the FP32 `fast_exp` softcap path to quantify the error introduced by the reduced-precision exp.

findings.claim: (1) The new comment block in cpu_arch_macros.h contains a duplicated/garbled clause: 'Accurate within 1 ULP for FP16 Accurate within 1 ULP for BF16 for inputs in [-87.683, 88.376]'. The 'Accurate within 1 ULP for FP16' fragment appears to be a copy-paste artifact merged with the BF16 sentence. (2) A wrong/garbled accuracy claim in a numerics routine is misleading — a reviewer or downstream user relying on '1 ULP FP16' accuracy could make incorrect precision assumptions (a third-degree FP32 polynomial exp is very unlikely to be 1 ULP accurate in FP32; the claim is presumably about the error being below FP16/BF16 rounding after downcast). (3) Fix: rewrite the sentence to state the intended guarantee precisely, e.g. 'Result rounds to within 1 ULP of exp(x) when subsequently cast to FP16 and to BF16, for inputs in [-87.683, 88.376]'.

(1) `neon_expf_f16` uses an integer scale built as `e = (int(round(x/ln2))) << 23`, added directly to the bit pattern of the polynomial `p` (`y = reinterpret(reinterpret_u32(p) + e)`), rather than forming a proper 2^n float with exponent bias like the original `neon_expf` (which uses `vaddq_u32(e, exponent_bias)` then multiplies). This relies on p being in [~1,2) so that adding n to the exponent field is valid; for the documented input range [-87.683, 88.376] n ranges roughly [-127, 127], and the resulting exponent field could reach/exceed the denormal (n very negative) or inf (n near +127) boundaries, where bit-additive scaling on a mantissa-carrying float can produce slightly wrong results near the extremes. The explicit lower/upper clamps to 0/inf mask the worst cases, but values just inside the bounds are not validated in the diff. (2) Risk: subtle exp inaccuracy near the range boundaries that the '1 ULP' claim would not cover. (3) Fix: confirm via test vectors across the full [-87.683, 88.376] range that the bit-additive exponent scaling matches a reference exp to the claimed tolerance, especially near ±the clamp bounds; document the assumption that p stays in the range where exponent-field addition is exact.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep inputs uniformly over [-87.683, 88.376] (and a few points just outside), compare neon_expf_f16 output against a high-precision reference exp, and report max relative error / ULP error after casting to FP16 and BF16.

findings.claim: (1) `self.fa_version` is computed via `get_flash_attn_version(head_size=head_size)` in `__init__` using the model's real head_dim, then applied uniformly to all three prefill flash_attn call sites (first-chunk prefill, `q_len==seq_len` continuation, and `_continuation_prefill`). The last call site (`_continuation_prefill`, ~line 763) calls FA with `max_seqlen_q=q_len, max_seqlen_k=seq_len` where seq_len > q_len (a proper cross-attention/continuation case attending to prior cached KV). (2) FA4 (`_flash_attn_fwd` cute path) and FA3 have differing support for varlen prefill where max_seqlen_k != max_seqlen_q with cu_seqlens_k built from raw tensors; FA4 in particular has known head-dim/TMEM constraints and the CuteDSL path may not support this cross-length varlen configuration identically to FA2. Silently selecting FA3/FA4 here could produce wrong results or fall over at runtime rather than at the well-tested FA2 path. (3) Verify FA3/FA4 correctness for the continuation case (max_seqlen_k>max_seqlen_q) explicitly, and add a targeted test/assert; if unsupported, force fa_version=2 for the continuation call site.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run gsm8k eval configs (now without --enforce-eager) on Hopper (FA3) and Blackwell (FA4) with multi-chunk prefill (max-model-len > chunk size) and compare accuracy vs the FA2 baseline; add a unit test exercising _continuation_prefill with seq_len > q_len under each fa_version.

findings.claim: (1) `fa_version` is resolved once at `__init__` and cached on the impl. `get_flash_attn_version` also honors `vllm_config.attention_config.flash_attn_version` and `VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT`, which are process-wide and stable, so caching is fine; however head_size passed is the model head_dim while the TQ cache/effective head sizes differ elsewhere in this file. (2) If any future code path relies on the FA4>128 TMEM guard using effective head size rather than model head_dim, the cached decision could be wrong. (3) Add an inline comment clarifying that `head_size` here is the model's real head_dim (matching the get_kv_cache_shape docstring) so the FA4 head-dim guard is evaluated correctly.

(1) `_flash_attn_varlen` duplicates the entire kwargs block just to conditionally include `fa_version`. Since `flash_attn_varlen_func` already defaults `fa_version=DEFAULT_FA_VERSION` (=2), the None case and the explicit case only differ in whether `fa_version` is passed. (2) The duplicated argument list is error-prone: a future edit to one branch (e.g. adding window_size or softcap) can easily be forgotten in the other, silently diverging behavior between FA-available-version and None paths. (3) Build a single kwargs dict and conditionally add `fa_version` before one call, e.g. `kwargs = dict(...); if self.fa_version is not None: kwargs['fa_version'] = self.fa_version; return flash_attn_varlen_func(**kwargs)`.

(1) The four gsm8k eval configs drop `--enforce-eager`, enabling CUDA graph capture for TQ. The TQ metadata builder declares `AttentionCGSupport.UNIFORM_BATCH` and fixes NUM_KV_SPLITS for cudagraph, but the newly-enabled FA3/FA4 prefill path is invoked from within forward; FA3 uses scheduler_metadata and FA4 uses batch-shape-dependent scheduling that can break under CUDA-graph capture/replay if shapes vary. (2) If prefill runs inside a captured graph with the new flash_attn path, dynamic seqlens could cause incorrect replay or capture failures — regressing accuracy silently. (3) Confirm prefill is excluded from CUDA-graph capture (decode-only capture) for TQ, or document why FA3/FA4 prefill is graph-safe here; keep the accuracy thresholds validated in CI on the target hardware.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the four TQ gsm8k configs without --enforce-eager on Hopper and Blackwell and confirm accuracy meets thresholds and no CUDA-graph capture errors occur.

findings.claim: (1) When every modality has limit 0 (fully text-only usage of an MM-capable model, e.g. all `--limit-mm-per-prompt X=0`), the filtered `mm_limits` becomes empty but the code still proceeds to call `processor.dummy_inputs.get_dummy_processor_inputs(...)` and `processor.apply(...)` with an empty `mm_counts`, doing wasted warmup work. (2) Impact is minor: some avoidable startup latency and a spurious 'Multi-modal warmup completed' log line. (3) Consider `if mm_limits:` guarding the try-block so MM warmup is skipped entirely when no modality is enabled. Note the PR's own test `test_all_zero_limits_passes_empty_mm_counts` documents that warmup still runs in this case, so this may be intentional; hence low severity.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure renderer.warmup() wall-time for an MM-capable model launched with all modalities set to limit=0, comparing current behavior vs. early-skip when filtered mm_limits is empty.

findings.claim: (1) The default `gpu_memory_utilization` is changed from 0.9 to 0.92, and simultaneously `VLLM_MEMORY_PROFILER_ESTIMATE_CUDAGRAPHS` default flips from False to True (envs.py). (2) These two changes together silently alter behavior for every user relying on defaults: enabling CUDA-graph memory estimation reduces the KV-cache budget (it subtracts `cudagraph_memory_estimate_applied`), while raising the util to 0.92 is intended to compensate. The net effective KV-cache size is not guaranteed identical to the old default, and on memory-constrained GPUs raising util to 0.92 while newly reserving CUDA-graph memory can push allocations closer to OOM. (3) Ensure the release notes / migration docs clearly document both coupled changes and confirm via profiling on a range of GPUs that the new (0.92 + estimate) combination does not regress into OOM relative to the old (0.9, no estimate) default.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Startup on a memory-constrained GPU (e.g. 24GB) with default settings before vs after this commit; verify no OOM and compare resulting num_gpu_blocks / effective KV cache size.

findings.claim: (1) The docstring for `gpu_memory_utilization` was updated to '0.92', but multiple docs/examples still reference the old 0.9 default (e.g. docs/design/metrics.md line 370 shows gpu_memory_utilization="0.9", docs/usage/usage_stats.md line 36, docs/benchmarking/sweeps.md). (2) Stale documented defaults are misleading to users reading generated metrics/usage docs and diverge from actual runtime behavior. (3) Update the affected docs (or auto-generated examples) to reflect the new 0.92 default, or note they are illustrative.

(1) With `VLLM_MEMORY_PROFILER_ESTIMATE_CUDAGRAPHS` now defaulting to True, the `else` branch (estimation disabled) is only reached when a user explicitly sets the env var to 0; that branch now logs a `warning` on every startup for such users. (2) This changes the log level from info to warning for a legitimate, explicit opt-out configuration, which may produce noisy/false-alarm warnings for users who intentionally disabled it. (3) Consider whether warning-level is appropriate for an explicit user opt-out, or downgrade to info to avoid alarming users who deliberately disabled the feature. Minor/behavioral only.

Incomplete revert: the diff reverts `set_start_method("forkserver", force=False)` back to `multiprocessing.set_start_method("forkserver")` and keeps the entire `if os.getenv("VLLM_WORKER_MULTIPROC_METHOD") == "forkserver":` forkserver-setup block (import `multiprocessing.forkserver`, `set_forkserver_preload`, `ensure_running`). However, the same PR also removes `"forkserver"` from the allowed values of `VLLM_WORKER_MULTIPROC_METHOD` in envs.py (`env_with_choices(..., ["spawn", "fork"])`). WHY IT MATTERS: (1) The forkserver block is now dead code — `env_with_choices` will reject `VLLM_WORKER_MULTIPROC_METHOD=forkserver` at env-parse time, so the `os.getenv(...) == "forkserver"` branch can no longer be reached through the supported config path; if a user sets the raw env var it now fails validation instead of taking this path. (2) This forkserver block and the `import multiprocessing.forkserver` were introduced by the very PR being reverted (#40331), so a full revert should also remove them to restore the pre-PR state. FIX: Verify whether the forkserver setup block in `build_async_engine_client` was pre-existing or introduced by #40331; if introduced by the reverted PR, remove the block and the `import multiprocessing.forkserver as forkserver` line to fully revert; otherwise re-add `forkserver` to the env choices so the branch remains reachable.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Start `vllm serve` with `VLLM_WORKER_MULTIPROC_METHOD=forkserver` and confirm whether env validation rejects it (dead-code path) vs. reaching the forkserver setup branch.

findings.claim: (1) The diff DELETES the definitions of `__HIP__GFX11__` (was `#define __HIP__GFX11__` guarded on `__gfx1100__/__gfx1101__/__gfx1150__/__gfx1151__`) and `__HIP__GFX12__` (guarded on `__gfx1200__/__gfx1201__`) from attention.cu, and rewrites their use sites to reference `__GFX11__` and `__GFX12__` instead (`#elif defined(__GFX11__)` at old line 1632, `#elif defined(__GFX12__)` at old line 2391). But no `#define __GFX11__` / `#define __GFX12__` is added anywhere in the diff. Clang/HIP does NOT predefine `__GFX11__`/`__GFX12__` — it only predefines lowercase per-arch macros like `__gfx1100__`, `__gfx1200__`. A grep of the parent tree confirms these bare macros exist nowhere, and CMakeLists.txt adds no `-D__GFX11__`/`-D__GFX12__` compile definition (it only extends HIP_SUPPORTED_ARCHS). (2) Impact: for ALL RDNA GPUs (gfx11xx and gfx12xx, including the newly-added gfx1102/gfx1103) the entire optimized attention kernel body under `#elif defined(__GFX11__)`/`#elif defined(__GFX12__)` is now dead, falling through to the generic branch or leaving the kernel undefined — silently disabling paged-attention support on the exact architectures this PR claims to enable. This is a functional/build regression, not a no-op rename. (3) Fix: add the macro definitions (e.g. `#if defined(__gfx1100__)||... #define __GFX11__` and the gfx12 equivalent) either in a shared header included by both files or restore them in each file, OR define them via CMake `add_compile_definitions` — and confirm the referenced-but-undefined symbols resolve.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compile csrc/rocm for gfx1100/gfx1102/gfx1200 and run the ROCm paged_attention unit test; confirm the optimized (non-UNREACHABLE) kernel path is actually taken on RDNA (assert kernel produces correct output rather than hitting assert(false)).

findings.claim: (1) Same undefined-macro problem here. The diff replaces the explicit arch-list definition of `__HIP__GFX1X__` with `#if defined(__GFX11__) || defined(__GFX12__)  #define __HIP__GFX1X__`, deletes the `#define __HIP__GFX12__` block, and rewrites ~14 use sites from `__HIP__GFX12__` to `__GFX12__` (wvSplitKQ_hf_sml_ / wvSplitKQ_hf_ kernel guards and their inner `#ifdef` branches). Since `__GFX11__`/`__GFX12__` are not defined by the compiler nor introduced in this diff, `__HIP__GFX1X__` is now never defined on any GPU, and every `#ifdef __GFX12__` inner branch is dead. (2) Impact: the wvSplitK skinny-GEMM kernels for RDNA (enabled by prior PR #34709 for RDNA4/gfx1x decode) and the gfx12 fp8 dot4 accumulation path all collapse to the `UNREACHABLE_CODE`/generic fallback, so skinny GEMM is silently broken/dispatched to a stub on gfx11 and gfx12 devices — a correctness and performance regression on the targeted hardware. (3) Fix: define `__GFX11__`/`__GFX12__` from the lowercase per-arch predefined macros (in a shared header or via CMake) before they are referenced; add a compile-time assert/static check that at least one arch macro path is live when building for a supported RDNA arch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Build skinny_gemms.cu for gfx1100/gfx1102/gfx1200 and run the wvSplitK/wvSplitKQ correctness tests on an RDNA GPU; verify results match reference GEMM instead of hitting UNREACHABLE_CODE.

findings.claim: (1) The two files previously each defined their OWN copies of the gfx11/gfx12 arch macros locally. This diff removes them from attention.cu entirely and consolidates in skinny_gemms.cu onto `__GFX11__`/`__GFX12__`, implying the intended definitions live in a shared header — but no shared header change is present in the changed-files list (only CMakeLists.txt, attention.cu, skinny_gemms.cu). Either a needed header edit is missing from this PR, or the rename was applied inconsistently. (2) Impact: if a header change was intended but omitted, the PR will not build/function on RDNA; if the header exists, this diff is incomplete/misleading. Reviewers cannot verify the symbols resolve. (3) Fix: include the file that defines `__GFX11__`/`__GFX12__` in the PR (and in this review context), or add the definitions locally. Verify `#include` order guarantees the macros are visible before first use in both translation units.

(1) `functools.cache` on `_hash_source_cached` retains a strong reference to every argument (str/type/FunctionType) for the lifetime of the process, and the cached string results grow unbounded. (2) Risk: the `CallableInductorPass` path passes arbitrary callables (including potentially closures/lambdas or dynamically-created functions) as cache keys; these — and any objects they close over — are never released, a mild memory leak. This codebase already introduced `weak_cache`/`weak_lru_cache` in `vllm/ir/util.py` specifically to avoid exactly this retention problem. (3) Fix: consider using a weak-referencing cache for the type/function keys, or document that keys are expected to be module-level long-lived objects so the retention is intentional and bounded. In practice the passes/patterns/callables here are module-level singletons, so impact is small — hence suggestion severity.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure process RSS growth after constructing many CallableInductorPass instances wrapping distinct locally-defined callables, comparing functools.cache vs a weakref-based cache.

findings.claim: (1) The default of `encoder_cudagraph_max_frames_per_batch` changed from `int = 0` to `int | None = None`, and its semantics changed (0 now means image-only rather than auto-infer). (2) External training/serving scripts or configs that pass `0` intending 'auto-infer' will now silently fall back to image-only mode (max_frames_per_batch=0), disabling video CUDA graph capture. This is a silent behavior change for anyone relying on the previous `0 = auto-infer` contract. (3) Consider treating `0` explicitly and documenting the migration, or keeping `0` as auto-infer while using a distinct sentinel for image-only; at minimum add a deprecation/migration note.

(1) `get_max_frames_per_video()` is only defined on the model interface but the new auto-infer path unconditionally calls `self.model.get_max_frames_per_video()` for any model with video limit > 0. Only `Qwen3VLForConditionalGeneration` implements it in this PR; other models implementing `SupportsEncoderCudaGraph` (protocol default is `...`) will raise AttributeError/NotImplementedError at manager init when video limit > 0. (2) This breaks encoder CUDA graph initialization for non-Qwen3-VL encoder-cudagraph models that support video. (3) Provide a default implementation in the protocol/base or gate the call on hasattr/capability, and add a shape/behavior test for models lacking the method.

(1) `get_processing_info` calls `_create_processing_info(model_config, tokenizer=None)`, which resolves the model class via `get_model_architecture` and constructs a fresh processing context/info each call. `Qwen3VLForConditionalGeneration.get_max_frames_per_video()` invokes this at manager init. (2) While this is init-time (not per-forward), `get_num_frames_with_most_features` for Qwen3-VL can iterate/compute over budget ranges; ensure it is called once and cached rather than repeatedly. (3) Confirm the call happens exactly once during setup; if it may be called repeatedly, memoize the result. Low risk but worth verifying it is not on any hot path.

(1) `get_max_frames_per_video` passes `mm_counts={"video": self.multimodal_config.get_limit_per_prompt("video")}` to `get_num_frames_with_most_features`. When the video limit is a large default (999), `get_num_frames_with_most_features(seq_len=max_model_len, mm_counts={video:999})` may return a small per-video frame count due to seq_len budget being divided across many videos, potentially making `max_frames_per_batch = max_batch_size * max_frames_per_video` smaller than expected or degenerate. (2) Could under-size the cu_seqlens buffer or produce a misleading budget. (3) Verify the interaction between a 999 video limit default and the seq_len-based frame computation yields a sensible max_frames_per_video; add an assertion/log if the computed value is unexpectedly small.

(1) The auto-infer branch computes `self.max_frames_per_batch = self.max_batch_size * max_frames_per_video`, where `max_batch_size` is the max number of vision items per batch. Naming-wise this multiplies items-per-batch by frames-per-video, which conflates 'vision items' (images or videos) with 'videos'. (2) If a batch can contain a mix where not all items are videos, the product overestimates frames; if it under-counts it could truncate cu_seqlens. The comment/name does not make the assumption (all items = videos) explicit. (3) Add a comment clarifying the worst-case assumption (all `max_batch_size` items are videos each at `max_frames_per_video`) so the buffer sizing rationale is clear.

(1) The parent-side prefetch worker in `_startup_prefetch_weights` reads the entire model weight shards (potentially tens/hundreds of GB) into the OS page cache from the PARENT APIServer process a few seconds before the child EngineCore reads the same files. (2) On memory-constrained hosts (weights close to or exceeding available RAM), racing the parent's page-cache fill against the child's actual read can cause page-cache thrashing/eviction: pages the parent read early may be evicted by the time the child faults them in, wasting bandwidth and possibly slowing startup rather than speeding it. The in-child prefetch (`_maybe_prefetch`/`prefetch_all_checkpoints`) already gates on `fits_in_ram` and NFS detection precisely to avoid this, but the new parent-side path has no such guard. (3) Add a RAM-fits / free-page-cache check (mirroring the in-child `fits_in_ram` logic) and/or an env flag to gate the parent-side prefetch, and skip it when weights don't comfortably fit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Cold-cache `vllm serve` startup latency (time-to-ready) for a large model (e.g. 70B, ~140GB weights) on a host where weights exceed free RAM, with and without parent-side prefetch, measuring both wall-clock startup and total bytes read from disk.

findings.claim: (1) The comment states "Match vLLM's in-child prefetch block size + thread count" but the values are hard-coded here (block_size=16MB, max_workers=8) and duplicated from `weight_utils._prefetch_all_checkpoints` (num_prefetch_threads=8) rather than imported/shared. (2) If the canonical in-child prefetch tuning changes later, this copy silently drifts, and the "match" invariant the comment relies on for correctness (same page-cache population strategy) breaks. Also the in-child path shards files across `rank::world_size` and skips NFS filesystems; this parent path reads ALL shards unconditionally, so it is not actually "matching" behavior. (3) Import the shared constants/helper from `weight_utils`, or at minimum reference the source of truth, and reconcile the comment with the actual (non-sharded, no-NFS-guard) behavior.

(1) In `_bg_prewarm_forkserver` in cli/main.py, `multiprocessing.set_start_method("forkserver", force=False)` runs in a daemon background thread concurrently with the main thread's `cli_env_setup()` (which the comment notes "sets spawn"). `set_start_method` is not thread-safe and can only be set once; a race between the two threads calling it can raise RuntimeError (suppressed here) or leave the start method in a non-deterministic state depending on ordering. (2) This makes the forkserver optimization non-deterministic — sometimes the process ends up with spawn instead of forkserver, silently losing the intended speedup, or vice versa overriding an explicit user setting. (3) Serialize the start-method decision (e.g. set it on the main thread before spawning the prewarm thread, or use a lock), and confirm ordering vs `cli_env_setup()`.

(1) The sidecar glob uses both `*.json` and `*tokenizer*` (and `tokenizer.model`), and shard globs may overlap with sidecar globs is unlikely, but `*tokenizer*` will re-match `tokenizer.json`/`tokenizer_config.json` already caught by `*.json`, so some files are read twice within the same worker pool. (2) Redundant duplicate reads waste a small amount of I/O and thread-pool slots; harmless correctness-wise but contradicts the goal of a lean prefetch. (3) De-duplicate `shard_paths` (e.g. `sorted(set(...))`) before submitting to the ThreadPoolExecutor.

(1) The `_bg_preload_torch` thread imports torch (and transformers) on a background thread while the main thread continues importing. Python's per-module import lock serializes concurrent imports of the same module: if the main thread reaches `import torch` (transitively via vllm.logger -> vllm/__init__ -> env_override) while the background thread is mid-import, the main thread blocks on the import lock until the background thread finishes, and the parallelism benefit is limited to the GIL-released dlopen I/O windows. (2) The realized speedup depends heavily on how quickly the main thread transitively imports torch; if that happens almost immediately (init_logger is imported right after), the overlap window is small and the added thread/complexity yields little. (3) Verify empirically that the main thread's torch import is meaningfully deferred; if not, the background torch preload provides negligible benefit and could be dropped.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure cold-import `vllm serve --help` / startup-to-argparse time with and without the background torch/transformers preload thread to quantify actual overlap benefit.

findings.claim: (1) `_find_cc_in_function` previously handled two AST patterns for compute-capability detection: `is_device_capability_family(N)` -> "M.x" and `is_device_capability(N)` -> "M.m". This commit narrows the guard to only match `is_device_capability_family` and drops the `is_device_capability` branch entirely. (2) This helper is called on four functions (`supports_trtllm_attention`, `use_trtllm_ragged_deepseek_prefill`, `use_flashinfer_prefill`, `use_cudnn_prefill`). If any of those functions gate on a plain `is_device_capability(N)` call, the generated docs table will now silently report the compute capability as `None`/`Any` (a stale/wrong doc entry) rather than the precise `M.m` value. (3) Confirm none of the four scanned functions use `is_device_capability(N)` for their CC gate; if any do, retain the `is_device_capability` branch. This is a doc-generation tool so runtime training behavior is unaffected, but the pre-commit hook may emit an incorrect attention backend docs table.

(1) The commit message states "Revert #38730 and #38791", where #38730 introduced the `is_device_capability(100)` -> `is_device_capability_family(100)` change to fix a GB300 (SM103) hang, and #38791 fixed the test mocks. However, the production `supports_trtllm_attention` in this file is NOT reverted to `is_device_capability` (it still uses `is_device_capability_family(100)`); only a clarifying comment is added. Meanwhile the tests are changed to mock `is_device_capability_family` (aligning to the family behavior, the opposite of a revert of #38791). (2) The stated intent ("revert") does not match the actual diff (behavior is retained, not reverted). If the true intent was to restore `is_device_capability(100)` and re-open the GB300/SM103 hang, the production change is missing; if the intent was to KEEP the family restriction, the commit message is misleading. Either way there is a mismatch to resolve. (3) Clarify the commit intent: if reverting, change `is_device_capability_family(100)` back to `is_device_capability(100)` and revert the test mocks accordingly; if keeping the fix, correct the misleading commit message.

(1) The docstring for `_find_cc_in_function` now says it looks for `current_platform.is_device_capability_family(N)`, but the AST guard only checks `n.func.attr == 'is_device_capability_family'` and does not verify the receiver is `current_platform`. (2) Minor: any object with an attribute call named `is_device_capability_family` would match, so the docstring is slightly more specific than the implementation. Low impact. (3) Either loosen the docstring to say it matches any `.is_device_capability_family(N)` call, or add a check that the attribute value is `current_platform`.

(1) The CPU communicator now unconditionally constructs AgRsAll2AllManager for all2all, but the previous NaiveAll2AllManager was the only backend the CPU path used, and it relied on get_ep_group().broadcast / all_reduce. AgRsAll2AllManager instead calls dist_group.all_gatherv(...) / reduce_scatterv(...) on get_dp_group()/get_ep_group(). CpuCommunicator does NOT implement all_gatherv or reduce_scatterv (only all_gather/gather/all_reduce are defined in this file; the vectorized variants live on the base class / cuda path and depend on pynccl). (2) This risks a runtime AttributeError / NotImplementedError or incorrect result the first time MoE dispatch/combine runs on CPU, i.e. it silently breaks CPU expert-parallel execution that previously worked via the naive manager. (3) Verify CpuCommunicator (or its base) actually provides functioning all_gatherv/reduce_scatterv for CPU groups before replacing the naive manager; if not, keep a CPU-capable fallback or implement these methods for the CPU backend.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run an MoE model with EP on the CPU backend and exercise a forward pass through FusedMoE dispatch/combine to confirm AgRsAll2AllManager.all_gatherv/reduce_scatterv resolve on CpuCommunicator.

findings.claim: (1) The branch now maps both 'naive' and 'allgather_reducescatter' to AgRsAll2AllManager, but ParallelConfig._validate_parallel_config already rewrites all2all_backend=='naive' to 'allgather_reducescatter' (with a deprecation warning) before any communicator is constructed. So the 'naive' arm here (and in xpu/cpu communicators) is effectively dead / unreachable in the normal config path. (2) Keeping it is harmless but adds a confusing implicit alias that duplicates the config-level migration and can mask future validator changes. (3) Consider dropping the 'naive' string from the communicator branch conditions and relying solely on the config validator, or add a comment noting it is a defensive alias.

(1) 'naive' remains a valid value in the All2AllBackend Literal (and is still accepted as a CLI/config value) even though NaiveAll2AllManager is removed; it is silently redirected to 'allgather_reducescatter' by the validator. This is a reasonable deprecation path, but the All2AllBackend Literal type and the all2all_backend docstring are now inconsistent (docstring omits 'naive' while the Literal still lists it). (2) Minor confusion for users/tooling reading the type or help text. (3) Ensure the deprecation warning wording and the Literal/docstring stay consistent; consider marking 'naive' as deprecated-alias in the docstring too.

(1) `supports_trtllm_attention()` in vllm/utils/flashinfer.py is changed from `current_platform.is_device_capability(100)` (exactly SM100 / CC 10.0) to `current_platform.is_device_capability_family(100)` (any 10.x, including SM103/GB300), and the guarding comment referencing flashinfer issue #2939 ("SM103 (GB300) hangs with FlashInfer >= 0.6.7") is deleted. (2) This directly reverts commit 6183cae1b ("Restrict TRTLLM attention to SM100, fixing GB300 (SM103) hang", merged 2026-04-01, just ~3 weeks earlier). The GB300 hang was a hard hang, not a perf regression, so re-enabling it on all 10.x parts risks re-introducing a training/inference hang on GB300 hardware. (3) The flashinfer bump to 0.6.8 is justified for the SM121 MoE fix (#2926), but the diff/commit message provides no evidence that the *separate* SM103 attention hang (#2939) is fixed in 0.6.8. Confirm #2939 is resolved in 0.6.8.post1 before broadening the capability check; if not, keep `is_device_capability(100)` for the attention path or add an explicit SM103 carve-out.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run FlashInfer TRTLLM decode/prefill attention on an SM103 (GB300) device with flashinfer 0.6.8.post1 and verify it does not hang (issue flashinfer#2939).

findings.claim: (1) The unit test `test_supports_sm100_with_artifactory` / `test_supports_non_sm100_platform` / `test_supports_sm100_without_artifactory` still patch `vllm.utils.flashinfer.current_platform.is_device_capability`, but the production code now calls `is_device_capability_family`. Only `test_supports_batch_invariant_disables` and the one edited test were touched; the other three tests now patch a method the code no longer calls. (2) These tests will exercise the *real* `is_device_capability_family` (returning False off-target hardware), so `test_supports_sm100_with_artifactory` will assert `True` but actually get `False` on CI runners that are not 10.x — the test becomes environment-dependent / silently misleading rather than validating the intended branch. (3) Update all three remaining tests to patch `is_device_capability_family` (matching the edit already made to `test_supports_batch_invariant_disables`'s sibling) so they mock the method the code actually invokes.

(1) In flashinfer_cutlass_moe.py, `_supports_current_device` switches SM120 handling from `is_device_capability(120)` to `is_device_capability_family(120)`, re-enabling SM121 (DGX Spark). This reverts the very recent guard commit 65b980896 (2026-04-14, ~6 days prior, "Disable FlashInfer CUTLASS MoE on SM121"). (2) This is well-justified: the deleted comment explicitly said to lift the restriction "once flashinfer >= 0.6.8 is the minimum" (upstream fix #2926), and this PR makes 0.6.8.post1 the minimum. (3) No fix needed — just noting the re-enable is intentional and matches the documented condition; optionally verify Nemotron-H bf16 unquantized CUTLASS MoE (Relu2) actually runs on SM121 with 0.6.8.post1 before merge.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run Nemotron-H bf16 unquantized CUTLASS MoE on an SM121 (DGX Spark) device with flashinfer 0.6.8.post1 to confirm the Relu2 'Invalid activation type' error is gone.

findings.claim: In video_to_pixel_values, the no-resize path changed semantics. Previously when no resize was needed the function returned `torch.from_numpy(video).permute(0,3,1,2) / 255.0` — i.e. the video was rescaled to [0,1] but NOT normalized with norm_mean/norm_std (normalization happened later via input_conditioner on the torch.cat result). Now the no-resize path calls `_bicubic_resize_and_normalize(tensor, size=None, norm_mean, norm_std, dtype)`, which fuses `(x/255 - mean)/std` normalization directly. Meanwhile the caller (_process_video_pixels / video_inputs construction) was changed to DROP the input_conditioner call entirely (comment: 'Normalization already fused into resize above'). These two changes are consistent for the resize path, but you must verify BOTH branches (resize AND no-resize) of _bicubic_resize_and_normalize apply normalization identically — they do here since size=None only skips interpolation. This is correct, but the risk is subtle: any code path still expecting un-normalized [0,1] output (e.g. model-side normalization) will now get double or wrong normalization. Verify the model consumes pixel_values_flat_video expecting pre-normalized input.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Numerically compare pixel_values_flat/pixel_values_flat_video against the pre-change output for both a resized and a non-resized image/video with mode=RGB and non-RGB, asserting max abs diff < 1e-4.

findings.claim: The batch tokenization change (replacing per-item `_seq2tokens(tokenizer, sep)` with `tokenizer(frame_separators, add_special_tokens=False, return_attention_mask=False)['input_ids']`) is only equivalent if every element of `frame_separators` is a `str`. `_seq2tokens` also handles the case where `seq` is already a `list[int]` (it returns it unchanged). If any frame separator can be a pre-tokenized list of ids, passing it into the HF `tokenizer(...)` batch call will raise or mis-tokenize. Verify frame_separators is always list[str] at this call site; if it can contain token-id lists, the batch call breaks that path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test tokenizing a batch of frame separators where one entry is a list[int] vs str, comparing new batch output to old per-item _seq2tokens output.

findings.claim: The batch tokenizer call omits `use_cache`. The old `_seq2tokens(tokenizer, sep)` used `use_cache=True` (LRU-cached encode). For repeated identical frame separators across many requests this cache provided real speedup. The new batch call bypasses the cache entirely. While batch encoding parallelizes within one call, if the same separators recur across separate process() invocations the previously-cached fast path is lost. Confirm this is a net win for the typical multi-request workload (many videos with identical separators), not just single large batches.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark process() throughput over N sequential requests with identical frame_separators comparing cached per-item tokenization vs uncached batch tokenization.

findings.claim: `_bicubic_resize_and_normalize` is decorated with `@torch.compile(dynamic=True)` at module import time. This is executed on CPU tensors during multimodal preprocessing (from_numpy of uint8 image arrays). torch.compile on CPU-side preprocessing with dynamic shapes triggers a costly graph compilation on first call and recompilations whenever shape buckets change; for a preprocessing function called with highly variable image/video sizes this can cause repeated recompilation overhead that dwarfs the fused-op savings, and the compile cache is per-process. Verify the shape variability is bounded (dynamic=True helps but symbolic guards can still recompile) and that the compiled path actually outperforms eager for these small CPU tensors.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end image+video preprocessing wall time (including first-call compile) across a distribution of real image sizes, eager vs torch.compile(dynamic=True), on CPU.

findings.claim: `_bicubic_resize_and_normalize` casts `tensor.to(dtype=torch.float32)` unconditionally at the start, then at the end casts to the target `dtype` and calls `.contiguous()`. If `dtype == torch.float32` the final `.to(dtype=dtype)` is a no-op copy path but `.contiguous()` may still copy. Interpolation already returns a contiguous fp32 tensor, so for the fp32 default the trailing `.contiguous()` can add an unnecessary allocation. Consider only forcing contiguous/cast when needed.

`self.dtype` is read via `getattr(config, 'dtype', torch.float32)`. HuggingFace configs commonly store `torch_dtype` (or a string like 'bfloat16') rather than a `torch.dtype` object under `dtype`. If a config carries a string dtype under `dtype`, it will be passed straight into `tensor.to(dtype=...)` and raise. Verify the config field is guaranteed to be a `torch.dtype`, or coerce string→torch.dtype.

The single-item fast path `pixel_values_flat = torch.cat(pixel_values_lst) if len(pixel_values_lst) > 1 else pixel_values_lst[0]` (and the analogous video path) assumes pixel_values_lst is non-empty. If images is empty this indexes [0] on an empty list and raises IndexError, whereas the old `torch.cat([])`-based path also raised but the guard's intent (avoid copy for a single element) should confirm the empty case is unreachable at this call site.

(1) The windowed scan only guarantees agreement with `is_reasoning_end` when the end-of-reasoning marker (`<|channel|>final ... <|message|>`) lies within the last `window = pattern_len + len(delta_ids)` tokens. If a request has already emitted the `final<|message|>` marker followed by more than `window` content tokens (e.g. long final answer) and `delta_ids` is small (typical single-token decode), the marker falls outside the slice `input_ids[n-window:]` and `is_reasoning_end_streaming` returns False while `is_reasoning_end` returns True. (2) This is a divergence between the streaming and non-streaming detectors; the added tests only cover short content after the marker so they do not catch it. (3) It is acceptable *only* because the real caller (`StructuredOutputManager.should_advance`) invokes this every decode step and caches `reasoning_ended` on the first True, and passes the newly-computed tokens as `delta_ids` — so the transition step is always covered. If any other caller invokes this on an already-past-transition sequence with a small delta it will get a wrong answer. Recommend documenting this contract explicitly in the method docstring (only correct at/around the transition step) or removing the `n <= window` short-circuit reliance and guaranteeing the delta spans from the last-checked position.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test: tokenize `REASONING_SECTION_START + reasoning + ASSISTANT_CONTENT_START + ('word '*200)`, call is_reasoning_end_streaming(ids, ids[-1:]) and compare to is_reasoning_end(ids).

findings.claim: (1) `is_reasoning_end` performs slice equality comparisons like `input_ids[i:i+k] == end_token_ids_prefix`, where the RHS is a `list` returned by `tokenizer.encode`. When `is_reasoning_end_streaming` slices `input_ids[n - window:]`, the result type is whatever `input_ids` is; if a caller passes a non-`list` Sequence (e.g. a tuple or a `ConstantList`/`np.ndarray` view), the slice will not compare equal to a `list`, so pattern matching silently fails and the method always returns False. (2) The actual caller passes `request.all_token_ids` which is a `ConstantList` (`__getitem__` slice behavior must be verified) — if its slice returns a ConstantList rather than a list, the equality check breaks. (3) Normalize the slice to a list before delegating, e.g. `return self.is_reasoning_end(list(input_ids[n - window:]))`, to make the comparison type-independent.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Call is_reasoning_end_streaming(tuple(output_ids), delta) on a positive case and assert it returns True.

findings.claim: (1) `delta_ids = tuple(delta_ids)` materializes the entire delta iterable, then only `len(delta_ids)` is used from it (the delta content itself is never scanned; only the underlying `input_ids` window is scanned). (2) For the intended speculative-decoding path delta is small, but the caller passes `itertools.islice(all_token_ids, start, None)` which can be a large slice of the whole sequence, so materializing it into a tuple is O(remaining tokens) work purely to compute a length. This partially undermines the stated performance goal of avoiding a full-sequence scan. (3) Compute the length without materializing when possible, or accept `delta_ids` as a Sequence and use `len()` directly; if it must stay an Iterable, document why the full materialization is needed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark is_reasoning_end_streaming with a 100k-token input_ids and delta = islice(input_ids, 0, None) vs delta = last-token, comparing wall time.

findings.claim: (1) The comment says 'The pattern window covers the end-of-reasoning marker itself. We add len(delta_ids) so that ... the entire accepted chunk is always inside the scan region.' but the code adds `len(delta_ids)` to a window that is anchored at the end of `input_ids`, not at the delta boundary. (2) This is slightly imprecise: the guarantee it provides is that all delta tokens are within the window only when delta is a suffix of input_ids (which the caller ensures); the comment does not state this precondition. (3) Clarify in the comment that `delta_ids` is assumed to be the trailing tokens of `input_ids`.

(1) NixlEplbCommunicator.execute() Phase 2 packs receive data from ALL peers sequentially into a single recv buffer at running offsets (recv_offset += actual_total_bytes), but the recv buffer is only sized to `self._peer_partition_bytes` (one peer's worth of data): `self._recv_buffer = torch.empty(self._peer_partition_bytes, ...)`. (2) When more than one peer sends data to this rank in a single rebalance step, the cumulative recv_offset can exceed the single-partition capacity, causing out-of-bounds descriptor offsets / buffer overrun into unregistered memory and silent data corruption or a NIXL transfer failure. In a real EPLB rebalance, a rank commonly receives experts from multiple source ranks simultaneously. (3) Size the recv buffer for the worst-case aggregate receive (e.g. (world_size-1) * peer_partition_bytes) or bound recv_offset and split transfers into multiple passes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-rank EPLB rearrange where one rank must receive experts from >=2 source ranks in one step; assert no overflow and correct weights.

findings.claim: (1) _wait_for_all_transfers busy-polls check_xfer_state in a Python loop with time.sleep(0.0005) between passes, holding the calling thread and issuing repeated pybind calls per handle. (2) During EPLB rebalance this adds host-side polling latency and CPU spin overhead proportional to transfer duration; for large expert weights the 0.5ms sleep granularity plus per-handle state queries can add measurable overhead and jitter to the rebalance critical path. (3) Consider a blocking/wait API from NIXL if available, or back off the sleep interval adaptively; document why polling is required.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time EPLB rebalance step with nixl backend for large MoE weights vs torch_nccl backend; compare wall-clock of transfer completion.

findings.claim: (1) The nixl send buffer is allocated as world_size * peer_partition_bytes of device memory (self._send_buffer = torch.empty(send_total_bytes, ...)), where peer_partition_bytes is sized to the full per-layer expert-weight bytes. The code's own TODO(ilmarkov) acknowledges this can OOM on large models. (2) This is a large persistent VRAM reservation held for the lifetime of the communicator, reducing memory available for KV cache / activations and potentially aborting startup on large models with high EP world size. (3) Implement the noted shrink to const * partition_bytes with multi-step communication, or gate the nixl backend behind a documented memory-budget check.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory with nixl EPLB backend at increasing EP world_size and model size; compare against torch_nccl staged-buffer approach.

findings.claim: (1) execute() packs send data starting at byte_offset = dst * self._peer_partition_bytes and packs each dtype's tensors sequentially, but the overflow check compares per-dtype `actual_bytes` against `self._dtype_max_bytes[dtype]` while the packing offset accumulates across dtypes within the same partition. If the per-dtype capacities were computed independently (max over that dtype) they may not reflect the actual within-partition layout used by _pack_send_buffer, so a dtype could pack correctly per its own budget yet the cumulative byte_offset within the partition still be consistent only if dtypes are laid out contiguously in the fixed order self._dtypes. Any change to dtype ordering or per-peer tensor distribution differing from the profiling layer breaks the fixed remote READ offset assumption (remote reads rank*partition_bytes for total_bytes). (2) Mismatched packing vs. remote descriptor offsets silently transfers wrong bytes. (3) Add an assertion that the packed byte_offset matches the expected per-peer layout, and document the invariant that all MoE layers share identical tensor count/shape/dtype ordering.

(1) execute() issues torch.distributed.monitored_barrier with a hardcoded 5-minute timeout before READs. monitored_barrier is only supported by the gloo backend; the communicator uses cpu_group which is typically gloo so this is likely fine, but the timeout is a fixed magic constant. (2) If cpu_group is ever backed by a non-gloo backend, monitored_barrier raises; and a fixed 5-min timeout may be too long/short for different deployments. (3) Assert/document that cpu_group is gloo-backed, and make the timeout configurable.

(1) create_eplb_communicator docstring and the NixlEplbCommunicator require that all MoE layers have identical tensor count, shapes, and dtypes because send/recv buffers are pre-sized to expert_weights of a single layer. This invariant is documented but not enforced at runtime. (2) A model with heterogeneous MoE layers (e.g. differing expert shapes across layers) would silently overflow or under-fill the fixed buffers, causing corruption. (3) Add a runtime assertion at communicator setup or first execute() validating that subsequent layers match the profiling layer's tensor count/shape/dtype.

(1) In execute(), for each dst/src the default `[[] for _ in range(self._world_size)]` list-of-lists is reconstructed on every dtype iteration when the dtype key is absent from _send_tensors/_recv_tensors. (2) This allocates world_size empty lists per (peer,dtype) pair on every rebalance for dtypes with no enqueued tensors — minor but unnecessary per-call allocation in the rebalance path. (3) Precompute an empty default once, or use .get(dtype, EMPTY)[peer] with a shared empty sentinel indexed safely.

(1) The fix changes inference-mode capture to `model[0].config.cuda_graph_scope = args.cuda_graph_scope` (was `[]`, i.e. full-layer capture). Previously inference always got an empty scope; now it uses the user's configured scope. If `args.cuda_graph_scope` contains training-oriented scopes (e.g. `attn`, `mlp`, `moe`, `moe_router`) but not `full_iteration_inference`, inference-mode capture behavior changes vs. the prior always-full-layer behavior. (2) Risk: for users who set a non-empty `cuda_graph_scope` for training, entering RL inference mode now inherits those partial scopes instead of the previously-guaranteed full-layer inference capture, potentially changing/regressing inference-graph capture semantics silently. (3) Confirm whether `args.cuda_graph_scope` is expected to already contain `full_iteration_inference` for the inference path, and add an assertion or explicit handling so a misconfigured training scope does not silently degrade inference capture.
findings.suggested_benchmark: RL rollout throughput with --cuda-graph-scope set to a partial training scope (e.g. attn) vs. default, measuring inference decode tokens/sec and verifying graph capture occurs.

findings.claim: (1) In the new `else` branch (dense/non-MoE restore), the training scope is set to `[s for s in args.cuda_graph_scope if s != CudaGraphScope.full_iteration_inference]`. This filters only `full_iteration_inference` but leaves `full_iteration` (and any other inference-only members) in place. (2) Why it matters: the comment says this restores the scope for *training*; if `args.cuda_graph_scope` legitimately contains inference-only markers other than `full_iteration_inference`, they would leak into the training capture scope. (3) Verify that `full_iteration_inference` is the only inference-only scope that must be stripped for training; if not, filter the full set of inference-only scopes.

(1) Behavior change: prior code unconditionally forced full-layer inference capture (`cuda_graph_scope = []`) regardless of user config; the new code makes inference capture depend on `args.cuda_graph_scope`. (2) This is a default/behavior change for existing RL training scripts that relied on the previous hardcoded empty-scope behavior; users who never set `cuda_graph_scope` (default) are unaffected, but users with a custom scope get different inference capture. (3) Document the new dependency on `args.cuda_graph_scope` in the changelog/argument help so the behavior change is discoverable.

(1) The `activation_recompute_in_mlp`-support detection in `_make_fused_ops` repeats the same `inspect.signature(...).parameters` guard-and-two-branch pattern four times (ScaledSwiGLU, two ScaledClampedQGeGLU branches, ScaledSReLU), producing a large block of near-duplicate code. (2) This inflates the method well past ~50 lines and mixes op-selection logic with TE-version capability probing, hurting readability and making future op additions error-prone. (3) Extract a small helper, e.g. `def _maybe_add_recompute_kwarg(op_cls, kwargs): if 'activation_recompute_in_mlp' in inspect.signature(op_cls).parameters: kwargs['activation_recompute_in_mlp'] = activation_recompute_in_mlp; return op_cls(**kwargs)`, and call it uniformly. This is behavior-preserving.

(1) In `_is_fused_impl_supported` the new code first computes `use_glu_fusion`/`use_srelu_fusion` and returns False if neither, then immediately re-tests `self.config.activation_func == F.silu / quick_gelu / squared_relu` again to decide the return value and import guards. The first `use_glu_fusion`/`use_srelu_fusion` gate is largely redundant with the subsequent per-activation branches. (2) The double-gating adds indirection and a subtle risk that the two condition sets drift out of sync (e.g. the `not self.config.gated_linear_unit` requirement for SReLU is expressed only in the first block, while the second block's `== squared_relu` branch does not re-assert it). (3) Consider collapsing to a single per-activation dispatch that checks the required config flags and import in one place per activation.

(1) `glu_interleave = self.config.moe_mlp_glu_interleave_size` is computed unconditionally at the top of the activation section but is only meaningful for the GLU branches; the new ScaledSReLU branch (non-GLU) ignores it (tests pass `moe_mlp_glu_interleave_size=None`). (2) Minor: a reader may assume the interleave value is relevant to all branches; a stale/misleading local. (3) Optionally move the assignment into the GLU branches or add a brief comment that SReLU does not use interleaving.

(1) The root cause of the empty-index dtype bug is at line 248 (in update_selected_channels): `param.selected_indices = torch.tensor(local_indices, device='cuda')`. When `local_indices` is an empty Python list, `torch.tensor([])` defaults to float32; using a float32 tensor as an index (grad_2d[param.selected_indices, :]) hangs/errors on CUDA. (2) The current fix special-cases only the empty branch at the consumption site (line ~281) with `if param.selected_indices.numel() == 0: param.selected_indices = param.selected_indices.long()`. This works but treats a symptom, and the same index is consumed elsewhere (deepspeed/ops/adam/zenflow_torch_adam.py lines 291/333/376/430/481/516) — relying on the fact that the fix must run first to correct the dtype in place. A more robust and uniform fix is to force the dtype at the source: `torch.tensor(local_indices, device='cuda', dtype=torch.long)` at line 248, which guarantees int64 regardless of empty/non-empty and removes the need for the conditional cast. (3) Change line 248 to construct the tensor with dtype=torch.long and consider dropping the special-case branch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run pytest -v runtime/zenflow/test_zf.py::TestZenFlowDistributed::test_zenflow_distributed[epoch-1-4-False-0-3] to confirm no hang; assert param.selected_indices.dtype == torch.int64 for both empty and non-empty topk results.

findings.claim: (1) The empty-index cast only guards the non-`num_row==1` branch here; the same float32-empty index is stored on `param.selected_indices` and later consumed by the selective optimizer (zenflow_torch_adam.py). Because the fix reassigns the attribute in place, downstream reads do get int64 — but only if this code path executes before every downstream consumer. (2) If any code path reads `param.selected_indices` before `_process_selected_fp32_groups_grad` runs (e.g. a first-step ordering where selection produced an empty set), the float32 empty tensor could still reach an indexing op and hang. (3) Fixing dtype at the construction site (line 248) removes this ordering dependency entirely; if kept here, add a brief comment explaining that empty torch.tensor([]) is float32 and why the long() cast is required.

(1) `hw_info.sm_count = min(cached_sm_counts[hw_info.device_id], INT_MAX);` is a no-op: `cached_sm_counts[...]` is an `int` (returned by `query_device_multiprocessor_count`), so `min(x, INT_MAX) == x` always. (2) It adds dead complexity and can mislead a future reader into thinking there is a meaningful clamp. (3) Replace with `hw_info.sm_count = cached_sm_counts[hw_info.device_id];`. Note: this is copied verbatim from the existing nvfp4 kernel, so it may be intentionally mirrored — low priority.

(1) `static std::unordered_map<int, int> cached_sm_counts;` is a function-local static mutated without any lock; if `cutlass_mxfp4_group_mm` is ever invoked concurrently from multiple host threads (e.g. multiple CUDA streams / model-parallel worker threads on distinct devices), the concurrent `find`/`operator[]` insert is a data race on the map that can corrupt it or crash. (2) Silent memory corruption / rare crashes are hard to diagnose. (3) Guard the cache with a `std::mutex` (or use a thread-safe once-per-device init). Same pattern exists in the sibling nvfp4 kernel, so this is a pre-existing convention rather than a new regression — flagging for awareness.

(1) The validation `STD_TORCH_CHECK(a_blockscale.dim() == 2, "expected a_blockscale to be of shape [num_experts, rounded_m, k // group_size], ...")` checks for a 2D tensor but the error message describes a 3D shape `[num_experts, rounded_m, k // group_size]`. (2) A misleading error message wastes debugging time when the check trips. (3) Correct the message to describe the expected 2D layout (e.g. `[sum_rounded_m, k // group_size]`) or fix the dim check to match the intended rank.

(1) In `__mxfp4_get_group_gemm_starts` the guard `if (expert_id >= gridDim.x * blockDim.x) return;` is effectively dead: the kernel is launched as `<<<1, num_experts>>>`, so `threadIdx.x < blockDim.x == num_experts` always holds and the bound is `gridDim.x*blockDim.x == num_experts` — the check never fires and does not actually protect against `expert_id >= num_experts` in any meaningful multi-block scenario. (2) Dead complexity; a reader may assume it bounds the write to per-expert arrays when it does not. (3) Either drop it or make it a correct bound `if (expert_id >= num_experts) return;` passing `num_experts` explicitly. Copied from nvfp4 sibling — low priority.

(1) `run_mxfp4_blockwise_scaled_group_mm_sm100` allocates ~11 small per-expert scratch tensors (a_ptrs/b_ptrs/out_ptrs/a_scales_ptrs/b_scales_ptrs/layout_sfa/layout_sfb/a_strides1/b_strides1/c_strides1 + workspace) with separate `torch::stable::empty` calls on every MoE GEMM invocation. (2) In decode this GEMM runs every forward step, so the per-call allocation + launch overhead of the offsets kernel is on the critical path and can hurt low-latency throughput. (3) Consider a single fused scratch allocation / caching the pointer buffers across calls. This mirrors the existing nvfp4 kernel, so it may be an accepted tradeoff.

(1) The refactored `apply` method drops the `shared_experts=shared_experts` argument when calling `self.moe_kernel.apply(...)`. The prior code passed both `shared_experts=shared_experts` and `shared_experts_input=shared_experts_input`; the new code passes only `shared_experts_input=shared_experts_input`. (2) Impact: for MoE layers with fused shared experts, the shared-expert computation would no longer be performed (or would silently produce wrong output / drop the shared-expert contribution), corrupting model output — a silent-accuracy regression. (3) Fix: restore `shared_experts=shared_experts` in the `moe_kernel.apply` call, matching the other apply call sites (e.g. QuarkNvfp4MoEMethod at line ~1743 still passes it).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run gsm8k eval on a model that uses fused shared experts (e.g. DeepSeek-style) through QuarkOCP_MX_MoEMethod and confirm accuracy is unchanged vs parent.

findings.claim: (1) The new `AiterExperts.is_supported_config` override is declared `@staticmethod` but calls zero-argument `super().is_supported_config(...)`. Inside a staticmethod there is no implicit first-argument binding for zero-arg `super()`. Zero-arg `super()` relies on the compiler-inserted `__class__` cell plus the first positional arg of the frame; in a staticmethod this is fragile and, depending on how `k_cls.is_supported_config(k_cls, ...)` is dispatched, can raise `RuntimeError: super(): no arguments` at call time. This code path is exercised by the oracle's `is_supported_config`/`_return_or_raise`. (2) Impact: W4A4 backend selection could raise at runtime on ROCm/GFX950 instead of returning (is_supported, reason). (3) Fix: use explicit `super(AiterExperts, cls).is_supported_config(...)` (or call the base class directly, `mk.FusedMoEExpertsModular.is_supported_config(cls, ...)`), consistent with the staticmethod convention used elsewhere.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test: call AiterExperts.is_supported_config(AiterExperts, cfg, kMxfp4Static, kMxfp4Dynamic, Standard) on a non-GFX950 or AITER-disabled setup and confirm it returns a tuple rather than raising.

findings.claim: (1) `_filter_by_activation` now keeps `Mxfp4MoeBackend.EMULATION` in the candidate list whenever a `requested_activation_key` is set, regardless of whether EMULATION's `_backend_activation_key` matches. Combined with the removal of `EMULATION` from `_get_priority_backends_for_gpt_oss()`, EMULATION becomes a silent fallthrough for any requested activation key when it was not user-selected. Because EMULATION is only added to the priority list via this filter special-case, but `_get_priority_backends_for_gpt_oss` no longer contains it, the auto path may no longer be able to reach EMULATION at all for gpt_oss (it was removed from the source list), meaning the special-case in `_filter_by_activation` has no effect there. (2) Impact: potential loss of the emulation auto-fallback for gpt_oss configs that previously silently fell back to emulation; users may now hit the NotImplementedError instead. This is a behavior/back-compat change that should be intentional. (3) Fix: confirm the intent — either keep EMULATION in the priority list, or document that emulation is now only reachable via explicit `--moe-backend emulation` (which the new error message and quark_moe NONE->EMULATION path suggest).

(1) `process_weights_after_loading` was rewritten to unconditionally call `self._setup_kernel(layer)`, deleting the entire legacy FP8-static-input-scale normalization path (max-across-experts input scale reduction, `normalize_e4m3fn_to_e4m3fnuz` for fnuz platforms) and the legacy AITER weight-shuffle path that existed for schemes where `mxfp4_backend is NONE`. Now, since NONE is remapped to EMULATION before `_setup_kernel`, all schemes go through the oracle/emulation kernel. (2) Impact: any Quark OCP-MX scheme that relied on the old fp8-static-input-scale handling (`self.static_input_scales and self.input_dtype == 'fp8'`) or the fnuz normalization now silently loses that preprocessing, which can produce wrong results on fnuz (MI300) hardware or for static-input-scale checkpoints. (3) Fix: verify `_setup_kernel`/oracle handles fp8 static input scales and fnuz normalization for the w_mxfp4_a_fp8 scheme, or retain the branch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: gsm8k on a w_mxfp4_a_fp8 Quark checkpoint with static input scales on MI300 (fnuz) hardware; compare accuracy to parent.

findings.claim: (1) In the new `AITER_MXFP4_MXFP4` branch of `convert_gpt_oss_weight_to_mxfp4_moe_kernel_format`, `fp4_dtype = getattr(torch, 'float4_e2m1fn_x2', None)` silently skips the `.view(fp4_dtype)` step if the attribute is absent, but then still shuffles/returns the (non-viewed) weights. (2) Impact: on a torch build lacking `float4_e2m1fn_x2`, weights are passed to the CK kernel in the wrong element interpretation with no error, producing silently wrong results rather than a clear failure. (3) Fix: assert `fp4_dtype is not None` for this backend (it is a hard requirement for the CK MXFP4 kernel) or raise a clear error.

(1) `MarlinFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel` was added to `_POSSIBLE_FP8_BLOCK_KERNELS[CUDA]`, but its `can_implement()` (in scaled_mm/marlin.py) returns `(True, None)` unconditionally, so it claims to implement *any* block-scaled FP8 config that reaches `choose_scaled_mm_linear_kernel`. (2) Risk: For a genuine per-group (block) activation quant config where the higher-priority DeepGEMM/Cutlass block kernels are unsupported (e.g. capability >=89 without VLLM_TEST_FORCE_FP8_MARLIN, or compute-capability boundaries), the selection loop could fall through to Marlin. Marlin is a weight-only FP8 kernel (`marlin_input_dtype=None`, `layer.input_scale=None; del layer.input_scale` in process_weights_after_loading) and only understands `weight_scale_inv`/`weight_scale`; it does not honor per-group *activation* quantization. That would silently produce numerically wrong results for a block-scaled config. (3) Fix: tighten `MarlinFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel.can_implement` to reject configs whose activation quant is dynamic per-group (i.e. only accept when weight_quant_key == kFp8Static128BlockSym with the weight-only path it actually implements), so it is never chosen as a fallback for a true activation-block-scaled GEMM.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Force-disable DeepGEMM/Cutlass block kernels (VLLM_DISABLED_KERNELS) on a block-quantized fp8 model and compare logits/perplexity of the Marlin-selected path against the reference block-scaled path.

findings.claim: (1) The new branch instantiates any `issubclass(kernel_type, FP8ScaledMMLinearKernel)` with the 4 layer_param_names `[weight, weight_scale, input_scale, input_scale_ub]`. But in the block-quant path, the weight scale is registered by Fp8LinearMethod.create_weights as `weight_scale_inv` (not `weight_scale`) — see fp8.py `layer.register_parameter("weight_scale_inv", scale)`. MarlinFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel handles this itself via its own `apply_weights`/`process_weights_after_loading` (reads `layer.weight_scale_inv` when block_quant), so it does not use `layer_param_names[1]`='weight_scale' for the block case. (2) Risk: the passed `layer_param_names` list is therefore misleading/inert for the block-quant Marlin path — `FP8ScaledMMLinearKernel._get_layer_params` would `getattr(layer, 'weight_scale')` and fail (AttributeError) if any generic base-class code path were exercised for a block-quantized layer, since only `weight_scale_inv` exists. It works today only because Marlin fully overrides those methods. (3) Fix: pass the correct scale name for the block case (or document that Marlin ignores layer_param_names for block_quant) so future FP8ScaledMMLinearKernel subclasses added to the block list don't silently break.

(1) The block-kernel dict type was widened to `list[type[Fp8BlockScaledMMLinearKernel | FP8ScaledMMLinearKernel]]`, and the block path's return type contract (`FP8ScaledMMLinearKernel | Fp8BlockScaledMMLinearKernel`) now genuinely can return a plain `FP8ScaledMMLinearKernel` from the per-group branch. (2) Risk: callers that assumed the per-group branch always yields a `Fp8BlockScaledMMLinearKernel` (e.g. code calling `apply_block_scaled_mm` or relying on `apply_input_quant`/block-specific attributes) will break when Marlin is returned. (3) Fix: audit callers of the block path (grep for apply_block_scaled_mm / block-specific attrs on the returned kernel) and confirm they only use the common `apply_weights` interface; the TODO already notes the intended unification under MMLinearKernel.

(1) The default of `disable_flashinfer_prefill` was changed from `True` to `None`. Combined with the migration logic in `_migrate_deprecated_mla_prefill_flags`, this silently changes behavior for users who relied on the default: previously the default (`True`) meant flashinfer prefill was disabled and FlashAttention was used; now `None` is falsy so the `if self.disable_flashinfer_prefill:` migration branch is skipped by default, leaving `mla_prefill_backend=None` (auto-select). (2) This alters the effective prefill backend selection for existing deployments that never set the flag — a silent default-behavior change on affected hardware (esp. SM100/Blackwell where auto-select now differs). (3) Confirm this behavior change is intended and documented in release notes; if backward compatibility for the old default is required, preserve the old auto-select outcome or emit a migration note.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instantiate AttentionConfig() with defaults on an SM100 device stub and assert the resolved mla_prefill_backend matches pre-change auto-selection.

findings.claim: (1) The migration in `_migrate_deprecated_mla_prefill_flags` has an ordering ambiguity: it checks `use_trtllm_ragged_deepseek_prefill` then `disable_flashinfer_prefill` in sequence, each guarded by `if self.mla_prefill_backend is None`. A user who previously set BOTH `use_trtllm_ragged_deepseek_prefill=True` AND `disable_flashinfer_prefill=True` (both were independently meaningful before) now gets `TRTLLM_RAGGED` (first branch wins) but still receives the `disable_flashinfer_prefill` deprecation warning — even though that flag's intent (FLASH_ATTN) is silently ignored. (2) This can silently pick a different backend than the user intended when multiple deprecated flags are combined, with a misleading warning. (3) Detect conflicting deprecated flags and either raise or emit a single, accurate warning describing which backend was selected.

(1) In `_migrate_deprecated_mla_prefill_flags`, the two migration branches wrap `self.mla_prefill_backend = ...` inside `if self.mla_prefill_backend is None:` but this check is redundant/dead for the first assignment path: the function already returns early at the top when `mla_prefill_backend is not None`, and within a single branch the inner guard for the first-encountered flag is always True. The inner guards only matter across branches. (2) Minor: adds indirection that obscures intent (the comment 'Only the first flag encountered sets the backend' is the real behavior). (3) Simplify by removing the redundant inner `is None` check in the first branch or restructure into an explicit precedence chain.

(1) `use_cudnn_prefill` is retained as a config field but its only remaining effect is to raise a hard ValueError during `__post_init__` when set True. The docstring says 'Deprecated: cuDNN prefill backend has been removed' but the field still accepts the value and errors at config-construction time rather than being rejected at CLI-parse time. (2) Users with existing scripts passing `--attention-config.use_cudnn_prefill=1` will now get a hard failure (acceptable for a removed backend) but the field remaining as a settable bool with default False is confusing surface area. (3) Consider clearly documenting the removal timeline and whether the flag should error immediately at parse or be fully removed after a deprecation window.

(1) `parse_mla_prefill_priorities` uses `ast.walk(node)` to find nested `if` statements, which will descend into ALL nested ifs (including unrelated conditionals) within `_get_mla_prefill_backend_priorities`, and the else-branch handling unconditionally overwrites `priorities['default']`. If the function body has multiple return-bearing if/else structures or nested conditionals, backend priority extraction may pick up the wrong list. (2) This is doc-generation tooling only (not runtime), but incorrect parsing yields silently wrong generated docs. (3) Restrict the walk to top-level statements of the function body or match on the specific capability check to avoid capturing unrelated conditionals.

(1) The fused path calls `_torchembed_rope_forward(t_kernel, t_kernel, freqs_cos_2d, freqs_sin_2d)` and returns `t_out`, but there is no evidence this triton kernel is autograd-differentiable, nor is it wrapped in a `torch.autograd.Function`. `apply_rotary_pos_emb` is used inside the training forward pass (layer.py:431-432, fpdt uses a hand-written backward), and the reference path `(t*cos)+(_rotate_half(t)*sin)` is fully autograd-traceable. If `fused_rope_forward` is a raw triton kernel op, autograd will either error ('does not require grad and does not have a grad_fn') or silently break the gradient graph, producing zero/wrong gradients for q/k in the training path. (2) Silent wrong gradients cause training divergence with no error. (3) Either wrap the fused call in a custom autograd.Function implementing the correct backward, or restrict the fused path to inference/no-grad contexts (e.g. guard with `torch.is_grad_enabled()`/`not t.requires_grad`). The PR's own added test `test_apply_rotary_pos_emb_fused_gradient_correctness` explicitly references a known upstream torchembed backward bug, confirming this risk is real.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run test_apply_rotary_pos_emb_fused_gradient_correctness on a CUDA box with torchembed installed; compare t.grad from fused vs reference path.

findings.claim: (1) The fused-path dispatch condition uses `freqs_cos.shape[0] == t.shape[0]` and `freqs_cos.numel() == freqs_cos.shape[0] * rot_dim` to decide the sequence dim matches, but this is not sufficient to guarantee that dim 0 of `t` is actually the sequence dimension. In the fpdt call site (fpdt_layer.py:212-224) the tensors passed have layout `[b, l, nh, hd]` after single_all_to_all with gather_idx=0 (batch at dim 0), and pos_emb is sliced as `pos_emb_cos[:, ...]`.
