If a caller ever passes a tensor whose dim 0 is batch while freqs happens to be effectively 2D `[seq, rot_dim]` with seq==batch (e.g. batch==seq_len), the guard passes but `movedim(0,-2)` mis-permutes and RoPE is applied along the wrong axis. (2) Silently wrong positional encoding -> wrong attention. (3) Pass an explicit `seq_dim` argument or verify against the documented contract rather than inferring from shape coincidences; add a shape/dtype assertion.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Construct t with shape [seq, 1, dim] where a batch dim collides with seq; verify fused vs reference agree.

findings.claim: (1) The fused path does `t.movedim(0, -2).contiguous()` before the kernel and `t_out.movedim(-2, 0).contiguous()` after, i.e. two extra full-tensor contiguous copies plus a `freqs_cos.reshape(...)`. For the common layer.py call site the reference path is a couple of elementwise ops with no copies. (2) The two forced .contiguous() materializations add memory traffic and latency that can offset or exceed the triton kernel speedup, especially for the small rot_dim/seq shapes RoPE typically uses. (3) Benchmark end-to-end apply_rotary_pos_emb latency (fused incl. movedim copies vs reference) to confirm a net win before enabling by default; consider requiring the caller to provide seq-second-to-last layout to avoid the transposes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark apply_rotary_pos_emb on CUDA for typical (seq_len, heads, head_dim) with torchembed installed vs reference path, including movedim/contiguous cost.

findings.claim: (1) Enabling the fused torchembed path automatically whenever the package is importable and the tensor is on the accelerator silently changes numerics/behavior for existing users who happen to have torchembed installed (transitively), with no opt-in flag. (2) A silent backend switch can alter training results (small numerical drift, or gradient behavior per the autograd concern) for users who did not intend to change anything. (3) Gate the fused path behind an explicit config/env flag (default off), following the pattern of other optional accelerated backends, so installation alone does not change behavior.

(1) The fused path passes `freqs_cos_2d`/`freqs_sin_2d` (reshaped to [seq, rot_dim]) but does not verify/cast their dtype/device to match `t_kernel`. If freqs are fp32 caches while t is fp16 (common), the triton kernel may require matching dtypes or produce an fp32 output that then differs from the reference path which broadcasts naturally. (2) Possible dtype-mismatch error or silent upcast/downcast differences vs reference. (3) Explicitly cast freqs to t's dtype (or assert equality) at the parallelism/kernel boundary before calling the kernel.

(1) The singleton fastpath in MOELayer.forward guards the all-to-all with `self.ep_size > 1`, but `self.ep_size` is derived from the constructor argument, not from the runtime world size of `self.ep_group`. The capacity all_reduce guards use the runtime-authoritative `dist.get_world_size(group=ep_group) > 1`. These two conditions can diverge if `ep_size` is set to 1 while the actual `ep_group` spans multiple ranks, or vice versa. (2) If they diverge, a rank could skip the all-to-all (treating it as identity) while peers do not, producing a hang or silent wrong dispatch layout. (3) For consistency and robustness, gate the all-to-all on the same authoritative runtime check, e.g. `dist.get_world_size(group=self.ep_group) > 1`, or add an assertion that `self.ep_size == dist.get_world_size(group=self.ep_group)`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: 2-rank run with ep_size=1 vs ep_size=2 verifying all-to-all invocation count matches ep_group world size, plus a divergence test where ep_size and ep_group world size disagree.

findings.claim: (1) In the ep_size==1 fastpath the code calls `dispatched_input.contiguous()` / `expert_output.contiguous()` to replicate the layout normalization `_AllToAll.forward` used to perform. This is correct, but the timers (FIRST/SECOND_ALLTOALL_TIMER) are still started/stopped around a path that no longer performs any communication. (2) Minor: the wall-clock breakdown will now attribute near-zero time to a2a for singleton groups, which is expected, but the `.contiguous()` may be a no-op copy elision most of the time. (3) Optional: leave as-is (harmless), or note in a comment that the contiguous() call preserves the layout contract of the removed collective.

(1) The fix depends on the internal ATen header `<ATen/native/cuda/Resize.h>` and the function `at::native::resize_bytes_cuda`, which are part of PyTorch's internal/native implementation rather than the stable public C++ API. (2) Internal native headers and their symbols are not covered by PyTorch's backward-compatibility guarantees; their location or signature can change between PyTorch releases, which would break the DeepCompile C++ extension build against newer torch versions. Since DeepCompile already gates on supported PyTorch versions, this is a maintainability/version-pinning risk rather than a current bug. (3) Consider adding a comment documenting the PyTorch version assumption, or guard the include/call behind a version check, and/or prefer a more stable path to release the storage (e.g. resizing via a public storage API) if one is available for the supported torch range.

(1) The new release logic relies on the ordering `record_stream(currentStream)` BEFORE `resize_bytes_cuda(storage, 0)` so that the CUDA caching allocator captures the consumer-stream usage as a pending event when the block is freed. This ordering-correctness is critical and only implicitly documented by the inline comment. (2) If a future refactor reorders these two calls (freeing the storage before recording the consumer stream), the caching allocator could reuse the block while consumer kernels queued behind `wait_allgather` are still reading it, causing silent data corruption that would not reliably reproduce (it is race/timing dependent). (3) Consider strengthening the comment to explicitly state that `record_stream` MUST precede the resize/free, and note that `test_consumer_stream_can_finish_before_storage_reuse` guards this invariant so it is not accidentally reordered.

(1) In _sync_memory_profile_complete the boolean flag is materialized as a default-dtype (float32) tensor: torch.tensor([1 if profile_complete else 0], device=...). (2) The rest of this module/file uses explicit integer flag tensors (e.g. cache_hit_flag with dtype=torch.int in graph_profile.py) for cross-rank boolean synchronization; the float here is functionally correct because ReduceOp.MIN over exact 0.0/1.0 is exact, but it is inconsistent and slightly surprising for a boolean status reduce. (3) Use dtype=torch.int (or torch.int32) for clarity and consistency with the surrounding flag-reduction pattern. No behavior change intended.

(1) When an entire param group is frozen (all params have requires_grad=False), the new `trainable` list is empty, and the next line `_flatten_dense_tensors([p.clone().detach() for p in self.fp16_groups[i]])` is called with an empty list, which raises in torch (`_flatten_dense_tensors([])` fails / returns a degenerate tensor). This is a NEW crash path introduced by the filter: previously `param_group['params']` was non-empty so flattening always succeeded. LoRA/PEFT setups commonly place all frozen params of a layer type into their own group, so hitting a fully-frozen group is plausible. (2) It aborts optimizer construction before training starts. (3) Guard the empty case, e.g. skip flatten and append an empty/zero-numel flat buffer when `len(trainable) == 0`, or filter groups upstream. The PR author acknowledges this gap and deliberately leaves it to mirror BF16_Optimizer; flagging so maintainers can decide to fix both together with an explicit TODO.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test: construct FP16_Optimizer/FP16_UnfusedOptimizer with a param group where every param has requires_grad=False; assert no crash at init.

findings.claim: (1) `state_dict()` saves `self.fp32_groups` (unfused) / `self.fp32_groups_flat` (fused) which now contain only trainable params. `load_state_dict()` restores master weights via `zip(self.fp32_groups, state_dict['fp32_groups'])` — a `zip` that silently truncates on length mismatch. A checkpoint saved by the pre-change code (all params, including frozen) but loaded by the post-change code (only trainable) would mis-align master params across groups where a frozen param precedes a trainable one, silently restoring wrong fp32 master weights with no error. (2) Silent divergence after resume for mixed frozen/trainable models. (3) Add a length/identity check in load_state_dict, or a migration note documenting that checkpoints are not cross-compatible across this change. Mitigating factor: the pre-change path OOM'd for the LoRA case this targets, so such checkpoints are unlikely to exist in practice — hence moderate confidence.

(1) The added `assert not debug_log or next_ag.free_acc_mem == 0` is tautologically true: `next_ag` is selected as `sorted_ags[0]` from `ags_with_no_additional_ag`, which by construction only contains tasks where `ag.free_acc_mem == 0`. (2) Impact: the assertion can never fire and adds no verification value; a wrong docstring/guard is misleading to future maintainers who may believe it protects against a real invariant. (3) Fix: either remove the redundant assert, or reframe it to assert an actually-derivable-elsewhere invariant (e.g. that the sort key really came from the filtered subset). Low impact since it only runs when debug_log is True.

(1) The zero-free-path ordering changes from a single `free_cost` key to `(n_scheduled_ags, allgather_acc_mem, free_cost, node.name)`, demoting `free_cost` from primary to tertiary selector. This alters which all-gather is scheduled first in cases previously decided purely by `free_cost`, changing the emitted schedule (and thus memory/comm timing) for existing workloads even when the old code already picked a zero-`free_acc_mem` candidate. (2) Impact: this is a heuristic behavior change, not a pure bug fix, for the subset where old behavior happened to be correct; it could regress schedules that were previously good. (3) This appears intentional per the commit message (graph-pressure metrics preferred), so no code change is strictly required, but it is worth confirming the reprioritization was validated against representative real models beyond the CPU-only FX unit tests, since the tests are synthetic.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare end-to-end DeepCompile ZeRO-3 all-gather scheduling (peak activation memory and step time) on a real transformer model before/after this change, not just the synthetic FX regressions, to confirm the pressure-first ordering does not regress schedules that the old free_cost ordering handled well.

findings.claim: (1) The new `elif backward_override == "dequantized": save_original_input = False` branch overrides a user-supplied `save_original_input=True` for the Linear module without any warning, mirroring the existing `high_precision -> True` override. (2) This is intentional and correct: in dequantized backward mode the backward pass dequantizes the saved quantized operand rather than re-quantizing the original high-precision input, so keeping the original fp input would waste memory and diverge from the intended path. The forward code below (`columnwise` usage guarded by `not save_original_input` and `backward_override is None`) already handles both flags consistently, so forcing the flag to False keeps the saved operand as the row-wise-only quantized tensor the dequantized backward expects. (3) No fix required; optionally add a short inline comment noting that dequantized backward intentionally ignores `save_original_input` (parallel to the high_precision comment) so future readers do not mistake the silent override for a bug.

(1) `should_release_reduce_buckets` releases the shared `reduce_buckets` on the *first* backward graph id (`graph_id == backward_graph_ids[0]`), and the C++ `end_backward` then calls `reduce_buckets->clear()`. In DeepCompile, backward graphs execute in reverse order, so the graph listed first in `graph_order` is actually the *last* backward graph to run — but the note in the C++ comment says the release should happen 'after the final backward graph has flushed its pending reductions'. Because `reduce_buckets` is shared across all graph executors, clearing it during the first-listed backward graph could destroy the double-buffered bucket state while other backward graphs still have pending reduce tasks queued, or (if ordering is opposite) release too early. (2) If the release fires before all executors have flushed, `getBuffer` will reallocate mid-step and pending copy/reduce events tracked in the cleared `CUDAEvent` maps are lost, risking a race between reduce_scatter and copy and thus silently wrong gradients. (3) Verify that `backward_graph_ids[0]` corresponds to the last-executed backward graph; if backward graphs run in reverse of `graph_order`, this should select the last element, not the first. Cross-check against `offload_adam_states.py` which uses `graph_order_with_backward[-1]` / `[0]` with explicit is_first/is_last semantics.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-graph (graph-break) DeepCompile ZeRO-1 run with GAS>1; assert gradient correctness against eager and confirm reduce_buckets is cleared exactly once after all backward graphs flush.

findings.claim: (1) `_build_flat_partition_grad_views` reimplements the flat-partition view construction that already exists in `optimizer.get_flat_partition`, but its narrowing/offset logic differs. Specifically it applies `first_offset` only to element counting and, when `tensor_offset==0 and num_elements==tensor.numel()`, reshapes the view to `tensor.shape`; otherwise it leaves a flat view. It also appends a trailing pad view when `current_size < partition_size`. This handcrafted layout must byte-for-byte match what ZeRO-1 expects when it later does `flat_grad_partition.view(-1)` and treats it as the optimizer-facing gradient partition. (2) A subtle mismatch (e.g. wrong handling of a param that straddles the partition boundary, or the padded tail) produces a misaligned fp32 grad partition and silently wrong optimizer updates. (3) Prefer reusing `get_flat_partition` (as the z2 path and old code did) rather than duplicating the partitioning math; if a bespoke builder is required, add an assertion/test that its views map to identical storage offsets as `get_flat_partition`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test comparing per-parameter grad view storage offsets/shapes from `_build_flat_partition_grad_views` vs `get_flat_partition` across param groups where params straddle partition boundaries and where padding is required.

findings.claim: (1) `updateGradBuffer` zeros the incoming grad_buffer (`if (grad_buffer.numel() > 0) grad_buffer.zero_();`) every time it is called. On the gradient-accumulation-boundary path this is called once per param per step to bind the step-local flat buffer. (2) The `zero_()` launches a kernel per parameter; for models with many parameters this adds per-step kernel-launch overhead on the compute stream that the previous clone-once-at-init design avoided. If the flat buffer is already freshly `torch.zeros`-allocated in `_build_flat_partition_grad_views`, the views are already zero, making this `zero_()` redundant. (3) Consider skipping the zero when the buffer is known to be freshly allocated, or zero the flat buffer once instead of per-view.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Per-step wall-clock and kernel-launch count for a model with many small parameter tensors under DeepCompile ZeRO-1, comparing with/without the per-param zero_().

findings.claim: (1) `_build_partition_grad_views` temporarily mutates `optimizer.all_grad_tensors[group_idx]` and restores it in a `finally`, using a `missing = object()` sentinel. This helper is only used on the `use_z2` path now. (2) The temporary global-state mutation on the optimizer is fragile; if `get_flat_partition` raises or the process is interrupted, and other code reads `all_grad_tensors` concurrently, it may observe the temporary value. It also duplicates the intent of the old inline code with added indirection. (3) Consider passing the grad tensors explicitly to `get_flat_partition` rather than round-tripping through the optimizer's mutable dict.

(1) In `step`, the assertion that `flat_grad_partition.numel() == self.partition_size[i]` for the pre-flattened path is only evaluated when `flat_grad_partition is not None`, but the subsequent generic assertion on `single_grad_partition.numel()` still runs for both paths. `del flat_grad_partition` after `.to(...)` is a no-op for freeing memory when `.to()` returns the same tensor (dtype already matches gradient_accumulation_dtype == fp32 single-partition dtype): `single_grad_partition` then aliases the pre-flattened buffer, and deleting the local name does not release it. (2) This means the step-local flat buffer may stay alive through `unscale_and_clip_grads`/optimizer step exactly as before if dtypes match, partially undermining the lifetime fix on fp32 configs. (3) Confirm the release path (`_release_preflattened_grad_buffers(i)` + `averaged_gradients[i]=None`) is what actually drops the buffer and that `del flat_grad_partition` is not relied upon for memory; if `.to()` is a copy the clone is intended, otherwise document that release happens via the registry, not the `del`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Peak-allocated-memory comparison for fp32 (gradient_accumulation_dtype == fp32) DeepCompile ZeRO-1 vs bf16 to confirm the buffer is actually released before the optimizer step in both dtype cases.

findings.claim: (1) In the columnwise (RETURN_TRANSPOSE) swizzled store, the swizzled column length passed to gemm_swizzled_scale_idx is computed as `col_length_t = rows / SCALE_DIM` (M/16). For the transposed operand the scale matrix has shape (cols x rows/16), so `col_length` — the number of scale COLUMNS of the transposed compact matrix — should indeed be rows/SCALE_DIM; this appears correct. However, the rowwise path passes `SFs_per_row = cols / SCALE_DIM` as col_length while the columnwise path passes `rows / SCALE_DIM`. This asymmetry hinges on the transpose swapping M<->N. It matters because a wrong col_length silently changes cbg_cnt and produces GEMM-incompatible scale layout (wrong results, not a crash), and the 128-alignment guard would hide it in tests that only check byte-equality against nvte_swizzle_scaling_factors on the SAME shape. (2) Risk: silent wrong GEMM results for the columnwise operand on non-square shapes. (3) Fix: add an explicit assertion/comment verifying col_length_t corresponds to the transposed compact matrix's last-dim tile count, and ensure the byte-equality tests cover non-square shapes for columnwise (they do include 8192x5120 etc., which is good — confidence lowered accordingly).

(1) In the columnwise swizzled store path, the previous vectorized store (`scales_vec.store_to`) is replaced by a per-element scalar loop (`for k: scales_t_ptr[swizzle::gemm_swizzled_scale_idx(...)] = ...`). The swizzled layout scatters columnwise scales so vectorization is genuinely impossible, but this converts one 4-8 byte vectorized store into `count` (up to SCALES_PER_CHUNK_Y = 8) scattered scalar stores per thread. (2) Impact: this is on the default cached-weight quantize path; scattered scalar SF stores add store-unit pressure and non-coalesced global writes, though SF volume is tiny (1 byte per 16 elements) so the absolute cost is small relative to the FP4 data stores. Net effect is still a win vs. a full separate swizzle kernel launch. (3) No fix required — but worth a benchmark to confirm the in-kernel scatter does not regress the columnwise-only case vs. compact+separate-swizzle for large tensors.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark quantize_transpose NVFP4 2D path (columnwise_only and both_directions) on 16384x8192 with and without WITH_GEMM_SWIZZLED_SCALES, comparing total quantize+swizzle wall time vs. compact+nvte_swizzle_scaling_factors.

findings.claim: (1) The gate function `nvfp4_emits_gemm_swizzled_scales` in quantizer.cpp duplicates the arch/128-alignment eligibility logic that also lives in `is_eligible_for_2d_swizzle_fusion` and in the kernel-side NVTE_CHECK (rows%128==0 && cols%128==0). Three independent copies of the 128-alignment condition risk drifting out of sync (e.g. if the tile size changes). (2) Impact: maintainability — a future change to the swizzle tile size must be updated in 3 places or the create_tensor gate and the kernel guard will disagree, turning a silent mismatch into an NVTE_CHECK abort at runtime. (3) Fix: have the kernel-side guard reference the same named constant/predicate, or add a comment cross-linking the three sites.

(1) The new helper `gemm_swizzled_scale_idx` in swizzle.cuh is documented as byte-compatible with `scale_factor_swizzled_offset` in quantize_transpose_vector_blockwise_fp4.cu. Comparing the two: both use kRowsPerBaseBlock=128, kRowsPerBaseBlockCol=32, kColsPerBaseBlockCol=4, and identical final expression `((rb*cbg_cnt+cbg)*32+d3)*16+d4*4+d5`; the new version computes cbg_cnt as `(col_length+3)/4` which equals the reference `DIVUP(col_length, 4)`. The formulas are byte-identical, so compatibility holds. However, there is now duplicated swizzle-offset logic in two files with no shared source of truth; if the reference in swizzle.cu changes, this copy will silently diverge. (2) Impact: future silent GEMM scale-layout mismatch. (3) Fix: factor the offset formula into one shared header and include it from both call sites, or add a static_assert-backed cross-reference/test asserting byte-equality (the added tests do compare against nvte_swizzle_scaling_factors, which mitigates this).

(1) The new `get_graph_order()` asserts `all(isinstance(needs_backward, bool) ...)` over every frame, but frames are created in `backend_fn` via `add_graph()` with `needs_backward=None` and only set to a bool later inside `make_fw_graph` via `set_needs_backward()`. If any code path calls `get_graph_order()` for a frame whose `make_fw_graph` has not yet run, the assert fires. This is safe only under the assumption that AOT autograd/inductor always invoke the forward compiler synchronously for a graph before any subsequent `get_graph_order()` read that includes that frame. (2) Risk: if a future torch version defers or reorders fw-compiler invocation (e.g. lazy/deferred compilation, or a graph that is registered but whose fw compile is skipped/delayed), the assert will crash training that previously ran fine. It converts a previously-tolerated None into a hard failure. (3) Consider making the assert defensive or documenting the invariant explicitly (add_graph immediately followed by fw compile), or default unresolved frames to False with a warning rather than asserting.

(1) `needs_backward` is now determined by `frame_id in frames_partitioned` inside `make_fw_graph`, which relies on the AOT-autograd/inductor ordering that the joint-graph partition function runs before the forward compiler. This ordering assumption is stated in the comment but is a torch-internal implementation detail. (2) Risk: a torch version change to the fw-compile vs partition ordering would silently set `needs_backward=False` for graphs that actually need backward, re-introducing the unbalanced fwd/bwd visit bug (unvisited frames in `frames_needing_bwd`) or, conversely, leaving stale entries. (3) Add a lightweight consistency check: when `make_bw_graph` runs for a frame, assert that frame was marked `needs_backward=True`, to catch ordering regressions early.

(1) `wrap_partition_fn` previously took a hardcoded `True` for `z3_partition` when calling `get_wrapped_partitioner`; it now threads the real `z3_partition` flag and is always installed (previously installed only under `if z3_partition`). This is a behavior change for the non-z3 inductor path: the partition function is now always wrapped. (2) The wrapping is required so `frames_partitioned.add(frame_id)` runs for non-z3 graphs, which is intentional and correct. But note that the previous `DSGraphParamManager(...)` and `fix_placeholder_meta` work is now correctly guarded by `if z3_partition`, so no functional regression for non-z3. Confirming intent: this is the desired behavior. (3) No change needed; flagged only to confirm the always-wrap is intentional.

In `_forward_grouped_tensor`, the wgrad rowwise-free guard was changed from `if fp8:` to `if fp8 and grouped_x.columnwise_data is not None:`. This is the module-level path (`transformer_engine/pytorch/module/grouped_linear.py`), which unlike the ops path (`ops/basic/grouped_linear.py`) was NOT extended to permit FP8 current scaling on Hopper in `_is_grouped_tensor_path_supported` (that function still returns False for current scaling, only allowing MXFP8/NVFP4). So on the module path, when this branch runs, `fp8` implies MXFP8/NVFP4 which always has columnwise_data set, meaning the added `columnwise_data is not None` condition is effectively always true and the guard is a no-op here. Verify this is intentional; if current scaling was meant to be enabled in the module path too, the `_is_grouped_tensor_path_supported` docstring/logic in this file is now inconsistent with the ops file and with the added comment referencing 'FP8 per tensor current scaling on Hopper'.

The docstring for `_is_grouped_tensor_path_supported` in the module file was updated to claim 'Hopper (CC 9.0): BF16/FP16 and FP8 per-tensor current scaling' and 'Blackwell ... and FP8 per-tensor current scaling', but the actual code in this file's `_is_grouped_tensor_path_supported` was NOT updated to accept `Float8CurrentScalingQuantizer` (the `if fp8:` branch still only returns True for MXFP8 or RHT-NVFP4 and requires CC>=10.0). Only the ops/basic/grouped_linear.py copy got the `Float8CurrentScalingQuantizer` short-circuit. This makes the module-path docstring misleading (a wrong docstring is worse than none) and means current scaling silently falls through to the legacy path here. If parity was intended, the module `_is_grouped_tensor_path_supported` needs the same `Float8CurrentScalingQuantizer` branch; if not, revert the docstring.

The heuristic dim assignment for avg_m/avg_n/avg_k was swapped in all three grouped-GEMM entry points (nvte_grouped_gemm, _with_discrete_inputA, _with_discrete_out): avg_m now uses last dim of D, avg_n uses first dim of D, and avg_k flips its transa branch. These feed CUBLASLT_MATMUL_PREF_GROUPED_DESC_D_AVERAGE_ROWS etc. and thus algorithm selection. The change is justified by 'cuBLAS views operands column-major', so it may be a genuine bug fix (matching the comment 'avg m = avg num of rows of D in column-major = avg last dim of D'). However, this is a subtle correctness/perf-critical change to heuristics that only affects algorithm choice (not numerical result) and must be validated: if the previous row-major interpretation was actually correct for these code paths, the swap would degrade kernel selection (throughput) on all grouped GEMMs, not just current scaling. Recommend a targeted grouped-GEMM performance regression benchmark across TP/EP MoE shapes on Hopper and Blackwell to confirm the new heuristics do not regress algorithm selection.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Grouped GEMM (MoE) forward/backward throughput sweep over representative expert shapes on SM90 and SM100, comparing algorithm selection latency before vs after the avg_m/avg_n/avg_k swap.

findings.claim: The comment '(For FP8 per tensor current scaling on Hopper --> Free Rowwise Data in backward pass)' is duplicated verbatim in two files/locations and phrased as a parenthetical fragment. Since the guard `grouped_x.columnwise_data is not None` now also affects MXFP8/NVFP4 (which always populate columnwise_data), the comment implies the change is current-scaling-specific when it is actually a general guard against freeing rowwise data when no columnwise copy exists. Clarify the comment to state the invariant ('only free rowwise data when a columnwise copy exists, e.g. current scaling may lack it') rather than tying it to one recipe/GPU.

The skip condition was changed to `quantization in ("fp8_delayed_scaling")` — this is a string, not a tuple, so `in` performs substring matching, not membership. It happens to still work because `"fp8_delayed_scaling" in "fp8_delayed_scaling"` is True and other values like 'fp8_current_scaling'/'mxfp8'/'nvfp4_rht' are not substrings of it, but this is fragile and misleading (a future value that is a substring, e.g. 'delayed', would wrongly match / non-substrings won't). Use a tuple `("fp8_delayed_scaling",)` for explicit membership.

(1) The default `ns_method` for `muon_update` and all Muon optimizer classes was changed from the implicit standard Newton-Schulz iteration to `"gram"` (Gram NS). Since Gram NS produces a numerically different orthogonalization (fp16 compute with a restart heuristic, different iterate trajectory) than the original bf16 standard NS, this silently alters the optimizer update for all existing users who do not set `ns_method`. (2) This is a default hyperparameter/algorithm change that can shift convergence behavior for existing training scripts and prior experiment reproducibility, without an opt-in. (3) Consider defaulting `ns_method="standard"` to preserve existing behavior, or explicitly document this as an intentional behavior change and gate it behind a version note / deprecation window.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Train a small transformer with the old standard-NS Muon vs. new default (gram) and compare loss curves over a few hundred steps to quantify the behavior shift.

findings.claim: (1) `zeropower_via_gram_newtonschulz` hard-codes fp16 compute (`torch.float16 if is_fp16_supported() else float32`) and normalizes by the Frobenius norm before iterating. The standard path (`zeropower_via_newtonschulz5`) uses bf16. fp16 has a much smaller dynamic range (~6e4 max) than bf16; the Gram matrix R = X@X.T and the R@R term (quartic in X entries) can overflow to inf in fp16 for larger matrices even after Frobenius normalization, since normalization only bounds the total norm, not per-element magnitudes after repeated matmuls. (2) An fp16 overflow silently produces inf/nan in the update, corrupting the parameter and diverging training, and would not be caught by the standard bf16 path used elsewhere. (3) Add an inf/nan guard or clamp on R after each iteration, or validate the fp16 stability claim against the maximum matrix sizes used in practice; at minimum document the size regime where fp16 is safe.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run zeropower_via_gram_newtonschulz on random rectangular matrices of varying size (e.g. 4096x20480) in fp16 and check for inf/nan and error vs. an fp32/fp64 reference orthogonalization.

findings.claim: (1) `muon_update` is decorated with `@compiler.compile()` (torch.compile) but now branches on the Python string argument `ns_method` (`if ns_method == "gram"`) and `zeropower_via_gram_newtonschulz` contains data-dependent Python control flow on tensor shapes (`if m <= n`, `if i == restart_at`, `if Q is None`). Under torch.compile these Python-side branches on non-constant values force graph breaks / recompilation for each distinct shape path, and `ns_method` being a plain kwarg (not a constant) can trigger recompiles. (2) This can add kernel-launch/recompilation overhead in the optimizer step and partially negate the claimed ~2x speedup. (3) Mark `ns_method` as a compile-time constant (e.g. specialize the function per method or split into two compiled entry points), and confirm the shape-dependent branches specialize cleanly rather than causing repeated graph breaks.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile muon_update under torch.compile with TORCH_LOGS=recompiles for a mix of matrix shapes and both ns_method values, counting graph breaks/recompilations.

findings.claim: (1) `muon_update` now saves `orig_dtype = grad.dtype` and conditionally casts the result back (`if update.dtype != orig_dtype`). The original code returned whatever dtype the NS iteration produced (bf16) and callers handled it (e.g. stage3 does `g.data.copy_(update)`). Restoring to `orig_dtype` changes the return dtype contract of `muon_update` relative to the pre-change behavior. (2) If any caller relied on the previous (bf16/compute-dtype) return, or if `grad` was itself fp16/bf16, the extra cast is either redundant (copy_ already casts) or a subtle behavior change. (3) Confirm the dtype restoration is intended for all call sites; if `copy_`/`add_` at the call sites already cast, the explicit `.to(orig_dtype)` is redundant work and can be dropped.

(1) `restart_at = 2` is hard-coded independent of `ns_steps`. For the default `ns_steps=5` this yields one restart, but the value is a magic number tied to half-precision stability with no assertion that `ns_steps` is large enough for the restart logic to be meaningful, and the R-update-skip guard `(i + 1) != restart_at` couples correctness to this constant. (2) If a user passes a small `ns_steps` (e.g. 2 or 3) the restart/guard interaction changes silently and the accumulated `Q`/`R` bookkeeping may not match the intended math, risking a subtly wrong orthogonalization. (3) Add an assertion or comment documenting the assumed `ns_steps` range and make `restart_at` a named, validated parameter.

(1) Single-letter names `Q`, `R`, `Z`, `RZ`, `A`, `B` are used throughout the Gram NS implementation in distributed/optimizer code where the rubric prefers descriptive names. (2) These abbreviations make the non-trivial polynomial-accumulation bookkeeping (Q = accumulated transform, R = Gram matrix) hard to verify and maintain. (3) Consider names like `accumulated_transform` (Q), `gram_matrix` (R) or at least a comment mapping each symbol to its mathematical role. (Low priority; matches the reference blog notation.)

(1) The THD `pad_between_seqs` auto-detect was changed from a value comparison (`not torch.equal(cu_seqlens_q_padded[:-1], cu_seqlens_q[:-1])`) to an identity/None check: it now returns True whenever `cu_seqlens_q_padded`/`cu_seqlens_kv_padded` are non-None and are not the *same Python object* as the unpadded versions. (2) Existing THD callers commonly pass distinct padded cu_seqlens tensors whose values equal the unpadded ones (only THD tail padding, no genuine inter-sequence padding). Under the old logic these correctly resolved to `pad_between_seqs=False` (keeping FlashAttention v2/v4 eligible); under the new logic they now resolve to True, silently altering backend selection and potentially degrading throughput or changing numerics for users relying on the default. (3) Preserve the value-based detection (or add a fast/sync-free equivalent) instead of collapsing to a pure object-identity/None test, or explicitly document and gate the new semantics.
findings.suggested_benchmark: THD forward/backward with distinct-object cu_seqlens_padded equal in value to cu_seqlens: assert selected backend and throughput match the pre-change baseline.

findings.claim: (1) In `AttnFuncWithCPAndKVP2P.backward` the newly added unconditional THD zero-fill (dq/dk/dv beyond cu_seqlens_*_padded[-1]) overlaps the pre-existing block that already zero-fills the same positions for the `qkv_format=='thd' and not use_fused_attention` case. (2) For the unfused THD path dq/dk/dv are now masked and written to zero twice per backward, adding redundant arange+mask+scatter kernels on the hot path. (3) Gate the new block on `use_fused_attention` (or remove the older block) so the zero-fill runs exactly once per path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: THD CP backward with unfused attention: count zero-fill kernel launches / measure backward latency before vs after deduplication.

findings.claim: (1) The new THD zero-fill in `FusedAttnFunc.backward` builds a fresh `torch.arange(dq.shape[0])` mask and does a boolean advanced-index assignment (`dq[q_pad_mask]=0`) for each of dq/dk/dv on every backward. (2) Boolean-mask scatter over the full token dimension is materially more expensive than the previous contiguous tail `fill_(0)` and runs every step; for large T this adds avoidable memory traffic. Note this is intentionally sync-free for CUDA-graph capture, but it also unconditionally penalizes the non-captured eager path. (3) Consider retaining the cheap contiguous `[cu_seqlens_padded[-1]:].fill_(0)` for the eager (non-capturing) path and only using arange+mask under `is_graph_capturing()`, mirroring the branch already used in context_parallel.py.
findings.suggested_benchmark: THD fused-attn backward eager mode: compare tail fill_(0) vs arange+mask scatter latency for large total token counts.

findings.claim: (1) In the graph-safe forward kernel, every CTA independently recomputes `C_coeff` from `*total_num_tokens_ptr` and each lane-0 does `atomicAdd(&Coeff_buf[1], block_sum*C_coeff)`, while only block 0 writes `Coeff_buf[0]=C_coeff`. This is functionally correct but reads the device token count once per CTA. (2) Minor: if `total_num_tokens` device value is 0 (empty microbatch), `C_coeff` divides by zero producing inf/nan silently propagated into aux_loss and the backward Const_buf — matching the host path's lack of guard but worth noting for graph replays where the count is dynamic. (3) Consider clamping/guarding total_num_tokens==0 on device, or document that callers must guarantee a positive token count across all replays.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Graph-captured aux-loss forward with a replay where total_num_tokens device value is 0; check for inf/nan in aux_loss and grad_probs.

findings.claim: (1) The clear_tensor_data(grad_output) guard is extended from `parallel_mode == "row" and sequence_parallel` to also cover `parallel_mode == "column" and fp8` under sequence_parallel, freeing the quantized grad_output for column-parallel SP FP8 backward. (2) This is correct only if `grad_output` in the column-parallel SP FP8 case is truly internal (a freshly quantized Float8TensorStorage) and never aliases a tensor the caller still needs (e.g. a saved/returned tensor). If in some column-SP FP8 sub-path grad_output was NOT re-quantized into a new internal tensor (e.g. it was already provided as a QuantizedTensorStorage from upstream, or aliased grad_output_arg), clearing it could corrupt data still referenced elsewhere, causing silently wrong gradients or a use-after-free. (3) Verify that in every column-parallel + sequence_parallel + fp8 branch reaching this point, grad_output is a locally-produced quantized tensor (from grad_output_quantizer(...) / grad_output_preprocess) and not an alias of grad_output_arg or a ctx-saved tensor before enabling clear_tensor_data. Recommend a shape/ownership assertion or explicit comment documenting the invariant.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Column-parallel Linear with sequence_parallel=True under FP8 (DelayedScaling and Float8CurrentScaling); run forward+backward and compare dgrad/wgrad against pre-change reference to confirm numerical identity, plus peak-memory measurement to confirm the intended early free.

findings.claim: (1) `destroy_ub()` now calls `torch.compiler.reset()` to invalidate the compile-time-constant caching of `is_fp8_ubuf()` baked via `@torch.compiler.assume_constant_result`. (2) `torch.compiler.reset()` is a *global* reset of all torch.compile/dynamo state, not scoped to the UB constants — every compiled function in the process gets its guards/caches invalidated and must recompile on next call. If a training run tears down and re-initializes UB communicators mid-run (or in tests), this forces full recompilation of unrelated compiled modules, which is an unexpected and potentially expensive side effect of a UB teardown call. (3) Consider documenting this global side effect prominently, or use a more targeted invalidation if available; at minimum note in the docstring that destroy_ub() triggers a full torch.compile reset.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compile a model with torch.compile, run steps, call destroy_ub(), then measure recompilation cost / dynamo recompile counter on the next forward to quantify the global-reset overhead.

findings.claim: (1) `LinearBwdArgs.fp8_recipe: Optional[Recipe]` field is removed and replaced with `dgrad_use_split_accumulator`/`wgrad_use_split_accumulator` bool fields. (2) `LinearBwdArgs` is an internal dataclass, but if any external code or subclass constructs/inspects it (e.g. custom backward overrides referencing `bwd_args.fp8_recipe`), this is a silent breaking change since the attribute no longer exists. (3) Confirm `fp8_recipe` is not read anywhere outside linear.py (grep across the repo, not just this module) before considering it fully internal; the diff only shows in-module callers being updated.

(1) The split-accumulator resolution logic (`if hasattr(_recipe, 'fp8_gemm_dgrad')...`) is now duplicated in the Linear.forward wrapper (lines ~1860) while the forward GEMM's own split-accumulator resolution and the old backward logic used the same `hasattr(recipe, ...)` pattern. (2) The resolution now happens eagerly for both dgrad and wgrad on every forward even when gradients are disabled (is_grad_enabled False / inference), doing two extra `hasattr` + attribute reads per call; minor but avoidable. Also there is now behavioral divergence risk: previously backward resolved from a live recipe, now it uses values snapshotted at forward time — correct for the compile goal, but if the recipe's use_split_accumulator could change between fwd and bwd it would differ (unlikely, but worth a comment). (3) Consider guarding the resolution behind `is_grad_enabled` and adding a brief comment that split-accumulator flags are intentionally snapshotted at forward time for torch.compile compatibility.

(1) The custom-recipe early-exit now adds `if recipe_state.recipe is recipe: return` (identity check) to avoid rebuilding quantizers when the recipe object is unchanged, fixing redundant per-forward quantizer re-init. (2) This is a genuine performance fix (avoids rebuilding quantizers every forward for custom recipes), but the identity-only comparison (`is`) means a semantically-equal-but-distinct recipe object will still trigger a rebuild — acceptable, but if callers recreate CustomRecipe objects each step (as the test's _make_qfactory does per-recipe), the cache still misses. Confirm the intended callers reuse the same recipe object across steps so the fast path is actually taken. (3) No change required if reuse is guaranteed; otherwise document that caching relies on recipe object identity.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Custom-recipe forward loop with the same recipe object reused across N iterations; count quantizer (re)initializations before/after the identity check to confirm the intended caching.

findings.claim: (1) The backward bias-gradient fusion is now gated on `isinstance(grad_output_quantizer, MXFP8Quantizer)` (previously gated only on `ctx.use_bias`). For NVFP4 (the newly-enabled quantized path), the fused `tex.bgrad_group_quantize` no longer runs, so dbias must be computed on the fallback/else branch. (2) If there is no else branch that computes the bias gradient when the quantizer is NVFP4, the bias gradient would be silently dropped, producing wrong/zero grads for biased NVFP4 GroupedLinear — a silent training-correctness bug. (3) Verify an else path (e.g. `compute_grouped_dbias` / non-fused bias grad) exists and is exercised for NVFP4-with-bias; add a test with `bias=True` for the NVFP4 recipe (the added correctness test uses bias=False). The C++ `bgrad_group_quantize` explicitly asserts MXFP8-only, so this gate is necessary; the risk is only in whether the fallback bias-grad path is present.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run test_grouped_linear_grouped_tensor_path_matches_legacy with fp8_recipe=NVFP4BlockScaling and bias=True; assert bias.grad matches the per-GEMM te.Linear reference.

findings.claim: (1) The FP8 branch of `_is_grouped_tensor_path_supported` now only checks `input_quantizers` type (MXFP8 or NVFP4-with-RHT). The previous code additionally required `all(isinstance(q, MXFP8Quantizer) for q in weight_quantizers)` and the grad_output quantizers. The `weight_quantizers`/`grad_output_quantizers` arguments were removed from the signature and the docstring now merely *assumes* input/weight/grad_output quantizers are the same type ('otherwise it would trigger a fatal error in the cuBLASLt grouped GEMM check'). (2) If a caller ever produces a mismatched input vs weight quantizer type (e.g. input NVFP4 but weight MXFP8), the path is now selected and only fails deep inside the C++ grouped GEMM as a fatal error instead of gracefully falling back to the legacy path — a regression in robustness/backward-compatibility of the fallback. (3) Confirm the caller guarantees homogeneous quantizer types across input/weight/grad_output, or retain a lightweight type-consistency check before selecting the fused path.

(1) The updated inline comment says the graph-safe path 'requires Hopper (SM90+)' but the code guard is a bounded range `not (9, 0) <= cc <= (11, 0)`, i.e. it also excludes SM > 11.0. (2) A comment saying 'SM90+' is misleading because future architectures (CC > 11.0) will silently fall back to the legacy path even though the comment implies they are supported; a wrong comment is worse than none. (3) Update the comment to reflect the closed upper bound (SM90 through SM110) matching the C++ `sm >= 90 && sm <= 110` gate.

(1) `grouped_gemm_supports_per_group_alpha_beta` and the `check_grouped_gemm_requirements` guards use hard upper bound `sm <= 110`. Compute-capability major/minor is encoded as sm = major*10+minor, so `sm <= 110` allows SM100..SM110 but rejects any SM in (110, ...]. This tightening from `>= 100` to `<= 110` means a newer Blackwell/next-gen part (e.g. SM120) will now be rejected with 'requires Blackwell (SM10x and SM110)' rather than being treated as 'or newer'. (2) If a future SM that is grouped-GEMM-capable ships, this closed upper bound will incorrectly disable the fused path until the constant is bumped — a forward-compatibility regression versus the previous open-ended `>=` checks. (3) Confirm the upper bound is intentional (i.e. cuBLASLt genuinely does not support SM>110 for grouped GEMM) and add a comment documenting why the range is closed rather than open-ended.

(1) Compute capability is queried twice: once at the top-level guard `not (9,0) <= get_device_compute_capability() <= (11,0)` and again inside the `if fp8:` branch `not (10,0) <= get_device_compute_capability() <= (11,0)`. (2) Redundant repeated calls add minor overhead and duplicate the upper-bound (11,0) logic in two places, risking them drifting apart on future edits. (3) Cache the result in a local variable and reuse; since the outer guard already bounds cc <= (11,0), the inner check simplifies to `cc < (10,0)`. Same pattern appears in ops/basic/grouped_linear.py.

(1) The parent `group_quantize` entry point in `transformer_engine/pytorch/csrc/extensions/cast.cpp` explicitly rejects non-MXFP8/non-NVFP4 quantizers (`NVTE_ERROR("group_quantize: only support NVFP4 or MXFP8 quantizer.")`), and `_group_quantize_for_grouped_mlp` in grouped_mlp.py routes all non-single-group quantizers (now including Float8CurrentScalingQuantizer) through `tex.group_quantize`. (2) This PR adds a new tensor-scaling (current-scaling) grouped path. If the new dispatch does not correctly gate unsupported FP8 tensor-scaling quantizers out of the grouped GEMM path (the commit message notes a fix 'keep unsupported FP8 tensor-scaling quantizers out of the grouped GEMM path'), delayed-scaling FP8 quantizers could silently be routed into the current-scaling grouped kernel, producing wrong scales. (3) Verify the dispatch in quantize.cuh/cast.cpp explicitly distinguishes DelayedScaling vs CurrentScaling FP8 quantizers and errors on the unsupported combination rather than falling through.

(1) In the current-scaling amax kernel (`amax_kernel` in current_scaling.cu, the per-tensor precedent for the new grouped amax kernel), the running max accumulator `max` is declared as `InputType` (e.g. `__nv_bfloat16`/`__half`) rather than `float`, and the `__builtin_assume(max >= InputType{0.f})` plus `__hmax(__habs(val), max)` accumulate the absolute-max entirely in low precision. (2) Accumulating amax in bf16/fp16 loses precision for large magnitudes and can produce a slightly-too-small amax, which yields a scale that overflows FP8 for a few elements — a silent correctness issue for current-scaling. The grouped amax kernel added in this PR (group_amax_fp8.cuh) likely mirrors this pattern. (3) Accumulate the per-thread absolute max in float (fp32) and only reduce/atomicMax in float, matching the final `atomicMaxFloat(amax, max)` which already expects float.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Correctness test: grouped current-scaling amax vs fp32 reference on inputs with large-magnitude outliers per group.

findings.claim: (1) The grouped current-scaling path launches at least four separate small kernels per quantize (zero_amax, amax reduction, compute_scale_from_amax, splits_to_offsets) plus the cast kernels, several of which are launch-overhead bound (`<<<1,1>>>` for zero_amax and compute_scale_from_amax per the current_scaling.cu precedent). (2) For grouped MoE quantize with many groups this multiplies kernel-launch overhead in the training hot path and can dominate when tensors are small. (3) Consider fusing zero-init + amax + scale-from-amax where possible, or using a single grid-wide kernel with grouped metadata; the benchmark harness in the PR already buckets these kernels, confirming they are individually measurable overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmarks/benchmark_group_quantize_current_scaling.py --profile (amax_zero / compute_scale bucket per-iter us)

findings.claim: (1) The overallocated (varying-first / varying-last) grouped FP8 path relies on `tensor_offsets`/`first_dims` to avoid reading/writing the over-allocated tail rows (the benchmark poisons tail rows with 1e30 and asserts amax stays small). (2) If the grouped current-scaling amax or cast kernel sizes its launch grid from the *allocated* buffer shape rather than the active `sum(first_dims)` region (the commit history shows repeated churn: 'avoid launching FP8 quantization over unused overallocated rows', 'Size grouped FP8 launches from active output shape'), it will read the poisoned tail rows and corrupt amax, or write garbage into unused output — a silent correctness bug. (3) Verify the launch grid and per-group tile mapping are derived from the active/logical output shape (device-side tensor_offsets) for all direction modes, and that the tail-read regression test (`_verify_no_tail_read`) is run for all overalloc shape cases, not only `varying-first-overalloc`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: test_cast_current_scaling_grouped.cu overallocated tail-read cases across rowwise/columnwise/both

findings.claim: (1) In `benchmark_group_quantize_current_scaling.py`, `_verify_no_tail_read` is only invoked for `shape_case == 'varying-first-overalloc'`, but the benchmark also defines `varying-first-overalloc-mild/zipf/heavy` cases that allocate poisoned tail rows via `_make_inputs`. (2) The tail-read correctness sanity check is therefore skipped for the imbalanced overalloc cases, which are exactly the ones most likely to hit tile-alignment / boundary bugs (the commit history notes nonaligned varying-last device errors). (3) Extend the `_verify_no_tail_read` gate to all `varying-first-overalloc*` shape cases.

(1) In quantizer.cpp `Float8Quantizer::set_quantization_params`, the line `at::TensorOptions opts = opts.dtype(torch::kFloat32).device(torch::kCUDA);` self-initializes `opts` from itself (uses `opts` before it is constructed) and the resulting `opts` is never used afterward. (2) This is dead/undefined-ish code (reading an uninitialized `TensorOptions`) that adds confusion; while likely harmless because it is unused, it is a latent defect and a maintenance hazard, especially if this file is being extended for current-scaling grouped support in this PR. (3) Remove the unused `opts` line entirely.

(1) The test now routes FlashAttention (head_dim>128) into the UnfusedAttention tolerance branch, which uses looser bf16 tolerances (1.2e-1 / 1e-1) than the previous FlashAttention path (else-branch: 8e-2 / 7e-2). (2) Widening the bf16 tolerance for FlashAttention by ~50% could mask real accuracy regressions in the FA2 head_dim=192-256 path that this PR is specifically enabling. (3) Consider keeping FlashAttention on a tighter tolerance (e.g. a dedicated FlashAttention branch matching the previously-calibrated 8e-2/7e-2 for bf16) rather than sharing UnfusedAttention's looser envelope, unless empirical results on the newly-enabled architectures (sm86/89/103) genuinely require the wider tolerance.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run test_kv_cache with backend=FlashAttention, head_dim=256 (infer_1 config) on sm80/sm90/sm103 and record actual max abs/rel error vs reference to confirm the 8e-2/7e-2 bf16 tolerance is insufficient before adopting 1.2e-1.

findings.claim: (1) The debug/log message dropped the '(>192 requires sm80/90/100+)' note, but the sm80+ requirement is still enforced (via the earlier 'device_compute_capability < (8,0)' filter that disables FA2). (2) Minor loss of diagnostic info: a user on sm7x hitting head_dim>192 no longer gets a hint about the architecture requirement from this message (though the earlier filter logs its own message). (3) This is acceptable as-is; optionally retain a brief note that FA2 requires >= sm80 for full head_dim range. The core fix (replacing the exact-match allowlist with reliance on the existing >= sm80 gate) is correct and matches flash-attn's own gate.

(1) The new FFI branch passes the zlib-compressed protobuf (raw bytes from zlib.compress) into ir.StringAttr.get(compressed_call_proto). MLIR's StringAttr conceptually models a string, and the compressed proto is arbitrary binary data (may contain embedded NUL bytes and non-UTF8 sequences). (2) If the jaxlib MLIR Python binding for StringAttr.get requires a str or performs UTF-8 validation/truncation-at-NUL, the backend_config opaque payload would be corrupted or raise, silently breaking Triton kernel dispatch under JAX>=0.10.1. (3) Confirm against the JAX>=0.10.1 API that ffi_lowering expects backend_config={'opaque': StringAttr.get(<bytes>)} with binary bytes accepted verbatim; if str is required, encode explicitly per the target ABI (e.g. the exact form used by jax.ffi examples for triton_kernel_call_ffi). Given this mirrors the intended JAX API this is likely correct, hence low severity.

(1) TRITON_EXTENSION_CUDA_GRAPH_MIN_JAX_VERSION is gated via jax_version_meet_requirement, which does a plain PkgVersion '>=' comparison, unlike is_triton_autotuned_alias_safe which uses separate nightly/stable floors precisely because packaging.version ranks a stable release above any pre-release of the same series (e.g. 0.10.1 > 0.10.1.devXXXXXXXX). (2) On a nightly build like '0.10.1.dev...' that already contains the FFI target, jax_version_meet_requirement('0.10.1') returns False, so users on such nightlies silently fall back to the legacy triton_kernel_call and lose CUDA-graph capture support even though the feature is present. (3) If nightly support matters here (as it did for the autotuning gate), apply the same dev/stable-floor pattern; otherwise document that the 0.10.1 floor intentionally excludes matching-series nightlies.

In NVFP4Quantizer::convert_and_update_tensor (quantizer.cpp ~line 2137), the diff moves the C++ `with_gemm_swizzled_scales` computation to AFTER the old unconditional `tensor.attr("_with_gemm_swizzled_scales") = py::cast(false)` line was removed and replaces it with `tensor.attr("_with_gemm_swizzled_scales") = with_gemm_swizzled_scales`. However the corresponding `out_cpp.set_with_gemm_swizzled_scales(with_gemm_swizzled_scales)` call (at parent line 2256) must now use the newly-computed value rather than the previous `false`. Verify that the C++ TensorWrapper (`out_cpp`) that is actually handed to the quantize kernel gets the SAME computed flag as the Python attr. If the C++ wrapper still carries the old constant while only the Python attr is updated (or vice-versa), the kernel's `use_swizzle_sf_output = output_.with_gemm_swizzled_scales` will disagree with what Python believes, causing either a mislabeled tensor (GEMM double-swizzles / skips swizzle incorrectly -> silently wrong NVFP4 GEMM results) or a hard abort. This is the highest-risk correctness path: the C++ flag drives the kernel, the Python flag drives the downstream GEMM's decision to skip swizzling; both must be identical.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run test_nvfp4_rht_quantize_swizzle_fusion.py end_to_end_swizzled and assert byte-equal SF against a swizzled reference for an eligible shape after convert_and_update_tensor path (update/inplace quantize), not only create_tensor path.

findings.claim: is_eligible_for_rht_cast_fusion (quantizer.cpp ~1792) omits the bf16 dtype check that the single-tensor eligibility predicate previously bundled: at parent line 2411 eligibility was `input.dtype()==DType::kBFloat16 && rows%64==0 && cols%128==0 && sm in [100,110]`. The new helper only checks rows/cols/arch, and quantize_impl re-adds `input.dtype()==kBFloat16 &&` in front of the helper call (line 2412 in diff). But create_tensor / convert_and_update_tensor / bulk_allocate compute `with_gemm_swizzled_scales = optimize_for_gemm && with_rht && is_eligible_for_rht_cast_fusion(shape)` WITHOUT the dtype check, because at allocation time the fp4 output dtype is not bf16. If a non-bf16 input (e.g. fp16/fp32) is quantized with optimize_for_gemm+with_rht on an eligible shape, create_tensor sets the flag True, but quantize_impl's eligible_for_rht_cast_fusion is False (dtype gate fails) -> dispatches to quantize_with_rht_unfused_helper -> the new NVTE_CHECK aborts. The allocation-time gate and the runtime dispatch gate diverge on dtype. Confirm all callers of this quantizer config are bf16-only, otherwise add the dtype consideration to the allocation-time gate or document the constraint.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Quantize an fp16 input of eligible shape (rows%64==0, cols%128==0) with optimize_for_gemm=True, with_rht=True on SM100 and assert it does not abort.

findings.claim: The bulk_allocate path (cast.cpp) uses is_eligible_for_rht_cast_fusion with for_grouped_kernel=true (row_align=128), while create_tensor/convert_and_update_tensor use the default false (row_align=64). This means the SAME logical tensor can get with_gemm_swizzled_scales=True when allocated individually (rows%64==0 but rows%128==64) yet False when bulk-allocated for the grouped kernel. If a tensor is created via create_tensor (flag True) but then processed by the grouped kernel, or the two allocation paths are ever mixed for the same tensor group, the SF layout will be inconsistent with the kernel that consumes it. Verify create_tensor / convert_and_update_tensor are never used to allocate tensors that are subsequently fed to the grouped Hadamard kernel; if they can be, the single-tensor callers also need for_grouped_kernel=true when destined for the grouped path.

is_eligible_for_rht_cast_fusion calls transformer_engine::cuda::sm_arch() twice (once for >=100, once for <=110). sm_arch() may query a device property each call; cache it in a local `const int arch = ...; return ... && arch >= 100 && arch <= 110;` to avoid the redundant call and make the range check clearer. Minor, but this helper is invoked per-tensor in the bulk-allocation loop.

group_hadamard_transform_cast_fusion (group_row_cast_col_hadamard_transform_cast_fusion.cu ~1424) now reads use_swizzle_sf_output from output_list[0] and NVTE_CHECKs the rest agree. Good, but the check only enforces the with_gemm_swizzled_scales flag agreement at the kernel entry; it does not verify that every tensor's shape actually satisfies the grouped-kernel row%128==0 requirement that this flag implies. The cast.cpp gate is what guarantees that, so if any other (non-bulk) caller sets the flag True on a group with an ineligible tensor, the kernel will proceed with swizzled output on a shape that violates first_logical_dim%128==0. Consider asserting the per-tensor shape alignment inside the kernel when use_swizzle_sf_output is true, as belt-and-suspenders symmetric to the quantize_with_rht_unfused_helper NVTE_CHECK added in this PR.

(1) In the NVFP4 SReLU recompute path, the FC2 input is re-quantized from BF16 via `_group_quantize_for_grouped_mlp` on the backward hot path, using a `.contiguous()` on `fc2_x_bf16` (`d_srelu_tensor.view(...).contiguous()`). The MXFP8 path (else branch) reuses the DSReLU kernel's returned column scale/data with zero extra quantization. (2) This adds an extra BF16->NVFP4 group-quantize kernel plus a contiguous copy every backward step for the NVFP4 SReLU case, adding kernel-launch and memory-traffic overhead versus the MXFP8 path. (3) If the cuDNN grouped dSReLU FP4 path can be made to emit columnwise FP4 data/scales directly (as MXFP8 does via `sfd_col_d_srelu_tensor`), prefer that to avoid the re-quantize; otherwise document that the re-quantize is a required consequence of cuDNN returning BF16.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark grouped MLP backward with NVFP4 recipe + ScaledSReLU vs MXFP8 SReLU, measuring wall-clock and re-quantize kernel time for the FC2-input recompute path.

findings.claim: (1) The NVFP4 SReLU recompute branch sets `fc2_input_quantizer.set_usage(rowwise=False, columnwise=True)` and `fc2_input_quantizer.optimize_for_gemm = True` but never restores the quantizer's prior usage/flags after use. (2) `fc2_ctx.input_quantizers[0]` may be a persistent/shared quantizer object; mutating its usage in-place inside backward can leak state into subsequent forward/backward invocations, potentially altering rowwise/columnwise production and causing subtle wrong or wasteful quantization on a later step. (3) Verify the quantizer is per-invocation (not persisted across steps); if it is persisted, save and restore `set_usage`/`optimize_for_gemm`, matching how other quantizer usages are scoped in this file.

(1) The new backward `fc2_alpha_tensor` uses two different formulas: for SReLU it is `dy_amax * weight_amax / denom**2`, for GLU it is `sqrt(dy_amax * weight_amax) / denom`. The added comments explain the divergence as 'DSReLU applies alpha once' vs 'DGLU applies alpha to both gate branches'. (2) This is a mixed-precision scaling factor whose correctness directly determines whether NVFP4 wgrad is numerically correct; a wrong global-scale factor silently produces wrong gradients. The SReLU branch is newly exercised (guards removed), so it is not covered by prior runs. (3) Confirm via a numerical test that the SReLU NVFP4 wgrad matches a high-precision reference within tolerance for non-trivial amax values (the added test appears to cover this, but the two-formula design warrants an explicit assertion/reference check in the comment or test).

(1) `nvte_splits_to_offsets_multi` always launches the inclusive-scan kernel with `<<<1, kThreadsPerBlock, 0, stream>>>` (a single thread block of 256 threads), and the multi-output chunk loop launches one such single-block kernel per group of up to 8 outputs. (2) Since this replaces the prior per-op device offset computation to reduce kernel-launch/host-sync overhead, a single-block launch under-utilizes the GPU when `num_splits` (number of experts/groups) is large, and the sequential shared-memory chunking loop over `num_splits` serializes the scan on one SM. For the grouped-MLP hot path this is on the critical path of every forward. (3) Confirm num_splits is small enough (typically == num_groups, e.g. 8-256) that a single block is intended; if larger split counts are expected, consider a multi-block scan or at least document the single-block assumption. This is likely acceptable given num_splits==num_groups, but worth verifying.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench nvte_splits_to_offsets_multi latency vs num_splits (8..1024) and num_outputs (1..16) on H100/B200, comparing single-block scan against the prior per-op path.

findings.claim: (1) In the multi-output kernel, the leading-zero fill (`store_output(..., 0, 0)` for outputs with include_leading_zero) is done by tid==0 without a `__syncthreads()` before the main scan loop begins writing. (2) The main loop writes to `write_idx = idx + (include_leading_zero ? 1 : 0)`, so for chunk 0 tid 0 the write index is 1, never 0 — thus there is no actual data race with the leading-zero slot. This is correct but subtle; a reader may mistake it for a missing barrier. (3) Add a brief comment noting that leading-zero writes (index 0) never overlap scan writes (index >= 1), so no barrier is needed. Purely a clarity suggestion.

(1) The prefix-sum accumulates split values into `int64_t block_scan[]` and computes `prefix * args.strides[out_idx]`, then `store_output` may narrow to int32 when an output tensor is int32. (2) If a caller requests an int32 output but the cumulative offset (sum(split_sizes)*stride) exceeds INT32_MAX (large token counts * large hidden dim for byte/element offsets), the narrowing `static_cast<int32_t>(value)` silently overflows, producing wrong offsets and corrupt grouped GEMM addressing. (3) Consider adding a device-side bounds assertion or documenting that int32 outputs are only safe when max offset fits in int32; the grouped-MLP callers use int32 `split_points` (row counts, safe) but element offsets go to int64 — verify no int32 output is ever used for element-scaled strides.

(1) The public kernel-launcher function is named `kernel` inside the anonymous namespace of `transformer_engine::splits_to_offsets`. (2) A bare name `kernel` is uninformative at call sites (`s2o::kernel<<<...>>>`) and violates the rubric's preference for descriptive names in distributed/compute code. (3) Rename to something like `splits_to_offsets_kernel` or `inclusive_scan_offsets_kernel` for readability.

num_warps default changed 32->4 and num_stages 1->3 applies to ALL non-autotuned kernels routed through triton_call_lowering, not just _make_chunk_sort_map_kernel. The RowIdMapPass1/2/3 kernels (_row_id_map_pass_1/2/3_kernel) and any autotuned config that leaves num_warps=None will now use these new defaults. For pass-1/2/3 this is presumably the intended fix and matches Triton/jax-triton defaults, so the change is beneficial in aggregate. Risk: any kernel that was silently relying on 32 warps for a large-block workload (e.g. a kernel whose BLOCK_SIZE genuinely needs many threads) could now be under-provisioned. Recommend verifying that none of the row_id_map passes regress at large num_experts/num_tokens, since their grids are (num_experts, cdiv(num_tokens, block_size)) with block_size up to 1024 and they run non-autotuned. Suggest a quick benchmark sweep across the row_id_map passes to confirm no regression from the reduced warp count.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark make_row_id_map (passes 1/2/3) across num_experts in {8,64,256} and num_tokens up to 524k, comparing num_warps=32/num_stages=1 vs num_warps=4/num_stages=3 wall time on GB200.

findings.claim: In the three modified lowering sites (PermuteWithMaskMapPrimitive.lowering ~line 594, UnpermuteWithMaskMapPrimitive.lowering ~line 1004, SortChunksByMapPrimitive.lowering ~line 1730), the line `block_size = _get_min_block_size(_permute_kernel)` (and equivalents) is now a dead assignment: the grid is computed via the `grid(meta)` closure using `meta["BLOCK_SIZE"]`, so the local `block_size` variable is no longer read anywhere in the function. This leftover call still invokes _get_min_block_size on every lowering with no effect. Remove the three dead `block_size = _get_min_block_size(...)` lines to avoid confusion and a needless call. Verify with grep that block_size is not referenced elsewhere in those methods.

Grid callable correctness depends on `meta["BLOCK_SIZE"]` being present in the dict passed to grid(). For the autotuned path this is guaranteed because config_constexprs merges config.kwargs (which contain BLOCK_SIZE) over the caller constexprs. For the non-autotuned dispatch path, grid is evaluated with kernel_constexprs (the caller's constexprs), which for these three sites includes "BLOCK_SIZE": block_size, so it is also fine. However, the new callable-grid contract means any future caller that forgets to include BLOCK_SIZE in constexprs while using a BLOCK_SIZE-dependent grid callable will hit a KeyError at lowering time rather than a clear diagnostic. Consider validating that keys referenced by the grid callable exist, or documenting this coupling more prominently near the assert. Low severity since current callers are correct.

(1) In ScaledSReLU.fuser_forward the per-row `scales` tensor is unconditionally passed to `ctx.save_for_backward(x, scales)`, whereas the analogous _ScaledGLU.fuser_forward saves `scales if ctx.input_requires_grad else None`. (2) When only `extra_input_requires_grad` is True but `input_requires_grad` is False, saving scales is not needed for the scales-grad path (which uses srelu_out and grad_output, not scales), so this keeps an extra activation tensor alive longer than needed, increasing peak memory. It also diverges from the GLU convention where scales is intentionally dropped. (3) Consider mirroring the GLU behavior: save scales only when input_requires_grad, or verify scales is truly required in both branches.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Peak-memory microbenchmark of ScaledSReLU backward with input_requires_grad=False, extra_input_requires_grad=True vs GLU baseline.

findings.claim: (1) `recompute_fc2_x_from_dsrelu` is computed as `bool(getattr(fc2_ctx, 'recompute_input_from_dsrelu', False)) and bool(fc2_ctx.weight_requires_grad)`, but `recompute_input_from_dsrelu` is never set anywhere in the diff or repo (grep at parent returns no matches, and the forward file sets `input_quantizers`/`grad_input_quantizers` on fc2_ctx but not this attribute). (2) If the forward path does not set `recompute_input_from_dsrelu`, this getattr always returns False and the entire SReLU recompute branch (`use_dsrelu_reuse`, `d_srelu_tensor`, `grouped_fc2_x` reconstruction) is dead code — the wgrad path would still consume `grouped_fc2_x` saved from forward. Verify the forward op actually sets this attribute; otherwise the recompute optimization silently never activates and the newly added kernel-return checks are unreachable. (3) Confirm ForwardGroupedMLP_CuTeGEMMUnary_MXFP8 sets `fc2_ctx.recompute_input_from_dsrelu` and wire it explicitly, or add an assertion/TODO.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run MXFP8 grouped-MLP SReLU backward with activation_recompute_in_mlp=True and assert d_srelu_tensor branch is exercised (e.g. via a counter).

findings.claim: (1) The change from `grad_scales = grad_scales.to(dtype=dtype)` to a guarded `if grad_scales is not None:` implies grad_scales may now be None in the SReLU path, yet `grad_scales` is later returned in `[(None,), (grad_scales,), fc2_grad_extra]` as the gradient for the scales extra input. (2) If grad_scales legitimately becomes None for SReLU when extra-input grad is required, the scales input would receive no gradient (silent wrong training for the scaling tensor); if it is never None in practice the guard is dead defensiveness. (3) Verify whether the SReLU kernel path (dprob_tensor) always populates grad_scales when the scales tensor requires grad, and document why None is possible.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Gradcheck the scales extra-input gradient for the fused SReLU grouped-MLP backward against the unfused ScaledSReLU reference.

findings.claim: (1) `_nvidia_cudnn_frontend_supports_wgrad()` is a thin one-line wrapper that just calls `_cudnn_frontend_version_supported()` and its name uses an inconsistent `_nvidia_` prefix not used by the sibling helpers (`_cudnn_frontend_supports_grouped_gemm_srelu`). (2) The extra indirection adds no abstraction value and the naming inconsistency reduces readability of the version-gating helpers. (3) Either remove the wrapper and call `_cudnn_frontend_version_supported()` directly, or rename to `_cudnn_frontend_supports_grouped_gemm_wgrad()` for consistency; also verify it has a real use path (grep suggests it may be unused in the diff).

(1) In ScaledSReLU.fuser_backward the scales-grad path recomputes `srelu_out = tex.srelu(x, None)` while the input-grad path already computed `tex.dsrelu(...)` from the same `x`; the forward SReLU output is not cached. (2) This is an extra kernel launch of srelu over the full activation tensor on every backward when extra_input_requires_grad — added compute/kernel-launch overhead in the MoE backward hot path. (3) This mirrors the GLU implementation's recompute pattern, so it may be intentional, but consider caching srelu_out in forward when memory permits, or noting the tradeoff.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Backward-throughput microbenchmark of ScaledSReLU with extra_input_requires_grad=True comparing recompute vs cached srelu_out.

findings.claim: (1) In naive_topk_and_mask_smallk the initial index for out-of-range lanes is set to -1 (int index = (lane_id < data_size) ? lane_id : -1;), whereas the generic naive_topk_and_mask_generic and the original naive_topk_and_mask use 0. (2) A -1 index would become topk_indices[k] = -1 and later index arrays as probs[pos_offset + topk_indices[i]] / routing_map[...], producing an out-of-bounds negative access if such a lane ever wins the warp reduction. Analysis shows it is benign for the normal case (data_size>0, lane 0 holds index 0 with -inf and strict-'>' reduction never replaces -inf, so chosen_index resolves to 0), but the divergence from the generic path's convention is fragile. (3) Use 0 as the sentinel initial index to match the generic path exactly, or add an assert that chosen_index >= 0 before writing topk_indices.

(1) In naive_topk_and_mask_smallk the per-lane scan loop starts at 'for (int i = lane_id; ...' which redundantly re-reads scores[lane_id] that was already used to seed val/index in the initializer above (unlike the generic version which seeds from lane_id then loops from lane_id + kThreadsPerWarp). (2) Harmless but the double-handling of the lane_id-th element is dead/redundant work and mildly obscures the intent. (3) Either drop the initializer's use of scores[lane_id] (start val=-inf/index from loop) or start the loop at lane_id + kThreadsPerWarp to mirror the generic path.

(1) warp_reduce_sum_float duplicates functionality already present in warp_reduce_on_shmem (SUM) and the manual __shfl_xor butterfly it replaces. The existing butterfly form (__shfl_xor) produces the full sum on all lanes in log2(32) steps; the new helper uses __shfl_down + a final __shfl_sync broadcast, which is behavior-equivalent. (2) Adding a third reduction idiom for the same operation increases surface area and inconsistency (the file already uses __shfl_xor elsewhere for identical warp-sum reductions). (3) Consider standardizing all warp-sum reductions on a single helper (either xor-butterfly or down+broadcast) to keep the codebase consistent; the refactor itself is correct.

(1) In `bulk_allocate_nvfp4_tensors`, the `contiguous_data_and_scale` detection logic was rewritten. The original code tracked a running cumulative `buffer_size` and set the flag to false whenever `roundup(buffer_size, 256)` (for data) or `roundup(buffer_size, 16)` (for scales) inserted any padding relative to the previous cumulative offset. The new code instead checks per-tensor `product(rowwise_data_shapes[i]) / 2 % 256 != 0` and `product(rowwise_scale_shapes[i]) % 16 != 0` in isolation. These are NOT equivalent: the original condition is about whether the *accumulated* offset was already aligned when the next tensor started, whereas the new condition is about whether each individual tensor byte-size is a multiple of the alignment. For example, if tensor 0 has data byte size that is a multiple of 256 but the scale after it is not a multiple of 16, the running offset seen by tensor 1's data could still require padding under the old logic even though the new per-tensor test on tensor 1's own data size passes. Conversely a tensor whose own size is not a multiple of 256 but that happens to land on an aligned boundary. (2) `contiguous_data_and_scale` is a returned boolean that downstream code uses to decide whether data+scale can be treated as one contiguous buffer (affects kernel dispatch / GEMM layout). A wrong value can cause silently incorrect memory interpretation or a fallback to a slower path. (3) Verify the new per-tensor divisibility test is provably equivalent to the old cumulative-padding test for all tensors in the group (including the first, where the old code implicitly never flags the first since it starts at offset 0), and add a comment/proof or a unit test covering multi-tensor groups with mixed alignment.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test bulk_allocate_nvfp4_tensors with num_tensors>=3 and shapes chosen so data/scale sizes are non-256/non-16 multiples; assert returned contiguous_data_and_scale matches an independent offset-walk reference implementation.

findings.claim: (1) `bulk_allocate` over-allocates the base buffer by `base_alignment` bytes whenever `base_alignment > 1` (`base_byte_size += base_alignment`) to allow manually re-aligning `base_ptr` after allocation. However, `at::empty` for a CUDA tensor returns memory from the caching allocator, which is already aligned to at least 256B (typically 512B) — so the manual realignment/padding is essentially always dead work: `roundup(base_ptr, base_alignment)` will be a no-op. This wastes up to `base_alignment` bytes per allocation and adds a needless pointer round-trip. (2) In the hot grouped-GEMM/quantize allocation path this is a minor per-call memory/latency overhead, but it also obscures intent. (3) Consider asserting the allocator returns a `base_alignment`-aligned pointer (it does for CUDA) and dropping the extra padding + realignment, or documenting explicitly why the defensive realignment is retained.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark bulk_allocate for typical grouped-linear shapes; compare allocated bytes and latency with vs. without the padding/realignment branch.

findings.claim: (1) When `alignments` is not provided, the default alignment for each tensor is set to `c10::elementSize(dtype)`. The previous per-call code aligned FP8/scale data to 256B and 16B respectively. Callers that were relying on those large alignments now must pass `alignments` explicitly (all current callers do). But the default of element-size alignment is weak for any future direct Python/C++ caller doing packed CUDA allocations for GEMM, where kernels typically require 16B/256B alignment. (2) A future caller omitting `alignments` could silently get under-aligned buffers that fail or slow down TE kernels. (3) Consider documenting the alignment contract in the header, or defaulting to a safer minimum (e.g. 16B) rather than raw element size.

(1) The two `NVTE_CHECK` argument-count messages report `shapes.size()` in the error text but validate `dtypes.size()` / `alignments->size()`; the second check also dereferences `alignments->size()` inside the message even though it is only reached when `alignments` has a value, which is correct, but the message wording ("Got N shapes and M alignments") is fine while the dtypes message reuses `shapes.size()` for the first number rather than a dedicated variable.
