This is cosmetic but a mismatched/duplicated size in a diagnostic can mislead during debugging. (2) Low impact — only affects error readability. (3) Print the actually-checked counts consistently.

(1) In `bulk_allocate_fp4_tensors`, `fp4_byte_shape` now asserts `NVTE_CHECK(shape.back() % 2 == 0)`, replacing the original ceil-division `(product(shape)+1)/2` which tolerated odd last dims. If any code path could pass an NVFP4 tensor whose last dim is odd (previously handled by ceil division), this introduces a new hard failure where before it silently rounded up. (2) Could turn a previously-working (if unusual) shape into a crash. (3) Confirm NVFP4 last dim is guaranteed even everywhere this is called (it typically is, since FP4 packs 2 elems/byte), and if so the assert is a correct hardening; otherwise keep ceil semantics.

(1) The new `is_fsdp2` property caches its result in `self._is_fsdp2` on first access, but the first access can happen before FSDP2 sharding has been applied. In the test suite `_shard_model()` calls `fully_shard()` after the module is constructed, and `is_fsdp2` is read inside forward (`cache_name = None if (is_first_microbatch is None or self.is_fsdp2) else 'weight'`). If any code path calls a TE forward or otherwise touches `self.is_fsdp2` prior to `fully_shard`, the property caches `False` permanently, and none of the FSDP2 memory-leak mitigations (skip columnwise, don't save workspace, clear caches) will ever activate — silently reintroducing the leak this PR fixes. (2) This is a correctness/robustness risk because the cache is never invalidated; there is no re-check after sharding. (3) Either compute `is_fsdp2` lazily without caching (small cost: `_get_module_fsdp_state` is cheap) or invalidate `_is_fsdp2` when FSDP2 hooks are installed, or document the ordering assumption that the first forward always occurs post-sharding.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Construct a TE module, run a forward pass before fully_shard(), then apply fully_shard() and verify is_fsdp2 reports True and workspace caching is disabled.

findings.claim: (1) In the LayerNormLinear backward FSDP2 recreate path, when `weight is None` and `saved_weight` is NOT a QuantizedTensorStorage, the code does `ctx.weight_quantizer.set_usage(rowwise=True, columnwise=True)` then `weight = ctx.weight_quantizer(saved_weight)`. But `clear_columnwise_cache(weight)` is later called on this freshly re-quantized weight after the dgrad GEMM. If the wgrad GEMM (which needs columnwise/transpose data) runs AFTER the dgrad GEMM and reuses this same `weight` object, clearing the columnwise cache mid-backward could force an expensive re-transpose or produce a wrong wgrad. (2) Silent wrong gradients or redundant recompute in FSDP2 FP8 training. (3) Verify that clear_columnwise_cache is only invoked after ALL consumers (both dgrad and wgrad) of the columnwise/transpose data have run, or scope the clear to the specific weight buffer that FSDP2 owns.
findings.suggested_benchmark: FSDP2 + FP8 LayerNormLinear backward numerical correctness test comparing wgrad against a non-FSDP2 reference.

findings.claim: (1) For FSDP2, `cache_name` is now forced to None, disabling the FP8 weight workspace cache across microbatches (`is_first_microbatch` optimization). This means the weight is re-quantized on every microbatch/forward even when `is_first_microbatch` would otherwise allow reuse. (2) This trades memory (the leak fix) for compute: extra FP8 quantization of weights each forward, which is a throughput cost for gradient-accumulation training under FSDP2. This applies to Linear, LayerNormLinear, and LayerNormMLP (fc1/fc2). (3) This appears intentional (the leak fix requires not persisting workspaces), but the perf regression should be documented and ideally benchmarked; consider whether FSDP2's own all-gather already re-materializes weights each step so the cache was never effective anyway.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step throughput of FSDP2 + FP8 gradient-accumulation training with is_first_microbatch set, before vs after this change, to quantify re-quantization overhead.

findings.claim: (1) The comment states `ctx.is_fsdp2` must not be gated on `is_recomputation` because backward cleanup runs after the real backward, not the recomputation forward. This is stored unconditionally which is correct, but the parallel `ctx.fsdp2_skip_columnwise` (which IS derived from is_recomputation) is also stored and used in the recompute forward. Having two closely-named flags (`is_fsdp2` vs `fsdp2_skip_columnwise`) with subtly different semantics is a readability/maintenance hazard. (2) A future edit could easily use the wrong flag, silently breaking either the leak fix or correctness. (3) Add an explicit assertion or clarify naming (e.g. `fsdp2_skip_columnwise_this_pass`) to make the distinction unmistakable.

(1) `clear_columnwise_cache` sets `_transpose_invalid = True` only if the attribute already exists via `hasattr`. For a tensor type that supports transpose but has never had `_transpose_invalid` set as an attribute, the transpose could be left in an inconsistent state (data cleared but invalid flag not set). (2) Low risk but could cause a stale transpose to be considered valid on a later access for certain tensor subclasses. (3) Consider always setting `_transpose_invalid = True` when `_transpose` is cleared, rather than gating both on independent hasattr checks; or assert the two attributes co-occur.

(1) The docstring for `clear_columnwise_cache` has grammatical/spacing issues ('data(by deriving' missing space, 'cant' -> "can't") and does not document the side effect of setting `_transpose_invalid = True`. (2) A misleading/incomplete docstring on a memory-management helper invoked in the backward hot path can lead future maintainers to misuse it. (3) Fix the docstring to fully enumerate which attributes are cleared/mutated.

The FA4 backward-kernel-bug workaround uses an `elif` chained to the head-dim check (`_fa4_hdim_ok`): after computing `_fa4_hdim_ok`, if it is False the code disables FA4; the SM100 MLA 2CTA workaround only runs in the `elif _fa4_hdim_ok and ...` branch, which is fine. But note the `elif` condition redundantly re-tests `_fa4_hdim_ok` (always True in the elif branch since the `if not _fa4_hdim_ok` branch was taken otherwise). This is dead/redundant complexity, not a correctness issue — the `_fa4_hdim_ok and` term in the elif is always True and can be dropped for clarity.

In the FA4 forward call in backends.py, `fa_4_optional_forward_kwargs` always passes `num_splits` to `flash_attn_func_v4`/`flash_attn_varlen_func_v4`. The backend-selection logic in utils.py disables FA4 for `num_splits` on SM<100 but leaves FA4 enabled with num_splits on SM100+. If `num_splits` defaults to 1 and the FA4 cute interface does not accept a `num_splits` kwarg for the non-varlen `flash_attn_func` signature, this raises a TypeError at runtime. Verify that both `flash_attn_func` and `flash_attn_varlen_func` in flash_attn.cute.interface accept `num_splits`; otherwise gate it like FA3 does (only when applicable).

The `use_flash_attn_4` selection uses `flash_attention_backend > PkgVersion("4.0.0b")`, while FA3 now uses the range `PkgVersion("3.0.0b") < flash_attention_backend < PkgVersion("4.0.0")`. FA4 has no upper bound, so a future FA5 version (e.g. "5.0.0b") would still satisfy `> 4.0.0b` and be wrongly treated as FA4. Add an upper bound `< PkgVersion("5.0.0")` for symmetry and future-proofing.

The FA4 forward always sets `window_size` in `fa_4_optional_forward_kwargs` unconditionally, unlike FA2 which gates it behind `fa_utils.v2_3_plus`. If `window_size` is `None` and the FA4 cute interface expects a specific sentinel (e.g. `(-1,-1)`) rather than `None`, this could error or silently mis-apply a sliding window. Confirm FA4 accepts `window_size=None` as 'no window'.

The FP8 filter block was refactored: the FA2 disable line `use_flash_attention_2 = False` was moved inside the `if use_flash_attention_2 and FlashAttentionUtils.is_installed:` block (previously it was unconditional at the same indentation as the debug log). This changes behavior: previously FA2 was always disabled for FP8; now it is only disabled when FA2 is actually installed. If FA2 is not installed but `use_flash_attention_2` was still truthy (from the env default), it would no longer be set to False here, potentially leaving it enabled for FP8. Verify this indentation change is intentional and does not leave FA2 erroneously enabled under FP8 when flash-attn is not installed.

New `use_v4 = False` class attribute is added to FlashAttentionUtils but never read anywhere (analogous to `use_v3` which is used elsewhere). Verify `use_v4` has a runtime read path; if it is a placeholder for deferred use, add a TODO/migration note, otherwise remove it as an unused new identifier.

The disable-log messages for FA4 (e.g. qkv_dtype filter) contain an unformatted literal `{torch.bfloat16, torch.float16}` inside a logger message using %-style args — the braces are literal text, not interpolated. This copies the FA2 message which has the same literal braces, so it is consistent, but the message reads as if it were an f-string. Minor: it's a cosmetic/misleading formatting artifact in the debug string.

(1) The block-level partial results are combined with a single float `atomicAdd(&Coeff_buf[1], block_sum * coeff)` per block, and the aux-loss value is now the sum of `grid_size = sm_count*2` non-deterministically ordered float atomics, whereas the previous kernel reduced per-expert sums deterministically. (2) Floating-point atomicAdd is not associative, so the aux-loss (and thus the loss curve) becomes run-to-run non-deterministic and its rounding differs from the prior implementation; this can break bitwise-reproducibility expectations and numerical-parity tests that compare against the reference/torch aux-loss. (3) If determinism/parity matters, reduce block partials into a fixed-size per-block scratch array and sum them in a deterministic order (or document the accuracy/determinism change explicitly).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run fused_moe_aux_loss forward repeatedly with fixed inputs (large num_experts, e.g. 256) and compare the output aux_loss across runs and against a float64 reference; check max abs error and run-to-run variance.

findings.claim: (1) `check_shared_memory_capacity_num_experts(smem_size, num_experts)` is now called with `smem_size = block_size * sizeof(CompType)` where `block_size <= 1024`, so smem is at most ~4KB and independent of num_experts — the check can essentially never fire. (2) The error message still tells the user to 'reduce num_experts', which is now misleading because shared memory no longer scales with num_experts in this kernel; a wrong/misleading diagnostic is worse than none. (3) Either drop the check here or replace it with a check that actually reflects the new resource constraint (or update the message).

(1) The forward now issues three device operations per call on the stream: a `cudaMemsetAsync` to zero `Coeff_buf[1]`, the main reduction kernel, and a separate `convert_accum_to_output<<<1,1>>>` kernel to cast one float to the output dtype. (2) The extra single-thread kernel launch plus the memset add fixed launch/latency overhead on every forward; for the common small-tensor aux-loss call this can dominate. (3) Consider folding the final float->DataType cast into the main kernel (e.g., have block 0 thread 0 write the output after a lightweight global sync or use a grid-wide reduction) or at least document that the extra launch is intentional; alternatively write `Coeff_buf[0]=C_coeff` from the host memset/copy instead of inside the kernel.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark forward latency (small num_tokens/num_experts) comparing the 3-op launch sequence vs a single fused kernel.

findings.claim: (1) The buffer was renamed `Const_buf` -> `Coeff_buf` in the forward path but the backward path (`fused_moe_aux_loss_backward`, `nvte_fused_moe_aux_loss_backward`) and the pytorch extension still use `Const_buf`, so the same physical buffer now has two different names across the fwd/bwd of the same op. (2) Inconsistent naming for the identical role hurts readability and makes it harder to reason that `Coeff_buf[0]` (fwd) and `Const_buf[0]` (bwd) are the same slot. (3) Use one consistent name (e.g. `coeff_buf`) across fwd, bwd, the NVTE C API, and the pytorch/jax extensions.

(1) `const_buf_aval` shape was updated 1->2 to match the C++ 2-element buffer, but the Shardy rule `-> , const_buf_one` and forward `partition` still use `PartitionSpec(None)`; the symbolic factor `const_buf_one` name now describes a size-2 (not size-1) dimension. (2) This is only a stale/misleading name (functionality is unaffected since the dim stays replicated), but the name `const_buf_one` implies size 1 and can mislead future maintainers. (3) Rename the Shardy factor to something size-agnostic (e.g. `const_buf_dim`).

(1) The workspace helpers `calc_aligned_size(...)` and `calc_aligned_pointers(...)`, plus `#include <vector>`, are defined at global scope in a header (`standalone_topk.cuh`), not inside any namespace. (2) This is namespace pollution: any translation unit that includes this header pulls these very generic free-function names into the global namespace, risking ODR/redefinition clashes with identically named helpers elsewhere (the same class of issue the PR explicitly set out to fix by moving WARP_SIZE/WARP_BITS/etc into `namespace nv`). Grep confirms these symbols exist nowhere else in the tree, i.e. they were newly introduced here. (3) Move `calc_aligned_size`/`calc_aligned_pointers` into `namespace nv` (or an internal detail namespace) as was done for the constants, or mark them `static`/anonymous-namespace to give them internal linkage.

(1) `calc_buf_len` computes the candidate buffer length via floating-point division: `constexpr float ratio = 128; return len / ratio;` where the return type is the integer index type `IdxT`. (2) Routing `len` through `float` loses exact integer representation once `len` exceeds 2^24 (~16.7M); the resulting truncated `buf_len` can differ from the intended `len/128` and, more importantly, the value is later compared against `previous_len` to decide whether to reuse/allocate candidate buffers. An off-by-a-few buffer length from float rounding at large row lengths can under-size the `out_buf`/`out_idx_buf` allocations, leading to out-of-bounds writes for very long sequences. (3) Use integer division (`return len / 128;` or `return len / static_cast<IdxT>(ratio);`) so the buffer sizing is exact and matches the allocation math.

(1) `standalone_topk.cuh` retains large blocks of commented-out dead code (e.g. the `scan_warp_version` `is_one_in_warp` fast-path and the `// return len;` alternate in `calc_buf_len`), plus vendored comments referencing RAFT paths not present in TE. (2) Dead complexity and stale comments referencing another codebase make the kernel harder to maintain and can mislead future readers about the intended algorithm. (3) Remove the commented-out branches or convert them into a documented TODO if the fast-path is intended future work.

(1) `nv::topk::round(int num, int round_value)` is a non-templated `__host__ __device__` free function whose name collides with the standard `round`/`std::round`. (2) With `--expt-relaxed-constexpr` and unqualified calls inside the header, name lookup for `round` can become ambiguous or accidentally resolve to the math builtin, and the name does not describe what it does (it is a round-up-to-multiple, i.e. `alignTo`). (3) Rename to something descriptive like `round_up_to_multiple` (or reuse the existing `alignTo` helper) to avoid shadowing and clarify intent.

(1) `vectorized_process` reinterpret-casts the input pointer to `WideT` (float4) and reads via a `union { WideT scalar; T array[]; }`, which the code itself flags with `// TODO: it's UB`. (2) Type-punning through a union member that was not the last written is undefined behavior in C++; while it works on current NVCC, it is a latent portability/miscompile risk especially under aggressive `-O3` and `--use_fast_math`-adjacent flags. (3) Use `memcpy`-based punning or `__ldg`/vector load intrinsics with explicit reinterpretation to remove the UB, or at minimum document the compiler/ABI assumption being relied on.

(1) This PR removes the `@torch.compile(fullgraph=True)` decorator from `rearrange_mixed_qkv` to fix a CUDA graph capture failure (a nested torch.compile inside an already-captured region). (2) Impact/risk: the docstring (retained in the pre-change file) explicitly states the decorator's purpose was to fuse the three split/rearrange/contiguous operations into a single Triton copy kernel instead of emitting three separate copy kernels; removing it means the method now runs in eager mode with additional kernel launches and materialization overhead on the decode/prefill fast path. This is a deliberate correctness-for-performance tradeoff (CUDA graph compatibility > kernel fusion), which is the right call, but there is a residual perf regression. (3) Fix/follow-up: verify the containing forward path is itself compiled by an outer torch.compile/CUDA-graph region so that these ops still get fused at the graph level; if not, consider fusing the split+rearrange+contiguous into a single custom op or the surrounding compiled region so the fusion benefit is not lost. Also update the now-stale docstring which still describes torch.compile emitting a single fused kernel.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark GDN decode/prefill throughput (tokens/s) for Qwen3-Next / Qwen3.5 before vs after removing the decorator, and confirm CUDA graph capture now succeeds.

findings.claim: (1) The docstring of `rearrange_mixed_qkv` still asserts that 'torch.compile emits one Triton copy kernel instead of three separate' — but the `@torch.compile(fullgraph=True)` decorator has just been removed, so this comment is now stale/misleading. (2) A wrong docstring is worse than none: future readers may re-add the decorator or reason about fusion that no longer happens, potentially reintroducing the CUDA graph capture failure this PR fixes. (3) Fix: update the docstring to remove the torch.compile fusion claim and instead note that the decorator was intentionally removed to allow CUDA graph capture (referencing PR #42070).

(1) In `indexer_k_quant_and_cache_kernel`, the K data offset was correctly switched to `block_idx * cache_block_stride` (block stride from `kv_cache.stride(0)`), but the scale-region offset in `dst_scale_idx` still uses `block_idx * cache_block_stride + cache_block_size * head_dim + ...`. The scale-region base `cache_block_size * head_dim` is a fixed intra-block offset assuming the block payload is exactly `cache_block_size * head_dim` fp8 elements packed contiguously, then scales. This is correct for the contiguous-per-layer case, and since `cache_block_stride` now covers the full inter-block gap, the per-block scale sub-region is still addressed correctly. (2) The risk: if a cross-layer view has `stride(0)` inflated but the scale region within a block does not sit at exactly `cache_block_size*head_dim` fp8 elems past the K start (e.g. layout with interleaving), scales would land in the wrong place, silently corrupting dequant. (3) Verify with the new test `test_indexer_k_quant_and_cache_into_unified_slot_view` that the scale region within the strided view matches the contiguous reference bit-exactly (the test appears to cover this). Given the test coverage this is likely correct; flagging to confirm the scale offset was validated alongside the data offset.

(1) `_page_stride(buf, page_size)` unflattens a 3D buffer via `buf.unflatten(-3, (-1, page_size))` then reads `buf.stride(-4)`. This assumes the 3D buffer's token dimension (dim -3) is evenly divisible by `page_size` and that pages are laid out contiguously along that dim so that the unflatten produces a valid page stride. (2) If a 3D buffer is itself a strided/non-contiguous view (unlikely here since the callers pass `k_buffer`/`v_buffer` from the cache), `unflatten` could fail or produce a misleading stride, and the resulting page stride would silently mis-address. (3) Add an assertion that `buf.size(-3) % page_size == 0` (and ideally that dim -3 is contiguous relative to the page grouping) inside `_page_stride`, or document the precondition. This is defensive; the current callers seem safe.

(1) The identical `get_kv_cache_stride_order` override returning `(1, 0, 2, 3)` / `(0, 1, 2)` is copy-pasted verbatim into five backends (cutlass_mla, flashattn_mla, flashinfer_mla, flashmla, triton_mla). (2) This duplication is a maintenance/consistency hazard: a future fix to the stride order (or the cross-layer opt-in semantics) must be applied in five places, and it is easy for one backend to drift. (3) Consider hoisting a shared mixin or a classmethod on `MLACommonBackend` like `supports_cross_layer_kv = True` that flips the base method's return, so each backend opts in with a single flag rather than duplicating the permutation logic.

This is the correct fix for a double-reduce bug. `output_is_reduced()` returning `False` in both FlashInfer NVLink backends was incorrect: the finalize step calls a combine kernel (`moe_alltoall.combine` in one-sided; `mnnvl_moe_alltoallv_combine` in two-sided) that performs the all-to-all combine and reduction of top-k expert outputs across EP ranks. When `output_is_reduced()` returned `False`, `MoERunner._maybe_reduce_final_output`/`_maybe_reduce_shared_expert_output` would perform an ADDITIONAL `tensor_model_parallel_all_reduce` on the already-reduced output (double-reduce), producing numerically wrong MoE outputs (roughly the correct result multiplied by TP/EP world size on the redundantly-summed contributions). Setting to `True` matches the convention used by DeepEP-HT and MoRI backends, whose combine kernels similarly reduce and correctly return `True`. Fix is correct as applied. Verify the combine kernels indeed reduce across all involved ranks (not just concat) with a small TP/EP MoE correctness test.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Numerical correctness test: run a MoE layer with FlashInferNVLink one-sided/two-sided backends under TP>1/EP>1 and compare output against a reference (non-distributed) MoE to confirm no residual scaling factor from a redundant all-reduce.

findings.claim: Same double-reduce fix applied to the two-sided backend `finalize`, which calls `flashinfer_alltoall_combine` -> `MnnvlMoe.mnnvl_moe_alltoallv_combine`. That combine already reduces top-k contributions across EP ranks, so `output_is_reduced()` must be `True` to prevent the runner from issuing a second `tensor_model_parallel_all_reduce`. Change is correct. Also confirm the shared-experts path: with `output_is_reduced()==True`, `_maybe_reduce_shared_expert_output` now all-reduces shared_output separately — verify this is the intended/expected behavior for these backends (i.e. that shared expert output was NOT previously reduced by the combine).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Add regression test covering the shared-experts + FlashInferNVLink two-sided path under TP/EP to ensure shared_output reduction path is correct after this flag change.

findings.claim: (1) In launch_grouped_bias_add, row_blocks is computed as std::min(max_row_chunks, num_sms * kBlocksPerSM / col_blocks). When col_blocks is large (wide hidden dim, e.g. n large enough that col_blocks > num_sms*kBlocksPerSM), the integer division yields 0 and std::max(1, row_blocks) forces a single row block. (2) With only 1 row block covering all row chunks via grid-stride, row parallelism collapses even though there is plenty of remaining work, hurting the advertised ~4TB/s throughput target on tall/narrow-column cases. (3) Consider deriving row_blocks = std::max(1, num_sms*kBlocksPerSM/std::max(1,col_blocks)) but also floor the total launched blocks to at least num_sms so SMs are not left idle, or bound the grid by max_row_chunks first and only then divide out column occupancy.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark grouped_bias_add with large n (e.g. hidden=16384, kVec=8 -> col_blocks big) and moderate total_rows to measure achieved bandwidth vs the tall-narrow-column configuration.

findings.claim: (1) The scaled-bias kernel processes rows only up to valid_rows = sum(first_dims), but nvte_grouped_scaled_bias_add validates scale size against outputD->logical_shape.data[0] (total_rows), and the scale is indexed as scale_base[row] for row < valid_rows. If total_rows > valid_rows (padded output buffer), rows in [valid_rows, total_rows) receive no bias/scaled-bias at all. (2) If callers rely on the full output buffer being bias-corrected, the trailing padded rows will silently retain raw GEMM output (no bias added), which can produce wrong results for consumers that read padded regions. (3) Document explicitly that only the first sum(first_dims) rows are updated, and add an NVTE_CHECK that valid_rows == total_rows (or that trailing rows are intentionally ignored) to prevent silent misuse.

(1) The bias-add kernel was rewritten to load/store via raw reinterpret_cast<VecType*> instead of the previous VectorizedLoader/VectorizedStorer with masking. Correctness depends on d_base and bias_base being aligned to kVec*sizeof(T) and on n being a multiple of kVec (checked). (2) If any caller passes a base pointer that is element-aligned but not vector-aligned (e.g. a sub-tensor view offset), the raw vectorized loads become misaligned and can fault or silently corrupt. (3) Add an NVTE_CHECK that reinterpret-cast base pointers are aligned to kVec*elem_size (the old masked loader tolerated this; the new one does not).

(1) The Triton kernel was renamed from _grouped_dbias_dscales_kernel to _grouped_dbias_kernel and its positional argument order changed (dbias_ptr/offsets_ptr moved earlier, scales/bias/dscales reordered). The parent pytorch wrapper transformer_engine/pytorch/triton/grouped_dbias_dscales.py imports and calls _grouped_dbias_dscales_kernel with the OLD positional order (dy, scales, bias, dbias, dscales, offsets, ...). (2) If the wrapper import/call site was not updated in this same PR, this is a hard ImportError / silently swapped arguments (dbias and scales would be transposed) causing wrong gradients. (3) Verify the wrapper hunk (truncated in the diff) updates both the import name and the positional argument order and passes HAS_SCALES; add a keyword-argument call to avoid future positional-order breakage.

(1) The constant kMaxTensorsPerKernel was renamed to kMaxGroups. (2) This is a pure rename with matching call-site updates and improves clarity for grouped-GEMM semantics; no behavior change. (3) No action needed — flagged only to confirm the rename is complete and all references updated (verified in diff).

The three call sites set `layer.w13_weight.is_shuffled = True` / `layer.w2_weight.is_shuffled = True` on the AITER MoE path, but the `is_shuffled` attribute is only *written*, never *read*, anywhere in the repository. `grep_at_parent("is_shuffled")` across `**/*.py` returns only these new writes plus the pre-existing writes in `quark/quark_moe.py:1286-1287` and an unrelated `is_shuffled_per_token_w8a8_gemm_tuned` helper. There is no consumer that reads `tensor.is_shuffled` to gate re-shuffling. WHY IT MATTERS: If nothing reads the flag, the fix is a no-op and the underlying bug (weights being re-shuffled or not correctly recognized as preshuffled by the AITER backend after `replace_parameter`) is not actually addressed — the flag was likely intended to be consumed by an AITER shuffle/convert helper that either lives out-of-tree (rocm_aiter kernels) or is expected to be added. FIX: Confirm the reader of `is_shuffled` exists (likely in the AITER `shuffle_weights`/convert path or an external AITER kernel wrapper). If the reader is not in-tree, add a TODO/migration note; if it is expected to be added in a follow-up, this PR alone does not fix the reported bug. Verify the attribute name matches exactly what the consumer checks.

The `is_shuffled` flag is set on `layer.w13_weight` / `layer.w2_weight` *after* `replace_parameter(...)` is called, which is the intended ordering (the comment says 'Re-tag ... after replace_parameter'). However, `replace_parameter` with the default `prefer_copy=False` re-registers a brand-new `torch.nn.Parameter` that does NOT carry over arbitrary tensor attributes — only `weight_loader` is copied via `set_weight_attrs`. This means any `is_shuffled` value that the convert/shuffle step (e.g. `convert_to_fp8_moe_kernel_format`, `convert_to_unquantized_kernel_format`, `convert_gpt_oss_weight_to_mxfp4_moe_kernel_format`) may have set on the intermediate tensor is dropped by `replace_parameter`, which is exactly why re-tagging is needed. This confirms the fix is placed correctly relative to `replace_parameter`. RISK: in the unquantized path, `_setup_kernel` calls `replace_parameter(..., prefer_copy=is_weight_update)`; on a weight *update* (prefer_copy=True) the parameter object is preserved and thus retains any prior `is_shuffled` attribute — the post-hoc set is still correct (idempotent) but confirms behavior is sound. No fix needed here; documenting the ordering is correct.

In `mxfp4.py`, `GptOssMxfp4MoEMethod._setup_kernel` and `Mxfp4MoEMethod._setup_kernel`, the `is_shuffled` re-tagging is placed after the branch that assigns `layer.w13_weight`/`layer.w2_weight`. For the TRITON_BACKENDS branch the weights are assigned directly (`layer.w13_weight = w13`) as wrapped triton tensors that 'don't support .detach()'. Setting an arbitrary `.is_shuffled` attribute on such wrapped tensor objects may fail if the wrapper is not a plain tensor/Parameter (some wrappers restrict attribute setting). However, the guard `self.mxfp4_backend == Mxfp4MoeBackend.AITER_MXFP4_BF16` should exclude TRITON backends, so this is only a latent concern if AITER ever routes through the wrapped-tensor branch. FIX: confirm AITER_MXFP4_BF16 is never in TRITON_BACKENDS; if it could be, guard the attribute set or set it before wrapping.

(1) The `gpt_static_inference_tp1_pp1_583m_fp8_cudagraphs` test case has its scope changed from `[mr, mr-github]` to `[mr-broken, mr-github-broken]`, which removes it from the standard MR CI pipeline, and this is done in the same PR that bumps TransformerEngine from 2.14.0 to 2.15.0. (2) This is a loss of CI coverage for the FP8 + CUDA graphs inference path — a correctness-sensitive combination — and disabling it alongside a TE bump suggests the new TE version broke this test. Silently marking it broken means FP8/CUDA-graph regressions can now land undetected. (3) Investigate the actual failure caused by the TE 2.15.0 bump and fix the test rather than disabling it; if a temporary disable is unavoidable, add a tracking issue reference / TODO in the recipe documenting why it is broken and the plan to re-enable.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run gpt_static_inference_tp1_pp1_583m_fp8_cudagraphs against TE 2.15.0 to confirm whether it fails and characterize the FP8+CUDA-graph regression.

findings.claim: (1) The TransformerEngine dependency is bumped by a full minor version (2.14.0 -> 2.15.0) via pinned git rev; the PR touches only dependency pins and one test scope. (2) TE minor bumps can change kernel/API behavior (e.g. attention, FP8 recipes, normalization) affecting numerical results and requiring golden-value updates. (3) Confirm functional/convergence tests and golden values were re-validated against 2.15.0 (beyond the single disabled fp8 cudagraphs case) so that no other silent numerical regressions are introduced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the full mr-scope functional inference/convergence suite against TE 2.15.0 and compare golden values to detect numerical drift.

findings.claim: (1) This PR hardcodes `attrs[0].val.programmaticStreamSerializationAllowed = 1` in `invokeRouterGemmFloatOutput` (and the bf16 variant), replacing the previous `getEnvEnablePDL()` gate that (a) required opt-in via `TRTLLM_ENABLE_PDL=1` and (b) only enabled PDL on SM90+ (`getSMVersion() >= 90`). This change is unrelated to the stated purpose (removing NormGateLinear) and is buried in the diff. (2) Programmatic Dependent Launch (PDL) / programmatic stream serialization is an SM90+ feature; unconditionally requesting it on the launch config changes runtime behavior for every existing DSV3 router GEMM user and removes the env-var escape hatch. The DSV3 kernel *is* built for arch >=9.0 only (per CMake `DSV3_ROUTER_GEMM_ARCHS` = 9.0a;10.x;11.0f), so hardware compatibility is likely preserved, but this silently forces PDL-on for all users who previously ran without `TRTLLM_ENABLE_PDL=1` — a behavior/perf change with no benchmark or justification in the commit. (3) Either keep the `getEnvEnablePDL()` gate, or if intentionally making PDL the default, do it in a dedicated commit with a benchmark and a note in the PR description; do not fold it into an unrelated 'Remove NormGateLinear' change.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run benchmarks/kernels/benchmark_dsv3_router_gemm.py (or equivalent) on SM90 and SM100 comparing PDL=off (prior default) vs PDL=on (new forced value) for E in {256,384}, H=7168, M=1..16 to quantify the latency delta this change introduces.

findings.claim: (1) `getEnvEnablePDL()` is deleted from dsv3_router_gemm_utils.h, but an identical `getEnvEnablePDL()` still exists and is used in csrc/libtorch_stable/dsv3_fused_a_gemm.cu (self-contained copy there, so no build break). However, the two call sites in the dsv3 router GEMM `.cu` files that used the header helper are now hardcoded to `1`, so the removed helper's SM-version guard and the `TRTLLM_ENABLE_PDL` env override are both lost. (2) Users who relied on `TRTLLM_ENABLE_PDL=1` semantics (opt-in) now get PDL forced on with no way to disable it; conversely there is no documented default-change note. This is a silent removal of a runtime knob. (3) If the knob is being retired intentionally, document it in the PR/release notes; otherwise retain the env gate.

(1) The DSV4 MTP weight loader (amd/mtp.py) still remaps checkpoint keys to `.ffn.norm_gate.norm.weight`, `.ffn.norm_gate.gate.weight`, `.ffn.norm_gate.tid2eid`, and `.ffn.norm_gate.e_score_correction_bias`, referencing the `norm_gate`/`NormGateLinear` submodule that this PR removes. (2) If the module is renamed/replaced (NormGateLinear removed), these WEIGHT_NAME_REMAPPING targets and the `.ffn.gate.bias -> .ffn.norm_gate.e_score_correction_bias` rewrite may now point at parameter paths that no longer exist, causing KeyError on `params_dict[name]` at load time or silently unloaded weights. (3) Verify the replacement structure (post-removal) exposes matching parameter paths, or update the remapping to the new module names. This diff hunk was truncated in the review input, so confirm the model.py changes keep these paths consistent.

(1) The added wait_stream fixes the cross-stream read-after-write race on max_logit_per_step[i-1] in AttnFuncWithCPAndKVAllGather.forward, but it is placed *inside* the `if return_max_logit:` block, and the subsequent `torch.maximum(max_logit, max_logit_per_step[i-1])` read is the only consumer requiring it, so it is correctly scoped. HOWEVER: the loop is `for i in range(len(local_seq_chunk_ids)+1)` and streams are indexed as `flash_attn_streams[i]` directly (not `i%2`). flash_attn_streams = [current_stream(), cp_stream] has only 2 entries, so any iteration i>=2 would index out of range for the *write* site (line 3274 uses flash_attn_streams[i-1], so i-1 up to len(local_seq_chunk_ids)). Verify that len(local_seq_chunk_ids) is always exactly 1 here (i.e. only i in {0,1,2}) so flash_attn_streams[i-1] never exceeds index 1; otherwise both the pre-existing write at 3274 and this new read-guard would IndexError. This should already hold for the all-gather path (2 local chunks), so the fix is consistent, but a shape/index assertion at the parallelism boundary would harden it. (2) Race matters: stale max_logit produces silently-wrong attention max_logit output values (~0.3 abs diff observed), corrupting downstream computation. (3) Fix is correct; consider asserting len(flash_attn_streams) covers the loop's max i-1 index.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run test_attention_with_cp all_gather + cp_1_0/cp_1_1 with return_max_logit=True in a persistent-worker process (warmed stream state) and assert bitwise max_logit match vs single-shot subprocess baseline.

findings.claim: (1) The new `torch.cuda.current_stream().wait_stream(flash_attn_streams[i-1])` only synchronizes the read of max_logit_per_step[i-1] with the write on flash_attn_streams[i-1]. But the write at line 3231 (max_logit_per_step[i] = max_logit_[0]) occurs inside `with torch.cuda.stream(flash_attn_streams[i])` during a *different* loop iteration than the maximum-read; the fix assumes the only unsynchronized write is on flash_attn_streams[i-1]. If max_logit_per_step is also produced on the default stream for i-1==0 (flash_attn_streams[0]==current_stream), the wait is a no-op as the comment states. This is correct, but the same forward function's line 1958 (AttnFuncWithCPAndKVP2P) has an identical `torch.maximum(max_logit, max_logit_per_step[i-1])` read pattern under multi-stream scheduling — verify that path is not subject to the same latent race, since only the all_gather path was patched. (2) If the P2P path has the same race, it would surface the same silent stale-max_logit corruption under warmed stream state. (3) Audit line 1958 region and add the analogous wait_stream if the write there also happens on a non-default stream.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run return_max_logit=True P2P CP configs under a persistent-worker process and compare max_logit against single-shot baseline for stream-scheduling-dependent drift.

findings.claim: (1) The PR removes `use_triton_conv1d` from `InferenceConfig` (config.py) and removes both `self.use_triton_conv1d` and `self._use_triton_conv1d_this_step` from `DynamicInferenceContext` (dynamic_context.py), but `mamba_mixer.py` still reads `context._use_triton_conv1d_this_step` at line 607 (and passes it into `_ssm_prefill` at 617). (2) If the diff does not also remove/replace this read in mamba_mixer.py, every hybrid dynamic-inference forward pass will raise AttributeError because the attribute no longer exists on the context — a hard crash on the main inference path. (3) Verify mamba_mixer.py was updated in this PR to stop referencing `_use_triton_conv1d_this_step`; if not, restore the attribute or refactor the mixer to no longer depend on it. Since mamba_mixer.py is in the changed-files list, confirm the corresponding hunk exists.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run tests/unit_tests/inference/engines/test_mamba_prefix_caching_e2e.py and any hybrid dynamic inference forward to confirm no AttributeError on context._use_triton_conv1d_this_step.

findings.claim: (1) `InferenceConfig.use_triton_conv1d` field is deleted in config.py, yet `get_inference_config_from_model_and_args` in utils.py still constructs `InferenceConfig(... use_triton_conv1d=getattr(args, 'inference_dynamic_batching_mamba_triton_conv1d', False) ...)`. (2) Passing a keyword that no longer exists on the dataclass raises `TypeError: __init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'use_triton_conv1d'`, breaking all inference-engine construction via this helper. (3) Since utils.py is in the changed files, confirm this line was removed; if not, delete the `use_triton_conv1d=` kwarg from the InferenceConfig(...) call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Call get_inference_config_from_model_and_args() and assert InferenceConfig constructs without TypeError.

findings.claim: (1) `is_chunked_prefill_enabled()` previously returned `self.enable_chunked_prefill and not self.is_creating_cuda_graphs` for hybrid models; the PR now unconditionally returns `self.enable_chunked_prefill` for all models, dropping the hybrid-specific `not self.is_creating_cuda_graphs` guard. (2) During CUDA graph capture/warmup (`is_creating_cuda_graphs == True`), chunked prefill logic that was previously disabled for hybrid models now runs, which can change control flow inside the captured region (data-dependent chunk boundaries) and lead to shape mismatches or incorrect graphs on hybrid models. (3) Verify the removed guard is genuinely obsolete given the new GPU-side intermediate metadata; if graph capture still needs deterministic non-chunked prefill for hybrids, retain a guard or document why capture is now safe.

(1) `_update_intermediate_metadata` calls `intermediate_counts_gpu.tolist()` on every update when `intermediate_offsets_gpu is not None`, forcing a GPU->CPU sync (device-to-host copy + implicit synchronize). (2) This runs in the per-step metadata update path that the PR is explicitly trying to make CUDA-graph / sync-free; a `.tolist()` here reintroduces a host-device sync each step and is incompatible with capturing this region in a CUDA graph, undermining the stated goal (comment: 'single sync'). (3) If `per_request_intermediate_counts`/`total` are only needed on the Python side outside capture, ensure this branch never executes during graph capture; otherwise compute `total` via a GPU reduction and avoid materializing the list, or precompute counts once outside the captured region.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile mamba_metadata.update() with intermediate extraction enabled and count cudaStreamSynchronize / D2H copies per step (nsys).

findings.claim: (1) `release_memory_blocks_from_request_indexes` now indexes `sa._intermediate_counts_gpu[request_indexes] = 0` etc. directly with `request_indexes`. If `request_indexes` is a Python list rather than a tensor, advanced-index assignment still works, but the removed `.tolist()` fallback means non-tensor/index-like inputs (e.g. a plain int or numpy array) may behave differently. (2) Silent behavioral differences in slot cleanup can leave stale intermediate offsets, causing incorrect prefix-cache reuse. (3) Add a shape/type assertion or normalize `request_indexes` to a 1-D int64 tensor before the four indexed writes.

(1) In the vectorized path, `chunk_indices_2d = cum_chunks_exp + offsets // chunk_size - 1` assumes every stored intermediate offset is a positive multiple of chunk_size mapping to a valid chunk boundary; padding slots are later overwritten with chunk index 0 / abs_position d_conv. If any real offset is 0 or not a multiple of chunk_size, `offsets // chunk_size - 1` can produce a negative chunk index, indexing chunk -1. (2) A negative chunk index would read the wrong (or out-of-bounds) chunk state and silently corrupt the cached Mamba state used for prefix reuse. (3) Add an assertion that valid offsets are positive multiples of chunk_size (the producer in mamba_slot_allocator already filters with `offset % mamba_chunk_size == 0 and offset > 0`, so a debug assert documents/guards the invariant here).

(1) In `_get_fp8_autocast_for_quant_recipe`, the FP4 error branch reads `raise ValueError(f"Unhandled fp4 recipe: {qrecipe.fp8_quantization_recipe}")` but references the FP8 attribute instead of `qrecipe.fp4_quantization_recipe`. (2) When an unsupported FP4 recipe (e.g. `Fp4Recipe.custom` reaching this branch, or a future recipe) is configured, the error message will print the wrong (likely None) value, making debugging misconfigured quant recipes confusing/misleading. (3) Change to `{qrecipe.fp4_quantization_recipe}`.

(1) The override/quantization decision is implemented twice: `_get_fp8_autocast_for_quant_recipe` (which actually opens the autocast) and `_get_should_context_be_quantized_recipe` (which predicts whether the layer will run quantized, used by fp8_utils padded_forward). Both re-derive `override_quantized_autocast` / `override_nonquantized_autocast` / recipe-None logic independently. (2) If these two code paths diverge (e.g. one branch updated but not the other), `padded_forward` in fp8_utils will pad/not-pad inconsistently with the actual autocast context, producing silently wrong shapes or skipped fp8 padding, i.e. wrong results only in specific TP/SP configs. (3) Factor the shared decision (is-context-quantized-after-override) into one helper consumed by both, so the prediction and the actual autocast cannot disagree.

(1) `TEQuantizationParams.parse_from_config` uses `assert len(config.keys()) == 2` / `== 3` to reject extra keys instead of an explicit validation with a descriptive error. (2) These asserts are stripped under `python -O`, and when they fire the message is opaque (no indication which unexpected key was present); also the count is coupled to `_TE_CONFIG_TYPE_KEY` + recipe keys in a fragile way. (3) Replace with an explicit set-difference check against the allowed key set that raises `ValueError` naming the offending key, mirroring `TEQuantizationRecipe.parse_from_config`.

(1) `RecipeConfig.from_yaml_file` switches the YAML loader from `yaml.FullLoader` to `yaml.SafeLoader`. (2) This is a safe hardening change but is also a behavioral/compatibility change: recipe YAMLs that relied on FullLoader features (e.g. arbitrary Python tags/tuples) will now fail to load. Given recipes are simple dicts this is almost certainly fine, but it should be intentional/documented. (3) Confirm no existing recipe files rely on non-Safe YAML constructs; otherwise document the loader change.

(1) `TEQuantizationRecipe` fields `fp8_quantization_recipe`/`fp4_quantization_recipe` are typed as `Optional[Fp8Recipe]`/`Optional[Fp4Recipe]` but `parse_from_config` assigns the raw string from the YAML dict without coercing to the enum. Comparisons work because `Fp8Recipe`/`Fp4Recipe` subclass `str`, so `"mxfp8" == Fp8Recipe.mxfp8` is True. (2) This is subtle: an invalid string (e.g. a typo like `"mxfp88"`) will silently not match any recipe branch and fall through to `raise ValueError("Unhandled fp8 recipe...")` only at first forward, not at config-parse time; and the stored value is a bare str, not the enum, which can surprise downstream `isinstance`/repr consumers. (3) Coerce to the enum in `parse_from_config` (e.g. `Fp8Recipe(value)`) so invalid recipe names fail fast at parse time with a clear error.

(1) When `use_persistent` is False, the code sets `metadata.work_meta_data = None` but still passes the stale/uninitialized `work_indptr`, `work_info_set`, `reduce_indptr`, `reduce_final_map`, `reduce_partial_map` buffers into the returned metadata. Because the persistent path is skipped, `get_mla_metadata_v1` is NOT called this step, so these buffers retain whatever they held from the previous (possibly persistent) build call. (2) The forward-path impl gates the persistent kernel solely on `attn_metadata.work_meta_data is not None` (line 681), so disabling via `work_meta_data=None` alone is the correct switch and the other buffers are harmless when unused. This is fine — but verify the aiter kernel never dereferences `work_indptr`/`reduce_*` when `work_meta_data is None`; if it does, stale data would be read. (3) Consider also setting the other work/reduce buffers to None in the non-persistent branch for defensiveness and clarity, matching the intent of the dataclass docstring ('only populated when persistent mode is enabled').

(1) `metadata_key`/`_prev_metadata_key` caching is now only updated when `use_persistent` is True (the `get_mla_metadata_v1` block is guarded by `use_persistent and metadata_key != self._prev_metadata_key`). If a chunked-continuation step (use_persistent=False) is followed by a decode step (use_persistent=True) whose `metadata_key` happens to equal the last cached `_prev_metadata_key` from before the chunked step, the persistent metadata launch will be skipped even though the buffers were never populated for the current shape — potentially leaving stale work metadata. In practice num_tokens/seq_lens differ between chunked-prefill and decode so the key usually differs, but the invariant 'buffers valid whenever key matches' is no longer strictly guaranteed across a non-persistent step. (2) Risk: subtle incorrect attention output if a key collision occurs. (3) Recommend also invalidating `self._prev_metadata_key = None` when taking the non-persistent branch, so the next persistent step always recomputes.

(1) `is_chunked_continuation.any()` on a NumPy bool array is a host-side reduction over `num_reqs` (CPU numpy arrays derived from `seg_lengths` and `seq_lens_cpu`), executed on every `build()` call. `seq_lens_cpu[:num_reqs].numpy()` is already CPU so no device sync is introduced. This is cheap and runs during metadata build (not inside a CUDA graph capture region), so throughput impact is negligible. (2)/(3) No action needed — flagged only to confirm no new CPU-GPU sync was introduced; `.numpy()` here operates on an existing CPU tensor.

(1) The new local `step_query_lens = seg_lengths` is an alias with no transformation. (2) It adds an indirection that obscures that `seg_lengths` (from `np.diff(starts)`) is per-request query length. (3) Either drop the alias and use `seg_lengths` directly in the boolean expression, or rename `seg_lengths` itself to a clearer name; the extra alias adds no abstraction value.

(1) The rewritten `_get_transformers_backend_cls` has an implicit `None` return path: in the `if runner == "pooling":` branch only `convert == "embed"` and `convert == "classify"` are handled and each `return`s; if `convert` is anything else while `runner == "pooling"` (e.g. `"reward"`, which is a valid pooling convert per `_RUNNER_CONVERTS['pooling'] = ['embed','classify','reward']`), execution falls off the end of the function and returns `None`. Note the code was restructured so the `else:` binds to `if runner == "pooling"`, meaning the non-pooling ForCausalLM append only runs when runner != pooling; a pooling+reward case returns None. (2) Returning `None` as a backend class name will cause a downstream crash/mis-resolution in model registry lookup rather than a clear error. The old code had the same theoretical gap (it also only handled embed/classify then fell through to the multimodal/causal returns, which for pooling+reward would have returned a ForMultimodalLM/ForCausalLM name — arguably wrong but non-None). (3) Add an explicit handling or error for the pooling+reward (and any unhandled convert) case, e.g. raise a descriptive NotImplementedError.

(1) Class-name assembly order changed to `Transformers` + `MultiModal` + `MoE` + suffix, producing names like `TransformersMultiModalMoEForCausalLM`. Verify every combination the resolver can emit has a matching key in `_TRANSFORMERS_BACKEND_MODELS`. The registry adds `TransformersMultiModalMoEForCausalLM` and `TransformersMultiModalForSequenceClassification`/`TransformersMultiModalEmbeddingModel`, but there is no `TransformersMultiModalMoEEmbeddingModel` nor `TransformersMultiModalMoEForSequenceClassification`. (2) If a model is simultaneously multimodal + MoE + pooling(embed/classify), the resolver would emit `TransformersMultiModalMoEEmbeddingModel`/`...ForSequenceClassification`, which is not registered -> lookup failure. (3) Either add these registry entries or explicitly reject that combination with a clear error.

(1) The registry renames the public backend class `TransformersForMultimodalLM` to `TransformersMultiModalForCausalLM` and removes `TransformersMoEForMultimodalLM`; the `Emu3ForConditionalGeneration` mapping is also repointed. (2) Users or external code referencing the old architecture string `TransformersForMultimodalLM` / `TransformersMoEForMultimodalLM` (e.g. via `--model-impl transformers` with an explicit architecture override, or saved configs) will break with no deprecation alias. (3) Consider keeping a backward-compat alias mapping old names to the new classes, or document the rename in the migration notes.

(1) `hf_config` and `hf_text_config` are now declared as dataclass `field(init=False)` fields whereas previously they were only set via `setattr` in `__post_init__`. This is functionally a formalization, but declaring them as fields (with no default) means the dataclass now expects them to be assigned before first access; any code path that constructs `ModelConfig` and reads `hf_config` before `__post_init__` assigns it (e.g. via `compute_hash` or a subclass) would now hit an AttributeError on an undefined field rather than the prior lazily-set attribute. (2) Low risk but worth confirming all construction paths still assign these in `__post_init__` before use. (3) Verify no serialization/asdict path now includes these non-init fields unexpectedly (pydantic dataclass may attempt to validate them).

(1) The new cpu_attn_has_isa("rvv") returns true only for __riscv_v_min_vlen == 128, but the RVV attention kernel is actually compiled whenever __riscv_v_min_vlen == 128 OR == 256 (see csrc/cpu/cpu_attn_rvv.hpp lines 14-15 and csrc/cpu/generate_cpu_attn_dispatch.py lines 155-156, which both use `(__riscv_v_min_vlen == 128 || __riscv_v_min_vlen == 256)`). (2) This makes the fix incomplete for its own stated goal: on VLEN=256 hardware that reports no zvl<N>b flag in /proc/cpuinfo, the Python _riscv_supports_rvv() falls through to this compile-time check, which returns false even though the RVV kernel exists and is correct. Result is a silent fallback to the much slower generic VEC path on exactly the class of hardware this PR intends to support. (3) Change the condition to `#if defined(__riscv) && defined(__riscv_v_min_vlen) && (__riscv_v_min_vlen == 128 || __riscv_v_min_vlen == 256)` to match the kernel's actual compilation guard.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a RISC-V VLEN=256 box whose /proc/cpuinfo lacks zvl256b, confirm _get_attn_isa selects 'rvv' and measure CPU attention throughput vs the 'vec' fallback path.

findings.claim: (1) The docstring on _riscv_supports_rvv() (unchanged by this PR) still says the RVV path 'is compiled whenever __riscv_v_min_vlen is defined' and that we 'check that at least one supported zvl<N>b is advertised', which no longer matches the new three-branch logic (512/1024 -> no, 128/256 -> yes, otherwise delegate to C++). (2) A stale/misleading docstring is worse than none: it contradicts the new fallback behavior and the actual VLEN==128||256 compile guard, misleading future maintainers about when RVV is available. (3) Update the docstring to describe the VLEN=128/256 support window and the C++ delegation fallback for hardware without zvl hints.

(1) `import torch` is placed inside _riscv_supports_rvv() and the module already imports torch at top level (line 10), making the local import redundant. (2) Minor: adds needless indirection; the module-level torch is guaranteed present. (3) Use the module-level torch and call torch.ops._C.cpu_attn_has_isa directly; the try/except around the ops call is still warranted (op may be unregistered on non-RVV builds), but the import itself can be dropped. Note _riscv_supports_rvv is lru_cached so runtime cost is negligible either way.

In handle_event, the `session.update` branch calls `self._check_model(event["model"])` but discards the returned ErrorResponse, then unconditionally sets `self._is_model_validated = True`. (1) An unsupported/invalid model is silently accepted and marked validated; no error is sent to the client. (2) This defeats the entire model-validation guard: downstream `input_audio_buffer.commit` will proceed to generation with an invalid model, producing an opaque failure or wrong-model transcription. (3) Fix: capture the return value, e.g. `err = self._check_model(event.get("model")); if err is not None: await self.send_error(err.error.message, err.error.type); return` and only set `_is_model_validated = True` when validation succeeds.

In the `input_audio_buffer.commit` branch, when `self._is_model_validated` is False the code sends an error via `send_error(...)` but does NOT `return`/`continue`. Execution falls through to `commit_event = InputAudioBufferCommit(**event)` and `start_generation()`. (1) Generation is started even though the client never validated a model. (2) This causes generation against an unvalidated/default model and contradicts the error message just sent to the client ('Model not validated... send a session.update event'). (3) Fix: add `return` after `send_error` in the not-validated branch so commit is rejected.

The audio file-size validation `if len(audio_array) / 1024**2 > self._max_audio_filesize_mb` is dimensionally wrong. `audio_array` is a float32 numpy array of PCM samples, so `len()` is the sample COUNT, not a byte count. Dividing sample count by 1024**2 yields mega-samples, not megabytes. (1) The limit is silently mis-scaled: e.g. VLLM_MAX_AUDIO_CLIP_FILESIZE_MB=25 actually permits ~25M samples (~50MB of PCM16 / ~100MB of float32), roughly 2-4x the intended byte limit. (2) This weakens a DoS/resource guard on an unauthenticated WebSocket append path where a client can stream unbounded audio. (3) Fix: compute size from bytes, e.g. `audio_array.nbytes / 1024**2` or check `len(audio_bytes) / 1024**2` against the intended raw-audio byte limit.

The `session.update` handler accesses `event["model"]` with a hard subscript. If a client sends `{"type":"session.update"}` without a `model` key, this raises KeyError, which is caught by the generic outer `except Exception` and surfaced as an opaque 'processing_error' rather than a clear validation message. (1) Poor client experience and harder debugging for a public WebSocket API. (2) Fix: use `event.get("model")` and let `_check_model`/`_is_model_supported` produce a proper 'model does not exist' error.

The newly added 'other endpoints' loop in serve_http duplicates route iteration and filters with `if endpoint is None or path is None or methods is not None: continue` to log WebSocket routes (which have methods=None). (1) This iterates app.routes a second time solely for logging and relies on the implicit 'methods is None => websocket' assumption, which is brittle. (2) Minor: it could be folded into the existing single loop with an else-branch. Not a performance concern (startup-only), but worth simplifying to a single pass and a clearer comment describing that methods-less routes are websockets.

The new disabled-cache path calls `fi_utils.autotune()` with no arguments, whereas the pre-existing (persistent-cache) leader path explicitly passes `autotune(tune_mode=True, cache=...)`. The correctness of the new path depends entirely on FlashInfer's `autotune()` defaulting `tune_mode` to True. If that default is (or later becomes) False, this warmup silently becomes a no-op: no tactics get tuned, FlashInfer falls back to heuristics, and the intended perf benefit of autotuning is silently lost with no warning. Fix: pass `tune_mode=True` explicitly in the new path to make the tuning intent unambiguous and robust to upstream default changes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run flashinfer_autotune on a Hopper/Blackwell GPU with _FLASHINFER_USE_PERSISTENT_CACHE=False and assert AutoTuner has recorded tuned tactics (non-empty configs) after the dummy run; compare decode/prefill kernel latency vs a run where autotune is skipped.

findings.claim: In the new path every rank runs autotune independently in-memory and there is no broadcast of chosen tactics, unlike the persistent-cache path which tunes only on rank 0 and broadcasts the resulting cache so all ranks dispatch the identical tactic (see docstring 'so all ranks dispatch the same kernel tactic'). If FlashInfer's per-rank autotuning uses timing-based tie-breaking, different TP/EP ranks could select different kernel tactics, which for collective/tensor-parallel kernels can cause inconsistent behavior or subtle mismatches across ranks. Impact is mitigated because shapes and hardware are identical per rank (and this matches pre-#42537 behavior), so severity is bounded. Fix: document that per-rank independent tuning is acceptable here, or retain a lightweight determinism guarantee across ranks.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a multi-GPU (TP>1) Hopper/Blackwell node, run flashinfer_autotune with the disabled-cache path and log the AutoTuner-selected tactic id per rank; assert all ranks converge to the same tactic across repeated runs.

findings.claim: Setting `_FLASHINFER_USE_PERSISTENT_CACHE = False` as a hard-coded module constant makes the entire persistent-cache branch below the `if not _FLASHINFER_USE_PERSISTENT_CACHE: ... return` guard dead code (the cache-path resolution, leader tuning, broadcast, and load logic never execute). This is intentional and correctly annotated with a TODO to remove once FlashInfer fixes the cache collision, so it is acceptable as a temporary shim; the note here is only to ensure the dead branch is actually removed (or re-enabled) when the upstream fix lands rather than lingering indefinitely.

(1) The CPU MoE SILU activation entry was changed from the zero-cost static method `SiluAndMul.forward_native` to `lambda x: SiluAndMul(compile_native=False).forward_native(x)`, which constructs a brand-new `SiluAndMul` CustomOp instance on every single expert activation call. (2) In `cpu_fused_moe_torch` this function is invoked once per expert per forward (inside the `for i, num_tokens in enumerate(tokens_per_expert)` loop), so a fresh nn.Module/CustomOp is instantiated for every expert on every forward pass — CustomOp construction is non-trivial (registration bookkeeping, attribute setup) and this adds per-expert Python object allocation overhead on the hot CPU inference path. (3) Instantiate the op once at module import (e.g. `_silu = SiluAndMul(compile_native=False)` then reference `_silu.forward_native`) or, since `forward_native` is a staticmethod that does not use `self`, keep referencing the staticmethod directly — the comment above the dict explicitly says these should avoid instantiating CustomOp classes, so the new lambda contradicts that stated intent.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark cpu_fused_moe_torch throughput with many experts (e.g. 256 experts, small tokens each) before/after, comparing per-call SiluAndMul instantiation vs a single cached instance.

findings.claim: (1) The comment directly above `_CPU_MOE_ACT_FN` states these entries must avoid instantiating CustomOp classes because that 'would call get_current_vllm_config() before config is set'. The new lambda `SiluAndMul(compile_native=False)` instantiates a CustomOp at call time. (2) If any of these callables are ever invoked during a code path before the vLLM config is set (the exact scenario the comment warns about), this reintroduces the get_current_vllm_config() failure the original static-method design was created to avoid. (3) Either revert to `SiluAndMul.forward_native` (the staticmethod does not require an instance) or verify/document that the CPU MoE activation path is always invoked after config is set, and update the now-misleading comment.

(1) `DeepseekV4MLP` is a near-verbatim copy of `DeepseekV2MLP` with only the addition of the `swiglu_limit` argument and the `SiluAndMulWithClamp` branch. (2) Duplicating the full MLP (gate_up_proj/down_proj wiring, sequence-parallel comment, activation validation) increases maintenance burden and risks divergence from DeepseekV2MLP over time. (3) Consider adding an optional `swiglu_limit` parameter to `DeepseekV2MLP` (defaulting to None → SiluAndMul) and reusing it, rather than forking the entire class.

(1) There are now three subtly different clamped-SwiGLU semantics in this file: `SwigluStepAndMul` clamps AFTER silu (`silu(gate).clamp(max)`), while the new `SiluAndMulWithClamp` clamps the gate BEFORE silu (`silu(clamp(gate,max))`). The docstrings differ but the class names are confusingly similar. (2) A future caller could easily pick the wrong op (post-silu vs pre-silu clamp produce numerically different results), causing silent accuracy regressions. (3) Add a note in `SiluAndMulWithClamp`'s docstring explicitly contrasting it with `SwigluStepAndMul` (pre-activation vs post-activation clamp) to prevent misuse.

(1) `SiluAndMulWithClamp.forward_native` (the CPU fallback and reference path) uses `torch.clamp` slicing which materializes two intermediate clamped tensors per call; this is on the CPU inference path when `current_platform.is_cpu()`. (2) For CPU shared-expert inference this adds two extra full-tensor allocations per forward vs a fused approach, though impact is minor. (3) This is acceptable as a native reference, but consider noting it is not performance-optimized for CPU serving; no action strictly required.

(1) launch_cooperative_topk_impl computes `stride = logits.stride(0)` and passes it as params.stride, and STD_TORCH_CHECK requires `stride % 4 == 0` for TMA alignment. However, the top-level `cooperative_topk` entry only validates that logits is 2D/float/CUDA — it never checks `logits.is_contiguous()` on the inner dimension nor that stride(1)==1. The TMA bulk loads (`cp.async.bulk`) treat each row as a flat contiguous float run of `stride` elements starting at row*stride; if the caller ever passes a logits tensor whose columns are non-unit-strided (e.g. a transposed/sliced view), the kernel silently reads the wrong elements. (2) Silent wrong topk indices -> wrong sparse-attention token selection, hard to detect. (3) Add an explicit `STD_TORCH_CHECK(logits.stride(1) == 1, ...)` (row-major contiguity along the sequence dim) alongside the existing stride%4 check.

(1) `launch_cooperative_topk_impl` takes `int64_t max_seq_len` and immediately discards it with `(void)max_seq_len; // Kept for signature parity with persistent_topk.` The op-level `cooperative_topk` signature also threads `max_seq_len` through only to drop it. (2) A parameter accepted purely for signature parity but never used is dead surface area that invites callers to believe it affects behavior (e.g. bounding CTA early-exit as persistent_topk does). (3) This is documented as intentional parity, so acceptable, but consider dropping the argument from the cooperative path or adding a TODO clarifying whether cooperative_topk should also honor max_seq_len for early exit like persistent_topk.

(1) The output-collection code relies on `static_assert(kAboveMask >= TopK)` and `static_assert(kAboveMask >= kMaxSinglePassPerBlock)` to prove the 16-bit packing of per-block counts cannot overflow, but there is no runtime guard that a block's above-threshold count `la` (counter_gt) stays < 65536. The invariant holds only because the histogram threshold guarantees above-count < TopK (<=2048); this is a non-obvious cross-function invariant. (2) If a future change to threshold selection allowed above-count to exceed the packed field width, `(le<<16)|la` would corrupt the equal-count field silently. (3) Add a brief comment at the packing site asserting `la < kAboveMask` is guaranteed by find_threshold, or a debug-only assert, to make the invariant explicit and reviewable.

(1) The cooperative_topk kernel is only compiled when `COOPERATIVE_TOPK_ARCHS` is non-empty (SM9.0a/10.x/12.x with CUDA>=12.0) and gated behind `-DVLLM_ENABLE_COOPERATIVE_TOPK=1`. The op `cooperative_topk` is still declared/registered unconditionally in ops.h/torch_bindings, but its body is compiled out for unsupported archs. (2) If the Python dispatch (sparse_attn_indexer.py decode path) is switched to call `cooperative_topk` on any CUDA device without checking the compile-time capability, users on SM8.x / older CUDA toolkits would hit a link/registration error or a stub. (3) Verify the Python caller (not fully shown in diff) gates the cooperative_topk call on the same arch/enable condition, or that it falls back to persistent_topk; add a runtime capability check mirroring the CMake gating.

(1) The new attribute is annotated `self.ca_comm: None = None`, using the literal type `None` (i.e. `NoneType`) as the declared type rather than a proper optional type. (2) This is inconsistent with `CudaCommunicator`, where `self.ca_comm: CustomAllreduce | None = None`; a `None`-only annotation is confusing and would break static typing if XPU later assigns a real allreduce object here. (3) Prefer `self.ca_comm = None` (untyped) or annotate as `CustomAllreduce | None` for parity with CudaCommunicator, documenting that XPU has no custom allreduce yet.

(1) The PR removes the branch that disabled `cudagraph_mode` when `parallel_config.world_size_across_dp > 1` ("XPU Graph doesn't support capture communication ops"). (2) This is a behavior change: multi-DP XPU runs that previously ran with cudagraph disabled will now attempt graph capture. If XPU graph capture of communication ops is still unsupported at runtime, this can cause capture failures or silently incorrect collective results in multi-DP deployments. (3) Confirm that XPU graph now supports capturing communication ops (the removal presupposes this); otherwise retain a guard. Since this changes default behavior for existing multi-DP XPU users, ensure it is documented/release-noted.

(1) The PR also removes the fallback that forced FLASH_ATTN to PIECEWISE cudagraph mode on XPU ("FMHA sycl-tla kernels cannot be captured with XPU graphs"). (2) Combined with the flash_attn.py change marking XPU FLASH_ATTN as `AttentionCGSupport.ALWAYS`, XPU FLASH_ATTN can now be captured in FULL cudagraph mode. If the sycl-tla FMHA kernels are still not fully capturable for all shapes, this could regress correctness/stability for XPU FLASH_ATTN users. (3) Verify the sycl-tla kernels are now capturable under FULL cudagraph before relying on ALWAYS support; otherwise keep a PIECEWISE constraint.

(1) `_cudagraph_support` is computed at class-definition (import) time as `ALWAYS if get_flash_attn_version() == 3 or current_platform.is_xpu() else UNIFORM_BATCH`. The preceding comment block extensively documents FA2 vs FA3 rationale but does not explain why XPU is granted `ALWAYS` support unconditionally. (2) A stale/incomplete comment next to a subtle cudagraph capability flag can mislead future maintainers about why XPU is treated like FA3. (3) Add a short comment justifying `current_platform.is_xpu()` being promoted to `ALWAYS` (e.g. XPU FA backend always supports full graph capture).

(1) The fix uses `self.a1_scale or self.a1_gscale` as the `A_scale` argument to `moe_kernel_quantize_input`. Both `a1_scale` and `a1_gscale` are `torch.Tensor | None` (see config.py properties). Python's `or` operator on a tensor invokes `torch.Tensor.__bool__()`, which raises `RuntimeError: Boolean value of Tensor with more than one element is ambiguous` whenever `a1_scale` is a non-None tensor with numel > 1 (e.g. per-token or per-expert activation scale). (2) This can crash the LoRA + DP/EP all2all path (the only branch that reaches line 248, since `expects_unquantized_inputs` gates it) for any quantized scheme whose `a1_scale` is multi-element, and it changes semantics: even for a scalar `a1_scale==0.0` it would silently fall through to `a1_gscale`. For the NVFP4/OCP-MX emulation case the fix works only incidentally because there `a1_scale is None` (so `or` returns `a1_gscale`) and `a1_gscale` is a scalar. (3) Prefer an explicit None check: `self.a1_scale if self.a1_scale is not None else self.a1_gscale`.

(1) The newly added `assert A_scale is not None` in the nvfp4 emulation branch guards `ref_nvfp4_quant_dequant`, which requires a single-element global_scale. (2) The assert is a good defensive check and correctly catches the previous bug where the emulation classes double-quantized and then passed `A_scale=None` into TritonExperts. However, `ref_nvfp4_quant_dequant`/`_triton_nvfp4_quant_dequant` additionally assumes `global_scale` has a single element (`tl.load(global_scale_ptr)` reads one value); the assert does not verify numel==1. If `a1_gscale` were ever a per-expert tensor (numel>1) reaching this path, only the first element would be used silently. (3) Consider `assert A_scale is not None and A_scale.numel() == 1` to match the documented contract of `ref_nvfp4_quant_dequant`.

(1) The OCP-MX emulation `apply()` now passes `a2_scale=None` and defers all activation QDQ to `TritonExperts.apply`, which calls `moe_kernel_quantize_input` with `self.quant_dtype` (= `self._quant_dtype`, e.g. 'mxfp4'/'mxfp6'/'mxfp8') for BOTH a1 and a2, and no longer passes `ocp_mx_scheme`. For schemes like `w_mxfp4_a_fp8` / `w_mxfp6_e3m2_a_fp8` the previous explicit call passed `ocp_mx_scheme` so `moe_kernel_quantize_input` took the `a_fp8` QDQ branch; the deferred call inside TritonExperts does NOT pass `ocp_mx_scheme`, so with `_quant_dtype='mxfp8'` it will hit the `quant_dtype=='mxfp8'` branch and raise NotImplementedError under `quantization_emulation=True`. (2) This means the `*_a_fp8` emulation schemes may now be broken rather than fixed. (3) Verify the a_fp8 schemes are exercised in the lm_eval config or add handling to route ocp_mx_scheme through the deferred quantization.

(1) The background `_pin_cpu_tensors` thread calls `cudaHostRegister` on `self.cpu_tensors` (or `self._mmap_region._base`) asynchronously, while `submit_store`/`submit_load` transfers can begin issuing `cudaMemcpyAsync` on those exact host buffers before pinning finishes (transfers are started via `start_kv_transfers` right after `__init__` returns, and the pin thread is only join()'d at `shutdown`). Registering a host memory region with `cudaHostRegister` that is concurrently the source/destination of an in-flight async copy is undefined behavior in CUDA — the runtime does not guarantee correctness when a buffer transitions from pageable to pinned during an outstanding DMA. (2) This can cause data corruption of the KV cache during offload/reload, silently producing wrong attention results, or a CUDA error, on exactly the overlap window the PR is trying to optimize. (3) Either gate the first transfers on pin-thread completion (e.g. check `pin_thread.is_alive()` and defer/serialize the initial transfers), or document/verify that no transfer can be submitted before the pin thread completes; at minimum add a synchronization guard so registration never races with an active copy on the same region.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Under CUDA, launch many concurrent submit_store/submit_load jobs immediately after worker init (before pin completes) and verify KV bytes round-trip correctly vs a serialized-pin baseline; also measure init latency improvement.

findings.claim: (1) `self._manually_pinned_tensors` is created in `CPUOffloadingWorker.__init__` and passed by reference to the store handler, but it is appended to by the background `_pin_cpu_tensors` thread. The store handler reads this list in `shutdown()` only after `self._pin_thread.join()`, which establishes the needed happens-before ordering — however the same worker init also passes the *bound method* `_pin_cpu_tensors` which mutates `self._manually_pinned_tensors`, `self._mmap_region.is_pinned`, and reads `self.cpu_tensors`; these fields are also accessible from the main thread with no lock. If any code path (e.g. a future/early shutdown or stats query) touches `self.cpu_tensors` or `is_pinned` before join, it races. (2) Currently benign but fragile; a partially-populated `_manually_pinned_tensors` on an exception path (e.g. worker init raising after thread start) would leak host registrations because join/unregister only runs via handler.shutdown() which may never be constructed. (3) Wrap thread start in try/except that joins and unregisters on init failure, and document the ownership/threading contract of these shared fields.

(1) On the mmap path (`self._mmap_region is not None`), the background thread registers `self._mmap_region._base` and sets `region.is_pinned = True`; unregistration then happens twice-eligible: the handler's shutdown loop iterates `_manually_pinned_tensors` (empty in mmap case, so OK) AND `mmap_region.cleanup()` unregisters via `is_pinned`. This is fine only because the register and the `is_pinned=True` set happen in the background thread and cleanup joins first. But if the pin thread has not completed when `shutdown()` runs, `self._pin_thread.join()` in the handler blocks until registration finishes, then `mmap_region.cleanup()` unregisters — correct. However, note the `_pin_thread` and `_mmap_region` are owned by the *store* handler; if the mmap registration is still mid-flight and the process is torn down abnormally, `is_pinned` may be set after cleanup already ran, leaking the host registration. (2) Resource/host-memory leak on abnormal shutdown ordering. (3) Ensure `mmap_region.cleanup()` is always sequenced strictly after the pin thread join, and set `is_pinned` under the same synchronization used for cleanup.

(1) The module-level function `pin_mmap_region(region)` was removed entirely (public-looking, non-underscore-prefixed helper in `megatron/... /gpu_worker.py`). Any external code, test, or downstream connector importing `pin_mmap_region` will break with ImportError. (2) Removing a non-underscore symbol without a deprecation shim breaks backward compatibility for anyone referencing it. (3) If it is genuinely internal, rename during the same PR is fine, but confirm no imports exist elsewhere (grep the tree); otherwise keep a thin backward-compat wrapper delegating to the new logic.

(1) The pin thread pins the entire set of CPU tensors (or full mmap) sequentially in one background thread while the main thread proceeds; but the store/load handlers begin using unpinned memory in the interim, meaning early transfers run as slower pageable DMA until pinning completes. (2) The stated benefit (non-blocking init) is real, but there is no mechanism to prioritize pinning the regions that are hit first, so throughput during the warm-up window is degraded relative to the old blocking approach for those early transfers. (3) Consider logging/exposing the pin-completion time and, if warm-up throughput matters, pin in an order aligned with expected first-touch, or expose a flag to fall back to blocking pinning for latency-insensitive deployments.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end offload throughput during the first N seconds after init with background vs blocking pinning.

findings.claim: The in-flight prefill-chunk deferral in the RUNNING loop is buggy for the token-budget accounting when combined with the WAITING-loop guard `prefill_capacity_bound`. In the running loop, when `defer_prefills and request.is_prefill_chunk`, the code does `req_index += 1; continue` and leaves the request in `self.running` without scheduling it. But `prefill_capacity_bound` is set at the end of the waiting loop as `bool(self.waiting)` ONLY on a release step (`not defer_prefills`). On a throttled step where `defer_prefills` is True, the WAITING loop `break`s at the first fresh prefill, so decodes proceed but the flag is not updated. If a rank has an in-flight chunked prefill that stalls indefinitely because `prefill_capacity_bound` stays False (never gets re-evaluated while decodes keep the running queue non-empty and the cadence keeps throttling on non-aligned steps), the chunked prefill only advances on cadence-aligned steps. That is the intended behavior, but note the risk: a very long chunked prefill combined with a small `prefill_schedule_interval` and continuous decode traffic will make the prefill progress at 1/N the rate, potentially causing large TTFT/latency regression for that request. Impact: latency regression for chunked prefills under DP throttling. Fix: verify the intended cadence-vs-chunk interaction and document/test the worst-case chunked-prefill slowdown; consider allowing an already-started chunked prefill to complete rather than throttling every chunk.
findings.suggested_benchmark: TTFT and per-request latency for a long chunked-prefill request under DP with prefill_schedule_interval=4 and continuous decode load
