(3) Gate the forkserver switch behind an explicit opt-in (e.g. only when the user already set VLLM_WORKER_MULTIPROC_METHOD=forkserver, or a dedicated feature flag), or document the default change loudly; the envs.py default remains `fork`, so there are now three inconsistent 'defaults' (envs=fork, cli_env_setup=spawn, serve prewarm=forkserver).

(1) The parent-side prefetch worker reads all shards with a ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=8), and the existing in-child prefetch (`_prefetch_all_checkpoints` in weight_utils.py) also uses 8 threads; since the parent thread is daemon and typically still running when EngineCore starts reading ~10-15s later, up to 16 concurrent readers can contend on the same files/device. (2) On disk-bound or network storage (NFS/cold spinning disk) this over-subscription can reduce sequential-read throughput and actually slow the load phase relative to a single warm reader, defeating the optimization; it is only avoided for the headless (`input_address`) case, not for the normal single-server case where parent and child both read. (3) Consider having the parent prefetch back off / abort once the child begins loading, or lower parent worker count, or coordinate via the existing in-child prefetch rather than duplicating the read pipeline.

(1) The sidecar glob includes both `*.json` / `tokenizer.model` / `*tokenizer*` patterns which overlap: `tokenizer.model` also matches `*tokenizer*`, and any `*tokenizer*.json` matches both `*.json` and `*tokenizer*`, so the same file can appear multiple times in `sidecar_paths` and be read redundantly. (2) Redundant reads waste a little I/O but more importantly the dedup was clearly intended (paths are sorted, meant to be a set of files). (3) Deduplicate with a set before reading, e.g. `shard_paths = sorted(set(shard_paths))` after extending sidecars.

(1) `_bg_preload_torch` imports `torch` (and then `transformers`) on a background thread while the main thread will shortly `import vllm.logger` -> `vllm/__init__.py` -> `import torch`. The comment claims the CPython import lock 'serializes the same-module import across threads' so this is safe, but concurrent import of the same module from two threads can, in some torch/extension init paths, deadlock or produce a partially-initialized module visible to the other thread if torch's __init__ re-enters the import system or spawns its own threads. (2) A hang or partially-initialized torch at startup would be an intermittent, hard-to-debug failure affecting every `vllm` invocation. (3) Consider joining/synchronizing on the preload thread before the main thread's first torch-dependent import, or verify against the torch versions vLLM supports that concurrent same-module import is safe (add a comment citing the specific guarantee).

(1) `_startup_prefetch_weights` is invoked in `build_async_engine_client_from_engine_args` regardless of the worker multiprocessing method and regardless of whether EngineCore runs in-process or is spawned; when the engine is in-process (no separate child) the page-cache warming still helps, but when using `load_format` paths that stream directly (e.g. runai streamer / fastsafetensors / remote object stores) the blind glob of `*.safetensors`/`*.bin` reads files that may never be read that way, wasting full-file I/O and page cache. (2) For very large models this can pull tens of GB into page cache unnecessarily and evict useful cache. (3) Skip the prefetch when the configured load format does not read local safetensors/bin sequentially, or make it opt-in.

(1) The `disable_flashinfer_prefill` field default was changed from `True` to `None` and its meaning was inverted in the migration path. Previously `disable_flashinfer_prefill=True` was the *default* value and simply meant "do not use FlashInfer prefill" — on SM100 with R1 dims the effective default backend was still TRT-LLM ragged. Now the migration logic `if self.disable_flashinfer_prefill:` forces `mla_prefill_backend = FLASH_ATTN`. (2) Any existing user/script/config that explicitly persisted `disable_flashinfer_prefill=True` (the historical default, and exactly what `benchmarks/attention_benchmarks/mla_runner.py` sets) will now be silently switched to FLASH_ATTN even on SM100, whereas before they got TRT-LLM ragged by default. This is a silent behavior/perf change on Blackwell. (3) Only migrate `disable_flashinfer_prefill` to FLASH_ATTN when it was *explicitly* set by the user (i.e. distinguish user-set from default), or document that True->FLASH_ATTN is intentional and note the Blackwell default change in the deprecation warning.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DeepSeek-R1 MLA prefill on SM100 with disable_flashinfer_prefill=True set explicitly (old default) and compare selected prefill backend + prefill throughput before/after this commit.

findings.claim: (1) `_migrate_deprecated_mla_prefill_flags` emits `logger.warning_once` for `disable_flashinfer_prefill` unconditionally whenever the value is truthy, even when the field is simply at (a formerly-default) True and even when `use_trtllm_ragged_deepseek_prefill` already set the backend to TRTLLM_RAGGED. (2) Because the historical default of this flag was `True`, a huge fraction of existing users who never touched it will now see a deprecation warning telling them to migrate — noisy and misleading since they never opted in. Additionally, when both trtllm and disable_flashinfer are set, the trtllm branch wins the backend assignment but the disable_flashinfer warning still fires, suggesting an override that did not happen. (3) Gate each `warning_once` behind whether the flag was explicitly provided by the user rather than firing on the default value, and skip the disable_flashinfer warning once mla_prefill_backend has already been assigned.

(1) In `_migrate_deprecated_mla_prefill_flags`, the inner guards `if self.mla_prefill_backend is None:` inside the `use_trtllm_ragged_deepseek_prefill` branch are dead/redundant: the function already returned early if `mla_prefill_backend is not None`, and cuDNN raises, so at the trtllm branch the backend is guaranteed None. (2) The redundant condition obscures the intended precedence order (trtllm-before-flashinfer) and makes the logic harder to reason about. (3) Drop the redundant inner `is None` checks or replace them with a single explicit precedence comment; keep only the check that is actually load-bearing (the disable_flashinfer branch after trtllm may have set it).

(1) `use_cudnn_prefill=True` now raises a hard `ValueError` in `__post_init__`. Previously this was a valid, silently-accepted flag with default False. (2) Any existing training/serving script or saved config that set `use_cudnn_prefill=True` will now fail to start entirely (hard crash) rather than degrade gracefully — this is a backward-incompatible removal without a fallback path. Since the field is retained with a deprecated docstring, users may reasonably expect a warning-and-fallback rather than a crash. (3) This is likely intentional (cuDNN backend removed), but confirm it is documented in release notes / migration guide, and consider a clearer error mentioning the removal version; at minimum verify no in-repo defaults or platform code still set use_cudnn_prefill=True (which would break startup for those platforms).

(1) The doc generator's `parse_mla_prefill_priorities` and `parse_mla_prefill_backends` rely on brittle AST heuristics: matching `capability.major == 10` for the Blackwell branch and using `priority_order[0]` + `priorities.get('blackwell')` to place the `‡` default marker. (2) If the selector's `_get_mla_prefill_backend_priorities` is later refactored (e.g. uses `>= 10`, a different attribute name, or a dict instead of if/else returning lists), the parser silently returns `{}`/empty and the generated docs table drops backends or the default marker without any error — producing stale/misleading docs. (3) Add a guard that raises (or emits a visible warning) when the registry is non-empty but priorities parse to empty, so the pre-commit doc generator fails loudly instead of silently emitting an incomplete table.

(1) The `del current_workspace` after `self._current_workspaces[ubatch_id] = None` is redundant for the loop's last iteration but harmless; more importantly the `del` alone does not guarantee immediate deallocation because CPython refcounting frees the object only when the *last* reference drops — which the code correctly handles by nulling the list slot first. This is correct, but the pattern deserves a note that it relies on CPython's deterministic refcounting (not GC). (2) On alternate Python implementations (PyPy) or if a future refactor introduces another reference (e.g. in DEBUG logging that captures the tensor), the memory would NOT be freed before `torch.empty`, silently reintroducing the spike. (3) Consider adding a brief comment that this relies on CPython refcounting semantics, or use an explicit local scope. Confidence low since vLLM targets CPython.

(1) The new code always frees the old buffer and calls `torch.empty` for the full `required_bytes` whenever the workspace needs to grow, whereas `resize_()` could sometimes grow in-place within an existing expandable segment (with PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF=expandable_segments:True) without a full realloc. (2) In the steady-growth path during warmup this trades a possible in-place expansion for a free+alloc round-trip; however this only runs before `lock_workspace()` (warmup/profiling), so it is not in the steady-state hot path and the throughput impact is negligible. (3) No change needed — correctness/OOM-avoidance rightly takes precedence over a warmup-only micro-cost; noting for completeness. The paired change adding expandable_segments:True in the LoRA TP test step further mitigates fragmentation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory (torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated) during workspace growth across warmup for a large-workspace MoE model before/after this change to confirm the spike is eliminated.

findings.claim: (1) get_configured_op() changed its return type/contract: previously it returned a registered PyTorch custom op (torch.ops.vllm_helion.<name>, i.e. an OpOverloadPacket), now it returns a ConfiguredHelionKernel instance. The public docstring and type annotation were updated, but any existing caller relying on the old behavior (obtaining the registered torch op to invoke via torch.compile / dispatcher) will now get a different object with different call/dispatch semantics. (2) This is an API contract change in a module that other kernel code depends on; callers that did `op = wrapper.get_configured_op(); torch.ops...`-style usage or expected dispatcher registration side-effects will silently break, since get_configured_op no longer registers the custom op. (3) Grep all callers of get_configured_op in the codebase; if any expected the registered torch op, route them through the new __call__ dispatch or _get_or_register_custom_op, and document the contract change in the PR.

(1) In _partition_args, positional args are mapped to names via params[i] where params = list(kernel.signature.parameters.keys()); there is no bounds check on i and no handling of VAR_POSITIONAL (*args)/VAR_KEYWORD parameters. If a kernel signature uses *args or if more positional args are passed than named parameters exist, params[i] raises IndexError, or a *args parameter name gets bound to a single positional value incorrectly. (2) This would cause a hard crash or silently wrong argument binding during FX tracing for kernels with variadic signatures, only surfacing at trace time rather than at registration. (3) Add a bounds/kind check: skip or handle inspect.Parameter.VAR_POSITIONAL/VAR_KEYWORD, and assert len(args) <= number of positional-capable params with a clear error message.

(1) In _call_via_hop, full_args is reconstructed by iterating kernel.signature.parameters and including a param only if it is in all_named OR has a non-empty default. A required parameter (no default) that was not supplied by the caller is silently omitted rather than raising. (2) A missing required kernel argument would be silently dropped and passed to infer_output_spec, producing a confusing downstream failure or wrong output spec instead of a clear 'missing argument' error. (3) Either bind via inspect.Signature.bind(...).apply_defaults() to validate arguments and get canonical ordering, or explicitly raise if a required (empty-default) parameter is absent from all_named.

(1) _call_via_hop returns None when output_spec.get('tree_spec_str') is None, discarding hop_result entirely. If a HOP was recorded but tree_spec_str is unexpectedly None (kernel with outputs but no serialized tree spec), the traced graph would contain the mutation op while the Python-level result is None, causing a mismatch between traced and eager semantics. (2) Silent None returns during tracing can lead to graphs whose outputs are dropped, producing incorrect compiled results that are hard to diagnose. (3) Confirm tree_spec_str is only None for genuinely no-output kernels; otherwise assert consistency between hop_result emptiness and tree_spec_str being None.

(1) _get_or_register_custom_op calls self.get_configured_op() (which now builds/caches the ConfiguredHelionKernel) purely to reach configured_kernel._decorated_kernel; the returned ConfiguredHelionKernel wrapper object itself is unused for the custom-op registration path. (2) Minor indirection: it couples the CustomOp fallback to the eager-cache field _configured_kernel, which is only otherwise meaningful for the HOP/eager paths; keeping them independent would make the fallback path's intent clearer. (3) Consider directly constructing the decorated kernel for the fallback, or add a comment clarifying the intentional reuse of the cached ConfiguredHelionKernel.

(1) The forward always computes router_logits externally via `self.gate(hidden_states)` and passes them into `self.experts(...)`, but the PR now also passes `gate=self.gate` into `SharedFusedMoE`. When `use_overlapped=True`, `SharedFusedMoE.is_internal_router` becomes True and the internal router path may run the gate a second time (or ignore the externally supplied router_logits), unlike the sibling model Qwen3Next which explicitly branches on `self.experts.is_internal_router` and passes `router_logits=hidden_states` in that case. (2) This risks either double gate computation (throughput/latency cost) or the externally-computed router_logits being silently ignored, depending on the SharedFusedMoE.forward semantics. (3) Mirror the Qwen3Next pattern: branch on `self.experts.is_internal_router` and pass `router_logits=hidden_states` when the router is internal; otherwise call `self.gate` externally. Verify whether passing `gate=` at all is intended for Qwen3MoE.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run Qwen3-MoE (e.g. Qwen3-30B-A3B) with TP>1 and compare gate compute count / MoE block latency before and after; assert output logits equivalence vs the FusedMoE baseline.

findings.claim: (1) The new non-SP TP-reduction path was added (`elif self.tp_size > 1: maybe_all_reduce_tensor_model_parallel(...)`), and `experts.reduce_results` was changed from True to False plus `shared_expert` uses `reduce_results=False`. This shifts the responsibility for the final all-reduce of the summed (shared + routed) output onto `maybe_all_reduce_tensor_model_parallel`. Need to confirm the shared expert output is also included in that reduce and that the routed-expert output is not double-reduced. (2) If the shared expert output was already reduced inside SharedFusedMoE (its `must_reduce_shared_expert_outputs`/`reduce_results` gate uses the FusedMoE `reduce_results` which is now False) while the routed output relies on the external reduce, the shared and routed contributions could be reduced inconsistently across TP ranks, producing silently wrong results. (3) Verify that with `reduce_results=False` on both experts and shared_expert, the single external `maybe_all_reduce_tensor_model_parallel` over `shared_out + fused_out` produces mathematically identical results to the previous `reduce_results=True` path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Numerical equivalence test: Qwen3-MoE with TP=2 (no shared expert, standard config) comparing per-token output logits before vs after this PR to confirm the reduce refactor preserves results.

findings.claim: (1) `shared_expert_intermediate_size` is read via `getattr(config, ..., 0)` and gates creation of the shared expert; standard Qwen3-MoE HF configs do not define this attribute, so `shared_expert` stays None and `SharedFusedMoE` returns `(None, fused_out)`. This is the backward-compatible path and appears correct, but it is worth an explicit shape/dtype assertion or comment that existing Qwen3-MoE checkpoints (no shared_expert/shared_expert_gate weights) still load cleanly. (2) Without a guard, a future config that sets the attribute but ships no shared-expert weights would fail weight loading opaquely. (3) Add a brief comment noting the shared-expert branch is only for Qwen3-MoE variants that ship shared_expert weights, matching the Qwen2Moe precedent.

(1) The new guard computes `spec_decode_common_attn_metadata.seq_lens.max()` on the GPU-resident `seq_lens` tensor and then compares it with a Python int (`+ num_speculative_tokens <= effective_drafter_max_model_len`). `CommonAttentionMetadata.seq_lens` is the device tensor (a CPU mirror `seq_lens_cpu` also exists). Evaluating a 0-d GPU tensor in a Python boolean comparison forces an implicit `.item()`/device-to-host synchronization on every model step whenever EAGLE speculative decoding is enabled. (2) This is squarely in the hot per-step path (`execute_model`) and defeats the purpose of `use_padded_batch_for_eagle`, which was specifically added to avoid waiting for bookkeeping/sync so drafting can overlap. A blocking CPU-GPU sync here stalls the pipeline and reduces throughput for every EAGLE decode step. (3) Use the already-materialized CPU tensor: `spec_decode_common_attn_metadata.seq_lens_cpu.max()` (or better, the precomputed `max_seq_len` int field on `CommonAttentionMetadata`) so the comparison stays host-side and introduces no device sync.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark EAGLE/EAGLE3 speculative decoding steady-state decode throughput (tokens/s) with padded drafter batch enabled, comparing seq_lens.max() (GPU) vs seq_lens_cpu.max()/max_seq_len; capture NVTX/torch profiler to confirm a per-step device-to-host sync is introduced by the GPU-tensor comparison.

findings.claim: (1) `CommonAttentionMetadata` already exposes a precomputed `max_seq_len: int` field representing the longest context length in the batch, yet the new code recomputes the max via a tensor reduction `seq_lens.max()`. (2) Recomputing a reduction that is already available adds an unnecessary kernel/reduction and (as noted separately) a likely sync, with no added correctness. (3) Replace `spec_decode_common_attn_metadata.seq_lens.max()` with the existing `spec_decode_common_attn_metadata.max_seq_len` int field.

(1) `_recompute_param_aliases` unconditionally calls `node.meta.setdefault("ac_graph_id", 1)` on *every* node of the joint graph, including graphs that already have activation checkpointing enabled. When some (checkpointed) nodes already carry an `ac_graph_id` and others (non-checkpointed inserts) do not, forcing the missing ones to a constant `1` collapses distinct checkpoint-block identities into the same id used by an existing block. (2) `min_cut_rematerialization_partition` relies on `ac_graph_id` to enforce save-at-boundary semantics between consecutive checkpointed blocks; an incorrect/collided id can change which activations are saved vs recomputed, silently altering memory/compute behavior for AC graphs. The PR description itself notes the AC test showed 'recomputations selected by min-cut is slightly different'. (3) Only set the default when NO node in the graph carries `ac_graph_id` (i.e. detect the fully-unchecked graph case once up front), rather than per-node `setdefault`, so mixed graphs are left untouched.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DeepCompile ZeRO3 with activation checkpointing enabled (the gist in the PR) on both eager and inductor backends; compare peak memory and step time, and diff the fwd/bwd saved-value sets against the pre-PR partitioner.

findings.claim: (1) In `wrap_partition_fn`, `get_wrapped_partitioner(True, param_indices, partition_fn=partition_fn)` is now reconstructed *inside* `wrapped_partition_fn`, i.e. on every partition invocation, instead of once when the wrapper is built. (2) It rebuilds a closure and re-enters the wrapper indirection each call; while partitioning is not per-step hot, this is needless work and obscures that the wrapper is stateless. (3) Hoist the `fn = get_wrapped_partitioner(True, param_indices, partition_fn=partition_fn)` construction out of `wrapped_partition_fn` into `wrap_partition_fn`'s body so it is created once.

(1) The inductor path no longer monkey-patches `torch._functorch.partitioners.choose_saved_values_set` (the `get_wrapped_choose_saved_values_set` patch was removed) and instead relies on `wrap_partition_fn` -> `get_wrapped_partitioner(True, ...)` marking `node.meta["recompute"]`. This depends on the AOT/inductor partitioner actually being routed through the DeepSpeed `partition_fn` (via `patch_create_aot_dispatcher_function` setting `kwargs["partition_fn"]`). (2) If inductor's compile path is configured to use a different/custom partitioner (the PR notes the inductor partitioner was 'made configurable a few weeks ago'), the recompute annotations may be ignored, producing different memory behavior than the eager path — consistent with the PR's observation that inductor memory was unchanged. (3) Add an explicit assertion or log that the recompute annotations were consumed (e.g. verify some node carries MUST_RECOMPUTE and lands in the bwd module), and document the dependency on the wrapped `partition_fn` being invoked.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare inductor-backend saved-value / recompute sets before and after the PR to confirm parameter aliases and downcast nodes are actually being recomputed in the backward graph.

findings.claim: (1) The `try/except ImportError: pass` now imports `from torch.utils.checkpoint import CheckpointPolicy` and `_is_primal`; if either import fails the module still loads but `_recompute_param_aliases` will raise `NameError` at partition time rather than at import. DeepCompile targets torch 2.6-2.7 (per util.is_deepcompile_supported) where CheckpointPolicy exists, so this is low risk. (2) A late NameError inside the partitioner is harder to diagnose than a clear unsupported-version error. (3) Consider guarding usage with `required_torch_version` or a clearer error message when CheckpointPolicy is unavailable.

(1) `need_recompute` is redefined as a nested function on every call to `_recompute_param_aliases`, and it re-fetches `no_copy_ops = get_no_copy_ops()` (lru_cached, so cheap) but the closure creation and per-node `is_cast_op` type dispatch add overhead across large joint graphs. (2) Minor kernel/Python overhead; not a hot training-loop path but runs over every node of the joint graph. (3) Optionally precompute cast-op targets set or inline the check; leave as-is if clarity is preferred.

(1) `has_mnnvl_fabric()` is called once inside `create_communicator_grouped2` and once per buffer registration inside `register_user_buffer_collective`. Each call re-runs a full `nvmlInit_v2` -> `nvmlDeviceGetHandleByIndex_v2` -> `nvmlDeviceGetGpuFabricInfoV` -> `nvmlShutdown` cycle plus two CUDA driver attribute queries. (2) Buffers are registered many times over the life of a job (every UB region / re-registration), so repeatedly initializing and shutting down NVML on the critical setup path adds avoidable latency and, more importantly, repeated nvmlInit/Shutdown pairs are fragile if any other component in the process also holds an NVML handle. (3) Cache the fabric-support result once on the communicator (e.g. a `comm->use_mnnvl` field set in create_communicator_grouped2) and reuse it in register_user_buffer_collective instead of re-probing NVML each time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark create_communicator + N buffer registrations, measuring wall-clock time spent in has_mnnvl_fabric with and without caching.

findings.claim: (1) In `has_mnnvl_fabric`, the NVML lifecycle is `nvmlInit_v2()` ... `nvmlShutdown()` on every call, and NVTE_CALL_CHECK_CUDA_NVML likely throws on non-success. If `nvmlDeviceGetGpuFabricInfoV` or an intermediate call fails/throws, `nvmlShutdown()` is skipped, leaking the NVML init refcount. (2) A leaked NVML init can leave the driver library in an inconsistent state across repeated failing probes and mask the true error. (3) Wrap the NVML calls so `nvmlShutdown()` runs even on the error path (RAII guard or try/finally-equivalent), or initialize NVML once at library load.

(1) In the MNNVL multicast-handle exchange, the code allocates `exphndls` sized `(*comm)->nvsize * sizeof(CUmemFabricHandle)` and calls `_allgather` with globalbytes = `(*comm)->nvsize * sizeof(CUmemFabricHandle)` over `comm_intra`. This assumes comm_intra size equals `nvsize`. The MPI intra communicator is now split by `myrank / tensorgpus` (see create_communicator_grouped2_mpi change), so comm_intra size == tensorgpus (== ar2_nvsize), which may differ from `nvsize` (full NVLINK domain). If comm_intra size != nvsize, the allgather's assert `globalbytes == numranks * localbytes` (ub_mpi_allgather) will trip or produce mismatched buffer sizes. (2) A size mismatch silently corrupts the imported fabric handles or aborts, breaking MNNVL init. (3) Verify comm_intra spans the full NVLINK domain (nvsize) for the fabric allgather; if it only spans the TP group, size exphndls by comm_intra size and index accordingly.

(1) In the MNNVL multicast branch, `exphndl` and `tmphndl` are heap-allocated with `malloc(sizeof(CUmemFabricHandle))` for single handles and freed manually, while `exphndls` uses `cudaMallocHost`. (2) The mixed malloc/cudaMallocHost/manual-free pattern is error-prone (already one early-return path in the surrounding function could skip frees) and adds noise. (3) Use stack-allocated `CUmemFabricHandle exphndl, tmphndl;` since they are single fixed-size POD structs, removing the malloc/free pair entirely.

(1) `has_mnnvl_fabric` guards MNNVL with `#if CUDA_VERSION < 12040`, but the fabric-handle fallback typedefs earlier in the file use `#if CUDART_VERSION < 12030`. Using `CUDA_VERSION` (driver API header macro) vs `CUDART_VERSION` (runtime) inconsistently within the same translation unit for the same feature gate is a latent portability bug — the two macros can differ. (2) If they diverge, the code may compile the fabric path while the fallback typedef is absent (or vice-versa), causing build failures or use of the fabric enum on toolkits that lack it. (3) Standardize on one macro (CUDART_VERSION is used elsewhere in this file) for all MNNVL/fabric version gates.

(1) The launcher previously raised a clear RuntimeError when neither mpirun nor torchrun was detected; the new `else` branch silently falls through, defaulting LOCAL_SIZE to `torch.cuda.device_count()`. (2) In an unrecognized launch environment (no RANK/WORLD_SIZE set) this now silently proceeds with RANK=0/WORLD_SIZE=1 instead of failing fast, hiding misconfiguration and potentially producing a single-rank run when the user expected multi-rank. (3) Keep an explicit validation that RANK/WORLD_SIZE env vars are present, or log a warning that the fallback path was taken. This is example code so severity is low.

Confirmation that this PR is a correct bugfix, not a regression. In the parent commit `FusedMoE` (in vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/layer.py) is a factory *function* (`def FusedMoE(...) -> MoERunner`), not a class. Therefore the pre-change call sites `FusedMoE.make_expert_params_mapping(...)` accessed an attribute on a plain function object, which would raise `AttributeError: 'function' object has no attribute 'make_expert_params_mapping'` at weight-loading time — i.e. these 5 models (cohere2_moe, hy_v3, hy_v3_mtp, deepseek_v4/xpu/model, deepseek_v4/xpu/mtp) were previously unloadable. The fix routes them to the module-level `fused_moe_make_expert_params_mapping`, which is exported from the package `__init__` and used identically by ~60 other models. Signatures match: the module-level helper forwards `model, ckpt_gate_proj_name, ckpt_down_proj_name, ckpt_up_proj_name, num_experts, num_redundant_experts, routed_experts_prefix` to `RoutedExperts.make_expert_params_mapping`, and all changed call sites pass only `self` + the three `ckpt_*_name` keyword args + `num_experts`, all of which are accepted. No behavioral regression; this is a genuine correctness fix. Note the `hy_v3_mtp.py` and `deepseek_v4/xpu/mtp.py` imports drop `FusedMoE` from the import list — verified no other `FusedMoE` reference remains in those files, so the narrowed imports are safe.

(1) The docstring update correctly changes the referenced helper name from `FusedMoE.make_expert_params_mapping` to `fused_moe_make_expert_params_mapping`, keeping the comment consistent with the actual (function-based) API. (2) Low-impact but good hygiene — a stale reference to a non-existent attribute would mislead future readers. (3) No further action needed; noting only that this is the sole doc-only change and it is accurate.

(1) The trailing comment now reads `# was: megatron-energon[av_decode]~=6.0.1`, but the actual previous loose spec in pyproject.toml was `~=6.0` (the `6.0.1` was the pinned version, not the loose spec). The comment convention on the other lines documents the previous *loose spec* (e.g. `~=0.8`), so this line is inconsistent/imprecise. (2) Minor: a stale/imprecise 'was:' annotation can mislead maintainers who regenerate the pin set about the prior constraint. (3) Change the comment to `# was: megatron-energon[av_decode]~=6.0` to match both the actual prior spec and the convention used on the other lines.

(1) When a chat request is made with token-id / non-echo input, `RequestOutput.prompt` (a.k.a. `res.prompt` / `final_res.prompt`) is only populated as the rendered prompt string when detokenization of the prompt is requested. That detokenization is gated by `ChatCompletionRequest.build_tok_params()` via `needs_detokenization=bool(self.echo and not self.return_token_ids)` (protocol.py:470). The new `return_prompt_text` flag is NOT wired into `needs_detokenization`, so setting `return_prompt_text=True` without also setting `echo=True` will very often leave `res.prompt`/`final_res.prompt` as None. (2) Impact: the feature silently returns `prompt_text=None` for the common case (token-id inputs / no echo), making the documented behavior ('response will include prompt_text') unreliable and confusing. For text prompts the rendered string may happen to be present, so the failure is data-dependent and easy to miss in tests. (3) Fix: OR `return_prompt_text` into the detokenization gate, e.g. `needs_detokenization=bool((self.echo or self.return_prompt_text) and not self.return_token_ids)` (and reconcile the `return_token_ids` interaction), or otherwise ensure the renderer produces `prompt` when `return_prompt_text` is set.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Functional test: POST a chat completion with token-id/message input and return_prompt_text=True (echo unset); assert response.prompt_text is a non-null rendered string in both streaming and non-streaming paths.

findings.claim: (1) In the streaming path `prompt_text` is computed once inside the `first_iteration` block and attached to every choice's first stream chunk, but the non-streaming and streaming code paths compute `prompt_text` independently with duplicated logic (`res.prompt if request.return_prompt_text else None` vs `final_res.prompt if request.return_prompt_text else None`). (2) This is minor but risks the two paths drifting (e.g. one path handling a None prompt or multi-prompt sub-requests differently). Note also that for multi-prompt requests each engine sub-request has its own `prompt`, but only the aggregate response carries a single `prompt_text`; behavior for the multi-input case is unspecified. (3) Fix: factor the 'rendered prompt' extraction into a small helper and add a test covering the multi-prompt / n>1 case to document the intended semantics.

(1) `ChatCompletionStreamResponse.prompt_text` is documented as 'only sent on the first chunk', and the serving code only sets it on the per-choice first stream chunk (ChatCompletionResponseStreamChoice), yet `prompt_text` was added to the top-level `ChatCompletionStreamResponse` model, not to `ChatCompletionResponseStreamChoice`. The diff sets `prompt_text=prompt_text` on the `ChatCompletionResponseStreamChoice(...)` constructor (serving.py:539) — verify that choice model actually declares a `prompt_text` field. As shown in the diff, `ChatCompletionResponseStreamChoice` was NOT given a `prompt_text` field, only `ChatCompletionStreamResponse` was, so passing `prompt_text=` to the choice constructor would be rejected by pydantic (extra field) or silently dropped. (2) Impact: streaming responses may never actually carry `prompt_text`, or raise a validation error at request time. (3) Fix: add `prompt_text` to `ChatCompletionResponseStreamChoice` (matching where it is assigned), or assign it on the `ChatCompletionStreamResponse` object instead of the choice.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Streaming test asserting first chunk contains prompt_text when return_prompt_text=True.

findings.claim: (1) `_consolidate_system_messages` merges ALL system messages into a single message at position 0, but it is unconditionally called after developer-to-system conversion whenever ANY developer message exists and the template lacks developer support. (2) This changes semantics for templates that legitimately allow system messages at non-first positions or multiple system turns interleaved in the conversation (e.g. mid-conversation system reminders). Consolidation reorders these messages to position 0, altering the rendered prompt and thus model behavior for users who previously relied on system messages appearing at their original positions. (3) Only consolidate when the target template actually requires system-first (or gate consolidation behind a template capability detection), and preserve original message ordering otherwise.

(1) `_consolidate_system_messages` drops all non-content keys from system messages during merge — the merged dict only contains `role` and `content`. Any other keys present on original system messages (e.g. `name`, multimodal parts that aren't plain text, cache_control, etc.) are silently discarded. (2) For system messages carrying structured/list content with non-text parts (e.g. image_url dicts without a `text` key), those parts are dropped entirely by the list-flattening loop, causing silent loss of content. (3) Preserve non-text content parts and additional message keys, or restrict consolidation to string-content system messages and pass through others unchanged.

(1) `_detect_developer_role_support` uses a naive substring check for the literal `"developer"` / `'developer'` in the raw chat template text. (2) This yields false positives (e.g. a template referencing the word 'developer' in a comment or an unrelated string, or a variable like `developer_token` as seen in tool_chat_template_apertus.jinja) and false negatives (templates that check role via a variable or list membership like `message['role'] in ['system', 'developer']` are matched, but templates building the role string dynamically are not). A false positive means developer messages are passed through to a template that doesn't actually render them, potentially raising an exception or dropping content. (3) Consider AST-based detection consistent with `_detect_content_format`, or document the heuristic's limitations.

(1) `_convert_developer_to_system` only pops the `tools` key from converted developer messages. If a developer message previously contributed tool definitions that the template relied on, converting to system silently discards them. (2) This is a behavior change: developer-provided tools disappear rather than being merged into the request's tool list, so tool-calling may break for developer-role tool specs. (3) Either merge the developer `tools` into the request tools or document explicitly that developer tools are intentionally dropped in the fallback path.

(1) The guard `any(msg["role"] == "developer" for msg in conversation) and not _detect_developer_role_support(chat_template)` iterates the full conversation on every call before short-circuiting. This is negligible relative to template rendering, but `_detect_developer_role_support` is `lru_cache`d on the full template string which for large custom templates hashes the entire string on each miss/hit. (2) Minor overhead only; not a hot path. (3) Acceptable as-is; noting for completeness.

(1) `DelayedScaling.backward_override` defaults to `os.getenv("NVTE_BACKWARD_OVERRIDE", None)` (same as all other recipes), but `DelayedScaling.__post_init__` unconditionally asserts `self.backward_override is None`. (2) This means that if a user exports `NVTE_BACKWARD_OVERRIDE=high_precision|dequantized` globally to enable the feature for MXFP8/current-scaling/blockscaling recipes and *also* constructs a `DelayedScaling` recipe (e.g. mixed usage or a default recipe path), the DelayedScaling constructor will hard-crash with AssertionError, silently breaking previously-working training scripts. This is a global env var that a user reasonably expects to be ignored by recipes that do not support it, not to fail construction. (3) For DelayedScaling, either do not read the env var into the default (set default to `None` literally) or downgrade the check to a warning-and-ignore path, so that setting the env var globally does not break DelayedScaling users.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Set NVTE_BACKWARD_OVERRIDE=high_precision and construct DelayedScaling(); assert it does not raise.

findings.claim: (1) In `dequantized` backward mode the saved inputmats are retargeted with `update_usage(rowwise_usage=True, columnwise_usage=False)`, while in the default path they are set to `columnwise_usage=True`. For MXFP8 (and NVFP4) the rowwise and columnwise quantized copies are NOT numerically equivalent (rowwise vs columnwise scaling directions produce different dequantized values, as documented in the MXFP8/NVFP4 recipe docstrings). (2) If forward only retained the columnwise copy for the input (which is the usual layout saved for wgrad), requesting rowwise_usage=True here would either fail (`Requested row-wise usage, but tensor is missing row-scaled FP8 data`) or dequantize from a different copy than intended, giving a slightly different wgrad than a true high-precision reference. The compensating `optimize_for_gemm=False` override and forward-side `columnwise=... and backward_override is None` changes suggest the forward is meant to keep the rowwise copy, but this coupling is fragile and easy to break. (3) Add an explicit assertion that the saved inputmat actually has rowwise data before calling update_usage(rowwise_usage=True), and add a shape/dtype/data-copy assertion at this parallelism boundary; verify unit tests cover MXFP8 with weight_requires_grad and non-trivial sequence-parallel gather.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare grouped_linear wgrad in dequantized mode vs a pure-BF16 reference for MXFP8 and NVFP4 recipes across several shapes; assert bitwise/tolerance match.

findings.claim: (1) In the `dequantized` wgrad path, empty grouped splits (`m_split == 0`) are special-cased by materializing `torch.empty((0, ctx.weights_shape_1), ...)`. The comment states dequant kernels reject empty M-dim inputs. (2) The dgrad path for `dequantized` mode (weights_for_dgrad) calls `weight.dequantize(...)` directly without the analogous empty-split guard; if any weight/GEMM path can hit a zero-sized dequantize it would fail the same way. Also the empty-tensor shape assumes the input feature dim equals `ctx.weights_shape_1`, which is correct for wgrad input but is worth an assertion. (3) Add a shape assertion and confirm the dgrad dequantize path cannot receive empty inputs, or apply the same guard for consistency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run grouped_linear dequantized backward with an m_split containing a zero-sized group across all quantized storage formats.

findings.claim: (1) The `dequantized` and `high_precision` branches building `weights_for_dgrad` are identical except for iterating over `weights` vs `origin_weights`. (2) This duplicates a non-trivial list comprehension (dequantize-or-cast) and increases maintenance risk that the two branches diverge accidentally. (3) Factor the dequantize/cast comprehension into a small local helper and select the source list (`weights` vs `origin_weights`) via a variable.

(1) `use_fp8_bwd = ctx.fp8 and ctx.backward_override is None` gates the bias-grad-fusion path. This relies on `ctx.backward_override` being present on every ctx that reaches `grad_reduce`/this code path. (2) In grouped_linear/layernorm_linear the code sets `ctx.fp8 = False` when backward_override is not None, so `ctx.fp8` alone would already be False in override mode; the extra `and ctx.backward_override is None` term is redundant there but is load-bearing for any module that sets `ctx.backward_override` without also clearing `ctx.fp8`. If some autograd Function reaching this method never sets `ctx.backward_override`, this will raise AttributeError. (3) Confirm every ctx path into this base helper sets `ctx.backward_override`, or use `getattr(ctx, "backward_override", None)` for safety.

(1) The CPU model runner (vllm/v1/worker/cpu_model_runner.py::_postprocess_triton) monkeypatches triton kernels by attribute assignment on the `vllm.v1.spec_decode.eagle` module namespace: `vllm.v1.spec_decode.eagle.eagle_prepare_inputs_padded_kernel = cpu_tl...`, `.eagle_prepare_next_token_padded_kernel`, and `.copy_and_expand_eagle_inputs_kernel`. In the pre-change code, `SpecDecodeBaseProposer` lived in eagle.py and used these kernels via `eagle_prepare_inputs_padded_kernel[grid](...)` / `copy_and_expand_eagle_inputs_kernel[grid](...)` (eagle.py lines 942, 719), which resolve against the `eagle` module globals — so the monkeypatch took effect. This PR moves `SpecDecodeBaseProposer` (and the code calling these kernels at lines 719/942) into the new module `llm_base_proposer.py`, where the kernel names are now imported into `llm_base_proposer`'s namespace, NOT `eagle`'s. (2) Impact: The CPU runner's `_postprocess_triton` still patches `vllm.v1.spec_decode.eagle.<kernel>`, but the base-proposer code now reads the kernel from `llm_base_proposer`'s globals, so the CPU fallback substitution silently no longer applies — CPU spec decode will attempt to run the GPU triton kernels (breaking CPU spec-decode, which the module comment claims is now supported). (3) Fix: update cpu_model_runner._postprocess_triton to patch the kernels on `vllm.v1.spec_decode.llm_base_proposer` (import it and assign the kernel attributes there), or centralize kernel indirection so both modules resolve to the same patched symbol.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run CPU spec-decode (EAGLE/DFlash/draft-model) end-to-end on CPU backend and confirm the cpu_tl kernel fallbacks are actually invoked (e.g. assert the padded-input path does not dispatch the GPU triton kernel).

findings.claim: (1) The diff lists cpu_model_runner.py as changed but the truncated diff does not show its updated `_postprocess_triton`. Verify that all module-level symbols the CPU runner monkeypatches (eagle_prepare_inputs_padded_kernel, eagle_prepare_next_token_padded_kernel, copy_and_expand_eagle_inputs_kernel) that were relocated into llm_base_proposer.py are now patched on the correct module. (2) Impact: a mismatch between the patch target module and the module where the kernel call actually resolves silently disables the CPU fallback. (3) Fix: ensure the patch targets match the new home of the relocated kernel call sites.

(1) load_endpoint_plugins accesses plugin.required_tasks (and later plugin.attach_router/init_state) without first validating the instantiated object actually satisfies the EndpointPlugin protocol. A misconfigured entry point factory that returns an object missing required_tasks will raise AttributeError, which is NOT caught (the try/except only wraps factory() instantiation, not the required_tasks access). (2) This aborts server startup for the whole API server rather than logging-and-skipping a single bad plugin, contradicting the documented promise that 'A factory that raises an issue during instantiation is logged and skipped. It does not abort server startup.' (3) Wrap the required_tasks access (and ideally an isinstance(plugin, EndpointPlugin) runtime_checkable check, which the interface deliberately marks @runtime_checkable) inside the try/except or a dedicated validation block, logging and continuing on failure.

(1) _attach_endpoint_plugins calls load_endpoint_plugins(supported_tasks) in build_app, and _init_endpoint_plugins_state iterates the stored state.endpoint_plugins in init_app_state. There is no re-entrancy guard: unlike load_general_plugins which uses the plugins_loaded global, build_app can in principle be called more than once (e.g. examples/tests), causing entry points to be re-discovered/re-instantiated. (2) Endpoint plugins registering routes twice on the same app or duplicating discovery work is wasteful and can produce duplicate/shadowed routes. (3) Consider documenting that build_app is expected to be called once per app, or guard against double attachment. Low severity since build_app already builds a fresh app each call.

(1) run_batch.py and other callers build a bare State and call init_app_state without going through build_app, so state.endpoint_plugins is absent; the getattr(state, 'endpoint_plugins', []) default in _init_endpoint_plugins_state handles this gracefully. This is correct behavior but relies on an implicit contract between build_app (Phase A setter) and init_app_state (Phase B reader). (2) If a future refactor calls init_app_state on an app whose build_app was skipped, plugins silently never get init_state even though attach_router may have been called elsewhere. (3) The getattr default is defensively sound; no change strictly required, but an explicit comment or assertion that Phase A ran before Phase B for the primary server path would harden the contract. The existing docstring already covers this reasonably.

Frame-invariance assumption for the sliding-window clamp only holds during prefill, but the clamp is applied in every forward. In flash_attn's `_make_mm_prefix_mask_mod` and the Triton `compute_kv_seq_mask`, the clamp uses `(q_idx - kv_idx) < sliding_window` where q_idx/kv_idx are *local* tile offsets (flash) / `(query_abs_pos - seq_offset)` (triton). The added docstring itself concedes 'q_idx/kv_idx are local offsets, but their difference is frame-invariant and image bidirectional attention only matters during prefill (where local == absolute).' For the flash-attn CuTE path this is only safe if the mask_mod is never used in a decode/chunked-prefill step that re-attends to an image range whose start is not at local 0. In chunked prefill, a later chunk's query for an image token can have kv_idx of the image start in a *previous* chunk that is not in the current tile's local frame — the (causal OR blockwise) span could then be evaluated with a wrong local difference. Recommend verifying mm_prefix ranges are only ever populated for full single-shot prefill, and add an explicit assertion/guard rather than relying on a documented invariant. Risk: silently wrong attention mask (image tokens attend to wrong keys) for chunked-prefill of large images.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Correctness test: Gemma4 with image span > sliding_window under chunked prefill (enable long-prefill chunking), compare logits vs HF (causal OR blockwise) AND sliding_window reference.

findings.claim: Off-by-one / semantic mismatch risk between the flash-attn window and the triton window. In flash_attn.py the clamp uses `mm_clamp_sw = sliding_window_size[0] + 1` (with comment 'self.sliding_window = (sw-1, 0)') and applies `(q_idx - kv_idx) < mm_clamp_sw`, whereas the Triton helper applies `(query_abs_pos - seq_offset) < SLIDING_WINDOW` where SLIDING_WINDOW is the layer's own constexpr window (not +1 adjusted). If the flash-attn `sliding_window_size[0]` is stored as `window-1` while the Triton `SLIDING_WINDOW` is the full window, the two backends clamp to windows that differ by 1 token, so a request routed to flash vs triton would produce slightly different attention masks. Verify both backends use the identical effective window so the two paths are numerically consistent, and document why +1 appears in one path and not the other.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Cross-backend consistency test: run identical Gemma4 image prompt through FLASH_ATTN and TRITON_ATTN backends, assert attention outputs match within tolerance.

findings.claim: New `Attention.__init__` argument `mm_prefix_clamp_sliding_window` is read only via `getattr(layer, 'mm_prefix_clamp_sliding_window', False)` in both backends (triton_attn.py and flash_attn.py), never via `self.mm_prefix_clamp_sliding_window`. The constructor stores it on `self.mm_prefix_clamp_sliding_window`, which is consistent, but the class-level attribute on `Gemma4ForConditionalGeneration` (`mm_prefix_clamp_sliding_window: bool = True` on the *model*, read by gpu_model_runner via `getattr(self.model, ...)`) and the per-*layer* attribute (read by backends) are two different flags with the same name serving two different roles (model-level opt-out of range skipping vs per-layer in-kernel clamp). This dual meaning is confusing and error-prone: a future model could set the model-level flag but forget to pass the per-layer arg (or vice versa), silently getting causal-only image attention or an unclamped window. Recommend distinct names, e.g. `mm_prefix_keep_oversized_ranges` (model/runner) vs `mm_prefix_clamp_sliding_window` (layer/kernel), with an assertion that they are set consistently.

In flash_attn.py the guard `sliding_window_size is not None and sliding_window_size[0] >= 0` sets `mm_clamp_sw = sliding_window_size[0] + 1`. If a full-attention layer erroneously has `mm_prefix_clamp_sliding_window=True` (in Gemma4 it is `self.is_sliding`, so this cannot happen today), `sliding_window_size` would be None/(-1,-1) and the clamp is skipped — behavior is correct. But there is no assertion tying `mm_prefix_clamp_sliding_window=True` to actually being a sliding layer. Consider asserting that when the flag is True the layer has a finite sliding window, to catch future misconfiguration where the flag is set on a full-attention layer and the image block is silently left unclamped (defeating the fix).

gpu_model_runner.py now calls `getattr(self.model, 'mm_prefix_clamp_sliding_window', False)` once per build (outside the req loop) — fine. But the per-range skip condition was inverted to `not _clamps_in_kernel and _bidi_sw is not None and (r[1]-r[0]+1) > _bidi_sw`. When `_clamps_in_kernel` is True, all oversized ranges are now kept, which increases MAX_MM_RANGES / range-tensor width and the per-tile mask-mod loop cost (the CuTE loop iterates `max_ranges` and the Triton loop iterates `MAX_MM_RANGES`). For Gemma4 large images this adds kernel work on every sliding layer. This is expected/required for correctness, but worth confirming the range count is not inflated versus before (previously oversized ranges were dropped entirely), as the mask-mod loop is O(MAX_MM_RANGES) per tile.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Prefill throughput for Gemma4 with multiple large images: compare tokens/s before vs after keeping oversized mm_prefix ranges on sliding layers.

findings.claim: (1) The change reorders `set_device_index` after device creation and now passes a `torch.device` object (`torch.accelerator.set_device_index(device)`) instead of the integer `local_rank`. (2) `torch.accelerator.set_device_index` expects an integer device index, not a `torch.device` object; passing a device object may raise a TypeError or silently misbehave depending on the torch version, breaking multi-GPU test setup. (3) Pass the integer index instead: `torch.accelerator.set_device_index(local_rank)` (or `device.index`). Confidence is moderate since some torch versions accept device-like inputs.

(1) `use_batched_activation_format` now returns `self.use_deepep_ll_kernels or self.use_nixl_ep_kernels`, resolving the prior TODO. (2) This is a behavior change: nixl EP now uses the batched activation format, affecting the oracle selection paths in oracle/{fp8,mxfp4,mxfp8,unquantized}.py that key off this property; it looks intentional and consistent with existing nixl-batched-format handling. (3) No fix needed, but confirm the nixl kernels/oracles are actually validated with the batched format path (this PR only adds test coverage for nixl_ep with limited quant methods).

(1) The newly introduced `ScheduleEndContext` (fields `new_req_ids`, `preempted_req_ids`) is constructed in `OffloadingConnectorScheduler.build_connector_meta` and threaded through `on_schedule_end(context)` in every manager/tier override, but no override actually reads either field — all bodies are unchanged (`self._lookup_manager.flush()`, `return`, or forwarding `context` to sub-tiers which also ignore it). (2) This is the rubric's 'unused new argument' pattern: the per-step `new_req_ids`/`preempted_req_ids` lists are computed on every scheduler step (list comprehension over `scheduled_new_reqs`) purely to be discarded, adding per-step allocation with no consumer. It also creates a risk of an incomplete/incorrect contract landing (e.g. semantics of what counts as 'new' this step) that no test exercises. (3) Either wire up the intended consumer in this PR, or if usage is intentionally deferred, add an explicit TODO/migration note documenting it as a temporary plumbing shim, per the Mandatory Check.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark build_connector_meta over a scheduler step with a large batch (e.g. 512 new + 128 preempted reqs) to measure the per-step list-comprehension allocation overhead vs. the previous no-arg call.

findings.claim: (1) `new_req_ids` is built as a fresh list comprehension `[req.req_id for req in scheduler_output.scheduled_new_reqs]` on every scheduler step even though no downstream code reads it. (2) This is setup/allocation work that runs on the hot per-step path with no functional effect. (3) Since `ScheduleEndContext` fields are `Collection[str]`, if a consumer is not landing in this PR, defer constructing these collections until a consumer exists, or pass the underlying objects lazily.

(1) The new suffixing mutates `dp_vllm_config.instance_id` per DP replica, but this same field is used elsewhere to derive cross-process shared resource names (e.g. RoutedExpertsCapturer/Reader build shm+lock names as f"{instance_id}_{dp_rank}" in routed_experts_capturer.py, and lmcache/cpu_communicator worker names). Any reader-side component that computes these names from a VllmConfig WITHOUT the `_dp{index}` suffix (e.g. constructed outside the CoreEngineActorManager loop) would attach to a different shm name than the capturer and silently fail to find the buffer. (2) Risk: silent breakage of cross-process shared-memory features that key off instance_id when DP>1 with Ray. (3) Fix: verify all consumers of instance_id-derived resource names (routed_experts_capturer, lmcache adapter, cpu/xpu worker names) receive the suffixed config, or scope this suffix narrowly to the Ray actor-name path rather than mutating the shared instance_id. Confidence lowered because these consumers likely all derive from the same suffixed dp_vllm_config passed into the actor.

(1) The comment claims that non-Attention layer types like Mamba1/ShortConv 'do not expose num_heads' so getattr falls back to 0. However, Mamba layers may in fact have a `num_heads` attribute (e.g. Mamba2 uses num_heads for its SSM head grouping), which would produce a nonzero fallback value. (2) If a Mamba/SSM layer does expose a `num_heads` attribute with a different meaning than attention Q-heads, the dedup key could partition SSM layers unexpectedly or the comment becomes misleading. (3) Recommend verifying that all non-Attention layer classes in a KV cache group genuinely lack `num_heads`, or gate the getattr on isinstance(layer, Attention/AttentionLayerBase) rather than relying on attribute absence. Since the comment also correctly notes such layers never share a KV cache group with Attention, this is low-risk but the comment's factual premise ('do not expose num_heads') may be inaccurate.

Legacy cache compatibility for consts=None may be incomplete on the serialize side, but the deserialize side is asymmetric. In `reconstruct_serializable_fn_from_mega_artifact` (caching.py ~528), the code guards on `if execution_code is not None and submod_names is not None:` and then reads `consts = state.get('consts')`. A cache artifact produced BEFORE this PR will not have a 'consts' key, so `state.get('consts')` returns None and `compile_execution_fn(..., None)` runs the legacy path — this is handled correctly. HOWEVER, an OLD `execution_code` string generated before this PR may still contain unqualified `device(type=...)` reprs (the exact bug being fixed), and reloading such a cached artifact will still fail at exec/call time because the code string itself is stale. There is no cache-version bump / invalidation to force regeneration of pre-fix execution_code. Recommend confirming the compilation cache key incorporates a version that changes with this codegen fix, otherwise users with existing caches containing the buggy code string will hit the same failure after upgrade. Impact: silent reuse of broken cached code. Fix: bump the compilation/artifact cache version or key so pre-fix execution_code is not reused.

The `_node_ref` dedup comment states objects 'must be picklable (for compile-artifact caching)' but there is no assertion or validation enforcing this. If a non-primitive, non-picklable object (e.g. a lambda, a local closure, or a custom type without a reduce method) ever appears as an FX arg, it will be silently lifted into `consts`, and the failure will only surface later at pickle time in `serialize_compile_artifacts` with an opaque error far from the origin. Consider adding a targeted assertion or clearer error at lift time, or documenting the assumption that aten-graph args are always picklable. Impact: hard-to-diagnose serialization failures. Fix: validate picklability when lifting, or narrow the accepted types.

id()-based dedup relies on FX graph args staying alive for the whole codegen pass (correctly noted in the comment). This is valid within a single `generate_execution_code` call since `split_gm` holds references to all node args. However, the guarantee that two logically-equal-but-distinct objects (e.g. two independently constructed `torch.device('cpu')` instances that are NOT the same singleton) get separate slots is acceptable, but merging by id() means equal-value distinct objects will NOT be deduped, producing two consts slots holding equal values. This is harmless for correctness but slightly bloats the consts list and artifact size. Not a bug, just noting the dedup is by identity not value as documented.

(1) The `dp_supervisor_probe_timeout_s` CLI arg docstring states it is the "Seconds to wait between retries when a child health probe fails with a connection error", but in the code this value is used only as the aiohttp `ClientTimeout(total=...)` in `_probe_all_children`, while the actual inter-retry delay (`conn_err_retry_delay`) passed to `_probe_endpoint` is `dp_supervisor_probe_interval_s`. (2) This mismatch is misleading: operators tuning `dp_supervisor_probe_timeout_s` expecting to control retry backoff will instead only change the per-request HTTP timeout, and retry spacing silently follows the probe interval. (3) Fix the docstring to describe the actual behavior (per-probe HTTP timeout) and/or wire `conn_err_retry_delay=dp_supervisor_probe_timeout_s` if the documented behavior is intended.

(1) `is_ready` is set True as soon as a single probe round reports all children healthy (`self._is_ready = True`), but the readiness probe uses `threshold=1` (no retry) only while not ready, meaning the FIRST successful round flips readiness with no debounce. (2) During slow child startup, a transient single-round success (e.g. a child that returns 200 on /health before the engine is fully loaded) can prematurely mark the supervisor ready and then, once ready, any subsequent probe failure triggers an immediate full shutdown of all DP servers — turning a transient blip into a fleet-wide restart. (3) Consider requiring N consecutive healthy rounds before declaring ready, mirroring the failure_threshold used post-ready, to avoid flapping-induced shutdowns.

(1) In `_probe_all_children`, `asyncio.gather(..., return_exceptions=True)` collects per-child results, and readiness is computed as `all(r is True for r in results)`. If a probe coroutine raises (returned as an exception object rather than a bool), it is correctly treated as not-True; however `num_unhealthy = sum(1 for r in results if r is not True)` will also count exception objects, which is fine, but the raised exception is never logged, silently swallowing programming errors (e.g. an unexpected exception inside `_probe_endpoint`). (2) Silent swallowing of unexpected exceptions makes diagnosing supervisor misbehavior hard in production. (3) Log the exception instances found in `results` before deciding unhealthy count.

(1) `validate_multi_port_external_lb_args` reads `args.data_parallel_size_local` and errors if it is None or < 2, but this validation runs on the raw argparse namespace (via `validate_parsed_serve_args`) BEFORE `EngineArgs.from_cli_args`/`create_engine_config` resolves the effective `data_parallel_size_local` (which defaults to None and is later inferred). Users who rely on the auto-inference of `data_parallel_size_local` (leaving it unset) will get a hard validation error even in valid single-node multi-DP setups. (2) This forces users to always pass `--data-parallel-size-local` explicitly in multi-port mode, an undocumented coupling that can break otherwise-valid configs. (3) Either document that multi-port mode requires explicit `--data-parallel-size-local >= 2`, or resolve/infer the local size before validating.

(1) `data_parallel_multi_port_external_lb` is added as an `EngineArgs` dataclass field and a manual argparse argument, but unlike the neighboring DP-LB flags it is not sourced from `ParallelConfig` via `parallel_kwargs`, meaning it lives only on `EngineArgs`/argparse and is never propagated into `ParallelConfig`/`VllmConfig`. (2) This is intentional (the supervisor consumes it purely at the CLI layer and strips it from child args), but the asymmetry with the other `data_parallel_external_lb`/`hybrid_lb` flags may confuse future maintainers who expect it on the config object. (3) Add a brief comment clarifying that this flag is CLI-only and deliberately not part of ParallelConfig.

(1) `_build_vllm_dp_server_env` computes visible devices as `local_rank * devices_per_rank` through `range(start, stop)`, assuming children occupy contiguous device indices starting at 0 on the node and that `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`-style filtering has not already been applied by the parent. If the supervisor process is itself launched with a pre-set device control env var (e.g. a subset of GPUs), the physical-device-id mapping via `device_id_to_physical_device_id` may double-apply or mis-map. (2) Could assign overlapping/incorrect GPUs to children on constrained nodes. (3) Assert/validate that the number of locally available devices >= data_parallel_size_local * devices_per_rank before spawning children.

(1) The new `skip_if_decorated` guard in `decorate_logs` checks `hasattr(sys.stdout, "_original_write")`. Since `_add_prefix` sets `file._original_write` on both stdout and stderr, checking only stdout is a reasonable proxy, but the semantics are subtle: if stdout was decorated but stderr was not (e.g. partial prior call), skip incorrectly bypasses stderr decoration. (2) Minor risk of stderr losing its prefix in edge cases. (3) Consider checking both streams, or document that decoration is always applied to both together.

(1) `_detect_merge_inline_system` is called at __init__ with `chat_template`, which is the raw arg stored as `self.chat_template` in the base OpenAIServingChat — often `None`, or a template name / file path rather than resolved Jinja source. The actual template used at render time is resolved lazily via `resolve_chat_template(tokenizer, ...)` (see vllm/renderers/hf.py) from the tokenizer's built-in HF chat template. (2) In the very common case where the server is started without `--chat-template` (chat_template=None), detection returns True and ALWAYS merges inline system messages, even for models (e.g. Llama) whose actual HF template happily accepts mid-conversation system messages. This silently changes conversion behavior for the majority of deployments and defeats the prefix-caching / cache-friendly path the PR's own test names advertise. Conversely, if a user passes a template *name* or *path* string, `env.from_string(name)` will render trivially without raising, returning False (no merge) even if the real resolved template requires system-first ordering — the opposite failure. (3) Detection should operate on the *resolved* Jinja template text (as done in `_resolve_chat_template_content_format`), not the raw constructor arg; or the merge decision should be deferred to request time once the effective template is known.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Start the Anthropic server against a Llama-style model with no --chat-template; send a [system, user, system, user] Anthropic request and assert the mid-conversation system message is preserved as a separate message rather than merged into the top block.

findings.claim: (1) The new code does `import jinja2` only, but then references `jinja2.sandbox.ImmutableSandboxedEnvironment`, `jinja2.ext.loopcontrols`, and `jinja2.TemplateError`. Accessing `jinja2.sandbox` / `jinja2.ext` as attributes of the top-level package requires those submodules to have been imported somewhere in the process; `import jinja2` alone does NOT guarantee the `sandbox`/`ext` submodules are bound as attributes. The reference file vllm/renderers/hf.py explicitly does `import jinja2.ext` and `import jinja2.sandbox` for exactly this reason. (2) If AnthropicServingMessages is constructed before hf.py (or any module that imports those submodules) is loaded, __init__ raises AttributeError, breaking server startup — a latent import-ordering bug. (3) Add explicit `import jinja2.sandbox` and `import jinja2.ext` at module top, mirroring vllm/renderers/hf.py.

(1) The detection renders the probe conversation with `add_generation_prompt=False` and no `tools`/`bos_token`/other kwargs that HF templates frequently reference; many real templates access variables like `tools`, `add_generation_prompt`, `documents`, or custom kwargs and will raise a non-`TemplateError` exception (e.g. `UndefinedError` is a subclass of TemplateError, but `TypeError`/`KeyError` from template logic are not) or raise TemplateError for reasons unrelated to system-message ordering. (2) A template that raises for an unrelated missing variable is misclassified as 'needs merge' (or, if it raises a non-TemplateError, the exception propagates and breaks __init__). This makes the heuristic fragile across the diverse set of production templates. (3) Broaden the except to also treat generic template execution errors conservatively, and/or pass the standard kwargs (tools=None, add_generation_prompt) the render path uses; document the heuristic's assumptions.

(1) The new `normalize_null_max_tokens` before-validator rewrites an explicit `max_tokens: null` in the request body to the field default (16). (2) This is a behavior change that overrides the previously well-defined handling: downstream `get_max_tokens()` already treated `max_tokens=None` specially (falling back to `default_sampling_params.get('max_tokens')`, and ultimately to `max_model_len - input_length` if no server default). With this change, a null `max_tokens` is now hard-coerced to 16 *before* it reaches `get_max_tokens`, so `get_max_tokens` will never again see `None` from a null request, and any server-side `default_sampling_params['max_tokens']` (from generation_config) can no longer take effect for null-max_tokens requests — it is silently shadowed by the literal 16. This silently truncates outputs to 16 tokens for clients that send explicit null relying on the server-configured generation default. (3) Consider whether null should keep flowing through as `None` so `get_max_tokens` can apply the server default; if the intent is to make null equivalent to omission, ensure that is consistent with the omitted-field path (which also defaults to 16), and add a test documenting the precedence vs `default_sampling_params`.

(1) The validator reads `cls.model_fields['max_tokens'].default` to obtain the fallback value (16). (2) This couples the runtime behavior to a Pydantic-internal introspection API; if the field default is ever changed to a `default_factory` or the field is redefined, `.default` may be `PydanticUndefined` rather than 16, silently coercing null to an undefined sentinel. (3) Consider hard-coding `16` (matching the field annotation) or asserting the resolved default is an int, to avoid a brittle dependency on Pydantic field internals.

(1) The shutdown `finally` block calls `health_servicer.set_not_serving()` then `await server.stop(grace=5.0)`. `set_not_serving()` marks the health service NOT_SERVING, but any in-flight `Watch` streams and probes only observe the new status if the servicer actually pushes the update to active watchers; the ordering here (set status, then stop with a 5s grace) is correct for allowing load balancers to drain, but the effectiveness depends entirely on the external `VllmHealthServicer` implementation (in `smg-grpc-servicer`, not in this repo). (2) If `set_not_serving()` only flips a local flag consulted by `Check` (per the tests, `Watch` re-evaluates on iteration), watchers blocked awaiting the next status change may not be notified during the 5s grace, so K8s readiness draining could be less graceful than the docs imply. (3) Confirm the external servicer's `set_not_serving()` notifies pending `Watch` streams, not just future `Check` calls; otherwise document the limitation.

(1) Per the docs note ('The gRPC health service checks the engine status on every probe') and the tests (`Check` awaits `async_llm.check_health()` each call), every liveness/readiness probe invokes `AsyncLLM.check_health()`. With `readinessProbe.periodSeconds: 5` and `livenessProbe.periodSeconds: 10` as in the sample manifest, this is a recurring call on the engine's async path. In the current repo `check_health` is a cheap debug-log no-op, so impact is low today, but if `check_health` becomes a real engine-core round-trip it would add per-probe RPC/sync load competing with inference. (2) Frequent synchronous engine health round-trips on a short probe interval can contend with the serving loop under load. (3) Consider caching/rate-limiting the health result (e.g., only re-check every N seconds) in the external servicer, or ensure `check_health` stays lightweight; at minimum keep probe `periodSeconds` conservative in docs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure p99 latency / throughput of gRPC generate requests under sustained load with readiness probes at periodSeconds=1 vs no probes, once check_health does real engine work.

findings.claim: (1) The newly added doc note in gpu.xpu.inc.md is internally inconsistent about the compute-runtime version: the prose says "compute runtime 26.18" but the hyperlink points to `.../releases/tag/26.14.37833.4` (26.14), while the Dockerfile.xpu in the same PR actually installs compute-runtime `26.18.38308.1`. (2) A misleading doc is worse than none — users following the link will download/reference the wrong (26.14) release rather than the 26.18 version the recommendation intends, causing exactly the compatibility issues the note warns against. (3) Fix the hyperlink to point at the 26.18 release tag (e.g. `26.18.38308.1`) so the linked version matches both the prose ("26.18") and the version installed by Dockerfile.xpu.

(1) The added line is formatted as a bullet (`- Start from ...`) inserted between the "Second, install Python packages..." bullet and its associated fenced code block, breaking the ordered First/Second instruction flow (there is no "Third" and the note interrupts the step-then-codeblock structure). It also has a grammar slip: "we recommend user upgrade driver". (2) This degrades readability of the install instructions; the recommendation is easy to miss or misread as part of the pip step. (3) Move the note to a standalone `!!! note` block after the driver bullet, and fix grammar to "we recommend users upgrade the driver to ...".

(1) `_is_supported_kv_cache_dtype` now returns `current_platform.has_device_capability(89)` for ANY `fp8`-prefixed kv_cache_dtype, including ROCm's `fp8e4m3fnuz`/`fp8_e4m3` path. On ROCm, `get_device_capability` returns capabilities parsed from the GCN arch (e.g. gfx942 -> (9,4) -> to_int()==94, gfx90a -> (9,0) -> 90, gfx1100 -> (11,0) -> 110). MI300X (gfx942, to_int 94) passes 89, but MI200/gfx90a (to_int 90) also passes while some RDNA gfx11 devices that previously worked would still pass — the deeper problem is that comparing a ROCm gfx-derived integer against the NVIDIA SM number 89 is semantically meaningless. (2) This can incorrectly reject or accept fp8 KV cache on AMD/XPU hardware that previously worked, silently breaking ROCm fp8 KV-cache serving even though native fp8 support there is unrelated to 'SM89'. (3) Gate the SM89/SM80 checks behind `current_platform.is_cuda()` (the checks are described as NVIDIA SM levels), or route through a platform-specific capability query for fp8/bf16 support.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run reshape_and_cache_flash unit test with fp8 kv cache on ROCm (gfx90a/gfx942) and XPU to confirm the new capability gate does not reject previously-supported configurations.

findings.claim: (1) The new SM89/SM80 gating is duplicated in two places with slightly inconsistent semantics: `TritonAttentionImpl.__init__` raises for `kv_cache_dtype.startswith('fp8')`/`=='bfloat16'`, while `_is_supported_kv_cache_dtype` in the ops file re-implements the same capability checks. The two error messages also disagree (backend suggests `float16`/`bfloat16` fallback via `--kv-cache-dtype`, while the reshape assert suggests `bfloat16 (or float16 on SM75)`). (2) Divergent copies of the same architectural gate will drift over time and give users contradictory remediation advice; if only one is updated a device could pass `__init__` but fail deep inside the kernel launch. (3) Centralize the capability check in a single helper (e.g. reuse `_is_supported_kv_cache_dtype` or a shared platform function) and derive both error messages from it.

(1) `suggested = 'float16' if (cap is None or cap.to_int() < 80) else 'bfloat16'` calls `DeviceCapability.to_int()`, which asserts `0 <= self.minor < 10`. If the device reports a two-digit minor (unlikely on current NVIDIA HW but possible on OOT/ROCm-derived capabilities), this assert fires and replaces the intended informative `ValueError` with a confusing `AssertionError`. (2) The whole point of this branch is to produce a helpful error message; an assertion crash defeats it. (3) Use the comparison operators already defined on `DeviceCapability` (e.g. `cap < DeviceCapability(8, 0)`) instead of `to_int()`, which avoids the minor<10 assertion.

(1) The removed `assert kv_cache_dtype != torch.uint8` asserts compared a `str` argument (`kv_cache_dtype`) against a `torch.dtype` (`torch.uint8`); this comparison was always True and thus dead — removing it is a correct cleanup and safe. (2) No behavioral change, noting only to confirm this is intentional dead-assert removal rather than an accidental drop of a real check. (3) No action needed; the real supported-dtype gating is now `_is_supported_kv_cache_dtype`.

(1) `triton_reshape_and_cache_flash_diffkv` had its `_is_supported_kv_cache_dtype` assert entirely removed (not replaced), so this path no longer validates kv_cache_dtype at all, whereas the non-diffkv path still keeps the assert. (2) An unsupported dtype passed to the diffkv kernel will now reach the Triton launch/`.view()` and fail with a lower-level, less actionable error instead of the intended message. (3) Keep an `_is_supported_kv_cache_dtype` assertion (with the improved message) in the diffkv function too, for parity with `triton_reshape_and_cache_flash`.

(1) `set_enable_structured_outputs_in_reasoning` / `get_enable_structured_outputs_in_reasoning` use a plain module-global `_enable_structured_outputs_in_reasoning` that is set exactly once at API-server startup from `vllm_config.structured_outputs_config.enable_in_reasoning`. This is a process-wide single value, but `enable_in_reasoning` is a per-config/per-engine setting; if multiple engines/configs with differing `enable_in_reasoning` values are served from the same process (or the value is not re-set on config reload), tool parsers will read a stale/incorrect flag. (2) A wrong flag silently changes whether the structural tag covers the `<think>...</think>` reasoning prefix, producing a structural-tag/bitmask-gating mismatch (the exact failure the comment in api_server.py warns about), which can cause the constrained decoding to reject valid tokens or misgate reasoning. (3) Bind the flag to the request/config path (e.g. read from the request's vllm_config or pass it explicitly into `get_structural_tag`) rather than a module global, or at minimum document the single-config assumption and assert it.

(1) In `get_deepseek_v4_structural_tag`, `suffix_tag` is only assigned inside the `if tool_choice == 'auto' / elif 'forced' / elif 'required'` branches, with no `else`. If `tool_choice` is ever a value other than these three normalized values, `suffix_tag` is referenced unbound and raises `UnboundLocalError`. (2) While `_normalize_tool_choice` currently maps to exactly these three, this coupling is fragile: any future normalized value (or a direct call to the builder) crashes request handling instead of producing a clear error. (3) Add a final `else: raise ValueError(...)` or initialize `suffix_tag` and validate. The Qwen builder has the same structure (line 233+).

(1) `DeepSeekV4ToolParser` implements `get_structural_tag` but (unlike `Qwen3CoderToolParser` in this same PR) does NOT set `supports_required_and_named = False`. When `VLLM_ENFORCE_STRICT_TOOL_CALLING` is off (the default), the base `adjust_request` still uses the JSON-schema path; the serving layer for DeepSeek V4 (XML/DSML output format) will treat `required`/named tool_choice as JSON-parseable via `supports_required_and_named=True` inherited default. (2) DeepSeek V4 emits `<｜DSML｜...>` XML, not JSON, so the JSON-based required/named parsing in the serving layer can silently misparse or fail for required/named tool_choice — the same reason GLM parsers set the flag False.
