(3) Verify whether DeepSeekV4/V32 should also set `supports_required_and_named = False`; if the XML format cannot be JSON-parsed, add it.

(1) In the new Step 1 block of `adjust_request`, when a structural tag is applied and `request.structured_outputs` already exists, the code sets `.structural_tag` but does not clear `request.response_format`. In the pre-existing JSON path (Step 2) `request.response_format = None` is explicitly set to avoid conflicting constraints. (2) Leaving a stale `response_format` alongside a structural_tag may lead to conflicting structured-output constraints and `StructuredOutputsParams.__post_init__` mutual-exclusion errors or unexpected behavior downstream. (3) Mirror the JSON path: set `request.response_format = None` when applying the structural tag, and consider that overwriting an existing `structured_outputs.structural_tag` may clobber a user-supplied constraint.

(1) `get_model_structural_tag` raises `ValueError('Unknown format type: ...')` when the model key is missing, but this is invoked per-request via `get_structural_tag`. Since builders are registered at import time, a mismatch between the registered key ('deepseek_v4'/'qwen_3_5') and the parser call is a hard request failure. (2) A typo/rename in either the registry key or the parser call raises on every request rather than gracefully degrading (base `get_structural_tag` returns None). (3) Consider returning None (falling back to the JSON path) or add an import-time consistency check/test that every parser's model key exists in the registry.

apply_weights passes x directly to quant_mxfp4/xpu_mxfp4_quantize and torch.ops._xpu_C.fp4_gemm without flattening leading dims. (1) The underlying custom op _xpu_mxfp4_quantize_impl asserts x.ndim == 2 ('input must be 2-D'). Activation tensors reaching a linear layer are frequently 3-D [batch, seq, hidden] (or the layer may pass higher rank). (2) This will raise an assertion / runtime error at inference for any 3-D input, and unlike FlashInferMxFp4LinearKernel (which does x_2d = x.reshape(-1, x.shape[-1]) and reshapes output back via out_shape), no reshape is done here. This is a real correctness/robustness gap versus the sibling kernel. (3) Fix: mirror the FlashInfer kernel: compute out_shape = x.shape[:-1] + (layer.output_size_per_partition,), reshape x to 2-D before quantize+gemm, and reshape the output back with out.view(out_shape). Confirm whether vLLM linear layers already flatten to 2-D before calling apply_weights; if so lower severity, but the sibling kernel defensively reshapes.

(1) process_weights_after_loading and apply_weights read layer.weight_scale but do not assert/validate the layer produced weight_scale/weight in the expected dtype (uint8 -> viewed as float8_e8m0fnu / float4_e2m1fn_x2). The .view(torch.float4_e2m1fn_x2) and .view(torch.float8_e8m0fnu) reinterpret raw bytes and silently produce wrong values if the loaded dtype/packing differs. (2) A mismatch would produce silently wrong GEMM results rather than an error. (3) Add shape/dtype assertions at this parallelism/quantization boundary (e.g. assert layer.weight.dtype == torch.uint8 before the view), consistent with the rubric guidance on shape/dtype assertions at boundaries.

is_supported ignores the compute_capability argument and only checks current_platform.is_xpu(). This is acceptable for XPU (no CUDA-style capability), and can_implement unconditionally returns True. Since torch.ops._xpu_C.fp4_gemm may not exist unless the xpu kernel extension is built, is_supported should also verify the op is available (e.g. hasattr(torch.ops._xpu_C, 'fp4_gemm')). Otherwise on an XPU platform without the kernel built, selection succeeds but apply_weights fails at runtime with an AttributeError. Fix: gate is_supported on op availability like _xpu_ops.py does elsewhere (hasattr(torch.ops._xpu_C, ...)).

(1) Line 1079 still references `context.parser` via `parser_state_to_response_output(context.parser, fn_names)`, but this refactor removes the `.parser` attribute from `ParsableContext` (it is inlined onto the context itself). This diff updates lines 705, 808, and 819 to use `context.response_messages` / `context.make_response_output_items()` / `context.finish_reason`, but leaves line 1079 untouched. (2) If this `context` is a `ParsableContext`, the call will raise `AttributeError: 'ParsableContext' object has no attribute 'parser'` at runtime, breaking the streaming/tool-call path. Even if `context` here is a `HarmonyContext` (which still has a `.parser` StreamableParser attribute), the reviewer should confirm the type; the semantics differ significantly. (3) Verify the type of `context` at line 1079; if it can be a `ParsableContext`, update it consistently with the other call sites; if it is exclusively `HarmonyContext`, it is fine but should be confirmed since the grep shows no other `context.parser` usages survive for ParsableContext.

(1) The entire module `vllm/entrypoints/openai/parser/responses_parser.py` is deleted, including public symbols `ResponsesParser` and `get_responses_parser_for_simple_context`. Existing tests `tests/entrypoints/openai/test_responses_parser_unified.py` import `ResponsesParser` and `get_responses_parser_for_simple_context` from this module (lines 21-23), and `tests/entrypoints/openai/test_tool_choice_content_none.py` has `test_responses_parser_allows_named_tool_choice_with_none_content`. (2) Deleting the module without updating/removing those importers will cause ImportError/collection failure in the existing test suite (and any external code importing these symbols). The commit adds a new test file but does not appear to delete or update `test_responses_parser_unified.py`. (3) Remove or migrate `tests/entrypoints/openai/test_responses_parser_unified.py` (and check `test_tool_choice_content_none.py`) to the new inlined ParsableContext API, or keep a thin compatibility shim.

(1) In the deleted `ResponsesParser.__init__`, the `tokenizer` was stored as `self.tokenizer` for potential later use. In the inlined version the tokenizer is only passed into `parser_cls(...)` and not retained. This is a behavior-preserving simplification (the old `self.tokenizer` was never read outside construction), so no attribute is needed. (2) Low risk; just confirm no downstream code accessed `context.parser.tokenizer`. (3) No action needed beyond confirming; noted for completeness.

(1) The commit title states "Upgrade CUDA Dockerfiles from GCC 10 to GCC 12" but the actual change installs gcc-11/g++-11 (and the CMake gate requires >= 11.3), not GCC 12. (2) This mismatch is misleading: reviewers/maintainers reading the changelog or git history will believe GCC 12 is the new toolchain, and future bisects or bump decisions may be based on a false premise. (3) Reconcile the commit message with the implementation — either bump the packages to gcc-12/g++-12 to match the title, or correct the title/message to say GCC 11.

(1) The new FATAL_ERROR gate requires GCC >= 11.3, but the Dockerfiles install the distro 'gcc-11' package rather than pinning a specific patch version. On the pinned Ubuntu 22.04 base image gcc-11 currently resolves to 11.4.x (satisfies the gate), but users/CI building on Ubuntu 20.04 or with an older toolchain PPA can get gcc-11.1/11.2, which is < 11.3 and would hit the new FATAL_ERROR — a confusing failure since they did install 'gcc-11'. (2) This creates a latent build-break for source builders outside the exact Docker environment. (3) Consider documenting the 11.3 patch-level requirement explicitly (or verifying `gcc-11 --version >= 11.3` in the Dockerfile) so the gate and the installed package cannot diverge.

(1) The C++20 compiler gate only checks CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER_ID == "GNU"; Clang/AppleClang and other compilers are not validated even though CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD is 20 and PyTorch's C++20 headers are the stated requirement. (2) A source build with an old Clang would silently proceed and fail later with a cryptic header error instead of the clear message this gate provides. (3) If Clang builds are supported, add an analogous minimum-version check for Clang; otherwise a comment clarifying the gate is intentionally GCC-only would prevent confusion.

(1) `_apply_composed_hf_override` calls `SpeculativeConfig.hf_config_override(hf_config)` first and then `target_hf_overrides(hf_config)`; the returned value from `hf_config_override` is passed on, but for the `NemotronH_Super_Omni_Reasoning_V3` case `hf_config_override` reassigns `hf_config = hf_config.text_config` and returns a *different* object — the composed path correctly forwards that return value, so this is fine. However, note that `hf_config_override` mutates its argument in-place for most archs while returning it; the ordering (arch-mapping before the user callable) is the documented and tested behavior. No correctness issue, but worth confirming that user callables which rely on observing the *original* (pre-arch-mapping) architecture will now see the rewritten architecture (e.g. `MiMoMTPModel`). This is a behavioral subtlety introduced by composing arch-mapping first.

(1) Previously, for non-medusa methods `draft_hf_overrides` was always exactly `SpeculativeConfig.hf_config_override`. Now, when the target has a callable `hf_overrides`, the draft receives a composed transform that also applies the target's callable. (2) Risk: this is a genuine behavior change for any production user who sets a callable `hf_overrides` on the target model and relies on the draft NOT inheriting it (previously the callable was silently dropped for the draft). The change is intentional and correct for the CI-shrink use case, but users depending on the old (draft-unaffected) behavior will now see their target callable applied to the draft config too. (3) Consider documenting this behavior change in release notes / the arg docstring.

(1) `compose_draft_hf_overrides` is called with `self.target_model_config.hf_overrides`, but by the time `__post_init__` runs on the target ModelConfig, the target's callable `hf_overrides` may have already been consumed/applied to the target's own hf_config (in ModelConfig.__init__ callable path at model.py:517). The callable object itself remains stored on `target_model_config.hf_overrides`, so re-applying it to the draft config re-runs the transform — this is the intended semantics. Verify the target callable is idempotent / safe to run twice (once on target, once on draft) since some callables mutate module-level state; the tested `shrink` example is idempotent, but arbitrary user callables may not be. Consider noting the idempotency expectation in the docstring.

(1) The newly added methods `_safe_get_token_id` and `_safe_get_token_str` on `MiniCPMOProcessor` are never called anywhere in the codebase (grep across the repo finds no callers, and the class's own `_convert` accesses `self.tokenizer.im_start_id`/`audio_start_id`/etc. directly rather than through these helpers). (2) Dead code adds maintenance burden and misleads readers into thinking token access is guarded/safe when it is not. (3) Either wire these helpers into `_convert`/`get_audio_placeholder` where token IDs/strings are read (they would also address the hardcoded `<audio>`/`</audio>` fallbacks), or remove them.

(1) `MiniCPMOProcessingInfo.get_hf_processor` overrides the base `MiniCPMVProcessingInfo.get_hf_processor` and only converts numpy arrays for the `('mean', 'std')` image-processor attributes, whereas the base implementation (kept in minicpmv.py) also handles the Transformers v5 renamed attributes `('image_mean', 'image_std')`. (2) Since this PR's stated purpose is to unblock Transformers v5, on v5 the MiniCPMO image processor may expose `image_mean`/`image_std` as numpy arrays that are left unconverted, reintroducing the serialization/Iterable-vs-Sequence issue the base code fixes — silently for the O (image+audio) path. (3) Iterate over `('mean', 'std', 'image_mean', 'image_std')` here to match the base implementation, or refactor to reuse the base numpy->list conversion logic.

(1) `MiniCPMOProcessingInfo.get_hf_processor` runs `import numpy as np`, constructs a fresh `MiniCPMOProcessor`, and rebuilds it on every call; the underlying `self.ctx.get_hf_processor(**kwargs)` and vendored-processor construction are re-executed for each invocation. (2) If `get_hf_processor` is called frequently during processing (e.g. once per request/audio placeholder computation via `get_audio_placeholder`), repeatedly rebuilding the ProcessorMixin and re-running numpy->list conversion adds avoidable per-call overhead. (3) Consider caching the vendored processor (e.g. via functools.lru_cache on the info or memoizing on ctx) if it is invoked on a hot path.

(1) In `ChatTTSProcessor.__call__`, the try/except around `self.audio_processor(audio)` catches `Exception as e` and immediately re-raises `e` unchanged, providing no additional context, cleanup, or handling. (2) This is dead complexity that adds no value and can obscure the original traceback origin. (3) Remove the try/except and call `self.audio_processor(audio)` directly.

(1) The openpangu_vl.py change is semantically different from all other files in this PR. Previously the code called `maybe_prefix("openpangu", "language_model")`, which produces the module prefix `openpangu.language_model` regardless of the outer `prefix`. The new code calls `maybe_prefix(prefix, "openpangu.language_model")`, which produces `<prefix>.openpangu.language_model` (or `openpangu.language_model` when prefix is empty). (2) This changes the fully-qualified module/parameter path of the language model submodule. If the model was previously constructed with a non-empty `prefix`, the resulting state-dict key namespace changes, which can break weight loading / checkpoint compatibility. Conversely if the intended fix is to prepend the container prefix, the previously hardcoded `"openpangu"` literal may not match the checkpoint's expected top-level name. (3) Verify against the actual checkpoint key layout for OpenPanguVL what the correct module path should be; confirm whether the outer prefix is expected to be empty in practice (in which case both forms yield `openpangu.language_model` and behavior is preserved), and add a test loading real weights to confirm no key mismatch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load an OpenPanguVL checkpoint and assert AutoWeightsLoader reports no missing/unexpected keys for the language_model submodule; also instantiate with a non-empty prefix and check module qualified names.

findings.claim: (1) In get_mla_prefill_backend, when a user explicitly sets mla_prefill_backend=MLAPrefillBackendEnum.CUSTOM without registering it, selected_backend.get_class() calls get_path() which raises ValueError ("must be registered before use"), but the surrounding try/except only catches ImportError. The ValueError therefore propagates uncaught rather than being converted into the framework's standard "invalid backend" ValueError with configuration/reason context. (2) Impact is minor: the user still gets an actionable message, but the error path differs from the other invalid-backend handling and skips validate_configuration. (3) Consider catching ValueError alongside ImportError in the explicit-selection branch (this file was not modified by the PR, so it is a follow-up consideration introduced by making CUSTOM user-selectable). Note the parent AttentionBackendEnum design has the identical behavior, so this is consistent with existing conventions.

(1) Adding CUSTOM = None as a new enum member makes it selectable via the string field_validator (MLAPrefillBackendEnum["CUSTOM"]) and via iteration over MLAPrefillBackendEnum, which several call sites do (docs generator, tests). (2) Verified this is safe: the doc generator (_extract_enum_values) skips falsy enum values via `if ... and item.value.value` and also lists CUSTOM in SKIP_BACKENDS, and _auto_select_mla_prefill_backend only iterates a fixed priority list that excludes CUSTOM, so auto-selection cannot accidentally pick an unregistered CUSTOM. This finding is informational; the design faithfully mirrors the established AttentionBackendEnum.CUSTOM pattern in vllm/v1/attention/backends/registry.py and carries no observed regression.

(1) The ROCm-aware timeout resolution logic (`eval_config.get("request_timeout_seconds", 600)` followed by the `is_rocm()` override) is duplicated verbatim in both `run_gsm8k_eval` and `test_gsm8k_correctness` (once for the actual call, once purely for the print statement). (2) Duplicated resolution logic can silently drift if only one copy is updated (e.g. changing the default from 600), causing the logged timeout to disagree with the timeout actually used. (3) Extract the resolution into a small helper (e.g. `resolve_request_timeout(eval_config)`) and call it in both places, or compute it once in the test and pass it into `run_gsm8k_eval`. This is test code and low severity.

(1) The new `_feed` / `_preprocess_feed` indirection adds two layers whose base implementation `_preprocess_feed` is a pure no-op that returns its arguments unchanged. (2) Adding an extra method call and tuple pack/unpack per streaming tick to every ParserEngine subclass (including Qwen3 and others that do not override `_preprocess_feed`) is a small but non-zero per-delta overhead on the hot streaming path, and adds indirection with no abstraction value for parsers that never override it. (3) This is acceptable as an extension point for Gemma4, but the base no-op could short-circuit (e.g. Gemma4 overrides `_feed` directly, or the base `_feed` skips the preprocess call when not overridden). Confidence that this is a meaningful perf issue is low; flagged as a minor structural note.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark ParserEngine.parse_delta over N small deltas comparing direct self._engine.feed vs the new self._feed indirection to quantify per-tick overhead.

findings.claim: (1) The Qwen3 `(ParserState.CONTENT, "TOOL_START")` transition was changed from emitting `(EventType.TOOL_CALL_START,)` to `(EventType.REASONING_END, EventType.TOOL_CALL_START)`. This now emits a REASONING_END event even when a `<tool_call>` appears from CONTENT with no active reasoning (e.g. thinking disabled, or reasoning already ended). (2) REASONING_END drives `self._reasoning_ended = True` in `_events_to_delta` and affects `_strip_trailing_reasoning` / `is_reasoning_end` semantics; emitting it spuriously when no reasoning is open can flip reasoning-ended state or interact with the skip_tool_parsing branch in `_on_terminal` (which specially handles REASONING_END-containing transitions by emitting a TEXT_CHUNK of the `<tool_call>` value). This change to Qwen3 is bundled into a PR whose stated purpose is replacing Gemma4 parsers, and could silently alter Qwen3 tool-call/reasoning output. (3) Verify Qwen3 regression tests cover CONTENT->TOOL_START (thinking disabled and post-reasoning) and confirm the extra REASONING_END is intended and idempotent; if only needed for Gemma4 semantics, keep Qwen3 unchanged.

(1) In `_gemma4_arg_converter`, a trailing `}` is stripped via `text.endswith("}")` before parsing, but the engine's `_feed_args_char` holds back the top-level closing brace during streaming (returns [] at brace_depth 0), so in the streaming path the converter typically receives args without the closing `}` anyway; in the finish/non-streaming path the `}` may be present. (2) The single unconditional strip assumes exactly one trailing brace; if the args string legitimately ends with `}` as part of a nested-object value that was fully closed but the outer tool brace not yet seen, stripping could remove a needed char — low risk given the format, but worth an assertion/comment. (3) Add a comment documenting the invariant that the stripped `}` is always the outer tool-call brace, or gate the strip on the non-partial path only.

(1) `Gemma4Parser._events_to_delta` re-implements streaming prefix-stripping of the `thought\n` label with several branches accumulating `self._reasoning_text` on every reasoning delta. It appends every reasoning delta to `self._reasoning_text` until the prefix decision is made, which is bounded but means comparing/allocating growing strings per tick until `_prefix_stripped` is set. (2) For long reasoning where the prefix never matches (e.g. `_GEMMA4_THOUGHT_PREFIX.startswith(self._reasoning_text)` stays true across many ticks — impossible here since prefix is short, so bounded), the accumulation is bounded to the prefix length; risk is minimal. (3) Consider stopping accumulation and setting `_prefix_stripped=True` as early as possible; the logic already largely does this, so this is a minor readability note.

(1) `Gemma4Parser.is_reasoning_end` iterates `input_ids` from the end and returns based on the first recognized boundary token, treating `<|tool_call>` and `<channel|>` (end) as reasoning-end and `<|channel>` (start), `<|turn>`, `<|tool_response>` as not-ended. This is O(n) per call and is invoked on the full growing token list; for very long sequences called repeatedly this is O(n^2) across a generation. (2) Reasoning-end detection is typically called per step, so scanning the entire history each step can add avoidable cost on long outputs. (3) Consider caching the last-seen boundary index or early-terminating once a decision token is found (already does) — the main concern is re-scanning full history each call; a cached scan position would make it amortized O(1).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark is_reasoning_end over an increasing token history (e.g. up to 8k tokens) called once per token to measure quadratic scaling.

findings.claim: (1) The new verify_models fixture adds a third check `missing_tags = {model for model, tag in _model_pipeline_tags.items() if not tag}` that fails if any pipeline tag is empty. (2) This branch is effectively dead: `_model_pipeline_tags` is a hard-coded literal whose values are all non-empty string constants, so `not tag` can never be true, and the module would already fail earlier if a tag string were malformed. It adds dead complexity that never triggers. (3) Consider dropping the empty-tag check (or, if it is meant as a guard for future edits, leave a brief comment noting its intent).

(1) The mapping assigns `cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-12-v2` the tag `"text-ranking"`, which is not present in `_test_tasks`; the comprehension `for task in [_model_pipeline_tags[model]] if task in _test_tasks` therefore silently excludes this model from `_model_w_tasks`, so it is never parametrized/tested. (2) If the intent was for this model to be exercised, it will now be silently skipped with no failure — a coverage regression that is easy to miss. (3) Confirm whether dropping cross-encoder from the tested matrix is intended; if it should be tested, map it to a task in `_test_tasks` (or add `text-ranking` to `_test_tasks`), otherwise add a short comment noting the intentional exclusion.

findings.category: Mandatory Check: Unused New Variables / Arguments
findings.claim: (1) The new `req_context: ReqContext` argument added to `CPUOffloadingManager.lookup`, `prepare_load`, and `prepare_store` is never read anywhere in the manager body — it is accepted and discarded. `FilterReusedOffloadingManager` only forwards it, and `RequestOffloadState` builds a `ReqContext(kv_transfer_params=...)` that is passed to the manager but never consumed. So across the whole change there is no meaningful runtime use path for `kv_transfer_params`. (2) Per the rubric's Mandatory Check, newly added arguments must have a read/use path; a passed-but-ignored context that also allocates a `ReqContext` object per request adds interface surface and a small per-request allocation with zero behavioral effect, and it silently commits every future OffloadingManager implementor to a parameter with no defined contract. (3) Either wire `req_context.kv_transfer_params` into an actual decision in one of the managers in this PR, or add an explicit TODO + migration note documenting that this is an intentional interface-plumbing shim awaiting the consuming logic.

(1) `lookup`, `prepare_load`, and `prepare_store` are abstract methods whose signatures now take a mandatory positional `req_context` argument with no default. (2) Any out-of-tree `OffloadingManager` subclass (this is a public-ish extension interface, e.g. custom offload backends) will break at call time because the scheduler now passes `req_context` positionally; there is no backward-compat shim. All in-tree implementations are updated, but external implementors get a hard break. (3) Consider giving `req_context` a default (`req_context: ReqContext | None = None`) on the abstract signatures, or documenting the interface break in release notes, since these managers are a pluggable extension point.

(1) When total_frames_num < extract_t (short/low-frame-count videos), np.linspace(0, total_frames_num - 1, extract_t, dtype=int) produces extract_t indices but many are duplicated (there are only total_frames_num distinct integer positions). The subsequent dedup step (uniq) then collapses these to at most total_frames_num unique indices, so len(uniq) can be far smaller than extract_t. (2) This silently breaks the contract that the loader returns ~extract_t frames — the model receives fewer frames than transformers' Glm46VVideoProcessor would produce, causing a mismatch in the number of video tokens vs. what the processor/model expects (potential shape/token-count errors or degraded accuracy). transformers' reference does NOT deduplicate after linspace for the upsampling branch. (3) Either drop the dedup step to faithfully replicate transformers (which allows repeated frame indices when upsampling), or document/handle the short-video case explicitly. The test test_glm46v_duration_estimation_from_fps (90 frames, extract_t=24) happens to pass because 90>=24, but any case where total_frames_num < extract_t will diverge.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test: call compute_frames_index_to_sample with total_frames_num < extract_t (e.g. total_frames_num=20, duration=10, fps=2 -> extract_t=60) and assert len == expected extract_t to expose the dedup shortfall vs transformers reference.

findings.claim: (1) max_second = int(duration) uses the ORIGINAL (uncapped) duration, not effective_duration, but target_fps and extract_t are computed from effective_duration (capped at 2400s). For videos longer than 2400s, the sampling loop's stop condition `current_second >= max_second` uses the uncapped duration while extract_t is capped, creating an inconsistency between the number of frames the loop is allowed to emit and extract_t. (2) This can produce a frame index list whose length/spacing diverges from transformers' reference for very long videos, changing which frames the model sees. (3) Verify against transformers whether max_second should be based on effective_duration; align the variable used for the loop bound with the one used for extract_t.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test with duration=5000s (exceeds _MAX_DURATION) comparing frame_indices against transformers Glm46VVideoProcessor.sample_frames output.

findings.claim: (1) The even-frame-count enforcement appends uniq[-1] (a duplicate of the last index) when the count is odd. This produces two identical frame indices at the end of the list, and the invariant `indices == sorted(set(indices))` asserted in the test would fail for that case (dedup removes the appended duplicate). More importantly, a duplicated frame index means read_frames/decode will yield one fewer unique frame than len(uniq) claims, since downstream _read_frames_no_recovery uses a set() of indices — so the actual returned frame array may have odd length again, defeating the even-count guarantee. (2) GLM-4.6V requires an even number of frames (temporal_patch_size=2 grouping); if the effective returned count becomes odd after set-based reads, the model's temporal patching breaks. (3) To pad, append a new distinct index (e.g. min(uniq[-1]+1, total_frames_num-1)) only if it doesn't already exist, or pad at the frame-array level after loading rather than the index level.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test forcing an odd uniq length, then run the full opencv/pyav load path and assert the returned frames array has an even leading dimension.

findings.claim: (1) `if not duration` treats duration==0 AND duration==0.0 as missing, but also treats any falsy value; fine. However when original_fps<=0 and duration is 0, effective_duration=0, target_fps=3.0, extract_t=0, and both branches produce an empty or degenerate list; duration_per_frame=0 makes all timestamps 0.0, so the else-branch loop appends only frame 0 then breaks (max_second=0 => current_second(=inv_fps) >= 0 true after first append). Edge case yields 1 frame -> padded to 2 duplicates. (2) Degenerate metadata (fps unknown) yields an almost-empty sample rather than a sensible fallback. (3) Add an explicit guard/fallback for original_fps<=0 to sample uniformly across available frames.

(1) GLM46VVideoBackend.load_bytes merely calls super().load_bytes(...) forwarding every argument unchanged with no added behavior. (2) This override adds indirection with no abstraction value and can silently drift from the base signature. (3) Remove the override and rely on the inherited VideoBackend.load_bytes, unless a GLM-specific default (e.g. max_duration) is intended — in which case set only that.

(1) `finish_reason: str` is added to `InferenceResponse` as a REQUIRED pydantic field with no default. (2) Any code path that constructs `InferenceResponse` or deserializes it (`InferenceResponse.model_validate(response.json())` in megatron/rl/server/inference/inference_interface_server.py:35) without providing `finish_reason` will now raise a pydantic ValidationError, breaking existing inference backends. Only `MegatronLocal.base_generate` was updated in this PR; other/external interfaces that produce these responses (and any serialized JSON lacking the field) will fail at runtime. (3) Give the field a safe default (e.g. `finish_reason: str | None = None`) matching the optional style of the surrounding fields, or update every producer/serializer to always populate it and document the contract.

(1) The new reward gate is `stopped = finish_reason != "length"`, treating everything except "length" as a clean stop eligible for full reward (1.0). (2) The dynamic text-gen server emits at least three finish_reason values: "stop", "length", and "tool_calls" (see chat_completions.py:712-716). With this logic a `tool_calls` finish (or any future/unknown value) is treated identically to a genuine end-of-text stop and can receive full reward 1.0, whereas the previous logic required an explicit end-of-text token in the response text. This can silently over-reward truncated-by-tool or otherwise non-cleanly-terminated generations, biasing the RL objective. (3) Gate on an allowlist of true completion reasons (e.g. `finish_reason in {"stop", "eos"}`) rather than a denylist of just `"length"`, so unexpected reasons default to partial/format reward.

(1) `finish_reason=choice.finish_reason` assumes the OpenAI chat completion `choice` always has a non-null `finish_reason`. (2) In streaming/partial or error responses the OpenAI schema allows `finish_reason` to be `None`; since `InferenceResponse.finish_reason` is now a required non-optional `str`, a `None` value would fail pydantic validation and abort rollout generation. (3) Coerce/guard the value, e.g. `finish_reason=choice.finish_reason or "length"` (or make the field Optional), and confirm the reward logic handles the null case as a non-clean stop.

(1) The base method `RewardOnlyAgent.get_reward` signature changed to add a required positional `finish_reason: str`, and all callers now pass `response.finish_reason`. (2) Any external/user-defined subclass overriding `get_reward(self, response, golden)` (the documented extension point, per its NotImplementedError contract) will now fail with a TypeError when called with the extra positional argument — a silent breakage for downstream RL environment implementations not in this repo. (3) Consider making `finish_reason` a keyword argument with a default (`finish_reason: str = "stop"`) or document the signature change as a breaking API change with a migration note.

(1) The `matched_format = None` local variable was removed in the boxed-vs-tagged refactor but verify no remaining reference exists; the diff dropped it cleanly. This is a note that the removed `end_tokens` list and its scanning loops are now fully replaced by the `finish_reason` gate — confirm no other module imported/relied on the previous end-token matching semantics (grep found none). No fix required if intentional. (2) Low impact; purely a confirmation of dead-code cleanup correctness. (3) None.

(1) The refactored RemoteOpenAIServerCustom._terminate_process_tree overrides the base class RemoteVLLMServer._terminate_process_tree, but the base class's _shutdown() already calls _terminate_process_tree() followed by _wait_for_gpu_memory_release(). The old __exit__ was replaced by this _terminate_process_tree; however the base RemoteVLLMServer._shutdown calls self._terminate_process_tree() -- so behavior is now routed through the inherited _shutdown machinery. Verify that the previous direct __exit__ (which used self.proc.terminate()/join()) is fully superseded and that _shutdown's subsequent _wait_for_gpu_memory_release still runs. (2) If the override changes the contract (e.g. base expected a subprocess.Popen with .wait(timeout=), but Custom uses a multiprocessing.Process with .join()), inherited helpers like shutdown_many that call _terminate_process_tree remain consistent, but any base code assuming Popen semantics on self.proc could misbehave. (3) Confirm the override is complete and that multiprocessing.Process.join semantics match what callers of _terminate_process_tree expect.

(1) _run_in_new_process_group calls os.setsid() then the child fxn. With the 'spawn' start method on XPU/ROCm, the target callable (self.child_process_fxn) and its args must be picklable. child_process_fxn is a module-level function (_server_with_logitproc_entrypoint) so it is picklable, but with spawn the child re-imports the module fresh, so any prior monkeypatch of importlib.metadata.entry_points in the parent is NOT inherited -- which is exactly why setup_fake_entrypoint switches to the on-disk dist-info + PYTHONPATH approach. Verify that the PYTHONPATH env var set via monkeypatch.setenv actually propagates to the spawned Process: multiprocessing spawn inherits os.environ at Process.start() time, and monkeypatch.setenv mutates os.environ, so this should work. (2) Risk: if env_dict passed to the child overwrites/omits PYTHONPATH via os.environ.update(env_dict), the entrypoint discovery would silently fail and the test would falsely pass/fail. (3) Confirm env_dict never clobbers PYTHONPATH.

(1) The removed module-level `dummy_module = types.ModuleType(...)` object was previously created and had DummyLogitsProcessor attached, but DUMMY_LOGITPROC_MODULE = 'tests.v1.logits_processors.utils' -- meaning the FQCN 'tests.v1.logits_processors.utils:DummyLogitsProcessor' resolves to the real module which genuinely contains DummyLogitsProcessor. So the synthetic dummy_module was likely dead/unused code, and removing it is safe. (2) If anything imported `dummy_module` from this file it would now break. (3) Grep confirms whether `dummy_module` had any external importers; if none, removal is clean.

(1) The `@pytest.mark.asyncio` decorator was removed from test_custom_logitsprocs, but the function body still calls asyncio.run(...) internally and is defined as a plain (non-async) def. This is consistent -- a sync test that drives its own event loop should not have the asyncio marker. (2) Risk: if pytest-asyncio was previously (incorrectly) wrapping this sync function, removing the marker is the correct fix; ensure no other async fixtures depend on the marker being present. (3) Verify the test still collects and runs as a sync test.

(1) fake_entry_points is now only referenced inside setup_fake_entrypoint's fork branch via monkeypatch.setattr; the EntryPoints/EntryPoint dummy classes are still used by fake_entry_points. This is fine. (2) Minor: register_fake_entrypoint returns the temp dir path (str) but the sole caller (setup_fake_entrypoint) discards the return value, and the mkdtemp directory is never cleaned up, leaking a temp dir per test invocation on ROCm/XPU. (3) Consider registering cleanup (e.g. via a fixture/tmp_path or atexit/shutil.rmtree) to avoid accumulating dummy-logitproc-* dirs across a CI run.

(1) The `needs_parsing` gate only inspects `chat_template_kwargs.get("enable_thinking")` and `tools`, but the docstring and existing online-serving reasoning parser (`vllm/reasoning/gemma4_reasoning_parser.py`) set `skip_special_tokens=False` whenever the Gemma4 reasoning/tool parser is active — not merely when the caller passed `enable_thinking`. A user calling `LLM.chat(...)` on a Gemma4 model with reasoning enabled at the config level (e.g. reasoning-parser configured, thinking default-on) but without explicitly passing `chat_template_kwargs={"enable_thinking": True}` will still have special tokens stripped, breaking downstream parsing. (2) This silently produces unparseable output for exactly the case the PR is trying to fix. (3) Consider aligning the trigger with the online path — check whether a reasoning/tool parser is configured on the model, not just the presence of caller kwargs.

(1) `chat_template_kwargs.get("enable_thinking")` treats any truthy value as enabling parsing, but if a caller explicitly passes `enable_thinking=False` while also passing `tools`, the `or tools` branch still triggers preservation — which is intended — however if `enable_thinking` is a non-bool truthy sentinel the condition semantics are loose. (2) Minor risk of surprising behavior. (3) Consider `chat_template_kwargs.get("enable_thinking") is True` for explicit intent, or document the truthiness contract.

(1) `_adjust_params_for_parsing` calls `tokenizer.get_vocab()` on every `chat(...)` invocation when thinking/tools are enabled. `get_vocab()` materializes the full vocabulary dict (tens of thousands of entries) and is not cheap. (2) For repeated `chat` calls this is redundant per-call overhead; the special-token membership check only depends on the fixed model tokenizer and could be computed once. (3) Cache the `needs_special` determination (or the resolved structured token ids) on `self` after first computation, keyed by tokenizer identity, rather than recomputing the full vocab lookup each call.

(1) The comment/docstring references `Gemma4's <|channel>` but the diff's inline banner comment also lists `<|"|>` as 'string quoting in tool args' — verify these token strings actually match the Gemma4 tokenizer's registered special tokens; a stale/incorrect token list here silently defeats the whole check (the `if tok in vocab` guard means a wrong string is simply skipped, so `needs_special` stays False and nothing is preserved). (2) A wrong token string would make this a silent no-op with no error. (3) Add a test asserting these specific token strings resolve to special ids for a real Gemma4 tokenizer, or derive the list from a model-provided constant.

(1) The hotwords branch uses str.format() to inject the raw user-supplied `hotwords` string into a fixed template that also contains no other placeholders. Because the base template contains literal characters, using .format() means any literal `{` or `}` inside the user's `hotwords` value would raise a KeyError/ValueError or be misinterpreted as a format field. (2) A crash or malformed prompt on adversarial/edge-case hotword input is a robustness bug. (3) Prefer f-string with the fixed prefix or concatenation, or escape braces; e.g. build the prompt via string concatenation of a constant prefix + hotwords + constant suffix so the user value is never passed through the format mini-language.

(1) The two funasr_prompt string literals share a large common prefix/suffix (the system message and the trailing `<|AUDIO|><|im_end|>\n<|im_start|>assistant\n` block); the hotwords variant only inserts an extra context block before `语音转写`. (2) Duplicating the entire multi-hundred-character template in two branches makes future edits error-prone (one branch can drift from the other). (3) Extract the shared prefix/suffix into named constants and compose the middle segment conditionally, so both variants stay in sync.

(1) New function parameters `hotwords: str = None` (in both sync_openai and stream_openai_response) and the SpeechToTextParams/protocol docstrings describe hotwords as 'a list of important words or phrases', but the type is a single `str`, and the default `None` is typed `str` rather than `str | None`. (2) The mismatch between docstring ('a list'), the actual `str` type, and the incorrect annotation is misleading to callers building requests, and a wrong docstring is worse than none. (3) Either type it `str | None = None` and clarify in the docstring that hotwords is a single string (e.g. space/comma-separated), matching how it is embedded into the prompt template.

(1) `hotwords` is added to SpeechToTextParams and passed from both TranscriptionRequest and TranslationRequest, but it is only consumed by FunASRForConditionalGeneration.get_generation_prompt; other SupportsTranscription models silently ignore it. (2) This is acceptable as an optional additive field (no backward-compat break), but a user sending `hotwords` to a non-FunASR model receives no error/warning and may assume it took effect. (3) Consider documenting in the field docstring that support is model-dependent (as done for `to_language`), so users are not misled into thinking hotwords are universally honored.

(1) safe_quantized_repr reads only `_fp8_dtype` via getattr(obj, "_fp8_dtype", None), but NVFP4Tensor / NVFP4TensorStorage store their dtype in `_fp4_dtype` (there is no `_fp8_dtype` attribute on NVFP4 tensors, confirmed by grep). So for the NVFP4 fallback path the dtype line is silently dropped and the repr shows no dtype at all. (2) Impact is purely cosmetic/debuggability: the fallback repr for NVFP4 tensors is less informative than intended, but nothing functional breaks (getattr default None avoids AttributeError). (3) Fix: also probe `_fp4_dtype` (e.g. `getattr(obj, "_fp8_dtype", None) or getattr(obj, "_fp4_dtype", None)`) or pass the dtype attribute name / value in via the NVFP4 call sites' `extras`.

The new `_quantized_layer_prefix_candidates` only rewrites the `language_model.model.` <-> `model.language_model.` prefix pair and the `.lm_head` -> `lm_head` case. For VLM checkpoints whose quantized_layers keys use a different multimodal prefix normalization (e.g. bare `model.` without the `language_model` component, or a `.lm_head` embedded under a nested module path other than exactly `*.lm_head`), the candidate set will still miss the entry and `_resolve_quant_algo` returns None, silently leaving those layers unquantized. This is a functional-coverage limitation rather than a bug in the added logic, but worth verifying against actual Qwen3.5/3.6 VLM key layouts and adding a test that exercises both prefix orderings. Impact: a missed candidate causes a layer that should be quantized to load as unquantized (correctness/accuracy regression), not a crash, so it is easy to miss. Fix: add unit tests covering both `language_model.model.*` and `model.language_model.*` orderings plus the `lm_head` case, and consider deriving the mapping from the WeightsMapper rather than hard-coded string surgery.

In branch 3 (prefix-based lookup for RoutedExperts/parent modules), the code now nests a full O(N) scan of `self.quantized_layers.items()` inside the loop over prefix candidates, making it O(candidates * N). Since candidates is at most 2 here, the extra cost is small, and this path only runs at model-load time (get_quant_method), not per-forward, so throughput impact is negligible. Noting only for completeness; not worth changing. Fix (optional): break out of the candidate loop as soon as a match is found (it already returns on first match, so this is already fine).

(1) In the new `sliding_window_left is not None` branch, the causal-plus-window term is computed as `keep = (kv_idx <= q_abs) & ((q_abs - kv_idx) < sw)`. This makes ALL non-mm_prefix tokens subject to the sliding-window constraint on this FA4 mask_mod path, even for layers where `mm_prefix_clamp_sliding_window` is False but `sliding_window_size` is present (i.e. `sw_val is not None` but `mm_clamp_sw == 0`). Previously the causal term was pure `kv_idx <= q_idx` with no window, and the window was applied only inside the mm block when clamping was requested. (2) Why it matters: the FA4 kernel already applies the layer's `window_size` (`sliding_window_size`) natively via the flash-attn call; adding the window again inside the mask_mod double-applies it, or worse, applies a window to a layer that was intended to be full-causal on the base attention but only clamp the mm block. This could silently change attention outputs (mask tokens that should be visible) for Gemma4 local layers and any other mm_prefix model that has a sliding window but does not set `mm_prefix_clamp_sliding_window`. (3) Fix: gate the causal-term window (`& ((q_abs - kv_idx) < sw)`) on whether the base attention window is actually enforced by this mask_mod vs. by the kernel; confirm with the Triton reference that `compute_kv_seq_mask` applies the window to the causal term in exactly this configuration, and only include the window term when the kernel is NOT independently given `window_size`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Numerical equivalence test: compare FA4 mm_prefix mask output vs Triton reference (compute_kv_seq_mask) for a Gemma4-style local sliding layer with an image mm_prefix range, across prefill chunks with nonzero context offset.

findings.claim: (1) The new code introduces `q_abs = q_idx + ctx_off` where `ctx_off = seqlen_k - seqlen_q`, correcting q_idx from local to absolute so that mm_prefix range comparisons (`q_in`) and causal comparisons use consistent absolute positions. This is the core bugfix, but note the mm_prefix ranges stored in `aux_tensors[0]` must themselves be expressed in absolute KV coordinates for `q_in`/`k_in` to be correct. `k_in` uses `kv_idx` (absolute) against `r_start/r_end`, while previously `q_in` used the *local* `q_idx` against the same `r_start/r_end`. The old code therefore compared q (local) and kv (absolute) against the SAME range bounds — inconsistent — which is exactly the bug being fixed. This confirms the ranges are in absolute coordinates. (2) Risk: if any caller populated `mm_prefix_range_tensor` assuming local q coordinates (matching the old buggy `q_in`), this fix will change behavior for that caller. (3) Fix/verify: confirm all producers of `mm_prefix_range_tensor` emit absolute KV-cache offsets; add a shape/coordinate-convention assertion or docstring note at the producer site.

(1) The two `@cute.jit`-decorated `mm_prefix_mask_mod` closures (the `sliding_window_left is not None` branch and the `else` branch) are almost entirely duplicated — identical ctx_off/q_abs computation and identical mm-range loop body, differing only in the causal-term window and the intra-mm window clamp. (2) Why it matters: duplicated JIT kernel bodies double the maintenance surface; a future fix to the range loop must be made in two places and can easily drift (a real correctness risk over time). (3) Fix: since `sliding_window` and `sliding_window_left` are compile-time (constexpr) closure captures, a single `@cute.jit` function can branch on `sliding_window_left is not None` / `sliding_window > 0` at trace time (Python-level `if`, resolved at JIT compile), avoiding runtime cost while removing duplication.

(1) The two parameters `sliding_window` (clamps the bidirectional mm block) and `sliding_window_left` (enforces the window on the causal term) are easily confused and their distinct roles are only clear from the docstring. (2) Why it matters: in distributed/attention masking code, ambiguous names invite incorrect call sites (e.g. passing the causal window into the clamp slot). The rubric asks for names that describe what the thing is. (3) Fix: rename to something like `mm_block_window` (clamp of bidirectional block) and `causal_window` (window applied to causal term), making the two roles self-documenting at call sites.

(1) The former module megatron/training/utils.py is being converted into a package (utils/__init__.py) that re-exports a curated list of names from common_utils/log_utils, but `unwrap_model` is NOT included in that re-export list. In the parent, `megatron/training/utils.py` bound `unwrap_model` at module scope (imported from megatron.core.utils), so `from megatron.training.utils import unwrap_model` worked everywhere. (2) Multiple in-repo callers that are NOT touched by this PR still import it via that path and will break at import time with ImportError: megatron/elastification/loss_func.py:14, megatron/elastification/pretrain_hybrid_flex.py, and several examples/post_training/modelopt/*.py (convert_model.py, export.py, finetune.py, generate.py, mmlu.py, prune.py, quantize.py, validate.py, offline_feature_extract.py). This is a backward-compatibility break for a public utility import. (3) Add `unwrap_model` to the re-export list in megatron/training/utils/__init__.py (it is already imported into common_utils, so simply add it to the `from megatron.training.utils.common_utils import (...)` block or re-export from megatron.core.utils).
findings.suggested_benchmark: python -c 'from megatron.training.utils import unwrap_model'

findings.claim: (1) `append_to_progress_log` signature changed from `append_to_progress_log(string, barrier=True)` to `append_to_progress_log(save, string, barrier=True)` (a new required first positional argument). (2) This is a breaking API-contract change to a helper that previously read `args.save` internally; any external/user script or downstream code calling `append_to_progress_log('msg')` will now pass the message as `save` and fail or silently write to the wrong path. All in-repo callers were updated (checkpointing.py x3, training.py x3), but there is no deprecation shim or keyword-compatible path. (3) Consider making `save` a keyword-only or optional argument that falls back to `get_args().save` when None, preserving the old call form, or document the break in release notes.

(1) `resolve_slurm_rank`/`resolve_slurm_world_size`/`resolve_slurm_local_rank` gate on `is_slurm_job()` (which checks SLURM_NTASKS) and then re-check the specific env var, returning None if absent. In practice `resolve_slurm_rank` returns None when SLURM_PROCID is unset even inside a SLURM job; the caller in safe_get_rank then falls through to the default-0 warning path. This is benign but means a SLURM-detected job can still silently default to rank 0. (2) Low impact since SLURM normally sets SLURM_PROCID, but the redundant double-check is slightly confusing. (3) Consider documenting that None is returned when the specific var is missing even under SLURM, or simplify by keying detection off the specific var.

The `@CustomOp.register("minimax_text01_rmsnorm_tp")` decorator changes the registered op name from `MiniMaxText01RMSNormTP` (the old `name` class attribute) to `minimax_text01_rmsnorm_tp`. (1) Issue: the custom-op enable/disable name used by `enabled()` (comparing `+{cls.name}`/`-{cls.name}` against the configured custom_ops list) is now `minimax_text01_rmsnorm_tp` instead of `MiniMaxText01RMSNormTP`. (2) Why it matters: any deployment or test that explicitly enabled/disabled this op via `--compilation-config` custom_ops (e.g. `-MiniMaxText01RMSNormTP`) would silently stop matching, changing whether the CustomOp forward native/compiled path is used. (3) Fix: confirm this rename is intentional and no external config references the old name; if there is any risk, keep the old registered name string.

In FusedMoEWithLoRA.__init__, for the `supports_internal_mk` path the PR now unconditionally sets `moe_kernel.impl.shared_experts = None` (fused_moe.py). (1) This mutates the shared kernel implementation object to strip shared-expert ownership, whereas previously the internal MK kernel retained whatever shared_experts it had. (2) The comment claims the intent is to let the runner overlap shared experts on a separate CUDA stream, but the newly-added `FusedMoEKernelModularImpl.shared_experts` attribute merely defaults to None in modular_kernel.py — if the internal-MK kernel already had shared experts wired through a different attribute/path, forcibly nulling only this new attribute could either be a no-op (dead code) or silently drop shared-expert computation, producing wrong MoE outputs when LoRA is enabled on models with shared experts. (3) Verify whether the internal-MK path actually populated `impl.shared_experts` before this change; if not, this assignment is a semantic change introduced under a 'mypy fix' PR and should be justified/tested, not slipped in.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a LoRA-enabled MoE model that has shared experts (e.g. DeepSeek-V2/V3 style) through the internal-MK quant path and verify output parity vs. non-LoRA baseline; confirm shared-expert contribution is still applied.

findings.claim: In MergedColumnParallelLinearWithLoRA.apply (column_parallel_linear.py), the pre-change guard used `type(self.base_layer).forward is not merged_cls.forward`. The PR replaces this with getattr-based lookups and requires BOTH `base_forward is not None` and `merged_forward is not None` before taking the `_apply_base_forward` branch. (1) This changes control flow: if either forward attribute is unexpectedly None (e.g. an OOT merged_cls stub without forward), the code now falls through to `_mcp_apply` instead of the base-forward path. (2) For an 'effectively unsharded subclass' whose base_layer has a custom forward but where merged_cls.forward resolves to None, behavior silently changes from custom-forward reuse to the generic merged path, which can alter numerics or lose the subclass's custom communication semantics. (3) Confirm merged_cls always has a callable `forward`; if so the None guards are dead defensiveness, if not the new fallthrough is a behavioral regression that should be validated.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test wrapping a MergedColumnParallelLinear subclass with tp_size==1 and a custom forward, asserting _apply_base_forward is taken (not _mcp_apply).

findings.claim: In MergedColumnParallelLinearVariableSliceWithLoRA (column_parallel_linear.py) the slice was changed from `lora_b[start_idx:end_idx, :]` to `lora_b[start_idx:end_idx]`. (1) These are equivalent only if lora_b is guaranteed 2-D. (2) If lora_b were ever >2-D, dropping the explicit `:, :` would still slice dim0 identically, so this is behavior-preserving for 2-D and 3-D — but it removes an implicit shape assertion that documented the expected 2-D layout. (3) Low risk; consider keeping `[..., :]` or adding a dim assertion at the parallelism boundary to preserve intent.

Multiple constructors/methods changed `vllm_config: VllmConfig | None = None` to a required `vllm_config: VllmConfig` (model_manager.py create_lora_manager/LoRAModelManager/LRUCacheLoRAModelManager; worker_manager.py create_lora_manager). (1) This is a signature/API contract change in the LoRA manager layer: callers that previously relied on the optional/None default now must pass vllm_config. (2) External code (or plugins) invoking `create_lora_manager` positionally may still work, but any keyword caller passing None or omitting it will now trip the added `if vllm_config is None: raise ValueError`. (3) If backward compatibility with out-of-tree callers matters, keep the parameter Optional with a runtime check rather than tightening the annotation and removing the default; otherwise document the break.

capacity property in LoRAModelManager (model_manager.py) now asserts `self.lora_config.max_cpu_loras is not None`. (1) In LoRAConfig.__post_init__, max_cpu_loras is defaulted from max_loras when None, so under normal construction it is non-None; the assert is a mypy-narrowing guard. (2) Risk: if a LoRAConfig is constructed bypassing __post_init__ (e.g. dataclasses.replace with max_cpu_loras=None, or direct field mutation), this assert now hard-fails at runtime in `capacity` where previously it would return None. (3) Acceptable as a type-narrowing guard, but prefer a descriptive error message over a bare assert since assertions are stripped under python -O.

(1) `QuarkConfig.get_cache_scale_mapper` references `WeightsMapper` without importing it, unlike `Fp8Config.get_cache_scale_mapper` which does a local `from vllm.model_executor.models.utils import WeightsMapper`. (2) If `WeightsMapper` is not already imported at module scope in quark.py, calling this method raises `NameError` at weight-load time for every Quark-quantized model with KV-cache scales, breaking loading. (3) Add the same local import `from vllm.model_executor.models.utils import WeightsMapper` inside the method (or a module-level import) as done in fp8.py. Verify quark.py's existing imports before finalizing.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a Quark fp8 checkpoint with kv-cache output_scale weights and assert k_scale/v_scale populate without NameError.

findings.claim: (1) The new suffix mapping changes the prob-scale rename semantics: the old `get_cache_scale` matched `name.endswith('self_attn.prob_output_scale')` and produced `...self_attn.attn.prob_scale` by replacing only `.prob_output_scale`->`.attn.prob_scale`. The new `orig_to_new_suffix` maps `.self_attn.prob_output_scale` -> `.self_attn.attn.prob_scale`. These should be equivalent, but the suffix key now requires the literal `.self_attn.` segment immediately before `prob_output_scale`, whereas the old code matched any name ending in `self_attn.prob_output_scale` (no leading dot required). (2) A checkpoint tensor named exactly `self_attn.prob_output_scale` (top-level, no leading dot) would have matched the old str.endswith but will NOT match the new suffix `.self_attn.prob_output_scale`, silently dropping the prob scale. (3) Confirm real checkpoints always have a leading module prefix (e.g. `model.layers.N.self_attn...`); if edge cases exist, use suffix `self_attn.prob_output_scale` -> `self_attn.attn.prob_scale` to preserve prior matching.

(1) The new early-return `if name in params_dict: return name` in `maybe_remap_kv_scale_name` short-circuits ALL callers, not only KV-cache scale names. Several model `load_weights` gate on `if 'scale' in name` and then call this function; any weight name that happens to already be present in params_dict now skips the historical remapping logic below (e.g. `.kv_scale` deprecation warning + rename, `.self_attn.` handling). (2) For names that both exist in params_dict AND previously needed remapping/warning, behavior silently changes: the deprecation warning for legacy `.kv_scale` is suppressed and any remap-on-collision is skipped. This is likely fine for the intended WeightsMapper-prerenamed path but is broader than the docstring claims. (3) Narrow the guard to only the pre-renamed vLLM scale suffixes (e.g. endswith .k_scale/.v_scale/.q_scale/.prob_scale) rather than any name in params_dict, to avoid changing behavior for unrelated already-present params.

(1) `get_cache_scale(name)` is removed from the base class and replaced by `get_cache_scale_mapper()`. This is a public method on `QuantizationConfig` in megatron/core-equivalent library code; third-party/out-of-tree quant configs or model `load_weights` implementations that call `quant_config.get_cache_scale(name)` will now get `AttributeError`. (2) Breaks API contract for external plugins relying on the old per-name scale lookup. (3) Consider keeping a deprecated `get_cache_scale` shim (returning None) or documenting the removal in release notes / migration guide.

(1) glm_ocr_mtp.py applies the mapper via `weights = cache_scale_mapper.apply(weights)` at the top of `load_weights`, but many other converted models (apertus, arcee, aria, commandr, etc.) simply DELETE the old block and rely on the mapper being applied elsewhere (AutoWeightsLoader.load_weights `mapper=` path). Verify every model whose inline block was removed actually has the mapper applied on its weight stream — models that call their own `load_weights` directly (not via AutoWeightsLoader with mapper) would now silently drop KV-cache scales. (2) If a model's `load_weights` is invoked without the mapper, `.k_proj.output_scale` etc. reach `maybe_remap_kv_scale_name`/stacked-mapping and are never renamed to `.attn.k_scale`, leaving scales at the -1.0 sentinel and defaulting to 1.0 (accuracy regression for fp8 KV cache). (3) Confirm the wiring point (AutoWeightsLoader passing `get_cache_scale_mapper()` as `mapper`) covers all touched models; add a test loading an fp8 kv-scale checkpoint per affected architecture.
findings.suggested_benchmark: For each converted model, load a checkpoint containing k_proj.output_scale and assert layer.attn.k_scale != -1.0 after load.

findings.claim: (1) `KVCacheScaleParameter.weight_loader` is a `@staticmethod`, but the docstring notes per-instance override still works via instance attribute shadowing. Being a staticmethod means `getattr(param, 'weight_loader', default_weight_loader)` returns the unbound static function; call sites that do `weight_loader(param, loaded_weight)` work, but ensure no call site relies on a bound-method signature `(self, ...)`. (2) Minor: the sentinel value -1.0 and the reshape(()) accept both () and (1,); the docstring says rejects anything else which matches numel()!=1 check — consistent. No functional issue, just verify staticmethod interplay with existing default_weight_loader signature (param, loaded_weight).

(1) In the generic ND path, `a = a.expand(broadcast_shape + a.shape[-2:])` followed by `a_3d = a.reshape(batch_dim, a.shape[-2], a.shape[-1])` forces PyTorch to materialize a full contiguous copy of the broadcast tensor whenever a batch dim was actually broadcast (stride-0 dim cannot be flattened as a view). For the common 2D x 3D and ND x 2D-style broadcasts this can allocate a large intermediate (batch_dim x M x K) that did not exist before, increasing peak memory and adding a copy kernel. (2) In hot matmul paths this is extra HBM traffic per call. (3) Where only `a` (or only `b`) is broadcast, consider passing the un-expanded operand's real strides to the bmm kernel with a batch stride of 0, or special-casing the pure ND x 2D case (which the earlier `b.ndim == 2` branch already handles without expansion) so the broadcast-copy is only paid when genuinely required.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark matmul_batch_invariant on 2D x 4D and 4D(broadcast batch) x 4D shapes vs the previous 4D x 4D path, measuring peak memory and latency to quantify the expand+reshape copy overhead.

findings.claim: (1) The generic branch condition is `a.ndim >= 2 and b.ndim >= 3`, but there is no explicit validation that the shared contraction dim matches (`a.shape[-1] == b.shape[-2]`) nor that broadcast_shapes succeeds before reshaping; a mismatch will surface as an opaque reshape/stride error deep inside rather than a clear message like the removed ValueError provided. (2) Debuggability regression for callers passing incompatible shapes. (3) Add an explicit assert on the contraction dim (and let torch.broadcast_shapes raise its own clear error) with the operand shapes in the message, mirroring the old descriptive ValueError.

(1) In stop_and_flush(), when there is buffered content the code emits a *new* text block: it calls start_block(text=""), emits the buffered text as a content_block_delta, then stop_active_block(). But start_block increments/sets content_block_index and mutates state (block_type/block_index), while the preceding stop_active_block() has already been called at the top of stop_and_flush. The buffered text is emitted immediately after closing the tool_use block. This ordering is correct for the [DONE] and empty-choices paths, but stop_and_flush() is also invoked at lines 710/738/776 in the middle of the stream when transitioning between block types (e.g. tool_use -> new tool_use, or tool_use -> text). In those mid-stream cases, flushing buffered content as a separate freshly-opened+closed text block inserts a text block *between* two tool_use blocks, reordering content relative to the model's actual emission order and producing extra open/close text blocks. (2) This changes the semantic ordering of Anthropic content blocks vs. the source stream and may surprise clients expecting content after tool calls. (3) Verify the intended ordering: buffered content generated while a tool_use block was active should likely be flushed only at natural stream end, or the mid-stream stop_and_flush call sites should use stop_active_block() (no flush) and defer the flush. Confirm whether emitting an interleaved text block mid-stream is the desired behavior.

(1) stop_and_flush() re-uses start_block() which reads/increments state.content_block_index and sets state.block_index; after appending the flushed text block it calls stop_active_block() again which relies on state.block_index being set correctly. If start_block() has side effects beyond returning the SSE string (it sets content_block_index += 1 and block_index), the manually-built content_block_delta at line 618 uses state.block_index — this coupling is correct only if start_block mutates state before the delta is built. (2) The correctness depends entirely on start_block()/stop_active_block() side-effect ordering, which is fragile and easy to break in future edits. (3) Add a short comment documenting that start_block() must set state.block_index before the content_block_delta is constructed, or restructure to make the index explicit.

(1) The new field is named pending_content but semantically it holds content-text fragments buffered while a tool_use block is active. (2) 'pending_content' is generic and does not convey that it is specifically text emitted during tool_use that must be flushed as a trailing text block. (3) Consider a more descriptive name such as buffered_text_during_tool_use or deferred_text_fragments to clarify intent.

(1) `_fini()` now issues `os.fsync(fd)` on every `FastFileWriter.close()`. The fd-leak bug only requires `os.close(fd)`; the `os.fsync()` is an added synchronous data+metadata flush on the checkpoint save hot path. (2) The PR itself reports ~5% wall-time overhead from this fsync at ~10 GB/s; on high-frequency / large checkpoint-save loops this is a measurable, recurring throughput cost paid on every save, not just once. (3) If durability at close is not a hard requirement here, drop the `os.fsync(fd)` and keep only the `os.close(fd)` (the leak is fixed by close alone, as the PR notes). If durability must be guaranteed, gate the fsync behind a config flag defaulting to off, or document the tradeoff so users can opt out.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end checkpoint save wall time and effective GB/s over an N-iteration save/close loop with vs. without the os.fsync() call, at representative payload sizes.

findings.claim: (1) The PR justifies the fsync as 'required for correctness on the unaligned-tail path that re-opens the file as buffered I/O', but by the time `_fini()` runs, `self._aio_fd` is the O_DIRECT append fd re-opened at the end of `_unaligned_drain()`; the buffered-tail bytes were written and closed via a separate buffered fp (`open(..., 'ab'); fp.close()`), not through `self._aio_fd`. (2) A misleading rationale invites future maintainers to keep an expensive call for the wrong reason (and note that fsync on the O_DIRECT fd does flush the inode's dirty pages, so it is not a no-op, but the stated reason is imprecise). (3) Add a brief accurate comment: fsync on this fd flushes all dirty pages for the inode (including the earlier buffered-tail write) before close; the buffered fp itself is only closed, not fsynced.

(1) This is the actual fix: `stride_x_token` and `stride_o_token` are changed from `tl.constexpr` to `tl.int64` in both `_causal_conv1d_fwd_kernel` and `_causal_conv1d_update_kernel`. In Triton, when a Python `constexpr` int stride multiplies an int32 token index (e.g. `sequence_start_index * stride_x_token` at line 129, `(sequence_start_index + idx_tokens_last) * stride_x_token` at line 208, `idx_token * stride_x_token` in the inner loop, `... * stride_o_token` at line 462), the product is computed in int32. For the `(dim, cu_seqlen)` layout, `stride_x_token`/`stride_o_token` can equal `dim` (thousands), so `token_index * dim` overflows the int32 range (~2.1e9) once total tokens × dim exceeds ~2^31. The overflow wraps to a negative/garbage pointer offset, producing the reported Illegal Memory Access on long sequences. (2) Impact: silent OOB reads/writes and hard crashes for long-sequence Mamba/conv1d workloads. (3) The fix promoting these two strides to int64 forces the address arithmetic into 64-bit, which is correct. NOTE: this is a bug-fix, not a regression introduced by this commit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run causal_conv1d fwd + update with a single long sequence (total_tokens * dim > 2^31, e.g. dim=4096 and seqlen>=~600k, or batched sums exceeding the threshold) and verify no IMA and numerically-correct output vs a reference conv1d.

findings.claim: (1) Potential incomplete fix: the int64 promotion only covers `stride_x_token` and `stride_o_token`. Other per-token/per-block strides that also scale by potentially large indices remain `tl.constexpr` — notably `stride_conv_state_seq`/`stride_istate_seq` multiplied by `conv_states_output_coord`/`conv_states_input_coord` (cache line index, can be large for big cache pools) and `stride_cache_indices` multiplied by `idx_seq`. If the conv-state cache (num_cache_lines) is large enough that `cache_line_index * stride_conv_state_seq` exceeds int32, the same overflow class could recur on the conv_states pointer path. (2) Impact: residual IMA risk on the state-cache side for very large cache pools, not covered by this PR. (3) Verify whether `conv_states_input_coord`/`conv_states_output_coord` are already `.to(tl.int64)` (they are cast at load) so their product is int64 — if so this is safe; otherwise consider promoting `stride_conv_state_seq` as well. Flagging for confirmation rather than as a definite defect.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Stress test with a large conv_state cache (large num_cache_lines) where cache_line_index * stride_istate_seq approaches/exceeds 2^31 to confirm the state-cache pointer path does not also overflow.

findings.claim: (1) Changing `stride_x_token`/`stride_o_token` from `tl.constexpr` to `tl.int64` means these strides are no longer compile-time constants and address computations now use 64-bit integer arithmetic instead of 32-bit. (2) Impact: minor — 64-bit address math is slightly more expensive than 32-bit on GPUs and the compiler can no longer specialize on the stride value, but this is negligible relative to the load/store bandwidth of the kernel and is the standard correct pattern for large-tensor indexing. No action needed; noting the tradeoff for completeness. (3) No fix required; the correctness benefit clearly outweighs the marginal arithmetic cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark causal_conv1d fwd/update throughput on short-sequence (int32-safe) shapes before vs after to confirm the 64-bit stride promotion has no measurable regression.

findings.claim: (1) In load_audio_soundfile, the decompression-bomb guard relies solely on f.frames (metadata) before calling f.read(). (2) For formats/streams where soundfile reports frames == 0 or an unreliable frame count (some pipe/streaming inputs), the check `file_duration_s > max_duration_s` is 0 and the full unbounded f.read() still allocates all PCM into memory, defeating the limit for the soundfile backend. (3) Consider guarding by reading in bounded blocks (e.g. f.blocks(blocksize=...)) and aborting once accumulated samples exceed max_samples, mirroring the incremental per-frame check used in the pyav path, rather than trusting metadata alone.

(1) A new default duration limit VLLM_MAX_AUDIO_DECODE_DURATION_S=600s is now enforced on the speech-to-text decode path unconditionally. (2) This is a behavior/default change: users who previously submitted valid audio longer than 10 minutes (the endpoint otherwise supports chunking via max_audio_clip_s) will now get their requests rejected with a ValueError, silently altering behavior for existing workloads. (3) Confirm 600s is compatible with the largest legitimately supported clip length (and with the audio chunking flow that assumes long inputs get split), and document the new limit prominently; consider a higher or unset default if long-form transcription is a supported use case.

(1) In load_audio_pyav, max_samples = int(sr * max_duration_s) is computed from the target/output sample rate `sr`, and total_samples is accumulated from arr.shape[-1] which is at the same rate (resampled frames at `sr`, or native frames where sr==native_sr because needs_resampling is False). This rate-consistency is correct, but is non-obvious and would silently break if the non-resampling threshold (abs_tol=1e-6) ever admits a case where native_sr != sr. (2) A future change loosening the resampling tolerance would make the sample-count limit off by the sr/native_sr ratio without any test catching it. (3) Add a brief comment stating that total_samples and max_samples are both in units of the effective output rate `sr`, or compute max_samples from native_sr in the non-resampling branch explicitly.

(1) The MoE marlin FP8 arch list `MARLIN_MOE_FP8_ARCHS "8.9;12.0;12.1"` (parent line 1131) was NOT updated in this PR, while the non-MoE `MARLIN_FP8_ARCHS` was updated to the version-conditional `8.9;12.0f` / `8.9;12.0a;12.1a` form. (2) The PR title/intent is specifically "Marlin MoE: include SM 12.x in default arch list", so leaving the MoE FP8 arch list on the plain `12.0;12.1` targets is inconsistent: on CUDA >= 13.0 the plain `12.0` target does not produce a family-conditional cubin covering the whole SM12x family (that is precisely why the sibling lists were switched to `12.0f`). MoE FP8 kernels may therefore fail to run on SM 12.1+ parts under CUDA 13, defeating the PR's goal. (3) Update `MARLIN_MOE_FP8_ARCHS` with the same version-conditional pattern (`8.9;12.0f` on >=13.0, `8.9;12.0a;12.1a` otherwise).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Configure with CUDA 13.0 targeting SM121 and verify a MoE FP8 (W4A8) marlin cubin is emitted and dispatches at runtime.

findings.claim: (1) The new FP8 capability check accepts `major_capability == 12` (any SM12x) as valid for Marlin W4A8-FP8, whereas the CMake arch lists only compile FP8 cubins for `12.0`/`12.1` (or the `12.0f` family target). (2) If a future SM12x part (e.g. 12.2+) exists for which no cubin was compiled, the runtime check would pass but kernel dispatch (`get_marlin_kernel`) could return `MarlinDefault` and hit the generic 'Unsupported shapes' error rather than a clear 'arch not compiled' message. This is a minor mismatch between the runtime gate and the compile-time arch set. (3) Consider keeping the runtime check aligned with what is actually compiled, or rely on the family-conditional `12.0f` cubin to genuinely cover the whole family. Low risk given `12.0f` is family-wide on CUDA 13.

(1) The change replaces `param_list[0].ds_tensor.ds_quant_scale.dtype` with `param.ds_tensor.ds_quant_scale.dtype` when allocating the flat output buffer for the quantize-scale all-gather in `_allgather_params_coalesced`. (2) This matters because each scale buffer is later filled by its own `all_gather_into_tensor(allgather_quantize_scale[param_idx], param.ds_tensor.ds_quant_scale, ...)` call, and torch's collective requires the output tensor dtype to match the input tensor dtype. With mixed scale dtypes (e.g. bf16 base weights + fp32 LoRA adapters gathered as a persistent coalesced set) the old code allocated every scale buffer with param_list[0]'s scale dtype, causing an 'output tensor must have the same type as input tensor' RuntimeError. (3) The fix is exactly the correct one: since the loop already binds `param = param_list[len(allgather_quantize_scale)]` and uses it for `tensor_size`, using `param.ds_tensor.ds_quant_scale.dtype` for the buffer dtype restores per-param consistency. This matches the main output buffer (fixed in #8073) and the sequential path `_allgather_params_sequential`. This is a correct and minimal fix; confidence reflects that the change is right and I'm confirming rather than flagging a defect.

(1) `query_fault()` computes `has_fault = (current != _last_mask).any()` but never updates `_last_mask` to the current mask after the comparison. Since `_last_mask` is initialized to zeros and never reassigned to `current`, every call compares against the initial all-zeros baseline rather than a genuine previous snapshot. (2) This defeats the diff-based fault-detection semantics: once any rank becomes active/masked (nonzero) the mask permanently differs from zeros and a fault will be reported on every subsequent step (false positive), while a fault that later 'heals' back to the same values as the initial state would go undetected. The intent (detecting a change relative to the previous step) is not achieved. (3) After computing `has_fault`, assign the snapshot back, e.g. `NixlEPAll2AllManager._last_mask.copy_(current)` (and analogously for DeepEPLL), so the next call compares against the actual previous mask. Confirm the intended baseline semantics — if instead the mask is a monotonically-updated buffer that only changes on real faults, this diffing scheme should be documented.

(1) In `get_output()`, `self._has_fault.item()` forces a host-device synchronization on every step when `check_ep_fault` is enabled. The fault tensor copy in `__init__` is issued with `non_blocking=True`, but `.item()` in `get_output()` will block until the copy completes. (2) This adds a per-step CPU-GPU sync on the async output path for every MoE+DP run with a fault-tolerant backend, which can degrade decode throughput — this is exactly the kind of Python-side tensor value check the training/inference loop should avoid. (3) Consider checking the fault less frequently (e.g. every N steps), or ensure the copy is well-overlapped and the `.item()` truly lands after unrelated compute so the sync cost is hidden; benchmark the throughput delta with fault checking on vs off.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode throughput (tokens/s) for an EP MoE model with data_parallel_size>1 and a fault-tolerant all2all backend, comparing check_ep_fault=True vs False.

findings.claim: (1) `get_handle()` assigns `DeepEPLLAll2AllManager._buffer = handle` (a class attribute) every time a handle is created, and `query_active_mask()` asserts on and reads this single class-level `_buffer`. With multiple DeepEPLL manager instances or multiple buffers per process, the class-level `_buffer` reflects only the most recently created handle, so `query_active_mask`/`query_fault` may query the wrong buffer. (2) This can cause fault queries against a stale/incorrect buffer, producing incorrect fault signals. (3) Prefer storing the buffer on the instance (or key it appropriately) unless a strict single-buffer-per-process invariant is guaranteed and documented. Note DeepEPLL currently sets `support_fault_tolerance = False`, so this path is not yet exercised — flagging for when FT is enabled.
