(1) `self.check_ep_fault` is set once in `__init__` based on `get_ep_all2all_manager().support_fault_tolerance`. For the NIXL EP backend this is True. However `query_active_mask`/`query_fault` for NIXL assert `NixlEPAll2AllManager._buffer is not None`. If a step issues the async output before any all2all buffer has been initialized (e.g. a dummy/profiling run or first step before get_handle), `query_fault()` will hit the assertion and crash. (2) A hard assert on the hot async-output path can abort the engine on legitimate pre-buffer-init steps. (3) Guard `query_fault` to no-op / return no-fault when the buffer is not yet initialized, or gate `check_ep_fault` on buffer readiness rather than only backend support.

(1) `query_fault()` returns a torch.Tensor (0-dim GPU scalar) but the docstring says 'Returns has_fault scalar' without specifying it is a device tensor requiring `.item()`/`.to('cpu')` by the caller. (2) The ambiguous return-type contract already led the caller to add a `.to('cpu', non_blocking=True)` + `.item()` dance; a clearer contract reduces misuse and hidden syncs. (3) Document the return as a device-resident bool tensor (and its device/dtype), and consider a shape/dtype assertion at this parallelism boundary.

(1) The refactor changed the condition that increments `history_tool_call_cnt` and sets `tools_streamed[i]`. Previously these were unconditionally updated on the first delta of a named tool call (when `function_name_returned[i]` was False), using `created_new_tool_call` semantics. Now the new code only increments when `delta_message.tool_calls[0].id is not None`. This depends on `extract_named_tool_call_streaming` returning a tool call with a non-None `id` exactly on the first delta. However, the loop that previously read `function_name_returned[i]` before the call now feeds `function_name_returned[i]` directly into the helper, and the helper sets it to True internally; the `id is not None` gate must reliably fire once. If the very first delta_text for a tool were empty/None-id (e.g. tokenizer edge cases) the counter and tools_streamed could desync from prior behavior. (2) Impact: mis-tracking history_tool_call_cnt affects tool_call_id generation (idx) for kimi_k2/multi-tool sequences and tools_streamed affects finish_reason emission. (3) Fix: assert the helper always yields id!=None on the transition function_name_returned False->True, and add a test with multiple sequential named tool calls verifying history_tool_call_cnt increments exactly once per tool.

(1) The fallback delegate call to `self.extract_tool_calls_streaming(...)` passes `request` with a `# type: ignore[arg-type]` because the signature now accepts `ChatCompletionRequest | ResponsesRequest` but the delegated `extract_tool_calls_streaming` still declares only `ChatCompletionRequest`. For the named/required tool_choice branches this is handled before reaching here, but a `ResponsesRequest` with tool_choice='auto' will now flow into a method typed for ChatCompletionRequest. (2) Impact: if the underlying tool_parser reads ChatCompletionRequest-only attributes, a ResponsesRequest could hit an AttributeError at runtime; the type:ignore hides this. (3) Fix: widen `extract_tool_calls_streaming` signature to the union or route ResponsesRequest auto-tool parsing explicitly, and add a ResponsesRequest streaming auto-tool test.

(1) `_extract_tool_calls_streaming` for the named-tool branch ignores `tool_call_array_index` (relies on the helper's new default of 0), whereas the chat serving path passes `tool_call_array_index=i`. In the Responses/DelegatingParser path there is no explicit array index, so all named tool call deltas will use index=0. (2) Impact: for a single forced named tool this is correct, but if multiple named tool deltas were ever streamed the array index would collide at 0. Currently forced named tool_choice yields a single tool, so behavior is fine, but the implicit default couples correctness to that assumption silently. (3) Fix: pass an explicit index or document that named tool_choice is always single-tool so index 0 is guaranteed.

(1) The pooling path's `_maybe_get_adapters` semantics changed: the parent `PoolingServingBase._maybe_get_adapters(ctx)` took a `ctx`, mutated `ctx.lora_request` as a side effect, and raised `VLLMNotFoundError` on an unknown model; the removed override is now inherited from `BaseServing._maybe_get_adapters(request)`, which instead *returns* the LoRARequest and raises a plain `ValueError` for unknown models. The call site was updated to `ctx.lora_request = self._maybe_get_adapters(ctx.request)`, but the raised exception type is now `ValueError` instead of `VLLMNotFoundError`. (2) `vllm/entrypoints/serve/utils/error_response.py` special-cases `VLLMNotFoundError` (-> 404 NotFound); a plain `ValueError` will be mapped to a different status (e.g. 400/500), changing the HTTP error contract for pooling endpoints on unknown-model requests. Existing test `test_pooling_maybe_get_adapters_unknown_model_raises` asserts `VLLMNotFoundError`. (3) Have `BaseServing._maybe_get_adapters` raise `VLLMNotFoundError` (matching the removed pooling override), or override in the pooling class to preserve the 404 behavior.

(1) The removed pooling `_maybe_get_adapters` supported `supports_default_mm_loras=True` and populated `ctx.lora_request` from `_get_active_default_mm_loras`; the new call site `ctx.lora_request = self._maybe_get_adapters(ctx.request)` invokes the inherited `BaseServing` version with the default `supports_default_mm_loras=False`. (2) If any pooling subclass relied on default multimodal LoRA matching, this silently disables it. (3) Verify no pooling caller depended on `supports_default_mm_loras=True`; if some did, thread the flag through the new call.

(1) The class was renamed `PoolingServingBase` -> `PoolingBaseServing` and its MRO changed to `class PoolingBaseServing(ABC, BaseServing)`. External/third-party code (and the existing test file `tests/entrypoints/serve/lora/test_serving_models.py` which imports `PoolingServingBase`) referencing the old name will break unless updated. (2) Renaming a public-ish base class without an alias breaks imports for downstream users. (3) Keep a backward-compat alias `PoolingServingBase = PoolingBaseServing` or confirm all references (including tests) are updated in this PR.

(1) MRO ordering `class PoolingBaseServing(ABC, BaseServing)` places `ABC` before `BaseServing`. Since `BaseServing` is a concrete (non-ABC) base defining `__init__`, `super().__init__(...)` still resolves correctly to `BaseServing.__init__`, so behavior is fine, but listing `ABC` first is unconventional (usually mixins/abstract markers go last). (2) Minor readability/consistency concern; no functional impact verified. (3) Consider `class PoolingBaseServing(BaseServing, ABC)` for consistency with `PoolingServing(PoolingBaseServing, ABC)`.

(1) `OpenAIServing` (previously in `vllm/entrypoints/openai/engine/serving.py`) is renamed/moved to `GenerateBaseServing` in the new `vllm/entrypoints/generate/base/serving.py`, and the helper functions `format_token_id_placeholder`, `resolve_token_id_placeholder`, `clamp_prompt_logprobs`, `GenerationError` re-export were relocated. Consumers importing `OpenAIServing` (or these helpers) from `vllm.entrypoints.openai.engine.serving` will break if the old module was left without shims. (2) This is a public entrypoint base class; downstream/plugin code and out-of-tree serving classes commonly subclass it. (3) Confirm the old `openai/engine/serving.py` retains backward-compat aliases (e.g. `OpenAIServing = GenerateBaseServing`) or that all in-repo imports are exhaustively updated; the diff is truncated so verify the old module's final state.

(1) `_setup_triton_jit_hook` installs `knobs.runtime.jit_post_compile_hook` globally in the Triton runtime, but this hook is *also* triggered by torch.compile/Inductor and any lazily-compiled Triton kernel that legitimately compiles the first time a new (but expected) batch shape is seen after warmup. Because vLLM warmup cannot possibly cover every input shape/sequence-length/num-tokens combination the server will actually serve, many *expected* first-time compilations will be logged as WARNINGs falsely claiming a 'latency spike'. (2) This produces misleading noise for operators and can hide real regressions. (3) Consider scoping the warning to compilations that were expected to be covered by warmup, or downgrade the message to indicate it may be a benign first-time compile, and document that it is heuristic.

(1) The chained `existing_hook` call is wrapped by the new hook, but if the previously-registered hook raises, the exception propagates out of `_on_jit_compile` into the Triton compile path even though the docstring's stated intent is to make the monitor's own hook crash-proof. (2) A third-party or later-registered hook raising would then break every subsequent JIT compilation during inference, a much worse failure than the monitor was designed to prevent. (3) Wrap the `existing_hook(**kwargs)` call in a try/except that logs and swallows, or explicitly document that chained hooks are trusted to be exception-safe.

(1) The warning uses `logger.warning_once`, which is backed by an unbounded `@lru_cache` keyed on `(logger, msg, *args)` where `args=(fn_name,)`. With many distinct kernel names (dozens of Triton kernels x variants) this grows the process-global lru_cache, and more importantly the dedup is per-kernel-name, not per-compilation-event, so repeated recompiles of the *same* kernel with different configs (the primary signal this monitor is meant to catch) are silently suppressed after the first. (2) This can mask ongoing recompilation churn — the exact latency problem being monitored. (3) Consider including the compile key/shape in the dedup args, or use a bounded counter with periodic reporting instead of `warning_once`.

(1) `knobs.autotuning.print = True` is set globally and never restored; it stays on for the entire process lifetime and is not gated by `is_active()`/deactivation. (2) There is no `deactivate()` path, so once activated the monitor's global Triton mutations (autotuning print + jit hook) persist even if a caller expected to toggle monitoring; this is only a concern for embedded/library usage but is undocumented. (3) Either add a `deactivate()` that restores the prior `jit_post_compile_hook` and autotuning.print value, or document explicitly that activation is permanent for the process.

(1) Semantic change in the fallback path: previously, when grad_scale_func was set, the MTP loss scale was computed as `config.grad_scale_func(torch.ones(1, device=device))`. The new code preserves this fallback but the priority ordering means that once a user sets `mtp_grad_scale_func`, the grad_scale_func is completely ignored for MTP. (2) Risk: For the MoE aux-loss path a few lines above, the loss scale is still derived exclusively from grad_scale_func. If a user configures mtp_grad_scale_func to return a value with different semantics/magnitude than what grad_scale_func(ones) would produce, the MTP and MoE aux-loss scales will silently diverge, potentially altering the effective loss balance for existing MTP+MoE models. This is a subtle behavioral change worth documenting. (3) Fix: Add a note in the docstring / arguments clarifying that mtp_grad_scale_func only affects the MTP loss and does not affect MoE aux-loss scaling, so users understand the two are decoupled.

(1) The fallback branch calls `config.grad_scale_func(torch.ones(1, device=device))` and then passes the result through `_normalize_loss_scale`, which additionally does `torch.as_tensor(loss_scale, device=device)`. Since grad_scale_func already receives a tensor on `device`, its output is normally on `device`; the extra as_tensor is a harmless no-op copy avoidance. However, for the mtp_grad_scale_func branch the function may return a scalar/tensor on an arbitrary device (as the test does with device='cpu'), so as_tensor performs a device transfer. This is fine, but if mtp_grad_scale_func returns a non-contiguous or non-float scalar there is no dtype normalization — the downstream MTPLossAutoScaler multiplies the loss by this scale, so a mismatched dtype (e.g. int) scale could change the loss dtype. (2) Impact: low, but a wrong-dtype scale could break autograd dtype expectations. (3) Fix: consider casting the normalized scale to the loss/output dtype, or assert a floating dtype.

(1) `_get_mtp_loss_scale` is called on every microbatch's forward_step_calc_loss when mtp_num_layers is set, invoking mtp_grad_scale_func()/grad_scale_func() and creating a fresh tensor each call. This mirrors the pre-existing MoE aux-loss pattern so it is not a regression, and the cost is negligible relative to a forward pass. (2) Impact: minimal. (3) No action needed; noting for completeness that the scale is recomputed per microbatch rather than cached, consistent with the existing design.

(1) The new alias-merging loop is inserted into `create_speculative_config` *before* the pre-existing `if self.speculative_config is None: return None` guard. In the diff, the new code assigns `self.speculative_config = {}` and populates it whenever any of `spec_method`/`spec_model`/`spec_tokens` is set. But the parent code (line 1637) already contained `if self.speculative_config is None: return None` followed by `self.speculative_config.update(...)`. Need to confirm the new block sits above the original guard so that a `speculative_config` freshly created from an alias is not immediately discarded by the `return None`. (2) If ordering is wrong, passing only `--spec-model X` (without `--speculative-config`) would still return None and silently disable speculative decoding. (3) Verify the new merge loop executes before the `None`-guard and that after the loop the guard sees the populated dict.

(1) `--spec-tokens` is registered with `speculative_kwargs['num_speculative_tokens']`, whose kwargs derive from the pydantic field `num_speculative_tokens: int = Field(default=None, gt=0)`. That field has a non-None `default` embedded in the argparse kwargs (default=None via the Field, but get_kwargs sets `default` to the field default). Because `spec_tokens` is a *separate* EngineArgs attribute with its own default None, the argparse `default` from `speculative_kwargs` should not clobber it — but the reused kwargs also carry the field's `help`/`type` referencing `num_speculative_tokens`. Confirm the argparse `default` pulled from the SpeculativeConfig field is None so it does not accidentally set spec_tokens to a non-None sentinel and trigger the mutual-exclusion / always-populate path. (2) A non-None default would make spec_tokens always populate speculative_config, unexpectedly enabling spec decode. (3) Explicitly set default=None on the three added add_argument calls to be safe.

(1) The alias `spec_tokens` maps to `num_speculative_tokens` and `spec_method`/`spec_model` map to `method`/`model`. These are convenience CLI aliases so abbreviation is acceptable, but `spec_tokens` is ambiguous (could read as 'speculative tokens list'). (2) Minor readability/discoverability concern only. (3) Consider documenting in help text that `--spec-tokens` == `num_speculative_tokens` (an int count), which the reused help string already does.

This PR deletes docs/user-guide/features/custom_fsdp.md (renamed/consolidated into megatron_fsdp.md) and updates the references in docs/index.md and docs/user-guide/features/index.md, but docs/user-guide/parallelism-guide.md line 230 still links to the deleted file: `Refer to [Megatron FSDP](features/custom_fsdp.md)`. This file is not in the changed-files set, so the link is now broken/dangling, which will fail doc builds that check links and produce a 404 for readers. Fix: update the link target to features/megatron_fsdp.md in parallelism-guide.md.

(1) Only the single ba.chunk() site in the CUDA/native forward path (diff line ~811) was replaced with self.split_ba(); the file has additional ba.chunk(2, dim=-1) sites in the ROCm forward (forward_hip, parent line 781), the CPU forward (forward_cpu, parent line 884), and the torch.compiled prepare_gdn_attention_core_inputs (parent lines 557 and 595). Those sites still assume ba was TP-sharded. (2) When in_proj_ba is replicated (maybe_disable_tp returns True), b/a produced from those unpatched sites will have the FULL num_v_heads dimension while the rest of the pipeline (dt_bias, A_log, mixed_qkv/z, state shapes) is sized to num_v_heads // tp_size. This produces shape mismatches / silently wrong results on non-CUDA-forward code paths, and for CUDA the maybe_disable_tp guard is is_cuda()-gated so forward_cuda is the intended path — but prepare_gdn_attention_core_inputs is used by the CUDA path too (parent line 1066 calls it). If the CUDA forward reaches prepare_gdn_attention_core_inputs for the interleaved-vs-non-interleaved branch, the replicated b/a will not be sliced there. (3) Fix: route ALL b/a extraction from in_proj_ba output through split_ba (or slice inside prepare_gdn_attention_core_inputs), or assert the disable-tp case only ever hits the patched forward path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run Qwen3.5 GDN with AWQMarlin/AutoGPTQ quant at TP>=2 through decode (prepare_gdn_attention_core_inputs) and CPU/ROCm forward paths; assert b/a last-dim == num_v_heads//tp_size and outputs match TP=1 reference.

findings.claim: (1) split_ba slices b/a with ba_chunk = self.num_v_heads // self.tp_size and ba_start = self.tp_rank * ba_chunk, assuming num_v_heads is evenly divisible by tp_size. (2) If num_v_heads is not divisible by tp_size, integer floor division silently drops the remainder heads and the per-rank slices will not tile the full head range, producing wrong/incomplete b/a without any error. Elsewhere in this class divide(self.num_v_heads, self.tp_size) is used which asserts divisibility. (3) Fix: use divide(self.num_v_heads, self.tp_size) here for consistency, or add an explicit assert that num_v_heads % tp_size == 0 in the disable-tp branch.

(1) maybe_disable_tp() reads self.gqa_interleaved_layout, but in __init__ the ba_proj call `self.disable_tp_for_ba_proj = self.maybe_disable_tp(quant_config)` runs after create_ba_proj which already used gqa_interleaved_layout; verify self.gqa_interleaved_layout is set BEFORE maybe_disable_tp is first invoked. In __init__ gqa_interleaved_layout is assigned early, so this is likely fine — but the diff also adds `disable_tp=self.maybe_disable_tp(quant_config)` inside create_ba_proj (the RowParallel/Merged linear construction shown near diff line 483). That call site depends on self.gqa_interleaved_layout being set; if create_ba_proj is ever invoked before self.gqa_interleaved_layout assignment (e.g. refactor), maybe_disable_tp would raise AttributeError. (2) Risk: fragile init ordering coupling. (3) Fix: pass gqa_interleaved_layout explicitly to maybe_disable_tp or assert it is set.

(1) maybe_disable_tp gates on isinstance(quant_config, (AWQMarlinConfig, AutoGPTQConfig, INCConfig)). AutoGPTQ/AWQ commonly resolve to a Marlin kernel only under certain conditions; INCConfig (Intel Neural Compressor) may not use Marlin at all and thus not need the MIN_THREAD_N=64 workaround. (2) Including INCConfig unconditionally could needlessly replicate the projection (extra memory + compute per rank) when Marlin's threshold does not apply, or conversely miss GPTQMarlinConfig/other configs that DO use Marlin. (3) Fix: gate on whether the resolved kernel is actually Marlin (e.g. check the linear method / min_thread_n) rather than an isinstance allowlist of config classes.

This fix silently drops the sleeping-engine (is_sleeping) guard. (1) The parent code intended two conditions: (a) skip the dummy pass when `self.is_sleeping()` (which the comment says also covers the KV-offload window before `model_executor.is_sleeping` flips), and (b) skip when `self.model_executor.is_sleeping`. The new code collapses this to only `elif not self.model_executor.is_sleeping:`, dropping the `self.is_sleeping()` check entirely. (2) Per the (now-removed) comment, `self.is_sleeping()` covers the KV-offload window before `model_executor.is_sleeping` flips; without it, a dummy batch could execute during that window and touch KV/model state that is mid-offload, causing incorrect behavior or errors in DP sleep/wake flows. (3) While fixing the IndentationError, verify whether the intended condition should be `elif not self.is_sleeping():` (or a combination `not self.is_sleeping() and not self.model_executor.is_sleeping`) rather than only `not self.model_executor.is_sleeping`. Confirm the semantic against the DP sleep design before merging.

(1) The new `--inference-shards` argument is added and parsed into `args.inference_shards`, but the value is never read/consumed anywhere in the codebase at this commit (grep for `inference_shards`, `spec_declares_disaggregation`, `normalize_shard_specs`, `build_inference_pg_collections_for_shards` finds no consumer). (2) This is a dangling public CLI arg: a user who sets it (e.g. `--inference-shards tp=2,role=prefill+tp=1,role=decode`) gets a silent no-op rather than the disaggregated inference they asked for, which is a confusing/misleading UX and a latent correctness trap. (3) Since this is 'MR1' of a series with deferred wiring, add a validation hook in `validate_args` that either raises NotImplementedError when `--inference-shards` is set (until wired) or a TODO/migration note documenting the deferred consumption, so the arg cannot be silently ignored.

(1) The `role` field on `InferenceShardSpec` (and `VALID_ROLES` validation, plus `spec_declares_disaggregation`) is declared and validated but has no runtime read/use path in this commit — `build_inference_pg_collections_for_shards` builds identical PG collections for prefill and decode shards and never branches on `spec.role`. (2) Per the Mandatory Unused-New-Identifier check, a validated-but-unconsumed field is dead complexity that can drift from the eventual consumer's expectations before it is wired. (3) Either add an explicit TODO + migration note that `role` consumption is deferred to a follow-up MR, or defer introducing the field until it is used.

(1) `InferenceShardSpec.to_dict()` omits the `cp` key while including tp/pp/ep/dp/expt_tp. (2) Round-tripping via `InferenceShardSpec(**to_dict())` still works because `cp` defaults to 1 and inference shards require cp==1, so today this is harmless; but it is an asymmetry that will silently drop `cp` if the cp==1 restriction is ever relaxed (the docstring already says cp is accepted 'for forward-compatibility'). (3) Include `cp` in `to_dict()` for consistency, or add a comment noting the intentional omission.

(1) `parse_inference_shards_spec` is documented (return type annotation and docstring) as returning `List[dict]` / `List[InferenceShardSpec]`, but it actually builds a `List[InferenceShardSpec]` and returns `_finalize_and_validate(...)` which is typed `List[InferenceShardSpec]`. The `-> List[dict]` annotation is stale/wrong. (2) A wrong type annotation is worse than none: it misleads callers and type checkers into treating elements as dicts when they are frozen dataclasses. (3) Fix the return annotation to `List[InferenceShardSpec]`.

(1) `build_inference_pg_collections_for_shards` requires every rank in the total world to call it so the world-collective `dist.new_group`/`new_subgroups_by_enumeration` calls succeed for every shard, but it does not assert that the sum of shard world sizes equals `total_world_size` at the shard-builder level (it relies on `normalize_shard_specs`/`_finalize_and_validate` doing so). If a caller ever passes pre-validated specs whose ranks under-fill `total_world_size`, ranks beyond the last shard's window would never enter any `dist.new_group` call, deadlocking the collective. (2) A partition/total mismatch causes a hard-to-diagnose distributed hang rather than a clear error. (3) This is currently covered by `_finalize_and_validate`, so low severity, but consider an explicit post-loop assert `offset == total_world_size` to make the world-collective contract self-evident at the call site.

(1) The `speedometer` timing loop wraps the JIT-compiled `train_step_fn` calls in `time.time()` without ever calling `.block_until_ready()` (or `jax.block_until_ready`) on the returned loss/grads. (2) JAX dispatches computations asynchronously; without blocking on the final result, the measured wall-clock time can under-measure or mis-attribute GPU compute time, making the tutorial's reported speedup numbers (e.g. '1.6x faster') unreliable — the whole point of this benchmarking doc. In practice back-to-back dispatch of the same fn does serialize on buffer reuse, but the last iteration's completion is not awaited before `end = time.time()`. (3) Add `jax.block_until_ready((loss, param_grads, other_grads))` inside/after the timing loop before recording `end`. Note this pattern is copied from the pre-existing docs/examples/quickstart_jax_utils.py, so it is a low-priority carry-over.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare reported 'Mean time' with vs. without block_until_ready on a single-GPU dense GEMM to confirm the numbers are stable/accurate.

findings.claim: (1) In `speedometer`, when `autocast_kwargs is None` the code sets `model_init_fn = None`, but `model_init_fn` is never read anywhere in the function afterward (it is an unused parameter). (2) Dead assignment adds confusion in an example intended to teach users. (3) Remove the unused `model_init_fn` parameter and the `model_init_fn = None` assignment, or wire it to an actual use. This is carried over from the original utils file.

(1) The `shard_variables` helper only shards the `Dense_0` kernel and hard-codes the module key `"Dense_0"` and closes over module-level globals `x`/`dy` rather than taking them as arguments. (2) For a tutorial this is brittle and slightly misleading — readers copying the pattern into a real model with different layer names or multiple Dense layers will silently only shard one kernel. (3) Consider documenting the assumption (single Dense layer named Dense_0) in a comment, or generalize to iterate params. Minor since it is illustrative example code.

(1) record_transfer/record_failed_* each acquire a threading.Lock on every KV transfer, and these run on the sender thread pool in the transfer hot path. (2) A global lock per record serializes all writer threads and adds acquire/release overhead per transfer; if transfer counts are high (many small descriptor batches), this is measurable contention. (3) Since transfers themselves are network-bound (ms-scale) and the critical section is tiny (list.append), the impact is likely negligible, but consider whether a lock is truly needed given CPython list.append is already atomic, or use per-thread accumulation. Only the clone_and_reset alignment invariant genuinely needs the lock.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark record_transfer throughput single-thread vs 4 concurrent writers with/without lock; also measure end-to-end P-worker transfer QPS with stats enabled vs disabled.

findings.claim: (1) is_empty() and the num_successful_transfers property read self.data lists (len()) without holding _lock, while writer threads append concurrently. (2) The dataclass docstring claims _lock 'serializes record_* against clone_and_reset so each row's appends are atomic and column lengths stay aligned', but is_empty()/num_successful_transfers are called from the reader (get_kv_connector_stats -> is_empty) outside the lock; reading len() of a list under concurrent append is safe in CPython but only because of the GIL, not because of the documented lock — the invariant claim is slightly misleading. (3) Either take the lock in is_empty()/num_successful_transfers for consistency, or clarify the docstring that unlocked len() reads rely on GIL atomicity. Low real risk given GIL.

(1) In _send_blocks the failure branch recomputes sum(lengths) and len(src_ptrs) for the warning message, and the success branch computes sum(lengths)/len(src_ptrs) for record_transfer. (2) Redundant recomputation is trivial but the values could be computed once (total_bytes = sum(lengths); num_descs = len(src_ptrs)) before the branch for clarity and to avoid double iteration over lengths. (3) Hoist total_bytes/num_descs above the if ret_value == 0 check.

(1) aggregate(other) iterates other.data.items() and indexes self.data[k], assuming both instances share the identical key set. (2) If a differently-versioned or differently-typed KVConnectorStats subclass is ever aggregated (e.g. via MultiConnector or a snapshot produced by an older worker before a schema change), self.data[k] will KeyError. This mirrors NIXL's implementation so it is consistent, but there is no assert/guard on matching schemas. (3) Optionally assert set(other.data) <= set(self.data) or handle missing keys, to fail loudly rather than KeyError in the logger path. Low priority since same-connector aggregation is the documented contract.

(1) The fix sets `return_dict=False` and then relies on direct positional indexing (`[0]`) / list semantics on the `apply_chat_template` return value, whereas the sibling `SFTTokenizer` (megatron/core/tokenizers/text/libraries/sft_tokenizer.py) already solved the same 'newer transformers changes return type' problem generically via the `_extract_token_ids` helper that handles dict/BatchEncoding/Encoding/list/ndarray. (2) The current approach is more fragile: it depends on `return_dict=False` remaining supported and on the exact shape contract (np array of shape (1, seq_len) for the first call, plain list for the second call, since only the first call passes `return_tensors="np"`). A future transformers change to the default/return contract could silently break masking again. (3) Consider reusing/extracting the same `_extract_token_ids` normalization logic in the multimodal tokenizer for consistency and robustness. The current fix is functionally correct for the pinned transformers version; this is a maintainability suggestion only.

(1) The new clamping logic changed the overflow guard. Previously the request was rejected if `len(prompt_tokens) + num_tokens_to_generate > max_sequence_length` OR `num_tokens_to_generate < 0`. The new code computes `remaining_tokens = max_sequence_length - len(prompt_tokens)` and only FAILS the request when `num_tokens_to_generate < 0 or remaining_tokens < 0`. But `remaining_tokens < 0` requires `len(prompt_tokens) > max_sequence_length`, i.e. the prompt alone strictly exceeds the sequence budget. If `len(prompt_tokens) == max_sequence_length` exactly, `remaining_tokens == 0` (not < 0), so the request is NOT failed; instead `num_tokens_to_generate` gets clamped to 0. (2) Impact: a request whose prompt exactly fills the sequence length previously failed cleanly, but now proceeds with a generation budget of 0 tokens. Downstream code at lines ~1216-1227 special-cases `num_tokens_to_generate == 0` with `[:keep]` slicing — worth verifying that a 0-token generation request does not cause an empty output slice, a hang in the step loop, or an assertion later. (3) Fix: verify that a clamped-to-0 request terminates gracefully (produces empty output and finishes), or explicitly FAIL when `remaining_tokens <= 0` rather than `< 0` to preserve prior reject semantics for the prompt-fills-sequence case.

(1) The clamp only guards against exceeding `max_sequence_length`, but the original code path immediately below still separately checks `len(prompt_tokens) > self.context.max_tokens` for the non-chunked-prefill case. The new clamp does not account for `max_tokens` (the per-step total-token budget) — only `max_sequence_length`. (2) Impact: for engines where `max_tokens < max_sequence_length`, clamping `num_tokens_to_generate` to `max_sequence_length - len(prompt_tokens)` may still leave a value that interacts with the max_tokens budget, though this typically manifests at scheduling time rather than at add. (3) Fix: confirm mirroring vllm's behavior (clamp to remaining sequence budget) is the intended semantics and that max_tokens scheduling handles the clamped value safely.

(1) In `decode_torchcodec_frames`, the code returns `list(frame_indices)` as the `valid` indices unconditionally, assuming `get_frames_at` always returns exactly the requested frames. Unlike the OpenCV/PyAV backends which track which frames were actually decoded (dropping failed ones), TorchCodec's `get_frames_at` will raise an exception for out-of-range indices rather than gracefully returning fewer frames. (2) Why it matters: If `compute_frames_index_to_sample` ever produces an index >= total_frames (which can happen when metadata is inexact, especially with `seek_mode="approximate"` where `total_frames` is derived from `int(duration * fps)`), `get_frames_at` will throw and the whole request fails, whereas other backends degrade gracefully. The `if len(valid) < len(frame_idx)` warning path in load_bytes is therefore unreachable for torchcodec. (3) Fix: clamp requested indices to `[0, total_frames-1]` before calling `get_frames_at`, or catch the error and fall back, mirroring the drop-invalid-frame behavior of the other backends.

(1) The new keyword-only parameters `num_ffmpeg_threads` and `seek_mode` were added only to `VideoBackend.load_bytes`, but the subclass overrides (`Qwen3VLVideoBackend`, `Qwen2VLVideoBackend`, `DynamicVideoBackend`, `GLM46VVideoBackend`, `GLMGAVideoBackend`, `NemotronVLVideoBackend`) still have signatures without these params and forward via `**kwargs`. Callers passing `num_ffmpeg_threads`/`seek_mode` through these subclasses will have them captured by `**kwargs` and forwarded — verify they reach `super().load_bytes` correctly rather than being silently dropped or duplicated. (2) Why it matters: if a subclass forwards `**kwargs` but also names `backend` explicitly, a user-supplied `seek_mode`/`num_ffmpeg_threads` may be forwarded into `compute_frames_index_to_sample(**kwargs)` in the base and raise an unexpected-keyword error, or be dropped for the torchcodec path. (3) Fix: confirm the forwarding path preserves these kwargs to the base load_bytes for torchcodec, and add tests exercising seek_mode/num_ffmpeg_threads through subclasses.

(1) `_check_frame_pixel_limit` is called for the torchcodec path AFTER `make_torchcodec_decoder`, and with `seek_mode="exact"` (the default) the decoder constructor scans the entire file on creation. This means the pixel-limit rejection cannot short-circuit the expensive full-file scan for oversized frames. (2) Why it matters: an oversized/malicious video still pays the full exact-mode scan cost before being rejected, defeating the purpose of the pre-decode pixel guard used by other backends. (3) Fix: this is inherent to needing metadata (width/height) before decode; consider documenting that the guard runs post-construction for torchcodec, or reading dimensions via a cheaper metadata probe. Low severity as it only affects rejected videos.

(1) `decode_torchcodec_frames` returns `np.empty((0,), dtype=np.uint8)` (a 1-D array) for the empty case, while a successful decode returns a 4-D NHWC array. Downstream code in `load_bytes` checks `len(valid) < len(frame_idx)` and the assembled frames are consumed as 4-D. (2) Why it matters: an empty 1-D array is shape-inconsistent with the non-empty path and may break downstream `frames.ndim == 4` / shape assertions if `frame_indices` is empty. This mirrors PyAV's questionable empty return but is worth an explicit shaped empty array `(0, H, W, 3)`. (3) Fix: return `np.empty((0, H, W, 3), dtype=np.uint8)` using decoder metadata dimensions for consistency.

(1) `torchcodec >= 0.14` was added as an unconditional hard dependency in `requirements/cuda.txt` and `requirements/xpu.txt` (no platform markers, unlike `cpu.txt` which excludes s390x/riscv64/ppc64le). (2) Why it matters: making torchcodec a mandatory install for all CUDA/XPU users adds a dependency solely for an opt-in, non-default backend (default remains opencv). The code already guards the import with a PlaceholderModule fallback, so a hard requirement is stronger than necessary and increases install surface/version-conflict risk. (3) Fix: consider whether torchcodec should be an optional/extras dependency given it is only needed when the user explicitly selects `backend="torchcodec"`.

(1) This PR removes the `mxfp8` entry from the quantization registry in `vllm/model_executor/layers/quantization/__init__.py` (removes `"mxfp8": Mxfp8Config` from `_registry`/method table and its import), but the diff does NOT delete the old file `vllm/model_executor/layers/quantization/mxfp8.py`, which still defines `Mxfp8Config` (a `Fp8Config` subclass) plus the old `Mxfp8OnlineLinearMethod`/`Mxfp8OnlineMoEMethod`. (2) Risk: the config-driven path — a checkpoint whose config declares `quant_method: mxfp8` (or callers doing `get_quantization_config("mxfp8")`) — was previously routed through `Mxfp8Config`; after this change `"mxfp8"` string now resolves only through the `OnlineQuantScheme.MXFP8` enum via `OnlineQuantizationConfig`. If any checkpoint or downstream code still expects the `Mxfp8Config` frontend (e.g. KV-cache method `Fp8KVCacheMethod`, `min_capability=80`, `ignored_layers`/`modules_to_not_convert` handling), that behavior is silently lost. The online frontend does not obviously provide the same `Attention`->`Fp8KVCacheMethod` routing or `modules_to_not_convert` fallback that `Mxfp8Config.from_config`/`get_quant_method` implemented. (3) Fix: either delete the now-orphaned `mxfp8.py` if fully superseded, or document/migrate the config-driven entrypoint (KV cache method, ignored-layer resolution, min-capability) into the online frontend so config-serialized `mxfp8` checkpoints keep loading.

(1) In the new `online/mxfp8.py`, `Mxfp8OnlineMoEMethod.__init__` no longer sets `self.block_quant = True` (the old `mxfp8.py` version did). (2) The old `_Fp8OnlineMoEBase.__init__` sets `block_quant` and derives `weight_scale_name` from it; the new `Mxfp8OnlineMoEMethod` hardcodes `weight_scale_name = "weight_scale"` and does not define `block_quant`. The MoE oracle (`oracle/fp8.py:426`) derives `block_quant` from `hasattr(layer, "weight_block_size")` (the layer attribute, set in `create_weights` to `[1, MXFP8_BLOCK_SIZE]`), so the kernel path appears unaffected. But any code that reads `quant_method.block_quant` on this method would now hit an AttributeError / get a wrong default. (3) Fix: confirm no consumer reads `self.block_quant` on the MoE method; if any might (e.g. via generic online-method introspection), set `self.block_quant = True` for parity with the previous implementation and the linear side.

(1) In `deep_gemm_warmup.py::_fp8_linear_may_use_deep_gemm`, the guard `and not isinstance(module.quant_method, Mxfp8OnlineLinearMethod)` is now redundant. (2) Previously `Mxfp8OnlineLinearMethod` subclassed `Fp8OnlineLinearMethod`(->`Fp8LinearMethod`), so the preceding `isinstance(module.quant_method, Fp8LinearMethod)` check matched mxfp8 and the explicit exclusion was required. After this PR the new `Mxfp8OnlineLinearMethod` subclasses `_Fp8OnlineLinearBase` (a `LinearMethodBase`, NOT `Fp8LinearMethod`), so the `isinstance(..., Fp8LinearMethod)` check already excludes it and the `not isinstance(..., Mxfp8OnlineLinearMethod)` clause is dead code. It also forces an otherwise-unnecessary import of the class. (3) Fix: drop the redundant `not isinstance(..., Mxfp8OnlineLinearMethod)` clause and its import, or add a comment noting it is defensive. Not a correctness bug (behavior unchanged), just dead complexity introduced by the refactor.

(1) In `Mxfp8OnlineMoEMethod.process_weights_after_loading`, `w13`/`w2` are allocated via `torch.empty_like(layer.w13_weight, dtype=fp8_dtype)` and immediately overwritten/reassigned by `w13, w13_scale = self._quantize_mxfp8_moe_weight(...)`. (2) These initial `torch.empty_like` allocations are never used (the names are rebound before any read), wasting an allocation of two fp8 tensors the size of the full MoE weights during weight loading. This mirrors dead code carried over from the per-tensor MoE method where the empties WERE used. (3) Fix: remove the two `torch.empty_like` lines; keep only the `layer.w13_input_scale = None`/`layer.w2_input_scale = None` and the quantize calls.

(1) The parent CMakeLists set CUDNN_FRONTEND_INCLUDE_DIR itself (to the vendored 3rdparty submodule path); the new code only *checks* CUDNN_FRONTEND_INCLUDE_DIR and expects it to be supplied externally via -D. It is now only passed from setup.py's setup_common_extension() (which was updated). (2) Any build path that includes/configures transformer_engine/common/CMakeLists.txt WITHOUT going through setup.py -- e.g. a direct CMake configure of the common library, or downstream integrations that used to rely on the submodule being auto-discovered -- will now fail the FATAL_ERROR (cudnn_frontend.h not found) because CUDNN_FRONTEND_INCLUDE_DIR is empty. This is a behavior/compatibility change for out-of-tree/manual CMake builds. (3) Consider providing a fallback in cuDNN discovery to locate the header from the installed nvidia-cudnn-frontend package (e.g. via find_path) when -DCUDNN_FRONTEND_INCLUDE_DIR is not provided, or document the required flag.

(1) tests/cpp/CMakeLists.txt was updated to include transformer_engine/common/cmake/cuDNN.cmake (library discovery) but nothing in the C++ test build sets CUDNN_FRONTEND_INCLUDE_DIR, and the tests no longer add the cudnn-frontend include directory to the compiler search path (previously implicitly available via the vendored submodule tree / common CMakeLists). (2) If any C++ test source #includes cudnn_frontend.h, the compile will fail to find the header now that the submodule is removed and no include dir is configured for the tests. The included cuDNN.cmake only configures the cuDNN *library* targets, not the frontend headers. (3) Verify the C++ tests either don't need cudnn_frontend headers, or add the nvidia-cudnn-frontend include path (e.g. via the new cudnn_frontend_include_path() equivalent or an include_directories() call) to tests/cpp.

(1) build_tools/pytorch.py install_requirements() adds 'nvidia-cudnn-frontend>=1.25.0' as a runtime dependency, but build_tools/jax.py install_requirements() was NOT updated -- it still returns only ['jax', 'flax>=0.7.1']. The cudnn-frontend was only added to the JAX build-system requires (pyproject.toml), which covers build time but not runtime. (2) The JAX flex_attention.py runtime code (_import_cudnn_for_score_mod) now relies exclusively on the installed 'cudnn' Python package (the vendored sys.path fallback was removed). A user installing TE/JAX from a wheel without separately installing nvidia-cudnn-frontend will hit an ImportError at runtime when using score_mod fused attention. (3) Add 'nvidia-cudnn-frontend>=1.25.0' to build_tools/jax.py install_requirements() for parity with the PyTorch side.

(1) CUDNN_LIBRARY_VAR is only set inside the `if(NOT CUDNN_STATIC)` branch. When CUDNN_STATIC is ON, CUDNN_LIBRARY_VAR is undefined, so find_package_handle_standard_args(LIBRARY REQUIRED_VARS CUDNN_INCLUDE_DIR ${CUDNN_LIBRARY_VAR}) expands to only CUDNN_INCLUDE_DIR and silently skips validating that any cudnn library was found. (2) This is faithful to the upstream cudnn-frontend cmake so it is not a regression introduced by this PR, but it means static-link builds get weaker validation. (3) Low priority; only worth addressing if TE ever enables CUDNN_STATIC. No action needed for behavior parity.

(1) The env-driven path of `VllmConfig.use_v2_model_runner` no longer excludes reasoning-config workloads from V2, but the fix relies solely on a runtime `ValueError` at request-validation time (in `InputProcessor._validate_params`) to reject `thinking_token_budget` on V2. Concretely, `_get_v2_model_runner_unsupported_features()` previously appended "reasoning budget enforcement" whenever `reasoning_config is not None`, which caused the *auto*-selection path (`use_v2_model_runner` property, when `VLLM_USE_V2_MODEL_RUNNER` is unset) to fall back to V1 for reasoning models. That guard was removed. As a result, for a default-V2 architecture (e.g. Qwen3ForCausalLM) with `--reasoning-config`/`--reasoning-parser` set but users NOT sending `thinking_token_budget`, the engine now silently auto-selects V2 whereas before it silently used V1. (2) Impact: this is a behavioral/back-compat change to which model runner is chosen for reasoning-enabled deployments; if V2 has any other reasoning-related gap not covered by the per-request check, it becomes reachable. (3) Fix: confirm this auto-select change is intentional (the PR seems to intend that reasoning models CAN use V2 as long as `thinking_token_budget` isn't requested); if so, add a test that a reasoning-config Qwen3 model without `thinking_token_budget` runs on V2, and if not, keep a narrower unsupported-feature gate.

(1) The new `_validate_v2_model_runner` warning path only fires when `VLLM_USE_V2_MODEL_RUNNER` is explicitly set (that env var forces V2 and triggers `_validate_v2_model_runner`). It emits `warning_once` saying V2 doesn't support `thinking_token_budget`, but this warning is unconditional on `reasoning_config is not None` and does not know whether any request will actually use `thinking_token_budget`. So every explicit-V2 run with a reasoning config emits a warning even when no request ever sets `thinking_token_budget`. (2) Impact: noisy/potentially misleading startup warning; users who never use the parameter get told to set `VLLM_USE_V2_MODEL_RUNNER=0`. (3) Fix: consider downgrading to an informational note or wording it as conditional ("if you send thinking_token_budget, ..."), which the message text partially does but is still emitted eagerly.

(1) The removal of `reasoning budget enforcement` from `_get_v2_model_runner_unsupported_features()` also affects the explicit-V2 validation path: previously `_validate_v2_model_runner()` would raise a hard `ValueError` at startup for any reasoning-config model under forced V2; now it only warns. This is a loosening of a previously fatal check. (2) Impact: users who explicitly forced V2 with a reasoning config and relied on the startup error to catch misconfiguration will now boot successfully and only fail later per-request. (3) Fix: this appears intentional (enabling V2 for reasoning models that don't use the budget), but worth a changelog/deprecation note since it changes startup behavior for `VLLM_USE_V2_MODEL_RUNNER=1` + reasoning-config.

(1) `InputProcessor.use_v2_model_runner` is cached as an instance attribute at `__init__` by reading the `vllm_config.use_v2_model_runner` property once. The property has side effects (`logger.warning_once`) and evaluates env/model-config state; caching a snapshot is fine here since config is immutable post-init, but note the value is read eagerly at InputProcessor construction rather than lazily. (2) Impact: negligible; just confirm no code path mutates the runner selection after InputProcessor construction. (3) Fix: none required if config is frozen after startup.

(1) The deprecation warning message ends with a trailing space and, unlike the docs (which reference issue #33702 in the !!! warning block), omits the tracking issue link. (2) Inconsistency between docs and runtime message makes it harder for users to find migration context; trailing whitespace is sloppy. (3) Remove the trailing space and consider appending the '#33702' reference to match the documentation.

(1) In test_explicit_kv_role_no_deprecation_warning the assertion is written as a bare tuple `(mock_logger.warning_once.assert_not_called(), (f"..."))` rather than `mock_logger.warning_once.assert_not_called(), "..."` inside an assert. The call to assert_not_called() does run and will raise if violated, but the failure message string is completely dead — it is never attached to any assertion. (2) A developer reading the test may believe the custom failure message is emitted, but on failure they only get the default AssertionError from the mock. (3) Rewrite as a proper assert or pass the message to assert_not_called via a wrapping assert with a real condition; at minimum drop the misleading dead message string.

(1) In test_kv_both_deprecation_warning the line `mock_logger.warning_once = mock_logger.warning_once` is a self-assignment no-op. (2) It adds confusing dead code implying some intentional rebinding that does nothing. (3) Delete the line.

(1) The removed `target/` cache mount changes rebuild behavior across all Dockerfiles: previously cargo built incrementally against a cached `rust/target`; now every image build recompiles the entire Rust workspace (and all crates.io dependencies) from scratch since only registry/git caches remain. (2) Impact: Rust frontend build time in CI increases substantially (a clean release build of a tonic/prost + native-tls project can be many minutes) on every rebuild, not just source changes; this is a CI throughput regression traded off against cache-staleness safety. (3) Fix: if the stated staleness concern is real, prefer keying/invalidating the target cache on a source-hash or cargo lockfile rather than dropping incremental caching entirely; at minimum document the expected build-time cost. Confidence moderate because the tradeoff may be intentional per the commit message, but the throughput cost is real and applies to every listed Dockerfile.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time a clean vs. cache-warm `rust-build` stage before/after this change (docker buildx with --progress=plain) to measure the added compile time per image build.

findings.claim: (1) `build_rust.sh` still contains the rustup detection/installation and toolchain logic and reads `rust-toolchain.toml`, but the actual cargo invocation is now delegated to `tools/build_rust.py` via setuptools-rust, which does NOT use `cargo +$TOOLCHAIN` — setuptools-rust invokes `cargo build` with whatever cargo is on PATH. (2) Impact: the `$TOOLCHAIN` variable is computed but no longer passed to the build; if the default active toolchain differs from the pinned one in rust-toolchain.toml, the build could silently use the wrong toolchain (setuptools-rust does respect rust-toolchain.toml via rustup's proxy, so this is usually fine, but the shell-side toolchain-install branch is now the only thing guaranteeing the pinned toolchain is installed). (3) Fix: confirm the toolchain-install branch in build_rust.sh still runs and that rust-toolchain.toml is present in the build cwd so rustup auto-selects it; otherwise document that toolchain selection is delegated to rust-toolchain.toml.

(1) The ROCm csrc-build stage adds `COPY tools/build_rust.py tools/build_rust.py` and setup.py now imports build_rust.py at module load (`rust_build = load_module_from_path(...)`), which requires `setuptools_rust` to be importable in every environment that runs setup.py — including the csrc-build/native-build stages that previously did not need setuptools-rust source-side. (2) Impact: if setuptools-rust is not installed in a stage that runs `python3 setup.py`, setup.py now fails at import time (build_rust.py does `from setuptools_rust import Binding, RustExtension` at top level). Previously the import lived only in setup.py which already imported setuptools_rust, so this is likely already satisfied, but the new file adds a second hard import site executed unconditionally at setup.py load. (3) Fix: verify setuptools-rust is present in the build deps of every stage invoking setup.py (it is in requirements/build/rust.txt, but that file is only pip-installed in the rust-build stage, not necessarily in csrc/wheel stages).

(1) `build_binary` calls `os.chdir(ROOT_DIR)` and mutates process cwd as a side effect without restoring it, and creates `vllm/` dir unconditionally. (2) Impact: minor — the module is invoked as a standalone script so cwd mutation is harmless there, but because setup.py imports this module (load_module_from_path), any accidental call to build_binary() from within setup.py context would silently change cwd for the rest of the build. (3) Fix: this is fine for the script entrypoint; no action strictly required, but consider guarding cwd or documenting that build_binary is script-only.

(1) The shared header `csrc/core/scalar_type.hpp` had its include changed from `<torch/library.h>` to `<torch/headeronly/util/Exception.h>` and all `TORCH_CHECK` -> `STD_TORCH_CHECK`. This header is included by NON-stable-ABI translation units (csrc/ops.h, csrc/quantization/machete/*, csrc/quantization/marlin/kernel.h & marlin_template.h & marlin_dtypes.cuh, csrc/moe/marlin_moe_wna16/*). (2) Any of those consumers that transitively relied on scalar_type.hpp to pull in `<torch/library.h>` (for TORCH_CHECK, torch::Library, TORCH_LIBRARY, at::Tensor, etc.) may now fail to compile after this change, since the header no longer transitively includes the full torch/library. This is a silent build-time regression risk that only surfaces on the specific arch/build config that compiles those files. (3) Verify each non-stable consumer of scalar_type.hpp still independently includes torch/library.h (or torch/all.h); if any relied on the transitive include, add the explicit include there or keep a lightweight torch include in scalar_type.hpp guarded for non-stable builds.

(1) This PR moves AWQ gemm_kernels.cu, AllSpark, DSV3 fused-a-gemm, CUTLASS MLA and Hadacore source files from the `_C` extension (VLLM_EXT_SRC) into the `_C_stable_libtorch` extension (VLLM_STABLE_EXT_SRC). (2) The corresponding op registrations (TORCH_LIBRARY def/impl) must move from csrc/torch_bindings.cpp to csrc/libtorch_stable/torch_bindings.cpp in lock-step; if any op (e.g. awq_gemm/awq_dequantize/hadacore_transform/dsv3_fused_a_gemm/cutlass_mla_decode) remains declared in the old bindings while its implementation now lives only in the stable extension, the op will be unregistered or the symbol will be missing at load — a silent runtime failure when the op is invoked. The truncated diff prevents confirming the bindings side fully. (3) Confirm every migrated kernel's schema def + kCUDA impl now lives in the stable torch_bindings.cpp and was removed from csrc/torch_bindings.cpp, and that the Python dispatch points to the stable library.

(1) The new CPU reference implementations for expand cast inputs/weights to the output dtype (fp16/bf16) and perform the matmul on CPU in that low precision (inp @ w.T with fp16/bf16 operands). The previous torch_ops reference ran the einsum on GPU. (2) CPU matmul in fp16/bf16 typically accumulates in the same low-precision type rather than fp32, giving different rounding than the GPU triton kernel; with the loose but not tiny tolerance (rtol/atol=6e-2), large hidden_size cases (up to ~128k) accumulate many terms and could occasionally exceed tolerance, causing flaky test failures. (3) Consider accumulating the reference matmul in float32 (cast inp/w to float32, matmul, then cast result to output.dtype) to make the reference numerically robust and closer to the kernel's fp32 accumulation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run test_kernels_hidden_size with the largest HIDDEN_SIZES entries (e.g. 128256) in bf16 repeatedly to check for tolerance-driven flakiness of the new CPU reference.

findings.claim: (1) After the rewrite, sgmv_shrink_for_nslices and sgmv_expand_for_nslices no longer use several of their parameters: b_seq_start_loc, batches, max_seq_length, num_tokens (they only use seq_len_tensor, prompt_lora_mapping, and the tensors). (2) Unused arguments left in the signature are dead complexity that can confuse future readers into thinking the reference depends on batch/loc metadata it no longer uses. (3) These params are supplied positionally via *sgmv_meta_args, so keeping the signature is acceptable for now, but consider documenting that they are unused/only present for call-site tuple unpacking, or accepting *_ for the ignored trailing metadata.

(1) The `except ImportError` clause only catches `ImportError` (and its subclass `ModuleNotFoundError`), but the docstring states "Any failure during the trial import is treated as the module being unavailable" and the accompanying tests explicitly assert that `OSError` (test_returns_false_on_os_error_during_import) and `RuntimeError` (test_returns_false_on_unexpected_error_during_import) during import return False. `OSError` and `RuntimeError` are NOT subclasses of `ImportError`, so with this implementation those exceptions propagate out of `_has_module` instead of being converted to `False`. (2) This is the exact bug the PR intends to fix: a broken native extension (missing `libcudart.so`, insufficient CUDA driver) often raises `OSError`/`RuntimeError` at import time, and these would now crash the availability probe at startup instead of gracefully reporting the dependency as unavailable. The two new tests will also fail. (3) Broaden the except clause to catch these, e.g. `except (ImportError, OSError, Exception)` or minimally `except Exception` (matching the docstring intent), while preserving the warning log. If catching all exceptions is undesirable, at least add `OSError`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run tests/utils_/test_import_utils.py::TestHasModule and confirm test_returns_false_on_os_error_during_import and test_returns_false_on_unexpected_error_during_import pass.

findings.claim: (1) `_has_module` now performs a full `importlib.import_module(module_name)` on the availability path, whereas previously it only did the non-executing `find_spec`. Callers such as `has_deep_ep`, `has_deep_gemm`, `has_triton_kernels`, `has_aiter`, `has_mori`, `has_fbgemm_gpu`, `has_cutedsl`, `has_tilelang` are pure availability probes — many are invoked during platform/backend selection before the code path is actually taken. (2) Merely *checking* whether a heavy GPU dependency exists now fully loads it (executing top-level module code, CUDA context/kernel registration, and pulling large libraries into memory), adding startup latency and memory footprint even when the dependency ends up unused. This is a behavioral regression versus the cheap `find_spec` check. (3) Consider whether a full import is necessary for all probes, or gate the trial import so it is only paid once and does not eagerly initialize expensive backends; alternatively document that these `has_*` helpers now have import side effects so callers avoid calling them speculatively on hot/startup paths.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure vLLM import/startup time and peak memory with several optional deps installed (e.g. deep_gemm, fbgemm_gpu) before/after this change, calling the has_* probes.

findings.claim: (1) `find_spec` can itself raise (e.g. `ModuleNotFoundError` for a dotted name whose parent fails to import) — the new test `test_returns_false_when_find_spec_raises` asserts this returns False. Currently the `find_spec` call is inside the `try` block, so `ModuleNotFoundError` (a subclass of `ImportError`) is caught and returns False, which happens to work. (2) This relies on find_spec's failure being an ImportError subclass; a non-ImportError from find_spec would propagate. (3) Given the broadened except recommended above, ensure find_spec-raised errors are also covered; if the except is broadened to Exception this is naturally handled.

(1) In FlexibleArgumentParser.parse_args, dotted-config values now go through `human_readable_int(value_str)` before falling back to the raw string, so ANY bare token matching an integer-like pattern with a k/K/m/M/g/G/t/T suffix is silently coerced to an int for dotted args (e.g. `--config.some_str_field 5m` or `--config.model 1t`). (2) Previously such values were passed through as strings; now string-typed config fields that happen to look like `<digits><suffix>` will receive an int and either fail pydantic validation or be silently misinterpreted — a behavior/backward-compatibility change for existing scripts. (3) Restrict the human-readable coercion to fields whose target type is int (or gate on the config field type), or at minimum document/scope it so string fields are never affected.

(1) `_expand_json_human_readable_numbers` unconditionally rewrites every bare `\d+(?:\.\d+)?[kKmMgGtT]` token in the non-string regions of a JSON value for ALL dataclass config args (it is called in `parse_dataclass` before `validate_json` for every dataclass field). (2) This means a genuinely intended plain integer that a user did not want expanded, or a value where the suffix letter is coincidental, gets silently multiplied; more importantly the expansion is applied globally to every JSON config even those with no size fields, changing parsing semantics for all dataclass args, not only kv-transfer/cache configs. This is a silent semantic change that can produce wrong config values with no error. (3) Scope the expansion to the specific fields that expect human-readable sizes, or make it opt-in per field, rather than applying it to every dataclass JSON argument.

(1) `_expand_json_human_readable_numbers` calls `human_readable_int(m.group())` inside `re.sub`; for a binary suffix with a decimal (e.g. `{"x": 1.5K}`) the regex matches but `human_readable_int` raises `argparse.ArgumentTypeError`. In `parse_dataclass` only `ValidationError` is caught, so this ArgumentTypeError propagates uncaught from the expansion step. (2) While argparse does treat ArgumentTypeError as a clean CLI error, the error message ('Decimals are not allowed with binary suffixes') is emitted mid-JSON-parse and may be confusing / not obviously tied to the offending token, and any non-ArgumentTypeError from int() (there should be none here) would crash. (3) Consider catching/normalizing errors from expansion in `parse_dataclass` to give a clear message referencing the JSON arg and token.

(1) `human_readable_int` and `human_readable_int_or_auto` were moved from `vllm/engine/arg_utils.py` to `vllm/utils/argparse_utils.py`. (2) Any external/downstream code importing these from `vllm.engine.arg_utils` will break with ImportError since the definitions were deleted there and are only re-imported (not re-exported explicitly). Within the repo the only users are inside arg_utils itself, so internal usage is fine, but these were module-level public-looking helpers. (3) If backward import compatibility matters, keep thin re-export aliases in `vllm/engine/arg_utils.py` (they are imported there, so they remain accessible — confirm this import is retained, which it is), otherwise document the move.

(1) The `\b` word boundary after the suffix letter in `_expand_json_human_readable_numbers` regex `\b\d+(?:\.\d+)?[kKmMgGtT]\b` will not match tokens where the digit sequence is immediately preceded by other word characters, but note that JSON exponent notation is unaffected only by luck: a value like `1e3` won't match (no k/m/g/t), however a numeric token such as `10g` embedded in a larger unquoted identifier region cannot occur in valid JSON. (2) Low risk, but the function assumes the non-string regions are well-formed JSON numeric contexts; malformed input could be silently transformed then produce a downstream validation error rather than a clear parse error. (3) Add a brief comment noting the function assumes JSON-shaped input and relies on validate_json to reject malformed results.

(1) In process_weights_after_loading, when self.w_gidx_name is None it is defaulted to 'g_idx' and when self.w_zp_name is None it is defaulted to 'w_zp', and these defaults are stored back onto self (self.w_gidx_name = 'g_idx' / self.w_zp_name = 'w_zp'). But the actual parameter registered on the layer by gptq_marlin/compressed_tensors schemes is 'weight_g_idx' (see compressed_tensors_wNa16.py w_gidx_param_name='weight_g_idx' and auto_gptq.py 'g_idx'). If the scheme passed w_gidx_param_name=None (e.g. compressed_tensors_w4a8_int passes None) but the layer nevertheless has a differently-named g_idx tensor, or if has_g_idx is False but the layer has no attribute named 'g_idx', then getattr(layer, self.w_gidx_name) at the transpose branch would raise AttributeError. More importantly, defaulting to literal 'g_idx'/'w_zp' assumes those attribute names, which only holds for gptq-style layers; for compressed_tensors the real name is 'weight_g_idx'. (2) Risk: AttributeError at weight load time, or silently creating a parameter under the wrong name that the GEMM never reads. (3) Fix: only default the name when has_g_idx/zero_points is False (i.e. when the tensor is being newly created), and use the actual passed-in param name for the transpose path; assert the attribute exists before getattr.

(1) The transpose decision uses `if qweight_shape[0] != scale_shape[0]: need_transpose = True`. For gptq-marlin the qweight is stored [in, out] (or packed) while scale is [num_groups, out]; comparing only dim-0 to decide whether to transpose the qweight is a shape heuristic that can be ambiguous — e.g. when num_groups happens to equal the qweight row count, the branch is silently mis-selected. (2) Risk: for certain group_size / matrix-shape combinations qweight_shape[0] may coincidentally equal scale_shape[0] (or differ) producing the wrong layout, leading to silently wrong GEMM results with no error. (3) Fix: determine layout from the quantization scheme / config explicitly (e.g. a flag on the config indicating compressed_tensors vs gptq_marlin layout) instead of inferring from tensor shapes.

(1) In the non-transpose branch the code writes `getattr(layer, self.w_s_name).data = getattr(layer, self.w_s_name).data` — an unconditional self-assignment that does nothing. In the original code the scale was always transposed (layer.weight_scale.data = layer.weight_scale.t().contiguous()); now for the compressed-tensors layout (need_transpose True) the qweight is transposed but the scale is left as-is, while for the gptq layout the scale is transposed. Verify this matches what int4_gemm_w4a16 expects for BOTH layouts — previously scale was ALWAYS transposed, so the compressed_tensors path (which now does NOT transpose scale) is a behavior change that could feed a wrongly-laid-out scale to the kernel. (2) Risk: wrong dequantization scale layout → silently incorrect outputs for one of the two quant schemes. (3) Fix: confirm and document the expected scale layout per scheme; remove the no-op self-assignment.

(1) The line `getattr(layer, self.w_s_name).data = getattr(layer, self.w_s_name).data` is a no-op self-assignment. (2) It adds confusing dead code that reads as if it intends to transform the scale but does nothing, making the two branches look symmetric when they are not. (3) Fix: remove the else-branch self-assignment entirely (the scale is simply left unchanged in the transpose case).

(1) In apply_weights the argument `bias if bias is not None else None` is a no-op ternary equivalent to just `bias`. (2) It adds indirection with no behavior change and suggests intent that isn't there. (3) Fix: pass `bias` directly.

(1) The default fallback name for the zero-point parameter is the literal 'w_zp', while every existing scheme names the zero point 'weight_zero_point' (the original code used layer.weight_zero_point). (2) This inconsistency means the newly-created symmetric zero-point Parameter is registered under 'w_zp' rather than the conventional 'weight_zero_point', and _get_weight_params reads it back via self.w_zp_name so it works internally, but it diverges from the naming convention used elsewhere and from the marlin comment which says these are marlin-required names. (3) Fix: use a name consistent with the rest of the codebase or document why 'w_zp' is chosen.

(1) The FIXME comment '(FIXME): maybe zero points should also be transposed.' indicates the author is uncertain whether the zero-point transpose in the zero_points branch is correct. (2) An unresolved correctness FIXME on a dequantization tensor layout can produce silently wrong results for asymmetric (AWQ-style) GPTQ int4 on XPU. (3) Fix: resolve the FIXME before relying on the asymmetric path, or gate the asymmetric path off until verified.

(1) In get_cached_tokenizer, the new mistral_tekkenizer detection eagerly imports vllm.tokenizers.mistral (`from vllm.tokenizers.mistral import mistral_common_tekkenizer`) whenever a tokenizer exposes a `.tokenizer.instruct_tokenizer` attribute. This is guarded by getattr chains that are safe, but the branch runs unconditionally at cache-construction time for every tokenizer, adding an attribute-probe + potential heavy import on the hot tokenizer-load path. (2) More importantly, correctness: the check `getattr(getattr(tokenizer, 'tokenizer', None), 'instruct_tokenizer', None)` will match any wrapper tokenizer that happens to expose those attributes but is NOT actually tekken-backed; `mistral_common_tekkenizer` returns None in that case (it isinstance-checks Tekkenizer), so mistral_tekkenizer stays None and behavior is correct — but the truthiness test on `instruct_tokenizer` could raise if the attribute is a property with side effects. (3) Recommend gating the import/probe behind the same isinstance(Tekkenizer) success as mistral_common_tekkenizer, i.e. call mistral_common_tekkenizer(tokenizer) directly and check for None, avoiding the redundant double attribute walk.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench get_cached_tokenizer() construction time for a standard HF fast tokenizer before/after this change (per-load, cached via lru_cache so amortized).

findings.claim: (1) The new `convert_ids_to_tokens` override on CachedTokenizer ignores the `skip_special_tokens` argument entirely when `mistral_tekkenizer is not None` (it calls `tekken_convert_ids_to_tokens(mistral_tekkenizer, ids)` which has no skip_special_tokens handling), whereas the non-mistral branch forwards it to super(). (2) A caller passing skip_special_tokens=True to a mistral-tekken-backed cached tokenizer would silently get special tokens back, a behavior divergence from the documented signature. (3) Either honor skip_special_tokens in the tekken path or assert/document that it is unsupported for this backend.

(1) The field rename `orig_to_new_renamings` -> `orig_to_new_renaming` changes a public-ish dataclass field name on WeightsMapper (used across model files, e.g. transformers/base.py). (2) This is a backward-compatibility risk: any external code or plugin constructing WeightsMapper(orig_to_new_renamings=...) as a keyword argument will now raise TypeError, with no deprecation shim. (3) If WeightsMapper is considered part of the model-loading API surface, add a compatibility alias or note the break; otherwise confirm it is internal-only.

(1) `_replace_video_token_placeholders` now uses `prompt_ids[i:i+k] == target` where target may be length 1 (new-style) or 3 (old-style). The list-slice equality is correct, but the old guard `i + 2 < n` was an explicit bounds check; slicing silently returns a short list near the end so a truncated placeholder (e.g. only 2 of 3 tokens remain) will simply not match — behavior is preserved. However, when `k == 1`, this now matches every occurrence of the bare video_pad_id anywhere in the prompt, including ids that are NOT placeholders if the model ever emits video_pad_id elsewhere. (2) Risk: over-replacement of legitimate video_pad tokens leading to wrong expansion counts. (3) Recommend adding an assertion that repl_idx == num_repl after the loop (already present per truncated hunk) and confirming new-style processors never emit stray video_pad_ids outside placeholders.

(1) `_expands_only_video_token` performs `getattr(ProcessorMixin, 'replace_video_token', None)` and `getattr(type(hf_processor), 'replace_video_token', None)` on every call; it is invoked in both `_call_hf_processor` and the prompt-replacement builder. (2) This is cheap but runs per-request during multimodal preprocessing; the result depends only on the processor class and is invariant across requests. (3) Consider caching via functools.lru_cache keyed on the processor type or computing once in ProcessingInfo.

(1) The mechanical rename `PreTrainedTokenizer|PreTrainedTokenizerFast -> PythonBackend|TokenizersBackend` hard-imports the new names from `transformers` at module top level in several files (benchmarks/backend_request_func.py, examples, funaudiochat.py, deepseek_v32/v4.py). (2) These symbols only exist in transformers>=5.10; importing this module against an older transformers (which the version pin now forbids, but plugins/examples may still run in mixed envs) raises ImportError at import time rather than a graceful failure. (3) Confirm the transformers pin (>=5.10.4) is enforced everywhere these modules load; benchmarks explicitly avoid importing vLLM to run standalone, so an older transformers there would break — consider a try/except alias or documenting the minimum version.

(1) The two new __init__ parameters `max_num_tokens` and `num_dispatchers` are added to `TrtLlmMxint4ExpertsMonolithic.__init__` but are never stored (no `self.max_num_tokens = ...`) nor forwarded to `super().__init__()`; they are silently discarded. (2) This matches the Mandatory 'Unused New Variables/Arguments' check — an accepted-but-dropped argument is a latent bug: if a caller later relies on this class actually honoring `max_num_tokens` (as the sibling batched/marlin/humming experts do, which store and use it for workspace sizing), it will silently do nothing. It also creates a misleading signature. (3) Either store them on self and wire them into the kernel/workspace path (consistent with cutlass_moe/marlin_moe/xpu_moe/fused_humming_moe which store `max_num_tokens`), or, if this monolithic kernel genuinely does not need them and they exist only for factory signature uniformity, add a comment/docstring documenting that they are intentionally accepted-and-ignored for interface compatibility.

(1) The 'skip build if image already exists' check uses `if [[ -z $(docker manifest inspect ...) ]]`, but `docker manifest inspect` prints to stderr and exits non-zero (with `set -e`) when the manifest is absent. (2) With `set -e` active, a non-zero exit from the command substitution inside `[[ ]]` could abort the script before the intended 'not found, proceed' branch, or the redirect of stderr may leak error text. In practice command substitution failures inside `[[ ]]` do not trip `set -e`, so this likely works, but it relies on that subtlety and mirrors the existing cpu_arm64 script. (3) Consider making the intent explicit, e.g. `if ! docker manifest inspect ... >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo 'not found'; else exit 0; fi`.

(1) The new arm64 branch runs `uv pip compile requirements/test/cuda.in -o requirements/test/cuda.txt` at image-build time to regenerate the lockfile for aarch64, but this recompiles from the loose constraints in cuda.in rather than a pinned arm64 lockfile, so the resolved versions are non-deterministic across builds and can drift from the committed x86_64 cuda.txt. (2) Non-reproducible test dependency resolution makes arm64 CI flaky/hard to reproduce and can silently pull in newer transitive deps. (3) Consider committing a dedicated arm64 lockfile (e.g. requirements/test/cuda-arm64.txt) or pinning the resolution, and document that this recompile is intentional.

(1) Packages arctic-inference, fastsafetensors, and instanttensor are now gated to `platform_machine == "x86_64"`, meaning the tests that depend on them (suffix decoding test, multi-GPU fastsafetensors mem-usage test) will silently not run / be skipped on arm64. (2) This reduces arm64 test coverage without an explicit skip marker, so failures specific to arm64 in those code paths go undetected. (3) Ensure the corresponding tests are guarded to skip (not error) when the dep is missing on arm64, and add a tracking note for eventual arm64 wheel availability.

(1) The refactor silently drops the previous upper bound on num_heads. The old validity check was `num_heads in (4,8) or (num_heads % 16 == 0 and 16 <= num_heads <= 128)`, whose assertion message explicitly documented AITER MLA supporting only 'multiples of 16 in [16, 128]'. The new `is_valid_num_heads` accepts ANY multiple of 16 for num_heads >= 16 (e.g. 144, 160, 256) with no upper bound. (2) If the AITER kernel genuinely cannot handle num_heads > 128, this converts a clear, actionable assertion into an opaque kernel-level failure or silently wrong results at runtime. (3) If >128 is intentionally supported now, keep the change but confirm the kernel limit; otherwise restore an explicit upper-bound check (e.g. `num_heads <= 128`) in `is_valid_num_heads`.

(1) The new check additionally accepts num_heads = 1 and 2 (divisors of 16) which the old check rejected (old accepted only 4, 8 among <16 values). (2) This is a behavioral broadening of accepted configurations; if the padding path (`repeat_interleave` to 16 heads) is not actually validated for num_heads=1/2 with the AITER kernel, it could allow previously-rejected configs to reach the kernel. (3) Confirm num_heads=1/2 are intended to be supported and covered by a test; if not, restrict the divisor branch to the previously validated set.

(1) The added TODO documents that `deepgemm_fp8_paged_mqa_logits_stage1` followed by `out_qk.sum(dim=0)` should be replaced by a fused variant, and that the `ChunkQ=heads` argument / ChunkQ path is a temporary workaround pending AITER PR #2891. (2) The `out_qk` tensor is allocated as `[heads, batch*next_n, max_model_len]` and reduced with `.sum(dim=0)`, which is an extra full-size allocation plus a reduction kernel on every call — a real throughput/memory cost for the sparse indexer path. (3) Acceptable as a documented temporary workaround; ensure the TODO is tracked to the linked AITER PR so the fused kernel replaces this once available.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark rocm_fp8_paged_mqa_logits on GLM-5.1 sparse decode: compare latency/peak-memory of the out_qk allocation + sum(dim=0) path vs the future fused variant.

findings.claim: (1) The previous constraint 'fastsafetensors>=0.2.2; platform_machine == "x86_64"' had its platform marker dropped, so fastsafetensors is now required unconditionally on all CUDA test platforms (e.g. aarch64/Grace), not just x86_64. (2) If a non-x86_64 CUDA wheel is unavailable for 0.3.2 on some target, dependency resolution/install could break on those platforms where it previously would have been skipped. (3) If the marker removal is intentional (0.3.2 ships wheels for all supported arches), keep as-is; otherwise re-add 'platform_machine == "x86_64"'. The generated cuda.txt still resolves, suggesting this is fine, so confidence is low.

(1) The comment on the `skip_unsupported` skipif in utils.py still states 'For devices with SM < 90, batch invariance does not support CUDA Graphs.' (2) This PR's entire purpose is to enable compile/CUDA-graph (non-eager) mode for batch invariance on SM80, and it removes the `enforce_eager=IS_DEVICE_CAPABILITY_BELOW_90` overrides that forced eager mode on SM<90. The comment is now stale and misleading, contradicting the new behavior. (3) Update or remove the comment to reflect that CUDA graphs are now supported on SM80.

(1) `is_device_capability_below_90()` in utils.py is now dead code: this PR removes the only import/usage (in test_batch_invariance.py), leaving the helper defined but unused across the repo. (2) Dead helpers accumulate maintenance burden and confuse future readers about whether SM<90 special-casing is still needed. (3) Remove `is_device_capability_below_90()` from utils.py since its sole consumer was deleted (verify no other importer relies on it).

(1) The PR drops the previously-pinned upper bound (`<= 0.10.1`) that existed on the `nixl` packages and replaces it with an open-ended `nixl >= 1.1.0` with no upper bound.
