(2) The git history shows the upper bound was added deliberately (#35495 'Bound NIXL upper bound version') and the cu12/cu13 split was added to fix a CI breakage (#39851 'Fix PD CI breakage: pin nixl-cu{12,13} versions'). Removing the upper bound means a future NIXL 2.x with breaking API changes could be pulled in silently and break disaggregated-prefill at install time. (3) Consider keeping an upper bound (e.g. `nixl >= 1.1.0, < 2.0.0`) consistent with the prior pinning practice, unless the maintainers intentionally want to track latest.

(1) The three prior entries (`nixl[cu13]`, `nixl-cu12`, `nixl-cu13`) that provided CUDA-12 vs CUDA-13 specific wheels are collapsed into a single unpinned `nixl`. (2) The cu12/cu13 split was introduced specifically to fix a CI/install breakage (#39851); consolidating to a single package assumes the 1.x `nixl` wheel auto-selects the correct CUDA variant. If nixl 1.x does not ship a CUDA-12 compatible wheel, users still on CUDA 12 could regress. (3) Confirm that `nixl>=1.1.0` publishes universal/auto-selecting wheels covering both CUDA 12 and CUDA 13 before removing the explicit cu12 target; note the separate `requirements/tpu.txt` still pins `nixl==0.3.0`, which is untouched and worth verifying is intentional.

(1) `extract_content_ids` runs a per-token Python loop that calls `self.model_tokenizer.decode(token_buf, skip_special_tokens=False)` on every single token id (and constructs a fresh `PyFilter(self.unary_opts)` each call). (2) This is invoked on the structured-output decode hot path (see `vllm/parser/abstract_parser.py:663` and `mistral_tool_parser.py:367`); a full re-decode + filter write for every token is O(N) tokenizer decodes and adds significant per-token CPU overhead that will hurt structured-output throughput for long sequences. (3) Decode incrementally (only the newly appended token / cached prefix) or reuse a persistent filter, and avoid re-instantiating `PyFilter` on each call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Structured-output decode with cohere_command3 reasoning parser on a 2k+ token generation; measure per-token latency of extract_content_ids vs incremental decode.

findings.claim: (1) `self.end_token_id = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids("<|END_THINKING|>")` is stored without checking the result; if the tokenizer does not contain this special token it returns the unk id (or None for some tokenizers). (2) `is_reasoning_end` then compares every token against a possibly-wrong `end_token_id`, so reasoning-end detection could silently never trigger (structured decoding never switches from reasoning to content) or trigger on unk tokens. (3) Validate the returned id is not None / not the unk id at construction and raise a clear error, matching how other parsers validate their end tokens.

(1) `is_reasoning_end` uses `any(tid == self.end_token_id for tid in reversed(input_ids))`, which returns True as soon as an END_THINKING token appears anywhere in the sequence. (2) For streaming, `is_reasoning_end_streaming` (inherited default) just calls `is_reasoning_end(input_ids)`; scanning the entire (growing) sequence every decode step is O(N) per step = O(N^2) overall, and unlike the deltas-based approach used by other streaming parsers it does not restrict the check to newly generated tokens. (3) Consider overriding `is_reasoning_end_streaming` to check only `delta_ids`, and note that `reversed()` gives no asymptotic benefit here since the token is expected early once reasoning ends.

(1) In `_schema_from_json_schema_field`, the `for by_alias in (True, False)` loop calls `js_wr.model_dump(...)` up to twice with a try/except on TypeError for each iteration. (2) This adds repeated (potentially expensive) Pydantic serialization on the request-adjustment path for every request using json_schema; the two dumps usually produce the same schema content. (3) Resolve the alias handling once (or detect the field directly) rather than dumping the model twice per request.

(1) Both new modules `raise ImportError` at module import time if `cohere_melody` is not installed, and the parser registry (`_REASONING_PARSERS_TO_REGISTER` / `_TOOL_PARSERS_TO_REGISTER`) lazily imports the module by name. (2) This is acceptable for a hard-optional dependency, but if any code path eagerly imports all registered parsers (e.g. listing/validation), a missing `cohere_melody` would break unrelated functionality; the doc note says the package is not installed by default. (3) Confirm registration is lazy-only (it appears to be via `register_lazy_module`) so the import error is only raised when the cohere parser is actually selected; otherwise gate the import behind a runtime check.

(1) `model_class_overrides` is not added to `compute_hash`'s `ignored_factors` set in `ModelConfig.compute_hash`. (2) `compute_hash` uniquely identifies configs that affect the computation graph and is used for compilation/prefix caching keying; because `model_class_overrides` swaps the actual model class, it genuinely *does* change the computation graph, so it should participate in the hash — but the reverse risk also exists: if it is silently omitted from consideration, two runs (one with an override, one without) that resolve to a different model class could collide on the same cache/compile hash, producing stale compiled artifacts. Sibling fields like `hf_overrides` are explicitly listed in `ignored_factors`; `model_class_overrides` is neither listed nor obviously handled. (3) Decide and document: either add it to `ignored_factors` (if considered debug-only and never cache-relevant) or ensure it is included in the hash factors so overridden runs get a distinct hash. Note this new field will fall into the non-ignored factors by default via `get_hash_factors`, which may be acceptable but should be verified against the intended cache semantics.

(1) `registry` is a `@property` and `_maybe_register_model_class_overrides()` now runs on *every* access of `self.registry`, not just once. (2) `.registry` is described as 'the single chokepoint through which every model-class inspect/resolve passes' and may be accessed frequently (per model resolution / repeatedly during startup and possibly hot paths). While the fast path returns early when `model_class_overrides` is empty, when it is set, the method builds a list comprehension over the dict and does a set-membership check on every property access even after everything is already registered. (3) The early-return `if not pending: return` mitigates repeated `register_model` calls, but the per-access list comprehension + membership checks still add small overhead on every `.registry` access. Since this is debug-only, impact is low, but consider caching a per-instance/process 'already fully applied' flag keyed on the override dict to skip the comprehension entirely once complete.

(1) The process-local guard `_REGISTERED_MODEL_CLASS_OVERRIDES` records `(arch, target)` pairs and never removes them; if two different `ModelConfig` instances in the same process register conflicting targets for the same `arch` (e.g. arch->targetA then arch->targetB), both will be registered sequentially and the *last* `register_model` call wins in `ModelRegistry`, but the guard treats them as independent entries. This can produce order-dependent behavior where the effective class for `arch` depends on which config's `.registry` was accessed last. (2) For a debug/development feature this is likely acceptable, but it is a silent last-writer-wins behavior across configs in one process. (3) Consider keying the guard on `arch` alone (and warning on conflicting re-registration) rather than on `(arch, target)`, or document that concurrent conflicting overrides in one process are unsupported.

(1) `_convert_args` finds the function name via `next((s.name for s in self._tool_slots if s.args == raw_args), None)`, matching a tool slot by comparing the fully-joined `args` string against `raw_args`. This lookup can match the wrong slot (or none) when two parallel tool calls have identical raw argument strings, or when `raw_args` is a partial prefix during streaming (the converter is also called for partial args at parser_engine.py:919/962/1017), because `slot.args` is the running accumulation that only equals `raw_args` at specific ticks. (2) A mismatched/`None` func_name causes `_unwrap_wrapper_args` to skip unwrapping (returns args_json unchanged) or unwrap against the wrong tool's schema, silently producing incorrect tool-call arguments for the user. (3) Prefer passing the tool index / function name explicitly through the arg-converter call path instead of reverse-matching on the args string; at minimum key on the active `tool_index` rather than string equality.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Streaming + non-streaming test with two parallel DeepSeek V4 tool calls that have identical argument payloads and a schema requiring wrapper unwrapping; assert each call unwraps to the correct inner args.

findings.claim: (1) `DeepSeekV4Parser._convert_args` and `DeepSeekV32Parser._convert_args` are byte-for-byte identical, as are the slot-matching + `_unwrap_wrapper_args` bodies. (2) Duplicated non-trivial logic in two parsers risks divergent bug fixes later (one gets patched, the other doesn't). (3) Hoist the shared `_convert_args` implementation into a small mixin or a module-level helper that both parsers call, since v3.2 already imports `_dsml_arg_converter`/`_unwrap_wrapper_args` from deepseek_v4.

(1) The `deepseek_v4` reasoning parser registration is switched from `DeepSeekV3ReasoningParser` to the new engine-based `DeepSeekV4ParserReasoningAdapter`, and the `deepseek_v32`/`deepseek_v4` tool parsers are re-pointed to new engine adapters while the old `deepseekv32_tool_parser.py` module is deleted. (2) Any user config or external code that referenced the removed `DeepSeekV32ToolParser`/`DeepSeekV4ToolParser` class or the `deepseekv32_tool_parser` module path directly (not via the registry name) will break with an ImportError; behavior of the streaming engine parser may also differ subtly from the old regex parser. (3) Confirm the registry name (`deepseek_v4`, `deepseek_v32`) is the only supported public entry point; if the class/module paths were public, keep a thin re-export shim or note the removal in release notes.

(1) The `_coerce_value` scalar branch changed the change-detection from `if coerced != value` to `if type(coerced) is not type(value) or coerced != value`. This fixes cases where coercion changes type but the values compare equal (e.g. bool `True` vs int `1`, or `1` vs `1.0`), which previously would report `changed=False` and leave the wrong-typed value in the emitted JSON. (2) This is a genuine correctness improvement, but it also means values differing only by type now trigger re-serialization on every coercion pass; verify no streaming prefix-stability invariant (`startswith(prev)`) is violated by the newly-triggered rewrites. (3) Add/confirm a test covering bool-vs-int and int-vs-float coercion to lock in the fix and guard the streaming path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test coercing a raw '1' against an integer schema and '1'/'true' against boolean schema; assert emitted JSON type matches schema and streaming deltas remain prefix-consistent.

findings.claim: (1) The new `anchor.terminal != DROP_TERMINAL` guard prevents deferring remaining content while waiting for DROP-anchor (EOS/special) text that never arrives in delta_text. This is correct for the described case, but when the branch is skipped the anchor is still appended to `self._deferred_terminals` and the trailing `delta_text[consumed:]` is not flushed here — verify the subsequent `if consumed < len(delta_text)` tail append still emits that text so no content is silently dropped for DROP anchors. (2) If the tail is not emitted, user-visible content following a dropped special token could be lost. (3) Add a scanner unit test where a DROP terminal's text is stripped by the detokenizer but real content follows in the same delta; assert the content is emitted, not deferred/dropped.
findings.suggested_benchmark: TokenIDScanner test: delta contains a special/EOS token whose decoded text is absent from delta_text, followed by literal content; assert content chunk is emitted in the same or next scan().

findings.claim: (1) The sync path `get_bytes` is decorated with `@_sync_retry` and uses `requests.Session.get(...).raise_for_status()`, so HTTP 4xx errors surface as `requests.exceptions.HTTPError` — NOT `aiohttp.ClientResponseError`. However, the sync tests (`test_fetch_image_404`, `test_fetch_image_connection_error`) mock `get_bytes.side_effect` with `aiohttp.ClientResponseError` / `aiohttp.ClientConnectionError`, which the real sync `requests`-based path can never raise. This means `_wrap_media_fetch_error`'s aiohttp branch will fire in the test but the actual production sync path only ever produces `requests.exceptions.HTTPError`. The tests pass but validate a code path that doesn't match reality. (2) Risk: the sync `fetch_image` 4xx→422 conversion is effectively untested against the real exception type (`requests.exceptions.HTTPError`), and any subtle mismatch (e.g. `exc.response is None`) would go unnoticed. (3) Fix: use `requests.exceptions.HTTPError` with a mock `.response.status_code` in the sync tests to exercise the actual production exception type.

(1) `_is_retryable` in connections.py already treats 4xx as non-retryable and 5xx/timeouts/connection errors as retryable. The new `_wrap_media_fetch_error` duplicates and re-derives this transient-vs-permanent taxonomy independently (special-casing 408/429, <500, DNS, etc.). The two classifiers can drift: e.g. `_is_retryable` retries `aiohttp.ClientResponseError` with `status >= 500` and treats 408/429 as NON-retryable (since only 5xx and timeouts/connection errors are retryable), whereas `_wrap_media_fetch_error` explicitly preserves 408/429 as-is 'to allow retry logic to handle them' — but by the time the exception reaches `_wrap_media_fetch_error`, retries are already exhausted and 408/429 will be returned as a raw exception that maps to HTTP 500, not 422 or a retry. (2) Risk: 408/429 image-URL failures still return 500 despite the stated intent; two divergent classification tables are a maintenance hazard. (3) Fix: reuse/centralize the classification (e.g. call `connections._is_retryable`) rather than re-implementing, and decide explicitly what status 408/429 should map to at the HTTP boundary.

(1) `_wrap_media_fetch_error` has a final `if isinstance(exc, ValueError):` branch that converts ANY ValueError to a 422 with message 'Invalid URL'. But `HTTPConnection._validate_http_url` raises a plain `ValueError('Invalid HTTP URL...')`, and `load_from_url` itself raises `ValueError('The URL must be either a HTTP, data or file URL.')` and `ValueError` for disallowed media domains / local path escapes. These ValueErrors are caught by the broad `except Exception` around `get_bytes`/`async_get_bytes`... actually they are raised OUTSIDE the try block (in load_from_url before/around the connection call), so most are not wrapped. However, since the wrap only surrounds the `get_bytes` call, a ValueError raised inside a custom connection's `get_bytes` would be relabeled 'Invalid URL' regardless of its true cause, producing a misleading message. (2) Risk: misleading error text for non-URL ValueErrors originating in the connection layer. (3) Fix: narrow the final branch to only the URL-parsing exceptions actually expected, or drop the catch-all ValueError→422 mapping.

(1) `import aiohttp` and `import requests` are added at module top-level of connector.py solely so `_wrap_media_fetch_error` can isinstance-check their exception types. These are already imported by `vllm.connections`, so they are loaded regardless; the impact is negligible but the dependency coupling of the multimodal connector directly on both HTTP client libraries is new. (2) Risk: minor import-time coupling; low impact. (3) Fix (optional): reference exception types via the connection abstraction or a shared helper in connections.py rather than importing both client libs into the media connector.

(1) `VLLMUnprocessableEntityError` is a near-verbatim copy of `VLLMValidationError` (same `__init__` signature, same `parameter`/`value` attributes, same `__str__` implementation). The only difference is the class identity used for HTTP status mapping. (2) Risk: code duplication; future edits to the `__str__`/attribute contract must be mirrored in both classes. (3) Fix: extract a shared base class holding the `__init__`/`__str__` logic and have both `VLLMValidationError` and `VLLMUnprocessableEntityError` subclass it (still both ValueError subclasses).

(1) The new static factory `ModelRunnerOutput.with_kv_conn_output_only` handles the `kv_connector_output is None` case by returning the shared `EMPTY_MODEL_RUNNER_OUTPUT` singleton, whereas at the removed v2 call site (gpu/model_runner.py:1218-1223) the previous code had `assert output_intermediate_tensors is not None` before attaching kv_connector_output. The new code no longer performs any post_forward on the non-last PP rank in that branch (it just returns output_intermediate_tensors), moving the post_forward + KVConnectorOutput return to the sample_tokens path. (2) This is a behavior relocation: the KV connector's finished-sending/recving notifications for non-last PP ranks are now produced in sample_tokens via `with_kv_conn_output_only`, and returned as a ModelRunnerOutput rather than being piggy-backed on the IntermediateTensors that flow to the next stage. Verify that the scheduler still aggregates KVConnectorOutput from every PP rank's ModelRunnerOutput (KVOutputAggregator) and that no rank's kv_connector_output is silently dropped now that it is no longer carried on IntermediateTensors. (3) Confirm the KVOutputAggregator collects outputs from all PP ranks' ModelRunnerOutput returns, not only the last rank.

(1) `IntermediateTensors` loses its public `kv_connector_output` field and constructor parameter (vllm/sequence.py). (2) Any external/plugin code constructing `IntermediateTensors(tensors=..., kv_connector_output=...)` or reading `.kv_connector_output` will break (AttributeError / unexpected kwarg). Since `IntermediateTensors` is a widely-used public sequence type, this is an API contract change. (3) If this is intended as fully internal, fine; otherwise consider a deprecation shim or note. The in-repo test at tests/distributed/test_comm_ops.py was updated to assert `_comm_handles` instead, confirming the field is gone.

(1) The docstring of `with_kv_conn_output_only` states 'Returns None if kv_connector_output is passed as None', but the implementation returns `EMPTY_MODEL_RUNNER_OUTPUT` (a non-None ModelRunnerOutput) when the arg is None or empty, never None. (2) A misleading docstring is worse than none: callers reading it may guard for `None` that never occurs, or expect None-propagation semantics that don't exist. Note the tests were written to accept `output in (EMPTY_MODEL_RUNNER_OUTPUT, None)`, reflecting this ambiguity. (3) Fix the docstring to say it returns EMPTY_MODEL_RUNNER_OUTPUT when kv_connector_output is None or empty.

(1) `with_kv_conn_output_only` uses a shallow `copy(EMPTY_MODEL_RUNNER_OUTPUT)` and then sets `output.kv_connector_output`. Because ModelRunnerOutput contains mutable default fields (e.g. `sampled_token_ids` list, `prompt_logprobs_dict` dict created via default_factory), the shallow copy shares those mutable containers with the module-level singleton EMPTY_MODEL_RUNNER_OUTPUT. (2) If any downstream code mutates these containers on the returned object (append to sampled_token_ids or insert into prompt_logprobs_dict), it would corrupt the shared singleton for all future callers. This risk pre-existed (the old inline code did the same copy.copy), so behavior is unchanged, but centralizing it makes it a single reviewable point. (3) Consider whether a fresh `ModelRunnerOutput(req_ids=[], req_id_to_index={}, kv_connector_output=...)` would be safer than shallow-copying the shared singleton.

In `vllm/config/parallel.py.__post_init__` (parent lines ~784-792), when `distributed_executor_backend == "external_launcher"`, `self.world_size` is multiplied by `data_parallel_size` (`self.world_size *= self.data_parallel_size`), so `world_size` is NOT always TP*PP. The new `/get_world_size` route computes the DP-inclusive world size as `world_size * data_parallel_size` (rust/src/server/src/routes/world_size.rs) and Python sends `world_size=parallel_config.world_size` (vllm/v1/engine/core.py). Under the external_launcher backend, `parallel_config.world_size` already includes DP, so the route would return `TP*PP*DP*DP` (DP double-counted) for `include_dp=true`, and would return `TP*PP*DP` (not TP*PP) for `include_dp=false`. This yields silently wrong world-size values reported to clients. Fix: compute the base TP*PP explicitly (e.g. `tensor_parallel_size * pipeline_parallel_size`) rather than reusing the post-init `world_size` field which is context-dependent, or document/guard the external_launcher case.

The Python `EngineCoreReadyResponse` (vllm/v1/engine/__init__.py) adds `world_size: int` and `data_parallel_size: int` as required (non-default) fields inserted BEFORE the existing optional fields (`kv_cache_size_tokens`, `kv_cache_max_concurrency`). This is a msgspec Struct/dataclass whose wire encoding uses positional array-like layout. Inserting new required positional fields in the middle changes the field ordering of the handshake payload; any engine and frontend that are not upgraded in lockstep (version skew) will deserialize misaligned fields or fail. The Rust side (handshake.rs) inserts the two new fields in the same relative position, so matched versions are consistent, but mixed old/new deployments break. Fix: append new fields at the end with defaults, or explicitly document that engine and frontend must be upgraded together and bump a protocol version.

The Rust `python_compat.py` test constructs `EngineCoreReadyResponse` passing `data_parallel_size=1` before `world_size=1` (keyword order reversed relative to field declaration order), while the msgspec-encoded golden hex depends on declaration order, not call order. This is harmless as keyword args, but the inconsistent ordering vs. the struct declaration (world_size then data_parallel_size) is confusing and could mask a real positional mismatch if these were ever passed positionally. Suggest ordering the kwargs to match field declaration order.

The `WorldSizeResponse` docstring/TODO in world_size.rs correctly notes that world size is captured statically at handshake and would be stale under elastic EP scaling / DP rank recovery. This is fine for the current static implementation and the per-request path is cheap (no ZMQ round-trip, no CPU-GPU sync), so no performance concern — noting only that the documented future `call_utility` RPC option would add a per-request ZMQ round-trip and should be gated/cached if adopted.

(1) Before this change, the register_fake for `moe_wna16_marlin_gemm` was nested inside `if hasattr(torch.ops._moe_C, "marlin_gemm_moe"):` — i.e. its registration was gated on a *different*, now-removed op. This PR fixes the guard to `hasattr(torch.ops._moe_C, "moe_wna16_marlin_gemm")`. (2) This is a latent-bug fix, not just cleanup: if a future build ever compiled `moe_wna16_marlin_gemm` without `marlin_gemm_moe`, the fake impl for `moe_wna16_marlin_gemm` would silently fail to register, breaking torch.compile / meta-tensor tracing of the marlin MoE path (used in vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/experts/marlin_moe.py). (3) No fix needed — verify a torch.compile smoke test exercises the marlin MoE expert path so the corrected guard is covered. Flagging as a note that the guard correction matters and should be regression-tested.
findings.suggested_benchmark: torch.compile trace of fused_moe marlin expert path (marlin_moe.py) to confirm moe_wna16_marlin_gemm fake registers and meta inference works

findings.claim: (1) The safetensors detection change from `if pattern == "*.safetensors"` to `if pattern.endswith(".safetensors")` broadens the condition to set `use_safetensors = True` for any pattern ending in `.safetensors`, including `consolidated*.safetensors` (mistral load_format) and now custom overrides like `transformer/*.safetensors`. Previously mistral already forced `use_safetensors = True` explicitly, so behavior for mistral is unchanged, but the broadened condition also now triggers the safetensors-index download/filter path (`filter_duplicate_safetensors_files` and `download_safetensors_index_file_from_hf`) for any override pattern with a directory prefix. (2) For Cosmos3, `allow_patterns_overrides=["transformer/*.safetensors"]` will now correctly set `use_safetensors=True` (previously it wouldn't, since the pattern != "*.safetensors"), which is the intended fix — but the same code path then calls `filter_duplicate_safetensors_files(hf_weights_files, hf_folder, index_file)` with `index_file=SAFE_WEIGHTS_INDEX_NAME` ("model.safetensors.index.json") at the repo root, which may not exist for a diffusers-style checkpoint whose weights live under `transformer/`. (3) Verify that `filter_duplicate_safetensors_files` gracefully handles a missing index file for these subdirectory-based checkpoints; if the index is absent it should skip filtering rather than dropping all files. Add a test covering the override-with-subfolder load path.

(1) The `hf_to_vllm_mapper` regex/substr/prefix rules rely on ordered application in `WeightsMapper._map_name`: regex first, then substr, then prefix. The regex rule `^(layers\.|embed_tokens\.|norm\.)(.+)$` rewrites `layers.0.self_attn.to_q.weight` to `language_model.model.layers.0.self_attn.to_q.weight`, and only afterward the substr rule `.to_q.` -> `.q_proj.` applies. This ordering works, but note the substr rule `.norm_q.` -> `.q_norm.` will ALSO match inside any key containing `.norm_q.` after the language_model prefix is added; verify no vision-tower keys (rewritten with `visual.` prefix) inadvertently contain these substrings and get corrupted. (2) A key like `blocks.0.attn.norm_q.weight` (vision tower) would first match the `blocks.` regex -> `visual.blocks.0.attn.norm_q.weight`, then the substr `.norm_q.` -> `.q_norm.` would rewrite it to `visual.blocks.0.attn.q_norm.weight`. If Qwen3-VL's vision attention does not use `q_norm`/`k_norm` naming, this silently mangles vision weights, causing load failure or wrong weights. (3) Confirm the vision tower attention naming and add explicit test cases for vision `norm_q`/`to_q` keys (the current test only checks `blocks.0.attn.qkv.weight`).

(1) The docstring explaining `secondary_weights` / diffusers checkpoint layout is placed as a bare string expression AFTER the `allow_patterns_overrides` class attribute and BEFORE `__init__`, so it is not a class docstring (that position is taken by the leading comment on `hf_to_vllm_mapper`) nor an attribute docstring — it is a dead no-op string literal. (2) This is misleading: readers may assume it documents `allow_patterns_overrides` or the class, and tooling won't surface it. It also duplicates/contradicts the inline comment above the mapper. (3) Move this explanation into a proper comment above `allow_patterns_overrides` or into the class docstring.

(1) `secondary_weights` uses `allow_patterns_overrides=["vision_encoder/*.safetensors"]` while the primary `allow_patterns_overrides` loads `transformer/*.safetensors`. The base Qwen3VLForConditionalGeneration.load_weights applies `self.hf_to_vllm_mapper` to ALL weights including secondary (vision_encoder) weights. Verify that vision_encoder weight keys are correctly rewritten to the `visual.` namespace by the mapper's `blocks.|merger.|patch_embed.|pos_embed.|deepstack_merger_list.` regex — if vision_encoder keys have an additional prefix (e.g. `vision_encoder.blocks.`) the anchored `^(blocks\.|...)` regex will not match and those weights will be dropped/unmatched. (2) This would cause missing vision weights and either a load error or silently uninitialized vision tower. (3) Add a test asserting a representative vision_encoder-directory key maps correctly.

(1) The mapper drops generation-tower weights via `orig_to_new_substr` entries mapping to `None` (e.g. `_moe_gen`, `.add_q_proj.`, etc.). Because substr matching short-circuits and returns `None` on the first matching drop-substr, ordering vs the earlier regex prefix rewrite matters. For a key like `layers.0.self_attn.q_proj_moe_gen.weight`, the regex `^(layers\.)...` rewrites it to `language_model.model.layers.0...q_proj_moe_gen.weight` first, then the substr `_moe_gen` matches and returns None (dropped) — correct per the test. However relying on substring `.add_q_proj.` etc. to drop weights is fragile: any future understanding-tower key legitimately containing these substrings would be silently discarded, and there is no assertion that the total count of dropped vs kept weights matches expectations. (2) Silent weight-dropping is hard to debug and risks under-loading. (3) Consider validating loaded weight coverage (the loader's `track_weights_loading` should catch missing params, but not extraneous silent drops).

(1) update_state() (called from Sampler.forward each step for spec decode, and from ThinkingBudgetStateHolder in the rejection path) iterates over every tracked request in pure Python and reslices/copies output_tok_ids and spec_token_ids lists on every sampling step; _update_think_state further does repeated _find_last_sequence_index O(len(output)*seq_len) scans. (2) This runs inside the hot sampling loop (once per decode step) and grows with output length, adding per-step host-side overhead that scales with sequence length and batch size — it can noticeably reduce decode throughput, especially since it partly negates the async/CUDA-graph fast paths. (3) Bound the search window (only scan the newly appended tokens plus a small overlap, as the original ThinkingTokenBudgetLogitsProcessor did via prev_output_length/check_count_down) instead of re-scanning full output each step, and avoid list copies when spec_len==0.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-throughput benchmark with speculative decoding enabled and reasoning_config set (thinking_token_budget) vs. disabled, at large batch size and long generated sequences, measuring tokens/s and per-step sampler CPU time.

findings.claim: (1) In update_state(), when repeat_indices is provided it calls repeat_indices.cpu().tolist(), forcing a GPU->CPU synchronization inside the sampling path. (2) Host-device syncs in the per-step sampling loop stall the pipeline and kill throughput, and are incompatible with CUDA graph capture / async scheduling — this is exactly the class of issue the rejection sampler elsewhere carefully avoids (see comments about avoiding cpu-gpu sync). (3) Pass the repeat mapping as a CPU-side list already available on host (the rejection sampler builds repeat_indices from a CPU tensor before moving to device), or keep a CPU copy, so no .cpu() sync is needed here.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile sampler step latency with nsys under spec decode + thinking budget, checking for added cudaStreamSynchronize from repeat_indices.cpu().

findings.claim: (1) In RejectionSampler.apply_logits_processors, holder.apply_to_logits is called but holder.update_state is never called on this path, unlike Sampler.forward which calls both holder.update_state(...) then holder.apply_to_logits(...). (2) apply_to_logits masks/forces end tokens based on state['in_end'] which is only set inside _update_think_state (invoked from update_state). If the state is never refreshed on the rejection-sampler path, the forcing decision uses stale think-state from a prior step, so budget enforcement can be off-by-one or fail to trigger during speculative decoding — the exact scenario this PR targets. (3) Verify where update_state is invoked for the rejection path (possibly in gpu_model_runner before forward); if it is not, add an update_state call before apply_to_logits, mirroring Sampler.forward.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Correctness test: spec decode + thinking_token_budget, assert thinking section is force-ended at the exact budget across draft/bonus token positions.

findings.claim: (1) ThinkingTokenBudgetLogitsProcessor is fully removed from builtin.py, the BUILTIN_LOGITS_PROCESSORS list, and __all__, replaced by the new ThinkingBudgetStateHolder mechanism. (2) This is a public symbol exported via __all__; external code or plugins importing ThinkingTokenBudgetLogitsProcessor from vllm.v1.sample.logits_processor will break with an ImportError, and any user relying on it being registered as a builtin logits processor loses the behavior silently. (3) If external import stability matters, keep a thin deprecation shim/alias for one release; otherwise document the removal in release notes as a breaking API change.

(1) The constructor takes is_pin_memory but discards it via `_ = is_pin_memory  # API parity with logits processors`. (2) An unused argument kept only for signature parity adds a misleading contract (callers may believe pinned-memory tensors are used) and dead parameter surface; the Mandatory Check flags placeholder discard patterns unless documented as a temporary migration shim. (3) Drop the parameter (and update maybe_create_thinking_budget_state_holder and callers) if pinned memory is genuinely not needed, or actually allocate mask/force_token_ids as pinned host buffers if a device-copy path is intended.

(1) The class uses abbreviated local attributes `rs`/`re` for reasoning_start/end token ids in __init__; `re` also shadows the standard library module name. (2) The rubric requires full names in this area and shadowing a stdlib name is error-prone if `re` is later used. (3) Rename to reasoning_start_token_ids/reasoning_end_token_ids locals.

(1) The new profiler hotfix block adds three separate RUN layers (`apt-get install rocm-llvm-dev`, `pip install CppHeaderParser`, and the clone+cmake+make) in the shared `base` stage, and the `apt-get update && install` does not run `rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*` afterward. (2) Because it lives in `base`, this heavy CLR rebuild (clone rocm-systems, cmake, make -j) is inherited by every downstream stage's FROM base, and the un-cleaned apt lists plus retained /tmp intermediate cmake artifacts inflate the final image size across all build stages. (3) Consolidate the three RUN commands into a single layer, append `&& rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*` to the apt step, and consider whether the CLR rebuild belongs only in the stage(s) that actually need the patched profiler rather than the common base. Also mark it clearly with the tracking removal condition (already noted: remove at ROCm 7.2.3).

(1) The kineto ROCm-remote checkout (`git checkout 2d73be3`) is removed and replaced by the CLR rebuild hotfix, but there is no inline comment linking the two changes. (2) A future maintainer removing the CLR hotfix at ROCm 7.2.3 may not realize the kineto pin was dropped as part of the same profiler fix and could inadvertently reintroduce a profiler regression. (3) Add a short comment near the removed kineto step (or in the hotfix block) stating that the kineto ROCm fork pin was dropped because the CLR rebuild now provides the profiler fix.

(1) The middleware now guards the `/inference` prefix (via `GUARDED_PREFIX`), meaning ALL paths starting with `/inference` now require authentication when `--api-key` is set. In the parent, only `/inference/v1/generate` existed under this prefix and the security doc explicitly listed it as an UNPROTECTED endpoint. This PR both (a) moves `/inference/v1/generate` from the 'Unprotected' section to the 'Protected' section of the docs, and (b) newly enforces auth on it. (2) This is a behavior change / potential backward-compatibility break: existing disaggregated-serving clients (e.g. examples/disaggregated/disaggregated_serving/example_mm_serve.py and examples/generate/token_generation_client.py which POST to `/inference/v1/generate` without any Authorization header) will now receive 401 when the server is started with `--api-key`. Also note the disagg router registers `/abort_requests` (no `/inference` prefix) which remains unprotected — inconsistent with the intent of guarding the disagg surface. (3) Confirm this auth enforcement on `/inference/v1/generate` is intended (it appears to be, given the doc move), and update the example clients / disagg docs to send the Bearer token; consider whether `/abort_requests` should also be guarded.

(1) `url_path.startswith(GUARDED_PREFIX)` uses a plain string-prefix match, so it also matches unintended sibling paths such as `/v1beta`, `/v2xyz`, or `/inference-internal` (any path literally beginning with the string, not a path segment). (2) Risk: future endpoints whose names merely start with these substrings would be unexpectedly auth-guarded (or a crafted path could be mistakenly classified). This is largely pre-existing behavior for `/v1` and not newly introduced, but broadening the prefix set increases the surface for such collisions. (3) If strict path-segment matching is desired, match against `('/v1/', '/v2/', '/inference/')` plus exact-equality checks, or split the path on `/` and compare the first segment.

(1) The `/v2/embed` and `/v2/rerank` endpoints referenced in docs and tests do not exist in the parent snapshot (no route registration found for `/v2` anywhere in the codebase at parent). (2) If the route-registration change is not included in this PR, the new tests `test_v2_endpoint_rejects_missing_api_token` / `test_v2_endpoint_accepts_valid_api_token` will fail (the `accepts_valid_api_token` test expects HTTP 200, but a non-existent route returns 404, and the `rejects_missing` test would pass only incidentally since auth is checked before routing). (3) Verify the `/v2` route handlers are added in the same PR/branch; otherwise the added tests are broken and the doc entries are premature.

(1) The docs sentence is now grammatically broken and has an unbalanced markdown emphasis: 'under the `/v1` path prefix**, and other similar `/v2`, `/inference` path prefix**.' The `**` bolding is mismatched and 'path prefix' should be plural. (2) A malformed security doc is user-facing and reduces clarity about exactly what is protected. (3) Rephrase to e.g. '**only for endpoints under the `/v1`, `/v2`, and `/inference` path prefixes**' with correctly paired `**` markers.

(1) The new `_apply_prefix_caching` method's Mamba-hybrid branch (`else` path) front-trims FA groups via `[:num_blocks]`, and the PR's own added test `test_mismatched_physical_per_logical_fails_with_prefix_caching` explicitly asserts this produces INCORRECT transfer ids (it asserts the result != the correct suffix-aligned ids). (2) Risk: if prefix caching were ever active for Mamba hybrid + heterogeneous TP, this silently pairs remote prefix kernel blocks with local uncached-suffix slots — silent KV data corruption. The code relies entirely on the RuntimeError guard added at worker.py:~1302 (`enable_prefix_caching` + mismatched physical_per_logical) to make this path unreachable. (3) Fix: verify the guard fully covers all entry paths into the Mamba front-trim branch; add an assertion inside the front-trim branch that `enable_prefix_caching` is False (or that no logical block was cached) so a future regression fails loudly rather than corrupting data. Confirm the guard also covers the homogeneous physical_per_logical + prefix-caching case where front-trim still runs.

(1) The new RuntimeError guard only triggers when `remote_physical_per_logical != self._physical_blocks_per_logical_kv_block`. For Mamba hybrid with prefix caching enabled AND homogeneous physical_per_logical (equal ratios), the guard is skipped but `_apply_prefix_caching` still enters the Mamba front-trim branch, which trims to `min(local, remote)` from the front. When ratios are equal and a prefix is cached locally, local has fewer blocks than remote and front-trim pairs remote prefix blocks with local suffix — still wrong. (2) Risk: partial prefix cache hit on a Mamba hybrid model with matched TP could still corrupt the transfer. (3) Fix: gate the guard on `enable_prefix_caching` alone for Mamba hybrid, independent of the physical_per_logical mismatch, or make the front-trim branch reject any case where num_local != num_remote for FA groups when prefix caching is enabled.

(1) The inline comment `# (NOTE: ZhanqiuHu) Mamba hybrid: no prefix caching support so far.HeteroTP` has a missing space before 'HeteroTP' and reads awkwardly. (2) Minor: reduces readability of a subtle correctness-critical comment. (3) Fix: add the missing space and rephrase to 'Mamba hybrid: no prefix caching support so far. HeteroTP can cause...'.

(1) In `DefaultChatOutputProcessor::new`, the unified-parser path is taken only when `tool_call_parser == reasoning_parser` AND a unified parser resolves; otherwise it falls through to the split path. In the split path, when the caller sets a mismatched selection but one side resolves to `gemma4` (now a `register_unified_dummy` entry), `resolve_tool_parser`/`resolve_optional_reasoning_parser` will invoke the dummy creator which returns `ToolParserError::DummyUnifiedParser`/`ReasoningError::DummyUnifiedParser`, surfacing as a `ParserInitialization` error. This is only well-defined for the *mixed* case the test covers (Auto/Explicit(gemma4)); however note the split path is also entered when `tool_call_parser == reasoning_parser` is true but `resolve_optional_unified_parser` returns `Ok(None)` (e.g. `ParserSelection::None` on both sides, or an Explicit name not registered as unified). For `None`/`None` this is correct (disabled). But for two equal `Explicit("gemma4")` where the unified factory DOES contain it, the unified path is taken — good. The risk: if both sides are equal `Explicit(x)` where `x` is a normal split parser (not unified), `resolve_optional_unified_parser` returns None and the split path runs normally — correct. The logic appears sound, but the `tool_parsing_enabled()` gate inside the split branch means that when tools are absent/tool_choice=None, `tool_parser` is None and only the reasoning dummy fires: for equal Explicit(gemma4) with no tools, the unified path is still taken first (independent of tool_parsing_enabled), which is the desired behavior. (2) Impact: subtle branch interactions could route a gemma4 request into the split path and produce a confusing DummyUnifiedParser error instead of using the unified parser, if the two selections are equal-but-both-None or differ. Verify the mixed/None permutations are all intended. (3) Fix: add explicit tests for (None,None), (Explicit(gemma4),Explicit(other)), and (Auto,Explicit(gemma4)) permutations, and document the branch precedence.

(1) `resolve_optional_unified_parser` ignores `tool_parsing_enabled()` — when the two selections are equal and gemma4 resolves, the unified parser is constructed and used even when `request.tools` is empty / `tool_choice == None` (a case where the old split path would have skipped the tool parser entirely). (2) Impact: for gemma4 with no tools, a unified parser that also handles tool-call extraction will now be active where previously tool parsing was disabled; if the unified parser emits tool-call events without any tools declared, downstream behavior could differ from the prior split behavior. (3) Fix: confirm `Gemma4UnifiedParser` behaves correctly (only reasoning, no tool extraction) when `tools` is empty, and add a test covering equal-selection gemma4 with empty tools.

(1) `ReasoningParserCreator` and `ToolParserCreator` are changed from plain `fn(...)` function pointers to `Arc<dyn Fn(...) + Send + Sync>`, and `ParserFactory` derives `Clone`. Each `create` call now goes through `creator.as_ref()(...)` (a dynamic dispatch + Arc deref) instead of a direct fn-pointer call. (2) Impact: negligible in practice — parser creation happens once per request, not in the token loop — but it adds an allocation per registration and a vtable indirection per creation. The change is only needed to support the closure-capturing `register_unified_dummy`; a non-capturing approach (e.g. a dedicated enum variant or a name-keyed match) would avoid boxing every creator. (3) Fix: acceptable as-is given it is off the hot path; optionally keep fn-pointers for the common case and special-case the dummy to avoid Arc allocation for every registered parser.

(1) The `winnow` dependency gains the `simd` feature in rust/Cargo.toml. (2) Impact: this is a performance-positive change (SIMD-accelerated parsing) but increases the compiled surface and may affect build reproducibility across targets that lack the assumed SIMD support; winnow's `simd` uses runtime feature detection so this is generally safe. (3) Fix: none required — flag only to confirm the feature is intentional and that CI targets support it; verify no measurable regression on non-x86 targets.

(1) `register_unified_dummy` is duplicated verbatim in both `reasoning/mod.rs` and `tool/mod.rs`, each cloning the name twice (`name`/`registered_name`) where a single clone suffices — `registered_name` is only used to pass `&registered_name` to `register_creator`, while `name` is moved into the closure. (2) Impact: minor readability/maintenance; the two `name.clone()` (one at construction, one inside the closure per call) are fine, but the extra `registered_name = name.clone()` is redundant since `register_creator` takes `&str` and could borrow before the move. (3) Fix: pass `name.as_str()` to `register_creator` before moving `name` into the closure, or restructure to a single clone.

(1) `NixlEPAll2AllManager.__init__` gains a new `assert tcp_store_group is not None`, but the signature still declares `tcp_store_group=None` as the default and other manager subclasses (AgRs, DeepEP*, FlashInfer*) permit `None`. (2) Risk: any construction path that still instantiates NixlEPAll2AllManager without a tcp_store_group (e.g. an older/other code path, or a caller unaware of the requirement) will now hard-crash with an opaque AssertionError instead of failing gracefully or being caught by type checking. (3) If tcp_store_group is truly mandatory for this backend, drop the `=None` default in the signature so the requirement is enforced at the call boundary and documented; keep the assert only as a redundant guard, or raise a descriptive error message.

(1) The new `if x_sf is not None` guards around the scale all2allv and the nvfp4 swizzle are correct and necessary: `moe_kernel_quantize_input` returns `x_sf=None` for `quant_dtype=None` and for the fp8_blocked path where scales aren't produced by this branch, so the previous unconditional `MnnvlMoe.mnnvl_moe_alltoallv(x_sf, ...)` and `x_sf.element_size()` would have thrown on None. (2) This matches the test enabling `fp8_blocked` and `None` for the two-sided backend. This is a legitimate fix, flagged only to confirm the analysis. No change needed. (3) N/A.

(1) `MoETestConfig.is_sequence_parallel` infers SP purely from `ep_size > 1 and tp_size > 1`, and `is_valid_config` then re-asserts `ep_size == dp_size * tp_size` for the SP branch. This overloads existing dimension fields to encode a distinct parallelism mode implicitly rather than with an explicit flag. (2) Risk: any future non-SP config that legitimately has both ep_size>1 and tp_size>1 (e.g. EP+TP combined) would be silently misclassified as SP and routed through sp_wrapper / skip weight-TP-chunking, producing wrong baselines. Currently PARALLEL_COMBOS avoids this, so it's latent. (3) Consider an explicit `is_sequence_parallel` field on MoETestConfig set at config-generation time, rather than deriving it from a heuristic on dimension sizes.

(1) The new `check_tool_usage` before-validator silently rewrites `tool_choice="auto"` to `"none"` whenever no tools are supplied (both when omitted and when explicitly passed as "auto"), as asserted by `test_responses_request_no_tools_tool_choice_auto`. (2) This is a behavior change: previously an `auto` request without tools kept `tool_choice="auto"`, and the OpenAI Responses API leaves an explicitly-provided `tool_choice="auto"` as `auto` (it only defaults to none when unspecified). Coercing an explicit `auto` to `none` can silently alter downstream behavior for callers relying on the value being preserved, and diverges from the stated goal of matching OpenAI behavior. (3) Consider only defaulting to `none` when `tool_choice` was not explicitly provided (e.g. check `"tool_choice" not in data`) rather than overwriting an explicit `auto`.

(1) In the named-tool-choice validation for the `has_tools` branch, tool names are extracted with `t.get("name")` for dicts and `getattr(t, "name", None)` for objects. Because this is a `mode="before"` validator, `tools` items may already be parsed `Tool` objects in some call paths (e.g. re-validation of an existing model), and non-function tool types (mcp, web_search, etc.) may not expose a `name` attribute, yielding `None` entries in `tool_names`. This is benign for matching (a real function name won't be None), but the set will contain a spurious `None`. (2) Low impact, only affects the constructed comparison set. (3) Optionally filter out `None` names when building `tool_names` for clarity.

(1) The `elif is_named_tool_choice and tools is not None:` guard is redundant: `has_tools` (the outer `elif` condition) already implies `tools is not None and len(tools) > 0`, so `tools is not None` is always true here. (2) No functional impact, just dead condition/indirection. (3) Drop the `and tools is not None` clause; the branch is already guarded by `has_tools`.

(1) The new `ChunkedEmbeddingMetadata.chunk_index` field is written in `_pre_process_chunked` but never read by any production code path; `_post_process_chunked` only consumes `chunk_metadata.prompt_index`, and the only reader of `chunk_index` is a test assertion. (2) An unused field adds maintenance surface and can mislead readers into thinking chunk ordering is tracked/needed for aggregation (it is not — MEAN aggregation is order-independent). (3) Either drop `chunk_index` from the dataclass, or add a comment/TODO explaining it is retained for diagnostics/future ordered aggregation.

(1) The comment/logic around removing the `short_prompts_results` branch is now implicit: every prompt in `_pre_process_chunked` is always emitted with a `-chunk-N` suffix and a corresponding metadata entry (since `chunk_list` yields at least one chunk for any non-empty prompt), so the old non-chunked/short-prompt fallback path is genuinely dead and its removal is safe. (2) However, an empty prompt (`prompt_token_ids == []`) produces zero chunks/metadata, so no aggregator entry is created for that `prompt_idx`, and the finalize loop will hit `raise ValueError("Result not found for prompt {prompt_idx}")`. This behavior is unchanged from the parent (which also raised) but is now the only path and is worth a note. (3) Consider adding an explicit guard/assert or comment documenting that empty prompts are unsupported in chunked processing, to avoid confusing errors.

(1) `load_draft_model` unconditionally reads `model.draft_id_to_target_id` (`if self.draft_logits is not None and model.draft_id_to_target_id is not None:`), but only the Qwen3 DSpark model (`Qwen3DSparkForCausalLM`) defines this attribute. The DeepSeek-V4 DSpark model (`DSparkDeepseekV4ForCausalLM`) never sets `draft_id_to_target_id` in its `__init__`. (2) When a DeepSeek-V4 DSpark checkpoint is loaded with probabilistic (gumbel) drafting enabled (`self.draft_logits is not None`), this raises `AttributeError` at model-load time, breaking the DSV4 DSpark path that previously worked. (3) Fix: use `getattr(model, 'draft_id_to_target_id', None)` here, or define `draft_id_to_target_id = None` as a class attribute on `DSparkDeepseekV4ForCausalLM`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a DSV4 DSpark checkpoint with probabilistic (temperature>0 gumbel) spec decoding and confirm load_draft_model does not raise AttributeError.

findings.claim: (1) `map_draft_to_target` computes `draft_ids + self.draft_id_to_target_id[draft_ids]`, i.e. it treats `draft_id_to_target_id` as an *offset* table (d2t delta), adding it to the draft id. (2) This must match how the checkpoint stores the `d2t` tensor: if the checkpoint's `d2t[i]` already holds the absolute target id (not the delta target-minus-draft), the result is wrong and drafted tokens map to incorrect target-vocab ids, silently corrupting acceptance/verification. In the argmax (greedy) path `map_draft_to_target` is applied, whereas in the probabilistic path the `_d2t_scatter_index = arange + d2t` scatter is used — both assume the delta convention, so they are at least consistent, but the delta-vs-absolute assumption should be asserted/validated against the checkpoint format. (3) Fix: document and assert the d2t delta convention (e.g. verify `d2t` is small offsets, not absolute ids) at load time.

(1) In the probabilistic branch, `buf = self._draft_scatter_buf[:num_reqs]` is filled once at init with -inf and then `buf.index_copy_(1, self._d2t_scatter_index, logits_i.to(buf.dtype))` overwrites only the target columns hit by `_d2t_scatter_index`. Across the sequential loop over `i` (and across successive `propose` calls) the same buffer rows are reused. (2) This is correct only if every draft column always scatters into the same set of target columns each step (it does, since `_d2t_scatter_index` is fixed), so non-scattered columns stay -inf — that is intended. However, `_d2t_scatter_index` has length `draft_vocab_size` and indexes into a buffer of width `self.vocab_size` (target vocab); if any `arange+d2t` entry is out of `[0, vocab_size)`, `index_copy_` will error or write out of range. (3) Fix: assert `(self._d2t_scatter_index >= 0).all() and (self._d2t_scatter_index < self.vocab_size).all()` at load_draft_model time.

(1) In the greedy (argmax) branch, `map_draft_to_target` is called per step inside the sequential loop and does a gather `self.draft_id_to_target_id[draft_ids]` plus add; in the probabilistic branch a full `[num_reqs, vocab_size]` scatter buffer `index_copy_` runs each of the N sequential steps. (2) The per-step full-vocab scatter (`self.max_num_reqs x self.vocab_size` buffer) adds a sizeable kernel over the whole target vocab at every one of the N Markov steps, on the CUDA-graph-captured hot path; for large vocab this is non-trivial launch/BW cost multiplied by num_speculative_steps. (3) Consider sampling in draft-vocab space and remapping the single sampled id to target (as the argmax path does) instead of scattering full logits, if the gumbel verification key allows it.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare draft-step latency of DSpark reduced-vocab probabilistic drafting with the full-vocab index_copy_ scatter vs a draft-space sample + id remap, over num_speculative_steps.

findings.claim: (1) `load_weights` now renames checkpoint keys containing `d2t` to `draft_id_to_target_id` and silently `continue`s (drops) any key containing `t2d`. (2) The `t2d` skip and `d2t` rename are broad substring matches; a legitimately-named weight that happens to contain these substrings would be dropped/renamed unexpectedly, and dropping `t2d` without registering it in `skip_substrs` relies on it never being a required param — acceptable here but worth a guard. (3) Fix: anchor the match to the expected key suffix (e.g. endswith) and/or log dropped `t2d` keys once.

(1) On XPU, topk_sigmoid now drops the routed_scaling_factor argument entirely and returns early, so routed_scaling_factor is never applied. (2) In the sigmoid branch of fused_topk_bias (vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/router/fused_topk_bias_router.py), unlike the softmax branch, there is NO subsequent `topk_weights *= routed_scaling_factor` fixup — the sigmoid path relies entirely on the kernel applying routed_scaling_factor internally (see commit #47408 'Applies routed_scaling_factor internally'). Therefore, for any XPU MoE model with routed_scaling_factor != 1.0 (e.g. DeepSeek-style models), the routed expert weights will be silently unscaled, producing numerically wrong MoE outputs / degraded model quality with no error. (3) Fix: on the XPU early-return path, apply the scaling in Python after the kernel call, e.g. `if routed_scaling_factor != 1.0: topk_weights *= routed_scaling_factor` before returning, matching the softmax-branch fixup, until the vllm-xpu-kernels bump adds native support.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run an XPU MoE routing correctness test with routed_scaling_factor != 1.0 (e.g. a DeepSeek-V-style config) and compare topk_weights against the reference/torch fallback (_topk_softplus_sqrt_torch style) which multiplies by routed_scaling_factor; assert weights match within tolerance.

findings.claim: (1) The new integrity check `missing_files = weight_files_in_index - hf_weights_files_set` and subsequent `raise FileNotFoundError` compares the full index set against the passed-in `hf_weights_files`. However, `hf_weights_files` is not guaranteed to be a superset of the index; callers (e.g. default_loader.py, bitsandbytes_loader.py) pass files gathered by glob patterns, and in legitimate scenarios (sharded models where a subset is downloaded, EP/TP weight filtering via `should_skip_weight`, or subfolder layouts) not every file referenced in the index may be present locally at this stage. This can turn previously-working loads into hard failures. (2) Risk: regression that breaks loading of models that loaded fine before, especially partial/filtered downloads. (3) Fix: confirm the invariant that all index-referenced files must always be locally present at every call site before making this a hard `raise`; otherwise downgrade to a warning or scope the check to the specific loader path that guarantees a full download.

(1) The check uses exact string equality on absolute paths: `weight_files_in_index` entries are built via `os.path.join(hf_folder, weight_map[weight_name])`, while `hf_weights_files` come from the caller. If path normalization differs (e.g. trailing slashes, symlinked cache dirs, relative vs absolute, or `hf_folder` not matching the prefix used to enumerate weight files), the set difference will spuriously report missing files. (2) Risk: false-positive FileNotFoundError. (3) Fix: normalize both sides with `os.path.realpath`/`os.path.normpath` before the set difference, and/or compare on basenames.

(1) After adding the integrity check, the subsequent line `hf_weights_files = [f for f in hf_weights_files if f in weight_files_in_index]` still filters out extra files, but the new raise only validates the reverse direction (index files present). The comment 'Filter out any fields that are not found in the index file' and the new check together are fine, but consider using `hf_weights_files_set` (already computed) in the filter comprehension via set membership to avoid recomputation clarity issues. Minor. (2) Low impact. (3) Reuse the already-created set variable for clarity.

(1) In compute_topk_logprobs, when num_logprobs == 0 the code now sets `topk_token_ids = logprob_token_ids_state.token_ids.gpu` purely to have a valid int32 tensor to pass as a pointer (the kernel's topk branch is a no-op since NUM_TOPK==0). This is a somewhat subtle placeholder. (2) Risk: a future reader may assume topk_token_ids holds real topk data; also the kernel still reads topk_token_ids.stride(0) which is now that state tensor's stride rather than 0. This is fine only because the topk branch is masked off entirely when NUM_TOPK==0. (3) The inline comment does document this ('This tensor just used as an int32 pointer, data not accessed'), which is good; consider asserting NUM_TOPK==0 implies the topk branch is unreachable, or reuse `logprob_token_ids` (int64) — but note that would break the newly-required int32 dtype invariant, so the current int32 choice is intentional and correct.

(1) The fix consolidates the `.to(tl.int64)` cast so it now applies to BOTH the custom-token branch and the topk branch (`tokens = tl.load(src + col, ...).to(tl.int64)`). Previously the topk branch loaded topk_indices without an explicit int64 cast, so when the output tensor is int64 and topk_indices was int64 this happened to work, but when topk_indices was int32 (torch.topk returns int64 indices by default though) a dtype mismatch on tl.store could corrupt token ids. (2) Why it matters: the whole PR is a dtype int64/int32 bug fix; the key correctness change is that topk indices are now explicitly produced as int32 on the torch side (`.to(torch.int32)`) and uniformly upcast to int64 in the kernel before storing into the int64 output. Verify that torch.topk(...).indices, which is normally int64, being forced to int32 does not truncate valid token ids — vocab sizes are far below int32 max so this is safe. (3) The consolidation is correct and the dtype path is now consistent; confidence this is a genuine, correct fix.

(1) New validation raises VLLMValidationError when both `logprobs` and `logprob_token_ids` are set and `logprobs != len(logprob_token_ids)`. (2) Backward-compat risk: previously a user could set both `logprobs=k` and `logprob_token_ids=[...]` with mismatched lengths without error (behavior may have been undefined/ignored). Existing scripts that set both now hard-fail. This is likely intended to enforce the sampler's row-width invariant, but it is a behavior change worth calling out. (3) If tolerating mismatch is not required, this is acceptable; otherwise document the new constraint in the `logprob_token_ids` docstring so users understand logprobs must equal len(logprob_token_ids) when both are supplied.

(1) The new teacher-loading block in load_checkpoint uses `unwrapped_model = unwrap_model(model)[0]` and only loads the teacher when `hasattr(unwrapped_model, 'teacher_model')`. But in load_modelopt_checkpoint's `else` branch, it calls `load_checkpoint(model, optimizer, opt_param_scheduler, ...)` which re-enters load_checkpoint. The recursion guard relies solely on the teacher submodel not having a `teacher_model` attribute. This is a fragile guard: if the teacher model itself is ever wrapped in a distillation module (or gains a `teacher_model` attribute), infinite recursion occurs. (2) Risk: silent infinite recursion / stack overflow during teacher checkpoint load. (3) Consider an explicit recursion flag/argument (e.g. a `_loading_teacher=True` kwarg) instead of relying on attribute presence, and document the invariant clearly.

(1) The teacher-loading block assumes `unwrap_model(model)[0]` is the module that carries `teacher_model`. With pipeline parallelism / virtual pipeline, `unwrap_model(model)` returns a list of chunks; taking `[0]` only inspects the first chunk. In the original code, the teacher was built and loaded inside `_build_teacher_model` regardless of PP arrangement, and the distillation wrapper (mtd.convert) attaches teacher_model to the same model chunk that the builder returned. If model_provider produced multiple chunks and the distillation model is not chunk 0 on some ranks, the teacher would silently not be loaded. (2) Risk: teacher weights left uninitialized on some ranks -> wrong KD loss / silent training corruption. (3) Iterate over all chunks in `unwrap_model(model)` and load teacher for each that has a `teacher_model` attribute; ModelOpt distillation asserts no interleaved PP, but iterating is still safer and clearer.

(1) Behavior change in load ordering: previously the teacher checkpoint was loaded inside model_provider/_load_teacher_model, i.e. before the student checkpoint load and before Float16Module wrapping. Now teacher loading happens at the very end of load_checkpoint, after the student checkpoint has been loaded and after Float16Module conversion. This is the intended fix (loading after Float16Module), but it means the teacher is only loaded if the user actually calls load_checkpoint on the student. If a training flow builds the KD model but relies on `--finetune`/no `--load` such that load_checkpoint is skipped or short-circuits early on 'no checkpoint found', the teacher will silently never be loaded (previously it always loaded during model build). (2) Risk: teacher weights uninitialized when student has no checkpoint to load. (3) Verify load_checkpoint always reaches the modelopt block even when student load_dir is empty/missing, or add a fallback to load the teacher during build when the student won't be loaded.

(1) `_load_teacher_model` was renamed to `_build_teacher_model` to reflect that it no longer loads the checkpoint. This is a good rename, but the function still calls `_add_load_convert_hooks(teacher)` and the NOTE comment points to checkpointing.py; ensure no other caller/import references the old name `_load_teacher_model`. (2) Risk: a stale reference to the old private name would raise AttributeError/ImportError. (3) grep the repo for `_load_teacher_model` to confirm the only usage was the one updated in this diff.

(1) The old skip gate `QUARK_MXFP4_AVAILABLE` required `amd-quark >= 0.8.99`; the replacement `QUARK_MXFP4_TORCH_COMPATIBLE` only enforces a version floor (>= 0.12.0) on torch >= 2.11, and on torch < 2.11 it evaluates to just `find_spec('quark') is not None` with no minimum version. (2) This is a behavioral change: on torch < 2.11 an environment with a very old amd-quark (e.g. < 0.9) that was previously skipped would now attempt to run `test_mxfp4_loading_and_execution_moe`, potentially failing rather than skipping. (3) If a lower bound is still needed for old torch, add it explicitly, e.g. `... else version.parse(...amd-quark...) >= version.parse('0.8.99')` instead of `else True`.

(1) `_apply_post_tokenization_to_token_type_ids` re-implements, by hand, the padding-then-truncation logic that already lives in `TokenizeParams._token_padding` and `TokenizeParams._token_truncation` (including the `< 0 -> max_input_tokens` remapping, the `truncation_side or tokenizer.truncation_side` fallback, and the `max_length == 0 -> [:0]` special case). The whole point of this PR is to keep `token_type_ids` aligned with `prompt_token_ids`, which are transformed via `tok_params.apply_post_tokenization` -> `_validate_tokens` -> (`_token_padding`, `_token_truncation`, ...). (2) These two code paths must stay byte-for-byte equivalent in their pad/truncate behavior and ordering forever; if a future change edits the ordering or logic in `TokenizeParams._validate_tokens` (e.g. reorders padding vs truncation, or changes the truncation-side fallback), this hand-copied duplicate will silently diverge and reintroduce exactly the misalignment bug this PR fixes, with no test guarding the coupling. (3) Expose a small public helper on `TokenizeParams` (e.g. `apply_post_tokenization_to_ids(tokenizer, ids, pad_value=...)`) that reuses `_token_padding`/`_token_truncation`, parameterizing only the pad value, and call it here so there is a single source of truth for pad/truncate semantics.

(1) When padding, the new helper pads `token_type_ids` with `token_type_ids[-1]` (the last segment's type id), whereas `TokenizeParams._token_padding` pads `prompt_token_ids` with `tokenizer.pad_token_id`. This is a deliberate and defensible choice (a padded slot's *type* should follow the trailing segment, not the pad token id), but it is undocumented. (2) A future reader comparing the two functions may 'fix' this to use pad_token_id, corrupting the type-id stream, or may misread the intent. Additionally, `token_type_ids[-1] if token_type_ids else 0` chooses 0 for empty input, which only matters in the degenerate empty-prompt case. (3) Add a one-line comment explaining that padding replicates the trailing segment's token type id (not the pad token id) so padded positions stay in the correct segment.

(1) The condition guarding token-type-id processing changed from `if token_type_ids := engine_prompt.pop(...)` (truthy) to `if token_type_ids is not None`. This is a behavioral change: an empty list `[]` returned as token_type_ids now enters the branch and produces `compressed_token_type_ids = compress_token_type_ids([]) = 0` and a cloned pooling_params, whereas before it fell through to the else branch reusing the shared pooling_params. (2) For the normal case this is harmless/more correct, but it is an intentional-looking semantic change bundled into a truncation fix; if some model can legitimately emit an empty token_type_ids list this now attaches a `compressed_token_type_ids=0` kwarg where none was attached before. (3) Confirm this change is intended (it appears to be, distinguishing 'no token_type_ids key' from 'empty list') and, if so, it is fine as-is; otherwise restore the truthy check.

(1) The Triton function `_e2m1_lookup` is defined but never referenced anywhere in the codebase — the dequantization kernel uses `_e2m1_inline` (the binary-tree variant) instead, and a grep of the parent tree confirms `_e2m1_lookup` has no callers. (2) Dead code adds maintenance burden and can mislead future readers into thinking there are two active decode paths; it also risks the two implementations drifting apart. (3) Remove `_e2m1_lookup`, or if it is intended to be the canonical reference, document why both exist and pick one for the kernel.

(1) `dequantize_to_dtype` now dispatches to `_triton_dequantize_nvfp4` whenever `not swizzle and current_platform.is_cuda_alike()`, but the Triton dequant path relies on the E2M1 lookup only implicitly and bypasses `break_fp4_bytes`/`kE2M1ToFloat_handle`. Unlike the QDQ path (which the oracle now explicitly moves `kE2M1ToFloat_handle.val` to device before capture), the standalone dequant call site in `nvfp4_emulation_moe.py` (weights dequantized on every forward) does not guarantee the E2M1 table is on-device — however the Triton dequant path does not use that table, so this is fine; the concern is that the CPU-reference fallback (`break_fp4_bytes`) still indexes `kE2M1ToFloat_handle.val` with a CUDA `abs_vals` tensor and will error if the table is left on CPU. Confirm the reference (non-Triton) fallback is never reached on device without the table having been moved. (2) A device-mismatch would raise at runtime on non-CUDA-alike or when Triton is disabled. (3) Consider making `break_fp4_bytes` device-aware (move `kE2M1` to `abs_vals.device`) so both paths are robust independent of the oracle's one-time `.to(device)`.

(1) The public return contract of `ref_nvfp4_quant_dequant` changed from `tuple[torch.Tensor, None]` to a single `torch.Tensor`. (2) This is a breaking API change for any external caller unpacking the tuple (e.g. `x_dq, _ = ...`); the in-repo callers (`fused_moe/utils.py` line 301, `run_nvfp4_emulations`) were correctly updated, but the function lives in a `utils` module without a deprecation shim, so out-of-tree or downstream code that imported it will break silently with an unpacking error. (3) Verify no other callers exist; if this is a stable-ish util, consider keeping the tuple return or adding a note in the changelog. Confidence lowered because all in-repo callers appear updated.

(1) `kE2M1ToFloat_handle.val = kE2M1ToFloat_handle.val.to(layer.weight.device)` mutates a module-global SimpleNamespace during `convert_to_nvfp4_moe_kernel_format`. In a multi-device setup (e.g. multiple GPUs in one process, or repeated layer conversion targeting different devices) this global table ends up bound to whichever device was last converted, and every re-conversion issues a host->device or device->device copy. (2) Minor: an unnecessary repeated `.to()` and a potential correctness hazard if layers span devices. (3) Since the Triton dequant path does not read this table at all, confirm whether this global move is even needed for the EMULATION MoE path, or scope the table per-device.

(1) `_triton_nvfp4_quant_dequant` asserts `x_k % block_size == 0` only when `not torch.compiler.is_compiling()`, but the kernel also implicitly assumes the input is 2D (`x_m, x_k = x.shape`). The Python reference `ref_nvfp4_quant` explicitly asserts `x.ndim == 2`; the Triton path drops that guard. (2) A 3D input would silently produce wrong indexing/results rather than a clear error. (3) Add an `x.ndim == 2` assertion (guarded by `is_compiling()` if needed) to match the reference contract.

(1) The new test downloads a multi-GB checkpoint (`nvidia/Qwen3-30B-A3B-NVFP4`, one safetensors shard) from HuggingFace Hub at test time and runs `triton.testing.do_bench` benchmarking with print statements. (2) This makes the test slow, network-dependent, and flaky in CI (download failures, no offline mode), and the benchmarking loop adds runtime without asserting anything. (3) Consider gating the checkpoint-download test behind a marker (e.g. `@pytest.mark.slow`/network) and moving the benchmark prints out of the correctness test, keeping only the `assert_close` in CI.

(1) Registering a broad `app.exception_handler(ValueError)` (and `TypeError`) means every uncaught ValueError/TypeError raised anywhere in request handling — including genuine server-side programming bugs that happen to surface as ValueError/TypeError — is now recorded as 4xx in http_requests_total instead of 5xx. (2) This slightly reduces the diagnostic value of the 5xx metric: a real internal defect manifesting as a ValueError will be silently classified as a client error, potentially hiding regressions in dashboards/alerts. (3) This is consistent with the pre-existing `create_error_response` mapping (ValueError→400 was already the client-facing behavior via the catch-all Exception handler), so the change only affects the metric, not client responses. If distinguishing user-input ValueErrors from internal ValueErrors matters for observability, consider raising a dedicated VLLM* exception at input-validation boundaries rather than mapping the base ValueError class. No change strictly required — flagging as an intentional trade-off to confirm.

(1) The new branch in `MimoV2ModelArchConfigConvertor.__init__` mutates `hf_config.architectures = ["MiMoV2OmniForCausalLM"]` when a `vision_config` is present. But `MimoV2ModelArchConfigConvertor` is only selected in `MODEL_ARCH_CONFIG_CONVERTORS` for `model_type` keys `mimo_v2` and `mimo_v2_flash` (text-only). If a MiMo-V2.5-Omni checkpoint reaches this convertor via one of those model_type keys, rewriting architectures to `MiMoV2OmniForCausalLM` will silently redirect the model registry lookup to the Omni multimodal implementation, which may not match the actual weights/config path. (2) This is a silent architecture override that can load the wrong model class. (3) Confirm which model_type value the Omni checkpoint's hf_config carries and whether the Omni path is registered under a distinct convertor (mimo_v2_omni). If the vision-config detection is intended, verify the Omni convertor is actually reachable and that overriding the architectures list here does not break the text-only MiMoV2 path when a vision_config is inadvertently present.

(1) The public model class `MiMoV2ProForCausalLM` is renamed to `MiMoV2ForCausalLM`, and the architecture registry key `MiMoV2ProForCausalLM` and convertor key `mimo_v2_pro` are renamed to `MiMoV2ForCausalLM`/`mimo_v2` respectively, with no backward-compat alias. (2) Any existing HF config.json with `architectures: ["MiMoV2ProForCausalLM"]` (the previously advertised architecture name, still shown as supported in docs) will no longer resolve to a registered model and will fail to load; likewise checkpoints/configs with model_type `mimo_v2_pro` will miss the convertor. (3) If the old name was ever released/advertised, add a backward-compat alias mapping `MiMoV2ProForCausalLM`/`mimo_v2_pro` to the new class/convertor. If it was never released (pre-release rename), this is safe to drop.

(1) The guard `if getattr(hf_config, "vision_config", None):` relies on truthiness of the vision_config object. A `PretrainedConfig` sub-config is generally truthy when present, but if vision_config could be an empty dict or a config whose __bool__/__len__ is falsy, the branch would be skipped. (2) Minor robustness concern for detecting multimodal variants. (3) Prefer explicit `getattr(hf_config, "vision_config", None) is not None` to detect presence rather than truthiness.

(1) Adding a `model_serializer(mode="wrap")` to `DeltaMessage` that pops empty `tool_calls` changes the serialized shape of every streaming delta chunk (the common case has empty tool_calls). (2) Downstream/client code and vLLM's own streaming assembly may have relied on the always-present `tool_calls: []` key in the SSE chunk payload; the standard OpenAI streaming schema always emits `tool_calls` as absent unless present, so this is generally correct, but any internal accumulator that indexes `delta["tool_calls"]` without a default will now KeyError. (3) Verify all consumers of the streamed DeltaMessage payload use `.get("tool_calls", [])`; add a regression test around the streaming delta accumulation path (the added tests only check top-level serialization, not the assembly consumers).

(1) The serializer uses `data.get("tool_calls", [])` and pops when empty, but `OpenAIBaseModel` sets `extra="allow"`, so if a caller ever explicitly passed `tool_calls=None` (not the default_factory list), `data.get(...)` returns None and `len(None)` raises TypeError. (2) `tool_calls` is typed `list[ToolCall]` with `default_factory=list`, so under normal validation it is always a list — but with `extra="allow"` and unvalidated dict construction paths this could surface. (3) Consider guarding: `tc = data.get("tool_calls"); if not tc: data.pop("tool_calls", None)` which handles both None and empty-list uniformly.

(1) `len(data.get("tool_calls", [])) == 0` is a two-step check that also fails on non-sized values. (2) Minor readability/robustness. (3) Simplify to `if not data.get("tool_calls"): data.pop("tool_calls", None)` — falsy covers both empty list and None, matching the identical pattern already used for `defer_loading`/`strict` elsewhere in the file (which use `is None`), keeping the codebase consistent.

(1) `get_text_config` uses signature `def get_text_config(self, decoder=False)` and returns `self.text_config` if set, else `self`. But the base `PretrainedConfig.get_text_config` in transformers v5 also accepts an `encoder`/keyword arguments and callers may pass keyword args; more importantly, when `self.text_config` is set, the return type is a plain sub-config whereas the model (`hyperclovax_vision.py`) reads `config.text_config` directly and also `config.anyres`, `config.max_num_grids`, `config.num_queries_vis_abstractor_image`, `config.num_queries_vis_abstractor_video_slow/fast`, `config.possible_resolutions`. Several of these top-level attributes (num_queries_vis_abstractor_image, *_video_slow, *_video_fast, image_token_id, video_token_id) are NOT declared in the vendored `__init__`. They will only be present if the on-disk config JSON supplies them and they flow through `**kwargs` into `PretrainedConfig.__init__`. Under transformers v5 @strict validation (the very thing this vendoring is meant to fix), unexpected kwargs may be rejected or dropped, potentially breaking `HCXVisionForCausalLM.__init__`. (2) Risk: model init raises AttributeError for `num_queries_vis_abstractor_image` etc. if v5 strict handling drops them. (3) Fix: verify these fields survive round-trip under v5 strict; add them explicitly to the vendored __init__ signature if they are required attributes.

(1) In the vendored `__init__`, `self.hidden_size` is only set inside the `if text_config is not None:` branch.
