When `text_config is None` (the empty-initialization path this class exists to support for v5 @strict validation), `self.hidden_size` is never assigned. (2) Any downstream code that does `config.hidden_size` on an empty-initialized instance would raise AttributeError; more subtly, the base `PretrainedConfig` may not define `hidden_size`, so attribute access could fail. (3) Fix: initialize `self.hidden_size = None` (or a default) before the conditional so the attribute always exists.

(1) `text_config[val] = text_config.pop(key)` mutates the caller-provided `text_config` dict in place. HyperCLOVAXConfig (the other class in this same file) explicitly shallow-copies dicts to 'avoid mutating the caller's dict' for the same reason. Here the incoming `text_config` dict (part of the parsed config_dict) is mutated. (2) If the same config_dict is parsed/instantiated more than once (e.g. re-registration, AutoConfig double-load, or shared references), the `n_embd`->`hidden_size` remap has already been applied and the keys no longer match, so the second pass silently no-ops — usually harmless, but mutating shared input is a latent aliasing bug. (3) Fix: shallow-copy `text_config` before mutating: `text_config = dict(text_config)`.

(1) `if text_config is not None and key in text_config` uses `key in text_config` which assumes `text_config` is a dict. If a caller passes an already-instantiated PretrainedConfig object (a common pattern when configs are re-serialized/round-tripped), `key in config` and `config.pop(...)` will fail (PretrainedConfig is not a mapping). The base HF multimodal configs typically handle both dict and config-object inputs for sub-configs. (2) Risk: TypeError/AttributeError when `text_config` is passed as an object rather than a dict, e.g. during deepcopy/round-trip. (3) Fix: normalize `text_config` to a dict (or handle the object case) before the remap loop.

(1) The condition guarding the "structured outputs OR tools, not both" check was changed from `count > 1` to `count > 0`. This is a real behavioral/semantic change, not the mechanical exception-type swap the PR title ("Use VLLMValidationError consistently") describes. `count` is the number of structured-output constraint kinds set (json/regex/choice). With `count > 1` the check was effectively dead (it could never fire in a way distinct from the preceding `if count > 1` block that already raised). With `count > 0`, ANY request that specifies a single structured-output constraint together with a named tool_choice (or any tool_choice outside none/auto/required) is now rejected. (2) Why it matters: this silently narrows the accepted request space at the OpenAI-compatible API boundary. Existing clients that legitimately combined a single structured-outputs constraint with tools/named tool_choice will start getting 400 errors after upgrade — a backward-compatibility break shipped under a "Misc" refactor label, where reviewers/users are unlikely to expect a validation semantics change. The old `count > 1` was almost certainly itself a bug, but flipping it to `count > 0` should be called out and land as an intentional, documented behavior change rather than hidden inside an exception-type cleanup. (3) Fix: split this into its own commit/PR with a clear title and changelog note; add/keep tests for the boundary (single-constraint + named tool_choice), and confirm with maintainers whether the intended rule is truly `count > 0` (reject any structured output alongside tools) vs allowing structured outputs with `auto` tool_choice, which the new test asserts is allowed.

(1) `VLLMValidationError` subclasses `ValueError`, but its `__str__` appends `(parameter=..., value=...)` to the message. Several call sites here now pass `parameter=` (e.g. `tool_choice`, `cache_salt`, `prompt`), which changes the string representation of the error compared to the previous plain `ValueError` messages. (2) Why it matters: any downstream code, client, or test that matches on exact error-message text (rather than a substring) could break, since the message now has a trailing `(parameter=...)` suffix. This is a subtle contract change on the error surface. (3) Fix: verify the error-serialization path (server_utils/entrypoints.utils VLLMValidationError handling) does not double-append or leak the internal `parameter=` suffix into the HTTP response body in an unintended format, and ensure tests match on substrings (the added tests correctly use `match=` substrings, so this is low risk).

(1) For the prompt_embeds-only path, the code synthesizes fake token IDs via `input_tokens = list(range(seq_len))`, relying on the assumption that `get_mrope_input_positions` only uses the sequence length (len(input_tokens)) when `mrope_features` is empty. (2) This is a cross-model contract assumption: there are ~17 model implementations of `get_mrope_input_positions` (qwen2_vl, qwen2_5_vl, glm4v, ernie45_vl, keye, minicpmv, isaac, etc.). If any implementation inspects the actual token *values* (e.g. searching for vision/image start token IDs, or computing text-segment boundaries from special tokens) even when mm_features is empty, then feeding `range(seq_len)` produces silently wrong M-RoPE positions rather than a crash. The comment claims the assumption holds 'here since prompt_embeds are filtered out above', but that filtering only removes prompt_embeds from mm_features; it does not guarantee every model implementation ignores token values on the empty-features path. (3) Verify each get_mrope_input_positions implementation only reads len(input_tokens) when features are empty, or restrict this fix to models known to satisfy that contract; add an assertion/comment referencing the audited implementations.

(1) `input_tokens = list(range(seq_len))` materializes a Python list of length seq_len purely to pass its length to `get_mrope_input_positions`, which (per the comment) only uses the length. For long prompt_embeds sequences (thousands of tokens) this allocates a throwaway list on every request whose only purpose is to be counted. (2) Minor per-request CPU allocation/overhead in the request-init path; not in the hot forward loop but avoidable. (3) If the interface can accept a length or a lightweight range object, prefer passing `range(seq_len)` (a lazy object supporting len()) instead of `list(range(seq_len))`, unless a downstream implementation requires an actual list/indexing.

(1) `seq_len = req_state.prompt_embeds.shape[0]` assumes prompt_embeds is laid out as (seq_len, hidden_size). This matches the test (torch.randn(15, 896)), but there is no shape/dtype assertion at this parallelism/modality boundary. (2) If prompt_embeds is ever batched or transposed for some code path, shape[0] would be the wrong dimension, silently producing incorrect position counts. (3) Consider adding a brief assertion or comment documenting the expected 2D (seq_len, hidden) layout of prompt_embeds at this point.

(1) The new `num_buckets` field is added to `DistributedDataParallelConfig` with a docstring promising "If `num_buckets` is specified, `bucket_size` will be determined at runtime", but there is no runtime consumption path: `DistributedDataParallel.__init__` (distributed_data_parallel.py L69-73) only reads `ddp_config.bucket_size` and still computes `bucket_size = max(40000000, 1000000*dp_size)` when it is None; `num_buckets` is never read anywhere in megatron/core/distributed. A grep for `num_buckets` in that package shows only the unrelated FSDP `num_buckets` property. (2) Impact: A user who sets `num_buckets` will pass the config validation (which even asserts `bucket_size is None`), then silently get the default bucket sizing behavior — `num_buckets` has zero effect, so the intended bucket count is silently ignored. This violates the Mandatory Check: newly added config field lacks a meaningful read/use path. (3) Fix: implement the runtime translation from `num_buckets` to `bucket_size` in DDP construction (e.g. `bucket_size = total_num_parameters // num_buckets`, analogous to the existing `args.ddp_num_buckets` logic in training.py L1564-1569), or mark the field as a temporary migration stub with an explicit TODO.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Construct a DistributedDataParallel with ddp_config.num_buckets=N and assert the resulting number of buckets/bucket_size matches expectation.

findings.claim: (1) The `__post_init__` validation only asserts `bucket_size is None` and `num_buckets > 0` when `num_buckets is not None`; combined with the DDP code that overwrites `ddp_config.bucket_size` in place, there is no guard that catches the fact that num_buckets is unused. (2) Impact: silent misconfiguration for users. (3) Fix: until runtime support lands, raise NotImplementedError when num_buckets is set, or add a clear TODO/migration note documenting that it is not yet consumed.

(1) The new BlockStored-emitting block is placed inside `get_computed_blocks()`, which is invoked from the scheduler's waiting-queue loop and may be called for the same request across multiple scheduling attempts (e.g. when the request is skipped due to insufficient free blocks / watermark / admission-cap and re-tried in a later schedule() call, or across chunked-prefill retries). Since `get_computed_blocks()` recomputes the prefix-cache hit each time and unconditionally emits BlockStored events for the reused blocks, the same reused-prefix blocks can generate duplicate BlockStored events for a single request admission. (2) Downstream consumers (gateway) tracking prefix-cache residency will over-count reused blocks and may build an inconsistent view of the cache. (3) Emit the events only at the point the request is actually admitted/scheduled (once per admission), or de-duplicate by tracking whether the reused-prefix event has already been sent for this request. Confirm the call-site cardinality of get_computed_blocks before finalizing.

(1) `emit_cached_block_events` emits a BlockStored event with `parent_block_hash=None` for ALL reused-prefix groups, and hardcodes `start_token_idx=0`. This is only correct for group 0 / when the reused prefix truly starts at block 0. In hybrid KV-cache setups with multiple groups (the exact case that `resolve_block_hashes` with differing block sizes handles), `find_longest_cache_hit` can return per-group computed-block counts that do not all start from a null parent in a consistent chain; more importantly, if a request already has `request.num_computed_tokens > 0` (resumed/preempted request re-entering with an existing computed prefix), the reused blocks do NOT start at token 0, so `start_token_idx=0` and `parent_block_hash=None` mislabel the event's token range and parent linkage. (2) Downstream cache-topology reconstruction on the gateway will attach reused blocks to the wrong parent / wrong token offset, corrupting the reconstructed prefix tree. (3) Derive `parent_block_hash` and `start_token_idx` from the request's actual computed-token offset rather than assuming a fresh prefix from block 0, or assert/guard that this path only runs for fresh requests with num_computed_tokens == 0.

(1) The new emit path reads the report mode via `getattr(request, "kv_cache_report_mode", "incremental")` even though `Request.__init__` now always sets `self.kv_cache_report_mode` (both branches). The defensive `getattr` with a default is dead defensiveness that hides bugs: if a code path ever constructs a Request without the attribute, this silently falls back to 'incremental' rather than surfacing the error. (2) It adds indirection and masks the invariant that the attribute is always set. (3) Access `request.kv_cache_report_mode` directly now that the field is unconditionally initialized.

(1) The new reused-block event emission runs inside `get_computed_blocks`, which is on the hot scheduling path executed for every waiting request every schedule() step. Although gated by `enable_kv_cache_events` and report mode == 'full', when enabled it iterates all groups and, in `emit_cached_block_events`, loops over every cached block calling `generate_block_hash_extra_keys` and `maybe_convert_block_hash` per block plus a `logger.debug` that eagerly slices `request.all_token_ids[...]`. For long reused prefixes this is O(num_cached_blocks) extra work per scheduling attempt on the critical path. (2) Adds scheduler latency proportional to prefix length when full report mode is on. (3) The logger.debug argument `len(request.all_token_ids[start_token_idx:end_token_idx])` materializes a slice unconditionally regardless of log level; compute it as `end_token_idx - start_token_idx` instead, and consider whether the per-block extra_keys recomputation can reuse values already computed during allocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Scheduler throughput with enable_kv_cache_events + full report mode on long shared-prefix workloads vs incremental mode.

findings.claim: (1) `kv_cache_report_mode` is read from `sampling_params.extra_args` as a free-form string with no validation of allowed values ('incremental' | 'full'). (2) A typo like 'Full' or an unexpected value silently falls through to the default-behavior branch (never equals 'full'), so users enabling full reporting via a misspelled value get incremental behavior with no error — hard to diagnose. (3) Validate the value against the allowed set at request construction (or in the sampling params processing layer) and raise on invalid input.

(1) `complete_store` on the base `OffloadingManager` inserts a new REQUIRED positional parameter `req_context` *before* the existing `success: bool = True` keyword-defaulted parameter, changing the signature from `complete_store(keys, success=True)` to `complete_store(keys, req_context, success=True)`. Likewise `touch(keys)` and `complete_load(keys)` gain a required `req_context` positional. (2) This is a public method on an abstract base in `vllm/v1/kv_offload` that external offloading backends/connectors subclass; any out-of-tree `OffloadingManager` subclass or caller invoking these methods positionally (e.g. `complete_store(keys, False)`) will now silently bind `req_context=False` or raise TypeError for the missing arg, an API contract break with no deprecation shim. (3) If backward compatibility for third-party managers matters, keep `req_context` optional (default `None`/empty `ReqContext`) or place it last; otherwise document the breaking change in release notes.

(1) In the completed-jobs handler, `req_status = self._req_status[job_status.req_id]` was hoisted above the `is_store`/`else` branch so it can be passed to `complete_store`/`complete_load`. (2) Previously the lookup happened only after the store/load branch; the reordering is behavior-preserving only if `self._req_status[job_status.req_id]` is guaranteed to exist at this earlier point and the store/load calls have no side effect on `_req_status`. Both hold here (the manager calls do not mutate `_req_status`, and the entry must exist for a pending job), so behavior is preserved. (3) No change needed; noting for reviewer confidence that the move is safe.

(1) The newly added `req_context` parameter on `CPUOffloadingManager.touch`, `complete_load`, and `complete_store` is accepted but never read in any of these method bodies (grep confirms no use beyond the signature). (2) Per the Mandatory Check on unused new arguments, an argument threaded through the whole hierarchy but never consumed is dead surface area that can mask a future wiring mistake and misleads readers into thinking the CPU manager varies behavior by request. (3) If `req_context` is only plumbed for future/other-backend use, add a brief TODO/comment noting it is intentionally unused by the CPU manager, or defer adding it to the CPU implementation until a consumer exists.

(1) The pixel-limit guard is added ad-hoc to individual video backends (PyNvVideoCodec at ~L746, VideoBackend.load_bytes opencv/av branches at ~L849/L868, Molmo2VideoBackend at ~L1629, OpenCVDynamicOpenPanguVideoBackend at ~L1782) rather than in a shared choke point (e.g. OpenCVVideoBackendMixin.open_video_capture / a base load_bytes). Other registered backends that decode frames — Qwen3VLVideoBackend, Qwen2VLVideoBackend, DynamicVideoBackend, GLM46VVideoBackend, GLMGAVideoBackend, NemotronVLVideoBackend, PyNvVideoCodecVideoBackend — do not appear to invoke _check_frame_pixel_limit in this diff. (2) This means the decompression-bomb protection is incomplete: a malicious oversized video routed to an unguarded backend still allocates unbounded raster memory, defeating the stated security goal. (3) Centralize the check inside open_video_capture()/PyAV container open (the common code paths) or in a shared VideoBackend.load_bytes wrapper so every backend is covered by construction.

(1) The opencv branch reads frame dimensions via cap.get(cv2.CAP_PROP_FRAME_WIDTH/HEIGHT) which can legitimately return 0 for some containers/codecs where the property is unavailable until a frame is decoded. When width or height is 0, width*height == 0 and the guard silently passes, allowing the bomb through. Similarly, cap.get returns a float and int() truncates; a corrupt header could report bogus small values. (2) This creates a bypass of the security check for exactly the untrusted-input scenario it is meant to defend. (3) Add a fallback: if reported dimensions are non-positive, either reject the video or defer the check to the first decoded frame's actual shape.

(1) The per-frame pixel check only bounds a single frame's resolution (width*height), not the total decoded video memory, which is num_frames * width * height * channels. A video within the per-frame pixel budget but with a very large frame count can still expand into gigabytes of raster memory. (2) The stated goal is preventing decompression-bomb OOM; the guard partially addresses image bombs but leaves the temporal dimension unbounded. (3) Consider also bounding total decoded frames or total pixels (frames*w*h) for videos, or document that frame-count limiting is handled elsewhere.

(1) The PR removes `Image.MAX_IMAGE_PIXELS = None` from nano_nemotron_vl.py and nemotron_vl.py, restoring PIL's default global MAX_IMAGE_PIXELS (~89M pixels, DecompressionBombWarning at 1x and DecompressionBombError at 2x). PIL's limit is a process-global mutable, and these modules previously disabled it globally. (2) For legitimate large images that these Nemotron VL models were designed to handle, PIL will now raise DecompressionBombError inside image.load() (in ImageMediaIO.load_bytes, which only catches OSError/UnidentifiedImageError, not Image.DecompressionBombError which subclasses Exception) — potentially breaking previously-working workloads with an uncaught exception rather than the intended clean ValueError. (3) Verify Image.DecompressionBombError is caught/handled where images are loaded, and confirm VLLM_MAX_IMAGE_PIXELS default (~179M) is >= PIL default so the vLLM check triggers first; otherwise raise PIL's limit in-process to match VLLM_MAX_IMAGE_PIXELS.

(1) ImageMediaIO.load_bytes reads image.size and enforces the pixel limit, but the same load_bytes error handler only catches (OSError, Image.UnidentifiedImageError). A truncated/corrupt image where Image.open succeeds but size access or subsequent load fails with a different exception (e.g. PIL DecompressionBombError now re-enabled) will propagate as an unwrapped error rather than the uniform 'Failed to load image' ValueError contract. (2) Inconsistent error surface for callers relying on ValueError. (3) Consider catching Image.DecompressionBombError alongside the existing exceptions.

(1) In `build_chat_params`, `merge_kwargs(self.chat_template_kwargs, dict(add_generation_prompt=..., continue_final_message=..., reasoning_effort=...))` now uses the request's `chat_template_kwargs` as the *defaults* (first arg) and the fixed generation-control dict as the *overrides* (second arg). Because `merge_kwargs` computes `defaults | {overrides that are not None/'auto'}`, any key the user places in `chat_template_kwargs` that collides with `add_generation_prompt`/`continue_final_message`/`reasoning_effort` will be silently overridden by the second dict. (2) Conversely, the intended semantic of a user passing `chat_template_kwargs` is that user values should take precedence over library defaults; here they are treated as the base layer only. Verify this is the intended precedence — for the three control keys the user cannot override them, which may or may not be desired. (3) If user override of those keys is intended, swap the merge order or exclude them; if not intended, add a short comment documenting that those three keys are reserved.

(1) `_effective_chat_template_kwargs` now calls `build_chat_params(...).with_defaults(self.chat_template_kwargs)`. `ChatParams.with_defaults` merges via `merge_kwargs(default_chat_template_kwargs, self.chat_template_kwargs)` = `defaults | {request-side values not in (None,'auto')}`, so the server-level `default_chat_template_kwargs` are the base and the *request-derived* params (which already include the merged user `chat_template_kwargs` from build_chat_params) win. (2) This double-merge means the request's `chat_template_kwargs` are folded in twice (once in build_chat_params, once via with_defaults), and the precedence chain is server-default < user-request. Confirm that server-level `--default-chat-template-kwargs` are intended to be the *lowest* priority (overridable by any request); this reverses the usual notion that server defaults constrain requests. (3) Add a unit test asserting the final precedence: server default overridden by per-request chat_template_kwargs, and both overridden by the reserved generation-control keys.

(1) The new per-request `chat_template_kwargs` field on `ResponsesRequest` is passed through to `preprocess_chat` as `default_template_kwargs`, but unlike the ChatCompletion path (render/serving.py `render_chat` -> `validate_chat_template`), the Responses path does NOT call `validate_chat_template` on the user-supplied `chat_template_kwargs`. This means a Responses request can smuggle a `chat_template` key inside `chat_template_kwargs` (or other unvetted template kwargs) without the `--trust-request-chat-template` guard that protects the ChatCompletion endpoint. (2) This is a security/consistency gap: untrusted request chat templates are rejected on /chat/completions but silently accepted on /responses. (3) Route the Responses `chat_template_kwargs` through the same `validate_chat_template(...)` check (or an equivalent guard) before merging.

(1) `chat_template_kwargs = self._effective_chat_template_kwargs(request)` is now computed independently in `_make_request` (line ~607), `_make_request_with_harmony`-adjacent path (line ~632), and already inside the reasoning-parser block. For a single request this recomputes `build_chat_params` + `with_defaults` multiple times. (2) Minor redundant work per request in the request-preprocessing path (not the hot token loop, so throughput impact is negligible), plus risk of the three call sites drifting. (3) Compute it once near the top of request handling and reuse.

(1) `render_for_completion` now rebinds a local `messages = auto_drop_analysis_messages(messages)` and renders from that filtered copy, but `auto_drop_analysis_messages` returns a NEW list, so the caller's `messages` object is unchanged. In HarmonyContext (context.py `render_for_completion` -> `render_for_completion(self.messages)`), the multi-turn agentic loop re-renders `self.messages` on every tool-call turn. Previously the drop was applied once when building the message list; now it is re-applied at each render, which is functionally fine, BUT the analysis messages are never actually removed from the persisted `self.messages`/context. (2) Risk: this is behavior-preserving only if `auto_drop_analysis_messages` is idempotent and always sees the full list. It is, so the render output is correct. The subtle risk is that any consumer that reads `context.messages` after render (e.g. for logging, response_messages construction, or storing to msg_store) still sees the un-dropped analysis messages, whereas the previously-dropped list built in `parse_chat_inputs_to_harmony_messages` was the persisted list. Verify no downstream consumer relies on the persisted list already being filtered. (3) Fix: confirm/document that filtering is now purely a render-time transformation and that persisted message lists are intentionally kept complete.

(1) The removed slice-delete-reappend block in `_make_request_with_harmony` (previous_response path) was a documented no-op that re-appended messages unfiltered. Its removal relies entirely on the new `auto_drop_analysis_messages` call inside `render_for_completion` to strip stale analysis messages from `prev_msgs` before rendering. (2) Risk: the previous-response path builds `messages` and passes them into a HarmonyContext, whose `render_for_completion` calls the harmony_utils `render_for_completion` — so the drop DOES apply. Correctness depends on `render_for_completion` being the single choke point for every render path (chat completions, responses, previous_input_messages, previous_response_id). The grep shows online_renderer, serving.py line 768, and context.py all funnel through it, so this holds. (3) Fix: none required, but a test covering the `previous_response_id` (msg_store) path (not just previous_input_messages) would guard against a future path that bypasses render_for_completion.

(1) `Message.from_author_and_contents(...)` was replaced with the direct constructor `Message(author=..., content=contents)`. These are only equivalent if the `Message` constructor performs the same defaulting/validation as the factory (e.g. field named `content` vs `contents`, no channel/recipient side effects). (2) Risk: if the openai_harmony `Message` constructor has different defaults or field semantics than the `from_author_and_contents` factory, named-author messages (tool outputs with a name) could be constructed subtly differently, affecting rendered tokens. (3) Fix: confirm the constructor and factory produce identical Message objects; the added render-parity tests decoding the prompt provide reasonable coverage, but an explicit equivalence assertion would be safer. Note the codebase already uses the direct `Message(author=..., content=..., recipient=..., channel=...)` constructor in context.py, so this is likely intentional and safe.

(1) `auto_drop_analysis_messages` is now invoked inside `render_for_completion`, which in the HarmonyContext agentic loop is called once per tool-call turn. It performs two O(n) passes over the full message list each render. (2) Risk: negligible for typical conversation lengths, but for long multi-turn agentic sessions with many tool calls this repeats the full-list scan on every turn rather than maintaining an incrementally-filtered list. (3) Fix: acceptable as-is; only worth revisiting if profiling shows message-list scanning is hot in long agentic runs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark render_for_completion over a 500-message multi-turn harmony conversation across N agentic tool-call turns.

findings.claim: (1) The check `"expandable_segments:True" not in os.environ.get("PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF", "")` is a substring match against the env var only. It will miss expandable-segments being enabled through other means (e.g. PyTorch runtime `torch.cuda.memory._set_allocator_settings("expandable_segments:True")`) and is sensitive to formatting variants like `expandable_segments: True` (with a space) or differing capitalization. (2) Impact is limited: a user who enables expandable segments via a non-env-var path or an oddly-formatted value would bypass this guard and hit the exact RDMA corruption this PR aims to prevent. (3) This exactly mirrors the substring check already used in `vllm/device_allocator/cumem.py` (`"expandable_segments:True" in conf`), so the behavior is at least internally consistent; if tightening is desired, parse the config into key/value pairs and normalize whitespace/case. Given the consistency with cumem.py, this is a minor robustness note, not a bug.

(1) `_verify_kv_transfer_compat` now raises `ValueError` for a configuration combination (any KV connector + PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF=expandable_segments:True without sleep mode) that was previously accepted at startup. (2) This is a deliberate behavior change: existing launch scripts that set expandable_segments:True and use e.g. OffloadingConnector/LMCacheConnectorV1 (which may not pin memory) will now fail to start, even though the corruption specifically concerns connectors that pin/register KV memory. The comment acknowledges the intentional over-broad rejection ("we conservatively reject the combination whenever any KV connector is configured"). (3) This is intended per the PR description and the error message provides a clear remediation path, so it is acceptable; noting it as a backward-compat surface for users of non-memory-pinning connectors who must now unset the env var. Consider narrowing to known memory-pinning connectors if false rejections become a problem.

(1) The moved flush logic in `_append_unstreamed_tool_args` only appends remaining args when the final `parse_delta` call already produced a `delta_message` with `tool_calls`. In the previous serving-layer implementation, the unstreamed-args flush ran on the finish iteration by inspecting `tool_parser.prev_tool_call_arr` directly, and could construct a brand-new delta (`_create_remaining_args_delta`) even when the final delta was empty/None. (2) This is a behavioral regression: for tool parsers that finish parsing arguments on the last token but return None from `extract_tool_calls_streaming` on the final delta (a common pattern where the closing brace/token is consumed without emitting a delta), the tail of the JSON arguments will now be silently dropped, producing truncated/invalid tool-call arguments for the client. The added test `test_parse_delta_finished_no_flush_without_tool_call_delta` actually asserts this drop as intended behavior. (3) Confirm whether the previous behavior of emitting a fresh delta on finish (even without a concurrent tool_call delta) is needed for the affected parsers; if so, `_append_unstreamed_tool_args` should be able to synthesize a DeltaToolCall when `delta_message` has no tool_calls but `get_remaining_unstreamed_args()` is non-empty.

(1) The `finish_reason` computation dropped the `auto_tools_called` term (previously `auto_tools_called or (tools_streamed[i] and not tool_choice_function_name) or ...`), leaving only `(tools_streamed[i] and not tool_choice_function_name) or (self.use_harmony and harmony_tools_streamed[i])`. `auto_tools_called` was derived from `len(tool_parser.prev_tool_call_arr) > 0`, while `tools_streamed[i]` is only set True when a delta with `tool_calls` was actually streamed. (2) If a tool call is detected by partial JSON parsing (prev_tool_call_arr populated) but no tool-call delta was ever streamed to the client (e.g. all args flushed only at finish, or a parser that populates prev_tool_call_arr without emitting deltas), the finish_reason will now be 'stop' instead of 'tool_calls', silently changing the API response for existing clients relying on finish_reason='tool_calls'. (3) Verify that with the new parser-side flush, `tools_streamed[i]` is guaranteed to be set True in every case where a tool call was actually produced; otherwise restore an equivalent auto_tools_called condition.

(1) `get_remaining_unstreamed_args` returns '' when `expected.startswith(actual)` is False, whereas the old serving-layer logic used `expected_call.replace(actual_call, '', 1)` and also trimmed the latest delta's already-streamed length (`actual_call[:-latest_delta_len]`) before diffing. The new code diffs against the full `streamed_args_for_tool[index]` without accounting for args streamed in the very same final delta. (2) If the final delta streamed part of the arguments (the common case), `streamed_args_for_tool` already includes those, so `startswith` should still hold and the remainder is correct; but the semantics differ from the old `replace(...)` (which removed the first occurrence anywhere). The stricter `startswith` is arguably more correct, but the loss of the `latest_delta_len` adjustment means the two implementations are not behavior-identical. (3) Confirm `streamed_args_for_tool[index]` is updated to include the current delta's args before finish is processed; add a shape/ordering assertion or a comment documenting the invariant.

(1) The public-ish static/instance methods `_should_check_for_unstreamed_tool_arg_tokens` and `_create_remaining_args_delta` on `OpenAIServingChat` were removed. (2) Although prefixed with underscore (private), out-of-tree parsers/tests or downstream forks may reference them; their removal is a silent breaking change and the accompanying test class `TestCreateRemainingArgsDelta` was deleted. (3) If backward compatibility for these helpers matters, keep a thin deprecated shim; otherwise this is acceptable given the private naming — noting for reviewer awareness.

(1) The change adds `ignore_patterns="*"` to `common_kwargs` in `get_model_path`, which is called (per `arg_utils.py:767,777`) only when `HF_HUB_OFFLINE` is set to resolve a model/tokenizer name to its local snapshot directory via `snapshot_download`. Passing `ignore_patterns="*"` instructs `snapshot_download` to ignore ALL files, so the function returns the snapshot directory path but will not (down)load/link any files into it. (2) Why it matters: this is intentional for the offline case (the files must already be cached, and huggingface-hub v1.22 requires ignore_patterns to avoid attempting downloads of missing files), but if the local cache is incomplete for the requested revision, the returned directory may be empty/partial and downstream model/tokenizer loading will fail with a confusing error rather than a clear 'files not cached' message. (3) Fix: confirm this matches the intended huggingface-hub v1.22 offline behavior and add a comment explaining that `ignore_patterns="*"` is required so snapshot_download only resolves the already-cached snapshot dir without attempting any download; consider validating that expected files exist in the returned path.

(1) The diff rewrites the `common_kwargs` dict literal `{"local_files_only": ..., "revision": ...}` into `dict(local_files_only=..., ignore_patterns="*", revision=...)`. (2) Why it matters: switching from a dict literal to the `dict()` constructor is a stylistic change unrelated to the functional addition of `ignore_patterns`; the only substantive change is the new key. Mixing the style change with the functional change slightly obscures the diff. (3) Fix: prefer keeping the dict-literal form and simply adding the `"ignore_patterns": "*"` key to minimize the diff and keep intent clear. Minor / non-blocking.

(1) An unrelated `max_num_seqs=16` argument is added to the `test_chatglm3_lora_tp4_fully_sharded_loras` LLM constructor in a PR whose stated purpose is bumping `huggingface-hub`. (2) Why it matters: this is a behavioral test change bundled into a dependency-bump PR, making it easy to overlook; it likely works around a default `max_num_seqs` change or memory constraint but is undocumented here. (3) Fix: confirm this is intentional and add a brief comment on why `max_num_seqs=16` is needed for this test, or split it into a separate change.

(1) The condition in `get_encode_kwargs` changed from `if self.truncation_side == "left"` to `if self.truncation_side is not None and self.truncate_prompt_tokens is not None`, and now unconditionally disables tokenizer-level truncation whenever a caller sets `truncation_side` (either 'left' OR 'right') together with `truncate_prompt_tokens`. Previously, right-side truncation was delegated to the fast/HF tokenizer via `truncation=True, max_length=...`. (2) Risk: for pooling models the tokenizer default is 'right' (see registry.py runner_type=='pooling'), and pooling requests already pass `self.truncation_side` (set explicitly or None). When a pooling caller now explicitly passes `truncation_side='right'` + `truncate_prompt_tokens`, tokenizer truncation is disabled and the full sequence must be tokenized then sliced by `_token_truncation`. This is a semantic change for the previously fast HF-truncation path (right side). The change appears intentional but shifts the truncation work from the tokenizer to Python-level slicing, and relies on `_token_len_check` and `_token_truncation` handling all sides correctly. Verify pooling models that historically relied on HF right-truncation still behave identically. (3) Fix: add/verify test coverage for pooling + right truncation, and confirm this behavior change is intended and documented in the changelog.

(1) When `truncation_side` and `truncate_prompt_tokens` are both set, `get_encode_kwargs` now returns `truncation=False` with no `max_length`, so the tokenizer must encode the ENTIRE input string/token sequence before Python-side `_token_truncation` slices it down. Previously right-side truncation used the tokenizer's `max_length` to stop early. (2) Risk: for very long prompts with a small `truncate_prompt_tokens`, this removes the tokenizer's ability to cap work at `max_length`, causing the full (potentially huge) prompt to be tokenized. Note the text-length pre-check `_text_len_check` is bypassed when `truncate_prompt_tokens is not None` (it only fires when truncate_prompt_tokens is None), so there is no early character-count guard either. This can increase tokenization latency/memory for large truncated prompts. (3) Fix: consider passing `max_length` bounded by max_input_tokens for the right-truncation case where correctness allows it, or document that explicit truncation_side implies full tokenization cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Tokenize a very long prompt (e.g. 200k chars) with truncate_prompt_tokens=8 and truncation_side='right' vs the parent behavior; compare tokenization wall-time.

findings.claim: (1) The stale comment cleanup is good, but the new comment says 'generation tokenizers default to left truncation while callers may request right truncation' — this only partly explains why BOTH sides now disable tokenizer truncation. For truncation_side='right' on a generation model (tokenizer default 'left'), disabling tokenizer truncation is necessary; but for truncation_side='right' on a pooling model (tokenizer default 'right') it is not strictly necessary and adds cost. (2) A partially-accurate comment can mislead future maintainers about when the full-tokenization path is required. (3) Fix: clarify the comment to state the rule precisely: whenever the caller explicitly specifies a side, defer to `_token_truncation` so the requested side is honored regardless of the tokenizer's own default.

(1) The new `truncation_side` validation in `__post_init__` raises `VLLMValidationError` if the value is not in (None, 'left', 'right'). However, the field is already typed `Literal["left", "right"] | None` in both the request protocols and TokenizeParams, and Pydantic enforces this at the API boundary, so the runtime check is largely redundant for API-originated requests. It is only reachable via programmatic construction or `with_kwargs` overrides. (2) Low risk — this is defensive and harmless, just slightly redundant. (3) Fix: keep it (defensive is fine for the `with_kwargs` path where kwargs are untyped), but consider noting it primarily guards the untyped `tokenization_kwargs` override path.

(1) The LTS Python deps in docker/lts/requirements.txt are installed via `uv pip install -r ...` WITHOUT the `--no-install-package torch/triton/nvidia-*` guards and WITHOUT `--locked`, unlike the main `uv sync` block above it which carefully excludes torch, triton, transformer-engine, and all the nvidia-cu12 wheels so the base NGC image's pre-installed CUDA/torch stack is preserved. Several pinned packages (flashinfer-python, datasets, megatron-energon, nvidia-resiliency-ext) transitively depend on torch and/or nvidia CUDA wheels. (2) Impact: `uv pip install` can pull PyPI torch/CUDA wheels on top of the NGC base image, silently replacing the tuned NGC torch build with a generic PyPI build (or a mismatched CUDA runtime), degrading training performance and potentially breaking TE/DeepEP ABI compatibility. This is exactly the failure mode the elaborate `--no-install-package` list in the block above is designed to prevent. (3) Fix: either add the same `--no-install-package` guards to the requirements install, or ensure requirements.txt has no transitive torch/CUDA pulls and pin/verify with `uv pip install --no-deps` where appropriate, plus install from a locked/compiled set for reproducibility.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Build docker/Dockerfile.ci.lts against nvcr.io/nvidia/pytorch:25.09-py3 and run `pip list | grep -E 'torch|nvidia-'` before/after the requirements.txt step; verify torch version/build hash and CUDA wheel set are unchanged from the base image.

findings.claim: (1) `emerging_optimizers @ git+https://github.com/NVIDIA-NeMo/Emerging-Optimizers.git@v0.2.0` is installed via `uv pip install -r requirements.txt` which is NOT `--locked` and pulls a git ref at build time, whereas the previous flow resolved it through the pinned `uv.lock`. The requirements.txt file also lacks a trailing newline (noted `\ No newline at end of file`). (2) Impact: LTS image builds are no longer bit-reproducible — the git tag could be re-pointed, and unlike the dev path (which uses `uv sync --locked`), there is no lockfile pinning transitive dependency hashes for the requirements.txt set. This undermines the stated purpose of the LTS lane (stability pinning). (3) Fix: pin exact commit SHAs for git deps and/or generate the requirements.txt via `uv pip compile` with hashes as the header comment suggests, and install with `--require-hashes` for reproducibility.

(1) The new LTS Dockerfile drops the `--extra ${IMAGE_TYPE}` argument entirely and instead installs LTS deps from requirements.txt. Worth confirming that the GitLab matrix change (removing `IMAGE_TYPE: lts`) and the GitHub workflow (which still exports `IMAGE_TYPE=...` at cicd-main.yml:619 as a build-arg) are consistent: `.gitlab/scripts/build.sh` never passed IMAGE_TYPE as a docker build-arg, so the LTS matrix rows were previously building with the dev extra (ARG IMAGE_TYPE=dev default) — this PR silently changes the effective LTS dependency set. (2) Impact: behavior change for LTS CI that may be intended but is not called out; existing LTS golden values / test expectations were validated against the old (accidentally dev) dependency set. (3) Fix: confirm the LTS CI lane is re-validated and that the residual IMAGE_TYPE build-arg export in cicd-main.yml is harmless (no ARG IMAGE_TYPE consumer remains in Dockerfile.ci.lts).

(1) In the second loop of `_get_reqs_to_store`, `num_group_blocks += block_size_factor` counts `block_size_factor` GPU sub-blocks for every stored offloaded block, but the inner loop only appends `block_id` to `src_block_ids` when `block_id != 0` (skipped blocks are dropped). When a stored offloaded block has leading skipped sub-blocks (block_id==0) — precisely the sliding-window/SSM partial-block case the code is trying to handle via `start_gpu_block_idx` — `num_group_blocks` over-counts relative to the number of appended block IDs. (2) This violates the `GPULoadStoreSpec` invariant `assert sum(group_sizes) == len(block_ids)` (in vllm/v1/kv_offload/mediums.py), which will raise at construction of `src_spec`, hard-crashing the scheduler whenever the first stored offloaded block straddles the sliding-window skip boundary with block_size_factor>1. (3) `group_sizes` should count only the GPU blocks actually appended to `src_block_ids` (i.e. increment per non-zero block, or subtract the number of skipped leading sub-blocks) so the sum equals `len(src_block_ids)`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test: single request with sliding-window attention, block_size_factor=3, whose first offloadable block has GPU sub-blocks [0,0,X]; assert _get_reqs_to_store constructs a valid GPULoadStoreSpec (no assertion error) and group_sizes sums to len(src_block_ids).

findings.claim: (1) `block_indices.append(start_gpu_block_idx or 0)` uses `or` truthiness: when the first non-skipped GPU block genuinely has index 0 (`start_gpu_block_idx == 0`, i.e. no skipping and the group starts at absolute block 0), `0 or 0` still yields 0, which happens to be correct here; however if a legitimately-computed `start_gpu_block_idx` were 0 it is indistinguishable from the 'no blocks stored' sentinel. More importantly, when a group contributes zero stored blocks (`num_group_blocks==0`) but a later group does, this group's `block_indices` entry is silently 0 while `group_sizes` is 0 — verify the worker interprets a zero-size group's block_index correctly. (2) Ambiguous sentinel handling risks off-by-partial-block reads on the first offloaded block. (3) Use an explicit `start_gpu_block_idx if start_gpu_block_idx is not None else 0` and document the zero-size-group contract.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Test with multiple KV groups where one group has no new blocks to store while another does; assert block_indices/group_sizes alignment matches worker expectations.

findings.claim: (1) `self.manager.touch(group_state.offload_keys)` is now called on the FULL offload_keys list for every group on each store step, whereas the previous code touched only `group_state.offload_keys[:num_blocks]` (the prefix up to the current block). (2) As requests grow long, offload_keys grows unboundedly and touch() is invoked over the entire history every scheduler step, turning a bounded-prefix operation into an O(total_blocks) per-step cost (LRU manager typically re-orders each key), degrading scheduler throughput for long sequences. (3) Restore touching only the relevant prefix, e.g. `group_state.offload_keys[:num_blocks]`, per group.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark _get_reqs_to_store latency vs. sequence length (num offloaded blocks) comparing full-list touch vs prefix touch.

findings.claim: (1) `req = req_status.req` is now fetched at the top of the loop before the preempted/new-block handling; it is only used to compute `num_tokens_after_batch`. This is fine, but the earlier removed assertion `assert len(req.block_hashes) >= num_gpu_blocks` was dropped without replacement. (2) That assertion guarded against reading `group_state.block_ids` / offload_keys beyond what the request has hashed; without it, an out-of-range slice would silently produce fewer blocks rather than failing loudly, making mis-indexing bugs (like the group_sizes mismatch) harder to detect. (3) Consider re-adding a bounds assertion tying num_blocks*block_size_factor to available block_ids/block_hashes.

(1) `advance_stored_idx` recomputes `num_blocks = num_offloadable_tokens // group_config.offloaded_block_size` per group — the identical computation is duplicated in three places inside `_get_reqs_to_store` (both loops) and in advance_stored_idx. (2) Duplication invites the loops and the fast-path advance to drift out of sync (e.g. if one computation changes rounding), causing next_stored_block_idx inconsistencies across the store/skip paths. (3) Extract a single helper returning per-group num_blocks and reuse it in all sites.

(1) The load() default async_strategy for checkpoints was changed from 'mcore' to 'nvrx'. Previously the code read the stored ckpt args and only used 'nvrx' if the checkpoint was saved with async_save and an explicit async_strategy; otherwise it defaulted to 'mcore'. Now `getattr(common_state_dict.get('args'), 'async_strategy', 'nvrx')` returns 'nvrx' for any checkpoint that lacks a stored `async_strategy` attribute (e.g. all existing/older checkpoints and checkpoints saved with async_save=False). (2) This silently switches the *load* strategy for legacy checkpoints to 'nvrx', which requires nvidia-resiliency-ext to be installed; on installations without NVRx this can raise ModuleNotFoundError at load time (see get_async_strategy error path), breaking loading of pre-existing checkpoints. It is a backward-compatibility regression for the common load path. (3) Preserve the previous fallback semantics: default to 'mcore' when the checkpoint was not saved with the nvrx async strategy, or gate the 'nvrx' default on HAVE_NVRX.

(1) get_save_and_finalize_callbacks now constructs `NVRxAsyncRequest(...)` directly, and NVRxAsyncRequest is set to `ABC` (abstract base class) when nvidia-resiliency-ext is not importable. (2) If this function is ever reached without NVRx installed, `ABC(save_fn, ...)` will fail with a TypeError instead of the previous behavior which routed through get_async_strategy() and would either use the mcore AsyncRequest or raise a clear ModuleNotFoundError with an actionable message. The prior signature also accepted an `async_strategy` argument allowing the mcore path; that path is now removed entirely, so torch_dcp/fsdp_dtensor async save is hard-wired to NVRx only. (3) Confirm this is intended (async save for these formats now requires NVRx) and, if so, ensure the config validation in CheckpointConfig.__post_init__ guarantees NVRx is installed before this path is reachable; otherwise restore a graceful error.

(1) The removed check for `get_write_results_queue(mp_mode=...)` support was dropped and the call was changed from `get_write_results_queue(mp_mode='fork')` to positional `get_write_results_queue('fork')`. (2) Older nvidia-resiliency-ext versions that do not accept an mp_mode parameter will now fail with a less clear TypeError (positional arg not accepted) instead of the previous explicit AssertionError telling the user to update the package. This is a minor UX/compat regression. (3) Keep an explicit capability check or keep the keyword form with a clear error message for unsupported NVRx versions.

(1) The sync save() now selects `strategy = 'nvrx' if HAVE_NVRX else 'mcore'`, and HAVE_NVRX is now determined solely by whether the top-level try-import of NVRxAsyncRequest/CheckpointMetadataCache succeeds, replacing the previous richer has_nvrx_async_support() symbol-completeness check. (2) An installed-but-incomplete/old NVRx package could set HAVE_NVRX=True while missing some symbols used later (e.g. get_write_results_queue, set_cached_global_metadata), causing failures deeper in the save path rather than a clean fallback to mcore. The removal of the `hasattr(..., 'set_cached_global_metadata')` guard (now unconditional call) compounds this. (3) Consider retaining a capability check for the specific NVRx symbols Megatron uses before setting HAVE_NVRX=True.

(1) CheckpointConfig.__post_init__ no longer forces async_strategy='mcore' when async_save is False (the `if not self.async_save: self.async_strategy = 'mcore'` block was removed here and in arguments.py). (2) With the default async_strategy now 'nvrx', configs/scripts relying on the previous implicit normalization (async_save off => strategy mcore) will now retain 'nvrx'. Combined with the serialization.py load default change, this alters which strategy is recorded/used and may surface NVRx requirements where users previously had none. (3) Verify the removed normalization is intentional and that no downstream code branches on async_strategy=='mcore' assuming it is set whenever async_save is False.

(1) The metadata JSON now echoes the raw, un-sanitized probe name directly into the file: `meta_file << "\"name\": \"" << name << "\", ";`. Unlike the filename (which is sanitized via SanitizeProbeName), the JSON value is not escaped, so a name containing a double-quote, backslash, or control character produces malformed JSON. (2) Any downstream analysis tool that parses `*_meta.json` will fail to load the dump for such names, and the PR's stated purpose is precisely to let tools recover the semantic label from JSON. (3) Escape at least `"` and `\` (and ideally control chars) when writing the name into the JSON string, e.g. a small JSON-escape helper applied to `name` before insertion.

(1) `shardy_sharding_rule` returns the literal `"..., , , , -> ..."`. This encodes 5 operands (x plus four rank-0 scalar stats) mapping to one output. Verify the empty factor lists `, , , ,` are the accepted Shardy syntax for rank-0 (scalar) operands in the installed JAX version; an off-by-one in operand count or an unaccepted empty-spec token would raise at partition time rather than silently mis-shard. (2) If wrong, sharded/multi-device inspect_array calls fail to compile. (3) Add a multi-device (mesh) unit test exercising the partition path; the added test only runs on gpu0 single-device and does not cover partition()/shardy_sharding_rule().

(1) The test only validates the single-device path (`my_tensor_gpu0_my_array.bin`) and never exercises the newly added `partition` and `shardy_sharding_rule` methods under a device mesh. (2) The core new functionality (name attribute flowing through custom_partitioning and correct identity sharding) is therefore untested; a regression in sharded execution would not be caught. (3) Add a sharded test with a jax mesh and PartitionSpec on x to cover the partition path.

The file `vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/cutlass_moe.py` is moved to `vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/experts/cutlass_moe.py`, but the buildkite CI trigger lists still reference the old path and are NOT updated in this PR. Specifically `.buildkite/test_areas/kernels.yaml` ("Kernels (B200)" step, `source_file_dependencies: vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/cutlass_moe.py`) and `.buildkite/test-amd.yaml:2957` still point to the now-nonexistent path. Impact: after the move, edits to `experts/cutlass_moe.py` will no longer trigger the B200 kernel test step (which runs nvfp4/cutlass MoE tests), creating a silent CI-coverage gap for the CUTLASS FP4/FP8 MoE kernels on B200. Fix: update those two `source_file_dependencies` entries to `.../experts/cutlass_moe.py`. Note the broader dir-level dependency `vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/` in the "Kernels MoE Test" and "FusedMoE Layer Test" steps still covers the new path, so only the file-specific B200/AMD triggers are affected.

(1) For method=="medusa", draft_hf_overrides is hardcoded to {"model_type": "medusa"}, which completely replaces the previously-passed SpeculativeConfig.hf_config_override callable AND ignores any user-supplied hf_overrides. (2) This means a user who legitimately wants to override draft-model config fields (e.g. via a custom hf_overrides callable/dict) for a Medusa draft will silently have those overrides dropped, and any medusa checkpoint that already declares a different model_type will be forced to 'medusa'. (3) Merge the injected model_type into the existing override rather than replacing it, e.g. start from the user/hf_config_override value and only add {'model_type': 'medusa'} when the checkpoint lacks one; or document that medusa deliberately ignores hf_config_override.

(1) The vocab_size alignment mutates self.draft_model_config.hf_config.vocab_size and truncated_vocab_size in place after ModelConfig construction, unconditionally setting truncated_vocab_size = target_vocab whenever vocab_size differed. (2) If a Medusa checkpoint legitimately uses a truncated vocab (token_map) with truncated_vocab_size < vocab_size (the documented Medusa speed-up path), this code overwrites truncated_vocab_size to the full target vocab, disabling/breaking the token_map path and could cause the load_weights assertion (truncated_vocab_size==orig_vocab_size or token_map is not None) or the token_map slicing to misbehave. (3) Only override vocab_size/truncated_vocab_size when the checkpoint used the default fallback (32001) and did not explicitly specify a truncated vocab; guard the truncated_vocab_size overwrite on the absence of a real truncated vocab.

(1) _remap_old_checkpoint_key discards parts[1] (the numeric layer index) in the non-linear (lm_head) branch: for '{head}.{N}.weight' it returns 'lm_heads.{head}.weight' regardless of N. (2) If old checkpoints ever have more than one non-linear submodule index per head (unlikely but not asserted), two distinct weights would collapse to the same target name and one would silently overwrite the other during load. (3) Add an assertion or handle the index explicitly, and add a shape/name-collision check in load_weights so silent overwrites surface as errors.

(1) The regression guard uses a hardcoded min_acceptance_rate=0.198 described as '90% of the measured baseline', implying a measured baseline ~0.22. (2) A near-zero absolute floor is a weak regression guard: a real regression that halves acceptance from ~0.5 to ~0.25 would still pass, so it only catches complete breakage of the speculation path, not throughput regressions. (3) Consider asserting closer to the observed baseline (e.g. within a tolerance band) to make it an effective performance regression detector.

(1) The default argument `timeout: float = envs.VLLM_ENGINE_READY_TIMEOUT_S` is evaluated once, at function-definition (module import) time, not per call. Python binds default argument values a single time when the `def` statement executes, so `envs.VLLM_ENGINE_READY_TIMEOUT_S` is resolved via the lazy `envs.__getattr__` exactly once at import and frozen. (2) Impact: if `VLLM_ENGINE_READY_TIMEOUT_S` is set/changed programmatically (e.g. via `os.environ[...]` or `monkeypatch.setenv`) *after* `vllm.v1.utils` is imported, the new value silently has no effect on this timeout, unlike every other consumer of the env var (core_client.py reads it at call time). This is a subtle behavioral inconsistency that can make the configured timeout appear ignored. (3) Fix: use a sentinel default (`timeout: float | None = None`) and resolve inside the body: `if timeout is None: timeout = envs.VLLM_ENGINE_READY_TIMEOUT_S`. This defers the env read to call time and matches the pattern used elsewhere.

(1) The PR's stated goal is to remove git-based packages (e.g. fastsafetensors installed from GitHub) because `uv pip` cannot handle `git+` URLs, and it removes fastsafetensors from requirements/rocm.txt. However requirements/test/rocm.txt (and its source requirements/test/rocm.in) STILL contain `fastsafetensors @ git+https://github.com/foundation-model-stack/fastsafetensors.git@...`. (2) The `test` stage of Dockerfile.rocm installs this file via `uv pip install --system -r requirements/test/rocm.txt`, so the exact `uv pip` incompatibility the PR is meant to fix will still occur when building the test image. The fix is incomplete for the CI/test path. (3) Also remove/replace the git+ fastsafetensors reference in requirements/test/rocm.in and recompile requirements/test/rocm.txt, or pin to a PyPI release.

(1) The new git+ guard only greps common.txt and rocm.txt, and is placed in the `build_vllm_wheel_release` stage which installs requirements via `python3 -m pip install` (which the comment itself admits CAN handle git+ URLs). The stages that actually use `uv pip install` (`test` and `final`) do not run this check, and the `test` stage additionally installs requirements/test/rocm.txt which is not covered by the grep. (2) This means the guard does not actually protect the code paths where the uv incompatibility manifests, giving a false sense of safety while the real failure path (uv installing test/rocm.txt containing git+) is unguarded. (3) Move the check earlier (a stage shared by uv-based installs) and extend it to grep requirements/test/rocm.txt (and any other requirements files consumed by uv pip).

(1) The file ends without a trailing newline (`\ No newline at end of file`) after `amd-quark>=0.8.99`. (2) Missing trailing newlines can cause issues when files are concatenated or appended to programmatically (e.g. pin_rocm_dependencies.py appends/modifies rocm.txt), and some tools warn on it. (3) Add a trailing newline to the file.

(1) After this change, update_dflash always populates dflash_config["causal"] (defaulting to False when sliding_window_non_causal is absent/True), whereas previously the key was absent. (2) In qwen3_dflash._resolve_layer_attention, config_causal = dflash_config.get("causal", None) is treated as an all-layer override: 'If dflash_config.causal is set, its value overrides causal for all layers.' Previously, with the key absent, config_causal was None and the per-layer default_causal (True for SWA layers when layer_types has sliding entries) applied. Now config_causal is always a concrete bool, so the per-layer default_causal path is unconditionally overridden for any config passing through update_dflash. This is behavior-preserving only when the effective per-layer default already equals the new value. (3) Consider only setting the key when config_dict actually contains 'sliding_window_non_causal' (e.g. `if 'sliding_window_non_causal' in config_dict:`), so speculators configs that don't specify it retain the prior per-layer default_causal resolution instead of being forced to causal=False.

(1) The new guard `if not self.symmetric or self.wna16_backend == WNA16MoEBackend.CPU:` unconditionally calls `replace_parameter(layer, "w13_weight_zero_point", w13_qzeros)` (and the w2 variant) for the CPU backend even when the quant is symmetric, but the previous code guarded each call with `if w13_qzeros is not None`. For symmetric quant on the CPU backend, `create_weights` only registers `w13_weight_zero_point`/`w2_weight_zero_point` inside `if not self.symmetric:`, so those parameters do NOT exist; `convert_to_wna16_moe_kernel_format` is then called with `w13_qzeros=getattr(layer, 'w13_weight_zero_point', None)=None`. If the CPU processing path returns `w13_qzeros=None`, `replace_parameter(layer, 'w13_weight_zero_point', None)` will `setattr(layer, 'w13_weight_zero_point', None)` — creating a `None` attribute that later `getattr(layer, 'w13_weight_zero_point', None)` in `get_fused_moe_quant_config` returns as None, which may be the intended behavior, but if CPU int4 experts require real zero points for symmetric quant (per the comment 'CPU fused_experts_cpu requires zero points even for symmetric quant'), then this branch silently passes None and the requirement is NOT satisfied. (2) Risk: symmetric-quant CPU MoE either crashes on a missing/None zero-point parameter or produces wrong results, undermining the stated fix. (3) Verify that convert_to_wna16_moe_kernel_format actually materializes non-None w13_qzeros/w2_qzeros for the CPU+symmetric case (e.g. synthesizes default zeros), and if not, either generate zero-point tensors here or register them in create_weights for CPU symmetric.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a symmetric GPTQ/compressed-tensors WNA16 MoE checkpoint on CPU backend and run a forward pass; verify no None-attribute error and numerically-correct output vs asymmetric baseline.

findings.claim: (1) The guard was changed from a per-tensor `if w13_qzeros is not None` / `if w2_qzeros is not None` to a single combined condition based on `self.symmetric`/backend that replaces both parameters together. This drops the defensive None-check: if for any backend/config combination `w13_qzeros` is None while the guard evaluates True (asymmetric or CPU), `replace_parameter` will set the attribute to None rather than skipping it, which is a behavior change vs the prior code that skipped None tensors entirely. (2) Impact: differing attribute state (None vs absent) can affect downstream `getattr(..., None)` fallbacks and any code that checks `hasattr`. (3) Consider retaining an inner `if w13_qzeros is not None` check within the branch to preserve the previous skip-on-None semantics unless setting to None is explicitly desired.

(1) The output processor only materializes `output.routed_experts` when the request is finished: `if finished and self.routed_experts_chunks: routed_experts = np.concatenate(...)`. So in the newly-added streaming path in serving.py, `output.routed_experts` is None on every intermediate DELTA chunk and only populated (with the FULL concatenated array) on the final chunk. (2) Impact: the streaming client will receive `routed_experts=None` on all but the last chunk, and then a single non-delta full array on the final chunk — inconsistent with the delta semantics of every other field (`token_ids`, `logprobs`) in the same chunk, which are per-delta. A client concatenating per-chunk routed_experts (mirroring how it concatenates token_ids) would end up with the wrong shape / duplicated data. (3) Fix: either (a) make the output processor emit per-delta routed_experts slices during streaming so each chunk carries only its incremental routing rows, or (b) explicitly document in `GenerateResponseStreamChoice.routed_experts` that it is non-None only on the final chunk and carries the complete array, and confirm the streaming consumer handles that. Verify against the intended contract before merging.

(1) The existing test `test_generate_routed_experts` only exercises the non-streaming path (`"stream": False`); the new streaming branch (`stream=True`, `RequestOutputKind.DELTA`) added in serving.py has no test coverage. (2) Impact: the per-chunk delta semantics of `routed_experts` in streaming mode (whether chunks carry deltas or a single final full array, and whether concatenation reconstructs the correct array) are unverified, so a regression or a shape/ordering mismatch would ship silently. (3) Fix: add a streaming test that sets `stream=True`, collects `routed_experts` across chunks, decodes/concatenates them, and asserts the reconstructed array matches the non-streaming result (same shape `(num_tokens-1, num_layers, topk)` and values).

(1) `GenerateResponseChoice.routed_experts` carries a detailed docstring describing encoding, decode recipe, and shape, but the newly added `GenerateResponseStreamChoice.routed_experts` field has no docstring at all. (2) Impact: for streaming the semantics differ (per-chunk vs whole-response), and an undocumented field on the streaming choice leaves consumers to guess whether to accumulate/concatenate across chunks — increasing the chance of incorrect client-side reconstruction. (3) Fix: add a docstring on `GenerateResponseStreamChoice.routed_experts` clarifying it is base64-encoded `.npy` bytes and precisely stating the streaming contract (which chunks it appears on and how to combine chunks).

(1) `get_mrope_input_positions` now recomputes `mm_token_type_ids` with a Python for-loop over `mm_features`, doing per-feature scalar slice-assignment (`mm_token_type_ids[offset:offset+length] = id`) on a CPU tensor on every request. (2) This is per-request host-side work; for requests with many mm items it launches many small tensor slice-assign ops instead of the previous single `torch.cat`/`torch.zeros` path. (3) Since offsets/lengths are Python ints, prefer building the type-id vector with numpy/list assembly then a single `torch.tensor(...)` conversion, or precompute where possible. Impact is modest (CPU-side, preprocessing), so this is a should-fix rather than critical.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark get_mrope_input_positions with a request containing many (e.g. 50+) image features and compare wall time vs the previous torch.cat implementation.

findings.claim: (1) The new loop does `_MODALITY_TO_TOKEN_TYPE_ID[feature.modality]` for every feature in `mm_features`. `_MODALITY_TO_TOKEN_TYPE_ID` only contains {image, video, audio}; any other modality (or a modality string variant) will raise an unguarded `KeyError`. (2) The earlier guard only raises the friendly `NotImplementedError("...only supports images")` when the *kwargs* dict has a truthy non-image value; it does not guard against `mm_features` containing e.g. a video/audio feature whose grid kwarg is empty, nor unknown modalities. So a supported-looking request could now crash with a raw KeyError instead of the intended NotImplementedError. (3) Use `.get(feature.modality)` and raise NotImplementedError for unknown/unsupported modalities, keeping the error consistent with the existing image-only contract.

(1) `mm_token_type_ids` is now built purely from `mm_features` mm_position/modality and no longer read from the processor output, whereas the previous code fell back to `torch.zeros` only when no ids were present. (2) This changes the source of truth for token-type ids from the HF processor's `mm_token_type_ids` to vLLM-side placeholder ranges; if placeholder ranges do not exactly cover the same positions the HF processor marked (e.g. models where image tokens include non-embed padding positions), the resulting rope index could differ from before. (3) Confirm that for all supported models the placeholder range [offset, offset+length) exactly matches the positions the HF processor would mark with the same type id; add a shape/coverage assertion if feasible.

(1) In `MultiModalProcessor.apply`, the token-type-id extraction was rewritten from `.get("mm_token_type_ids", .pop("token_type_ids"))` to two sequential `.pop` calls. (2) The old code used `.get` for `mm_token_type_ids` (leaving it in `processed_data`) but the new code `.pop`s it, so `mm_token_type_ids` is now removed from `processed_data` before it flows into `_get_mm_fields_config`/`MultiModalKwargsItems`. Given the PR removes downstream consumers of `mm_token_type_ids`, this is likely intentional, but the behavioral difference (key no longer present in mm_kwargs) should be confirmed not to break `_get_mm_fields_config` which iterates over all keys. (3) Verify no downstream code still expects `mm_token_type_ids` in `processed_data`/mm_kwargs.

(1) This PR renames cohere_moe.py to cohere2_moe.py (new file added, registry/router imports updated to point at cohere2_moe), but the old vllm/model_executor/models/cohere_moe.py is NOT removed and is not in the changed-files list, so two nearly-identical modules now coexist, each defining its own `token_choice_with_bias` and `CohereMoe*`/`Cohere2Moe*` classes. (2) Dead duplicate code diverges over time (e.g. the new force_rope/first_k_dense_replace/RMSNorm logic exists only in cohere2_moe.py), causing maintenance drift and confusion; the identity comparison `self.custom_routing_function == token_choice_with_bias` in custom_routing_router.py now only matches the cohere2_moe symbol, so if anything still instantiates the old cohere_moe module its routing would silently fall through to RoutingMethodType.Custom instead of SigmoidRenorm. (3) Delete vllm/model_executor/models/cohere_moe.py (and confirm no remaining references) as part of this rename.

(1) The router's routing_method_type property compares `self.custom_routing_function == token_choice_with_bias` (line 44) against the symbol imported at line 37, which now resolves to cohere2_moe.token_choice_with_bias. (2) `token_choice_with_bias` is decorated with `@torch.compile(...)`, so equality is by the compiled-wrapper object identity; if the FusedMoE for a Cohere2Moe model is ever wired with the function object from a different import path (e.g. the still-present cohere_moe module, or a re-import), the `==` check fails and routing silently degrades from RoutingMethodType.SigmoidRenorm to Custom, changing the fused-kernel path selection and potentially numerical results/perf. (3) Ensure there is exactly one canonical definition (remove cohere_moe.py) and consider matching by function name/attribute rather than object identity to be robust to import-path aliasing.

(1) In Cohere2MoeDecoderLayer.forward the first statement `residual = hidden_states` unconditionally overwrites the incoming `residual` argument before it is ever used, and the function then returns this local `residual` (== the layer input) rather than an accumulated residual. (2) This makes the `residual` parameter effectively dead on input and the returned `residual` just equals the input hidden_states; while functionally the model uses the parallel (attention+mlp) residual add correctly, the residual plumbing between layers/PP-stages is misleading — a reader may assume residual carries state across layers when it does not. Verify this matches the intended Cohere parallel-block architecture (it mirrors the existing commandr design, so likely intentional). (3) If intentional, drop the `residual` input parameter or add a comment clarifying it is unused-on-input; otherwise fix the residual accumulation.

(1) New `force_rope` logic: RoPE is applied when `self.sliding_window or self.force_rope`, where force_rope is true for prefix-dense layers (layer_idx < first_k_dense_replace) only when prefix_dense_sliding_window_pattern == 1. This means non-sliding, non-prefix-dense global-attention layers with layer_idx >= first_k_dense_replace do NOT get RoPE applied. (2) If the Cohere2 MoE architecture expects RoPE on all sliding+the prefix-dense layers but NoPE (no positional embedding) on the remaining global layers, this is correct; but the condition is easy to get wrong and there is no shape/behavior assertion or test coverage shown. A wrong RoPE-application predicate silently produces incorrect attention scores. (3) Add a unit/generation test asserting the per-layer RoPE application matches the reference HF implementation for a config with first_k_dense_replace>0 and mixed layer_types.

(1) `Cohere2MoeMLP.reduce_results` defaults to False, but the RowParallelLinear down_proj it wraps then does not reduce across TP ranks by default; the shared-experts path relies on FusedMoE performing the final reduction, while the dense-MLP path explicitly passes reduce_results=True. (2) This split behavior is correct but subtle — a future caller instantiating Cohere2MoeMLP standalone (not as shared expert, not passing reduce_results=True) would get partial (un-reduced) TP outputs and silently wrong results across TP ranks. (3) Add an assertion or docstring note that reduce_results=False is only valid when the reduction is handled by an enclosing FusedMoE/parallel block.

(1) In the non-streaming path, num_groups is now set to n_prompts instead of args.rl_generation_batch_size. Inside GroupedRolloutGenerator.get_grouped_rollouts, num_groups is interpreted as groups_per_worker, and there is an assertion `if groups_per_worker > 1: assert not request.filter_groups_with_same_reward`. Since n_prompts is typically > 1, this makes the combination of non-partial (non-streaming) rollouts with grpo_filter_groups_with_same_reward=True now raise an AssertionError, whereas previously (with rl_generation_batch_size potentially == 1) it could work. (2) This silently breaks a previously-valid configuration (GRPO reward-std filtering + non-partial rollouts) at runtime. (3) Either decouple the 'total groups to produce' bound from 'groups_per_worker' (they are now conflated into num_groups) so the filter path can still run with groups_per_worker==1, or explicitly document/guard the incompatibility with a clear error.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test: run get_grouped_rollouts with streaming=False, num_groups>1, filter_groups_with_same_reward=True and confirm intended behavior (currently asserts).

findings.claim: (1) request.num_groups is now overloaded with two conflicting meanings: `groups_per_worker = request.num_groups` (line 219, per-worker batch size) AND the total-groups termination bound `submitted_groups < request.num_groups` (line 250). In the non-streaming path this forces the entire batch to be generated by a single worker in one asyncio.gather (num_workers = pgt // num_groups workers, but the first worker submits all num_groups groups and the loop then terminates for all others), so parallel_generation_tasks no longer controls concurrency granularity as before. (2) This is a subtle behavior change: it changes how work is distributed and how backpressure/scheduling behaves for non-streaming rollouts, and the dual meaning of num_groups is error-prone for future maintainers. (3) Consider introducing a separate field for total-groups-to-produce vs groups_per_worker, or add a comment at line 250 documenting that in non-streaming mode num_groups == total groups == groups_per_worker on purpose.

(1) With num_workers > 1 (non-streaming, e.g. num_groups=4, parallel_generation_tasks=8 => 2 workers), multiple generate_task coroutines share the `submitted_groups < request.num_groups` check and the `submitted_groups += groups_per_worker` increment. This is currently safe only because there is no `await` between the check and the increment (single-threaded asyncio). (2) If future edits insert an await between the loop condition and the increment, workers could over-submit past num_groups. (3) Add a brief comment noting the check-then-increment must remain await-free, or capture/reserve the slot atomically before any await.

(1) The helper `_import_nixl_ep_cpp` is now dead code: its only call site (previously at line 69, `nixl_ep_cpp = _import_nixl_ep_cpp(nixl_ep)`) is removed by this diff, and the test now simply checks `nixl_ep.Config is not None`. (2) Leaving the ~30-line helper defined but unreferenced adds maintenance noise and can mislead future readers into thinking the cpp-extension resolution path is still exercised. (3) Remove the unused `_import_nixl_ep_cpp` function (and its now-unused `types` import if nothing else uses it) since the ldd/subprocess-based extension verification it supported was also deleted.

(1) After deleting vllm/model_executor/models/aya_vision.py, the header comment in cohere2_vision.py still reads '# Adapted from vllm/model_executor/models/aya_vision.py', which now points at a file that no longer exists. (2) This is a stale/misleading reference; a wrong comment is worse than none since a future reader will chase a non-existent source. It is comment-only, so no functional impact. (3) Update the comment to point at the upstream HF source (e.g. the transformers aya_vision path that the deleted file itself was adapted from) or drop the reference.

(1) model_arch_config_convertor.py is in the changed set for this removal PR; verify the diff to it is only a comment tweak ('For Command-R / Cohere, Cohere2 / Aya Vision models' on derive_max_model_len_and_key) and not a behavioral change, since AyaVisionConfig relies on model_max_length via that fallback and Cohere2/CommandR still need it. (2) If the model_max_length fallback logic itself were altered while removing the Aya Vision mention, it could silently change derived max_model_len for the still-supported Cohere2/Command-R/Command-A-Vision models. (3) Confirm only the comment string changed and the model_max_length branch is untouched.

(1) The parent test file defines 'aya_vision' and 'aya_vision-multi_image' VLMTestInfo entries; ensure the PR removes both (and any auto_cls / prompt_formatter refs) so no test parametrization references the deleted architecture. (2) A leftover VLMTestInfo entry would cause CI collection/parametrization failures once the model is unregistered. (3) Verify both entries are deleted from VLM_TEST_SETTINGS.

(1) tests/models/registry.py contains _HfExamplesInfo entries for both 'MusicFlamingoForConditionalGeneration' (line ~783) and 'AyaVisionForConditionalGeneration' (line ~790); both must be removed to match the deleted vllm registry entries. (2) A registry mismatch between vllm/model_executor/models/registry.py and tests/models/registry.py triggers the registry consistency test to fail. (3) Confirm both example-info entries are deleted.

(1) The new file tests/lora/test_qwen3_with_multi_loras.py is a byte-for-byte duplicate of the existing tests/lora/test_llm_with_multi_loras.py, and this PR does NOT delete the original file.
