(2) This defeats the PR's stated goal of *reducing* LoRA tests and actually risks increasing test load: in the sharded run (`pytest -v -s lora ...`), the `--ignore` entry was changed from `--ignore=lora/test_llm_with_multi_loras.py` to `--ignore=lora/test_qwen3_with_multi_loras.py`. As a result the old file test_llm_with_multi_loras.py is no longer ignored and will now be collected and executed in the shard, while the new duplicate runs in the dedicated multi-GPU section — so the same multi-lora tests run twice (duplicated model downloads, GPU time, and duplicate global mutable state LORA_NAME_ID_MAP/INCREASE_LORA_ID collisions across files). (3) Delete tests/lora/test_llm_with_multi_loras.py as part of this rename, or keep it in the shard `--ignore` list.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run `pytest --collect-only -q lora --ignore=lora/test_qwen3_with_multi_loras.py ...` and confirm test_llm_with_multi_loras.py is no longer double-collected.

findings.claim: (1) test_qwen3_with_multi_loras.py uses module-level mutable globals (INCREASE_LORA_ID, LORA_NAME_ID_MAP) mutated via `global` in make_add_lora_request. (2) With the original test_llm_with_multi_loras.py still present as an identical copy, if both are collected in one pytest process, these globals are per-module so they won't cross-contaminate; but within pytest -x sharded collection the reliance on INCREASE_LORA_ID monotonic ordering couples test_multi_loras_with_tp_sync's assertions ([1],[1,2],[1,2,3]) to import-time state, making the test order-dependent and fragile. (3) Prefer explicit fixture-scoped state or reset the counter in a fixture rather than module globals.

(1) test_qwen3_with_multi_loras.py adds test_load_inplace_offline_reload and test_load_inplace_false_no_reload which depend on fixtures qwen3_meowing_lora_files and qwen3_woofing_lora_files; a grep of the parent tree finds no definition of these fixtures anywhere (they are not in tests/lora/conftest.py). (2) If the fixtures are not introduced elsewhere, these two tests will error at collection/setup with 'fixture not found' whenever they are run (they are unconditionally collected, not CUDA-skipped). (3) Confirm the fixtures qwen3_meowing_lora_files/qwen3_woofing_lora_files are defined in a conftest reachable from tests/lora/, and add them in this PR if missing.
findings.suggested_benchmark: pytest --collect-only tests/lora/test_qwen3_with_multi_loras.py and check for fixture resolution errors.

findings.claim: (1) In tests/lora/test_qwenvl.py the skipif(is_cuda_alike) decorator was added to test_qwen25vl_lora and test_qwen25vl_vision_lora, but NOT to test_qwen2vl_lora_beam_search in the same file. (2) If the intent was to skip all redundant Qwen-VL model tests on CUDA-alike platforms, leaving the beam-search test unskipped is inconsistent and keeps a heavy VL model download/run on CUDA; if intentional (keep one representative), fine. (3) Confirm whether test_qwen2vl_lora_beam_search should also be skipped for consistency with the stated 'reduce redundant model tests' goal.

(1) The compressed-decode branch of `_build_flashinfer_mixed_sparse_indices_kernel` (around cache_utils.py:886) applies `_remap_flashinfer_index(values, compressed_block_size, compressed_block_span)` after a code path where `values` can be assigned either from a global-index branch or a local-index branch (DECODE_COMPRESSED_INDICES_ARE_LOCAL). The remap converts `block*block_size+off -> block*block_span+off` by reversing the packed layout. This is only correct if, whenever remap is applied, `values` was constructed as `block_numbers * compressed_block_size + block_offsets`. If the alternate branch produces `values` that are already physical token strides (global slot ids not built with `compressed_block_size` decomposition), the `// compressed_block_size` / `% compressed_block_size` reconstruction would corrupt them. (2) Impact: wrong sparse KV indices -> garbage attention output, i.e. the exact class of bug this PR fixes, but for a different code path. (3) Verify that in ALL branches feeding into the remap call site, `values` are encoded as `block_number * compressed_block_size + offset` (with offset < compressed_block_size), and add an assertion/comment documenting that invariant.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSV4 c128a / c4a decode correctness test with packed KV (alignment=512) comparing against reference dense attention output

findings.claim: (1) `_remap_flashinfer_index` recomputes `safe_values // block_size` and `safe_values % block_size` to translate logical slot ids into packed physical strides. When `block_span == block_size` (unpacked KV) the doc claims it is a no-op, which holds. But when packed, the transform assumes the incoming `values` are logical slot ids of the form `block_number*block_size + offset` with `0 <= offset < block_size`. If any upstream index already exceeds this contract (e.g. an index that is a raw token position rather than a paged slot id, as in the SWA slot_ids branch which uses `block_numbers * swa_block_size + block_offsets` where block_numbers come from the block table), the modulo/division reconstruction silently produces a valid-looking but wrong physical offset. (2) Impact: for TP configs where alignment differs (the TP16 case in this PR), a miscomputed span silently corrupts a subset of gathered KV -> garbage output. (3) Add a debug-mode assertion that `block_span % block_size == 0` and `block_span >= block_size` inside the caller (`_packed_block_span` only checks `block_stride % token_stride == 0`, not that the result is a multiple of `block_size`).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test on _packed_block_span + remap with block_size=256, alignment=512 vs 576 verifying slot id translation matches physical layout

findings.claim: (1) The default `alignment` for non-fp8 caches is changed from `None` to `512` in four KVCacheSpec constructions (attention.py DeepseekV4IndexerCache, compressor.py CompressorStateCache, sparse_swa.py DeepseekV4SWACache, and the indexer spec). Previously `None` meant 'no forced page alignment / share pages with contiguous KV', per the removed comment 'V3.2 keeps the legacy layout' and 'padding would break page matching'. Forcing 512 now pads pages. (2) Impact: this changes the physical KV cache page size / memory footprint for ALL non-fp8 DSV4/V3.2 caches, not just the FlashInfer-sparse path that needs it — it may increase KV cache memory (reducing max batch size / context) and could break the compressor state/KV page-sharing invariant the removed comment warned about ('state pages share physical tensor with KV pages, must use the same page size'). (3) Confirm 512-alignment is safe for the compressor state-cache page-sharing path (CompressorStateCache), and that V3.2 (compress_ratio<=1) which is not FlashInfer-sparse still functions; if the 512 alignment is only required for the FlashInfer sparse backend, gate it on backend selection rather than applying unconditionally to `else`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure KV cache page bytes and max num blocks for DSV4 bf16 and V3.2 before/after; run V3.2 end-to-end correctness

findings.claim: (1) The stale comment removed in attention.py's indexer spec ('DeepseekV4 aligns indexer pages to FlashMLA's 576B ... V3.2 keeps the legacy layout') was replaced with '576B for FlashMLA packing; 512B for FlashInfer sparse (#44577)'. However the compressor.py CompressorStateCache still keeps its old explanatory comment ('Plain full-cache rows share state pages with contiguous KV pages, so padding would break page matching') directly above a line that now DOES pad to 512. (2) Impact: the retained comment now contradicts the code (a wrong comment is worse than none), potentially misleading future maintainers into thinking non-fp8 caches are unpadded. (3) Update the CompressorStateCache comment to reflect the new 512B alignment behavior and why it is safe.

(1) `_packed_block_span(swa_k_cache)` and `_packed_block_span(compressed_kv_cache)` are computed on every call to `_build_sparse_index_metadata` when `cached_sparse is None`. These are pure functions of the cache tensor strides, which are fixed after allocation. (2) Impact: minor per-step Python/host overhead (stride reads + division + branch) inside the attention forward path; negligible but avoidable. (3) Since the result feeds a cached `sparse_indices`, and the strides never change, the spans could be computed once (e.g. cached on the layer/metadata) rather than per uncached build. Low priority.

(1) `copy_to_persistent` was rewritten from `dst.as_strided(src.shape, src.stride()); dst.copy_(src)` to `sliced = dst[tuple(slice(0, s) for s in src.shape)]; sliced.copy_(src)`. These are not semantically equivalent: the old path re-strided the persistent buffer to *match src's stride* (so the returned tensor shared src's memory layout, which matters for downstream flex_attention kernels that may assume a particular stride/contiguity), while the new path returns a plain contiguous-slice view of the row-major persistent buffer. (2) If src is non-contiguous or has a stride layout the flex_attention BlockMask machinery relies on, the new slice view will have a different stride than before, potentially producing a differently-laid-out tensor passed into the compiled kernel — a silent behavioral change rather than a pure refactor. (3) Verify that all callers (persistent_kv_indices, persistent_kv_num_blocks, persistent_doc_ids, inverse_block_table, offset_tensor) tolerate a row-major contiguous slice, and that src is always contiguous at these call sites; if not, add a `.contiguous()`/stride assertion or document the layout contract.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run tests/v1/determinism suite with FLEX_ATTENTION backend plus a mixed prefill/decode workload; compare flex_attention outputs before/after the copy_to_persistent rewrite for numerical equality across multiple batch shapes.

findings.claim: (1) The new `copy_to_persistent` implementation slices `dst` to `src.shape` with `dst[tuple(slice(0, s) for s in src.shape)]` but no longer validates that the persistent buffer `dst` is large enough in every dimension; if `src` is larger than `dst` along any dim, the slice silently truncates to dst's size and `sliced.copy_(src)` will raise a shape-mismatch error with a less informative message than the previous explicit RuntimeError. (2) This removes the diagnostic context ('Fail to re-stride a persistent tensor of shape ...') that helped debug persistent-buffer capacity overflows, making future capacity bugs harder to diagnose. (3) Consider adding an assertion that each src dim <= dst dim with a descriptive message.

(1) The rewritten `copy_to_persistent` builds a Python tuple of slice objects per call (`tuple(slice(0, s) for s in src.shape)`) on the metadata-build hot path (called ~5x per attention metadata build). (2) This is negligible CPU overhead but the returned view now differs in stride from the previous implementation; if the downstream compiled flex_attention path recompiles due to a differing stride/contiguity signature, it could trigger dynamo recompiles (recompile_limit is 16). (3) Confirm no additional torch.compile recompilations are induced by the changed tensor layout.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable TORCH_LOGS=recompiles and run the FLEX_ATTENTION determinism test to check for new recompilation events after the copy_to_persistent change.

findings.claim: (1) The RMSNorm submodule was renamed from `embedding_post_projection_norm` to `embedding_pre_projection_norm` in `Gemma4MultimodalEmbedder`. (2) This changes the parameter name in the module's state_dict; existing/HF checkpoints whose weight keys carry the norm under the old name (`...embedding_post_projection_norm.weight`) will no longer match unless the checkpoint itself uses the pre-projection name, and any WeightsMapper / weight-loading logic keyed on the old name will silently skip the tensor, producing an uninitialized (or missing-key) norm. (3) Verify against the actual HF Gemma4 checkpoint key name and, if the on-disk key differs, add a WeightsMapper entry mapping the old key to the new module name; also confirm loader does not error on unexpected/missing keys.

(1) The forward pass math changed from `norm(projection(x))` to `projection(norm(x))`, and the norm now operates on `embedding_dim` (input dim) rather than `text_hidden_size` (output dim). (2) This is the core intended fix, but note the RMSNorm is now constructed with `embedding_dim` as its normalized size instead of `text_hidden_size`; if `embedding_dim != text_hidden_size` the previous norm weight/shape assumptions no longer hold, and any pretrained norm weights sized for `text_hidden_size` would be shape-incompatible. Since `has_weight=False`, there are no learned weights so shape is only the normalization dimension — confirm this matches the HF reference (norm applied over the embedder input dim before projection). (3) Confirm dtype/shape parity with HF `Gemma4MultimodalEmbedder.forward` and add a numerical parity test (image + audio embed) against the transformers reference.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Numerical parity test: run Gemma4MultimodalEmbedder.forward on a fixed random inputs_embeds tensor and compare against transformers reference implementation output (allclose).

findings.claim: (1) The class docstring still documents the old data flow `inputs_embeds → embedding_projection → embedding_post_projection_norm`, which now contradicts the corrected forward order (norm then projection). (2) A stale docstring describing the wrong order is misleading for future maintainers of this exact correctness-sensitive path. (3) Update the docstring at line 825 to `inputs_embeds → embedding_pre_projection_norm → embedding_projection`.

(1) The weight-loading comment at line 1337 still references `embedding_projection + embedding_post_projection_norm`. (2) After the rename this comment is now inaccurate and could mislead anyone debugging weight loading for the renamed norm module. (3) Update the comment to reference `embedding_pre_projection_norm`.

(1) The old Dockerfile.cpu forced `sentence-transformers==5.3.0` for CPU test images via `sed -i 's/^sentence-transformers.*/sentence-transformers==5.3.0/g'`, with a comment referencing a CPU-specific issue (PR #38800#issuecomment-4228314305). The new pip-compile-cpu hook compiles requirements/test/cuda.in directly and the generated requirements/test/cpu.txt now pins `sentence-transformers==5.2.0` (matching cuda.in's `>=5.2.0`), silently dropping the 5.3.0 override. (2) Impact: if the reason for pinning 5.3.0 on CPU (per PR #38800) still holds, CPU CI tests (embedding/sentence-transformers tests) may regress or fail. This override was intentionally added and is now lost with no migration note. (3) Fix: either bump the constraint in cuda.in / add a CPU-specific constraint, or document why the 5.3.0 override is no longer needed; confirm CPU embedding tests still pass with 5.2.0.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the CPU embedding/sentence-transformers test suite against the new cpu.txt (sentence-transformers==5.2.0) and compare pass/fail vs the prior 5.3.0 pin.

findings.claim: (1) The old Dockerfile stripped version pins from torchaudio/torchvision before compiling (`sed 's/torchaudio.*/torchaudio/g'`, `sed 's/torchvision.*/torchvision/g'`), leaving them unpinned so the CPU resolver could pick compatible CPU wheels. The new pip-compile-cpu hook compiles cuda.in verbatim, which pins `torchaudio==2.11.0` and `torchvision==0.26.0`. (2) Impact: if CPU wheels for those exact versions are not published on the CPU index for the target platform/python, the pre-commit compile (and CPU image build) will fail to resolve; the previous flow deliberately avoided pinning these to sidestep exactly this. (3) Fix: verify CPU wheels exist for torch/torchaudio/torchvision at the pinned versions under `--torch-backend cpu`; if not, keep them unpinned via a separate cpu.in or `--no-emit`/override mechanism.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run `uv pip compile requirements/test/cuda.in -o requirements/test/cpu.txt --torch-backend cpu --python-platform x86_64-manylinux_2_28 --python-version 3.12` and confirm resolution succeeds for pinned torchaudio/torchvision CPU wheels.

findings.claim: (1) The pip-compile-cpu hook `files` regex is `^requirements/(common|cpu|test/(cuda|cpu))\.(in|txt)$`, which triggers a recompile when the runtime `requirements/cpu.txt` changes, but the hook does NOT pass `-c requirements/cpu.txt` as a constraint (unlike the CUDA hook which uses `-c requirements/cuda.txt` and the XPU hook which uses `-c requirements/xpu.txt`). (2) Impact: test-dependency versions in cpu.txt are resolved independently of the CPU runtime requirements, so they can drift/conflict with what the base image installs from requirements/cpu.txt; also the `cpu` trigger in the regex has no effect on output since it isn't used as a constraint. (3) Fix: either add `-c requirements/cpu.txt` to the args for consistency with the other backends, or drop `cpu` from the `files` trigger to avoid a misleading dependency.

(1) On aarch64 the Dockerfile now post-processes the x86-compiled cpu.txt with `sed -i '/^decord==/d; /^terratorch==/d'`. Because cpu.txt is compiled with `--python-platform x86_64-manylinux_2_28`, platform markers are resolved away, so `decord==0.6.0; platform_machine == "x86_64"` becomes an unconditional `decord==0.6.0` line that the sed correctly strips. However, if any of these packages later appear as transitive dependencies of another emitted package, the anchored `/^decord==/d` sed would still remove the top-level pin while leaving the transitive requirement, or vice-versa, producing an inconsistent resolution on arm. (2) Impact: potential arm build breakage or silently missing/kept package if dependency graph changes. (3) Fix: add a comment/test asserting decord/terratorch are only ever top-level in cpu.txt, or filter more robustly.

(1) In `_log_jit_compile`, the `kwargs` parameter has no type annotation while other functions in the module are annotated (fn_name: str). (2) Minor readability/consistency issue only. (3) Annotate as `kwargs: dict` (or the appropriate type) for consistency with the codebase's typing conventions.

(1) In the verbose branch, `compile_info.get("key") or kwargs.get("key")` uses `or`, so a compile_info key that is falsy (e.g. 0 or empty string) will silently fall through to `kwargs.get("key")`. Given the fragility comment already in the module about Triton's changing internal API, the exact shape of `compile` is uncertain. (2) Low impact: this is a debug-only log line, and a wrong/None key merely produces a slightly misleading log. (3) If falsy-but-present keys are possible, prefer explicit `compile_info.get("key", kwargs.get("key"))`; otherwise the current form is acceptable for a debug path.

(1) The dispatch rewrite removed the `TYPE_CHECKING` guard, so mypy now statically sees the ROCm/`amd` branch as the default import (it's now the first `if` arm), yet the retained comment still states "The NVIDIA branch is the static default that mypy sees". (2) The comment is now stale/misleading and the type-checked default silently switched from nvidia to amd, which can change which class signatures mypy validates against and mask nvidia-side type regressions. (3) Update the comment to reflect the new default arm, and if nvidia must remain the type-checked default, restructure to `if TYPE_CHECKING or (not is_rocm() and not is_xpu()): nvidia` so mypy still resolves nvidia.

(1) `HCHeadOp.forward_xpu` unconditionally calls the triton path `torch.ops.vllm.hc_head_triton`, duplicating the non-tilelang branch of `forward_hip` verbatim while `MHCPreOp.forward_xpu`/`MHCPostOp.forward_xpu` simply delegate to `forward_native`. (2) The inconsistency (some XPU ops delegate to native, one inlines the triton branch) adds maintenance risk: if the triton op signature changes, this copy must be kept in sync manually. (3) Consider factoring the shared triton-fallback body of `forward_hip`/`forward_xpu` into a private helper to avoid divergence.

(1) In the XPU `DeepseekV4MegaMoEExperts`, `_check_runtime_supported()` unconditionally raises `NotImplementedError("DeepSeek V4 MegaMoE is not supported on XPU.")`, but `finalize_weights()` (which calls it) and `_run_mega_moe()` remain fully fleshed out and reachable via the registered custom op `deepseek_v4_mega_moe_experts`. (2) This is dead/misleading code: the entire MegaMoE forward path can never execute on XPU, yet a large amount of DeepGEMM-dependent code is carried and could confuse readers into thinking MegaMoE works on XPU. (3) Either gate `use_mega_moe` off entirely on XPU at config time with a clear error, or drop the unreachable `_run_mega_moe`/`finalize_weights` bodies; at minimum document that these are placeholders.

(1) The XPU `_map_global_expert_id` returns a single `int` (or `-1`) and `weight_loader` handles only a single local expert id, whereas the nvidia counterpart returns a `list[int]` and iterates to support EPLB replica placement (`p % num_logical_experts == expert_id`). (2) This divergence means the XPU path silently lacks EPLB expert-replication support; if a checkpoint/config relies on logical→physical replica mapping, XPU will map experts incorrectly or drop weights. (3) Confirm EPLB is intentionally unsupported on XPU and, if so, add an explicit guard/error when EPLB is enabled rather than silently loading under the simplified mapping.

(1) The new e8m0->float32 conversion is applied to the weight scale in `process_weights_after_loading`, which is correct, but the added block comment asserts activation scales are "always float32 (use_ue8m0=False on XPU)" as a load-time invariant; there is no assertion enforcing this at the apply/gemm boundary. (2) If a future path produces e8m0 activation scales on XPU, the oneDNN block-quant dispatch condition described in the comment would again fail silently (NaN), with no guard to catch it. (3) Consider adding a lightweight dtype assertion on the activation scale (`As`) in `apply_block_scaled_mm` to fail loudly rather than produce NaN.

(1) The updated Responses API doc link has an inconsistent display text vs. target: the visible link text still reads `examples/online_serving/openai_responses_client_with_tools.py` while the actual href was changed to `../../examples/tool_calling/openai_responses_client_with_tools.py`. (2) Since this PR moved the file into `examples/tool_calling/`, the display text now points to a path that no longer exists, misleading readers who copy the visible path rather than clicking the link. (3) Update the display text to also read `examples/tool_calling/openai_responses_client_with_tools.py` so text and href match, consistent with the other link edits in this PR (e.g. the transcription/translation client links which updated both text and href).

(1) In TieringOffloadingManager.lookup, the primary-tier result is checked with two separate if-branches that return the same enum value they matched (`if primary_hit is LookupResult.HIT: return LookupResult.HIT` and `if primary_hit is LookupResult.HIT_PENDING: return LookupResult.HIT_PENDING`). (2) This is minor indirection; the primary tier can only ever return HIT/HIT_PENDING/MISS (its own lookup never returns RETRY), so both short-circuit branches could collapse to `if primary_hit in (HIT, HIT_PENDING): return primary_hit`. (3) Consider collapsing to a single branch for clarity, or leaving as-is if the explicit enumeration is preferred for readability. Non-blocking.

(1) The SecondaryTierManager.lookup docstring documents only HIT/MISS/RETRY as possible return values, but the abstract signature now returns LookupResult which also includes HIT_PENDING. (2) A reader implementing a new secondary tier may be unsure whether HIT_PENDING is a valid return; in practice secondary tiers never return HIT_PENDING (only the primary CPU tier does, for in-flight writes), so the docstring is actually correct-by-contract but leaves the exclusion implicit. (3) Add one sentence clarifying that secondary tiers must not return HIT_PENDING (that state is reserved for the primary tier), to make the contract explicit for future implementers.

(1) The QKV split order for gated attention is [query, gate, key, value] in `_get_qkv_split_shapes` (emerging_optimizers.py, gated branch returns [query_projection_size, query_projection_size, kv_channels, kv_channels]), which matches the physical weight layout in attention.py get_query_key_value_tensors (split_arg_list = [q, gate, k, v] at line ~1604, `SplitAlongDim(mixed_qkv, 3, split_arg_list)` yielding (query, gate, key, value)). This ordering appears correct. However, verify that the query and gate segments actually have equal size query_projection_size when num_query_groups != num_attention_heads: the physical layout is [ng, (2*np/ng + 2)*hn], i.e. per-query-group the layout is 2 groups of (np/ng)*hn (query+gate) then 2*hn (k+v). The reshape in Muon's orthogonalize uses grad.view(num_query_groups, qkv_split_dim, -1) and splits on dim=1 with [q, gate, k, v]. Since the weight's physical dim0 is ordered by query-group blocks, this per-group split is consistent. (2) If the split order were wrong, Muon would orthogonalize incorrectly-mixed slices, silently corrupting the update. (3) Confirm via a unit test that the split shapes correspond to the interleaved-per-group weight layout (the added test only checks the returned list, not that it maps to actual attention weight partitioning).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test constructing a gated attention layer, extracting linear_qkv.weight, and asserting Muon's per-group QKV split reconstructs the true query/gate/key/value sub-weights.

findings.claim: (1) The public type annotation `qkv_split_shapes` on `TensorParallelMuon.__init__` and `TensorParallelAdaptiveMuon.__init__` was changed from `tuple[int, int, int] | None` to `list[int] | None`, and `_get_qkv_split_shapes` return type from `List[int]` to `list[int]`. (2) The tuple->list change is a behavioral requirement now since gated attention returns 4 elements (tuple[int,int,int] would have been wrong), but existing callers passing a 3-tuple still work at runtime since torch.split accepts any sequence; only the annotation contract changed. This is low risk but worth noting for any downstream code doing isinstance(tuple) checks. (3) No fix required; the change is intentional to support variable-length splits.

(1) In __init__.py, `qkv_split_shapes` is computed once per model_chunk via `_get_qkv_split_shapes(model_chunk.config)` and attached to each qkv param, but the per-param divisibility guard `param.shape[0] % sum(qkv_split_shapes) == 0` only sets `is_qkv=True` when divisible; otherwise it silently logs at DEBUG and leaves the param treated as a normal 2D matrix by Muon. (2) If divisibility genuinely fails for a real gated-QKV weight (e.g. a config mismatch), the param is silently orthogonalized as a whole matrix rather than per-QKV, which is a numerically different (and likely wrong) update, with only a DEBUG log that most users will never see. (3) Consider raising or logging at WARNING level when a param name matches 'linear_qkv.weight' but the computed split shapes do not divide its shape, since that indicates a config/layout inconsistency rather than an expected skip.

(1) The per-param `qkv_split_shapes` attribute now duplicates the same list object across all qkv params in a chunk (each param.qkv_split_shapes points to the shared `qkv_split_shapes` computed once). In orthogonalize(), `getattr(p, 'qkv_split_shapes', None)` falls back to `self.qkv_split_shapes`. Since the optimizer already receives a global `qkv_split_shapes` via `_muon_config_to_kwargs`, the per-param attribute is only needed when different params require different splits (which currently they do not within a chunk). (2) Minor: adds an attribute-copy path (copy_tensor_model_parallel_attributes must now propagate qkv_split_shapes) and per-param memory/state. (3) Acceptable given future MLA/heterogeneous-layer support, but a comment documenting why per-param override exists (vs. relying on the constructor-level value) would clarify intent.

(1) `cusolvermp_pypi_package_name()` is added unconditionally to `install_reqs`, so EVERY Transformer Engine install now pulls in the `nvidia-cusolvermp-cuXX` runtime wheel, even for builds that were NOT compiled with cuSolverMp (i.e. when `NVTE_WITH_CUSOLVERMP` is unset, which is the default). (2) This silently forces a large new mandatory runtime dependency on all users, breaking installs in constrained/offline environments and diverging from the established pattern for the analogous cuBLASMp integration, which is gated purely behind the build flag and is NOT in install_requires. It also contradicts the runtime loader in transformer_engine/common/__init__.py, which treats cuSolverMp as OPTIONAL (only loads it if the package happens to be installed). (3) Make the dependency conditional on the build actually enabling cuSolverMp, e.g. only append `cusolvermp_pypi_package_name()` when `bool(int(os.getenv('NVTE_WITH_CUSOLVERMP','0')))` is true (mirroring how the CMake flag is gated in setup_common_extension), or move it into an optional extras_require entry.

(1) The runtime loader calls `_load_cuda_library_from_python('cusolverMp', strict=False)` using the mixed-case name `cusolverMp`, but `_load_cuda_library_from_python` globs the nvidia python package tree by that literal string (e.g. `nvidia/cu13/cusolverMp/lib/...`, `nvidia/cusolverMp/lib/...`). The installed `nvidia-cusolvermp-cuXX` wheel materializes a lowercase directory (`nvidia/cusolvermp` or `nvidia/cuXX/cusolvermp`), and glob is case-sensitive on Linux filesystems. (2) With `strict=False`, a case mismatch silently returns not-found and `_CUSOLVERMP_LIB_CTYPES` stays None, so the library the wheel just added as a dependency never gets loaded (RTLD_GLOBAL) — the Newton-Schulz feature can then fail at link/symbol-resolution time despite the dependency being present. Contrast with existing lowercase names (`cublas`, `cudart`, `cudnn`) which match their package dirs. (3) Verify the actual directory casing shipped by `nvidia-cusolvermp-cuXX` and use the matching lowercase `cusolvermp` (or make the glob case-insensitive), then confirm the .so is actually loaded.

(1) `cusolvermp_pypi_package_name()` with no argument calls `cuda_version()`, which raises `RuntimeError('Could neither find NVCC executable nor CUDA runtime Python package.')` when neither nvcc nor the cuda-runtime pip package is present. This call now runs inside `setup_requirements()` for the normal (non-metapackage) install path. (2) This introduces a new hard failure during dependency resolution for environments that previously could compute requirements without a discoverable CUDA version, regressing `pip install`/`setup.py` behavior. (3) Guard the CUDA-version lookup (e.g. fall back to a default major version or only compute the package name when cuSolverMp is actually enabled for the build), so requirement setup does not fail when CUDA cannot be probed.

(1) `_is_cusolvermp_installed_in_system()` shells out to `ldconfig -p` at import time on every TE import (on Linux, when the cusolvermp pip package is also present it is short-circuited, but in the common case the pip check may run first). (2) The subprocess launch adds a small but non-trivial fixed cost to `import transformer_engine`; it is cached via lru_cache so only incurred once per process, limiting impact. (3) Acceptable as-is given the lru_cache, but consider checking the cheaper pip-package predicate first (short-circuit ordering) so the ldconfig subprocess is skipped when the wheel-provided library is already known to be installed.

(1) A new block is added to __init__ that scans self._kv_cache_config.kv_cache_groups and sets self._hs_group_idx by matching any layer name containing the substring "cache_only_layers". But register_kv_caches() (unchanged, ~line 260) already computes the exact same _hs_group_idx using a more precise match (self.cache_layers[0] in group.layer_names, where cache_layers is filtered to CacheOnlyAttentionLayer). (2) This creates two independent detection paths for the same field; the __init__ heuristic uses fragile substring matching on layer names rather than the authoritative CacheOnlyAttentionLayer filter, and both write the same attribute. If the two ever disagree, the register_kv_caches value wins (it runs later), so the __init__ computation is effectively dead work in the normal worker flow. It only matters if _hs_group_idx is read before register_kv_caches runs. (3) Confirm whether _hs_group_idx is genuinely needed before register_kv_caches (e.g. on the scheduler role where register_kv_caches is not called). If it is needed early, keep only one detection method and make both call sites share a single helper; if not, drop the __init__ block as redundant.

(1) The __init__ group detection matches on the substring "cache_only_layers" appearing anywhere in a layer name (any("cache_only_layers" in n for n in group.layer_names)). (2) This is a brittle string heuristic: it depends on the module-path naming convention `cache_only_layers.<idx>` staying stable, and could false-match an unrelated layer whose name coincidentally contains that substring. register_kv_caches uses the robust CacheOnlyAttentionLayer type filter instead. (3) Prefer matching against the resolved CacheOnlyAttentionLayer layer names (as register_kv_caches does) rather than substring matching, or share the same detection routine.

(1) The humming import guard was changed from `try: ... except ModuleNotFoundError: HummingMethod = None` to `if current_platform.is_cuda():` with the imports no longer wrapped in try/except. (2) `humming.py` is eagerly imported inside `get_quantization_config()` (via `from .humming import HummingConfig`), which runs for ANY quantization method request, not just 'humming'. Previously, on a CUDA machine without the `humming` package installed, this module imported cleanly (HummingMethod=None) and only failed with a clear, actionable assertion when a user actually selected humming quantization. After this change, on any CUDA machine lacking the `humming` package, the unguarded imports raise `ModuleNotFoundError` at import time, which propagates and breaks resolution of ALL quantization methods (fp8, awq, gptq, etc.), not just humming. While the PR adds humming-kernels to requirements/cuda.txt for docker builds, users installing vLLM outside the full CUDA requirement set (source builds, custom environments) will now hit a hard failure with no helpful message. (3) Keep a graceful fallback: wrap the `if current_platform.is_cuda():` import block in try/except ModuleNotFoundError (retaining HummingMethod=None and a restored assert_humming_available() invoked lazily in HummingConfig.__init__), or make the humming import lazy inside the methods that use it.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a CUDA host WITHOUT the humming package installed, run `python -c "from vllm.model_executor.layers.quantization import get_quantization_config; get_quantization_config('fp8')"` and confirm it does not raise ModuleNotFoundError.

findings.claim: (1) `assert_humming_available()` and its call in `HummingConfig.__init__` were removed. (2) This removes the clear user-facing error message ('humming is not available, please run pip install ...') that previously guided users when humming was missing. With the new import scheme, a missing humming package now surfaces as a raw ModuleNotFoundError at module import time (breaking unrelated quant methods) rather than a targeted, instructive assertion when humming is actually requested. This is a behavioral/UX regression for the error path. (3) Restore a lazy availability check (assert HummingMethod is not None with the install hint) invoked in HummingConfig.__init__, combined with a guarded import that tolerates the package being absent.

(1) The runtime `if current_platform.is_cuda():` block imports the humming symbols, and a separate `if TYPE_CHECKING:` block re-imports a subset (BaseInputSchema, BaseWeightSchema, HummingInputSchema, HummingWeightSchema) from humming.schema. (2) The TYPE_CHECKING re-import of the same names is redundant on CUDA (they are already bound at runtime) and, more importantly, means that on non-CUDA platforms these names are ONLY available to the type checker, not at runtime; several methods (e.g. get_layer_weight_schema uses BaseWeightSchema, get_layer_input_schema uses BaseInputSchema) reference them at runtime. On a non-CUDA platform these would raise NameError if reached — though such methods are presumably unreachable off-CUDA. The dual-import pattern is confusing and risks NameError if any of these symbols is referenced at runtime on a non-CUDA path. (3) Consolidate: rely on the runtime import for actual symbols and keep only forward-reference string annotations for type-only usage, or document clearly that HummingConfig is CUDA-only and guard its construction accordingly.

(1) A new hard dependency `cupy-cuda13x < 14.1.0` is added to kv_connectors.txt with a comment that CuPy 14.1.0 imports pytest from cupy.testing._random. (2) Pinning to a strict upper bound `< 14.1.0` freezes users out of all future CuPy 14.1+ releases even after the pytest-import bug is fixed upstream; the comment acknowledges this is a temporary workaround ('until a fixed newer release is verified'). This can silently hold back security/bug fixes. (3) Add a TODO with a tracking issue reference so the pin is revisited, and consider excluding only the known-bad version (e.g. `!=14.1.0`) if the issue is isolated to that release rather than an open-ended lower ceiling.

(1) The guard was broadened from the specific substring `.mlp.experts.` to the very general `.experts.`, and the actual rename now uses `name.replace(".experts.", ".experts.routed_experts.", 1)`. (2) Risk: any checkpoint weight whose name contains `.experts.` anywhere but which is NOT a routed-expert parameter — e.g. `shared_experts.` in some MoE architectures, or any module literally named `*experts*` — will now be caught by the broadened guard and its name rewritten. In mllama4.py the caller already excludes `.shared_expert` (singular), but a `.shared_experts.` (plural) or other `*experts*` submodule would still match `.experts.` and be incorrectly remapped, silently loading a weight into the wrong param or falling through. The `replace(..., 1)` only inserts after the FIRST `.experts.`, so nested names like `a.experts.b.experts.w13` remap the wrong segment. (3) Fix: keep the match anchored to the expert-module boundary (e.g. require a full module segment `.experts.` preceded by a routing container, or reuse an allow-list of expert-param suffixes) and add a test covering shared-expert and non-routed `*experts*` names to confirm they are left untouched.

(1) The skip-guard `if ".experts.routed_experts." in name: return name` and the insertion both rely on the literal `.experts.` boundary; combined with `replace(..., 1)` this assumes there is exactly one meaningful `.experts.` segment per name. (2) Risk: for any future model that legitimately has two `experts` scopes in a param path, the first occurrence would be remapped, producing a name absent from params_dict and (per the existing `if new_name in params_dict` guard) silently returning the original unremapped name — a silent load skip rather than an error. (3) Fix: document the single-segment assumption in the docstring, or assert the constructed name exists and log at debug level when the remap is skipped so silent no-load is diagnosable.

(1) `maybe_remap_moe_expert_param_name` is now called unconditionally on every weight in the Lfm2MoeModel loader loop (unlike llama4/mllama4 which gate it behind expert/scale-name checks). (2) Impact: the helper's early-return `if ".experts." not in name: return name` makes this cheap and safe for non-expert weights, so this is fine functionally; but because lfm2 does not restrict to scale/expert names the broadened `.experts.` match now applies to lfm2 checkpoints too, changing behavior for existing lfm2 checkpoints that previously loaded without remapping. Verify existing lfm2_moe checkpoints still resolve to the same param names after this change (regression test with a real lfm2-moe checkpoint). (3) Fix: add/confirm a load test for lfm2_moe to guard against a name that now unexpectedly matches `.experts.`.

(1) The new ROCm memory-settle block reads `gpu_memory_utilization` via `kwargs.get("gpu_memory_utilization", CacheConfig.gpu_memory_utilization)`, but `CacheConfig.gpu_memory_utilization` is a Pydantic `Field(default=0.92, gt=0, le=1)` (see vllm/config/cache.py:67), not a plain class attribute. Accessing it on the class object returns the `FieldInfo` descriptor / Pydantic sentinel, not the float `0.92`. (2) When a caller omits `gpu_memory_utilization`, `threshold_ratio=1.0 - gpu_memory_utilization` would then attempt arithmetic on a FieldInfo and raise a TypeError (or use a wrong value), defeating the OOM fix on the default path. (3) Use the concrete default explicitly, e.g. `CacheConfig.model_fields["gpu_memory_utilization"].default` or a module-level constant `0.92`, and add a regression check that the default path yields a float threshold.

(1) `wait_for_rocm_memory_to_settle(threshold_ratio=1.0 - gpu_memory_utilization)` is called unconditionally before every ROCm LLM construction and blocks (polls amdsmi VRAM with sleeps) until free memory drops below the threshold. (2) On a clean device this adds a fixed polling/settle latency to every ROCm test that builds a VllmRunner, increasing CI wall time; if the threshold is mis-derived (see the CacheConfig default issue) it could block up to the full timeout. (3) Consider short-circuiting when current free memory already satisfies the requirement (fast path) and confirm the function returns immediately when memory is already settled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-test ROCm VllmRunner construction latency with and without the wait on an idle device.

findings.claim: (1) In the parent code, the RLHF weight-transfer endpoints (`/pause`, `/resume`, `/is_paused`, `/init_weight_transfer_engine`, `/start_weight_update`, `/update_weights`, `/finish_weight_update`, `/get_world_size`) were attached only inside the `if "generate" in supported_tasks:` block of `build_app` (in addition to the dev-mode self-guard). In the new code they are attached via `register_vllm_dev_api_routers()`, which `build_app` calls unconditionally whenever `VLLM_SERVER_DEV_MODE` is set — BEFORE and independent of the `supported_tasks` check. (2) This silently changes behavior: for non-generate servers (pooling/embedding/reward/classification) running with dev mode enabled, the RLHF routes are now registered where they previously were not. These routes call `engine.pause_generation`/`update_weights`/etc. which may be unsupported for such engines, and it also expands the exposed API surface. (3) If the intent is to keep RLHF endpoints generate-only, move the rlhf `attach_router` call back inside the `"generate" in supported_tasks` branch rather than into the unconditional dev-router registration.

(1) The new `vllm/entrypoints/serve/dev/server_info/api_router.py` defines a `GET /server_info` route identical to the pre-existing one in `vllm/entrypoints/serve/instrumentator/server_info.py`, which was registered by `register_instrumentator_api_routers` under `if envs.VLLM_SERVER_DEV_MODE`. `register_vllm_serve_api_routers` still calls `register_instrumentator_api_routers(app)` unconditionally. (2) If the instrumentator `__init__.py` change (in this PR) did not remove the old dev-gated `server_info` registration, `/server_info` would be registered twice under dev mode, causing a duplicate-route/ambiguous-routing situation. Even if removed, having two identical `/server_info` implementations in the tree is a maintenance hazard. (3) Verify `instrumentator/__init__.py` no longer registers `server_info_router`, and delete the now-dead `instrumentator/server_info.py` to avoid drift between the two copies.

(1) The new dev routers (`dev/cache`, `dev/rlhf`, `dev/rpc`, `dev/sleep`, `dev/server_info`) dropped the per-router `if not envs.VLLM_SERVER_DEV_MODE: return` self-guard that existed in the parent `attach_router` functions; the guard is now centralized in `build_app` (`if envs.VLLM_SERVER_DEV_MODE: register_vllm_dev_api_routers(app)`). (2) This is correct for the single caller in `api_server.py`, but any other code path that imports these `attach_router`/routers directly (e.g. tests, external tooling, or the rust frontend server that also builds routers) would now attach dev endpoints without checking dev mode, since the safety guard moved out of the router module. (3) Confirm `register_vllm_dev_api_routers` is the sole entry point for these routers; if not, keep a defensive dev-mode check or document that callers are responsible for gating.

(1) The docs (`docs/serving/online_serving/README.md`) advertise `/init_weight_transfer_engine` and `/update_weights` and `/get_world_size` under dev mode, but omit the also-present `/start_weight_update` and `/finish_weight_update` routes defined in `dev/rlhf/api_router.py`. (2) Incomplete endpoint documentation for RL weight-transfer workflows may confuse users integrating the RLHF API. (3) Add the `/start_weight_update` and `/finish_weight_update` endpoints to the Weight Transfer APIs section of the docs.

(1) The new guard block accesses `draft_embed = self.model.model.embed_tokens` unconditionally, but the immediately following existing block (that actually deletes/shares the embedding) is guarded with `if hasattr(self.model.model, "embed_tokens")`, implying `embed_tokens` may not always exist on `self.model.model`. (2) If a draft model reaches this path without an `embed_tokens` attribute, the new code raises AttributeError during initialization instead of gracefully skipping the share, breaking Eagle3 init for such models — the opposite of the PR's intent. (3) Wrap the new dimension check in the same `hasattr(self.model.model, "embed_tokens")` guard, or fetch `draft_embed` only after confirming the attribute exists.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run tests/models/test_initialization.py test_can_initialize_large_subset across the Eagle3 draft-model variants (including any without a top-level embed_tokens) and confirm initialization succeeds.

findings.claim: (1) The dimension-mismatch guard is duplicated conceptually with the existing weight-equality/isinstance check above it (both use the same isinstance-on-.weight pattern) and both feed the same `share_embeddings` flag. (2) Two adjacent isinstance/shape checks separated by an `if share_embeddings:` block add indirection and make the control flow harder to follow; a future edit could easily update one branch and not the other. (3) Consider folding the width comparison into the existing weight-equality check so the eligibility decision is made in a single place.

(1) The diff removes the `try/except ModuleNotFoundError` guard around the humming imports and replaces it with `if current_platform.is_cuda():`, with no `else` branch. On non-CUDA platforms (CPU, ROCm/HIP) the module-level names `DataType`, `HummingMethod`, `quantize_weight`, `BaseWeightSchema`, `HummingWeightSchema`, `BaseInputSchema`, `HummingInputSchema`, and the fused-moe experts (`HummingGroupedExperts`, etc.) are never defined. Since `humming.py` is still lazily importable via `get_quantization_config('humming')` (its class bodies only reference these names inside method bodies / string annotations), importing succeeds but any subsequent use path (e.g. `HummingLinearMethod.create_weights`, `HummingLayerQuantizationConfig.from_config` -> `BaseWeightSchema.from_config`, `HummingMethod.*`) raises a raw `NameError` on a non-CUDA platform. (2) Previously `HummingMethod = None` plus `assert_humming_available()` produced a clear, actionable error message; that path is now deleted, so users on non-CUDA (or a CUDA box where the wheel silently failed to install) get a cryptic `NameError`/`ModuleNotFoundError` instead. (3) Keep a fallback: either wrap the CUDA-gated import in try/except and re-introduce an availability assert, or add an explicit guard in `HummingConfig.__init__`/`get_quant_method` that raises a friendly 'humming is only supported on CUDA / please install humming-kernels' message.

(1) `assert_humming_available()` and its call site in `HummingConfig.__init__` were deleted, removing the only place that verified humming was importable before use. Combined with removing the `except ModuleNotFoundError` fallback, a missing/broken humming install (e.g. the git-based wheel failing to build, or a CUDA-major/extra mismatch) now surfaces as an unguarded import error deep in the import machinery instead of the previous descriptive message telling the user how to install it. (2) This degrades the failure mode for a dependency that is installed from a version-pinned wheel and can realistically be absent. (3) Retain an availability check (try/except around the imports setting a sentinel, and assert in `__init__`) so the friendly install guidance is preserved.

(1) `humming-kernels[cu13]==0.1.0` is added only to `requirements/cuda.txt`, and `setup.py` only rewrites the extra to `[cu12]` for `cuda_major == '12'`; there is no handling/exclusion for ROCm (`_is_hip()`) or CPU builds. (2) This is consistent with the module now being CUDA-gated, but because humming becomes a hard requirement pulled into CUDA wheels, verify the `humming-kernels` wheel actually publishes both `cu12`/`cu13` extras for the pinned `0.1.0`; otherwise CUDA-12 or CUDA-13 installs will fail at dependency-resolution time. (3) Confirm both extras exist for 0.1.0 and that the pin matches the git source the removed error message referenced (inclusionAI/humming), to avoid an install-time mismatch.

(1) `_pad_shared_expert_weight` computes the scale-axis pad using `mult = tp_size * step` with `step = 1` for `weight_scale_inv`, but the corresponding weight axis is padded with `step = block_size[0]` (e.g. 128). The padded weight's intermediate axis becomes a multiple of `tp_size * 128`; the number of scale rows must equal `padded_intermediate / block_size[0]`. Rounding the scale axis up to a multiple of `tp_size` (step=1) only yields a consistent count if the *unpadded* scale count is exactly `unpadded_intermediate / block_size[0]` AND cdiv rounding lands on the same shard count for both. If the checkpoint's scale tensor is not exactly `intermediate/128` rows (e.g. it is itself padded to blocks), the weight and scale can be padded to inconsistent shard counts, producing a scale/weight row-count mismatch inside the block-FP8 linear. (2) Risk: silent wrong dequantization or a load-time shape assertion failure at high TP (this is the TEP=16 case the PR targets). (3) Fix: derive the scale pad from the already-padded weight intermediate size (padded_intermediate // block_size[0]) rather than independently cdiv-ing the raw scale row count, guaranteeing weight and scale agree on the per-rank block count.

(1) The padding in `DeepseekV4MLP.__init__` rounds `intermediate_size` up so the merged/row-parallel linear splits evenly across TP, and `_pad_shared_expert_weight` zero-pads the loaded checkpoint tensors to match. However `down_proj` (RowParallelLinear over the intermediate dim) sums partial results across TP ranks with an allreduce. Zero-padded rows on trailing ranks contribute zero to gate/up outputs, and after SiLU(0)*0 = 0 the padded activation channels are zero, so down_proj's contribution from padded input columns is zero — this is only correct if the down_proj weight columns aligned with the padding are also zero (they are, via the same pad). Confirm the activation for padded gate/up channels is exactly zero and not NaN: SiLU(0)=0 but if the gate weight pad interacts with a nonzero up pad the product is 0; verify no bias is added. Since bias=False and pads are zero this appears correct, but the down_proj input scale/quantization of zero-padded activation channels should be validated — block-FP8 activation quantization over a block containing only zeros can yield a zero or degenerate scale. (2) Risk: degenerate per-block activation scale (0 or inf) on padded blocks could poison a shared 128-wide quant block that also contains real channels at the boundary. (3) Fix: add a test asserting shared-expert output matches the unpadded reference at TEP=16, and confirm padded channels never share a quant block with real channels (pad is a whole multiple of block_size[0], so blocks are fully-zero — document/assert this).

(1) The padding logic in `DeepseekV4MLP.__init__` uses `is_sequence_parallel` to gate, while the loader `_pad_shared_expert_weight` gating in `DeepseekV4Model.load_weights` uses `self.parallel_config.use_sequence_parallel_moe`. These two conditions must be exactly equivalent for the module-side padded shapes to agree with the loader-side padded tensors. If `is_sequence_parallel` for the shared-expert MLP is derived from something other than `use_sequence_parallel_moe` (e.g. a per-layer flag), the module will allocate padded params while the loader leaves the weight unpadded (or vice versa), causing a load-time shape mismatch. (2) Risk: shape-mismatch load failure or silently wrong sharding when the two SP conditions diverge. (3) Fix: derive both gates from a single source of truth (pass the same boolean into both, or compute `pad_shared_expert` from the exact predicate used to construct the shared-expert MLP).

(1) `_pad_shared_expert_weight` matches scale tensors by `name.endswith("weight_scale_inv")`, but the WeightsMapper renames `.shared_experts.w2` -> `.shared_experts.down_proj` (seen at parent line ~1209). If the incoming `name` for the scale is e.g. `...down_proj.weight_scale_inv` the endswith check still works, but the dim selection uses `".down_proj." in name` to pick dim=1 — this must fire for the scale tensor too. Verify the scale weight names contain `.down_proj.` at the point `_pad_shared_expert_weight` is called (i.e. after any name remapping). If the remap happens later, the down-proj scale would be padded on dim 0 instead of dim 1. (2) Risk: down-proj scale padded on the wrong axis -> wrong per-rank scale shape -> load failure or wrong dequant. (3) Fix: assert the name-to-dim mapping against the actual pre-remap checkpoint names, or key off the stacked/expert mapping rather than substring matching.

(1) `TE_DType_To_Torch` is now built by inverting `TE_DType` (`{value: key for key, value in TE_DType.items()}`), and its keys are the new Python `DType` IntEnum members, but existing quantized tensors store `_fp8_dtype` as the pybind `tex.DType` enum (e.g. `dst._fp8_dtype = self.dtype` where quantizers may still carry `tex.DType`). Callsites like `float8_tensor_storage.py:40` and `float8_blockwise_tensor_storage.py:205/277` do `TE_DType_To_Torch[tensor._fp8_dtype]`. A dict keyed by `DType` looked up with a `tex.DType` key only succeeds if `hash(tex.DType.kX) == hash(DType.kX)` AND cross-type equality holds. (2) If the pybind enum's `__hash__` does not equal the int hash of its value, this becomes a silent `KeyError` on the dequantize path for any tensor that stored a `tex.DType` — breaking backward compatibility for existing/serialized tensors and any caller still passing `tex.DType`. (3) Verify pybind `tex.DType.__hash__` matches `int.__hash__(value)` (the new `DType.__hash__` returns `int.__hash__`); if not guaranteed, normalize keys via `DType.cast(...)` at lookup sites, or make `TE_DType_To_Torch` accept both enum types.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Round-trip test: create a Float8Tensor with `_fp8_dtype = tex.DType.kFloat8E4M3` (pybind enum) and call `.dequantize()`, asserting `TE_DType_To_Torch[tensor._fp8_dtype]` resolves without KeyError; repeat with the new `DType` enum.

findings.claim: (1) `DType.__eq__` only special-cases `isinstance(other, tex.DType)` and otherwise delegates to `int.__eq__`. Comparisons against a bare Python `int` will therefore return True (since IntEnum == int by value), which may be intended, but comparisons where `other` is `NotImplemented`-returning types are not handled symmetrically; more importantly `__eq__` does `other.cast<int>()` on the pybind side unconditionally in pybind.cpp. (2) The pybind `__eq__`/`__ne__` registered in pybind.cpp calls `other.cast<int>()` with no type guard — comparing `tex.DType.kX == some_non_int_object` (e.g. `== None` or `== torch.float32`) will raise a `cast_error`/TypeError instead of returning `False`/`NotImplemented`, breaking the Python data-model contract that `==` never raises. This can crash previously-working code that compares a dtype against an arbitrary object. (3) Wrap the `other.cast<int>()` in a try/`py::isinstance<py::int_>` check and return `py::cast(false)` / `Py_NotImplemented` when `other` is not int-like.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Assert `(tex.DType.kFloat8E4M3 == None) is False` and `(tex.DType.kFloat8E4M3 == torch.float32) is False` do not raise.

findings.claim: (1) `MakePythonDType` builds a per-value cache by iterating `dtype_cls` members and indexing `members[value]`, guarded by `NVTE_CHECK(idx < num_dtypes)` where `num_dtypes = kNumTypes`. If the C++ enum has enumerators (e.g. kInt64, or an 8-bit E8M0 scale type) that are NOT mirrored in the Python `DType` (the Python enum in constants.py lists only 8 members), then for those DType values `cache[idx]` stays a default-constructed (null) `py::object` and the second `NVTE_CHECK(static_cast<bool>(member))` fires at runtime. (2) The import-time assertion in constants.py checks `{m.name for m in DType} == set(tex.DType.__members__)`, which would catch a mismatch — but only if constants.py is imported before any C++ path calls `MakePythonDType`. The C++ `typeToNumBits` switch references `kInt64`, suggesting the C++ enum may have more members than the Python enum, which would make the assertion fail at import (fail-fast) — confirm the two enums truly match. (3) Confirm the Python `DType` enumerates every `tex.DType` member (including any int64/scale types) so the import assertion passes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Import `transformer_engine.pytorch` and assert no AssertionError from the DType-sync check; enumerate `tex.DType.__members__` vs `DType` members.

findings.claim: (1) `MakePythonDType` uses a function-local static `std::array` initialized via a lambda that imports the Python module and iterates members. This is a good caching pattern, but the initializer acquires Python attributes/imports; the 'magic static' guarantees thread-safe one-time init, yet if first invocation happens without the GIL held it is undefined. (2) Any of the `bulk_allocate_*` call paths in cast.cpp now call `MakePythonDType(fp8_dtype)` per tensor in a loop — the cache makes this cheap (array index + refcount), so no per-iteration import happens; this is fine. Note only to ensure `MakePythonDType` is always called with the GIL held (it manipulates py::object). (3) Add an assertion/comment that the GIL must be held, or document the invariant.

(1) The new `if mla_rotary_interleaved is None: mla_rotary_interleaved = config.multi_latent_attention` block introduces a `None`-sentinel semantics for `mla_rotary_interleaved`, but the parameter's signature default is still `mla_rotary_interleaved: bool = False` (unchanged in this diff). Therefore the new branch is only reached when a caller explicitly passes `None`; all existing callers that omit the argument still get `False`, so the auto-derivation from `config.multi_latent_attention` never actually fires for them. (2) This means the intended fix (defaulting MLA-style interleaving on for MLA configs) is silently inert for the common call path, and if a caller does start passing `None` the semantics diverge from `False` in a non-obvious way. (3) Change the signature default to `mla_rotary_interleaved: Optional[bool] = None` so the sentinel logic is actually exercised, and update the type annotation/docstring accordingly.

(1) `config.multi_latent_attention` is read to derive `mla_rotary_interleaved`, but `_apply_rotary_pos_emb_bshd` and `_apply_rotary_pos_emb_thd` previously treated `multi_latent_attention` as a deprecated alias for `mla_rotary_interleaved` (they emit a DeprecationWarning). Verify that `config.multi_latent_attention` (a TransformerConfig field) semantically maps to MLA-style RoPE interleaving; conflating the deprecated argument name with the config field name could silently enable/disable interleaving for non-MLA models that happen to set the config flag. (2) Wrong interleaving flips even/odd rotary pairing, silently corrupting attention scores and degrading training convergence for affected configs. (3) Confirm the mapping is intended and add a shape/semantics assertion or comment; ensure only MLA rope paths flip interleaving.

(1) The rewritten CASE 2 replaces the previous single fused `_apply_rotary_pos_emb_bshd` call over the concatenated packed tensor with a Python loop that calls `_apply_rotary_pos_emb_bshd` once per packed sequence and does a per-sequence `output.narrow(...).copy_(...)`. (2) For packs containing many short sequences this multiplies kernel-launch overhead (cos/sin computation, rotate_half, cat) by the number of sequences and adds one extra copy per sequence in a hot training path, hurting throughput. (3) If per-sequence correctness can instead be achieved by building the per-sequence freq slices and concatenating them (as CASE 1 does) and applying RoPE once to the whole packed tensor, prefer that fused form; otherwise document why per-sequence application is required.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark _apply_rotary_pos_emb_thd (CASE 2) with cu_seqlens containing many short sequences (e.g. 64 sequences of length 128) vs a single long sequence, comparing wall time and kernel launches before/after this change.

findings.claim: (1) `total_seqlen = int(cu_seqlens[-1].item())` introduces a `.item()` call (host-device sync) that runs on every RoPE application in the training loop; the prior code compared `freqs.size(0) == cu_seqlens[-1]` (tensor comparison) without a forced sync via `.item()`. (2) `.item()` forces a CUDA sync each forward pass, which can measurably reduce throughput and breaks CUDA-graph capture if cu_seqlens is on GPU. (3) Compute `total_seqlen` from a CPU-resident cu_seqlens if available, or compare against `freqs.size(0)` using tensor ops without `.item()`; if cu_seqlens is already on CPU this is minor, so verify device.

(1) The guard `if ensure_ec_transfer_shutdown is not None:` is copied from the KV-transfer pattern, but the accompanying comment (line 1101 "has_kv_transfer_group can be None during interpreter shutdown") explains that the module-level *name* can become None during interpreter teardown. `ensure_ec_transfer_shutdown` is a plain module-level function imported at the top of the file, so the `is not None` check is only meaningful as protection against the module globals being torn down during interpreter shutdown (same rationale as the KV case). (2) This is harmless but the reason it works is non-obvious and there is no explanatory comment for the EC branch. (3) Consider relying on the shared comment above the KV block or adding a brief note so a future reader does not think the guard is dead code and remove it.

(1) In `Scheduler.shutdown()` both `self.connector.shutdown()` (KV) and now `self.ec_connector.shutdown()` are invoked at scheduler-process level, while `gpu_worker.py` also calls `ensure_ec_transfer_shutdown()` at worker-process level. Confirm the EC connector's `shutdown()` is idempotent / safe to call once per role, since scheduler-side and worker-side connectors are distinct instances (roles SCHEDULER vs WORKER) — the base class default returns None so this is fine for the base, but any concrete subclass overriding `shutdown()` must be safe when only one side has a live connector. (2) A non-idempotent or role-unaware shutdown in a subclass could raise or double-free during teardown. (3) Document in the base `shutdown()` docstring that it may be called on either role and should be idempotent.

(1) In `OpenVLAForActionPrediction.__init__`, `self.n_action_bins = config.n_action_bins` and `self.image_token_id = config.image_token_index` are stored as instance attributes, but only `self.image_token_id` and `self.num_patches` have any read path; `self.n_action_bins` is never read anywhere in the model (no action-decoding/de-tokenization logic exists — this is a generative wrapper that just returns LM logits). (2) An unused config-derived attribute is dead weight and suggests the action-prediction post-processing that the `ForActionPrediction` name implies was not actually wired in, which can mislead users expecting discretized-action outputs. (3) Either wire `n_action_bins` into an action de-tokenization path or drop the attribute and document that only raw token generation is supported.

(1) `OpenVLAConfig.image_token_index` defaults to `32000`, and `_get_prompt_updates` uses `hf_config.image_token_index` as the placeholder token id that gets scattered with image embeddings; the base OpenVLA/Llama-2 tokenizer vocab is 32000 (or 32064 with added tokens), so an id of 32000 may be out-of-vocab for the embedding table. The framework handles OOV via `_has_oov_mm_tokens`, but this only works if `configure_mm_token_handling` is invoked with this id. (2) If the id is silently in-vocab-but-wrong or the OOV path is not configured, image placeholder tokens would be embedded from a real vocab row and produce corrupted/incorrect vision-token merges. (3) Verify `image_token_index=32000` matches the actual `openvla/openvla-7b` config and that OOV masking is configured for this token id; add a shape/vocab assertion at merge time.

(1) `PrismaticVisionBackbone.forward` calls `get_intermediate_layers(pixels, n={num_dinov2_blocks - 2})` passing `n` as a *set* containing a single index. This matches the upstream Prismatic/OpenVLA convention (timm interprets a set/collection of block indices), but it is subtle and easy to misread as a typo for `n=num_blocks-2` (which would mean 'last N-2 layers'). (2) A future refactor could 'fix' the braces and silently change which transformer block's features are extracted, corrupting all vision features. (3) Add a short comment explaining that `n` is deliberately a set of block indices selecting the second-to-last block, matching the reference implementation.

(1) In `to_rgb_image` / `preprocess_openvla_image` (processor), image inputs that are torch tensors call `.detach().cpu().numpy()`. If GPU tensors are ever passed as image inputs this forces a device-to-host sync per image. (2) In the normal preprocessing path this runs on the host anyway, so impact is limited, but a GPU tensor input would introduce an avoidable sync in the input pipeline. (3) This is acceptable for CPU-side preprocessing; only flag if image inputs can originate on GPU — consider documenting that image inputs are expected on CPU.

(1) `PrismaticVisionBackbone.forward` and `_process_image_input` both guard `if self.dinov2_featurizer is None: raise RuntimeError(...)`, but `dinov2_featurizer` is unconditionally created via `timm.create_model(...)` in `__init__` and can never be None. (2) Dead defensive checks add confusion about the possible states of the module. (3) Remove the impossible `is None` checks, or make the field genuinely optional if that was the intent.

(1) `get_num_image_tokens` and `get_mm_max_tokens_per_item` in `OpenVLAProcessingInfo` ignore their `image_width`/`image_height` / `seq_len`/`mm_counts` arguments and always recompute from `image_sizes[0]` (fixed 224). This is correct because OpenVLA only supports 224x224 (enforced in the backbone), but the signature suggests variable-size support. (2) Minor: a reader may assume dynamic sizing works. (3) Add a brief note that image size is fixed, or `assert image_width == image_height == image_sizes[0]` to catch unexpected inputs.

In vllm/model_executor/layers/linear.py QKVParallelLinear.weight_loader_v2 (~line 1120), the new branch `if isinstance(param, BlockQuantScaleParameter):` calls `adjust_block_scale_shard(weight_block_size, shard_size, shard_offset)` but retrieves `weight_block_size = getattr(self, "weight_block_size", None)`. QKVParallelLinear does not set a `weight_block_size` attribute on `self` (the block size lives on the quant_config, e.g. `self.quant_config.weight_block_size`), so `getattr` returns None. `adjust_block_scale_shard` then hits `assert weight_block_size is not None` and raises, breaking block-FP8 loading for QKV of MiMo-V2 / any block-quant model. Compare with the existing correct usages elsewhere in this file (lines 815/964/1169) which resolve the block size from the quant config. Fix: retrieve the block size the same way the sibling branches do (e.g. from self.quant_method/quant_config.weight_block_size) rather than a non-existent self attribute.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a block-FP8 quantized MiMoV2Pro checkpoint and verify QKV scale shards load without assertion error.

findings.claim: In vllm/config/model.py __post_init__, the assignment `self.model_arch_config = self.get_model_arch_config()` was moved to execute immediately after `self.hf_text_config = get_hf_text_config(...)` and now precedes the `get_hf_image_processor_config(...)` call (previously it ran after both). If `get_model_arch_config()` (or the arch-config convertors it invokes) depends on `self.hf_image_processor_config` or on side effects of the image-processor config fetch, this reordering silently changes behavior. Why it matters: for multimodal models like MiMo-V2.5-Omni this could produce a subtly wrong arch config. Fix: confirm get_model_arch_config has no dependency on hf_image_processor_config; if the reorder is only to make model_arch_config available earlier, add a comment documenting the ordering requirement, otherwise revert.

In vllm/config/speculative.py hf_config_override, the two new MiMo-V2 branches unconditionally do `hf_config.update({"num_hidden_layers": 0, ...})`. For the MiMoV2FlashForCausalLM / MiMoV2Pro / Omni cases the comment says 'vLLM currently supports only the first MiMo-V2 MTP layer' and hardcodes n_predict from module-level constants rather than reading `num_nextn_predict_layers` from the checkpoint (as every other branch does). If a future checkpoint ships more MTP layers, num_nextn_predict_layers will be forced to the constant, silently ignoring the model's declared value. Consider reading the config value with the constant as a fallback/cap, and add a TODO documenting the single-layer limitation.

In vllm/model_executor/models/mimo_audio.py AudioRotaryEmbedding.forward, `self.inv_freq[:, None].float().expand(-1, 1)` and the subsequent freq/emb/cos/sin construction run on every forward call. For the audio encoder path this recomputes RoPE tables per invocation. Since this is ported encoder code (typically run once per audio clip rather than per decode step) impact is limited, but if invoked in a hot loop consider caching cos/sin for repeated position_ids. Verify call frequency before optimizing.

(1) `_set_torch_accelerator_to_noop()` permanently reassigns the process-global `torch.accelerator.synchronize` and `torch.accelerator.empty_cache` to no-ops with no restoration, unlike the sibling `_torch_cuda_wrapper` context manager which restores `torch.Event`/`torch.cuda.Stream` in a `finally` block. (2) This is a durable, module-wide monkeypatch: once any `CPUModelRunner` is constructed, these two accelerator APIs are silenced for the entire Python process. In a pure single-process CPU deployment this is effectively harmless (the intent is to stop inherited GPU-path methods from throwing at shutdown when no accelerator exists). But it is a fragile global side effect that will silently no-op these calls for any other code sharing the interpreter (e.g. test suites that instantiate a CPUModelRunner alongside other components, or future mixed CPU/accelerator setups), masking real synchronization/cache-release that some code assumes happened. (3) Prefer scoping the patch to the inherited call sites that actually need it (e.g. via a context manager mirroring `_torch_cuda_wrapper`, or overriding `_sync_device`/the specific shutdown method on CPUModelRunner) rather than a permanent global reassignment; if a global patch is truly required, add a comment documenting that it is intentionally never restored.

(1) The helper is named `_set_torch_accelerator_to_noop` but it only overrides two specific functions (`synchronize` and `empty_cache`), not the accelerator API generally. (2) The broad name overstates the scope of the effect and could mislead a reader into thinking all accelerator calls are neutralized, when in fact device-count / current-device / memory-stats calls still hit the real API. (3) Consider a more precise name such as `_disable_accelerator_sync_and_empty_cache`, or a short comment enumerating exactly which functions are patched.

(1) The shared helper `make_valid_python` now performs an extra `ast.parse` of the candidate and returns `None` if parsing fails, the body is empty, or any `ast.Set` node is present. This helper is used not only by the new LFM2 parser but also by the existing `pythonic_tool_parser`, `llama4_pythonic_tool_parser`, and `olmo3_tool_parser` streaming paths. (2) This silently changes streaming behavior for those existing models: inputs that previously round-tripped (returned a completed string) may now return `None`, causing the streaming loop to skip the chunk (`return None`) instead of emitting a partial tool delta. A pythonic call argument that is legitimately a Python set literal (e.g. `foo(x={1,2})`) will now be rejected as `None` rather than raising the previous `UnexpectedAstError`, altering the failure mode. (3) Verify the added validation is intended to apply to all pythonic parsers; if the Set/parse guard is LFM2-specific, gate it behind a flag or move it into the LFM2 parser rather than the shared utility.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run existing pythonic/llama4/olmo3 tool-parser streaming tests against the modified make_valid_python to confirm no previously-passing streaming case now returns None.

findings.claim: (1) In `extract_tool_calls_streaming`, `withheld_suffix = added_text[:-2]` and `added_text[-2]` are indexed when `new_call_complete` is False. `new_call_complete` is False only when `index == len(tool_calls)-1` and `")]" in added_text`, which guarantees `len(added_text) >= 2`, so the index access is safe today. (2) The safety is implicit and coupled to the exact `")]"` substring check; a future change to `make_valid_python`'s `added_text` construction could silently make `added_text` shorter and trigger an IndexError in the streaming hot path. (3) Add an explicit `len(added_text) >= 2` guard or an assertion documenting the invariant, mirroring the fragile pattern copied from `pythonic_tool_parser`.

(1) In `_extract_tool_call_text`, the incomplete branch (`end_idx == -1`) returns `content_before` stripped but does NOT strip or echo-clean the returned `tool_text`, whereas the complete branch strips and runs `_strip_echo` on trailing content. (2) For a still-streaming/truncated non-streaming output this asymmetry is minor but could pass whitespace-padded tool text to the downstream `ast.parse`/regex, which the caller re-strips anyway. (3) Low impact; consider documenting that the incomplete branch intentionally defers stripping to the caller for consistency.

(1) The trailing-echo suppression heuristic in `extract_tool_calls_streaming` relies on instance state `self._trailing_emitted` accumulated across deltas. If a single `Lfm2ToolParser` instance were ever reused across multiple requests (rather than instantiated per-request), stale `_trailing_emitted` from a prior response would corrupt the trailing-content prefix comparison. (2) vLLM currently instantiates tool parsers per request so this is not triggered today, but the state is unguarded and undocumented. (3) Document the per-request lifetime assumption for `_trailing_emitted`, or reset it defensively when no tool block is present.

(1) The FLASHINFER parametrize cases are gated on `CudaPlatform is not None`, so on ROCm only the two FLASH_ATTN cases remain, yet the test body was generalized to `platform = CudaPlatform or RocmPlatform` and now patches `current_platform` with `RocmPlatform()`. (2) This means on ROCm the test asserts that requesting `FLASH_ATTN` returns a backend whose `get_name() == 'FLASH_ATTN'` under RocmPlatform; if ROCm's selector maps/falls back FLASH_ATTN to a ROCm-specific name (e.g. ROCM_ATTN) or FA3 is unsupported (as another test in this file explicitly skips), the assertion could fail or the test could silently pass for the wrong reason. (3) Confirm on ROCm hardware that FLASH_ATTN selection under RocmPlatform yields exactly 'FLASH_ATTN', or gate the FLASH_ATTN causal/non-causal expectations per-platform the same way FLASHINFER was gated.

(1) The new boolean parameter `is_extra` on `_pack_fp8_ds_mla_cache`/`_read_fp8_ds_mla_cache` switches the fp8 dtype to `torch.float8_e4m3fn` (vs `current_platform.fp8_dtype()`, which is e4m3fnuz on ROCm) but its name describes the caller ('extra cache') rather than what it controls (the fp8 encoding). (2) A reader cannot tell from the name that this changes numerical encoding, and future callers may pass the wrong value, silently packing the extra cache in the wrong fp8 format and masking or introducing kernel mismatches. (3) Rename to something describing the behavior (e.g. `force_e4m3fn` / `use_ocp_fp8`) and/or add a comment explaining why the extra cache must use e4m3fn while the main cache uses the platform fp8 dtype.

(1) `QDTYPES` was simplified from an explicit `[None, torch.float8_e4m3fnuz]` on ROCm to `[None, current_platform.fp8_dtype()]`. This is only equivalent if `current_platform.fp8_dtype()` returns `float8_e4m3fnuz` on all ROCm archs the CI targets; note this PR adds mi325/mi355 (gfx950) targets where the platform fp8 dtype may now resolve to OCP e4m3fn instead of e4m3fnuz. (2) If the resolved dtype differs from what the triton kernel path was validated against, the fp8 quantized attention test could either exercise an untested code path or fail. (3) Verify `current_platform.fp8_dtype()` returns the intended fp8 variant on each newly-enabled AMD arch (gfx942 vs gfx950), since that is the behavioral point of this simplification.

(1) The PR removes `_set_visual_token_mask` and its call in `embed_input_ids` from `SkyworkR1VChatModel`, but leaves behind `self.visual_token_mask = None` in `__init__` (parent line 203) and the guard block in `forward` (parent lines 420-422): `if self.visual_token_mask is not None: forward_kwargs.update({"visual_token_mask": ...}); self.visual_token_mask = None`. Since `_set_visual_token_mask` is now gone and nothing reassigns `self.visual_token_mask`, this attribute is permanently `None` and the forward branch is dead code. (2) Impact: harmless at runtime but leaves confusing dead code and an inconsistency with the sibling `internvl.py`, which fully removed the analogous `self.visual_token_mask = None` init and the forward guard block. (3) Fix: also delete `self.visual_token_mask = None` from `SkyworkR1VChatModel.__init__` and the `if self.visual_token_mask is not None:` block from `SkyworkR1VChatModel.forward`, matching the cleanup done in internvl.py.

(1) The old `nvtx_decorator` returned the *bare* undecorated `func` when NVTX profiling was disabled (`if _nvtx_enabled and HAVE_NVTX: return nvtx.annotate(...)(func); return func`).
