(1) The torch version auto-detection regex `grep -E '^torch==.+==\s*"ppc64le"'` is brittle and likely fails against the real requirements/cpu.txt line, which is `torch==2.11.0; platform_system == "Darwin" or platform_machine == "ppc64le" or platform_machine == "riscv64"`. If any match does occur, `grep -Eo '\b[0-9\.]+\b'` may emit multiple tokens (e.g. 2.11.0 then trailing platform digits), producing a multi-line/garbled TORCH_VERSION. (2) Impact: the intended dynamic version pinning silently never works, so torch always falls back to the hardcoded default 2.11.0; when the requirements file bumps torch, the Power image will silently build an outdated/mismatched torch. (3) Fix: parse the version robustly, e.g. `grep -E '^torch==' requirements/cpu.txt | grep -q 'ppc64le' && sed -nE 's/^torch==([0-9.]+).*/\1/p' <line>`, or restrict extraction with `head -n1`.

(1) `sed -i.bak -e 's/.*torch.*//g' pyproject.toml requirements/*.txt` blanks every line containing the substring 'torch' across all requirements files, not just the `torch`/`torchvision`/`torchaudio` pins. This will also strip lines like `torchcodec >= 0.14; ...` and any other transitively 'torch'-named dependency. (2) Impact: legitimate dependencies whose names merely contain 'torch' get silently removed from the resolved requirement set, which can produce an image missing runtime deps that only surface at model-serving time. (3) Fix: anchor the sed to the package names actually pre-installed, e.g. `s/^torch\(vision\|audio\)\?[=<>~ ].*//` rather than a broad `.*torch.*`.

(1) TORCHVISION_VERSION defaults to 0.26.0 while TORCH_VERSION defaults to 2.11.0. torchvision 0.26.x is not the compatibility-matched release for torch 2.11.0 (the paired version is typically 0.x that tracks the torch minor), and the script installs torchvision from devpi with `--index-strategy unsafe-best-match`, so a mismatched pin can silently pull an ABI-incompatible wheel. (2) Impact: import-time failures or subtle runtime breakage that only appear after the image is built. (3) Fix: derive TORCHVISION_VERSION from the same source/matrix as TORCH_VERSION, or document the intended pairing explicitly.

(1) `try_install_from_devpi` is named 'try' (implying best-effort) but has no fallback/retry logic and runs under `set -e`; a failed devpi install aborts the whole build. (2) Impact: misleading name suggests graceful degradation that does not exist; a transient devpi outage hard-fails the image build with no fallback to source build. (3) Fix: either rename to `install_from_devpi` to reflect fail-fast behavior, or add an actual fallback path (e.g. source build) matching the 'try' semantics.

(1) The validation now hard-fails (ValueError) on extra_config that the previous code silently tolerated: unknown keys, wrong-typed values (e.g. distributed="yes", concurrency="16"), and non-positive integers (concurrency=0 / memory_limit=0 were previously accepted and written to os.environ). (2) This is a deliberate and desirable bugfix, but it is a real behavior change: any existing training/serving script whose model_loader_extra_config contained a stray key or a value of 0 will now crash at loader construction instead of proceeding. (3) Consider noting this in release notes/changelog and, if a value of 0 was ever a meaningful 'unlimited/default' sentinel for RUNAI_STREAMER_CONCURRENCY / RUNAI_STREAMER_MEMORY_LIMIT, confirm rejecting <=0 does not remove a supported configuration. If 0 was never valid, no code change needed.

(1) The `category` argument is forwarded verbatim to `nvtx_domain.push_range(message=msg, category=category)`, and the test passes a plain string ("zero"). The NVIDIA `nvtx` package generally expects a category that has been registered (via `nvtx.get_category`/`register_category`) or an integer id; passing an arbitrary unregistered string may be silently ignored or behave inconsistently across `nvtx` versions. (2) Impact is limited to profiling annotation quality (this is instrumentation-only code disabled during compile), so no training-correctness risk, but the categorization may not appear as intended in Nsight. (3) Consider registering categories through the domain or documenting that `category` must be a valid nvtx category id/registered name.

(1) `_range_push`/`_range_pop` re-check `getattr(accelerator, 'supports_nvtx_domain', False)` on every instrumented call. Since `supports_nvtx_domain` is a class-level constant, the getattr default fallback is only needed for legacy third-party accelerators. (2) Impact is negligible because this path only runs when `enable_nvtx` is True and not compiling (a profiling-only code path), so throughput is not affected. (3) No change strictly required; noted only for completeness. This is intentional backward-compat handling for accelerators lacking the attribute, so likely should not be changed.

(1) The HWC/CHW disambiguation heuristic `image.shape[-1] in (1, 3, 4)` is ambiguous: a genuine CHW image of shape (C, H, W) whose width W is 1, 3, or 4 pixels will be misclassified as HWC, and (conversely) an HWC image with an atypical channel count (e.g. C=2, or grayscale-with-alpha layouts not in the set) falls through to the CHW branch. Since this function feeds the multimodal placeholder/embedding count used by many model preprocessors (get_image_size is called across ~30 model files), a misclassification produces a placeholder/embedding count mismatch at inference. (2) For the vast majority of real images W >> 4 so the heuristic is safe in practice, but the failure mode is silent and hard to debug. (3) Consider disambiguating more robustly where possible (e.g. threading the known layout from the caller / _get_video_with_metadata, which already knows the source is np.array(PIL.Image)) rather than inferring from shape, or at minimum document the known ambiguous-width limitation.

(1) The inline comment in ImageProcessorItems.get_image_size says 'HWC format (e.g. from np.array(PIL.Image)). PIL images are always channels-last.' which is accurate, but note the VideoProcessorItems.get_frame_size copy references '_get_video_with_metadata' as the source of HWC arrays. The image path comment omits the corresponding provenance for images; keeping the rationale symmetric/precise across the two near-identical blocks aids future maintenance. (2) Minor documentation clarity issue only. (3) Align the two comments and, ideally, note the shape-inference limitation for narrow images.

(1) The commit removes the `#ifdef RISCV_BF16_SUPPORT` guard around `VLLM_DISPATCH_CASE_FLOATING_TYPES`, so BFloat16 is now always dispatched on RISC-V (relying on the FP32-simulation fallback structs when native BF16 is absent). This change is entirely unrelated to the stated commit purpose ('Clamp exp() input to prevent NaN'). (2) It is scope creep that silently enables a new execution path for every CPU kernel using VLLM_DISPATCH_FLOATING_TYPES (activation, attention, fused_moe, layernorm, mla_decode, pos_encoding, shm, dnnl_kernels). Previously BF16 inputs on non-native-BF16 RISC-V would fail the dispatch (loud error); now they execute through the fallback path whose numerical correctness/perf across all these kernels is not exercised by this bugfix. Bundling it under an unrelated bugfix hides it from reviewers and makes bisection harder. (3) Split this dispatch-macro change into its own PR/commit with explicit justification and tests that the FP32-simulation BF16 fallback is correct across the affected kernels, or revert it from this bugfix commit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run all CPU kernels (activation, layernorm, fused_moe, attention) with bfloat16 inputs on a RISC-V target built WITHOUT RISCV_BF16_SUPPORT and compare against a reference (e.g. x86 CPU) for numerical parity.

findings.claim: (1) The exp() clamp fix is correct and well-reasoned: for -inf input, x_scaled = -inf, the fp->int conversion of -inf plus the biased_exp max(.,0) clamp yields scale=0, while r = x_scaled - n_float becomes inf-inf = NaN, giving NaN*0 = NaN; clamping the input to [ln(FLT_MIN), ln(FLT_MAX)] fixes this. (2) Minor: the two exp() bodies (FP32Vec8 and FP32Vec16) now duplicate identical clamp+poly logic differing only in LMUL/width; the same NaN class could recur in other transcendental helpers. (3) Consider verifying whether erf/other poly helpers that feed exp handle -inf/+inf, and note that tanh() already pre-clamps to [-9,9] so it was safe — the exposed callers are direct exp() users such as softmax.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Unit test exp() on a FP32Vec8/16 filled with -inf, +inf, and large magnitude values; assert exp(-inf)==0, exp(+inf)==+inf (or FLT_MAX-saturated), and no NaN.

findings.claim: (1) The `Auto` renderer resolution needs to map `model_type == "gpt_oss"` to `RendererSelection::Harmony`, but the parent `RendererSelection::resolve` only handles `deepseek_v32`/`deepseek_v4` and otherwise falls through to `Hf`. The new test `auto_uses_harmony_renderer_and_output_processor_for_gpt_oss_model_type` expects `Prompt::TokenIds(_)`, which requires the Harmony renderer to be selected for gpt_oss. If the diff did not add a `gpt_oss` arm and a `HARMONY_LITERAL`/`Harmony` variant to `resolve`, the auto path will silently render gpt_oss with the generic HF template instead of Harmony token IDs, breaking gpt_oss output correctness. (2) This is a silent behavior mismatch: output parsing uses Harmony (via new_chat_output_processor) while prompt rendering would use HF, producing a train/serve prompt mismatch for GPT-OSS. (3) Verify the diff adds a `Self::HARMONY_LITERAL => Self::Harmony` arm plus a `gpt_oss` -> `Harmony` mapping in `resolve`, and that FromStr/Display/EnumIter round-trip covers the new variant.

(1) `HarmonyChatRenderer::new()` resolves the local date once via `env_system_start_date()` at construction and caches it in `options.system_start_date` for the process lifetime. (2) A long-running server constructed before midnight will keep emitting a stale `Current date:` in the Harmony system preamble across day boundaries, which can subtly shift model behavior for date-sensitive prompts and diverge from the Python Harmony path that may recompute per request. (3) Confirm whether the Python reference computes the date per request; if so, compute the date at render time (guarded to avoid per-request syscall cost) or document the intentional pin. Since `VLLM_SYSTEM_START_DATE` can pin it, the caching is likely intentional — low severity.

(1) `harmony_message_text` uses `unreachable!("Harmony parser emits one text content block per parsed message")` on any message whose content is not exactly one `Content::Text`. (2) This is an assumption about the external `openai-harmony` parser's output shape; if a future harmony version emits zero or multiple content blocks (e.g. multimodal or empty final), this panics inside the async event stream and takes down the request/worker rather than returning a `HarmonyOutputParsing` error. (3) Prefer returning `Error::HarmonyOutputParsing` for the unexpected-shape case instead of `unreachable!`. This is pre-existing code moved/kept by the PR, so low priority.

In `EngineCoreClient::abort` (client.rs), the consumer streams are finalized locally (`abort_requests_locally` synthesizes a terminal `Abort` output and removes the request from the registry) *before* the engine round-trip `do_abort_requests`. If `do_abort_requests` subsequently fails (engine send error / transport error), `abort()` returns `Err`, but the caller-facing stream has already been finalized as a clean abort and the request has been removed from the local registry. The engine was never told to abort, so the request continues running (and consuming KV cache / compute) on the engine side with no frontend tracking able to retry the abort — a silent request leak. Consider finalizing streams only after a successful engine round-trip, or documenting/handling the partial-failure case (e.g. re-registering or logging the orphaned internal ids).

`RequestRegistry::abort_many` (state.rs) sets the synthesized output's `engine_index` to `engine_id.engine_index().unwrap_or(0)`. If `engine_index()` ever returns `None` (engine id not derived from an index), the abort output is silently attributed to engine 0, which can misroute the `record_finished`/`request_success{engine=0}` metric for that request. Since the request was successfully registered against a concrete engine, prefer propagating the real engine index (it is known at registration) or asserting it is `Some` rather than defaulting to 0.

The metrics change in `request_metrics.rs` moves the `last_token_ts = batch_timestamp` assignment (and the entire TTFT / is_prefilling / inter-token block) inside a new `if !output.new_token_ids.is_empty()` guard. This is correct for the synthesized zero-token abort output, but it also changes behavior for any *legitimate* engine output that carries no new tokens (e.g. a terminal `finish_reason` output with no final token, or a chunked-prefill intermediate step that emits stats/events but no tokens): previously such outputs advanced `last_token_ts` and could flip `is_prefilling`, now they do not. This alters `decode_time_seconds`, `inter_token_latency`, and `time_per_output_token` for those cases. If engine-core can emit token-less non-terminal outputs, verify the new timing semantics match Python-frontend behavior; otherwise gate specifically on the synthesized-abort case rather than on token emptiness generally.

`abort_requests_locally` (imp.rs) recomputes `SystemTime::now()` on every call and passes it to `abort_many` as a single timestamp shared by all synthesized outputs. The crate already exposes `current_unix_timestamp_secs()` in `request_metrics.rs` for exactly this wall-clock-seconds purpose (with an `expect` on clock-before-epoch rather than silently coercing to 0.0). Reusing a shared helper would keep the timestamp semantics consistent between the metrics path and the abort path; the current `.unwrap_or(0.0)` on clock error would feed a 0.0 timestamp into `record_finished`, skewing latency histograms, whereas the metrics helper panics loudly. Low impact but worth aligning.

(1) In `MarlinExperts.apply` the call to `fused_marlin_moe(...)` at line 741 already passes `clamp_limit=self.gemm1_clamp_limit` (line 867 in parent), whereas `BatchedMarlinExperts.apply` calling `batched_fused_marlin_moe(...)` did NOT propagate the clamp limit because `batched_fused_marlin_moe` had no `clamp_limit` parameter. This diff correctly plumbs `clamp_limit` through `batched_fused_marlin_moe` into the shared `_fused_marlin_moe`, where it drives the `swiglu_limit_func` clamped-SwiGLU path. Prior to this fix, the batched marlin path silently used the unclamped `activation_func` branch for DSV4/SwiGLU-limit models, producing numerically incorrect activations (missing the clamp on the gate/up projections). (2) This is a correctness/output-quality bug for models requiring swiglu_limit on the batched marlin backend. (3) The fix is correct as written. Verify that both MarlinExperts (non-batched) and BatchedMarlinExperts now take the clamped path when gemm1_clamp_limit is set.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DSV4 (or a gpt-oss/mxfp4 model with a non-None gemm1_clamp_limit) through the batched marlin MoE backend and compare logits/perplexity against the reference (e.g. trtllm/cutlass) backend to confirm the clamped SwiGLU path is now active and numerically matching.

findings.claim: (1) In get_video_replacement, out_mm_kwargs.get("video") is indexed with item_idx and then .get("video_image_sizes").data is read, but out_mm_kwargs is a MultiModalKwargsItems whose per-item elements are MultiModalKwargsItem accessed via out_mm_kwargs["video"][item_idx]. Other models in this codebase (e.g. glm4_1v.py, keye.py, gemma4_mm.py) access it as out_mm_kwargs[modality][item_idx] and then .get(field), never as out_mm_kwargs.get(modality)[item_idx]. Using .get on the modality key may return the sequence, but the subsequent .get("video_image_sizes") returns a MultiModalFieldElem-like object; reading .data must match the actual API. (2) If this access pattern is wrong the new branch either raises or silently returns None and the fix never applies, so the placeholder-count-mismatch crash is not actually fixed. (3) Align the access pattern with the established convention: out_item = out_mm_kwargs["video"][item_idx]; image_sizes = out_item.get("video_image_sizes").data, guarding for None.

(1) The new get_video_replacement branch derives num_frames and frame_size from video_image_sizes but always uses image_sizes[0] (the first frame's size) as the single frame_size passed to get_video_prompt_texts. get_video_prompt_texts multiplies a per-frame placeholder block by num_frames, assuming all frames have identical size. If frames within one video have differing resolutions (which the per-frame recording code explicitly supports by storing one (W,H) per frame), the placeholder count computed from frame[0] will not match the actual visual embedding count produced per-frame by the vision tower, reintroducing the very mismatch this PR fixes. (2) This causes the same placeholder-vs-embedding count crash for heterogeneous-resolution videos. (3) Either assert all frames share the same size, or compute per-frame placeholder blocks summed over the actual per-frame sizes rather than replicating frame[0].

(1) The HWC-vs-CHW frame-shape detection in both minicpmv4_6.py and parse.py uses the heuristic `image.shape[-1] in (1, 3, 4)` to decide HWC. This is ambiguous: a genuinely CHW tensor whose spatial width happens to be 1, 3, or 4 (e.g. a 3-wide slice, or a C=3,H=?,W=3 frame) would be misinterpreted as HWC, swapping H and W. Likewise an HWC frame with C not in {1,3,4} is treated as CHW. (2) Wrong W/H assignment feeds get_sliced_grid / find_best_resize and changes the computed placeholder token count, causing silent wrong prompts or a mismatch crash. (3) Prefer authoritative metadata (channel dimension known from the loader) or a more robust check (e.g. compare shape[0] vs shape[-1] against a channel whitelist with tie-breaking), and add a shape/dtype assertion at this parsing boundary.

(1) The per-frame (W,H) extraction logic in process_videos (minicpmv4_6.py) duplicates almost verbatim the HWC/CHW branching that VideoProcessorItems.get_frame_size / ImageProcessorItems.get_image_size in parse.py now perform. (2) Duplicated shape-parsing logic can drift and be independently buggy (as noted, the heuristic is fragile). (3) Extract a single shared helper (e.g. in parse.py) that returns ImageSize for a PIL/np/tensor frame, and call it from both sites.

(1) frame_sizes builds one torch.tensor per frame in a Python loop and then torch.stack(frame_sizes); for long videos this is many tiny tensor allocations. (2) Minor host-side overhead in the preprocessing path (not the training hot loop, so low impact). (3) Accumulate (w,h) pairs in a plain Python list and construct a single torch.tensor([...], dtype=torch.long) once per video.

(1) `_patch_instrumentator_route_walk` unconditionally replaces the private symbol `prometheus_fastapi_instrumentator.routing._get_route_name` with a full reimplementation, and the docstring notes the bug only affects versions 'up to 8.0.0'. (2) Monkeypatching a private (`_`-prefixed) third-party function is fragile: if a newer instrumentator version fixes this itself or changes `_get_route_name`'s signature/behavior, this patch silently overrides the corrected implementation and can reintroduce the bug or diverge from upstream logic. (3) Guard the patch with a version/attribute check (e.g. only patch when `importlib.metadata.version('prometheus-fastapi-instrumentator')` is in the affected range, or only when `hasattr(_pfi_routing, '_get_route_name')`), and add a TODO to remove once the pinned dependency includes the upstream fix.

(1) The reimplemented `_get_route_name` reproduces upstream's `Mount` recursion (`route_name + child`) and PARTIAL-match logic by hand rather than wrapping the original. (2) Any subtle divergence from the installed library version's implementation (e.g. how PARTIAL matches or nested mounts are handled) would silently mislabel metrics for HTTP requests, since the only added semantic is the `getattr(route,'path',None) is None: continue` skip. (3) Prefer a minimal wrapper that delegates to the original `_get_route_name` and only pre-filters path-less routes, or filter path-less routes at the call site, so the patch tracks upstream logic instead of duplicating it.

The fix slices only the block table to the unpadded batch_size but does not verify the rest of common_attn_metadata (seq_lens, query_start_loc, slot_mapping) is already unpadded/consistent. The comment states the drafter operates on the unpadded batch and cu_seqlens_q has dimension batch_size, which is consistent with the fix (batch_size()==seq_lens.shape[0]). This appears correct: the per-group block tables are stored at num_reqs_padded from the target forward and slicing [:batch_size] aligns them with the drafter's unpadded batch. No correctness issue found, but a shape assertion (e.g. assert self._per_group_block_tables[gid].shape[0] >= batch_size) at the parallelism boundary would guard against the reverse case where the stored table is smaller than batch_size, which would silently produce a short block table rather than an error.

(1) The new `_is_string_type` changes behavior for a property that exists in the schema but has no discernible type info (e.g. `{"description": "..."}` with no `type`/`enum`/`anyOf`). `extract_types_from_schema` returns its fallback `["string"]` in that case, so `set(["string"]) - {"null"} == {"string"}` evaluates True and the value is treated as a raw un-deserialized string. The old implementation returned `arg_type == "string"` with `arg_type=None`, i.e. False (the value would be deserialized). (2) This silently alters parsing for previously-untyped arguments: a numeric/bool value under a typeless property that used to be deserialized to int/bool will now be emitted as a raw string, potentially producing a different JSON arguments payload for the same model output. (3) If the intent is to only force-string when the schema explicitly declares `string`, consider distinguishing the 'no type info' fallback from an explicit `string` type (e.g. have the caller check whether the schema actually declares a type before applying the fallback), or document that typeless properties intentionally default to string handling.

(1) send_profile_cmd builds the downstream URL from urlparse(inst['request_address']).hostname/.port, but request_address is registered as f"http://{data['http_address']}/v1" where http_address may be a bare host without an explicit port (e.g. 'myhost'). In that case urlparse(...).port returns None and the URL becomes 'http://myhost:None/start_profile', which fails to connect. (2) Profiler start/stop would silently fail (or raise) for any instance registered without an explicit port. (3) Prefer deriving the base URL by stripping the known '/v1' suffix from request_address (or storing http_address directly) instead of reconstructing host:port, e.g. build url as request_address.removesuffix('/v1') + f'/{profiler_cmd}_profile', so the original scheme/host/port are preserved verbatim.

(1) The gathered aiohttp responses from session.post(...) are ClientResponse objects that are never used as context managers or explicitly released; only responses[0].json() is read. Connections/response bodies for the other instances are left unread until the session closes. (2) In a toy example this is benign, but it can leak connections and the unread bodies are only cleaned up when the ClientSession __aexit__ runs. (3) Either iterate responses reading/closing each, or use 'async with session.post(...)' per request; at minimum call r.release() for non-first responses.

(1) 'return await responses[0].json()' returns only the first instance's profile response, discarding results/status of the remaining prefill and decode instances (aside from error checks). (2) A caller cannot tell whether all instances actually started/stopped profiling from the returned body. (3) Consider aggregating per-instance results (e.g. a list keyed by instance address) so the proxy response reflects all backends.

(1) For `tool_choice=required` and named-function tool choice, `apply_structural_tag_constraint` returns early with `Ok(())` when `parser.structural_tag_model()` is `None` (the trait default), i.e. for parser families that were NOT updated in this PR (hermes/llama/qwen-json, kimi_k2, glm_xml, minimax_m2, qwen_coder, etc.). In that path no structured-output constraint is installed at all, so the model is free to emit plain text and NOT call any tool. (2) This silently violates the OpenAI `required`/named semantics, which guarantee at least one (or the named) tool call. Before this PR, such requests were rejected outright by `validate_request_compat` with a clear 'not supported yet' error, so users got correct behavior or a clear failure; now they get a request that is accepted but unconstrained — a silent behavioral regression / spec violation. (3) When `tool_choice` is `Required`/`Function` and the resolved parser lacks structural-tag support, either reject the request (as before) or fall back to a JSON-schema/grammar constraint that still forces a tool call, rather than returning `Ok(())` with no constraint.

(1) The entire `tool_choice` validation block (rejecting `required`, named function, and `allowed_tools`) was removed. `allowed_tools` is now handled only by the catch-all `_ => bail_invalid_request!` in `convert_tool_choice`, but `required`/named are now accepted for ALL models regardless of whether a tool parser is resolvable. (2) For a model with no auto-resolvable tool parser, `required`/named now reaches `resolve_tool_parser` and fails with `ParserUnavailableForModel`, whereas before it returned a targeted 'tool_choice=required is not supported yet' message. The error surface/semantics changed for the same input. (3) Confirm this error-path change is intended; consider keeping an explicit validation message for models that cannot support `required`/named tool choice so clients get an actionable error.

(1) `apply_structural_tag_constraint` unconditionally overwrites `request.sampling_params.structured_outputs` with the structural tag, discarding any caller-supplied `json`/`json_object`/grammar guidance (tests confirm this is intentional for Auto+strict and Required). (2) A user who explicitly set a JSON-schema structured output AND `tool_choice=required` will have their schema silently dropped in favor of the tool structural tag; there is no warning or merge. (3) Consider logging when existing structured-output guidance is overwritten, or documenting this precedence explicitly in the public API docs so it is not surprising.

(1) The `ChatToolChoice` enum variant order was reordered (`None` moved before `Auto`) and `Copy` was dropped because of the new `Function { name: String }` variant. Dropping `Copy` is required, but any code relying on `ChatToolChoice` being `Copy` (pass-by-value copies) now silently switches to move semantics. (2) This can cause borrow/move compile changes at call sites; while the compiler catches these, reviewers should confirm no clone-heavy hot path was introduced by the loss of `Copy` on a type compared frequently (e.g. `tool_parsing_enabled` uses `matches!` on a reference, which is fine). (3) No action needed if all call sites use references/`matches!`; just verifying no per-request deep clone of tool_choice.name was introduced in a hot loop.

(1) The PR removes the `arbitrary_precision` feature from `serde_json` at the workspace root (`serde_json = { ... features = ["preserve_order"] }`), which is a global, workspace-wide change affecting every crate that serializes/deserializes JSON numbers, not just the chat renderer. (2) Without `arbitrary_precision`, serde_json numbers are parsed into i64/u64/f64, so original textual spelling and full precision are lost: `1.00`->`1.0`, `1e0`->normalized, and high-magnitude decimals like `9223372036854775807.5` can no longer round-trip verbatim (the parameters.rs and tojson.rs tests were changed/commented out precisely because of this). Any other crate in the workspace that relied on verbatim number fidelity for tool-call arguments or JSON passthrough silently changes behavior. (3) Confirm no other workspace crate depends on arbitrary-precision round-tripping; if the requirement is only local to the chat renderer, prefer scoping the feature change rather than flipping it workspace-wide, and document the precision-loss regression in a changelog/migration note.

(1) `TemplateMap::enumerate` uses `self.mapped_rev_enumerator(...)` which produces a reversed (rev) enumerator over `self.0.keys()`. (2) The stated purpose (docstring) is to preserve original key order via `IndexMap`; if `mapped_rev_enumerator` reverses iteration order relative to insertion order, HF templates iterating `dict.items()` would see keys in reverse of the JSON source order, contradicting the 'preserve the original key order' intent. The added test only checks a 2-3 key case where reversal could still pass if the framework re-reverses internally, so it may not catch an ordering bug. (3) Verify that `mapped_rev_enumerator` yields keys in original insertion order (minijinja's rev-enumerator contract typically expects the closure to yield in reverse so the engine can present forward order) — add a 3+ key ordered-iteration assertion to lock the behavior.

(1) `to_template_value` recursively rebuilds every JSON object/array into `minijinja::Value` via a new `TemplateMap`/IndexMap and `from_object`, allocating a fresh IndexMap and boxed Arc<Object> per nested object on every render call. (2) For requests with large tool-parameter schemas or deeply nested tool-call arguments, this recursive re-allocation runs on each `render()` and adds per-request overhead vs. the previous direct `Value` clone; in a high-throughput serving frontend this is on the hot path. (3) This is likely acceptable for correctness reasons (needed to fix the `.items()` shadowing), but consider whether the conversion could be cached for the static `parameters` schema (which is constant per tool set) rather than reconstructed per request.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark render() throughput with a large tools schema before/after, e.g. 50 tools each with nested object parameters, measuring renders/sec.

findings.claim: (1) prune.py made `--prune-export-config` `required=True` and removed all the previous `--target-*` arguments (`--target-num-layers`, `--target-hidden-size`, etc.) and `--layers-to-drop`. The example driver `examples/post_training/modelopt/prune.sh` (also in this PR) previously built `PRUNE_ARGS` from `TARGET_*`/`LAYERS_TO_DROP` env vars and emitted `--target-num-layers <X>` style flags. (2) If prune.sh was not fully rewritten to emit `--prune-export-config` instead, any invocation via the old env-var path will now hard-fail with 'unrecognized arguments' / 'the following arguments are required: --prune-export-config', breaking all existing user pruning scripts and CI that set `TARGET_NUM_LAYERS=...`. (3) Verify prune.sh no longer references PRUNE_ENV_VARS/PRUNE_ARGS and that any downstream automation and docs referencing `TARGET_*`/`LAYERS_TO_DROP`/`--pruning-scores-path` are migrated; consider keeping a deprecation shim that maps old env vars to the new JSON config.

(1) prune.py removed the `--layers-to-drop` code path entirely (previously handled by `mtp.mcore_minitron.drop_mcore_language_model_layers`) and folded depth pruning into `--prune-export-config {"num_layers": N}`. It also removed `--pruning-scores-path` in favor of `--prune-intermediate-ckpt`. (2) These are removed arguments with no deprecation path; users/scripts passing `--layers-to-drop` or `--pruning-scores-path` will fail with unrecognized-argument errors, and the direct layer-drop semantics (explicit 1-indexed layer list) is no longer expressible via the num_layers target (which prunes by Block-Influence score, not by explicit index). (3) Either retain `--layers-to-drop` as a supported path or document the removal prominently and provide migration guidance in the README (the README currently drops the LAYERS_TO_DROP option silently).

(1) In mmlu.py the `--mmlu-dataset` argument is retained but hidden via `argparse.SUPPRESS`, while the new implementation calls `megatron_mmlu(...)` which takes no dataset argument, so `--mmlu-dataset` is now a silent no-op. Similarly `--disable-tqdm` is kept as a no-op. (2) Users who previously pointed MMLU at a non-default dataset (e.g. a local mirror of cais/mmlu) will silently have that override ignored and evaluate against whatever `megatron_mmlu` hardcodes, potentially producing misleading accuracy numbers rather than an error. (3) Since the value is silently discarded, prefer raising a clear error/warning when `--mmlu-dataset` is set to a non-default value, or wire it through to `megatron_mmlu` if the plugin supports a dataset override.

(1) In prune.py's new `check_arguments`, `args.prune_export_config` is mutated in place from a JSON string to a dict via `args.prune_export_config = json.loads(...)`. (2) This overwrites the parsed string arg with a dict; any later code (or logging/serialization of args, e.g. saved to a checkpoint config or printed) that assumes `prune_export_config` is a string will see a dict instead, which can surprise downstream consumers and makes the arg's type inconsistent with its argparse declaration (`type=str`). (3) Consider storing the parsed dict in a separate attribute (e.g. `args.prune_export_config_dict`) rather than shadowing the string arg, to keep argparse-declared types consistent.

(1) prune.py adds `import gc` (and the README/diff context suggests calibration now loads real datasets). If `gc` is imported to force garbage collection between calibration and pruning but is not actually called (the visible diff does not show a `gc.collect()` use), it is an unused import. (2) Unused imports add noise and can mask intent. (3) Verify `gc` has a real use path (e.g. `gc.collect()` after calibration to free dataloader/activation memory before `mtp.prune`); if not present, either add the intended `gc.collect()` call or remove the import. Use grep to confirm usage in the final file.

(1) New abstract-base method `SecondaryTierManager.get_stats()` is a concrete default returning None (base.py) and is consumed by `TieringOffloadingManager.get_stats()` (manager.py), but the base method `build_metric_definitions` is a plain @classmethod returning {} that is not wired into any enforcement that a tier which emits stats has also declared them. (2) A secondary tier can `increase_counter(name, ...)` in its own OffloadingConnectorStats for a metric it never returned from `build_metric_definitions`; on the consumer side `OffloadPromMetrics.observe()` asserts `key in self._offloading_metric_defs`, so an undeclared metric name from a secondary tier will raise AssertionError at metrics-emission time in the scheduler, killing the stats path rather than being silently dropped. (3) Consider validating in `TieringOffloadingManager.get_stats()`/aggregate that all emitted metric keys are among the declared definitions, or document that tiers MUST declare every metric they emit.

(1) In `SecondaryTierFactory.create_secondary_tier`, `get_tier_class(tier_config)` reads `tier_config.get("type")` and validates it, then the caller separately does `config = tier_config.copy()` and `tier_type = config.pop("type")`, so the 'type' key is looked up twice. (2) This is minor duplicated lookup with two parallel notions of how 'type' is extracted; low risk but slightly redundant. (3) Could have `get_tier_class` return the popped type too, or keep the single-pass flow; behavior is otherwise equivalent to the previous implementation (validation still occurs before instantiation and `config.pop("type")` without default is safe because get_tier_class guarantees a truthy 'type').

(1) `TieringOffloadingManager.get_stats()` defensively handles `stats is None` from the primary tier (`if stats is None: stats = tier_stats`), but the primary `CPUPrimaryTierOffloadingManager` always returns a non-empty stats object (it unconditionally sets the CPU_CACHE_USAGE_PERC gauge), so the initial `if stats is not None and stats.is_empty()` branch and the later `stats is None` branch are effectively dead for the CPU primary tier. (2) Dead defensive branches add slight indirection and can mask assumptions; the accompanying test comment even documents that get_stats() never returns None. (3) Either drop the never-taken branches or add a comment clarifying they exist to guard against future primary-tier implementations that may report empty/None stats.

(1) The refactor drops the distinction between autotune/warmup-candidate compilations and real kernel compilations: the old code chose the event string via `kwargs.get("warmup")` and emitted "autotune/warmup candidate JIT compilation" vs "kernel JIT compilation"; the new `_log_triton_jit_compile` hardcodes `event="kernel JIT compilation"` and never reads `kwargs.get("warmup")`. (2) This is a diagnostic-quality regression: warmup-candidate compilations (which are benign autotuning artifacts) will now be reported identically to genuine post-warmup cache-miss kernel compilations, making the monitor's output more misleading and potentially triggering false alarms in `error` mode. (3) Preserve the warmup-based event selection (or explicitly document in the PR why it was intentionally removed).

(1) `_get_compile_info` returns `kwargs.get("compile")` only when it is a `dict`, but the new formatting helpers (`_format_constants`, `_format_signature`, `_format_extra_compile_info`) all check `isinstance(..., Mapping)` on sub-values — an inconsistency where the top-level check is stricter (`dict`) than the child checks (`Mapping`). (2) Minor: if Triton ever passes a non-dict Mapping for `compile`, it is silently discarded, losing all verbose detail. (3) Consider using `Mapping` consistently for the top-level `compile_info` check as well.

(1) The `lora_info.update(...)` call is placed inside the `if let Some(scheduler_stats) = batch.scheduler_stats.as_ref()` block, so the `vllm:lora_requests_info` gauge is only refreshed on batches that happen to carry scheduler stats. (2) Risk: LoRA request lifecycle events (Queued/Scheduled/Preempted) and request finish/removal are processed on every output batch (via `sender_for_output`/`finish_requests`), but the exported gauge only reflects them when a scheduler_stats batch subsequently arrives. If a LoRA request starts, runs, and finishes between two scheduler_stats emissions, or if scheduler_stats stops arriving, the gauge can carry stale running/waiting adapter sets (or fail to clear a finished adapter) until the next stats batch. This diverges from the registry's actual state and from the Python frontend which updates the gauge on every processing step. (3) Fix: call `lora_info.update(...)` unconditionally per batch (outside the scheduler_stats guard), or on the same cadence the registry state actually changes, so the gauge tracks the frontend's true LoRA tracking rather than the incidental presence of scheduler stats.

(1) `inner.lora_adapter_states()` acquires the request-registry mutex and clones every tracked adapter name into two fresh BTreeSets on every scheduler_stats batch, and `LoraAdapterNames::encode` re-joins the set into a comma string on every Prometheus render. In multi-engine replicas, `update()` is invoked once per engine that reports stats in a tick, each time snapshotting the entire (replica-wide) registry and re-cloning all adapter names, so the same replica-wide series is recomputed and re-written N times per tick. (2) Impact: extra registry-lock contention and String allocations on the hot output-dispatch path scaling with (num engines * num in-flight LoRA requests); largely redundant since all N writes produce the identical series. (3) Fix: consider deriving/exporting the union once per tick rather than once per engine batch, and/or short-circuiting when the snapshot is unchanged before allocating (the `current != next` check in `LoraInfoExporter::update` already helps, but the snapshot allocation happens first).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark run_output_dispatcher_loop throughput with many in-flight LoRA requests across multiple engines, comparing per-engine vs per-tick lora_adapter_states() snapshotting.

findings.claim: (1) In `apply_lora_events`, events are applied in order and the last event wins for `phase`. This matches last-write-wins, but note that a non-LoRA request (lora_name == None) never records phase and is silently skipped — this is intended. However, `apply_lora_events` is called for every output including the terminal one, right before `finished()` triggers removal; the phase update on a finishing output is computed and then immediately discarded when the request is removed. (2) Impact: negligible correctness issue, minor wasted work on the finish path. (3) Fix (optional): skip `apply_lora_events` when `output.finished()` since the state is about to be dropped, or document that the redundant update is intentional for simplicity.

(1) The oneAPI library paths with pinned version numbers (ccl/2021.15, mpi/2021.15, compiler/2025.3) are now hardcoded in three separate places: the new /etc/ld.so.conf.d/oneapi-ccl.conf file, the LD_LIBRARY_PATH ENV, and the test script's runtime source of ccl/2021.15/env/vars.sh. (2) On a future oneAPI/oneCCL version bump these must all be updated in lockstep; missing one leads to silent runtime linker resolution to a stale/absent path and hard-to-debug 'library not found' failures at container run time rather than build time. (3) Consider deriving the CCL version from the existing ONECCL_INSTALLER ARG / a single build ARG and reusing it, or use the setvars-managed 'latest' symlink (already created via ln -s .../2021.15 .../latest) instead of pinning explicit version dirs.

(1) LD_LIBRARY_PATH was changed from appending ($LD_LIBRARY_PATH:/usr/local/lib/) to a fully explicit list that no longer preserves any inherited $LD_LIBRARY_PATH. With the old deep-learning-essentials base image, the base likely pre-set LD_LIBRARY_PATH with additional oneAPI/system runtime paths; the new ubuntu:24.04 base sets none, so dropping the prefix is intended. (2) Risk: if any downstream stage (ucx-nixl-build sets ENV LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/tmp/ucx_install/lib:${LD_LIBRARY_PATH}) or a base-provided path was relied upon, resetting it here could omit a needed directory. Verify the vllm-openai final image still resolves all XPU/oneCCL/UCX shared objects (e.g. via ldd on the built extensions). (3) If confirmed complete, no change needed; otherwise re-append prior paths.

(1) TTFT and total_time are measured from `start = time.perf_counter()` set at the top of `_print_chat_stream`/`_print_completion_stream`, i.e. AFTER `client.chat.completions.create(...)` has already been invoked in the caller. (2) The network round-trip / request submission latency is therefore excluded from the reported 'Time To First Token', so the printed TTFT systematically underestimates the true end-to-end TTFT a user would perceive. (3) Move the `start` timestamp to immediately before the `client.*.create(...)` call in `ChatCommand.cmd` / `CompleteCommand.cmd` (or pass a start time into the printer) so TTFT includes the request submission. Confidence is moderate because with the OpenAI streaming client most latency is incurred during iteration, so the discrepancy may be small.

(1) In `_print_completion_stream` the guard was changed from `if text is not None:` to `if text:`, which now also skips empty-string ('') deltas. (2) This is a behavior change beyond the stats feature: previously an empty-string chunk would still execute `print('', end='', flush=True)` (a harmless no-op) and be appended to output; now empty strings are skipped entirely. The effect is benign (empty content contributes nothing), but it is an unflagged behavioral tweak folded into a feature commit. (3) If intentional (aligning with the chat path's `if delta.content:`), keep it but note it in the commit; otherwise revert to preserve identical behavior. Low severity as output is unaffected.

(1) The standalone module-level `chat()` helper still calls `_print_chat_stream(stream)` without stats and does not request `include_usage`; it was not updated for the new feature. (2) This is fine functionally (stats defaults to False), but it means there are now two divergent chat-streaming code paths (`ChatCommand.cmd` vs `chat()`), increasing maintenance risk of the metrics logic drifting. (3) Consider consolidating `ChatCommand.cmd` and the `chat()` helper, or verify `chat()` is still referenced anywhere; if unused/dead it could be removed.

(1) TPS is computed as `completion_tokens / total_time` where `total_time` spans the entire stream including the prefill/TTFT window. (2) This reports end-to-end throughput rather than decode throughput; users comparing against server-side 'TPS' (which usually excludes prefill, computed over decode time = total_time - ttft) may find the numbers inconsistent. (3) Consider clarifying the label or computing decode TPS as `(completion_tokens) / (total_time - ttft)`; the label 'tokens/s (N tokens in Ts)' is honest but ambiguous about which interval it covers.

(1) `from vllm.platforms import current_platform` binds the value returned by the module-level `__getattr__`, which lazily invokes `resolve_current_platform_cls_qualname()`. This forces eager platform resolution at the moment `vllm/triton_utils/importing.py` is imported, whereas before this module had no dependency on platform detection. (2) Risk: platform detection runs NVML/amdsmi init and plugin loading; forcing it during triton_utils import could change initialization ordering or trigger platform resolution earlier than intended (the __getattr__ docstring explicitly warns that resolving current_platform too early, before OOT plugins are loaded, causes test failures). (3) Safer fix: import the module (`from vllm import platforms`) and reference `platforms.current_platform.is_rocm()` lazily inside the `if HAS_TRITON:` block at use time, rather than binding the resolved value at top-of-module import time.

The `norm.weight` offset condition was silently changed and may now mismatch the actual parameter name, breaking weight loading. In the old `Plamo2ForCausalLM.load_weights`, the RMSNorm offset was applied via `elif "model.norm.weight" in name:` because weights arrived with the `model.` prefix at the top-level ForCausalLM loader. The new `Plamo2Model.load_weights` uses `elif name == "norm.weight":`. Because `AutoWeightsLoader` strips the `model.` prefix before dispatching to `Plamo2Model.load_weights` (child module receives names relative to itself), `name == "norm.weight"` is the correct match here. However this is a fragile exact-equality comparison whereas the sibling conditions (`.pre_mixer_norm`, etc.) use substring `in`. Verify (a) that AutoWeightsLoader indeed strips the prefix so the final RMSNorm weight is offset by +1.0, and (b) that no other parameter ends in `norm.weight` at the model root. If the prefix is NOT stripped as assumed, the final norm weight silently loses its +1.0 offset, producing wrong outputs with no error. This is the single most delicate behavioral change in the refactor and must be validated against a real checkpoint load.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a real Plamo2 checkpoint and assert model.norm.weight == checkpoint_value + 1.0 after load; compare generation output logits pre/post PR for identical prompts.

findings.claim: The refactor drops the explicit skip of `AutoWeightsLoader.ROTARY_EMBEDS_UNUSED_WEIGHTS` (the old GPTQModel workaround `if any(substr in name for substr in AutoWeightsLoader.ROTARY_EMBEDS_UNUSED_WEIGHTS): continue`). The new code relies on `AutoWeightsLoader` to handle these unused rotary-embed weights internally. Verify that `AutoWeightsLoader.load_weights` still filters `ROTARY_EMBEDS_UNUSED_WEIGHTS` automatically for GPTQ-quantized models; if it does not, loading a GPTQ Plamo2 checkpoint will now raise a KeyError on `params_dict[name]` for rotary inv_freq / unused weights. Since the constant lives on AutoWeightsLoader itself this filtering is likely internal, but it must be confirmed for the quantized path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a GPTQ-quantized Plamo2 checkpoint and confirm load_weights succeeds without KeyError on rotary/unused weight names.

findings.claim: The `tie_word_embeddings` handling changed semantics. Old code explicitly skipped `lm_head.weight` when tie_word_embeddings=True (and asserted it was absent from params_dict). New code passes `skip_prefixes=["lm_head."]` to AutoWeightsLoader only when tie_word_embeddings=True. This is functionally correct for skipping lm_head, but note the old code skipped the exact key `lm_head.weight` while the new code skips the entire `lm_head.` prefix. If a checkpoint contains other lm_head sub-parameters (e.g. quantization scales `lm_head.weight_scale`) that ARE needed even under weight tying, they would now be silently skipped. Confirm no such non-tied lm_head params exist for supported Plamo2 checkpoints.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a tie_word_embeddings=True Plamo2 checkpoint (both unquantized and quantized lm_head) and verify weight coverage matches the pre-PR loader.

findings.claim: In the new `Plamo2Model.load_weights`, the `.q_weight`/`.k_weight` replacements map to `.q_norm.weight`/`.k_norm.weight`. Because these run inside `Plamo2Model` (child of ForCausalLM) rather than the top-level loader, ensure the substring `.q_weight` cannot accidentally match a legitimately named parameter after prefix stripping (e.g. any `*.q_weight` intended to stay). This was equally true in the old code, so behavior is preserved, but the move to the child module changes the name namespace and is worth a quick confirmation.

(1) The new `assert is_nvrx_min_version()` is placed inside `has_nvrx_async_support()`, which is a capability-probe function whose contract is to return a bool (it returns False on ImportError). When nvrx modules are importable but the installed version is < 0.6.0, this assert raises AssertionError instead of returning False. (2) `has_nvrx_async_support()` is evaluated at module import time in strategies/torch.py (`HAVE_NVRX = has_nvrx_async_support()`) and in megatron/training/utils.py; an AssertionError here will crash the import of the entire dist_checkpointing/torch strategy module for any user who has an older nvidia-resiliency-ext installed, rather than gracefully disabling the NVRx async path. This is a hard backward-compat regression for existing environments. (3) Change the probe to fold the version check into the boolean result, e.g. `return is_nvrx_min_version() and all(symbol is not None ...) and hasattr(...)`, so an old version simply reports 'no async support' instead of raising.

(1) In `is_nvrx_min_version`, when nvrx is NOT installed the code sets `nvrx_version = '0.0.0'` and then compares `PkgVersion('0.0.0') >= PkgVersion('0.6.0')` -> returns False without raising. But this branch is only reachable after `has_nvrx_async_support`'s import of nvrx submodules already succeeded; if reached standalone it would swallow the not-installed case as a version mismatch rather than an absence. More importantly, because the assert (line 44) calls this AFTER the module imports succeed, the not-installed path can't be hit there — but if `HAVE_NVRX` is False the reference `nvrx.__version__` is guarded, which is fine; however the combination of (a) raising in one path and (b) silently returning False in another makes the semantics inconsistent. (2) Inconsistent handling means callers can't rely on a single behavior (bool vs exception). (3) Make the function purely return a bool for all cases and keep the ImportError-for-packaging as the only raising path, or document clearly.

(1) `is_nvrx_min_version` re-imports `nvidia_resiliency_ext` and re-derives HAVE_NVRX locally, duplicating import work already done by `has_nvrx_async_support` (and the module-level HAVE_NVRX pattern used elsewhere in tensor_aware_state_dict.py). (2) Minor redundant import/version-parse cost on each call; also the local `HAVE_NVRX` shadows the established module-level convention, hurting readability. (3) Consider caching the version parse or reusing the already-imported core module's package version rather than re-importing.

(1) The dev dependency was pinned from an unconstrained `nvidia-resiliency-ext` (previously sourced from a git rev) to an exact `nvidia-resiliency-ext==0.6.0`, and the git-source override was removed. (2) Exact-pinning to a single version removes flexibility for users/CI who need a newer patch release and may conflict with downstream environments; combined with the new min-version assert it hard-fails older versions. This narrows the supported version window to exactly 0.6.0. (3) Consider `>=0.6.0` to match the runtime `NVRX_MIN_VERSION` gate rather than `==0.6.0`, unless an exact pin is intentional for reproducibility (in which case document why).

(1) This PR enables `arbitrary_precision` (and `preserve_order`) on the workspace-wide `serde_json` dependency. (2) `arbitrary_precision` is a global, non-additive feature with well-known serde_json footguns: it changes `serde_json::Number`/`Value` to store numbers as strings internally, which breaks `#[serde(flatten)]`, `#[serde(untagged)]`, and other custom deserializers anywhere in the whole Rust workspace that deserialize structs containing numeric fields — not just the tool-parser code that motivates the change. This can silently break unrelated request/response (de)serialization paths (e.g. OpenAI protocol structs). (3) Verify no `flatten`/`untagged`/tagged-enum-with-number deserialization exists across the workspace, or scope arbitrary-precision handling to the tool-parser number path only (e.g. via a local RawValue/string parse) instead of flipping the global feature.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Full workspace test suite plus targeted (de)serialization tests for OpenAI protocol structs that use serde flatten/untagged with numeric fields, before and after enabling arbitrary_precision.

findings.claim: (1) `convert_number` now tries `serde_json::from_str::<Number>(value)` first, then falls back to `i64`, then `f64`. With `arbitrary_precision` enabled, values like `"5."` or `"+1"` are not valid JSON numbers and fall through to the i64/f64 fallbacks, which is why the tests still pass. (2) The behavior is subtle and depends entirely on the `arbitrary_precision` feature being active; if that feature is ever removed, `from_str::<Number>` semantics change and the spelling-preservation guarantee (e.g. `5.00`, `1e0`) silently regresses without an obvious compile error. (3) Add a comment documenting that spelling preservation depends on the `arbitrary_precision` serde_json feature, and consider an explicit test asserting the dependency.

(1) `hf_tojson_filter` now takes `ViaDeserialize(value): ViaDeserialize<JsonValue>` and removes the intermediate `serde_json::to_value` conversion — a clean simplification. (2) Minor: the docstring still references generic behavior and the removal now means the filter deserializes the minijinja value directly into a JsonValue relying on `preserve_order`/`arbitrary_precision` to round-trip number spelling and key order; this coupling is implicit. (3) Add a one-line note that key-order and number-spelling fidelity of this filter depend on the `preserve_order`/`arbitrary_precision` serde_json features, so future feature changes don't silently break `tojson` output determinism.

(1) The new `sock: Optional[socket.socket]` parameter is added to `start_text_gen_server` but the function's docstring ("Start the text generation server.") is not updated to document it, nor the interaction that when `sock` is provided the `server_port` argument is effectively ignored (overwritten by the socket's actual bound port). (2) An undocumented parameter with non-obvious semantics (must be pre-bound, makes `server_port` moot) increases the chance callers misuse it — e.g. passing an unbound socket or expecting `server_port` to still take effect. (3) Add a docstring note describing `sock` and that it must be bound to a real (non-zero) port, and that `server_port` is derived from the socket in that case.

(1) When the caller passes in a pre-bound `sock`, `stop_text_gen_server()` still unconditionally calls `_SHARED_SOCKET.close()`, closing a socket the caller created and may still own/reuse. (2) If the caller intends to reuse or manage the socket lifecycle itself (a plausible reason to pass a pre-bound socket), silently closing it can lead to surprising errors on the caller side. (3) Track whether the socket was externally provided (e.g. an `_OWNS_SOCKET` flag) and only close it in `stop_text_gen_server` when it was internally created, or explicitly document that ownership transfers to the server on the passed socket.

(1) When a pre-bound `sock` is passed, the code sets `setblocking(False)` and `set_inheritable(True)` but does not verify/enforce `SO_REUSEADDR`/`SO_REUSEPORT`, which the self-created branch sets. Since the socket is shared across `num_replicas` child processes via fd inheritance (bind is not re-done in children), this is likely fine, but there is no check that the passed socket is a listening/streamable AF_INET SOCK_STREAM socket. (2) A caller passing e.g. a UDP or already-listening socket with incompatible options could cause obscure failures in the worker processes. (3) Consider a lightweight assertion on `sock.type == socket.SOCK_STREAM` / family, to fail fast at the parallelism boundary rather than deep in a child process.

(1) The refactor of `no_invalid_types` in test_openai_schema.py silently dropped the `structured_outputs.grammar == ""` filter rule. In the parent code the grammar check lived inside the outer `if hasattr(case, 'body') and isinstance(case.body, dict):` block but OUTSIDE the `messages` sub-block, so it ran for every dict body. The new code merges `hasattr/isinstance` with the `messages` conditions into one `and`-chain and deletes the entire grammar block. (2) This matters because the removed rule was intentionally guarding against empty-string grammar in structured_outputs that causes a documented server-side EBNF parsing error (referenced PR #22587); dropping it may re-expose flaky server errors in the schema test. Additionally, the retained `@settings` comment still explicitly references "the grammar=='' rule" as the reason for suppressing HealthCheck.filter_too_much, so the justification is now stale/misleading. (3) Fix: re-add the grammar=='' filter (adapting it to schemathesis 4.x Case API), or if intentionally removed, update the @settings comment to remove the grammar-rule justification.

(1) The `@settings(...)` docstring comment above `test_openapi_stateless` still explains suppress_health_check in terms of "the no_invalid_types filter (notably the grammar=='' rule)", but that grammar rule was removed in this same diff. (2) A wrong/stale comment is misleading to future maintainers who may look for a filter that no longer exists. (3) Fix: update the comment to reflect the current filter contents (tool_calls type check only) or restore the grammar rule.

(1) requirements bump schemathesis from >=3.39.15 to >=4.0.0 across cuda.in, rocm.in, and nightly-torch.txt. This is a major-version bump with breaking API changes (from_uri->from_url, from_pytest_fixture->pytest.from_fixture, GenerationConfig moved into SchemathesisConfig, experimental.OPEN_API_3_1 removed, models.Case->Case). (2) Any other test or tooling in the repo still importing the old schemathesis 3.x API would break; this test file was updated but the constraint change is repo-wide. (3) Fix: confirm no other files import schemathesis 3.x-only symbols (grep for `schemathesis.experimental`, `schemathesis.from_pytest_fixture`, `schemathesis.models`, `from_uri`) before merging.

(1) `_is_pre_v11_tokeniser` now calls `getattr(model_tokenizer, "get_vocab", lambda: {})()` for HF (non-Mistral) tokenizers, and `get_vocab()` on HuggingFace tokenizers materializes the full vocabulary dict (tens of thousands of entries). (2) Although the constructor now caches the result in `self._is_pre_v11` and the streaming hot path was updated to read the cached flag (line 566), the function is still a module-level helper that rebuilds the whole vocab on each invocation. Any future/other caller that invokes `_is_pre_v11_tokeniser` per request or per chunk (as the old code at line 568 did before this fix) would incur a large per-call allocation. (3) Cache the `[ARGS] in vocab` check once (e.g. store the boolean at init, which this PR does) and/or use a cheaper membership probe such as `model_tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids('[ARGS]')`/`tokenizer.get_added_vocab()` instead of building the entire vocab dict.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark `_is_pre_v11_tokeniser` with a large HF tokenizer (e.g. 32k-128k vocab) comparing `get_vocab()` membership vs `convert_tokens_to_ids`.

findings.claim: (1) `tool_name = tool_name.replace("[ARGS]", "")` (non-streaming) and the analogous streaming replace strip ALL occurrences of the literal `[ARGS]` from the tool name. (2) If a legitimate tool name ever contained the substring `[ARGS]` it would be silently mangled; more relevantly, the marker is expected only as a trailing separator, so a blanket global replace is broader than needed. (3) Consider stripping only a trailing `[ARGS]` token (e.g. `removesuffix('[ARGS]')` or splitting on the first `[ARGS]`) to match the tokenizer's actual delimiter semantics.

(1) In the streaming path the `[ARGS]` marker is stripped from `self.current_tool_name` only inside the branch that runs when the delta contains `{` (start of arguments). (2) If the `[ARGS]` marker arrives in a delta chunk before the `{` (e.g. name and `[ARGS]` split across chunks), the accumulated name may still be emitted with `[ARGS]` in the incremental `DeltaFunctionCall.name` before the strip runs, producing a transiently incorrect streamed function name. (3) Apply the `[ARGS]` strip to `current_tool_name` at the point the name is emitted (name delta construction), not only when arguments start, to guarantee no chunk leaks the marker.

(1) Type annotations are now stale: register_kv_caches(kv_caches: dict[str, torch.Tensor]) and the field self.kv_caches: dict[str, torch.Tensor] | None still declare values as torch.Tensor, but the new code explicitly handles values that are list/tuple of tensors (Mamba/KDA state). (2) This misleads readers and static type checkers into thinking values are always tensors, exactly the assumption the PR is fixing. (3) Update the annotations to dict[str, torch.Tensor | list[torch.Tensor] | tuple[torch.Tensor, ...]] (or a suitable alias) to match actual runtime types.

(1) For list/tuple (Mamba/KDA) caches, _fill_state_tensor fills the ENTIRE non-block-indexed state buffer, and start_fill_kv iterates over multiple requests and multiple layer_names per group; the same shared state tensor is therefore re-filled once per request/layer visit, and later fills overwrite earlier ones. (2) For a benchmark connector whose goal is to make the cache non-zero this is functionally harmless, but it does redundant full-buffer writes (potentially large normal_/fill_ ops repeated N_requests times) each scheduler step. (3) Consider filling non-block-indexed state tensors at most once (e.g. dedup by tensor identity) or documenting that repeated full fills are intentional and cheap for the benchmark.

(1) The second `if rotary_dim is None: rotary_dim = self.head_dim` guard is dead code. (2) The preceding block `if rotary_dim is None: ... rotary_dim = int(self.head_dim * partial_rotary_factor)` always assigns a non-None integer whenever rotary_dim was None, so the second check can never be True; it adds confusing dead complexity implying a code path that cannot execute. (3) Remove the second `if rotary_dim is None:` block entirely — the partial_rotary_factor branch (defaulting to 1.0 -> head_dim) already covers the fallback that this dead block was meant to provide.

(1) `config.rope_parameters["partial_rotary_factor"] = rotary_dim / self.head_dim` performs float division then get_rope recomputes `int(head_size * partial_rotary_factor)`, i.e. int->float->int round-trip. (2) For non-power-of-two ratios floating-point representation could in rare cases round down by one (e.g. int(96 * (64/96))), silently producing a rotary_dim off by 1 versus the intended value; also it mutates the shared config object in place (side effect on each layer's __init__). (3) Consider storing the intended integer rotary_dim directly (e.g. pass rope_dim/rotary_dim through rope_parameters) rather than round-tripping through a float factor, to guarantee the exact rotary_dim is used.

(1) The list[float] fast-path uses `isinstance(audio_url_or_urls[0], float)`, which only detects Python `float` (and `np.float64`, which subclasses it) but returns False for `np.float32`/`np.integer` scalars. (2) A list of numpy float32 scalars would then be treated as a list of audio items and recursed element-wise, where each scalar is neither valid audio, list/tuple, nor str, raising TypeError. On transformers 5.10 this path is masked by is_valid_audio accepting list[float]; on 5.9 it could misbehave for the (unusual) list-of-np-scalar case. (3) Consider using a numeric-scalar check (e.g. `isinstance(x, (float, int)) or np.isscalar(x)`) if such inputs are possible; otherwise document that only Python float lists are supported. Low practical risk since ProcessorMixin.prepare_inputs_layout passes decoded arrays/tensors.

(1) merge_added_tokens_from_config dedups only by numeric id (existing_ids), never by token content. If tokenizer.json's added_tokens already contains an entry with the same `content` (e.g. `<|image_pad|>`) but under a different id, or if that content already exists in the model vocab at an id not present in added_tokens, this pushes a second added_tokens entry with a duplicate content string. (2) Duplicate content in added_tokens can make HfTokenizer/FastokensTokenizer construction fail or produce an ambiguous token_to_id mapping — the exact class of bug this PR is trying to fix could re-manifest as a build error or wrong id resolution for real HF checkpoints where content overlaps but ids differ between tokenizer.json and tokenizer_config.json. (3) Also skip entries whose `content` already appears in tokenizer.json's added_tokens (build a HashSet<String> of existing contents in addition to existing_ids).

(1) AddedTokenConfig derives `normalized` via `#[serde(default)]`, which yields `false` when the key is absent. HuggingFace's semantics use `normalized=false` for special tokens but `normalized=true` for non-special added tokens by default; a tokenizer_config.json entry that omits `normalized` for a non-special token will be materialized with the wrong flag. (2) Wrong `normalized` affects whether the added token is normalized before matching during encode, which can change tokenization of the surrounding text. (3) Either default `normalized` based on the `special` flag, or document that only entries with explicit `normalized` are handled correctly. Note the test only covers special=true tokens, so this path is untested.

(1) new_hf switched from HfTokenizer::from_file(path) to serde_json::from_value::<HfTokenizer>(load_tokenizer_json_with_extra_tokens(path)?). from_file uses the tokenizers crate's own deserialization path (which may apply post-load fixups such as decoder/normalizer resolution); deserializing via serde_json::from_value on the raw HfTokenizer may not be exactly equivalent for all tokenizer.json variants. (2) A subtle divergence would silently change tokenization behavior for existing models even when no tokenizer_config.json extra tokens exist. (3) Confirm HfTokenizer's Deserialize impl is the same path from_file uses internally (in current tokenizers crate from_file does read+from_str, so likely equivalent) and add a regression test asserting identical encode output before/after for a representative real tokenizer.

(1) load_tokenizer_json_with_extra_tokens now reads the whole tokenizer.json into a String, parses it into a serde_json TokenizerJson, then re-serializes to serde_json::Value which is re-parsed by the backend, versus the previous single from_file. (2) This adds a full extra parse+serialize of potentially large (multi-MB) tokenizer.json files at load time; it is one-time startup cost, not per-request, so impact is minor but non-zero. (3) Acceptable given it is load-time only; noting for awareness — could be avoided by patching added_tokens in-place on a single parsed Value rather than round-tripping through a typed projection.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a large real-world tokenizer.json (e.g. Qwen2-VL, several MB) and compare wall-clock of new_hf/new_fastokens before vs after this change.

findings.claim: (1) Callers such as `gauge_engine_sleep_state.labels(engine=idx, ...)` build a labeled child once at __init__ and then repeatedly call `.set(...)` on it later (e.g. via record_sleep_state). Under the OLD behavior, `labels()` returned `self`, so a single wrapper object was reused and the last `set_default_tags` won at call-time. Under the NEW behavior a labeled clone is stored per (state, idx); this is exactly what the fix intends and is correct. HOWEVER, the same pattern at loggers.py:1137 chains `self.gauge_lora_info.labels(**lora_info_labels).set_to_current_time()` where `gauge_lora_info` is the BASE (unlabeled) wrapper reused every iteration. With the fix, each iteration produces a fresh clone (fine), but any code path that calls `.labels()` on an ALREADY-labeled stored child a second time now raises ValueError('already-labeled'), which was previously silently allowed. (2) This is a behavior change that can crash at runtime for any caller that re-labels a stored labeled metric. (3) Audit all `.labels()` call sites (loggers.py:510/646/679/1056/1137) to confirm none re-label a previously-labeled child; if any do, they must call `.labels()` on the base metric instead.

(1) `labels()` uses `clone = copy.copy(self)` (shallow copy). Because `_is_labeled` is a class attribute default (`_is_labeled: bool = False`) and the clone sets `clone._is_labeled = True` as an instance attribute, this is correct. But the shallow copy shares `clone.metric` with the parent, which is intended (single underlying Ray metric, per-child tags). Verify that no other mutable per-instance state exists on subclasses that would be unintentionally shared through the shallow copy; currently only `metric` and `_tags` exist and `_tags` is reassigned to a fresh dict, so aliasing is avoided. (2) If a subclass later adds mutable instance state, shallow copy would silently alias it across children. (3) Consider documenting the shallow-copy contract (metric shared intentionally, _tags replaced) to prevent future aliasing bugs.

(1) `labels()` now performs a `copy.copy(self)` and builds a fresh dict on every call. In hot paths where `.labels(...).inc()` is invoked per-request (e.g. request_success counter incremented on every finished request), this allocates a new wrapper object and dict each time. (2) For high-QPS serving this adds per-request Python allocation/GC overhead versus the previous mutate-in-place approach. It is likely negligible relative to request handling cost, but the old code avoided the allocation. (3) If profiling shows overhead, cache labeled children (keyed by label tuple) at construction time as loggers.py:510 already does for sleep_state, so `.labels()` is not called on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark: repeatedly call RayCounterWrapper.labels('stop').inc() in a tight loop (e.g. 1e6 iterations) and compare wall time / allocations before vs after the change.

findings.claim: (1) `_build_tags` returns only the coerced `labelskwargs` (which now always includes ReplicaId), but it no longer includes positionally-derived keys unless they were merged into labelskwargs via `labelskwargs.update(zip(...))`. This merge happens earlier in the same method, so it is correct. However the returned dict comprehension `{k: v if isinstance(v,str) else str(v) ...}` silently drops nothing — good. Minor: the method name `_build_tags` and the fact that it mutates `labelskwargs` (via update) before comprehending is slightly non-obvious. (2) Low risk; purely readability. (3) Consider not mutating the incoming `labelskwargs` and instead build the result dict directly to avoid confusion.

(1) The refactored language-selection logic changes the behavior for the `transcribe` task: pre-change, the `assistant` turn appended `language {Lang}<asr_text>` whenever `to_language` was not None, regardless of task type; post-change, for `task_type == 'transcribe'` it now uses `lang_code = stt_params.language` (the source language) instead of `to_language`. (2) This is a behavior change that alters the forced-language directive in the generation prompt for existing transcription requests — the model may now be forced to a different language token than before, silently changing transcription output for users who relied on the prior default (which keyed on `to_language`). (3) Confirm the intended semantics against the Qwen3-ASR SDK: for transcription the forced language should typically be the source `language`, but verify that `to_language`-only callers who previously got language forcing still get it, and add a test covering the transcribe-vs-translate language selection.

(1) For the `transcribe` path, `lang_code` is now taken from `stt_params.language`, which upstream (`_preprocess_speech_to_text`) may be auto-detected/validated and thus non-None far more often than `to_language` was. (2) This means the forced `language {Lang}<asr_text>` assistant prefix will now be emitted in many transcription cases where previously (to_language=None) it was NOT emitted — a subtle default-behavior change that can constrain the model's language auto-detection during decoding. (3) Verify this is intended; if the goal is to preserve prior no-force behavior when the user did not explicitly request a language, gate on an explicit user-provided language flag rather than the resolved/auto-detected `language`.

(1) `_CHATML_LIKE_TOKEN` is compiled at module import (good), and `_sanitize_transcription_user_text` loops re.sub+replace to a fixpoint. For pathological/adversarial deeply-nested inputs the loop runs O(n) passes each O(n), i.e. O(n^2) on the user-controlled `prompt`/`response_prefix`. (2) This is on the request preprocessing path (per chunk), not the training/decode hot loop, and prompt length is small/bounded, so impact is negligible in practice — but it is unbounded work on untrusted input. (3) Consider a single-pass tokenizer-aware strip or a bound on iterations; low priority given typical prompt sizes.

(1) When a tool call is suppressed (index >= 1 with parallel_tool_calls=false), `next_tool_call_index` is still incremented and `start_tool_call` runs the full `close_open_text_block`/`close_open_tool_call` prologue before the suppression check. (2) This is harmless today because index 0 is never suppressed and open_tool_call is already None by the time suppression is set, but it makes the state machine harder to reason about: the incremented index has no northbound consumer while suppressing, and the ordering of close-vs-check is load-bearing for correctness. (3) Consider adding a short comment documenting that the suppression check must come after the close prologue (so a preceding surfaced tool call is properly finalized) and that the incremented index is intentionally discarded during suppression.

(1) `close_open_tool_call` clears `suppressing_tool_call` and returns early when suppressing. This flag is also implicitly cleared whenever a text/reasoning delta arrives (process_text_delta calls close_open_tool_call first). (2) This means a text or reasoning block interleaved after a suppressed tool call will reset suppression state; a subsequent tool call is then re-evaluated purely on `index >= 1`, which still correctly suppresses it — but the coupling between text-block handling and tool-call suppression is non-obvious and only covered by the single new happy-path test. (3) Add a test covering [tool_call_0 surfaced] -> [tool_call_1 suppressed] -> [text delta] -> [tool_call_2] to lock in that later tool calls remain suppressed after interleaved text, guarding against future regressions in the suppression state machine.

(1) The previously-returned 400 rejection for `parallel_tool_calls` is removed and the parameter is now accepted, defaulting to true in convert.rs via `unwrap_or(true)`. (2) This is a permissive/behavior-relaxing change (previously-rejected requests now succeed), which is backward compatible; however, `parallel_tool_calls=false` now silently changes streamed output to drop all tool calls after the first, which is a semantic change clients previously could not trigger. (3) No code fix needed, but confirm downstream non-streaming aggregation and any client expecting the old 400 are updated; the default-true test and mapping test adequately cover the happy path.

(1) The new normalization for direct PIL inputs hardcodes the target mode to "RGB" and uses convert_image_mode's default white background (255,255,255), whereas images loaded through the media path (ImageMediaIO) honor a configurable image_mode and rgba_background_color from --media-io-kwargs / runtime media_io_kwargs. (2) This means direct PIL image inputs bypass the user's configured background color and image mode, so RGBA-with-transparency images passed directly could be composited on a different background than the media-loaded path, producing inconsistent/behavior-changing results for existing users relying on custom rgba_background_color. (3) Consider threading the parser's configured image mode / background color into this normalization (e.g. accept an ImageMediaIO-like config) rather than hardcoding "RGB" and defaults, or at minimum document the intentional divergence.

(1) The list comprehension calls normalize_image (ImageOps.exif_transpose) unconditionally on every direct PIL image. (2) For images already loaded via ImageMediaIO.load_bytes, normalize_image was already applied, so this re-applies exif_transpose; while exif_transpose on an image without EXIF orientation is a no-op-ish call, it still constructs/copies work and iterates the full list. (3) This is minor and only affects direct-PIL-input path (not media-loaded, which are MediaWithBytes wrappers and not PILImage.Image instances here), so impact is low; no change strictly required, just noting the redundant transform if any path double-normalizes.

In `_build_grid` (topology.py ~line 135) three new dims combinations are created — `["tp","dp"]`, `["tp","dp","cp"]`, and `["tp","cp","dp","pp"]` — but `pg_collection_from_grid` only retrieves the first two (`pgc.tp_dp` and `pgc.tp_dp_cp`). The `["tp","cp","dp","pp"]` group is created via a collective `new_subgroups_by_enumeration` on every grid but never fetched via `get_pg` nor assigned into the ProcessGroupCollection. Each unused create_pg incurs an NCCL communicator allocation (GPU memory + init-time collective) that is never used. (Why it matters: wasted NCCL communicator resources and startup latency, and the intent is unclear.)
