Impact: release-branch / force-run-all CI loses its Megatron-Bridge downstream coverage without any indication. Fix: either add the `is_ci_workload`/`force_run_all` (and `deploy-release`) cases to the `RUN_MBRIDGE` computation so it stays consistent with the job's OR clause, or intentionally narrow the OR clause in the `if:` and document that release-branch pushes no longer trigger MBridge.

Pre-existing (not introduced by this diff, but adjacent and semantically related to the new code): the line `if [ "$HAS_MBRIDGE" == "true" || $IS_MERGE_GROUP == "true" ]; then` uses `||` inside a single `[ ]` test and an unquoted `$IS_MERGE_GROUP`, which is not valid `test` syntax (the `||` should join two separate `[ ]` invocations, as the newly added `RUN_MBRIDGE` block correctly does). Under `bash -e` this can mis-evaluate `MBRIDGE_SUITE`. Since the new PR introduces the correct `[ ... ] || [ ... ]` pattern a few lines below, consider fixing this pre-existing `mbridge_suite` computation for consistency. Note: outside the strict diff scope, low priority.

(1) When multiple image tags are passed (e.g. image_build.sh calls annotate_image_tags with both IMAGE_TAG and IMAGE_TAG_LATEST), the loop emits every line with the identical prefix `**${label}**` (from BUILDKITE_LABEL), so the annotation shows two lines with the same label and no way to distinguish the primary tag from the -latest tag. (2) Low impact — purely cosmetic on the Buildkite build page, but partially defeats the stated goal of surfacing which tags were pushed. (3) Consider differentiating the tags (e.g. label the primary vs latest) or dropping the per-line label duplication in favor of a single header plus a bulleted list of tags.

(1) In image_build.sh the actual build uses `docker buildx bake` with TARGET, and the tags baked into the image come from the bake/HCL config, not necessarily from ${IMAGE_TAG}/${IMAGE_TAG_LATEST}. The new annotate_image_tags reports the env vars IMAGE_TAG/IMAGE_TAG_LATEST rather than the tags actually produced by bake. (2) Low risk — these env vars are exported and consumed by bake so they should match, but if the HCL adds/derives additional tags the annotation could be incomplete or misleading. (3) Confirm IMAGE_TAG/IMAGE_TAG_LATEST are the exact and only tags bake pushes; if bake can emit other tags, source the annotation from the bake config instead.

(1) `printf '%s' "$content"` intentionally omits a trailing newline while each list item already ends in a newline via the $'\n' concatenation, so the piped-in markdown ends without a final newline. (2) Negligible — Buildkite markdown rendering tolerates this, so no functional impact. (3) No change strictly needed; noting for consistency only.

(1) `num_requests_total = len(requests)` is computed twice: once inside the `if args.drain_between_batches and args.batch_boundaries:` block and again unconditionally a few lines later. (2) Impact is negligible (example script), but the duplicate assignment is dead in the branch case and adds noise/confusion about which binding is authoritative. (3) Compute `num_requests_total` once before the batch-boundary block and reuse it. Low value since this is example, non-training-loop code.

(1) The base-image bump to nvcr.io/nvidia/pytorch:26.06-py3 is accompanied by updating BERT functional-test golden values so that lm-loss (and num-zeros, mem-allocated-bytes, mem-max-allocated-bytes, iteration-time) become the string "nan" from step 23 onward, whereas the parent golden values show a healthy, finite, monotonically decreasing loss (10.5 -> 9.5) across all 50 steps. (2) This does not merely re-baseline for minor numerical drift from a new toolchain; it encodes training divergence (NaN loss) as the expected/accepted behavior. It masks a genuine numerical regression introduced by the new base image — BERT training now blows up mid-run — and any future real NaN regression on this model will silently pass CI because NaN is now the golden value. (3) Do not bump the base image while accepting NaN goldens. Root-cause the divergence in the 26.06 image (e.g., cuDNN/attention/LayerNorm/optimizer kernel change, mixed-precision behavior), fix it so loss stays finite, and only then re-baseline goldens to finite values. If the image must land, gate/xfail the affected tests explicitly rather than baking NaN into golden_values.

(1) Same issue as bert_mcore_tp1_pp2: golden lm-loss/num-zeros/mem/iteration-time are changed to "nan" starting at step 14 (parent shows finite decreasing loss through step 50). (2) The tp1_pp4_vp2 configuration (pipeline + virtual pipeline parallel) diverges even earlier than tp1_pp2, indicating a broad numerical regression in the 26.06 base image affecting BERT training across parallelism layouts, not a config-specific fluke. Accepting NaN as golden defeats the purpose of the loss-convergence regression test. (3) Investigate why BERT diverges under the new image (esp. earlier divergence with VP), fix root cause, and re-baseline to finite values; do not merge NaN goldens.

(1) The PR touches many additional golden_values_dev files across gpt3, moe (deepseek/8experts/muon/dist_optimizer), and multimodal-llava test cases alongside the same image bump. (2) If any of these were likewise updated to encode NaN or non-convergent loss (as the two BERT files were), the divergence regression is systemic across model families, not isolated — greatly increasing the risk that the 26.06 image is numerically broken for training and that real regressions will be silently masked. (3) Audit every changed golden_values file in this PR for newly-introduced "nan" loss/num-zeros entries; block the image bump until all affected models converge to finite loss, and re-baseline only to finite values.

(1) The base image is advanced two minor NGC releases (26.04 -> 26.06). (2) Given the observed NaN divergence in BERT goldens, this bump changes runtime numerics/kernels in a way that alters training behavior for all users pulling the dev image; without a documented investigation it risks propagating a numerically-regressed toolchain. (3) Include in the PR description the root-cause analysis of the golden-value changes and confirmation that finite-loss convergence is preserved, or defer the bump until the divergence is resolved.

(1) The client builds the prompt as ('hello ' * num_input_tokens).strip() and then asserts server-reported usage.prompt_tokens == num_input_tokens. (2) There is no guarantee the tokenizer maps each ' hello ' word to exactly one token — the TikTokenizer used by these checkpoints may merge/split whitespace-delimited 'hello' repetitions, so prompt_tokens can differ from num_input_tokens, causing a hard AssertionError that fails the perf test for tokenizer reasons unrelated to performance. (3) Either construct the prompt from a fixed token-id list of the exact desired length and send it as list[int] (the server supports list[int] prompts), or drop/relax the strict equality assertion (e.g. warn instead of assert).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Tokenize ('hello ' * 512).strip() with the TikTokenizer vocab used by gpt_583m and check whether it yields exactly 512 tokens.

findings.claim: (1) tpot_ms = avg_wall * 1000 / num_output_tokens uses the whole-batch wall clock (which includes prefill + concurrent decode of all requests) divided by output tokens. (2) For batch>1 this is not true time-per-output-token (decode-only) since prefill and concurrency are folded in; the metric label 'tpot_ms_per_tok' is misleading and mixes prefill latency into a decode metric. (3) Either rename the field to reflect it is per-request end-to-end time normalized by output length, or subtract/measure prefill separately (e.g. via streaming/TTFT) to report a true TPOT.

(1) _percentile computes idx = min(int(len*pct), len-1); for p99 with only num_iters*batch_size samples (e.g. 5*1=5 samples at batch_size=1) the p99 index collapses to the max, and for small sample counts the 'p99' is effectively the max latency. (2) This makes the p99 baseline extremely noisy and prone to false regressions given the 10% tolerance, since a single slow request dominates. (3) Increase timed iterations for small batch sizes or exclude p99 from the compared METRICS for low-sample configs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Re-run the batch_1 case several times and measure run-to-run variance of p99_latency_ms vs the 10% tolerance.

findings.claim: (1) The comparison only iterates over batch keys present in the baseline; if a run produces an extra batch key not in baseline it is silently ignored, and metrics missing from either side are skipped via 'continue'. (2) A silently skipped metric (e.g. baseline recorded without one metric) will report OK even though nothing was actually validated, weakening regression detection. (3) Warn/fail when a requested METRIC in model_config.yaml is absent from either results or baseline rather than silently continuing.

(1) _single_request returns 'actual_output or num_output_tokens' — if the server reports completion_tokens==0 (falsy), the code falls back to the requested num_output_tokens instead of the true 0. (2) This masks a degenerate server response (no tokens generated) as a full-length success, inflating throughput and hiding failures. (3) Use an explicit 'actual_output if actual_output is not None else num_output_tokens' check rather than the truthy 'or'.

(1) The RECORD_BASELINE path invokes the inline merge Python via `uv run --no-sync python - "$RESULTS_JSON" "$BASELINE_PATH" "$PLATFORM" <<'PY'`, but the heredoc script parses only sys.argv[1..3] with the '-' stdin marker; when python reads the script from stdin (`python -`) the three trailing tokens ARE the argv, which is correct — however the merge overwrites `baseline[platform] = results` where `results` is the entire results.json. If results.json ever nests a platform key (future change) or the schema differs, the merge silently corrupts the file. (2) Risk: a bootstrap run on the wrong node writes a mislabeled subtree that then passes comparison forever. (3) Suggest asserting the results.json shape (dict of batch_* keys) before assigning it under the platform key, and warn if the platform subtree already existed (overwrite).

(1) The comparison loop iterates `for batch_key, baseline_entry in baseline.items()` where `baseline = full_baseline[platform]`. It fails when a baseline batch_key is missing from results, but it does NOT fail when results contains extra batch keys not present in the baseline — i.e. a newly added BATCH_SIZE in model_config.yaml would be silently un-checked until the baseline is refreshed. (2) Risk: perf regressions on newly added batch sizes go undetected. (3) Consider warning when `results` has batch keys absent from the platform baseline subtree.

(1) PLATFORM auto-detection uses `nvidia-smi -L | head -1` with a case-match on GPU name substrings (GB200/B200/H100/A100). On a heterogeneous or unrecognized GPU (e.g. GH200, H200, future SKUs) it hard-exits with code 2, breaking any existing perf-test invocation that previously worked without a PLATFORM concept. (2) Risk: existing CI recipes on non-listed hardware start failing immediately after this merge. (3) The recipes were updated in this PR, but any out-of-tree/downstream caller of run_perf_test.sh now must set PLATFORM; document this as a breaking change or add H200/GH200 to the case list.

(1) The new GB200/bare-NGC mamba install path runs `uv pip install --no-build-isolation mamba-ssm causal-conv1d` and only emits a warning on failure (`|| { echo warning ... }`), continuing execution. The subsequent hybrid test will then fail later with an opaque ImportError deep in the server launch rather than at the install site. (2) Risk: harder-to-diagnose CI failures; the warning suggests non-fatal but the run is effectively broken. (3) Consider `exit`ing non-zero on install failure for hybrid_*/mamba_* models so the failure is attributed correctly, or at least tightening the warning to state the run will abort.

(1) `uv pip install ... mamba-ssm causal-conv1d` installs unpinned latest versions at test runtime; the comment notes wheels exist 'starting mamba-ssm 2.2.2 / causal-conv1d 1.4.0' but no minimum version is pinned. (2) Risk: non-reproducible perf baselines — a future wheel bump could shift kernel performance and silently invalidate the recorded GB200 baselines, or a yanked/newer incompatible wheel breaks the run. (3) Pin explicit versions (e.g. `mamba-ssm>=2.2.2,<X` and matching causal-conv1d) matching what the recorded baseline was measured against.

NVTX push/pop can become unbalanced in the backward path. `pre_backward()` pushes an NVTX range and is registered unconditionally via `register_full_backward_pre_hook`, but the matching `range_pop()` lives in `post_backward()`, which is only invoked from the per-parameter `grad_hook` when `len(self._ready_grad_parameters) == self._num_training_parameters`. If an FSDP unit has no trainable parameters (`_num_training_parameters == 0`, no grad hooks registered in `_register_hooks`), backward will still flow through the module and fire `pre_backward` (push), but `post_backward` (pop) will never fire. This corrupts the thread-local NVTX range stack, mislabeling all subsequent ranges and potentially causing pops to close the wrong range across the whole training step. Impact: profiling traces become wrong/unusable and, in nested cases, an under-popped stack silently attributes time to the wrong module. Fix: guarantee 1:1 pairing — either push/pop within the same hook, or only push in pre_backward when `_num_training_parameters > 0` (and ensure post_backward always runs to pop, e.g. via a module backward hook fallback).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Construct an FSDP unit whose parameters all have requires_grad=False (or _num_training_parameters==0), run forward+backward, and assert NVTX push/pop events remain balanced.

findings.claim: `_nvtx_label` relies on `post_backward` firing exactly once per `pre_backward` firing, but the two use different trigger mechanisms (full_backward_pre_hook vs. per-parameter post-accumulate-grad completion). Under gradient accumulation or when a shared FSDP submodule is invoked multiple times in one forward, the number of `pre_backward` pushes may not equal the number of `post_backward` pops within a single backward, risking NVTX stack imbalance. This should be validated: the added tests only cover a single forward/backward where each unit is called once. Fix: add a test exercising a module reused multiple times and/or grad accumulation, and document the assumed 1:1 invariant.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Reuse the same FSDP submodule twice in forward, backward, and check push/pop balance.

findings.claim: In `_nvtx_label`, `self.name if self.name else "<root>"` evaluates the `name` property twice and relies on it returning a falsy empty string for the root. But the `name` property raises RuntimeError when `_name is None` — so this expression assumes name is already initialized. Since `_nvtx_label` is also called from `pre_backward` (where lazy init already ran in the prior forward), this is fine at runtime, but the double property access is redundant and the intent (root → "<root>") would be clearer as `name = self.name; return f"MFSDP {name or '<root>'} {phase}"`.

(1) NCCL_ALGO is unconditionally defaulted to "Ring" in _run_training.sh, but Megatron's arguments.py (validate path around line 1433) asserts that when certain features are enabled, os.getenv("NCCL_ALGO") must be one of a restricted all_reduce_choices set, and "Ring" is valid there. Additionally per-test model_config.yaml files already set NCCL_ALGO: Ring via ENV_VARS. (2) Risk: the ENV_VARS block in _run_training.sh is exported AFTER this new export block, so per-test config values still win — but this ordering is not obvious and a future reorder could silently override per-test NCCL settings (e.g. a test that needs NCCL_ALGO=^NVLS). (3) Fix: confirm/document that ENV_VARS from model_config.yaml are intentionally applied after these defaults, or move these defaults before the mandatory-var block with a comment noting per-test override precedence.

(1) These NCCL env defaults (NCCL_PROTO=simple, NCCL_ALGO=Ring, NCCL_COLLNET_ENABLE=0, NCCL_NVLS_ENABLE=0, NCCL_BUFFSIZE=4MB) constrain NCCL to a low-performance, deterministic collective path and are now applied to ALL functional-test runs that go through _run_training.sh, not just the nemo_run docker path where they previously lived. (2) Impact: this is the intended source of the golden-value shifts seen in this PR (loss/num-zeros/tpot all changed), and it may make the memory_speed test (gpt3_7b_tp4_pp1_memory_speed) report slower throughput vs. the previous unconstrained NCCL config, since NCCL_ALGO=Ring + NVLS/COLLNET disabled forgoes faster algorithms. If any perf-threshold assertion exists it should be re-baselined. (3) Fix: verify the memory_speed golden values / throughput thresholds were re-baselined under these settings (the golden JSON was updated, so likely yes) and that broadening scope from docker-only to all launchers is intended.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run gpt3_7b_tp4_pp1_memory_speed on H100 with and without the new NCCL env defaults and compare reported step time / throughput.

findings.claim: (1) The same NCCL env vars now live in both launch_nemo_run_workload.py's DockerExecutor env_vars and _run_training.sh. Since _run_training.sh uses ${VAR:-default}, the DockerExecutor-injected values still take precedence for the nemo_run path, so the shell defaults are effectively a no-op there and only active for the jet/slurm path. (2) Impact: duplicated definitions of the same constants risk drift — a future change to one (e.g. NCCL_BUFFSIZE) will not update the other, causing the nemo_run docker path and the slurm path to diverge silently. The PR description says these were 'moved' from the docker launcher, but they were not removed from launch_nemo_run_workload.py. (3) Fix: if the intent was to centralize in the shell script, remove the NCCL_* entries from launch_nemo_run_workload.py's env_vars (keeping only NCCL_DEBUG/NCCL_DEBUG_FILE which are launcher-specific); otherwise document that the duplication is intentional.

(1) The new MTP acceptance-rate assertion block (the `if config.expected_acceptance_rate is not None:` branch that fetches `vllm:spec_decode_num_draft_tokens_total` and checks `acceptance_rate`) is dead code: neither ModelConfig in MODEL_CONFIGS sets `expected_acceptance_rate`, so it stays at its default `None`. The only MTP config (`qwen35-0.8b-mtp`) sets only `expected_acceptance_length=1.798`. Therefore the MTP test validates exactly the same single metric as EAGLE3's overall check, and the entire rate branch never executes. (2) This adds ~20 lines of untested logic plus a new `_fetch_metric` call for a metric that is never queried, giving a false impression that MTP has a distinct/stricter acceptance-rate assertion. If the metric name or math were wrong, no config would ever catch it. (3) Either populate `expected_acceptance_rate` on the qwen35 MTP config (so the branch actually runs), or drop the branch and the `expected_acceptance_rate` field until an MTP baseline is available (add a TODO/migration note if deferred).

(1) The new `expected_acceptance_rate: float | None` dataclass field is declared but has no read/use path exercised by any configured model (see the dead MTP branch above). (2) Per the Mandatory unused-new-field check, an added field with no live use path is a maintenance hazard and signals an incomplete feature. (3) Wire it into at least one config's assertion path, or remove it and mark the MTP-rate check as an explicit TODO.

(1) `ENABLE_HMA_VAR` and `EXTRA_SERVE_ARGS` are expanded unquoted/word-split into the `vllm serve` command (`${ENABLE_HMA_VAR}` and `${EXTRA_SERVE_ARGS[@]+"${EXTRA_SERVE_ARGS[@]}"}`). With `set -u` now active (`set -ex`), the array is guarded correctly with the `[@]+` idiom, but `${ENABLE_HMA_VAR}` relies on the variable always being initialized to "" — which it is here, so it is safe. This is informational: the empty-string default protects against nounset, but a future refactor that drops the `ENABLE_HMA_VAR=""` initialization would break under `set -u`. (2) Low risk today. (3) Consider using `${ENABLE_HMA_VAR:-}` at the expansion site for defensive robustness.

(1) The Buildkite step's command is switched from directly running `spec_decode_acceptance_test.sh` to the new `config_sweep_spec_decode_test.sh`, which now runs EAGLE3 (Llama-3.1-8B) *and* MTP (Qwen3.5-0.8B) sequentially in one step. (2) This roughly doubles the wall-clock and GPU-hold time of that CI step (two full prefill+decode server spin-ups plus MT-Bench runs), and a hard `exit 1` on the first failing config means the second config is never exercised when the first flakes — reducing signal. (3) Confirm the CI timeout budget accommodates two sweeps, or split into two Buildkite steps so EAGLE3 and MTP report independently.

(1) The fix now calls `tensor.clone()` on every tensor yielded by the RunAI streamer iterator, materializing a fresh copy of every weight tensor for every model. (2) This is the correct fix for the underlying bug (the RunAI SafetensorsStreamer reuses a single internal buffer across `get_tensors`/iterator steps, so consumers that retain references — e.g. building a dict or lazily consuming the generator — would see later tensors overwrite earlier ones), but it doubles peak host memory transiently for each tensor and adds a full memcpy per weight across the entire checkpoint, measurably increasing weight-load latency for large models. (3) Consider cloning only when necessary — i.e. detect whether the streamer actually reuses buffers (gate on the `RUNAI_STREAMER_MEMORY_LIMIT`/streaming mode), or clone lazily so consumers that copy tensors immediately (most `load_weights` paths that `.copy_` into pre-allocated params) don't pay the extra copy. At minimum document that this is a required correctness copy so it is not later 'optimized' away.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end weight-load wall-clock and peak host RSS for the RunAI streamer loader on a large (e.g. 70B) safetensors checkpoint, before vs after the added clone().

findings.claim: (1) The clone() correctness fix is applied inside `runai_safetensors_weights_iterator`, but this iterator is consumed in multiple call sites (runai_streamer_loader.py, sharded_state_loader.py line 168 `yield from`, and RunaiModelStreamerLoader). (2) Worth confirming the buffer-reuse hazard only affected the safetensors iterator and not the analogous fastsafetensors / other streaming iterators in the same module, otherwise the same latent bug (tensors aliasing a reused buffer) could still exist on those paths. (3) Verify sibling iterators do not share the reused-buffer behavior; if they do, apply the same clone or a shared helper.

(1) The out-of-order buffering warning uses logger.warning_once with a message string that embeds dynamic values (mem_used float and the sorted layer names). warning_once is backed by @lru_cache keyed on (logger, msg, *args), so distinct (mem_used, names) tuples each produce a fresh log line rather than being deduped. (2) Because mem_used changes as more weights buffer, the intended 'log once' behavior does not hold and the warning can fire repeatedly during a single out-of-order reload, spamming logs. (3) If a single de-duplicated warning is desired, log a constant message via warning_once (put the dynamic numbers in a separate debug log), or use a module-level 'already warned' flag; if per-episode surfacing is intended, document that repetition is expected.

(1) LOADING_LAYERS.discard(layer) is only executed in the fully-loaded fast path (info.load_numel >= load_numel_total inside online_process_loader). Non-attention layers that never reach load_numel_total during loading (e.g. padded layers finalized later, or layers whose remaining weights load out of order) stay in LOADING_LAYERS until finalize_layerwise_processing calls LOADING_LAYERS.clear(). (2) This inflates both the reported mem_used and the 'names' list in the warning while those layers are still tracked, making the diagnostic overstate buffered memory. It is logging-only so it cannot corrupt weights, but the guidance message could mislead users. (3) Also discard from LOADING_LAYERS in the delayed/partial processing branch of finalize_layerwise_processing, or key the set off actual buffered LAYERWISE_INFO entries rather than maintaining a parallel set.

(1) get_info_size iterates over every buffered arg of every layer in LOADING_LAYERS and is invoked from online_process_loader on each weight-load call whenever len(LOADING_LAYERS) >= 2. (2) In a persistently out-of-order reload this is O(total buffered weights) recomputed on every subsequent weight load, an avoidable quadratic accumulation of Python-level work in the loading path (though only metadata reads: .nbytes and .device.type incur no CUDA sync). (3) Compute mem_used lazily only right before actually emitting the warning (i.e. after the warning_once dedup decides to log), or cache per-layer sizes, so the recomputation does not run on every buffered load.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Reload a model with deliberately out-of-order weight iterator and measure reload_weights wall time with/without the per-call get_info_size computation.

findings.claim: (1) The new `_rms_norm_input_generator` initializes `weight` with `torch.randn` (mean 0, includes negatives), whereas the previous `rms_norm_inputs` helper used `torch.rand` (uniform [0,1)). (2) This silently changes the distribution of the weight used by both correctness tests and benchmarks; RMSNorm weight is a learned scale that in practice is centered near 1, and negative/zero-crossing weights change the numerical error profile (and are what motivated the loosened float16 tolerance below). (3) If the change is intentional (stress-testing wider dynamic range), keep it; otherwise revert to `torch.rand` or use `torch.randn(...)+1` to better match realistic weights and the prior test semantics.

(1) `save_results(...)` is called inside the per-op loop and always writes `summary.csv` from the ever-growing `all_summary_rows` accumulator, so the summary file is fully rewritten once per op. (2) This is redundant disk I/O and means a crash mid-run leaves a partial-but-plausible summary; it also couples per-op detail writing with global summary writing. (3) Write per-op detail files inside the loop but move the single `summary.csv` write (and metadata) to after the loop completes.

(1) The tolerance-override comment cites a shape of `32768x16384` as the motivating case, but the largest benchmark/test shape in `shapes.py`/`ir_test_utils.py` is `16384x16384` (num_tokens max 16384, hidden max 16384); no 32768-token case exists. (2) A stale/incorrect justifying comment misleads future maintainers about why the float16 tolerance was loosened and whether it is still needed. (3) Update the comment to reference an actual configured shape (e.g. 16384x16384) or add the cited shape to the configs if it is the real trigger.

(1) `_bench_one` clones inputs exactly once (`_clone_args`) before the timing loop; the code's own TODO acknowledges that once inplace ops land, both CUDA-graph and eager paths will accumulate drift from repeated in-place mutation across replays/iterations. (2) For non-inplace ops today this is correct, but the benchmark will silently produce meaningless (drifting) results for inplace ops once they exist, and the TODO defers a real correctness requirement. (3) Acceptable as documented deferral, but ensure the inplace re-clone-per-iteration is implemented before any inplace op is added to SHAPE_CONFIGS, ideally guarded by an assertion that rejects inplace ops until then.

(1) The refactor replaces the `torch.testing.assert_close(..., rtol=get_default_rtol(...), atol=1e-3)` comparisons in `test_impls` with the new `assert_close` helper (dtype-driven tolerances), but the `get_default_rtol` import is retained. (2) If `get_default_rtol` is no longer referenced anywhere in the file it becomes a dead import (lint/noise); if it is still used in the truncated portion this is a non-issue. (3) Verify remaining usages and drop the import if it is now unused.
